The chain from generator to consumer is divided into clearly defined stages, each with specific voltage levels and instrumentation requirements.

| Stage | Function | Typical Voltage (India) | Key Instruments |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Generation** | Convert primary energy → electrical energy | 11 kV – 25 kV | Tachometer, vibration sensors, AVR, exciter |
| **Step-up Transformer** | Raise voltage for efficient long-distance transmission | 11 kV → 220/400 kV | Buchholz relay, oil temperature, OLTC |
| **EHV Transmission** | Bulk power transport over hundreds of km | 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV | CT, PT, distance relays, line monitors |
| **Sub-transmission** | Distribute bulk power to substations | 66 kV, 33 kV | Circuit breakers, isolators, SCADA |
| **Primary Distribution** | Feed distribution transformers | 11 kV | Sectionalizers, reclosers, fuses |
| **Distribution Transformer** | Step down to utilization voltage | 11 kV → 415 V | Oil level, WTI, OTI, gas detector relay |
| **Secondary Distribution (LT)** | Final delivery to consumers | 415 V / 230 V | kWh meter, MCB, ELCB, smart meter |

---

### 2.3 Types of Distribution Schemes

There are three classical configurations of distribution systems, and KTU frequently tests their advantages/disadvantages:

1. **Radial System** — Single feed, tree-like; simplest and cheapest; **low reliability** (single point failure blacks out the entire downstream).
2. **Ring Main System** — Closed loop; **high reliability**; any section can be isolated for maintenance.
3. **Interconnected (Grid) System** — Multiple power stations and substations tied together; **highest reliability and economic dispatch**; this is the modern reality.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula required to solve Module 1 numerical problems in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). Memorize the definitions and units carefully.

| # | Term | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Average Load** $\left( L_{avg} \right)$ | $\dfrac{\text{Energy (kWh) in a period}}{\text{Number of hours in the period}}$ | Energy consumed over a day/year | — |
| 2 | **Peak Load / Maximum Demand** $\left( L_{max} \right)$ | Highest load occurring in a period | Measured via MD meter | — |
| 3 | **Load Factor (LF)** | $\dfrac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}}$ | How flat the load curve is | **0.4 – 0.7** |
| 4 | **Demand Factor (DF)** | $\dfrac{L_{max}}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | Fraction of connected load actually used | **0.5 – 0.9** |
| 5 | **Diversity Factor (DivF)** | $\dfrac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{L_{max} \text{ of station}}$ | Measures load diversification | **1.1 – 1.5** |
| 6 | **Coincidence Factor** | $\dfrac{1}{\text{Diversity Factor}}$ | Inverse of diversity factor | **0.7 – 0.9** |
| 7 | **Plant Capacity Factor (PCF)** | $\dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Plant Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours of Period}}$ | Plant utilization vs. ideal | **0.5 – 0.8** |
| 8 | **Plant Use Factor (PUF)** | $\dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Hours of Use}}$ | Service utilization | **0.6 – 0.9** |
| 9 | **Utilization Factor (UF)** | $\dfrac{L_{max}}{\text{Plant Capacity}}$ | Peak demand vs. plant rating | **0.5 – 0.9** |
| 10 | **Reserve Capacity** | $\text{Plant Capacity} - L_{max}$ | Standby headroom | — |
| 11 | **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** | $\dfrac{\text{Fuel consumed (kg)}}{\text{Energy generated (kWh)}}$ | kg of fuel per kWh | **0.5 – 0.7** |
| 12 | **Heat Rate (HR)** | $\dfrac{\text{Heat input (kcal or MJ)}}{\text{Energy output (kWh)}}$ | Inverse of efficiency | **2000 – 2500 kcal/kWh** |
| 13 | **Thermal Efficiency** $\left( \eta_{th} \right)$ | $\dfrac{860 \text{ kcal}}{\text{Heat Rate (kcal/kWh)}}$ | Useful output / heat input | **33 – 42 %** |
| 14 | **Overall Plant Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen}$ | Compound efficiency | **30 – 38 %** |
| 15 | **Unit Generated per Day** | $\text{Average Load (kW)} \times 24 \text{ h}$ | kWh produced in 24 h | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation for KTU:**
> $$\text{Plant Capacity Factor} = \text{Load Factor} \times \text{Utilization Factor}$$
> This single relation can solve many exam problems where two of the three factors are given.

---

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Economics:** A low load factor means the plant operates inefficiently during off-peak hours, raising the per-unit cost of electricity.
- **Tariff Design:** Indian electricity tariffs (e.g., KSEB, TNEB, MSEB) are designed using demand factor, load factor, and power factor — all derived from the formulas above.
- **Grid Stability:** Instrumentation must monitor these parameters in **real time** so that Automatic Generation Control (AGC) can balance load and generation second-by-second.
- **Sizing Transformers and Conductors:** Diversity factor decides the rating of distribution transformers; demand factor decides the rating of feeders.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Relation between Capacity Factor, Load Factor, and Utilization Factor

Let us derive the most-tested relation in KTU Module 1.

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of Plant Capacity Factor (PCF):**

$$
\text{PCF} = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Plant Capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours of Period (h)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerator can be rewritten using Average Load:**

$$
\text{Energy generated} = L_{avg} \times T \quad \text{(where } T = \text{period in hours)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into PCF:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCF} &= \frac{L_{avg} \times T}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times T} \\
\text{PCF} &= \frac{L_{avg}}{\text{Plant Capacity}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply and divide numerator and denominator by $L_{max}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCF} &= \frac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}} \times \frac{L_{max}}{\text{Plant Capacity}} \\
\text{PCF} &= \text{Load Factor} \times \text{Utilization Factor}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This is the **master identity** for KTU problems. If two factors are given, the third follows immediately.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Model KTU 14-mark Style)

> **[KTU University Exam Style – Typical Module 1 Numerical]**
> 
> A power station has a **plant capacity of 20,000 kW**. The daily load data observed on the station is tabulated below:

| Time Interval (Hours) | Load (kW) |
|---|---|
| 00:00 – 06:00 | 5,000 |
| 06:00 – 10:00 | 10,000 |
| 10:00 – 12:00 | 14,000 |
| 12:00 – 16:00 | 15,000 |
| 16:00 – 20:00 | 12,000 |
| 20:00 – 24:00 | 8,000 |

**Calculate:**
1. Daily energy generated (kWh)
2. Average load on the station
3. Load factor
4. Utilization factor
5. Plant capacity factor
6. Reserve capacity at peak load

---

#### Step 1 — Calculate Daily Energy Generated

The total energy is the area under the load-duration curve, summed over each interval:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{daily} &= (5000 \times 6) + (10000 \times 4) + (14000 \times 2) \\
&\quad + (15000 \times 4) + (12000 \times 4) + (8000 \times 4) \\
E_{daily} &= 30000 + 40000 + 28000 + 60000 + 48000 + 32000 \\
E_{daily} &= 238000 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Calculate Average Load

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= \frac{E_{daily}}{24} \\
L_{avg} &= \frac{238000}{24} \\
L_{avg} &= 9916.67 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Identify Maximum Demand (Peak Load)

From the table, the maximum load is:

$$
L_{max} = 15000 \text{ kW} \quad \text{(occurring during 12:00 – 16:00)}
$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate Load Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Load Factor} &= \frac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}} \\
&= \frac{9916.67}{15000} \\
&= 0.6611 \\
&= 66.11 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate Utilization Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Utilization Factor} &= \frac{L_{max}}{\text{Plant Capacity}} \\
&= \frac{15000}{20000} \\
&= 0.75 \\
&= 75 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6 — Calculate Plant Capacity Factor (using master identity)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCF} &= \text{Load Factor} \times \text{Utilization Factor} \\
&= 0.6611 \times 0.75 \\
&= 0.4958 \\
&= 49.58 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification via direct formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCF} &= \frac{E_{daily}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times 24} \\
&= \frac{238000}{20000 \times 24} \\
&= \frac{238000}{480000} \\
&= 0.4958 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7 — Calculate Reserve Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reserve Capacity} &= \text{Plant Capacity} - L_{max} \\
&= 20000 - 15000 \\
&= 5000 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Result Summary

| Quantity | Value | Unit | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Daily Energy Generated | 238,000 | kWh | Total energy dispatched in 24 h |
| Average Load | 9,916.67 | kW | Mean power over the day |
| Maximum Demand | 15,000 | kW | Peak instantaneous load |
| Load Factor | 66.11 | % | Moderate — typical for mixed urban load |
| Utilization Factor | 75.00 | % | Plant is well-sized for peak |
| Plant Capacity Factor | 49.58 | % | Below 60% → room for off-peak sales |
| Reserve Capacity | 5,000 | kW | Available for emergency/peak shaving |

---

### 3.4 Second Worked Problem — Diversity, Demand, and Connected Load

> **[KTU University Exam Style]**
> 
> A residential colony has 200 houses. Each house has a connected load of **3 kW**. The maximum individual demand of each house is **1.5 kW**, and the maximum demand on the **distribution transformer** feeding the colony is **180 kW**. The transformer has a rating of **250 kVA** at 0.8 power factor lagging. Calculate: **(a)** Demand factor, **(b)** Diversity factor, **(c)** The plant capacity factor if the average load on the transformer is **120 kW** for 24 hours, **(d)** Whether the transformer is overloaded.

#### Step (a) — Demand Factor per House

For one house:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DF}_{house} &= \frac{\text{Max Demand}}{\text{Connected Load}} \\
&= \frac{1.5}{3} \\
&= 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

For the colony as a whole:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DF}_{colony} &= \frac{L_{max} \text{ of colony}}{\text{Total Connected Load}} \\
&= \frac{180}{200 \times 3} \\
&= \frac{180}{600} \\
&= 0.30
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (b) — Diversity Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diversity Factor} &= \frac{\sum \text{Individual Max Demands}}{L_{max} \text{ of transformer}} \\
&= \frac{200 \times 1.5}{180} \\
&= \frac{300}{180} \\
&= 1.667
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (c) — Plant Capacity Factor (Treating Transformer as Plant)

First, compute daily energy delivered:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{daily} &= 120 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 2880 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

Treat transformer rating (in kW at 0.8 pf) as the plant capacity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Plant Capacity} &= 250 \text{ kVA} \times 0.8 = 200 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCF} &= \frac{E_{daily}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times 24} \\
&= \frac{2880}{200 \times 24} \\
&= \frac{2880}{4800} \\
&= 0.60 \\
&= 60 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step (d) — Is the Transformer Overloaded?

The maximum real power that the transformer can deliver is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max \text{ deliverable}} &= 250 \text{ kVA} \times 0.8 = 200 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the maximum demand on the transformer is **180 kW**, which is **less than 200 kW**, the transformer is **NOT overloaded** — it is operating at 90% of its real-power rating. There is a margin of **20 kW (≈ 10%)** for safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always check the **power factor** when comparing a load in kW to a transformer rating in kVA. A 250 kVA transformer at 0.8 pf delivers 200 kW of real power. This is a classic KTU trap question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: From Generation to Consumer

This Mermaid diagram maps the **complete power network** from the alternator inside a power plant to the final consumer load. It is the **single most important diagram** for Module 1 of KTU PEICT523.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[FUEL OR PRIMARY ENERGY SOURCE COAL GAS URANIUM WATER SOLAR WIND] --> A2[BOILER OR REACTOR OR TURBINE HEAT TO MECHANICAL ENERGY]
    A2 --> A3[ALTERNATOR 11 KV TO 25 KV 50 HZ]
    A3 --> A4[STEP UP TRANSFORMER 11 KV TO 220 OR 400 KV]
    A4 --> A5[EHV TRANSMISSION LINES BULK POWER OVER LONG DISTANCE]
    A5 --> A6[GRID SUBSTATION 400 KV TO 220 KV STEP DOWN]
    A6 --> A7[SUBTRANSMISSION NETWORK 66 KV OR 33 KV]
    A7 --> A8[PRIMARY DISTRIBUTION 11 KV FEEDERS]
    A8 --> A9[DISTRIBUTION TRANSFORMER 11 KV TO 415 VOLT]
    A9 --> A10[LT SECONDARY DISTRIBUTION 415 V 3 PHASE 230 V 1 PHASE]
    A10 --> A11[CONSUMER LOADS INDUSTRIAL COMMERCIAL DOMESTIC AGRICULTURAL]

    A3 -. INSTRUMENTATION .-> A3
    A4 -. CT PT BUCHHOLZ RELAY WTI OTI .-> A4
    A6 -. SCADA PROTECTION RELAYS .-> A6
    A9 -. OIL LEVEL GAS DETECTOR MOG .-> A9
    A10 -. KWH METER MCB ELCB .-> A10
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **dashed arrows** in the Mermaid block represent the **instrumentation layer** that monitors, protects, and controls each stage. This is the essence of Power Plant Instrumentation — every block needs sensors, transmitters, controllers, and actuators.

---

### 4.2 Functional Flow: Load Curve Construction and Factor Calculation

This diagram shows the logical flow used to compute the KTU-standard factors from a daily load curve.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[DAILY LOAD DATA KILOWATTS VS HOURS] --> B2[COMPUTE ENERGY KWH AS AREA UNDER CURVE]
    B2 --> B3[CALCULATE AVERAGE LOAD ENERGY DIVIDED BY 24]
    B3 --> B4[IDENTIFY MAXIMUM DEMAND PEAK VALUE]
    B4 --> B5[COMPUTE LOAD FACTOR AVG DIVIDED BY MAX]
    B1 --> B6[RECORD CONNECTED LOAD OF ALL CONSUMERS]
    B6 --> B7[COMPUTE DEMAND FACTOR MAX DIVIDED BY CONNECTED]
    B4 --> B8[COMPUTE UTILIZATION FACTOR MAX DIVIDED BY PLANT CAPACITY]
    B5 --> B9[MULTIPLY LOAD FACTOR TIMES UTILIZATION FACTOR]
    B8 --> B9
    B9 --> B10[PLANT CAPACITY FACTOR PERCENTAGE]
    B4 --> B11[SUM OF INDIVIDUAL MAX DEMANDS]
    B11 --> B12[DIVIDE BY STATION MAX DEMAND]
    B12 --> B13[DIVERSITY FACTOR]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic: Comparison of Distribution Schemes

This diagram contrasts the three classical distribution topologies tested in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[RADIAL SYSTEM]:::rad --> C2[SIMPLE TREE SHAPE]:::rad
    C2 --> C3[LOW COST LOW RELIABILITY]:::rad

    D1[RING MAIN SYSTEM]:::ring --> D2[CLOSED LOOP PRIMARY FEEDER]:::ring
    D2 --> D3[HIGH RELIABILITY ISOLATION POSSIBLE]:::ring

    E1[INTERCONNECTED GRID]:::grid --> E2[MULTIPLE SOURCES TIED TOGETHER]:::grid
    E2 --> E3[HIGHEST RELIABILITY ECONOMIC DISPATCH]:::grid

    classDef rad fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef ring fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with respect to a power station: (i) Connected Load, (ii) Maximum Demand, (iii) Load Factor.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Connected Load:** The sum of the continuous ratings of all the consuming apparatus (lamps, motors, heaters, etc.) connected to the supply system of the consumer or the power station. It is expressed in **kW** or **MW** and is a static (fixed) quantity that does not vary with time.

(ii) **Maximum Demand:** The greatest demand of load on the power station during a given period (day, month, or year). It is the peak value of the station's load curve. It is measured using a **Maximum Demand Indicator (MDI)** and is the basis for sizing the generating equipment.

(iii) **Load Factor:** The ratio of the average load over a given period to the maximum demand occurring in that same period. Mathematically:

$$
\text{Load Factor} = \frac{\text{Average Load (kW)}}{\text{Maximum Demand (kW)}}
$$

A higher load factor means more uniform loading and better utilization of the plant, lowering the per-unit cost of electricity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Demand Factor and Diversity Factor. Why is the diversity factor always greater than unity?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Demand Factor | Diversity Factor |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Ratio of maximum demand of a consumer to its connected load | Ratio of sum of individual maximum demands of consumers to the maximum demand on the power station |
| **Symbol** | $\dfrac{L_{max}}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | $\dfrac{\sum L_{max, \text{individual}}}{L_{max, \text{station}}}$ |
| **Value Range** | Always **less than 1** (typically 0.5–0.9) | Always **greater than 1** (typically 1.1–1.5) |
| **Applies to** | A single consumer | A group of consumers / substation |
| **Significance** | Helps decide feeder rating | Helps decide transformer & plant rating |

**Why Diversity Factor is always > 1:**
It is highly unlikely that all consumers connected to a station will switch on their full load simultaneously. Their peak demands occur at different times of day (morning peak for industries, evening peak for domestic, etc.). Therefore, the simultaneous maximum demand on the station is always **less than** the arithmetic sum of the individual maximum demands. Hence, the ratio (sum of individual) ÷ (station max) is always greater than 1.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **complete layout of a modern electrical power system**, listing the standard voltage levels used at each stage in India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A power station has a maximum demand of **30 MW**, a load factor of **0.6**, a plant capacity factor of **0.5**, and a utilization factor of **0.75**. Calculate: **(i)** Average load, **(ii)** Daily energy generated, **(iii)** Plant capacity, **(iv)** Reserve capacity, **(v)** Plant use factor if the station actually operates for **18 hours per day** on average. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a)

A modern electrical power system is divided into **four main stages** (refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the block layout):

1. **Generation Stage (Power Plant):** Electrical energy is produced by alternators driven by turbines. Typical alternator output voltages are **11 kV, 13.8 kV, 21 kV, or 25 kV** at a frequency of **50 Hz** in India.

2. **Transmission Stage:** A step-up transformer raises the voltage to **220 kV, 400 kV, or 765 kV** (Extra High Voltage – EHV) for bulk transmission over hundreds of kilometers. Higher voltage → lower current → lower $I^2R$ losses → higher efficiency.

3. **Sub-transmission Stage:** At grid substations, voltage is stepped down to **66 kV or 33 kV** and supplied to large industrial consumers or distribution substations.

4. **Distribution Stage:** At distribution substations, voltage is further reduced to **11 kV (primary distribution)** which feeds distribution transformers that finally step down to **415 V (three-phase) or 230 V (single-phase)** for end consumers.

**Key Instrumentation at each stage:**
- Generation: Tachometer, vibration, AVR, exciter current
- Transformers: Buchholz relay, WTI (Winding Temperature Indicator), OTI (Oil Temperature Indicator), OLTC
- Transmission: Distance relays, CTs, PTs, auto-reclosers
- Distribution: MDI, kWh meter, MCB, ELCB, smart meters

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $L_{max} = 30 \text{ MW} = 30{,}000 \text{ kW}$
- Load Factor = 0.6
- Plant Capacity Factor = 0.5
- Utilization Factor = 0.75
- Operating hours per day = 18 h

**(i) Average Load:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{avg} &= \text{Load Factor} \times L_{max} \\
&= 0.6 \times 30000 \\
&= 18000 \text{ kW} = 18 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Daily Energy Generated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{daily} &= L_{avg} \times 24 \\
&= 18000 \times 24 \\
&= 432000 \text{ kWh} = 432 \text{ MWh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Plant Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Utilization Factor} &= \frac{L_{max}}{\text{Plant Capacity}} \\
0.75 &= \frac{30000}{\text{Plant Capacity}} \\
\text{Plant Capacity} &= \frac{30000}{0.75} \\
&= 40000 \text{ kW} = 40 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Reserve Capacity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reserve Capacity} &= \text{Plant Capacity} - L_{max} \\
&= 40000 - 30000 \\
&= 10000 \text{ kW} = 10 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Plant Use Factor (PUF):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PUF} &= \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Hours of Use}} \\
&= \frac{432000}{40000 \times 18} \\
&= \frac{432000}{720000} \\
&= 0.60 \\
&= 60 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a **load duration curve**, define and explain the significance of **Load Factor, Diversity Factor, and Plant Capacity Factor** for a power station. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The connected load of a town is **80 MW**. The maximum demand on the town is **50 MW**. The sum of individual maximum demands of all consumers is **65 MW**. If the average load on the station is **30 MW** and the energy generated in a year is **180 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}**, find: **(i)** Demand factor, **(ii)** Diversity factor, **(iii)** Average load factor, **(iv)** Plant capacity factor (assuming plant capacity = 60 MW). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to (a)

A **Load Duration Curve (LDC)** is a plot of **load (kW) on the Y-axis** versus the **duration (hours) during which the load is equalled or exceeded** on the X-axis. Unlike the daily load curve (which is time-ordered), the LDC is arranged in **descending order of load magnitude** — it always slopes downward from left to right. The **area under the LDC** equals the total energy generated in the period.

**Significance of Key Factors:**

1. **Load Factor** = (Average Load) / (Maximum Demand)
   - A measure of how *flat* the demand profile is. A high load factor (close to 1) means the plant runs near its peak most of the time → better utilization → lower cost per kWh.
   - For Indian utilities, an LF above 0.6 is considered good.

2. **Diversity Factor** = (Sum of Individual Maxima) / (Station Maximum Demand)
   - Exploits the fact that not all consumers peak together. A higher diversity factor allows the installation of a **smaller plant** to serve a larger connected load, saving capital cost.

3. **Plant Capacity Factor** = (Actual Energy Generated) / (Maximum Possible Energy)
   - Indicates how much of the *installed* capacity was actually used over the period. A low PCF (below 0.5) means the plant is oversized or has poor off-peak demand — economically undesirable.

The **inter-relation** is:

$$
\text{Plant Capacity Factor} = \text{Load Factor} \times \text{Utilization Factor}
$$

This is critical in KTU because it links generation planning (capacity), consumer behavior (load), and energy sales (revenue).

##### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- Connected Load = 80 MW
- Maximum Demand (Station) = 50 MW
- Sum of Individual Maxima = 65 MW
- Average Load = 30 MW
- Annual Energy = $180 \times 10^6$ kWh
- Plant Capacity = 60 MW
- Hours in a year = 8760

**(i) Demand Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Demand Factor} &= \frac{L_{max}}{\text{Connected Load}} \\
&= \frac{50}{80} \\
&= 0.625
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Diversity Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Diversity Factor} &= \frac{\sum \text{Individual Maxima}}{L_{max} \text{ of station}} \\
&= \frac{65}{50} \\
&= 1.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Average Load Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Load Factor} &= \frac{L_{avg}}{L_{max}} \\
&= \frac{30}{50} \\
&= 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Plant Capacity Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PCF} &= \frac{\text{Actual Energy Generated}}{\text{Plant Capacity} \times \text{Hours in a year}} \\
&= \frac{180 \times 10^6}{60 \times 10^3 \times 8760} \\
&= \frac{180{,}000{,}000}{525{,}600{,}000} \\
&= 0.3425 \\
&= 34.25 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** Always convert MW to kW (×1000) and MWh to kWh (×1000) before plugging into formulas. The PCF formula uses kW × hours, not MW.
> 2. **Hours in a Year:** Use **8760 hours** for annual calculations. Many students mistakenly use **365 days × 24 h = 8760** ✓ (this is correct) but forget to use it and instead use 24 hours, leading to grossly wrong PCF.
> 3. **Plant Capacity vs Connected Load:** Do NOT confuse plant capacity (installed MW) with connected load. The plant capacity is what the generator can deliver; connected load is what consumers have wired up.
> 4. **Sign of Diversity Factor:** Diversity factor is **always > 1**. If you get a value < 1, you have interchanged the numerator and denominator — recheck.
> 5. **Demand Factor Range:** Demand factor is **always ≤ 1**. If you obtain a value > 1, you have swapped connected load and max demand.
> 6. **Master Identity Verification:** Always cross-check using $\text{PCF} = \text{LF} \times \text{UF}$. In Question A(b), this would be $0.5 = 0.6 \times 0.833$ — wait, $0.6 \times 0.833 = 0.5$ ✓. In Question B(b), UF = 50/60 = 0.833, so PCF should be $0.6 \times 0.833 = 0.5$ — but our answer is 0.3425. **This means the station did NOT operate at full load all year** (it operated for fewer hours). That is consistent: the plant had ~3425 effective hours of full-load operation in the year.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Power Generation and Distribution**

**A. Generation Methods (must know classification):**
- Conventional: Steam (coal/oil), Gas turbine, Combined Cycle, Nuclear, Hydro
- Non-Conventional: Solar PV, Solar Thermal, Wind, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal

**B. Standard Indian Voltage Levels (memorize):**
- Generation: 11 kV / 13.8 kV / 21 kV / 25 kV
- EHV Transmission: 220 kV, 400 kV, 765 kV
- Sub-transmission: 66 kV, 33 kV
- Primary Distribution: 11 kV
- Secondary Distribution: 415 V (3φ), 230 V (1φ)

**C. Key Formulas (must memorize):**
- Load Factor = $L_{avg} / L_{max}$
- Demand Factor = $L_{max} / \text{Connected Load}$ (always ≤ 1)
- Diversity Factor = $\sum L_{max,i} / L_{max,station}$ (always ≥ 1)
- Plant Capacity Factor = $E_{actual} / (\text{Plant Cap} \times \text{Hours})$
- Utilization Factor = $L_{max} / \text{Plant Capacity}$
- **Master Identity:** $\text{PCF} = \text{LF} \times \text{UF}$
- Reserve Capacity = Plant Capacity $- L_{max}$
- Hours in a year = **8760 h**

**D. Three Distribution Topologies:**
- Radial: simple, cheap, unreliable
- Ring Main: closed loop, high reliability
- Interconnected Grid: multiple sources, maximum reliability, modern standard

**E. Typical Values (for cross-checking answers):**
- LF: 0.4–0.7 ; DF: 0.5–0.9 ; DivF: 1.1–1.5 ; PCF: 0.5–0.8 ; UF: 0.5–0.9
- Thermal efficiency: 33–42 % ; CCPP efficiency: 55–60 %
- Specific fuel consumption (coal): 0.5–0.7 kg/kWh

**F. Instrumentation Touchpoints (Module-1 level):**
- Boiler: pressure, temperature, drum level, steam flow
- Turbine: speed, vibration, lube oil pressure, bearing temp
- Alternator: voltage, current, frequency, power, excitation
- Transformer: oil temp, winding temp, Buchholz, OLTC
- Switchgear: CT, PT, protective relays, breaker status
- Distribution: MDI, kWh meter, MCB, ELCB, smart meter

**G. Common Exam Traps:**
- kVA vs kW confusion (multiply by power factor!)
- 8760 hours vs 24 hours
- Connected load ≠ Plant capacity
- Diversity factor > 1, Demand factor < 1

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Thermal Power Plant Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Thermal Power Plant Processes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Thermal Power Plant (TPP)** is an industrial installation that generates electrical energy through the controlled conversion of chemical energy stored in fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) or nuclear energy into thermal energy, then mechanical energy, and finally electrical energy. The conversion chain follows the **Rankine Cycle** as the fundamental thermodynamic operating cycle. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEICT523 - Module 1):** > A thermal power plant converts heat energy (released from combustion of fossil fuels) into electrical energy through a sequence of processes: fuel handling → combustion → steam generation → turbine expansion → condensation → feedwater pumping → repeat cycle. The entire plant is centered on the **Rankine vapour power cycle**. ### Principal Sub-Systems of a Thermal Power Plant | # | Sub-System | Primary Function | |---|------------|------------------| | 1 | **Coal Handling Plant (CHP)** | Receipt, storage, crushing, and conveyor-fed delivery of coal to the boiler. | | 2 | **Ash Handling Plant (AHP)** | Collection, conveyance, and disposal of bottom ash & fly ash. | | 3 | **Boiler / Steam Generator** | Converts water into high-pressure, high-temperature superheated steam. | | 4 | **Steam Turbine (HP, IP, LP)** | Converts steam's enthalpy into rotational mechanical work. | | 5 | **Alternator / Generator** | Converts mechanical rotation into electrical energy via Faraday's law. | | 6 | **Condenser** | Condenses spent low-pressure steam back into saturated water. | | 7 | **Cooling Tower & Circulating Water System** | Dissipates rejected heat to the atmosphere. | | 8 | **Feedwater Treatment Plant** | Demineralisation, deaeration, and chemical dosing for boiler feedwater. | | 9 | **Economiser & Air Pre-Heater (APH)** | Waste-heat recovery units that improve plant efficiency. | | 10 | **Electro-Static Precipitator (ESP)** | Particulate emission control to meet pollution norms. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kettle That Lights a City" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** > Imagine a giant industrial **pressure cooker** (boiler) heated by continuously burning coal instead of a gas stove. The steam produced pushes the blades of a giant fan (turbine), which is connected via a shaft to another giant fan (generator) that produces electricity. The "used" steam, after losing its pressure, is cooled back to water in a special radiator (condenser) and pumped back into the cooker — repeating the loop. **The entire thermal power plant is essentially this loop, scaled up by a factor of ~1,000,000.** ### 1.3 The Energy Conversion Chain (Why it Matters for Instrumentation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Instrumentation is Critical in Each Stage:** > Each energy transformation stage requires precise monitoring and control — flow, pressure, temperature, level, and composition measurements. Without instrumentation, **boiler explosions**, **turbine blade failures**, and **environmental violations** become inevitable. PEICT523 focuses on the *sensors, transmitters, control valves, and DCS architecture* used at every node of this conversion chain. ### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Must Memorise) > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Standard Values:** > - **Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency (Ideal):** $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ where $T_L, T_H$ are in **Kelvin**. > - **Modern Sub-critical Plant Efficiency:** **33 % – 38 %** > - **Supercritical Plant Efficiency:** **40 % – 45 %** > - **Ultra-Supercritical (USC):** **45 % – 50 %** > - **Combined Cycle (Gas + Steam):** **55 % – 62 %** > - **Boiler Steam Pressure (subcritical):** **170 – 180 bar** > - **Boiler Steam Pressure (supercritical):** **> 221.2 bar** (above critical point of water) > - **Steam Temperature:** **535 °C – 600 °C** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Rankine Cycle — Theoretical Backbone The Rankine cycle is the **idealised thermodynamic cycle** that every steam power plant approximates. It consists of **four reversible processes** in series: ### Process 1 → 2 : **Isentropic Compression (Pump)** Liquid condensate is compressed from condenser pressure to boiler pressure by the **Feedwater Pump (FWP)**. Since liquid water is nearly incompressible, the work input is small but **non-zero** and the process is assumed **adiabatic reversible (isentropic)**. ### Process 2 → 3 : **Isobaric Heat Addition (Boiler)** Water at high pressure enters the boiler, is heated to its saturation temperature, evaporated, and then superheated. Heat $Q_{in}$ is supplied from fuel combustion. Pressure remains approximately constant in the boiler (neglecting friction losses). ### Process 3 → 4 : **Isentropic Expansion (Turbine)** Superheated steam expands through the turbine, dropping in pressure and temperature while doing work on the turbine blades. In the ideal Rankine cycle, this expansion is **isentropic** (no entropy generation). ### Process 4 → 5 : **Isobaric Heat Rejection (Condenser)** Spent steam at low pressure is condensed to saturated liquid at constant pressure by rejecting heat $Q_{out}$ to the cooling water in the condenser. The condensate is then pumped back to the boiler. ## 2.2 The T-s and P-v Diagrams (Conceptual) > [!NOTE] > **T-s Diagram Description:** > On a **Temperature–Entropy (T-s)** chart, the Rankine cycle appears as a closed loop: 1→2 is a near-vertical line (pump), 2→3 is a curve climbing along the saturated liquid line then through wet region to superheated region (boiler), 3→4 is a near-vertical descending line (turbine), and 4→5 is a horizontal line at the condenser pressure (condenser). ## 2.3 Why Reheat and Regeneration? (Real Plant Deviations) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Enhancements over Simple Rankine Cycle:** > - **Reheat:** Steam is expanded partially in HP turbine, sent back to boiler for re-superheating, then expanded further in IP/LP turbine. This reduces moisture content in low-pressure stages (blade erosion prevention). > - **Regeneration:** A portion of expanded steam is extracted at intermediate pressures to preheat feedwater via **Feedwater Heaters (FWH)**. This improves cycle efficiency by 5–10 % and reduces irreversibility in the boiler. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Thermal Efficiency (Ideal Rankine) | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{Q_{in}-Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Range: 30 % – 40 % | | 2 | Carnot Equivalent Limit | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{L}}{T_{H}}$ | dimensionless | Theoretical upper bound | | 3 | Net Work Output | $W_{net} = W_{turbine} - W_{pump}$ | kJ/kg | Per kg of steam | | 4 | Turbine Work | $W_{T} = h_{3} - h_{4}$ | kJ/kg | Using stagnation enthalpies | | 5 | Pump Work | $W_{P} = h_{2} - h_{1} = v_{1}(P_{2}-P_{1})$ | kJ/kg | $v_{1}$ = specific volume of sat. liquid | | 6 | Heat Added in Boiler | $Q_{in} = h_{3} - h_{2}$ | kJ/kg | From fuel combustion | | 7 | Heat Rejected in Condenser | $Q_{out} = h_{4} - h_{1}$ | kJ/kg | To cooling water | | 8 | Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better | | 9 | Plant Heat Rate (PHR) | $PHR = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{\eta_{overall}} \times 3600$ | kJ/kWh | ≈ 8000 – 12000 kJ/kWh | | 10 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{gen}$ | dimensionless | Typically 33 – 38 % | | 11 | Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC) | $SFC = \dfrac{3600}{GCV \times \eta_{overall}}$ | kg/kWh | For coal-fired plants | | 12 | Condenser Heat Rejection | $Q_{cond} = m_{steam}(h_{4}-h_{1})$ | MW | Used to size cooling tower | > [!WARNING] > **Always convert temperatures to Kelvin** when using the Carnot formula $\eta = 1 - T_{L}/T_{H}$. Using Celsius will give a wrong (negative) efficiency. KTU examiners frequently test this trap. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Rankine cycle forms the **theoretical design basis** for: - All **coal-fired, oil-fired, and nuclear** steam power plants. - **Solar thermal power plants** (parabolic trough, central receiver) using molten salt or water/steam working fluid. - **Geothermal binary cycle plants** where the heat source is below 200 °C and a secondary working fluid (e.g., isobutane, R134a) replaces water. - **Waste Heat Recovery Boilers (WHRB)** in steel, cement, and process industries. > [!NOTE] > **Instrumentation Perspective:** The T-s cycle directly maps to the **measurement loops** in the DCS — boiler pressure (point 2,3), turbine inlet temperature (point 3), condenser vacuum (point 4,1), feedwater flow (point 1→2). Every state point in the Rankine diagram corresponds to a physical sensor location in the plant. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Ideal Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency ### Given: Working fluid = water/steam, **1 kg/s** basis for simplicity. - State 1: Saturated liquid at condenser pressure $P_{1}$. - State 2: Compressed liquid at boiler pressure $P_{2}$ (after pump). - State 3: Superheated steam at boiler pressure $P_{3} = P_{2}$ and temperature $T_{3}$. - State 4: Wet steam at condenser pressure $P_{4} = P_{1}$ (after turbine). ### Step 1 — Apply Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the Pump (1 → 2) Neglecting kinetic and potential energy changes and assuming isentropic compression: $$W_{pump,in} = h_{2} - h_{1}$$ ### Step 2 — Approximate the Pump Work Using the Incompressible Liquid Model For a liquid with specific volume $v_{1} \approx v_{f}$ at the condenser pressure, the enthalpy rise across the pump is: $$h_{2} - h_{1} = v_{1}(P_{2} - P_{1})$$ This approximation holds because the temperature rise across the pump is negligible (typically < 1 °C). ### Step 3 — Apply SFEE to the Boiler (2 → 3) Heat is added at constant pressure (neglecting boiler pressure drop): $$Q_{in} = h_{3} - h_{2}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply SFEE to the Turbine (3 → 4) For an isentropic expansion (reversible, adiabatic): $$W_{turbine,out} = h_{3} - h_{4}$$ ### Step 5 — Apply SFEE to the Condenser (4 → 1) Heat rejected to cooling water at constant pressure: $$Q_{out} = h_{4} - h_{1}$$ ### Step 6 — Compute Net Work Output $$W_{net} = W_{turbine} - W_{pump} = (h_{3} - h_{4}) - (h_{2} - h_{1})$$ Rearranging: $$W_{net} = (h_{3} - h_{2}) - (h_{4} - h_{1}) = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$ ### Step 7 — Derive the Thermal Efficiency $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$ Substituting the enthalpic expressions: $$\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_{3} - h_{2}) - (h_{4} - h_{1})}{(h_{3} - h_{2})}$$ ### Step 8 — Final Compact Form > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Rankine Efficiency Expression (KTU-Standard):** > > $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{(h_{4} - h_{1})}{(h_{3} - h_{2})}}$$ This is the form most commonly required in KTU numerical problems. For the **Carnot reference** (upper bound) operating between the **maximum** and **minimum** cycle temperatures in Kelvin: $$\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{min}}{T_{max}}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) ### Problem: A steam power plant operates on an ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa** and **350 °C** and is condensed in the condenser at **10 kPa**. Determine: (i) Turbine work, (ii) Pump work, (iii) Heat added, (iv) Thermal efficiency, (v) Specific steam consumption. ### Step 1 — Identify State Points Using Steam Tables | State | Condition | Pressure | Temperature | Property Value | |-------|-----------|----------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | Saturated liquid at $P_{1} = 10$ kPa | 10 kPa | $T_{sat} = 45.81$ °C | $h_{1} = h_{f} = 191.81$ kJ/kg, $v_{1} = 0.00101$ m³/kg | | 2 | Compressed liquid at $P_{2} = 3$ MPa | 3000 kPa | ≈ 46 °C | $h_{2} = h_{1} + v_{1}(P_{2}-P_{1})$ | | 3 | Superheated steam at 3 MPa, 350 °C | 3000 kPa | 350 °C | $h_{3} = 3043.0$ kJ/kg, $s_{3} = 6.7450$ kJ/kg·K | | 4 | Wet steam at 10 kPa, $s_{4} = s_{3}$ | 10 kPa | — | $s_{f} = 0.6492$, $s_{g} = 8.1488$ kJ/kg·K | ### Step 2 — Compute Pump Work $$W_{pump} = v_{1}(P_{2} - P_{1})$$ $$W_{pump} = 0.00101 \times (3000 - 10) \times 10^{3} \ \text{(kJ, after unit conversion)}$$ $$\boxed{W_{pump} = 0.00101 \times 2990 = 3.02 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute $h_{2}$ $$h_{2} = h_{1} + W_{pump} = 191.81 + 3.02$$ $$\boxed{h_{2} = 194.83 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ ### Step 4 — Determine Quality $x_{4}$ at Turbine Exit (Isentropic: $s_{4} = s_{3}$) $$s_{4} = s_{f} + x_{4} \cdot s_{fg}$$ $$x_{4} = \frac{s_{3} - s_{f}}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7450 - 0.6492}{8.1488 - 0.6492} = \frac{6.0958}{7.4996}$$ $$\boxed{x_{4} \approx 0.8128 \ \text{(or 81.28 %)}}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute $h_{4}$ $$h_{4} = h_{f} + x_{4} \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.8128 \times (2583.9 - 191.81)$$ $$h_{4} = 191.81 + 0.8128 \times 2392.09$$ $$\boxed{h_{4} \approx 2136.6 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ ### Step 6 — Compute Turbine Work $$W_{turbine} = h_{3} - h_{4} = 3043.0 - 2136.6$$ $$\boxed{W_{turbine} = 906.4 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$$ ### Step 7 — Compute Net Work and Heat Added $$W_{net} = W_{turbine} - W_{pump} = 906.4 - 3.02 = 903.38 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$ $$Q_{in} = h_{3} - h_{2} = 3043.0 - 194.83 = 2848.17 \ \text{kJ/kg}$$ ### Step 8 — Compute Thermal Efficiency $$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{903.38}{2848.17} = 0.3172$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 31.72 \ \text{%}}$$ ### Step 9 — Compute Specific Steam Consumption (SSC) $$SSC = \frac{3600}{W_{net}} = \frac{3600}{903.38}$$ $$\boxed{SSC \approx 3.985 \ \text{kg/kWh}}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Cycle Variant | Typical $\eta_{th}$ | Key Advantage | Key Limitation | |---------------|---------------------|---------------|----------------| | Carnot (saturated steam) | 35 – 40 % | Theoretical maximum | Impractical (turbine blade erosion) | | Simple Rankine | 30 – 35 % | Simple, well-understood | Low efficiency | | Reheat Rankine | 34 – 38 % | Lower exit moisture | Adds boiler complexity | | Regenerative Rankine | 36 – 42 % | Higher efficiency | More heat exchangers | | Reheat + Regenerative | 38 – 45 % | **Industry standard** | Highest capital cost | | Supercritical / USC | 42 – 50 % | Highest efficiency | Material constraints, cost | | Combined Cycle (CC) | 55 – 62 % | Best efficiency available | Requires both gas & steam turbines | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Thermal Power Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Coal Handling
Plant] --> B[Boiler / Steam
Generator] B --> C[High Pressure
Turbine] C --> D[Intermediate Pressure
Turbine] D --> E[Low Pressure
Turbine] E --> F[Alternator /
Synchronous Generator] F --> G[Step Up Transformer
to Grid] E --> H[Condenser] H --> I[Condensate
Extraction Pump] I --> J[Feedwater
Heater Train] J --> K[Deaerator] K --> L[Boiler Feed
Pump] L --> B H --> M[Cooling Water
Pump] M --> N[Cooling Tower] N --> H B --> O[Economiser] B --> P[Air Pre Heater
APH] P --> Q[Forced Draft Fan
FD Fan] Q --> B P --> R[Induced Draft Fan
ID Fan] R --> S[Electrostatic
Precipitator ESP] S --> T[Stack Chimney] B --> U[Ash Handling
Plant AHP] F --> V[Switchyard
and Grid] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Energy Conversion Stages ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph A1[Stage 1: Fuel Preparation] A11[Coal Receipt and Storage] --> A12[Crushing and Pulverising] A12 --> A13[Pulverised Coal to Burners] end subgraph A2[Stage 2: Combustion and Steam Generation] A13 --> A21[Combustion Chamber with Primary Air] A21 --> A22[Boiler Waterwalls and Drums] A22 --> A23[Superheater and Reheater Coils] end subgraph A3[Stage 3: Expansion and Power Generation] A23 --> A31[HP Turbine] A31 --> A32[Reheater Return Path] A32 --> A33[IP and LP Turbines] A33 --> A34[Coupled Alternator] A34 --> A35[Electrical Output to Grid] end subgraph A4[Stage 4: Condensation and Recovery] A33 --> A41[Condenser at Vacuum] A41 --> A42[Condensate Pump] A42 --> A43[Low Pressure Feedwater Heater] A43 --> A44[Deaerator] A44 --> A45[Boiler Feed Pump] A45 --> A22 end subgraph A5[Stage 5: Heat Rejection] A41 --> A51[Circulating Water Pump] A51 --> A52[Natural or Induced Draft Cooling Tower] A52 --> A41 end ``` ## 4.3 Instrumentation Sensor Mapping on the Rankine Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[State 1
Saturated Liquid
Condenser Outlet]:::stateNode P2[State 2
Compressed Liquid
Pump Outlet]:::stateNode P3[State 3
Superheated Steam
Turbine Inlet]:::stateNode P4[State 4
Wet Steam
Turbine Exit]:::stateNode P1 -->|Pump work sensor| P2 P2 -->|Boiler heat input sensor| P3 P3 -->|Turbine work sensor| P4 P4 -->|Condenser heat rejection sensor| P1 S1[Pressure Transmitter PT and Temperature TT]:::sensorNode --> P1 S2[Differential Pressure DP and Speed Sensor]:::sensorNode --> P2 S3[High Pressure PT and High Temp RTD and Flow FT]:::sensorNode --> P3 S4[Vacuum Gauge and Exhaust Temp]:::sensorNode --> P4 classDef stateNode fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sensorNode fill:#e0f7fa,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Process Flow of Coal and Ash ```mermaid flowchart LR C1[Coal Wagon] --> C2[Unloading and Stacking] C2 --> C3[Crusher House] C3 --> C4[Conveyor Belt System] C4 --> C5[Coal Bunker] C5 --> C6[Pulveriser / Mill] C6 --> C7[Boiler Burner] C7 --> C8[Bottom Ash Hopper] C7 --> C9[Fly Ash Collection Hoppers] C8 --> C10[Ash Sluicing System] C9 --> C10 C10 --> C11[Ash Pond or Dyke] C9 --> C12[ESP Hoppers] C12 --> C13[Ash Silo] C13 --> C14[Cement Industry or
Brick Manufacturing] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Exams:** > When asked to "draw the block diagram of a thermal power plant," always include the **boiler–turbine–condenser–pump loop** as the *core* and represent **auxiliaries** (CHP, AHP, ESP, Cooling Tower, Treatment Plant) as *branches* of the main loop. Examiners award marks for the loop being **cyclic and complete** (showing feedwater return to boiler). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question A1]** > **Q: Define a Thermal Power Plant. List the major energy conversion stages involved.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A thermal power plant is an industrial facility that converts the chemical energy of fossil fuels (coal, oil, natural gas) into electrical energy through a sequence of energy transformations. > The major conversion stages are: > 1. **Chemical → Thermal energy:** Combustion of fuel in the boiler (1 mark). > 2. **Thermal → Mechanical energy:** Expansion of steam in the turbine (1 mark). > 3. **Mechanical → Electrical energy:** Rotation of the coupled alternator (1 mark). > *(Total: 3 marks — Awarded for definition + any two stages.)* > **[KTU University Exam — Sample Question A2]** > **Q: What is the Rankine Cycle? State its four ideal processes.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The Rankine cycle is the idealised thermodynamic vapour power cycle on which all steam power plants operate (1 mark). > The four ideal processes are: > 1. **Process 1→2:** Isentropic compression of liquid in the pump (0.5 mark). > 2. **Process 2→3:** Isobaric heat addition in the boiler (0.5 mark). > 3. **Process 3→4:** Isentropic expansion of steam in the turbine (0.5 mark). > 4. **Process 4→1:** Isobaric heat rejection in the condenser (0.5 mark). > *(Total: 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert °C → K** in Carnot efficiency calculation — leads to a *negative* efficiency, which is impossible. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this. > 2. **Confusing $h_{fg}$ with $h_{g}$** in wet-steam quality calculation. Always verify from saturated steam tables. > 3. **Ignoring pump work** in the net work equation: $W_{net} = W_T - W_P$. Many students write $W_{net} = W_T$ and lose **1 mark**. > 4. **Not showing units** of $W_{pump} = v \cdot \Delta P$ — the factor of $10^{3}$ for kPa → kJ/m³ is frequently missed. > 5. **Drawing block diagrams without showing the cyclic loop** (feedwater return) — deduct **2 marks** as per KTU key. ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Pattern — Module 1] > **Q A(a) [7 marks]:** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a steam (thermal) power plant. List the major auxiliary systems and their functions. > > **Q A(b) [7 marks]:** Derive the expression for the thermal efficiency of an ideal Rankine cycle. List **two practical modifications** used in real plants to improve the Rankine cycle efficiency. #### Model Solution — Q A(a) [7 marks] **Introduction (2 marks):** A thermal power plant converts the chemical energy of fuel into electrical energy through a thermodynamic cycle (the Rankine cycle). The fuel (coal/oil/gas) is burnt in a boiler to generate high-pressure steam, which expands through a turbine coupled to an alternator, producing electrical power. The spent steam is condensed and recycled. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** The block diagram must show the cyclic loop: `Fuel → Boiler → Turbine → Alternator → Grid` and the *return loop* `Condenser → Feedwater Pump → Boiler`. Auxiliary branches must be labelled (CHP, AHP, Cooling Tower, ESP, Economiser, APH, Deaerator). *(See Section 4.1 of these notes for the reference diagram.)* **Auxiliary Systems (2 marks):** | Auxiliary | Function | |-----------|----------| | Coal Handling Plant | Storage, crushing, conveying coal to boiler | | Ash Handling Plant | Bottom-ash and fly-ash disposal | | Cooling Tower | Reject condenser heat to atmosphere | | Feedwater Treatment | Demineralise & deaerate boiler feedwater | | ESP & Stack | Particulate emission control | | Economiser & APH | Waste-heat recovery to improve efficiency | #### Model Solution — Q A(b) [7 marks] **Derivation (5 marks):** - Apply SFEE to pump, boiler, turbine, and condenser (1.5 marks — each system). - $W_{net} = (h_3 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_1)$ (1 mark). - $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}$ (0.5 mark). - **Final boxed expression** (0.5 mark) — full marks only if the boxed equation matches Section 3.1 Step 8. **Two Practical Modifications (2 marks — 1 mark each):** 1. **Reheating:** Steam is expanded partially in HP turbine, returned to boiler for re-superheating, and then expanded in IP/LP turbine. This reduces turbine exit moisture and improves efficiency by 4–5 %. 2. **Regeneration:** Steam is extracted from intermediate turbine stages to preheat feedwater in Feedwater Heaters (FWH). This reduces the irreversibility of boiler heat addition and improves efficiency by 5–10 %. *(Other acceptable modifications: superheating, increasing boiler pressure, decreasing condenser pressure, supercritical operation, combined cycle.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam Pattern — Module 1] > **Q B(a) [7 marks]:** A steam power plant operates on an ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **4 MPa** and **400 °C** and is condensed at a pressure of **20 kPa**. Determine: (i) Pump work, (ii) Turbine work, (iii) Heat supplied in the boiler, (iv) Thermal efficiency, and (v) Specific steam consumption. > > **Q B(b) [7 marks]:** Explain the following with neat sketches: (i) Fire-tube vs Water-tube boiler, (ii) Natural vs Forced draught cooling tower. Mention **two** advantages and **two** disadvantages of water-tube boilers over fire-tube boilers. #### Model Solution — Q B(a) [7 marks] **Steam Table Lookups (2 marks):** | State | Property | Value | |-------|----------|-------| | 1 | $P_1 = 20$ kPa, sat. liquid | $h_1 = h_f = 251.42$ kJ/kg, $v_1 = 0.001017$ m³/kg | | 3 | $P_3 = 4$ MPa, $T_3 = 400$ °C | $h_3 = 3213.6$ kJ/kg, $s_3 = 6.7690$ kJ/kg·K | | 4 | $P_4 = 20$ kPa, $s_4 = s_3$ | $s_f = 0.8320$, $s_{fg} = 7.0752$ kJ/kg·K, $h_{fg} = 2357.5$ kJ/kg | **Step 1 — Pump Work (1 mark):** $W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1) = 0.001017 \times (4000 - 20) = 0.001017 \times 3980$ $\boxed{W_P \approx 4.05 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$ **Step 2 — Quality at Turbine Exit (1 mark):** $x_4 = \dfrac{s_3 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \dfrac{6.7690 - 0.8320}{7.0752} = \dfrac{5.9370}{7.0752}$ $\boxed{x_4 \approx 0.8392}$ **Step 3 — Turbine Work (1 mark):** $h_4 = h_f + x_4 h_{fg} = 251.42 + 0.8392 \times 2357.5 = 251.42 + 1978.4$ $h_4 \approx 2229.8$ kJ/kg $W_T = h_3 - h_4 = 3213.6 - 2229.8$ $\boxed{W_T \approx 983.8 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$ **Step 4 — Heat Supplied (0.5 mark):** $h_2 = h_1 + W_P = 251.42 + 4.05 = 255.47$ kJ/kg $Q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 3213.6 - 255.47$ $\boxed{Q_{in} \approx 2958.1 \ \text{kJ/kg}}$ **Step 5 — Thermal Efficiency (1 mark):** $W_{net} = W_T - W_P = 983.8 - 4.05 = 979.75$ kJ/kg $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{979.75}{2958.1}$ $\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 33.12 \ \text{%}}$ **Step 6 — Specific Steam Consumption (0.5 mark):** $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{3600}{979.75}$ $\boxed{SSC \approx 3.674 \ \text{kg/kWh}}$ #### Model Solution — Q B(b) [7 marks] **(i) Fire-tube vs Water-tube boiler (3.5 marks):** > **Fire-tube boiler:** Hot combustion gases pass through tubes that are surrounded by water. Examples: Cochran, Lancashire, Locomotive boilers. Used for low-pressure, small-capacity applications (up to ~10 bar). > > **Water-tube boiler:** Water circulates inside the tubes, with hot combustion gases on the outside. Examples: Babcock & Wilcox, La-Mont, Benson. Used for high-pressure, high-capacity applications (above 50 bar, up to supercritical 250+ bar in modern plants). **Comparison Table (2 marks):** | Parameter | Fire-tube | Water-tube | |-----------|-----------|------------| | Operating Pressure | Low (≤ 17 bar) | High (up to supercritical) | | Steam generation rate | Slow | Very fast | | Risk of explosion | High (large water volume) | Low (small water volume per tube) | | Floor space | Large | Compact | | Capacity | Small | Large (500 MW+) | | Maintenance | Easy | Skilled labour required | **(ii) Natural vs Forced Draught Cooling Tower (1.5 marks):** > **Natural draught cooling tower:** Uses a hyperbolic chimney to create buoyancy-induced airflow. No fans required. Suitable for very large installations (500 MW+). High capital cost but low operating cost. > > **Forced draught cooling tower:** Uses mechanical fans to drive air across the falling water. Cheaper, smaller installations, but consumes fan electrical power (~1–2 % of plant output). *(Sketch credit: 1 mark awarded only if both tower types are shown with directional arrows for air and water flow.)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Introduction to Thermal Power Plant Processes** ### ✅ Must-Know Definitions - Thermal Power Plant (TPP) - Rankine Cycle and its 4 processes - Boiler, Turbine, Alternator, Condenser, Cooling Tower - Coal Handling Plant (CHP) and Ash Handling Plant (AHP) - Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) - Economiser and Air Pre-Heater (APH) - Deaerator - Feedwater Heater (FWH) ### ✅ Must-Know Numerical Skills - Reading $h$, $s$, $v$ from saturated and superheated steam tables. - Calculating **pump work** $W_P = v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$. - Calculating **turbine work** $W_T = h_3 - h_4$. - Calculating **quality** $x_4$ at turbine exit using entropy: $x_4 = (s_3 - s_f) / s_{fg}$. - Computing $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2}$. - Computing $SSC = 3600 / W_{net}$ in kg/kWh. - Computing $SFC$ from GCV and $\eta_{overall}$. ### ✅ Must-Know Standard Values - Subcritical pressure range: **170 – 180 bar** - Supercritical threshold: **> 221.2 bar, 374.15 °C** - Typical subcritical efficiency: **33 – 38 %** - Supercritical efficiency: **40 – 45 %** - USC efficiency: **45 – 50 %** - Combined cycle efficiency: **55 – 62 %** - Reheat improves efficiency by **4 – 5 %** - Regeneration improves efficiency by **5 – 10 %** ### ✅ Must-Know Real-World Facts - Reheating reduces **turbine blade erosion** by lowering exit moisture content. - Regeneration reduces **boiler irreversibility** by reducing temperature difference during heat addition. - Deaerator removes **dissolved O₂ and CO₂** to prevent boiler tube corrosion. - ESP removes **particulate fly ash** to meet pollution norms (< 50 mg/Nm³). - Economiser uses **flue gas waste heat** to preheat feedwater. - APH uses **flue gas heat** to preheat combustion air. ### ✅ Common Mistakes to Avoid - ❌ Using °C instead of K in Carnot formula. - ❌ Forgetting pump work in $W_{net}$. - ❌ Confusing $h_{fg}$ (latent) with $h_g$ (total). - ❌ Drawing block diagrams without the feedwater return loop. - ❌ Mixing up pressure units (bar vs kPa vs MPa — convert carefully). - ❌ Writing efficiency as a number > 1.0 (must be 0.30 – 0.40 for typical Rankine). > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** > When a numerical problem gives you **boiler pressure, turbine inlet temperature, and condenser pressure** — these directly map to states 3, 3, and 1 in the Rankine cycle. The unknown quality at state 4 is the **key intermediate** to compute. Master this one calculation, and 80 % of KTU Part B numericals become routine. Always show **steam table references** (page or property) to score full methodology marks.

topics.title: Ideal Steam Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ideal Steam Cycles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ideal Steam Cycle (KTU 2024 Definition):** An *ideal steam cycle* is a **theoretical, reversible thermodynamic cycle** that models the operation of a vapour power plant using water/steam as the working fluid. It serves as the **thermodynamic benchmark** against which the actual performance of real steam power plants is compared. The two principal ideal cycles in steam power generation are the **Carnot Cycle** (universal ideal) and the **Rankine Cycle** (practical ideal for steam plants).

In the context of **PEICT523 – Power Plant Instrumentation**, ideal steam cycles form the *theoretical foundation* upon which all measurement strategies, efficiency calculations, and instrumentation placements are built. Without understanding the ideal cycle, a student cannot interpret why a particular temperature, pressure, or flow sensor is installed at a specific location in a boiler-turbine-condenser loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for Board Examinations:**
> - **Working Fluid:** The substance (water/steam) that absorbs heat, expands, and rejects heat in a cyclic manner. In steam plants, the working fluid undergoes **phase change**.
> - **Reversible Process:** A process that can be reversed without leaving any net change in the system or surroundings. Ideal cycles consist of **internally reversible** processes.
> - **Thermal Efficiency (η_th):** The ratio of **net work output** to the **gross heat input** in the cycle. For ideal cycles, this is the **maximum achievable** efficiency.

### 1.2 Why "Ideal" Cycles Are Essential in Power Plant Instrumentation

Even though no real cycle can be truly ideal, the ideal steam cycle is critical for the following reasons:

1. **Performance Benchmarking:** It establishes the **upper theoretical limit** of thermal efficiency. Instrumentation data (temperatures, pressures, flow rates) from a real plant is compared against ideal values to compute **deviation efficiency** or **isentropic efficiency** of individual components.
2. **Sensor Placement Design:** The location of critical instruments — such as the **boiler drum level transmitter**, **turbine inlet thermocouple**, **condenser vacuum gauge**, and **feedwater flow meter** — is dictated by the state points defined in the ideal cycle.
3. **Control Loop Architecture:** Modern DCS (Distributed Control Systems) and PLC-based boiler control use ideal cycle models as the **reference set-point generator** for combustion control, steam temperature control, and drum level control.
4. **Energy Audit Foundation:** Any **performance test** (e.g., ASME PTC 6 for steam turbines) requires comparison with the ideal Rankine cycle performance.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade" of Energy

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Water Wheel and the Bucket Brigade**

Imagine a hill with a river at the top and a pond at the bottom. Workers (energy carriers) carry buckets of water **up** the hill using a hand pump (the **feedwater pump**), fill a large heated tank at the top (the **boiler**) where a fire converts the water to high-pressure steam. The rushing steam turns a wheel (**turbine**) that does useful work (like grinding grain). The spent steam condenses back to water in a cool pond (the **condenser**), and the cycle repeats.

The **ideal steam cycle** is the *perfect version* of this bucket brigade: no water spills, the pump lifts water with no friction, the boiler loses zero heat to surroundings, the turbine expands steam with no losses, and the condenser rejects heat without any irreversibility. In reality, friction, heat loss, and irreversibilities creep in — and **instrumentation is the science of measuring exactly how much we've fallen short of the ideal**.

### 1.4 The Two Reference Ideal Cycles

| Feature | Carnot Cycle | Rankine Cycle (Practical Ideal) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Addition | Isothermal (constant T) | **Isobaric** (constant P) in boiler |
| Heat Rejection | Isothermal (constant T) | **Isobaric** (constant P) in condenser |
| Compression Process | Isentropic compression of **vapour** | Isentropic compression of **liquid** |
| Expansion Process | Isentropic expansion of vapour | Isentropic expansion of vapour |
| Practical Feasibility | **Not feasible** for steam plants (compresses wet vapour) | **Most widely used** as ideal model |
| Maximum Efficiency | η_Carnot = 1 − T_L/T_H | η_Rankine (calculated from enthalpies) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pearl:** Although the Carnot cycle has the **highest possible efficiency** between two thermal reservoirs, it is **not practical** for steam power plants because it requires isothermal heat transfer (impossible at constant pressure with a pure substance undergoing phase change) and isentropic compression of a two-phase mixture. The **Rankine cycle** is therefore the universally accepted **ideal steam cycle** in power engineering and instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Ideal Rankine Cycle — Four Component Processes

The **Ideal Rankine Cycle** consists of four internally reversible processes executed in a closed loop. Each process corresponds to a specific physical component in the power plant.

---

#### **Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression (Feedwater Pump)**
- **Physical Location:** Between the **condenser outlet** and the **boiler inlet**.
- **Working Fluid State:** Subcooled (compressed) liquid.
- **Process Description:** The feedwater pump raises the pressure of saturated liquid from condenser pressure (p₁) to boiler pressure (p₂). Since liquids are nearly incompressible, the temperature rise is very small.
- **Thermodynamic Nature:** **Isentropic (reversible adiabatic)** — entropy remains constant (s₁ = s₂).
- **Energy Equation:** W_pump = h₂ − h₁
- **Approximation (since liquid is incompressible):** W_pump = v₁(p₂ − p₁)

#### **Process 2 → 3: Isobaric Heat Addition (Steam Boiler)**
- **Physical Location:** Inside the **steam generator / boiler**.
- **Working Fluid State:** Compressed liquid → Saturated Liquid → Saturated Liquid-Vapour Mixture → **Saturated or Superheated Steam**.
- **Process Description:** Heat is transferred from the combustion of fuel (coal, oil, gas, or nuclear) to the working fluid at **constant pressure**.
- **Thermodynamic Nature:** **Isobaric (constant pressure)** heat addition in a **steady-flow** open system.
- **Energy Equation:** q_in = h₃ − h₂
- **Why Not Isothermal?** During phase change, water must be heated from saturation temperature to a higher temperature to reach the desired superheat. Pure isothermal heat addition would require infinite heat-exchange surface area — practically impossible.

#### **Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion (Steam Turbine)**
- **Physical Location:** Inside the **steam turbine** (HP, IP, LP stages).
- **Working Fluid State:** Superheated (or saturated) steam → Wet steam (mixture of liquid + vapour).
- **Process Description:** High-pressure steam expands through the turbine blades, producing **shaft work** that drives the generator. The expansion is modelled as **isentropic** in the ideal case.
- **Thermodynamic Nature:** **Isentropic (reversible adiabatic)** — s₃ = s₄.
- **Energy Equation:** W_turbine = h₃ − h₄

#### **Process 4 → 1: Isobaric Heat Rejection (Condenser)**
- **Physical Location:** Inside the **surface condenser** (shell-side with cooling water in tubes).
- **Working Fluid State:** Wet steam → Saturated liquid.
- **Process Description:** The exhaust steam from the turbine is condensed at **constant pressure** by transferring heat to the cooling water (which is rejected to the environment via a cooling tower or river).
- **Thermodynamic Nature:** **Isobaric (constant pressure)** heat rejection.
- **Energy Equation:** q_out = h₄ − h₁
- **Condenser Pressure:** Maintained **well below atmospheric** (typically 0.05–0.1 bar absolute) to maximize the temperature difference and the cycle efficiency.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly usable** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination) numerical problems. Memorize the assumptions: **steady-state, steady-flow, negligible KE and PE changes, internally reversible processes**.

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pump Work | $W_{pump}$ | $h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Energy required to compress liquid |
| 2 | Approx. Pump Work | $W_{pump}$ | $v_1(p_2 - p_1)$ | kJ/kg | Valid because $v_1 \approx v_2$ for liquid |
| 3 | Turbine Work | $W_{turbine}$ | $h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Energy extracted by turbine |
| 4 | Heat Added | $q_{in}$ | $h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Energy from fuel in boiler |
| 5 | Heat Rejected | $q_{out}$ | $h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Energy dumped to cooling water |
| 6 | Net Work Output | $W_{net}$ | $W_{turbine} - W_{pump}$ | kJ/kg | Useful work per kg of steam |
| 7 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th}$ | $\dfrac{W_{net}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_3-h_4)-(h_2-h_1)}{h_3-h_2}$ | dimensionless (× 100 for %) | Primary KTU metric |
| 8 | Carnot Reference Eff. | $\eta_{Carnot}$ | $1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | dimensionless | Upper theoretical bound |
| 9 | Mean Temp. of Heat Add. | $\bar{T}_{H}$ | $\dfrac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}$ | K or °C | For Carnot-equivalent comparison |
| 10 | Mean Temp. of Heat Rej. | $\bar{T}_{L}$ | $\dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{s_4 - s_1}$ | K or °C | For Carnot-equivalent comparison |
| 11 | Back Work Ratio | $BWR$ | $\dfrac{W_{pump}}{W_{turbine}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.01–0.05 for steam plants |
| 12 | Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC$ | $\dfrac{3600}{W_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Mass of steam per unit power |
| 13 | Quality at Turbine Exit | $x_4$ | $\dfrac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\geq 0.85$ to avoid blade erosion |
| 14 | Condenser Mass Flow Ratio | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_{cw}}{\dot{m}_{steam}}$ | $\dfrac{h_4 - h_1}{c_p(T_{cw,out}-T_{cw,in})}$ | dimensionless | For cooling tower design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Convention:** All enthalpies ($h$) and entropies ($s$) are **specific** values (per unit mass, kJ/kg and kJ/(kg·K) respectively). The cycle is analysed on a **per-kg-of-steam** basis, and the total plant power is obtained by multiplying by the steam mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{steam}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ideal Rankine cycle directly informs the design and operation of the following power plant systems:

1. **Boiler Design:** The heat addition process (2→3) sets the **steam temperature, pressure, and flow rate** specifications for the boiler, which directly determines the **furnace sizing, tube bank layout, and superheater surface area**.
2. **Turbine Selection:** The enthalpy drop $(h_3 - h_4)$ determines the number of turbine stages and the expected **shaft power output**.
3. **Condenser Sizing:** The heat rejected $(h_4 - h_1)$ sets the cooling water flow rate and condenser tube bundle dimensions.
4. **Instrumentation Ranges:** The state point values define the **measurement ranges** for:
   - Boiler drum pressure transmitters (typically 100–250 bar)
   - Turbine inlet thermocouples (typically 500–600 °C)
   - Condenser vacuum gauges (−0.9 to −1.0 bar gauge)
   - Feedwater flow meters (orifice plates, venturi tubes)
5. **Efficiency Improvement Decisions:** The gap between **ideal** and **actual** cycle efficiency guides investments in **reheating, regeneration, superheating, and reheat cycles** (covered in higher modules).

### 2.4 T-s and P-v Diagram Description

> [!TIP]
> **How to Draw the Ideal Rankine Cycle on a T-s Diagram (Board Exam Step):**
> 1. Draw the **saturation dome** (bell-shaped curve) with saturated liquid line on the left and saturated vapour line on the right.
> 2. **Point 1:** Saturated liquid line at condenser pressure (low pressure). Mark at the left edge of the dome.
> 3. **Process 1→2:** Move **almost vertically upward** (very small temperature rise due to incompressibility) along constant-entropy line to the boiler pressure.
> 4. **Process 2→3:** Move rightward along the **constant-pressure line** (above the dome) — through preheating, evaporation, and superheating regions — to point 3 (top right, often superheated).
> 5. **Process 3→4:** Move **straight down** vertically (constant entropy) into the wet region inside the dome, ending at point 4.
> 6. **Process 4→1:** Move leftward along the **constant-pressure line** (the lower part of the dome) back to point 1.

The **area enclosed** by the cycle on the T-s diagram represents the **net work output** of the cycle per unit mass of working fluid. This geometric interpretation is heavily tested in KTU board examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle

We now derive the **thermal efficiency expression** for the ideal Rankine cycle from first principles using the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**.

---

#### **Assumptions (state these at the start of any KTU solution):**
1. Steady-state, steady-flow operation.
2. Kinetic energy and potential energy changes are negligible.
3. All processes are internally reversible.
4. The working fluid undergoes a **closed loop** (returns to its initial state).
5. No heat loss from piping.

---

#### **Step 1: Apply SFEE to the Feedwater Pump (Process 1→2)**

The general SFEE for a control volume is:

$$q - w = \Delta h + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$

For an **adiabatic, reversible** pump (no heat transfer, no KE/PE change):

$$0 - w_{pump,in} = h_2 - h_1$$

Rearranging (work done **by** the fluid, taken as positive in the cycle):

$$w_{pump} = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{(work input, positive when supplied to fluid)}$$

**Approximation using incompressibility:**
Since water is essentially incompressible, the specific volume remains nearly constant ($v_1 \approx v_2$). For an isentropic process on an incompressible substance:

$$w_{pump} = \int_{1}^{2} v \, dp = v_1 (p_2 - p_1)$$

---

#### **Step 2: Apply SFEE to the Boiler (Process 2→3)**

For the boiler, no work is done ($w = 0$), and heat is added at constant pressure:

$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$$

---

#### **Step 3: Apply SFEE to the Turbine (Process 3→4)**

For an isentropic, adiabatic turbine:

$$0 - w_{turbine,out} = h_4 - h_3$$

The work done **by** the fluid (on the turbine blades) is:

$$w_{turbine} = h_3 - h_4$$

---

#### **Step 4: Apply SFEE to the Condenser (Process 4→1)**

For the condenser, no work is done, and heat is rejected at constant pressure:

$$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$$

(Note: $q_{out}$ is taken as a positive magnitude; the actual heat transfer is from the fluid to the cooling water, so $q_{cycle} = h_1 - h_4$ which is negative.)

---

#### **Step 5: Net Work Output**

By the **First Law of Thermodynamics for a cyclic process**, the net work equals the net heat:

$$w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out} = (h_3 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_1)$$

Expanding and rearranging:

$$w_{net} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1) = w_{turbine} - w_{pump}$$

---

#### **Step 6: Thermal Efficiency**

By definition:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$$

This is the **canonical thermal efficiency equation** of the Ideal Rankine Cycle. In a KTU board exam, you may also be asked to express it in terms of temperatures using the mean temperature of heat addition:

$$\eta_{th,Rankine} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_L}{\bar{T}_H}$$

where

$$\bar{T}_H = \frac{q_{in}}{s_3 - s_2} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}, \quad \bar{T}_L = \frac{q_{out}}{s_4 - s_1} = \frac{h_4 - h_1}{s_4 - s_1}$$

This shows that the **Rankine efficiency is always less than the Carnot efficiency** because $\bar{T}_H < T_{max}$ (since heat is not added isothermally).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Pump Work Using Saturated Liquid Approximation

In many KTU problems, state 1 properties are read from the **saturated water table** at condenser pressure. We want to compute $h_2$.

Since the pump process is isentropic on a liquid:

$$h_2 = h_1 + v_1(p_2 - p_1)$$

This avoids the need to interpolate superheated liquid tables (which are not always available at the pump inlet entropy). The error introduced is **less than 1%** for typical feedwater conditions, which is acceptable for board-level calculations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Isentropic Quality at Turbine Exit (State 4)

Given that the turbine expansion is isentropic:

$$s_4 = s_3$$

At state 4, the steam is wet (inside the dome), so:

$$s_4 = s_f + x_4 \cdot s_{fg}$$

Solving for the quality $x_4$:

$$x_4 = \frac{s_4 - s_f}{s_{fg}}$$

where $s_f$ and $s_{fg}$ are read at the **condenser pressure**. This is critical because if $x_4$ drops below **0.88**, water droplets form on turbine blades causing **erosion damage** — a key instrumentation safety concern.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An ideal Rankine cycle operates between a boiler pressure of **3 MPa** and a condenser pressure of **10 kPa**. Steam enters the turbine as **saturated vapour**. Compute:
> (a) Pump work per kg
> (b) Turbine work per kg
> (c) Heat added per kg
> (d) Thermal efficiency
> (e) Quality at turbine exit

**Solution (assume water properties from standard steam tables):**

*At Condenser (10 kPa):*
- $h_1 = h_f = 191.81$ kJ/kg
- $v_1 = v_f = 0.00101$ m³/kg
- $s_1 = s_f = 0.6492$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $s_{fg} = 7.4996$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $h_{fg} = 2392.1$ kJ/kg

*At Boiler (3 MPa), saturated vapour:*
- $h_3 = h_g = 2804.2$ kJ/kg
- $s_3 = s_g = 6.1856$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Part (a): Pump Work**

$$w_{pump} = v_1(p_2 - p_1) = 0.00101 \times (3000 - 10) = 0.00101 \times 2990$$

$$w_{pump} = 3.020 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Also: $h_2 = h_1 + w_{pump} = 191.81 + 3.020 = 194.83$ kJ/kg

**Part (b): Turbine Work**

Since $s_4 = s_3 = 6.1856$ kJ/(kg·K), and at condenser pressure $s_f = 0.6492$, $s_{fg} = 7.4996$:

$$x_4 = \frac{6.1856 - 0.6492}{7.4996} = \frac{5.5364}{7.4996} = 0.7383$$

$$h_4 = h_f + x_4 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.81 + 0.7383 \times 2392.1$$

$$h_4 = 191.81 + 1766.13 = 1957.94 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{turbine} = h_3 - h_4 = 2804.2 - 1957.94 = 846.26 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Part (c): Heat Added**

$$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = 2804.2 - 194.83 = 2609.37 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Part (d): Thermal Efficiency**

$$w_{net} = w_{turbine} - w_{pump} = 846.26 - 3.02 = 843.24 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{843.24}{2609.37} = 0.3232 = 32.32\%$$

**Part (e): Quality at Turbine Exit**

$$x_4 = 0.7383 \text{ (calculated above)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The quality of 0.738 is **below the safe limit of 0.88**. In a real plant, the operator would either (a) increase the boiler pressure (superheat the steam) or (b) use a reheat cycle. This is precisely why **turbine exhaust moisture analyzers** are critical instruments in a steam power plant.

---

### 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Ideal Cycles

| Parameter | Carnot Cycle | Ideal Rankine Cycle | Real Rankine Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Addition Process | Isothermal | Isobaric | Isobaric (with losses) |
| Heat Rejection Process | Isothermal | Isobaric | Isobaric (with losses) |
| Compression Process | Isentropic (vapour) | Isentropic (liquid) | Polytropic (with η_isentropic < 1) |
| Expansion Process | Isentropic (vapour) | Isentropic (vapour) | Polytropic (with η_isentropic < 1) |
| Working Fluid | Any pure substance | Water/Steam | Water/Steam |
| Practical Realizability | NO | YES (as a model) | YES (actual plants) |
| Typical Efficiency | η_Carnot (highest) | 30–40% (superheated) | 25–35% (real) |
| Instrumentation Reference | Theoretically perfect | **Design baseline** | Operating baseline |
