```mermaid
    C1[A1 DCS CONTROLLER PID]:::control
    C2[A2 BOILER PROTECTION BMS]:::control
    C3[A3 TURBINE TRIP SYSTEM]:::control
    C4[A4 GENERATOR PROTECTION RELAY]:::control

    O1[O1 OPERATOR HMI SCADA]:::op
    O2[O2 PERFORMANCE CALCULATOR KPI]:::op
    O3[O3 ALARM AND EVENT LOGGER]:::op

    E1[E1 GRID LOAD DISPATCH]:::ext
    E2[E2 CPCB CEMS PORTAL]:::ext
    E3[E3 PLANT MANAGEMENT MIS]:::ext

    F1 --> C1
    F2 --> C1
    F3 --> C1
    F4 --> C1
    F5 --> C1
    F6 --> C3
    F7 --> C4
    F8 --> E2

    C1 --> O1
    C2 --> O1
    C3 --> O1
    C4 --> O1

    O1 --> O2
    O1 --> O3

    O2 --> E1
    O2 --> E3
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Boiler Combustion Control (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef proc fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef inst fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef ctl fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000

    P1[BOILER FURNACE]:::proc
    P2[STEAM GENERATION]:::proc

    I1[O2 ANALYZER IN FLUE GAS]:::inst
    I2[CO ANALYZER IN FLUE GAS]:::inst
    I3[STEAM PRESSURE XMTR]:::inst
    I4[FUEL FLOW METER]:::inst
    I5[COMBUSTION AIR FLOW METER]:::inst

    CT1[FURNACE PRESSURE CONTROLLER]:::ctl
    CT2[O2 TRIM CONTROLLER]:::ctl
    CT3[CROSS LIMITED FUEL AIR MASTER]:::ctl

    A1[FUEL VALVE ACTUATOR]:::act
    A2[AIR DAMPER ACTUATOR]:::act
    A3[FD FAN SPEED DRIVE]:::act

    P1 --> I1
    P1 --> I2
    P2 --> I3
    P1 --> I4
    P1 --> I5

    I1 --> CT2
    I2 --> CT2
    I3 --> CT3
    I4 --> CT3
    I5 --> CT3

    CT2 --> CT3
    CT3 --> A1
    CT3 --> A2
    CT2 --> A3
```

### 4.4 Hierarchy of Control Levels (ISA-95 / Purdue Model Adapted)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4[L4 ENTERPRISE MIS ASSET MANAGEMENT] --> L3
    L3[L3 PLANT OPERATIONS PERFORMANCE KPI] --> L2
    L2[L2 SUPERVISORY CONTROL DCS SCADA] --> L1
    L1[L1 LOCAL LOOPS PLC PID] --> L0
    L0[L0 FIELD SENSORS TRANSDUCERS ACTUATORS]:::field
    classDef field fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The arrows show **information flow upward** (telemetry, KPIs) and **command flow downward** (setpoints, overrides). This is the same architecture used in **ABB 800xA**, **Siemens SPPA-T3000**, and **GE Mark VIe** controls deployed in Indian supercritical units like NTPC's **Kudgi (3×800 MW)** or **Barh (3×660 MW)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] *List any three reasons why instrumentation is essential in a modern thermal power plant.*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Safety of personnel and equipment** — Trip systems, overspeed governors, drum level interlocks prevent catastrophic failures (e.g., boiler explosion, turbine overspeed).
> 2. **Efficiency optimization** — Continuous monitoring of O₂, temperature, pressure allows closed-loop combustion control, reducing heat rate by 1–3%.
> 3. **Statutory environmental compliance** — Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) are mandatory under CPCB / MOEFCC norms for SO₂, NOₓ, and PM.
> *(3 points × 1 mark = 3 marks)*

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] *Define Net Plant Heat Rate (NPHR) and state its typical value for an Indian coal-fired subcritical plant.*
>
> **Model Answer:**
> NPHR is the ratio of the thermal energy input from fuel (in kJ or kcal) to the net electrical energy output (in kWh) over a specified period.
> $$NPHR = \dfrac{m_{fuel} \times GCV}{P_{net}} \quad \text{(units: kcal/kWh or kJ/kWh)}$$
> Typical value for Indian **subcritical** 500/210 MW units: **2300–2500 kcal/kWh**. For **supercritical** units: **2000–2200 kcal/kWh**. World-class ultra-supercritical: **<1900 kcal/kWh**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3 (A).** [KTU University Exam — Module 1 Model Paper] *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the hierarchical levels of instrumentation and control in a modern thermal power plant. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A 500 MW thermal unit has flue gas exit temperature of 150 °C and ambient of 30 °C. Stack loss constant $K = 0.6$. If the O₂ analyzer drifts by +1%, estimate the change in calculated boiler efficiency. Assume complete combustion, no moisture, radiation loss = 1.5%, and dry gas loss dominates. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(a) — 7 Marks

**Hierarchical levels (ISA-95 / Purdue adapted):**
1. **Level 0 — Field instruments:** RTD, TC, DP, orifice, accelerometer, O₂ probe, CEMS analyzer. Output: 4–20 mA / HART / Fieldbus.
2. **Level 1 — Direct control:** Single-loop PID controllers, PLCs, burner management system (BMS), turbine trip system.
3. **Level 2 — Supervisory control:** Distributed Control System (DCS) — operator HMI, alarm management, trending, sequence-of-events recording.
4. **Level 3 — Plant operations:** Performance calculators (ASME PTC 4, 6), heat-rate monitors, online efficiency dashboards, condition-based maintenance alerts.
5. **Level 4 — Enterprise level:** MIS reports, fuel inventory, ERP integration, regulatory reporting (CPCB CEMS portal, grid EMS).

*Diagram reference: see SECTION 4.4 above.* **(7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for level names, 3 marks for description, 2 marks for real-world example such as ABB 800xA)**

#### Model Solution for Q3(A)(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Stack loss constant $K = 0.6$
- $T_{fg} = 150\,^\circ C$, $T_{amb} = 30\,^\circ C$, so $\Delta T = 120\,^\circ C$
- O₂ analyzer drift = **+1%** (reads higher than actual)
- Radiation loss $L_5 = 1.5\%$, complete combustion ($L_2 = L_3 = 0$)

**Step 1 — Calculate nominal stack loss (dry gas loss):**
$$L_1 = K \times \Delta T = 0.6 \times 120 = 72\%$$

**Step 2 — A +1% O₂ drift changes effective excess air:**
Higher indicated O₂ ⇒ controller **thinks** more excess air ⇒ reduces fuel/air, leading to **higher unburnt loss** and **higher true stack loss**.

**Step 3 — Empirical sensitivity:** A 1% O₂ error in measurement typically causes a **0.5–0.8%** change in dry gas loss (a textbook rule; verify with plant test data).

$$\Delta L_1 \approx 0.6\%$$

**Step 4 — Nominal efficiency:**
$$\eta_{boiler} = 100 - (L_1 + L_5) = 100 - (72 + 1.5) = 26.5\%$$

**Step 5 — Revised efficiency with drift:**
$$\eta_{boiler}' = 100 - (72.6 + 1.5) = 25.9\%$$

**Step 6 — Net change:**
$$\boxed{\Delta \eta_{boiler} = -0.6\%}$$

**Economic impact (in 1 year, 500 MW, 80% PLF):**
- Annual generation: $500 \times 8760 \times 0.8 = 3.504 \times 10^9$ kWh
- Extra fuel = $0.006 \times 3.504 \times 10^9 \times$ (1 / η) ÷ GCV ≈ **₹10–12 crore/year** (assumed coal cost ₹3.5/kg).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Do **not** confuse *Net Plant Heat Rate* with *Boiler Efficiency* — they are different! Heat rate includes the entire plant (boiler + turbine + generator + auxiliaries).
> - When computing efficiency, students often **forget to convert % to fraction** before multiplying by generation.
> - Always state **units explicitly** — examiners deduct marks for missing kcal/kWh or kJ/kWh.

---

> **Q3 (B).** [Alternate choice — same marks, different focus] *(a)* Explain the role of Distributed Control System (DCS) in a 2×500 MW thermal power station. List any three major process loops controlled by the DCS. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A boiler drum uses a differential pressure (DP) transmitter to measure water level. The transmitter range is 0–500 mm H₂O, output 4–20 mA. If the level is 320 mm, calculate: (i) the current output, (ii) the percentage level, and (iii) the output if a 5% upscale calibration error exists. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(a) — 7 Marks

**Role of DCS:**
- **Centralised monitoring & control** of 3000+ I/O points across boiler, turbine, generator, and auxiliaries.
- **Redundant controllers, servers, and networks** ensure 99.99% availability.
- **Sequence-of-events (SOE)** recording with 1 ms resolution for post-trip analysis.
- **Advanced control** — model-predictive control (MPC), fuzzy logic for supercritical units.
- **Integration** with PLCs, vibration monitors, CEMS, and plant information (PI) system.

**Three major DCS-controlled loops:**
1. **Combustion control loop** — fuel, air, and flue gas O₂ trim (cross-limited).
2. **Drum level / feedwater control** — three-element (steam flow, feedwater flow, level).
3. **Superheater / reheater steam temperature control** — attemperator spray water flow.

*(3 marks for DCS role + 3 marks for three loops + 1 mark for example: ABB 800xA / Siemens SPPA-T3000)*

#### Model Solution for Q3(B)(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** Range 0–500 mm, output 4–20 mA, level = 320 mm.

**(i) Current output:**
$$I = 4 + 16 \times \dfrac{320 - 0}{500 - 0} = 4 + 16 \times 0.64 = \boxed{14.24 \text{ mA}}$$

**(ii) Percentage level:**
$$\%L = \dfrac{320}{500} \times 100 = \boxed{64\%}$$

**(iii) Output with 5% upscale calibration error:**
A 5% upscale error means the transmitter reads 5% higher than actual for any input. Effective measured value = $320 \times 1.05 = 336$ mm.

$$I_{erroneous} = 4 + 16 \times \dfrac{336}{500} = 4 + 10.752 = \boxed{14.752 \text{ mA}}$$

The error in current = $14.752 - 14.240 = +0.512$ mA.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Some students apply 5% to the *current* output (i.e., 14.24 × 1.05), which is **wrong**. Upscale error must be applied to the **input (process variable)**, not the output, *unless the question explicitly says "5% of span on output"*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ⚡ **Instrumentation cost = 5–8% of plant cost, but saves 10× over plant life.**
- ⚡ **Four pillars:** Safety, Efficiency, Economy, Environment.
- ⚡ **5,000 sensors** in a typical 500 MW unit; 3,000+ I/O into the DCS.
- ⚡ **ISA-95 / Purdue model:** 5 levels (Field → Local → Supervisory → Operations → Enterprise).
- ⚡ **NPHR** for Indian subcritical: **2300–2500 kcal/kWh**; supercritical: **2000–2200 kcal/kWh**.
- ⚡ **1% efficiency gain** ≈ **₹15–20 crore/year** for a 500 MW unit.
- ⚡ **Excess O₂** optimum: **3–5%** for gas, **15–20%** for coal.
- ⚡ **CEMS is mandatory** in India under CPCB / MOEFCC norms.
- ⚡ **Triple-redundant** overspeed probes + 2-out-of-3 voting = essentially zero spurious trip probability.
- ⚡ **4–20 mA live-zero** convention: 4 mA = 0% (also detects cable break), 20 mA = 100%.
- ⚡ **PT100 RTD**: $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at 0 °C; sensitivity ≈ **0.385 Ω/°C**.
- ⚡ **K-type thermocouple** range: **−200 °C to +1350 °C**, Seebeck coefficient ≈ **41 µV/°C**.
- ⚡ **ISO 10816-3** vibration limits for >300 kW machines: Alarm at **4.5 mm/s**, Trip at **7.1 mm/s**.
- ⚡ **Functional safety standard** for power plants: **IEC 61511 / SIL ratings**.
- ⚡ **In exams:** Always quote **units**, mention **standards (ISA, IEC, IEEE)**, and give a **real-world example** to fetch full marks.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line answer for "Importance of Instrumentation":** *"Instrumentation is the nervous system of a power plant — without it, the plant cannot be safe, efficient, economical, or compliant."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Power Plant Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Power Plant Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Nuclear Power Plant? (Formal KTU Definition)

A **Nuclear Power Plant (NPP)** is a thermal power station in which the heat source is a **nuclear reactor** where controlled, sustained **nuclear fission** of heavy isotopes (typically **U-235**, **Pu-239**, or **U-233**) liberates enormous amounts of thermal energy. This thermal energy is transferred via a primary coolant to a **heat exchanger (steam generator)**, producing steam that drives a conventional turbo-alternator set, ultimately generating electrical energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT523 - Module 1):**
> The instrumentation focus is NOT on reactor physics design, but on the **measurement, control, and protection instrumentation** required to safely operate the reactor — i.e., *what we measure, how we measure it, and how the control loop acts on the measurement.*

## 1.2 Classification of Nuclear Reactors (KTU-Required Taxonomy)

For instrumentation design, reactors are broadly classified by **moderator/coolant combination**, because this dictates the **type, range, and location** of sensors used:

| Reactor Type | Moderator | Coolant | Fuel | Example Plant |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PWR** (Pressurized Water Reactor) | Light Water (H₂O) | Light Water (H₂O) under high pressure (~155 bar) | Enriched UO₂ (3–5% U-235) | Tarapur (India) |
| **BWR** (Boiling Water Reactor) | Light Water (H₂O) | Light Water (boils inside core) | Enriched UO₂ | Kakrapar, Japan |
| **PHWR** (Pressurized Heavy Water Reactor) | Heavy Water (D₂O) | Heavy Water (D₂O) | Natural Uranium | Kaiga, Kalpakkam |
| **GCR** (Gas-Cooled Reactor) | Graphite | CO₂ / Helium | Natural U | Magnox, AGR |
| **LMFBR** (Liquid Metal Fast Breeder Reactor) | None (Fast neutrons) | Liquid Sodium (Na) | PuO₂-UO₂ (MOX) | PFBR, Kalpakkam |

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Nuclear Pressure Cooker"

Imagine a domestic **pressure cooker** on a kitchen stove:
- The **fire** under the cooker = uncontrolled energy source.
- The **pressure regulator (whistle)** = safety valve that releases steam if pressure exceeds a limit.
- The **simmer knob** = control rod that modulates how much "fuel" reacts.

A nuclear reactor is a **pressure cooker where the "fire" is invisible** (sub-atomic neutron chains), the "food" is **fissile material**, and the cooker never gets switched "off" — it can only be **moderated**. Hence, instrumentation must continuously monitor **temperature, pressure, neutron flux, coolant flow, and radiation leakage** — because unlike a stove, the "fire" cannot be extinguished; it can only be dampened.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constant (KTU favourite):**
> - **1 MeV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹³ J**
> - **Energy per fission of U-235 ≈ 200 MeV ≈ 3.2 × 10⁻¹¹ J**
> - **1 kg of U-235 ≈ 8.2 × 10¹³ J ≈ 24,000 MWh of heat** — equivalent to ~3,000 tonnes of coal.

## 1.4 Why Instrumentation is Mission-Critical in NPP

A nuclear power plant has **three non-negotiable operational envelopes**:

1. **Normal Operation** — Sensors must provide **high-accuracy, drift-free** measurements to maintain rated power output.
2. **Transient / Load-Following** — Sensors must respond **fast (<1 s for neutron flux)** to allow power maneuvering.
3. **Emergency / SCRAM (Safety Control Rod Activation Mechanism)** — Instrumentation must trigger **automatic shutdown** within milliseconds if any parameter breaches safety limits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** *"Justify why redundant and diverse instrumentation is mandatory in nuclear reactors."*
> **Model Answer:** Reactor protection must be **single-failure-proof (redundancy)** AND **common-cause-failure-proof (diversity)** because any undetected sensor failure combined with a process upset can lead to core meltdown (e.g., Chernobyl 1986, Fukushima 2011).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nuclear Fission — The Underlying Physics

When a **slow (thermal) neutron** strikes a **U-235 nucleus**, it becomes unstable and splits into two lighter fission fragments, releasing **2–3 fast neutrons** and ~200 MeV of energy:

$$^{235}_{92}\text{U} + ^{1}_{0}\text{n} \rightarrow ^{141}_{56}\text{Ba} + ^{92}_{36}\text{Kr} + 3^{1}_{0}\text{n} + \text{~}200\,\text{MeV}$$

### 2.1.1 The Four-Factor Formula (Infinite Multiplication Factor)

For an **infinite reactor** (no neutron leakage), the neutron population at each generation is governed by:

$$k_{\infty} = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$

Where each factor is defined as:

| Symbol | Name | Physical Meaning | KTU-Required Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ (eta) | Neutron yield per absorption | Avg. fast neutrons produced per thermal neutron absorbed in fuel | ~2.07 for U-235 |
| $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) | Fast fission factor | Ratio of total fast neutrons (including fast fission of U-238) to fast neutrons from thermal fission only | ~1.03–1.07 |
| $p$ | Resonance escape probability | Probability that a neutron slows down past U-238 resonance without being absorbed | ~0.87–0.95 |
| $f$ | Thermal utilization factor | Fraction of thermal neutrons absorbed in fuel (vs. moderator, coolant, structure) | ~0.70–0.85 |

### 2.1.2 The Six-Factor Formula (Refined for Non-Leakage)

To account for **fast-neutron leakage** and **thermal-neutron leakage** in a finite reactor:

$$k_{\text{eff}} = k_{\infty} \cdot P_{\text{NL}} \cdot P_{\text{TL}} = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f \cdot P_{\text{NL}} \cdot P_{\text{TL}}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{NL}}$ = Probability of **non-leakage** of fast neutrons during moderation.
- $P_{\text{TL}}$ = Probability of **non-leakage** of thermal neutrons during diffusion.

### 2.1.3 Reactivity — The Control Variable

**Reactivity** quantifies the deviation of $k_{\text{eff}}$ from unity (criticality):

$$\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}}$$

| Condition | $k_{\text{eff}}$ | Reactivity $\rho$ | Reactor State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subcritical | $< 1$ | Negative | Power decaying |
| **Critical (Steady-State)** | $= 1$ | $= 0$ | Power constant |
| Supercritical | $> 1$ | Positive | Power rising |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse reactivity $\rho$ with excess reactivity. Excess reactivity is the **total available positive reactivity** when all control rods are withdrawn, used for fuel burn-up compensation.

### 2.1.4 Reactor Period (Doubling Time)

The **stable reactor period** $\tau$ defines how fast neutron population (and hence power) changes:

$$P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{t/\tau}$$

The period is related to reactivity via the **Inhour Equation** (simplified for delayed-neutron regime):

$$\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_i \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

Where:
- $\Lambda$ = Mean neutron generation time (~10⁻⁵ s for thermal reactors).
- $\beta_i$ = Delayed neutron fraction for group $i$ (total $\beta \approx 0.0065$ for U-235).
- $\lambda_i$ = Decay constant of delayed neutron precursor group $i$.

### 2.1.5 Reactivity in Dollar Units (KTU Common Question)

Because the delayed-neutron fraction $\beta$ is so small, reactivity is often expressed in **dollars** and **cents**:

$$\rho(\$) = \frac{\rho}{\beta}, \qquad \rho(\text{¢}) = 100 \cdot \frac{\rho}{\beta}$$

> **Critical:** \$1.00 reactivity. **Prompt critical:** >\$1.00 — the reactor is then controlled only by prompt neutrons (~microsecond timescale — extremely dangerous).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k_{\infty} = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$ | Four-factor formula (infinite reactor multiplication) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $k_{\text{eff}} = k_{\infty} \cdot P_{\text{NL}} \cdot P_{\text{TL}}$ | Effective multiplication factor (finite reactor) | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}}$ | Reactivity | Dimensionless, $\Delta k/k$ |
| 4 | $\rho(\$) = \rho / \beta$ | Reactivity in dollars | Dollars ($) |
| 5 | $P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{t/\tau}$ | Reactor power vs. time | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $\tau = \frac{\Lambda}{\rho}$ (approximate, prompt critical) | Reactor period approximation | Seconds (s) |
| 7 | $E_{\text{fission}} \approx 200$ MeV | Energy released per fission | MeV |
| 8 | $E_{\text{per kg U}} \approx 8.2 \times 10^{13}$ J | Specific energy yield of U-235 | J/kg |
| 9 | $\eta = \nu \cdot \frac{\Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a}$ | Neutron yield per absorption | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $f = \frac{\Sigma_{a,\text{fuel}}}{\Sigma_{a,\text{total}}}$ | Thermal utilization | Dimensionless |
| 11 | $R_{\text{det}} = \epsilon \cdot n \cdot q \cdot \Omega / 4\pi$ | Radiation detector count rate (intrinsic) | Counts/s |

## 2.3 Instrumentation Architecture — The Three Loops of NPP

Every nuclear plant has **three concentric control/measurement loops**:

| Loop | Function | Parameters Measured | Typical Sensors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 — Nuclear Instrumentation (Ex-Core / In-Core)** | Measure neutron flux, reactor period, power distribution | Neutron flux (10⁻⁸ to 10¹⁰ n/cm²·s), $\gamma$ dose, core temp | BF₃ / He-3 proportional counters, fission chambers, ionization chambers |
| **2 — Process Instrumentation (Primary + Secondary)** | Monitor coolant T, P, flow; steam generator level; turbine inlet conditions | T (0–360°C), P (0–175 bar), flow (m³/h), level (%) | RTD, thermocouple, pressure transmitter, venturi, DP cell, level probes |
| **3 — Radiation Monitoring & Containment** | Monitor release, area dose, containment pressure, iodine activity | Dose rate (μSv/h), activity (Bq/m³), H₂ concentration, containment P | GM tubes, scintillation detectors, aerosol monitors, iodine cartridges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three loops?** This is a **defense-in-depth** philosophy mandated by **IAEA NS-G-1.3**. If one loop fails (e.g., neutron sensor), the second independently maintains control. If two fail, the third independently triggers **SCRAM**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Four-Factor Formula from Neutron Life Cycle

We derive the **four-factor formula** $k_\infty = \eta \varepsilon p f$ by tracking **one thermal neutron** through one full generation cycle.

### Step 1 — Neutron Absorption in Fuel
Start with **1 thermal neutron absorbed in fuel** (call this $N_0$). The number of **fast neutrons** produced (before any leakage) is:

$$N_1 = \eta \cdot N_0$$

The **thermal utilization factor** $f$ tells us what fraction of thermal neutrons is absorbed in fuel (vs. moderator, coolant, structure). So if we instead start from **1 thermal neutron absorbed anywhere in the system**, the fraction absorbed in fuel is $f$. Therefore fast neutrons produced from **1 thermal neutron absorbed in the system** is:

$$N_1 = \eta \cdot f \cdot N_0$$

### Step 2 — Fast Fission in U-238
Some of these fast neutrons cause **fast fission** in U-238 (which has a fission threshold ~1 MeV). The **fast fission factor** $\varepsilon$ accounts for this:

$$N_2 = \varepsilon \cdot N_1 = \varepsilon \cdot \eta \cdot f \cdot N_0$$

Typical value $\varepsilon \approx 1.03$.

### Step 3 — Moderation & Resonance Escape
Fast neutrons must slow down via collisions with the moderator. During this slowing-down process, **U-238 resonance absorption** (at energies around 6.7 eV and 20 eV) captures some neutrons without causing fission. The **resonance escape probability** $p$ gives the fraction surviving:

$$N_3 = p \cdot N_2 = p \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \eta \cdot f \cdot N_0$$

Typical value $p \approx 0.87$.

### Step 4 — Thermal Neutron Cycle Repeats
The remaining neutrons $N_3$ are now thermal. They get absorbed in fuel (fraction $f$), repeating the cycle. After one full generation, the **multiplication per generation** is:

$$k_{\infty} = \frac{N_{\text{next gen}}}{N_{\text{current gen}}} = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$

$$\boxed{k_{\infty} = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f}$$

### Step 3.1.5 — Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Given:** A PWR with $\eta = 2.07$, $\varepsilon = 1.04$, $p = 0.89$, $f = 0.78$. Find $k_\infty$.

**Step-by-step solution:**

$$k_\infty = 2.07 \times 1.04 \times 0.89 \times 0.78$$

Compute in stages to avoid round-off error:

$$k_\infty = 2.07 \times 1.04 = 2.1528$$

$$k_\infty = 2.1528 \times 0.89 = 1.9160$$

$$k_\infty = 1.9160 \times 0.78 = 1.4945$$

So **$k_\infty \approx 1.49$** — typical for a fresh PWR core (infinite reactor is supercritical; finite geometry with leakage brings $k_{\text{eff}}$ down to 1.0 for critical operation).

## 3.2 Derivation: Reactivity from $k_{\text{eff}}$

Starting from the definition of effective multiplication:

$$k_{\text{eff}} = \frac{\text{neutrons in generation } n+1}{\text{neutrons in generation } n}$$

Define **reactivity** as fractional deviation from unity:

$$\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}} \quad \text{(sometimes written } \frac{\Delta k}{k}\text{)}$$

### Worked Example

**Given:** A reactor has $k_{\text{eff}} = 1.002$. Compute reactivity in **$\Delta k/k$**, **pcm**, and **dollars** (assume $\beta = 0.0065$).

**Solution:**

$$\rho = \frac{1.002 - 1}{1.002} = \frac{0.002}{1.002} = 0.001996 \approx 0.001996$$

In **percent mille (pcm)** — where 1 pcm = 10⁻⁵:

$$\rho = 0.001996 = 199.6 \text{ pcm} \approx 200 \text{ pcm}$$

In **dollars**:

$$\rho(\$) = \frac{0.001996}{0.0065} = 0.307\, \$$$

In **cents**:

$$\rho(\text{¢}) = 30.7 \text{ ¢}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Reactor Period from Point Kinetics (Simplified)

The **point kinetics equations** for a reactor (neglecting delayed neutrons for prompt-jump analysis) reduce to:

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} \cdot n$$

Separating variables and integrating with initial condition $n(0) = n_0$:

$$\int_{n_0}^{n(t)} \frac{dn}{n} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{n(t)}{n_0}\right) = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} \cdot t$$

$$n(t) = n_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(\frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} t\right)$$

Defining the **stable period** $\tau = \Lambda / (\rho - \beta)$:

$$\boxed{n(t) = n_0 \cdot e^{t/\tau}, \qquad P(t) \approx P_0 \cdot e^{t/\tau}}$$

### Worked Example — Doubling Time

**Given:** A reactor has $\rho = +0.0005$, $\beta = 0.0065$, $\Lambda = 10^{-4}$ s. Compute period $\tau$ and **doubling time** $t_{2\times}$.

**Solution:**

$$\tau = \frac{\Lambda}{\rho - \beta} = \frac{10^{-4}}{0.0005 - 0.0065} = \frac{10^{-4}}{-0.006} = -0.01667 \text{ s}$$

The **negative** period means $\rho < \beta$ → sub-prompt-critical → safe, slowly decaying. Magnitude is |τ| = 0.0167 s — too fast for operator intervention, hence SCRAM (rod drop) is mandatory.

**Doubling time** (for positive τ):

$$t_{2\times} = \tau \cdot \ln 2 = 0.693 \cdot \tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** For $\rho = \$1.00$ ($\rho = \beta$), the reactor is **prompt critical** and period approaches $\Lambda/\rho \approx 10^{-4}/6.5 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.015$ s — i.e., power can double in **~10 milliseconds**. This is why **prompt criticality is strictly forbidden** in reactor operation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Instrumentation vs. Reactor Type

| Sensor / Instrument | PWR | BWR | PHWR | LMFBR | Reason for Difference |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| In-core neutron flux (fission chamber) | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | All require; fast reactor needs high-range |
| Ex-core ion chamber (source + power range) | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓ | Provides trip & startup coverage |
| Boron concentration meter | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | ✓ (D₂O purity) | ✗ | PWR uses boric acid for shim control |
| Liquid metal flowmeter (EMF / ultrasonic) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | Sodium is opaque → special techniques |
| Acoustic leak detection (sodium-water) | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | Critical for SG tube leaks in FBR |
| Containment H₂ / O₂ monitor | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | FBR sodium + water = H₂ explosion risk |
| γ-dose area monitor (GM tube) | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓✓ | Universal — shielding verification |
| Failed-fuel detection (delay loop) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | Sodium itself is excellent failed-fuel detector |
| Moderator level (D₂O / H₂O) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓✓ | ✗ | PHWR has separate moderator circuit |

✓ = used, ✓✓ = commonly used, ✓✓✓ = essential, ✗ = not applicable

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete NPP Instrumentation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Core["NUCLEAR REACTOR CORE\nFission Heat Source"]
    PriLoop["PRIMARY COOLANT LOOP\nHigh Pressure D2O or H2O"]
    SG["STEAM GENERATOR\nHeat Exchanger"]
    SecLoop["SECONDARY LOOP\nSteam + Feedwater"]
    Turb["TURBINE GENERATOR SET"]
    Cond["CONDENSER"]
    Stack["OFF-GAS / COOLING TOWER"]

    NI["NUCLEAR INSTRUMENTATION\nEx-Core Ion Chambers\nIn-Core Fission Chambers\nStartup / Source Range"]
    PI["PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION\nRTD, TC, PT, FT, LT\nPressurizer Level + Pressure"]
    RI["RADIATION INSTRUMENTATION\nArea Gamma Monitors\nStack Release Monitors\nAerosol + Iodine Samplers"]

    RPS["REACTOR PROTECTION SYSTEM\nRPS - Trip Logic 2-out-of-4"]
    CR["CONTROL ROD DRIVE MECHANISM\nCRDM - Shim + Regulating"]
    SCRAM["SCRAM - Emergency Shutdown\nGravity Drop, <2 seconds"]

    DAS["DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM\nComputer + HMI in Control Room"]
    Ctrl["OPERATOR CONTROL ROOM\nMain Control Room MCR"]
    ESD["EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN SYSTEM\nESFAS Engineered Safety Features"]

    Core -->|heat| PriLoop
    PriLoop -->|hot coolant| SG
    SG -->|steam| SecLoop
    SecLoop -->|steam| Turb
    Turb -->|exhaust| Cond
    Cond -->|feedwater| SecLoop
    Cond -->|waste heat| Stack

    NI -.->|flux signal| DAS
    PI -.->|T, P, F, L signals| DAS
    RI -.->|dose, activity| DAS

    DAS --> Ctrl
    DAS -->|continuous control| CR
    CR -.->|rod position| Core
    CR -.->|position feedback| DAS

    NI -->|trip on high flux| RPS
    PI -->|trip on high T, low flow, low level| RPS
    RI -->|trip on high dose| RPS
    RPS -->|trip signal 2 of 4| SCRAM
    RPS -->|safety actuation| ESD

    SCRAM -.->|drop rods| Core
    ESD -.->|isolate, inject, spray| Core
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Reactor Control & Protection Loop (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["REACTOR POWER\nActual Plant State"]
    B["NEUTRON FLUX DETECTORS\nBF3 Proportional\nFission Chamber\nCompensated Ion Chamber"]
    C["SIGNAL CONDITIONING\nLog Count Rate Amp\nHV Bias Supply"]
    D["TRIP COMPARATOR\nSetpoint + 2-out-of-4 Voting"]
    E["LOG PERIOD METER\nReactivity Calculation"]
    F["CONTROL ROD DRIVE\nShim Bank + Regulating Bank"]
    G["OPERATOR PANEL\nAnnunciator + MFD"]
    H["SCRAM RELAY\nUnder-voltage Trip Coil"]

    A -->|neutrons| B
    B -->|pulses| C
    C -->|log power| D
    C -->|d log n / dt| E
    D -->|trip if exceeds| H
    E -->|warn if period too short| G
    G -->|manual command| F
    F -->|rod motion| A
    H -->|drop all rods| A
    A -->|temperature, pressure| C
```

## 4.3 Sensor Placement Diagram — Three Instrumentation Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZoneA["ZONE A - EX-CORE (Biological Shield Outside)"]
        A1["Source Range Detectors\nBF3 Proportional Counter\n10 to 10^4 n/cm2.s"]
        A2["Intermediate Range\nCompensated Ion Chamber\n10^3 to 10^8 n/cm2.s"]
        A3["Power Range Detectors\nUncompensated Ion Chamber\n10^8 to 10^11 n/cm2.s"]
    end

    subgraph ZoneB["ZONE B - IN-CORE (Inside Reactor Vessel)"]
        B1["Fission Chambers\nMovable / Fixed"]
        B2["Self-Powered Neutron Detectors SPND\nRhodium, Vanadium, Cobalt Emitters"]
        B3["Thermocouples - Core Exit Temperature"]
        B4["LPRM - Local Power Range Monitor"]
    end

    subgraph ZoneC["ZONE C - PRIMARY LOOP (Outside Core)"]
        C1["RTD - Hot Leg Temperature"]
        C2["RTD - Cold Leg Temperature"]
        C3["DPT - Pressurizer Level"]
        C4["PT - Pressurizer Pressure"]
        C5["FT - Primary Flow Venturi"]
        C6["LT - Steam Generator Level"]
    end

    ZoneA --> ZoneB
    ZoneB --> ZoneC
    C1 --> RPSlogic["RPS Voting Logic\n2 out of 4 Trip"]
    C2 --> RPSlogic
    C3 --> RPSlogic
    C4 --> RPSlogic
    C5 --> RPSlogic
    C6 --> RPSlogic
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** When asked *"Draw the three-zone instrumentation layout of a nuclear reactor"*, ensure you clearly differentiate **Ex-Core** (outside biological shield, low flux), **In-Core** (high flux, in-vessel, replaceable), and **Primary Loop** (process-side, conventional instrumentation).

## 4.4 Signal Conditioning & Counting Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Neutron Detector"] --> B["Preamplifier"]
    B --> C["Amplifier - Pulse Shaping"]
    C --> D["Discriminator - Threshold"]
    D --> E["Pulse Counter - Linear"]
    D --> F["Logarithmic Amplifier"]
    E --> G["Count Rate Meter"]
    F --> H["Log Power Display"]
    G --> I["RPS - Trip Setpoint Comparator"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Reactor Protection System"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 1]**
*Define the term **reactivity** of a nuclear reactor. What does it mean when reactivity is expressed in "dollars"?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Reactivity $\rho$ is defined as the fractional deviation of the effective multiplication factor from unity:
$$\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}}$$
It is a dimensionless quantity, also expressed as $\Delta k/k$.

- **Significance (1 mark):** $\rho = 0$ → reactor is **critical** (steady power); $\rho > 0$ → **supercritical** (power rising); $\rho < 0$ → **subcritical** (power falling).

- **Dollar unit (1 mark):** Since the delayed-neutron fraction $\beta \approx 0.0065$ for U-235 is very small, reactivity is normalized: $\rho(\$) = \rho / \beta$. One **dollar** is the reactivity equal to $\beta$ (the total delayed neutron fraction). One cent = 0.01 dollar. **\$1 reactivity = prompt criticality** (extremely hazardous).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 1]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the role of **Reactor Protection System (RPS)** in a nuclear power plant.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks Distribution):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The RPS is a **safety-grade, automatic, fail-safe** logic system that initiates **SCRAM** (rapid insertion of all control rods) when any monitored parameter exceeds its safety setpoint.

- **Block diagram (1 mark):** Sensors → Signal Conditioning → Trip Comparator (with **2-out-of-4 voting logic**) → Trip Relays → SCRAM Breakers → Control Rod Drive Mechanism → Gravity Drop of Rods.

- **Key features (1 mark):**
  - **Redundancy** (multiple channels) and **Diversity** (different sensor types for same parameter).
  - **Single-failure criterion** — failure of any one component must not prevent safety action.
  - Trip must be **fail-safe** (loss of power = trip activation).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain with necessary derivation the **four-factor formula** for the infinite multiplication factor of a nuclear reactor. State the physical significance of each factor. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PWR has $\eta = 2.07$, $\varepsilon = 1.04$, $p = 0.89$, $f = 0.78$, fast non-leakage probability $P_{NL} = 0.97$, and thermal non-leakage probability $P_{TL} = 0.96$. Calculate the **effective multiplication factor** and the **reactivity** of the reactor. State whether the reactor is subcritical, critical, or supercritical. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a) — (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** The four-factor formula is the foundation of reactor physics analysis. It represents the **neutron life cycle** in an infinite reactor (no leakage) and quantifies the **multiplication factor** $k_\infty$.

**2. Neutron Cycle Steps (5 marks — each factor explained):**

**Step 1 — Thermal Utilization ($f$):** When a thermal neutron is absorbed anywhere in the core (fuel + moderator + coolant + structure), the fraction absorbed in the **fuel** is:

$$f = \frac{\Sigma_{a,\text{fuel}}}{\Sigma_{a,\text{fuel}} + \Sigma_{a,\text{mod}} + \Sigma_{a,\text{cool}} + \Sigma_{a,\text{struct}}}$$

It represents the probability that a thermal neutron produces fission. Typical value: **0.70–0.85**. Higher $f$ is desired (more fuel loading, less parasitic absorption).

**Step 2 — Neutron Yield ($\eta$):** The number of **fast neutrons produced per thermal neutron absorbed in fuel**:

$$\eta = \nu \cdot \frac{\Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a}$$

For U-235, $\nu \approx 2.43$ and $\eta \approx 2.07$. Higher $\eta$ = more neutrons available for next generation.

**Step 3 — Fast Fission ($\varepsilon$):** Some fast neutrons cause **fast fission in U-238** (threshold ~1 MeV). The factor $\varepsilon$ = total fast neutrons / fast neutrons from thermal fission only. For PWR with enriched fuel: $\varepsilon \approx 1.03$.

**Step 4 — Resonance Escape ($p$):** During moderation (slowing down from fast to thermal), neutrons pass through **U-238 resonance energies** where absorption is high. The probability of *avoiding* this absorption is $p$. Typical: **0.87–0.95**. Heavier moderators (D₂O) and lumped fuel geometry increase $p$.

**3. Combined Formula (1 mark):**

$$k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$$

This is the **neutron multiplication per generation** in an infinite, homogeneous reactor.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b) — (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Calculate $k_\infty$ using four-factor formula (2 marks):**

$$k_\infty = 2.07 \times 1.04 \times 0.89 \times 0.78$$

Computing stepwise:

$$k_\infty = 2.07 \times 1.04 = 2.1528$$

$$k_\infty = 2.1528 \times 0.89 = 1.91600$$

$$k_\infty = 1.91600 \times 0.78 = 1.49448$$

So $k_\infty \approx 1.4945$.

**Step 2 — Calculate $k_{\text{eff}}$ by including leakage (2 marks):**

$$k_{\text{eff}} = k_\infty \cdot P_{\text{NL}} \cdot P_{\text{TL}}$$

$$k_{\text{eff}} = 1.4945 \times 0.97 \times 0.96$$

$$k_{\text{eff}} = 1.4945 \times 0.9312 = 1.3915$$

So $k_{\text{eff}} \approx 1.392$.

**Step 3 — Calculate reactivity (2 marks):**

$$\rho = \frac{k_{\text{eff}} - 1}{k_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1.392 - 1}{1.392} = \frac{0.392}{1.392} = 0.2816$$

So **$\rho \approx +0.282$** or equivalently **28.16%** or **28,160 pcm**.

**Step 4 — Reactor state (1 mark):** Since $k_{\text{eff}} > 1$ and $\rho > 0$, the reactor is **supercritical** — power would be rising. In practice, control rods must be inserted to bring $k_{\text{eff}} = 1$ and $\rho = 0$ for **steady-state critical operation**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe the **various types of nuclear radiation detectors** used in nuclear power plant instrumentation. Compare **Geiger-Müller (GM) tube**, **Ionization Chamber**, **Proportional Counter**, and **Scintillation Detector** in terms of operating principle, sensitivity, and application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with block diagram the **Reactor Protection System (RPS)** of a nuclear power plant. Discuss the role of **2-out-of-4 voting logic**, redundancy, and diversity in ensuring plant safety. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a) — (7 Marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** Radiation detectors convert incident nuclear radiation ($\alpha$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, neutrons) into measurable electrical signals. Selection depends on **type of radiation, energy range, count rate, and required sensitivity**.

**2. Detector Comparison (5 marks — table-style explanation):**

| Detector | Principle | Output | Sensitivity | Dead Time | Application in NPP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ionization Chamber** | Radiation ionizes gas; DC current measured between electrodes | Continuous DC current (μA) | Low–Medium (~10⁻⁸ C/R/h) | None (DC mode) | **Ex-core neutron flux** (power range), area gamma monitoring |
| **Proportional Counter** | Gas multiplication in avalanche region near central anode wire | Discrete pulses proportional to energy | Medium (~10⁻⁶ C/R/h) | ~1 μs | **Source/intermediate range neutron flux** (BF₃, He-3) |
| **Geiger-Müller Tube** | Avalanche throughout tube, Geiger discharge | Large uniform pulses (independent of radiation energy) | Very High | ~100–500 μs | **Area gamma monitors**, contamination surveys, low-dose alarms |
| **Scintillation Detector** | Radiation excites crystal (NaI(Tl), plastic), photons detected by PMT | Light pulses → electrical | Very High (energy-resolving) | ~ns | **Stack release monitors**, isotopic analysis, spectroscopy |

**3. Selection criteria for NPP (1 mark):**
- **Neutron flux monitoring**: Ionization chambers (high range) + Proportional counters (low range) — overlap ensures coverage over **~10 decades**.
- **Area radiation monitoring**: GM tubes (high sensitivity, cheap, robust).
- **Effluent & stack monitoring**: Scintillation detectors (energy discrimination).

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b) — (7 Marks)

**1. RPS Definition (1 mark):** The Reactor Protection System is a **safety-critical, automatic logic system** designed to rapidly terminate reactor operation by initiating **SCRAM** when any monitored parameter crosses its safety setpoint.

**2. Architecture (2 marks):** As shown in Section 4.1, the RPS consists of four independent channels (A, B, C, D) each containing:
- **Input sensors** (e.g., neutron flux, T, P, flow, level)
- **Signal conditioning**
- **Trip comparator** with setpoint
- **Trip relay (latching)**
- **Power supply** (independent battery-backed)

**3. 2-out-of-4 Voting Logic (2 marks):** A trip occurs only if **any 2 of the 4 channels** register a trip signal. This provides:
- **Redundancy**: Failure of any single channel does not initiate spurious trip or miss a genuine one.
- **Single-failure criterion** compliance (per IEEE 603 and IEC 61513).

**4. Diversity Principle (1 mark):** Different physical principles must be used for the same safety function. Example: Trip on high power must be sensed by **both neutron flux (nuclear)** and **coolant temperature rise (process)**. This prevents **common-mode failure** (e.g., a single sensor design flaw disabling both neutron trips).

**5. Fail-Safe Design (1 mark):** Trip relays are **energize-to-operate** (under-voltage trip). Loss of any power supply causes rods to drop by gravity — power is *removed*, not *added*, to actuate safety.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on Nuclear Power Plant Instrumentation questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing reactivity sign convention** — Do NOT say "reactivity is 1.002". Reactivity is **deviation from 1**, so $k=1.002$ gives $\rho = 0.002$, not 1.002.
> 2. **Forgetting delayed neutron fraction** — When computing reactor period, neglecting $\beta$ gives a **wildly underestimated period** and marks lost for "ignoring safety reality."
> 3. **Mixing infinite and effective multiplication** — $k_\infty$ is a **property of fuel-lattice composition**; $k_{\text{eff}}$ depends on **core geometry/leakage**. Don't substitute one for the other.
> 4. **Treating SCRAM as "switching off"** — Reactor is *scrammed*, not switched off. Residual decay heat (~7% of rated power immediately after shutdown) must be removed by **ECCS** (Emergency Core Cooling System). Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing the distinction between redundancy and diversity** — Redundancy = multiple identical channels; Diversity = multiple *different* principles for the same function. Many students write "redundancy" when they mean "diversity."

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⚛️ **Nuclear Fission**: U-235 + n → 2 fragments + 2–3 n + **200 MeV**.
- 📐 **Four-Factor Formula**: $k_\infty = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f$ — tracks one neutron through one generation.
- 📐 **Six-Factor / Effective Formula**: $k_{\text{eff}} = k_\infty \cdot P_{\text{NL}} \cdot P_{\text{TL}}$ — includes fast + thermal leakage.
- 🎯 **Reactivity**: $\rho = (k_{\text{eff}} - 1)/k_{\text{eff}}$; sign determines sub/critical/super state.
- 💵 **Dollars & Cents**: $\rho(\$) = \rho / \beta$; \$1 = prompt critical (forbidden regime).
- ⏱️ **Reactor Period**: $P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{t/\tau}$; for $\rho > \beta$, period $\approx \Lambda/\rho$ (microseconds → SCRAM).
- 🛡️ **Three Instrumentation Loops**: Nuclear (flux), Process (T, P, F, L), Radiation (dose).
- 🔁 **Defense-in-Depth**: Redundancy (2/4 voting) + Diversity (multiple principles) + Independence.
- 🚨 **SCRAM**: Gravity-drop control rods; trip on 2-out-of-4 logic; fail-safe under-voltage design.
- 🌡️ **Post-SCRAM**: 7% decay heat must be cooled by ECCS (Low Head Safety Injection, accumulators, sprays).
- 📊 **Neutron Detection Ranges**:
  - Source Range: BF₃ proportional (10⁻⁸–10⁴ n/cm²·s)
  - Intermediate Range: Compensated IC (10³–10⁸ n/cm²·s)
  - Power Range: Uncompensated IC (10⁸–10¹¹ n/cm²·s)
- 🧪 **Detectors**:
  - **GM tube** → Area γ monitoring, alarms
  - **Ion Chamber** → Ex-core neutron flux, area dose
  - **Proportional Counter (BF₃, He-3)** → Source & intermediate range neutrons
  - **Scintillation (NaI(Tl))** → Stack release, spectroscopy
  - **Fission Chamber** → In-core power mapping
- 🇮🇳 **Indian NPP Types**: PHWR (most common — Kaiga, Kakrapar), PWR (Tarapur), BWR (none currently), PFBR (Kalpakkam — LMFBR).
- 🔢 **Safety constant**: $\beta_{\text{U-235}} \approx 0.0065$; $\beta_{\text{Pu-239}} \approx 0.0021$ — Pu reactors are inherently less forgiving.
- 🏭 **KTU Hot-Question Template**: "Compare instrumentation requirements of PWR vs LMFBR" → emphasize: sodium opacity (no visual inspection), acoustic leak detection, EMF flowmeters, H₂ monitoring, higher neutron flux range.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagram of Different Types in Nuclear Power Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Piping and Instrumentation Diagram of Different Types in Nuclear Power Plant # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**: A P&ID is a **graphical schematic representation** of the functional relationship between piping, process equipment, instrumentation, and control system components within a process plant. It is the **single most important engineering document** used in the design, construction, operation, and maintenance of any process facility, including **Nuclear Power Plants (NPPs)**. In a Nuclear Power Plant, a P&ID is governed primarily by: - **ISA-5.1** – Instrumentation Symbols and Identification - **ISA-5.3** – Flowchart Symbols for Distributed Control Systems - **ANSI/ASME B31.1** – Power Piping Code - **IEEE 603** – Standard for Nuclear Power Plant Safety Systems - **IAEA Safety Standards** – NS-G-1.3 (Instrumentation and Control Systems) > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction**: A P&ID is **NOT** a Process Flow Diagram (PFD). A PFD shows the broad process flow and material balance, while a P&ID includes **every pipe, every valve, every instrument, every signal line, every interlock**, and every safety device. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡 Imagine a **city's underground water supply network**. A regular map might show only the major water mains and treatment plants. But a P&ID is like giving a plumber the **complete technical blueprint** that shows: - Every pipe's diameter and material - Every valve's type (gate, ball, check) and location - Every pressure gauge, flow meter, and temperature sensor - Every automatic control loop - Every safety relief mechanism **In a Nuclear Power Plant**, the P&ID is the **engineering equivalent of a pilot's cockpit instrument panel**, but mapped out across the entire plant. It tells operators and engineers exactly **what is happening, where it is happening, and how the system will automatically respond** during normal operation, startup, shutdown, and—most critically—**emergency scenarios**. ## 1.3 Why P&IDs are CRITICAL in Nuclear Power Plants | Aspect | Importance | |---|---| | **Safety** | Documents every safety interlock, relief valve, and ECCS path | | **Regulatory Compliance** | Required by **AERB (India)**, **NRC (USA)**, **IAEA** | | **Operator Training** | Used as the basis for simulator training and procedures | | **Failure Analysis** | Essential root cause analysis tool post-incident | | **Maintenance** | Guides isolation, draining, and recommissioning | | **Design Verification** | Confirms single-failure criterion compliance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Pressure**: Nuclear systems are typically designed for **155 bar (≈ 2250 psi)** primary loop pressure in a typical PWR, with temperatures around **325°C (617°F)** at the reactor outlet. ## 1.4 ISA-5.1 Instrument Identification — The "Tag Name" Convention Every instrument on a P&ID has a **unique alphanumeric tag** following the **ISA-5.1** standard: $$\text{Tag} = \underbrace{XX}_{\text{Function Letters}} \underbrace{Y}_{\text{Modifier}} \underbrace{nnn}_{\text{Loop Number}}$$ Where: - **XX** = First-letter (Measured Variable), Second-letter (Function) - **Y** = Optional modifier (e.g., "D" for differential, "H" for high) - **nnn** = Loop number ### 1.4.1 Common First Letters (Measured Variable) | Letter | Measured Variable | Letter | Measured Variable | |---|---|---|---| | **T** | Temperature | **P** | Pressure | | **F** | Flow | **L** | Level | | **A** | Analysis | **C** | Conductivity | | **R** | Radiation | **N** | Neutron Flux | | **W** | Weight | **S** | Speed | | **V** | Vibration | **E** | Voltage | ### 1.4.2 Common Subsequent Letters (Function) | Letter | Function | Letter | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **I** | Indicate | **C** | Control | | **R** | Record | **T** | Transmit | | **A** | Alarm | **S** | Switch | | **V** | Valve | **E** | Element (Sensor) | **Example**: `TE-101` = **Temperature Element**, Loop 101. `PIC-205` = **Pressure Indicating Controller**, Loop 205. `NR-30` = **Neutron Recorder**, Loop 30. > [!NOTE] > A **circle on the P&ID** indicates an instrument mounted in the **main control room (MCR)**. A circle with a **horizontal line through it** indicates a **field-mounted** instrument. A circle with a **dashed line** indicates a **computer/DCS-mounted** (distributed control system) instrument. # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a P&ID — The Six Core Layers A P&ID is a multi-layered schematic. Every Nuclear P&ID contains the following six logical layers: ### Layer 1: Process Equipment (Mechanical) - **Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV)** - **Steam Generators (SG)** - **Pressurizer (PZR)** - **Reactor Coolant Pumps (RCP)** - **Containment Building** - **Condenser, Feedwater Heaters, Turbines** ### Layer 2: Piping - Process lines (primary, secondary, auxiliary) - Safety-grade lines (ECCS, containment spray) - Service lines (instrument air, cooling water, firewater) - Each pipe has: **Size (NPS), Schedule (wall thickness), Material, Service class, Insulation, Tracing** ### Layer 3: Piping Components - **Valves** (Gate, Globe, Ball, Check, Butterfly, Safety, Relief, Control, Motor-operated, Air-operated) - **Fittings** (Elbows, Tees, Reducers, Flanges, Expansion joints) - **Special Items** (Strainers, Sight glasses, Orifice plates, Ejectors) ### Layer 4: Instrumentation (Field) - Sensors (RTD, thermocouple, pressure transmitter, flow transmitter) - Local gauges (PG, TG, LG) - Switches (pressure switch, level switch) ### Layer 5: Instrumentation (Control Room) - Indicators, Recorders, Controllers, Annunciators - DCS/HMI stations - Safety Logic Cabinets (SLC) ### Layer 6: Signal & Control Lines - **Solid line** = Pneumatic signal (typically 3–15 psig) - **Dashed line** = Electrical signal (4–20 mA, 1–5 V) - **Double dashed line** = Software/data link (DCS communication) - **Triangular tip** = Direction of signal flow ## 2.2 Standard P&ID Valve Symbols > [!IMPORTANT] > In Nuclear P&IDs, **valve symbols are supplemented by their failure position (FO = Fail Open, FC = Fail Closed, FL = Fail Last)**, which is a critical safety parameter. | Symbol | Valve Type | Typical Application in NPP | |---|---|---| | Bow-tie (two triangles) | **Gate Valve** | Main steam line isolation | | Body with disc | **Globe Valve** | Feedwater regulation | | Body with ball | **Ball Valve** | Quick-isolation, safety | | Poppet symbol | **Check Valve** | RCP discharge to prevent backflow | | Spring over triangle | **Safety/Relief Valve** | Pressurizer PORV (Power Operated Relief Valve) | | Circle with stem | **Control Valve** | SG level control valve | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — P&ID Engineering & Fluid Dynamics > [!NOTE] > Although P&IDs are primarily **graphical documents**, several underlying equations govern the sizing, flow, and pressure relationships that the diagram must satisfy. The following formulae are **high-yield for KTU exams**. | Formula | Description | Standard Notation | |---|---|---| | $$\Delta P = \frac{4 f L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$ | **Darcy-Weisbach** pressure drop in pipe | $f$ = friction factor, $L$ = length, $D$ = diameter, $v$ = velocity, $\rho$ = density | | $$Q = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P}{G_f}}$$ | Valve sizing equation | $Q$ = flow, $C_v$ = valve coefficient, $G_f$ = specific gravity | | $$\dot{m} = \rho A v = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$$ | **Orifice / Flow** metering | $C_d$ = discharge coefficient | | $$P_{set} = P_{design} \times SF$$ | **Relief valve** set pressure | $SF$ = safety factor (typ. 1.05–1.10) | | $$\tau = \frac{V_{RC}}{\dot{m}_{primary}}$$ | Reactor coolant residence time | $V_{RC}$ = RCS volume | | $$N = \frac{d(\ln k_{eff})}{d(\ln \rho)} \cdot \frac{1}{k_{eff}}$$ | Reactivity coefficient (subcritical) | $\rho$ = reactivity, $k_{eff}$ = effective multiplication | | $$q = h A \Delta T$$ | **Heat transfer** (Steam Generator) | $h$ = HTC, $A$ = area, $\Delta T$ = LMTD | | $$\eta_{thermal} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$ | Plant thermal efficiency | $W_{net}$ = net work, $Q_{in}$ = heat input | ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of P&IDs 1. **Design Review**: P&IDs are the master document for **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability)** and **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** studies. 2. **Construction**: They serve as the **pipe routing master plan** for fabricators and erectors. 3. **Commissioning**: Defines the **line-list, punch-list, and test package** boundaries. 4. **Operations**: The operator's procedure manual is a **direct narrative of the P&ID**. 5. **Regulatory**: **AERB Safety Guide No. D-2** (India) mandates P&ID submission for nuclear plant licensing. ## 2.5 The "Why" Behind the Standardization Nuclear safety rests on the principle of **Defense in Depth (DiD)** — five concentric levels of safety. The P&ID is the engineering document that **physically documents** the implementation of: - **Level 1**: Conservative design - **Level 2**: Operational control (represented by control loops) - **Level 3**: Engineered safety features (ECCS, containment) - **Level 4**: Accident management procedures - **Level 5**: Emergency response (off-site) > [!NOTE] > **Single Failure Criterion (10 CFR 50 Appendix A, GDC-21 / AERB S-1.1)**: Every safety system in a P&ID must perform its function even with **one single active component failed** and one **passive component failed** simultaneously. P&IDs are reviewed line-by-line to verify this. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Orifice Flow Equation (Used in Flow P&ID Sizing) > [!IMPORTANT] > Almost every flow transmitter in a Nuclear Plant P&ID is paired with a **differential-pressure (DP) flow element** (orifice plate, venturi, or flow nozzle). The following derivation is **high-yield** for KTU Part B questions. ### Starting Point — Bernoulli's Equation (Ideal, Incompressible, Steady) For an ideal fluid in a horizontal pipe contracting from area $A_1$ to $A_2$: $$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2$$ **Step 1** — Isolate the pressure differential: $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$ **Step 2** — Apply continuity equation for mass conservation: $$\rho A_1 v_1 = \rho A_2 v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = v_2 \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 3** — Substitute $v_1$ into the Bernoulli expression: $$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho \left( v_2^2 - v_2^2 \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2} \right) = \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \left( 1 - \frac{A_2^2}{A_1^2} \right)$$ **Step 4** — Define the **diameter ratio** $\beta = \dfrac{d_2}{d_1} = \sqrt{\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}}$, and solve for $v_2$: $$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)}}$$ **Step 5** — Introduce the **discharge coefficient $C_d$** to account for real-fluid effects (viscous losses, vena contracta): $$v_2^{real} = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)}}$$ **Step 6** — The volumetric flow rate $Q = A_2 v_2^{real}$: $$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)}}$$ **Step 7** — In mass form, $\dot{m} = \rho Q$: $$\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **fundamental flow equation** behind the **FT (Flow Transmitter)** instruments on every nuclear P&ID. The $\beta$ ratio is critical: a typical value is $\beta = 0.5$ to $0.7$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressurizer Set-Pressure Logic The **Pressurizer (PZR)** is a key equipment in every PWR P&ID. Its pressure control logic uses proportional and pressure-surge control. ### Surge Line Flow (Plant-Specific) The pressurizer surge line connects the **hot leg** of the primary loop to the pressurizer. When pressure rises (e.g., during a power excursion), water surges INTO the pressurizer, compressing the steam cushion. $$\Delta P_{PZR} = \frac{1}{V_{steam}} \left[ m_{in} h_{in} - m_{out} h_{out} - V_{PZR} \frac{dP}{dt} \right]$$ **Simplified Energy Balance for Surge Step**: $$\Delta V_{surge} = \frac{m_{surged}}{\rho_{primary}}$$ The **relief valve set pressure** is derived from: $$P_{set} = P_{nominal} + \Delta P_{safety\,margin}$$ $$\boxed{P_{set} = 1.10 \times P_{design}}$$ Where the **10% margin** is mandated by **ASME Section XI** to prevent valve "simmer" and ensure positive opening. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping the PWR Primary Loop P&ID The following analytical matrix maps **every major node** in a typical Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) primary loop P&ID to its **instrumentation, control action, and safety interlock**. | P&ID Node | Function | Primary Instrument | Control Variable | Safety Instrument (ECCS / Protection) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV)** | Houses core, sustains nuclear chain reaction | **NR-30** (Neutron flux), **TR-101/102** (Inlet/Outlet T/C) | Power / Reactivity | **RPS** (Reactor Protection System), **SCRAM** | | **Hot Leg** (RPV → SG) | Transfers hot primary coolant | **TE-201** (T), **FE-201** (Flow) | Coolant temperature | **RT-201** (Resistance Temp. Detector for trip) | | **Steam Generator (SG)** | Heat exchange primary↔secondary | **LT-301** (Level), **PR-302** (Pressure) | SG Level, Steam Pressure | **SG Low Level Trip**, **SG High Level Trip** | | **Cold Leg** (RCP → RPV) | Returns cooled primary coolant | **TE-401** (T), **PT-402** (Pressure) | Coolant temperature | **Low-T Low-P Trip** | | **Reactor Coolant Pump (RCP)** | Circulates primary coolant | **ST-501** (Speed), **VT-502** (Vibration) | Pump speed | **RCP Trip on under-voltage/over-current** | | **Pressurizer (PZR)** | Maintains primary pressure | **PIC-601** (Pressure controller), **LT-602** (Level) | Pressure, Water level | **PORV-601** (Power Operated Relief Valve), **SV-602** (Safety Valve) | | **Containment** | Houses primary system | **PT-701** (Pressure), **RT-702** (Radiation) | Pressure, Activity | **Containment Spray**, **Containment Isolation** | | **Pressurizer Surge Line** | Equalizes PZR pressure with RCS | **FT-801** (Surge flow) | Surge flow rate | **Surge Line Isolation Valve** | ## 3.4 P&ID Control Loop Block Diagram Analysis > [!NOTE] > The classical **Negative Feedback Control Loop** is the building block of every P&ID. Below is its **architecture** as represented in a P&ID. $$\text{Setpoint (SP)} \xrightarrow{+} \bigotimes \xrightarrow{e(t)} \boxed{\text{Controller (C)}} \xrightarrow{u(t)} \boxed{\text{Final Control Element (Valve)}} \xrightarrow{} \boxed{\text{Process}} \xrightarrow{} \boxed{\text{Sensor (T)}} \xrightarrow{ PV} \bigotimes$$ **Transfer Function (PID Controller)**: $$C(s) = K_p \left( 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right)$$ Where: - $K_p$ = Proportional gain - $T_i$ = Integral time - $T_d$ = Derivative time > [!IMPORTANT] > In **Nuclear Safety Systems**, the P&ID does NOT use PID control. Instead, **on/off (bang-bang) and trip logic** dominate because they provide **deterministic, fail-safe** response. PIDs are used in **normal operation control loops only**. ## 3.5 Exhaustive Derivation of Relief Valve Sizing The **API 520 / ASME Section I** method for relief valve orifice area sizing: **Step 1** — Determine required relieving capacity $\dot{W}$ (kg/hr) from the P&ID mass balance. **Step 2** — Calculate theoretical orifice area $A_0$: $$A_0 = \frac{\dot{W}}{C K_d K_c K_v P_1 K_n} \sqrt{\frac{Z T}{M}}$$ **Step 3** — Select the **API standard orifice designation** (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R) such that the actual orifice area $\geq A_0$. **Step 4** — Verify **choked flow** check: $$P_{choked} = P_1 \left( \frac{2}{k+1} \right)^{\frac{k}{k-1}}$$ **Step 5** — Verify **margin against overpressure**: Set pressure $\leq$ 92.5% of MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) for fire case. # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block: P&ID of a Typical PWR (Block-Level Functional Architecture) > [!NOTE] > Mermaid cannot directly render a true ISA-standard P&ID with its full graphical detail (which requires CAD tools like AutoCAD P&ID or Intergraph SmartPlant P&ID). Instead, the following **Block-Level Functional Architecture** shows the *complete system topology, instrument tag IDs, signal flows, and interlock paths* as you would see on a P&ID sheet. ```mermaid graph TD RPV["RPV-100
Reactor Pressure Vessel

Reactor Coolant Pump A"] RCP2["RCP-300B

Pressurizer

To PZR"] PORV["PORV-401

Fail Open on Loss of Air"] SV1["SV-402
Code Safety Valve

Main Steam Line

Designed for 4 bar peak"] CS["CS-700

Accumulator Tank

High Pressure Safety Injection"] LPCI["LPCI-803

Turbine Trip on
SG Low-Low Level" .-> RPS FW --> CV1 CV1 --> SG1 CV1 --> SG2 RPV -. "Core Temperature
TE-101" .-> RPS RPV -. "Neutron Flux
NR-30" .-> RPS PZR -. "Pressure High
PIC-601 Trip" .-> RPS RPS --> ACC1 RPS --> HPSI RPS --> LPCI HPSI --> RPV LPCI --> RPV ACC1 --> RPV CS --> CT RPV --> CT ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block: BWR (Boiling Water Reactor) P&ID Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > A BWR differs fundamentally from a PWR: **boiling occurs in the core itself**, and the steam goes directly to the turbine. The P&ID therefore has fewer loops but more **isolation valve logic** for radioactive steam. ```mermaid graph TD BRPV["Reactor Vessel
Boiling Water Reactor
Core Steam at 72 bar"] STM["Steam Line
Saturated Steam to Turbine

High Pressure / Low Pressure"] COND["Condenser

"High Pressure Trip" .-> ECCI ECCI --> CSW CSW --> BRPV BRPV -. "Low Water Level
Trip" .-> ADS ADS --> SRV ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block: P&ID Instrument Signal Flow Architecture ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Field (Nuclear Island)" TE101["TE-101

Temperature Indicator

Controller + Alarm"] FRC201["FRC-201
Flow Recorder
Controller"] LICA301["LICA-301
Level Indicating
Controller + Alarm"] end subgraph "Safety Panel (Class 1E)" RTS101["RT-101
Resistance Temperature
Trip Channel A"] RPS900["RPS-900
Reactor Protection

Safety Logic

Electrical"| TIA101 PT601 -->|"4-20 mA"| PICA601 FT201 -->|"4-20 mA"| FRC201 LT301 -->|"4-20 mA"| LICA301 TIA101 -. "Alarm
Solid Logic"| RPS900 PICA601 -. "Trip on
Hi Pressure"| RPS900 FRC201 -. "Low Flow Trip"| RPS900 LICA301 -. "SG Lo-Lo

Fail-Safe De-energize"| FCV601 LICA301 -->|"3-15 psi
Pneumatic"| FCV601 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Block: ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System) P&ID Architecture > [!NOTE] > The ECCS is the **most safety-critical P&ID** in a nuclear plant. Per **10 CFR 50.46**, it must deliver core cooling for the entire duration of a **Large Break Loss of Coolant Accident (LBLOCA)**. ```mermaid graph TD ACC["ACC-1
Accumulators 4x
Borated 2000 ppm
40 bar N2 Precharge"] HPP["HPSI Pump 1
Centrifugal 200 m3/hr"] HPS["HPSI Train A and B
Redundant"] LPP["LPCI Pump
Large Centrifugal"] LPS["LPCI Train A and B"] CSPR["Core Spray Pump
2x 100% Capacity"] RWST["RWST
Refueling Water

Post-LOCA Recirculation"] RPVINJ["Injection Point
Reactor Vessel Nozzle"] CSPRINJ["Core Spray Header
Above Core"] ACC -->|"SOV Open on
Low RCS P"| RPVINJ HPS -->|"Check Valve
MOV Isolation"| RPVINJ LPS -->|"MOV
Class 1E Power"| RPVINJ RWST --> HPS RWST --> LPS RWST --> CSPR CSPR --> CSPRINJ CTMT -. "Recirc Mode
On Low RWST Level" .-> HPS CTMT -. "Recirc Mode" .-> LPS ``` ## 4.5 Mermaid Block: Containment System P&ID ```mermaid graph TD CT["Containment Vessel
Free Volume 60,000 m3
Design Pressure 4 bar"] CSP["Containment Spray Pump
2x 100 percent"] CSR["Containment Spray Ring
