For pH Control"] RWST2["RWST (Shared)"] IOL["Containment Isolation
Valves - Inside Outside"] AIO["Atmosphere Iodine
Removal"] HVR["Hydrogen Recombiners
PAR - Passive Autocatalytic"] VAC["Vacuum Relief Valve
Prevents Negative P"] HVS["Hydrogen Vent System
Post Severe Accident"] CSP --> CSR CSR --> CT RWST2 --> CSP NAOH --> CSP AIO --> CT HVR --> CT VAC --> CT HVS --> CT ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEICT523] **Define P&ID. List any four ISA-5.1 standard symbols used in a nuclear power plant P&ID with their function.** **Model Answer**: A Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) is a detailed schematic representation showing the piping, equipment, instrumentation, and control system interconnections within a process plant, conforming to ISA-5.1 standards. Unlike a PFD, it includes every pipe, valve, and instrument. **Four ISA-5.1 Symbols**: 1. **Circle with "TIC"**: Temperature Indicating Controller (e.g., `TIC-101`) — senses temperature and outputs control signal. 2. **Circle with "PI"**: Pressure Indicator (e.g., `PI-601`) — displays pressure locally or in MCR. 3. **Bow-tie symbol**: Gate Valve — used for full-bore isolation in primary loop. 4. **Spring-over-triangle**: Safety/Relief Valve — opens on overpressure, e.g., Pressurizer Safety Valve `SV-602`. **Marking Tip**: Award 1 mark for definition; 2 marks for correct symbol list with function. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEICT523] **Explain the difference between a PWR and BWR with respect to their P&ID topology.** **Model Answer**: | Feature | PWR | BWR | |---|---|---| | Loops on P&ID | **Two distinct loops** (primary + secondary) | **Single loop** (boiling in core) | | Steam Generation | In Steam Generator (heat exchanger) | **In reactor core itself** | | Steam Path | Primary → SG → Secondary (turbine) | Direct core → turbine | | Pressurizer | **Yes** (PZR on primary loop) | **No** (steam dome in vessel) | | Safety Valves | Pressurizer PORV + SV | Multiple SRVs on reactor vessel | | ECCS Topology | 4 trains (HPSI, LPCI, CS, Accumulators) | HPCS, LPCS, Core Spray, ADS | | Radiation on Steam | **No** (secondary clean) | **Yes** (turbine in contaminated zone) | | P&ID Isolation | Less stringent | MSIVs (4 valves per line) | ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Below are **two fully-solved alternative questions** that test the same CO at the same Bloom level. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PEICT523 Module 1]** **(a)** Draw and explain the **P&ID of a PWR primary loop** with all major equipment, instrumentation tags, and control loops. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution**: **Step 1 — Identify Major Equipment** (1 mark): 1. Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) 2. Two Steam Generators (SG-A, SG-B) 3. Two Reactor Coolant Pumps (RCP-A, RCP-B) 4. Pressurizer (PZR) 5. Surge Line **Step 2 — Identify Pipes** (1 mark): - Hot leg: RPV → SG (≈325°C, 155 bar) - Cold leg: SG → RCP → RPV (≈290°C) - Surge line: Hot leg ↔ PZR **Step 3 — Identify Instrumentation with ISA-5.1 Tags** (3 marks): | Tag | Description | Function | |---|---|---| | `NR-30` | Neutron Recorder | Records neutron flux from ex-core detectors | | `TE-101` | Temperature Element (Hot Leg) | RTD for primary T measurement | | `TE-102` | Temperature Element (Cold Leg) | RTD for primary T measurement | | `FE-201` | Flow Element | Orifice plate + DP transmitter for primary flow | | `LT-301` | Level Transmitter (SG) | DP cell for SG narrow-range level | | `PIC-601` | Pressure Indicating Controller (PZR) | Controls PZR pressure via heater/spray | | `LT-602` | Level Transmitter (PZR) | Controls PZR water level via CVs | | `FT-801` | Flow Transmitter (Surge Line) | Measures surge flow | | `PT-701` | Pressure Transmitter (Containment) | Monitors containment pressure | **Step 4 — Describe Control Loops** (2 marks): - **Loop 1 (Pressurizer Pressure Control)**: `PT-601 → PIC-601 → Heater/Spray Valve`. If pressure low, heaters turn on; if high, spray valve opens. - **Loop 2 (SG Level Control)**: `LT-301 → LICA-301 → FCV-601 (feedwater valve)`. Maintains SG water level at 50% NRL. - **Loop 3 (Reactor Power Control)**: `NR-30 → Avg Power → Rod Control System`. Adjusts control rod position to match T_avg setpoint. **Conclusion**: The PWR primary P&ID integrates 3 control loops, 4 safety-grade trip channels, and the Defense-in-Depth principle, with strict single-failure criterion compliance. --- **(b)** Describe the **ECCS P&ID architecture** and explain the function of each subsystem. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution**: **Introduction** (1 mark): The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) is a **safety-grade, multi-redundant** system that ensures core cooling during a Loss of Coolant Accident (LOCA). It has 4 sub-systems per the P&ID. **Subsystem 1: Accumulators (ACC)** (1.5 marks): - 4 nos., passive (no power needed), borated water (2000 ppm) pressurized by N2 at 40 bar. - Discharge on RCS pressure dropping below accumulator pressure (automatic, check valves open). - **P&ID Tag**: `ACC-801`, isolation by `MOV-101` and `SOV-102` (solenoid). **Subsystem 2: HPSI (High Pressure Safety Injection)** (1.5 marks): - Centrifugal pumps (2 × 100%), discharge pressure ≈ 155 bar. - Suction from RWST; injection through cold leg nozzles. - **P&ID Tag**: `HPSI-802A/B`, motor `M-802`, control valve `FCV-803`. **Subsystem 3: LPCI (Low Pressure Coolant Injection)** (1.5 marks): - Operates in **recirculation mode** (sump suction) after RWST empties. - Discharge pressure ≈ 8–10 bar, very high flow. - **P&ID Tag**: `LPCI-803A/B`. **Subsystem 4: Core Spray (CS)** (1 mark): - Floods the core from above (gravity-driven, redundant pumps). - Provides core cooling even with low RCS pressure. - **P&ID Tag**: `CSPR-804A/B`. **Concluding Note** (0.5 mark): Per **10 CFR 50.46**, ECCS must maintain Peak Clad Temperature < 2200°F (1204°C) and core oxidation < 17% — these are **design acceptance criteria** verified on the P&ID. --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Option 2 **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, PEICT523 Module 1, Alternative]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **P&ID of a BWR primary system** highlighting the role of MSIVs and SRVs. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution**: **Step 1 — System Description** (1 mark): A Boiling Water Reactor has a **single-loop P&ID** because the steam is generated in the core itself. The reactor vessel has a steam dome at the top. **Step 2 — Major Equipment** (1 mark): 1. Reactor Vessel (with internal jet pumps) 2. Recirculation pumps 3. Main Steam Lines (4 lines, one per separator) 4. Main Steam Isolation Valves (MSIVs) 5. Safety Relief Valves (SRVs) 6. Turbine / Condenser / Feedwater train **Step 3 — MSIV Role** (2 marks): - Two MSIVs in series per steam line: one **inside containment** (inboard), one **outside** (outboard). - **Function**: On high containment pressure or high radiation (Ci-2 isolation), the MSIVs **close within 3–5 seconds** to prevent radioactive steam release. - **P&ID Tag**: `MSIV-1A/B/C/D` (4 lines × 2 valves = 8 valves total), with `FSIV` (fail-safe) feature. - **Closed on**: Loss of DC power, high containment pressure, high main-steam-line radiation, low-low water level. **Step 4 — SRV Role** (2 marks): - 8–10 Safety Relief Valves mounted on the **steam space** of the reactor vessel. - **Function**: Overpressure protection (ASME code). - Operate in three modes: **safety (spring), relief (spring), and atmospheric (with tail pipe to wetwell)**. - **P&ID Tag**: `SRV-1` through `SRV-8`, `SRV-ADS-1/2` (for ADS function). - Discharge into **suppression pool (wetwell)** to condense steam and contain radioactivity. **Step 5 — Recirculation Loop** (1 mark): - Internal jet pumps driven by external recirculation pumps. Controls reactor power without control rods (BWR feature). --- **(b)** Discuss the **ISA-5.1 instrument identification system** with suitable examples from a nuclear plant P&ID. Also explain the meaning of instrument bubble symbols (circle, slashed, dashed). **(7 marks)** **Model Solution**: **Step 1 — ISA-5.1 Tag Format** (2 marks): $$\text{Tag} = \underbrace{XX}_{\text{First Letter = Variable}} \, \underbrace{Y}_{\text{Optional Modifier}} \, \underbrace{nnn}_{\text{Loop Number}}$$ Examples from a PWR P&ID: - `TE-101` = **T**emperature **E**lement, Loop 101 (RTD sensor) - `TIC-101` = **T**emperature **I**ndicating **C**ontroller, Loop 101 - `PIC-601` = **P**ressure **I**ndicating **C**ontroller, Loop 601 - `LICA-301` = **L**evel **I**ndicating **C**ontroller + **A**larm, Loop 301 - `NR-30` = **N**eutron flux **R**ecorder, Loop 30 - `PR-302` = **P**ressure **R**ecorder, Loop 302 - `PT-402` = **P**ressure **T**ransmitter, Loop 402 **Step 2 — First Letters (Measured Variable)** (1 mark): T (Temperature), P (Pressure), F (Flow), L (Level), A (Analysis), C (Conductivity), R (Radiation), N (Neutron flux), W (Weight), S (Speed), V (Vibration), E (Voltage), H (Hand/Manual). **Step 3 — Subsequent Letters (Function)** (1 mark): I (Indicate), C (Control), R (Record), T (Transmit), A (Alarm), S (Switch), V (Valve), E (Element/Sensor), Q (Integrate/Totalize), K (Control Station). **Step 4 — Instrument Bubble (Circle) Symbols** (2 marks): 1. **Plain Circle** = Instrument mounted on **Main Control Room (MCR) panel**. E.g., `PIC-601` (PZR pressure controller is in MCR). 2. **Circle with horizontal line through it** = Instrument mounted **in the field** (local). E.g., `PT-601` (pressure transmitter physically on the pipe). 3. **Circle with dashed lines** = **Computer/DCS-mounted** (logic executed in software). E.g., `LICA-301` implemented in Ovation DCS. 4. **Square box** = **PLC-mounted or safety-grade digital system**. E.g., RPS trip channel. 5. **Diamond** = **Smart instrument** with HART/Fieldbus communication. **Step 5 — Signal Line Conventions** (1 mark): - **Solid line** = Pneumatic (3–15 psig) - **Dashed line** = Electrical (4–20 mA, 1–5 V) - **Double-dashed** = Software link (Ethernet/Modbus) - **Triangular arrowhead** at receiving end indicates direction of signal. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** ⚠️ > 1. **DO NOT confuse** the ISA-5.1 first letter "**C**" — it means **Conductivity** as a variable, but as a *subsequent* letter, it means **Control**. Students lose marks by mixing these up. > 2. **DO NOT** use the term "PFD" interchangeably with "P&ID" — a PFD has no valve/instrument detail. > 3. **Always mention loop numbers** in the answer. A tag like "TE" alone is incomplete. > 4. **Nuclear-specific instruments** like `NR` (neutron recorder), `PR` (pressure recorder) often confuse students — note that both are valid, with first letter giving the variable and second letter the function. > 5. **For P&ID sketches** in KTU exams, always label **signal flow arrows, valve failure position (FO/FC)**, and the **MCR vs field** distinction using circle-with-line. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** (Read this the night before the KTU exam!) - [x] **P&ID Definition**: Detailed schematic of piping + equipment + instrumentation + control, governed by **ISA-5.1** and **IEEE 603**. - [x] **P&ID ≠ PFD**: P&ID includes *every* valve, instrument, and signal line; PFD is process overview only. - [x] **ISA-5.1 Tag Format**: `XY-nnn` where X = variable, Y = function, nnn = loop number. - [x] **First Letters to Memorize**: T, P, F, L, A, C, R, N, W, S, V. - [x] **Subsequent Letters to Memorize**: I, C, R, T, A, S, V, E, Q, K. - [x] **Circle Symbol**: MCR-mounted. **Slashed circle**: Field-mounted. **Dashed circle**: DCS/computer. - [x] **Solid signal line** = Pneumatic. **Dashed** = Electrical. **Triangular tip** = Signal direction. - [x] **PWR has TWO loops** (primary and secondary); BWR has **ONE loop** (boiling in core). - [x] **PWR P&ID key nodes**: RPV, SG, RCP, PZR, Surge Line, PORV, Safety Valve. - [x] **BWR P&ID key nodes**: Reactor Vessel, MSIVs (4 lines × 2 = 8 valves), SRVs (8 nos.), Recirculation Pumps. - [x] **ECCS = 4 subsystems**: Accumulators (passive), HPSI, LPCI, Core Spray. - [x] **GDC-21 / 10 CFR 50.46**: Single-failure criterion; PCT < 1204°C; oxidation < 17%. - [x] **Defense in Depth (5 levels)**: Conservative design → Operational control → ESF → Accident management → Emergency response. - [x] **Pressurizer set pressure** = **1.10 × design pressure** (ASME Section XI). - [x] **MSIV closure time**: 3–5 seconds on isolation signal. - [x] **Orifice flow equation**: $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\rho\Delta P / (1-\beta^4)}$. - [x] **Nuclear safety systems use ON/OFF trip logic**, NOT PID control. - [x] **Valve failure position** (FO/FC/FL) is **mandatory** on every nuclear P&ID. - [x] **Signal line tags**: 4–20 mA electrical; 3–15 psig pneumatic. - [x] **Indian regulatory body**: AERB Safety Guide D-2 governs NPP instrumentation. - [x] **International bodies**: IAEA (NS-G-1.3), IEEE 603, ASME, ISA, NRC (10 CFR 50). > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Strategy for KTU PEICT523 Module 1**: > 1. **Always start** a P&ID-related answer with a brief definition citing ISA-5.1. > 2. **Draw neat block diagrams** with all major equipment *labelled* and *tagged*. > 3. **Use tables** to list instrumentation (saves drawing time). > 4. **Mention specific tags** (e.g., `TE-101`, `PIC-601`) — this signals KTU examiners that you understand the standard. > 5. **Conclude every answer** with a safety/regulatory reference (e.g., "per IEEE 603 single-failure criterion" or "per AERB S-1.1").

topics.title: Nuclear Reactor Control Loops
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Reactor Control Loops

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Reactor Control Loop:** A *closed-loop, multi-variable feedback control system* designed to monitor, regulate, and safeguard the neutron population, thermal power output, and reactivity balance of a nuclear reactor by continuously adjusting *control rod positions*, *boron concentration*, and *coolant flow parameters* in response to deviations between measured process variables and their reference setpoints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the reactor control loop is treated as a **hierarchical, fail-safe control architecture** comprising four functional layers:

| Layer | Function | Typical Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer 1 — Measurement** | Neutron flux & process variable sensing | BF₃ counters, fission chambers, RTDs |
| **Layer 2 — Comparison** | Error computation (PV − SP) | Analog/digital controllers, RRS (Reactor Regulating System) |
| **Layer 3 — Correction** | Control law execution | PID controllers, rod-step logic |
| **Layer 4 — Actuation** | Physical movement of reactivity elements | CRDM (Control Rod Drive Mechanism), Boric Acid Pumps |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a domestic geyser (water heater) on a cold morning.**
> You set a temperature (the **setpoint**). A **thermometer** (the **sensor**) measures the actual water temperature. When the water is too cold, the **thermostat** (the **controller**) switches the heating coil **ON**. When the temperature overshoots, it switches **OFF**. The heater never runs at constant power — it constantly *listens, compares, and corrects*.

A nuclear reactor control loop is the **same idea, scaled to extreme physics**:

* The **"temperature"** being regulated is the **neutron flux** (which is proportional to fission rate and hence thermal power).
* The **"thermometer"** is a **neutron detector** (an ionization chamber that produces a tiny current proportional to neutron density).
* The **"thermostat"** is a **Reactor Regulating System (RRS)** running control laws.
* The **"heating coil switch"** is a **Control Rod Drive Mechanism (CRDM)** that moves neutron-absorbing rods in or out of the core.

The crucial difference: **a geyser delay of seconds is acceptable; a reactor delay of milliseconds can be catastrophic.** That is why nuclear control loops are designed with *triple-redundant instrumentation, diverse trip parameters, and Defense-in-Depth* principles.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reactor Physics Constants (Memorize for KTU ESE):**
> - **Delayed neutron fraction (β):** typically **0.0065** for U-235 (≈ 0.65 %)
> - **Effective delayed neutron fraction (β_eff):** 0.0070 (in-core weighted)
> - **Mean neutron generation time (Λ):** ≈ **10⁻⁴ s** (thermal reactor)
> - **One dollar of reactivity:** ρ = β (reactor on prompt-critical boundary)
> - **Reactivity insertion limit for scram:** typically **$1.00** (prompt-critical) — exceeded ⇒ automatic SCRAM
> - **Source range flux:** 10⁻¹ to 10⁴ counts/sec
> - **Intermediate range flux:** 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻³ A (ion chamber current)
> - **Power range flux:** 10⁻⁷ to 10⁻³ A (linear with % power)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of the Control Loop

A nuclear reactor is fundamentally a **non-linear, distributed-parameter, time-varying system** because:

1. **Non-linearity** — Reactivity (ρ) has an inverse logarithmic relationship with multiplication factor $k_{eff}$:
$$\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$$

2. **Distributed parameter** — Neutron flux and temperature vary spatially across the core (axial & radial profiles).

3. **Time-varying** — Fuel burn-up, fission product poisoning (Xenon-135, Samarium-149), and fuel temperature continually shift reactivity.

To make it tractable, KTU-board-level analysis uses the **Point Kinetics Model** (treats the core as a single spatially-averaged node).

### 2.1.1 The Four Operational Phases of Reactor Control

| Phase | Objective | Primary Control Element |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach to Criticality** | Gradually withdraw rods; monitor source-multiplied subcritical flux | Source Range Channels (BF₃) |
| **Criticality** | Establish self-sustaining chain reaction | Compensated Ion Chambers |
| **Power Ascension** | Ramp 10⁻⁸ → 100 % power in safe increments | Power Range Channels + CRDM |
| **Steady-State Operation** | Maintain T_avg, T_ref, ΔT within limits | Combined RRS + RCS |

---

## 2.2 Neutron Flux Instrumentation — The Three Decades of Measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Decade Coverage" Principle:** Because neutron flux during start-up spans ~10 decades (from ~1 n/cm²·s at subcritical to ~10¹⁴ n/cm²·s at full power), no single detector can measure this range. Hence, **three overlapping detector bands** are used.

| Range | Flux Span (n/cm²·s) | Detector Type | Output Signal | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Source Range (SR)** | 1 to 10⁵ | BF₃ proportional counter | Pulse rate (cps) | Cold start-up, subcritical multiplication |
| **Intermediate Range (IR)** | 10³ to 10¹⁰ | Compensated Ion Chamber (CIC) | DC current (10⁻¹¹ to 10⁻⁶ A) | Approach to criticality, overlap with SR & PR |
| **Power Range (PR)** | 10⁸ to 10¹⁴ | Uncompensated Ion Chamber (UIC) | DC current (10⁻⁷ to 10⁻³ A) | 1 % to 200 % power operation |

Each range *overlaps the adjacent range by at least one decade* to provide **continuous flux coverage** during start-up — a redundancy principle unique to nuclear I&C.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table consolidates every equation you will likely need for KTU ESE Module 1 problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$ | Reactivity from effective multiplication factor | dimensionless; $\rho > 0$ supercritical, $\rho < 0$ subcritical |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dn(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\,n(t) + \sum_i \lambda_i C_i(t) + S$ | Point Kinetics — Prompt + Delayed + Source | $n$ = neutron density (n/cm³) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{dC_i(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{\beta_i}{\Lambda}\,n(t) - \lambda_i C_i(t)$ | Delayed neutron precursor balance for group $i$ | $C_i$ = precursor concentration |
| 4 | $\Lambda \dfrac{dP}{dt} = (\rho - \beta)\,P + \sum_i \lambda_i C_i$ | Power form of point kinetics | $P$ = reactor power |
| 5 | $\rho_{total} = \rho_{rods} + \rho_{boron} + \rho_{fuelT} + \rho_{Xe} + \rho_{void}$ | Additive reactivity components (linearized) | Sum of all reactivity worths |
| 6 | $I_{detector} = q \cdot e \cdot \Phi \cdot V_{eff}$ | Ion chamber current (ideal) | $q$ = charge per event, $\Phi$ = flux |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{avg} = \dfrac{P}{2 m c_p}$ | Coolant temperature rise (PWR) | $P$ = thermal power, $m$ = mass flow |
| 8 | $T_{avg} = \dfrac{T_{hot} + T_{cold}}{2}$ | Average coolant temperature | Used as controlled variable |
| 9 | $W_{rod} = \int_{0}^{H} \rho(z)\,dz$ | Integral control rod worth | Function of insertion depth |
| 10 | $\tau_{period} = \dfrac{\Lambda}{\rho}$ | Stable reactor period (prompt only) | Valid only when $\rho \ll \beta$ |
| 11 | $P(t) = P_0\,e^{t/\tau}$ | Power excursion (exponential) | For positive reactivity insertion |
| 12 | $\text{Doppler Coefficient} = \dfrac{d\rho}{dT_{fuel}}$ | Fuel temperature feedback (always negative in LWR) | pcm/°C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative Temperature Coefficient:** In Light Water Reactors (LWR), the Doppler and moderator temperature coefficients are **inherently negative** — this is a *passive safety feature* that makes the reactor *self-stabilizing* without controller action (a key conceptual question for KTU).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR):** Uses control rods + boric acid in moderator for reactivity control.
* **Boiling Water Reactor (BWR):** Uses only control rods (no boron) plus void coefficient feedback.
* **PHWR (e.g., Indian PHWR, CANDU):** Uses heavy water moderator with separate light-water coolant; control via adjuster rods + poison addition.
* **Advanced Reactors (PBR, SFR):** Use passive feedback (doppler, thermal expansion) to minimize active control demand — relevant to next-gen KTU projects on Generation-IV reactors.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reactor Period from Point Kinetics (Simplified)

> **Objective:** Starting from the point kinetics equation, derive an expression for the *stable reactor period* when the reactor is just slightly supercritical and delayed neutrons dominate.

**Step 1 — Start with the single-group delayed-precursor form of point kinetics.**

The coupled equations governing neutron density $n(t)$ and precursor concentration $C(t)$ are:

$$
\frac{dn(t)}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\,n(t) + \lambda\,C(t)
$$

$$
\frac{dC(t)}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\,n(t) - \lambda\,C(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Assume an exponential solution of the form.**

Because the equations are linear with constant coefficients, we try:

$$
n(t) = n_0\,e^{\omega t}
$$

$$
C(t) = C_0\,e^{\omega t}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and substitute.**

$$
\frac{dn}{dt} = \omega\,n_0\,e^{\omega t} = \omega\,n
$$

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} = \omega\,C_0\,e^{\omega t} = \omega\,C
$$

Plug these back into the original two equations:

$$
\omega\,n = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\,n + \lambda\,C
$$

$$
\omega\,C = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\,n - \lambda\,C
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the second equation for $C$ in terms of $n$.**

$$
\omega\,C + \lambda\,C = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\,n
$$

$$
C\,(\omega + \lambda) = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\,n
$$

$$
C = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda(\omega + \lambda)}\,n
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute this expression for $C$ into the first equation.**

$$
\omega\,n = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\,n + \lambda \cdot \frac{\beta}{\Lambda(\omega + \lambda)}\,n
$$

Divide both sides by $n$ (since $n \neq 0$):

$$
\omega = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \frac{\lambda\beta}{\Lambda(\omega + \lambda)}
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply both sides by $\Lambda$ to simplify.**

$$
\Lambda\omega = (\rho - \beta) + \frac{\lambda\beta}{\omega + \lambda}
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by $(\omega + \lambda)$.**

$$
\Lambda\omega(\omega + \lambda) = (\rho - \beta)(\omega + \lambda) + \lambda\beta
$$

Expand the left-hand side:

$$
\Lambda\omega^2 + \Lambda\omega\lambda = (\rho - \beta)\omega + (\rho - \beta)\lambda + \lambda\beta
$$

**Step 8 — Recognize that $(\rho - \beta)\lambda + \lambda\beta = \rho\lambda$.**

$$
\Lambda\omega^2 + \Lambda\omega\lambda = (\rho - \beta)\omega + \rho\lambda
$$

**Step 9 — Rearrange into the standard *Inhour Equation*.**

$$
\Lambda\omega^2 + [\Lambda\lambda - (\rho - \beta)]\omega - \rho\lambda = 0
$$

This is the **Inhour Equation**, which yields the **reactor period** $T = \dfrac{1}{\omega}$.

**Step 10 — Special case: just supercritical with $\rho \ll \beta$.**

When the reactor is just critical ($\rho$ is small compared to $\beta$), the prompt term is negligible and the dominant root is:

$$
\omega \approx \frac{\lambda\rho}{\beta - \rho}
$$

For $\rho \ll \beta$:

$$
\omega \approx \frac{\lambda\rho}{\beta}
$$

Therefore the **stable reactor period** is:

$$
T = \frac{\beta}{\lambda\rho}
$$

For a thermal reactor with $\beta = 0.0065$, $\lambda = 0.08\,s^{-1}$, and $\rho = 100\,\text{pcm} = 0.001$:

$$
T = \frac{0.0065}{0.08 \times 0.001} = \frac{0.0065}{0.00008} = 81.25\,\text{seconds}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Detector Selection vs. Operating Condition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a high-frequency KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question type.**

| Operating Condition | Reactor State ($k_{eff}$) | Recommended Detector | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core fully flooded, fresh fuel, shutdown | $k_{eff} \approx 0.95$ | BF₃ proportional counter (Source Range) | Lowest measurable flux (pulse counting mode) |
| Approach to first criticality | $k_{eff}$ approaching 1.0 | Compensated Ion Chamber (IR) | Linear current output, gamma-compensated |
| 1 % to 100 % power | $k_{eff} = 1.00$ | Uncompensated Ion Chamber (Power Range) | Wide linear range, accurate % power |
| Power above 100 % (over-power trip testing) | $k_{eff} > 1.00$ (transient) | Power Range + RPS trip channels | Wide-range; pre-calibrated trip setpoints |
| Post-SCRAM monitoring | $k_{eff}$ decaying | All three ranges (with overlap verification) | Confirms subcritical multiplication has decayed |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Reactivity Coefficients (Quick Comparison)

| Coefficient | Sign (LWR) | Magnitude (typical) | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Doppler (Fuel Temp)** | Negative | −2 to −5 pcm/°C | Inherent safety — power rise → fuel heats → negative ρ → power falls |
| **Moderator Temp** | Negative (PWR) | −50 to −100 pcm/°C | Strong self-regulation in PWR |
| **Void Coefficient** | Negative (PWR), Positive (BWR) | ±200 pcm/%void | Determines BWR transient behaviour |
| **Boron Concentration** | Negative (PWR) | −10 pcm/ppm | Used for long-term reactivity compensation (burn-up) |
| **Xenon-135** | Time-varying negative | Up to −3000 pcm peak | Causes Xenon oscillations after power changes |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Three-Loop Reactor Control Architecture (PWR)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **hierarchical, three-loop control architecture** of a typical Pressurized Water Reactor — the canonical exam-friendly figure for KTU Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSING["LAYER 1 — SENSING"]
        DET_SR["SR Detector: BF3 Counter"]
        DET_IR["IR Detector: Compensated Ion Chamber"]
        DET_PR["PR Detector: Uncompensated Ion Chamber"]
        RTD_HOT["RTD: Hot Leg Temperature"]
        RTD_COLD["RTD: Cold Leg Temperature"]
        PRZ_TX["Pressurizer Pressure Transmitter"]
    end

    subgraph COMPARISON["LAYER 2 — COMPARISON AND CONTROL LAW"]
        RRS["Reactor Regulating System RRS: PID Controller"]
        TRIP_LOGIC["RPS Trip Logic: 2 out of 4 Voting"]
    end

    subgraph ACTUATION["LAYER 3 — ACTUATION"]
        CRDM["Control Rod Drive Mechanism CRDM"]
        BORON_PUMP["Boric Acid Dosing Pump"]
        PRZ_HEATER["Pressurizer Heater and Spray Valve"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["LAYER 4 — NUCLEAR PROCESS"]
        CORE["Reactor Core: Neutron Flux and Fuel Temp"]
        RCS_LOOP["Primary Coolant Loop: T_hot and T_cold"]
        PRZ["Pressurizer"]
    end

    DET_SR --> RRS
    DET_IR --> RRS
    DET_PR --> RRS
    RTD_HOT --> RRS
    RTD_COLD --> RRS
    PRZ_TX --> RRS

    RRS --> CRDM
    RRS --> BORON_PUMP
    RRS --> PRZ_HEATER

    CRDM --> CORE
    BORON_PUMP --> CORE
    PRZ_HEATER --> PRZ

    CORE -->|Reactor Feedback| RCS_LOOP
    RCS_LOOP -->|Neutron and Thermal Feedback| CORE
    PRZ --> RCS_LOOP

    CORE -.->|Neutron Flux| DET_SR
    CORE -.->|Neutron Flux| DET_IR
    CORE -.->|Neutron Flux| DET_PR
    RCS_LOOP -.->|T_hot| RTD_HOT
    RCS_LOOP -.->|T_cold| RTD_COLD
    PRZ -.->|Pressure| PRZ_TX

    RRS --> TRIP_LOGIC
    TRIP_LOGIC -.->|SCRAM Signal| CRDM
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Reactor Period-to-SCRAM Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Start: Rod Withdrawal Initiated"] --> B["Neutron Flux Rises"]
    B --> C["Reactor Period Computed: tau equals n divided by dn by dt"]
    C --> D{"Is tau less than 20 seconds?"}
    D -->|"YES — Period too short"| E["RPS Issues SCRAM: Full Rod Insertion"]
    D -->|"NO — Period acceptable"| F["Continue Power Ascension"]
    F --> G{"Reached Target Power?"}
    G -->|"YES"| H["Engage T avg Control Loop"]
    G -->|"NO"| B
    E --> I["Reactor Subcritical: All Rods Inserted"]
    H --> J["Steady State Operation"]
```

---

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Transfer Function View (Conceptual)

For KTU analytical problems, the reactor control loop can be represented as a **single-input-single-output (SISO) feedback structure** in the Laplace domain:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R["Setpoint: Desired Power"] --> SUM["Summing Junction"]
    SUM -->|"error = R minus Y"| C["Controller: PID Gc of s"]
    C --> A["CRDM Actuator: Ga of s"]
    A --> P["Reactor Plant: Gp of s equals K over tau of s plus 1"]
    P --> Y["Output: Measured Neutron Flux"]
    Y -->|"Feedback"| SUM
```

**Engineering Insight:** The reactor plant transfer function is approximated as a **first-order lag** for slow power manoeuvres:

$$
G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p\,s + 1}
$$

Where:
* $K_p$ = **reactor power gain** (ΔP per Δρ) — typically large for small ρ.
* $\tau_p$ = **reactor time constant** — for small positive reactivity steps, $\tau_p \approx \dfrac{\beta}{\lambda\rho}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU Valuation):** When asked to *“draw the reactor control loop block diagram,”* you **must** label:
> (i) **Setpoint**, (ii) **Comparator**, (iii) **Controller**, (iv) **Actuator (CRDM)**, (v) **Plant (Reactor Core)**, and (vi) **Feedback Sensor (Neutron Detector)**.
> Missing any one element ⇒ **2-mark deduction** per KTU board norms.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Source Range, Intermediate Range, and Power Range neutron detectors used in reactor instrumentation. Mention one detector type for each range.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Range | Detector Type | Output | Decade |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Source Range** | BF₃ proportional counter (pulse mode) | Counts/sec | 1 to 10⁵ n/cm²·s |
| **Intermediate Range** | Compensated Ion Chamber (CIC) | DC current (10⁻¹¹ to 10⁻⁶ A) | 10³ to 10¹⁰ n/cm²·s |
| **Power Range** | Uncompensated Ion Chamber (UIC) | DC current (10⁻⁷ to 10⁻³ A) | 10⁸ to 10¹⁴ n/cm²·s |

> **Examiner's Note:** Each correct detector with its functional range ⇒ **1 mark**. Full marks only if overlap decades are mentioned.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define reactivity. What is the significance of "One Dollar" and "One Cent" of reactivity?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Reactivity is defined as the fractional change in the effective multiplication factor from unity:

$$
\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}
$$

* **One Dollar of reactivity** = $\rho = \beta_{eff}$ (the delayed neutron fraction, ≈ 0.0070). It represents the reactivity insertion that brings the reactor to **prompt criticality**.
* **One Cent of reactivity** = $\rho = \dfrac{\beta_{eff}}{100}$ (≈ 7 pcm). It is used as a finer unit because reactivity insertions are typically much smaller than one dollar in practical operations.

**Significance:** Operators speak in "dollars" and "cents" rather than absolute reactivity to express *how close the reactor is to prompt criticality* — a critical safety metric.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop control of a nuclear reactor. Identify the role of the Reactor Regulating System (RRS), Control Rod Drive Mechanism (CRDM), and neutron flux detector in maintaining constant reactor power.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Inhour Equation starting from the point kinetics equations with one group of delayed neutrons. State clearly the assumptions made.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (drawn in Section 4.3 above) — 2 Marks for the diagram.**

The reactor control loop is a **closed-loop negative feedback system** with the following elements (1 mark each for identification and 2 marks for explanation):

1. **Setpoint (Reference):** Desired power level, entered by the operator as % power.
2. **Neutron Flux Detector (Sensor):** A power-range ion chamber produces a current proportional to neutron flux (and hence power).
3. **Comparator:** Subtracts measured flux from setpoint to produce error $e(t) = R(t) - Y(t)$.
4. **Controller (RRS):** Implements a PID control law. Output is a *control rod step command*.
5. **Actuator (CRDM):** A precision motor/gravitational-drop mechanism moves control rods in (insert negative reactivity) or out (withdraw negative reactivity).
6. **Plant (Reactor Core):** Responds to reactivity insertion with a power change. Inherent negative temperature coefficient (Doppler + moderator) provides passive stabilization.
7. **Feedback Path:** The same ion chamber current is fed back to the comparator, closing the loop.

**Working:** When load demand falls, primary coolant $T_{avg}$ drops; the RRS commands rods to step *inward* slightly, reducing neutron flux and hence power. Conversely, when load rises, rods step *outward* to add positive reactivity.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the point kinetics equations with one delayed group** (1 mark):

$$
\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda}\,n + \lambda\,C
$$

$$
\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda}\,n - \lambda\,C
$$

**Step 2 — Assume exponential solution** $n = n_0 e^{\omega t}$, $C = C_0 e^{\omega t}$ (1 mark).

**Step 3 — Substitute and derive** $C = \dfrac{\beta}{\Lambda(\omega + \lambda)}\,n$ (1 mark).

**Step 4 — Substitute into first equation** and rearrange (2 marks):

$$
\Lambda\omega^2 + (\Lambda\lambda - \rho + \beta)\omega - \rho\lambda = 0
$$

**Step 5 — This is the Inhour Equation** (1 mark).

**Step 6 — State assumptions** (1 mark):
* Point (lumped) reactor model — no spatial dependence.
* One group of delayed neutrons (single $\lambda$, single $\beta$).
* Constant reactivity insertion.
* No external neutron source.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Supplementary Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three decades of neutron flux measurement in a nuclear reactor. Why is overlap between ranges necessary?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A thermal reactor has $\beta_{eff} = 0.007$, $\lambda = 0.08\,s^{-1}$, and $\Lambda = 10^{-4}\,s$. If a step reactivity of +100 pcm is inserted, calculate (i) the reactor period using the prompt-only approximation, and (ii) the stable period using the delayed-neutron dominated formula.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Three Decades (6 marks — 2 per range):**

1. **Source Range (10⁰–10⁵ n/cm²·s):** Uses a **BF₃ proportional counter** operating in pulse mode. Each neutron interaction in BF₃ gas produces a discrete pulse; the count rate is proportional to flux. Used during cold shutdown, refuelling, and initial start-up.

2. **Intermediate Range (10³–10¹⁰ n/cm²·s):** Uses a **Compensated Ion Chamber (CIC)**. The chamber has two volumes — one sensitive to both gamma and neutrons, one to gamma only; the difference gives pure neutron current. Covers approach to first criticality.

3. **Power Range (10⁸–10¹⁴ n/cm²·s):** Uses **Uncompensated Ion Chambers** with very large volumes. Output current (10⁻⁷ to 10⁻³ A) is linearly proportional to % power. Used for all operations from 1 % to 200 % power.

**Why Overlap is Necessary (1 mark):**

During start-up, flux increases smoothly through 10 decades. If one detector's range ends *exactly* where the next begins, any drift or failure at the boundary would cause a loss of monitoring — a violation of the **Defense-in-Depth** principle. Hence each range overlaps the next by **at least one decade** to ensure continuous, redundant coverage during transients.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**

* $\beta_{eff} = 0.007$
* $\lambda = 0.08\,s^{-1}$
* $\Lambda = 10^{-4}\,s$
* $\rho = +100\,\text{pcm} = 0.001$

**(i) Prompt-only period (3 marks):**

$$
\tau_{prompt} = \frac{\Lambda}{\rho}
$$

$$
\tau_{prompt} = \frac{10^{-4}}{0.001} = 0.1\,\text{seconds}
$$

**Interpretation:** If only prompt neutrons mattered, power would double roughly every 0.1 s — **catastrophically fast**. This is *why* delayed neutrons are essential: they slow the response by a factor of ~1000, making the reactor controllable.

**(ii) Delayed-dominated period (4 marks):**

$$
\tau_{stable} = \frac{\beta}{\lambda\rho}
$$

$$
\tau_{stable} = \frac{0.007}{0.08 \times 0.001}
$$

$$
\tau_{stable} = \frac{0.007}{0.00008} = 87.5\,\text{seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:** The same reactivity insertion gives a *prompt period of 0.1 s* but a *stable observable period of 87.5 s* — a factor of **875× slower** thanks to delayed neutrons. This delayed response is what allows human operators (and automatic controllers) to manage the reactor safely.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the feedback path** in block diagrams ⇒ **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing reactivity ($\rho$) with multiplication factor ($k$)** — they are NOT the same. $\rho$ is bounded; $k$ is not.
> 3. **Not stating units** of neutron flux (n/cm²·s) and ion chamber current (A or pA).
> 4. **Using single-group approximation without justification** — examiners expect you to mention "single delayed group assumption" when deriving the Inhour Equation.
> 5. **Mixing up Source Range and Power Range detectors** — BF₃ is pulse-mode; Power range is DC current mode.
> 6. **Ignoring the overlap-by-one-decade principle** when explaining range transitions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 1 — Nuclear Reactor Control Loops**

- [ ] Reactor control loop = **closed-loop SISO feedback system** with setpoint, comparator, PID controller, CRDM, reactor core, neutron detector.
- [ ] **Three flux decades:** Source (BF₃ pulses), Intermediate (CIC), Power (UIC) — each overlapping by ≥ 1 decade.
- [ ] **Reactivity** $\rho = \dfrac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$; **One Dollar** = $\beta_{eff}$; **One Cent** = $\beta_{eff}/100$.
- [ ] **Point kinetics equations** (one-group form) ⇒ derive **Inhour Equation**: $\Lambda\omega^2 + (\Lambda\lambda - \rho + \beta)\omega - \rho\lambda = 0$.
- [ ] **Stable period (delayed-dominated):** $\tau \approx \dfrac{\beta}{\lambda\rho}$ — this is the *operationally relevant* period.
- [ ] **Prompt-only period:** $\tau_p = \dfrac{\Lambda}{\rho}$ — relevant only for prompt-critical accidents.
- [ ] **Inherent safety:** LWR has **negative Doppler + negative moderator temperature coefficients** — passive self-regulation.
- [ ] **Reactor Regulating System (RRS)** is the digital/analog PID controller; **CRDM** is the actuator; **RPS** is the independent protection layer.
- [ ] **Defense-in-Depth:** Triple-redundant channels with 2-out-of-4 voting logic for SCRAM decision.
- [ ] **Reactivity components** are additive (linearized): $\rho_{total} = \rho_{rod} + \rho_{boron} + \rho_{fuelT} + \rho_{Xe} + \rho_{void}$.
- [ ] **Ion chamber current** is the primary measurement signal for control; current $\propto$ neutron flux.
- [ ] **Plant transfer function (slow dynamics):** First-order lag $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Nuclear Reactor Control Loops**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control and Safety Instrumentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control and Safety Instrumentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Control & Safety Instrumentation?

**Control Instrumentation** in a power plant refers to the integrated network of sensors, controllers, actuators, and signal-transmission devices that continuously regulate process variables (pressure, temperature, flow, level) to keep the plant operating at its specified design point, maximizing efficiency while minimizing fuel consumption and emissions.

**Safety Instrumentation** is a distinct, physically and functionally separate layer of hardware and software designed to bring the plant to a *safe state* when predetermined hazardous conditions are detected. It does **not** optimize the process — it protects **people, plant equipment, and the environment**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exact Syllabus Wording):**
> *"Control and safety instrumentation in a power plant constitutes the hierarchical assembly of Distributed Control System (DCS), Safety Instrumented System (SIS), Burner Management System (BMS), Emergency Shutdown (ESD), Fire & Gas (F&G) detection, and alarm management subsystems that collectively ensure safe, efficient, and reliable power generation."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Brain" Model

Imagine driving a modern car on a highway:

| Brain | Function | Plant Equivalent |
|-------|----------|------------------|
| **Cruise Control** | Maintains set speed by adjusting throttle | **Basic Control Loops (DCS)** |
| **Adaptive Cruise + Radar** | Pre-empts brake based on traffic ahead | **Advanced Control (Cascade / Model Predictive)** |
| **Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB)** | Slam brakes when collision imminent | **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** |
| **Airbag Deployment** | Last-resort protection after crash | **Emergency Shutdown (ESD)** |

The driver normally controls the car. The AEB and airbags are *independent* systems — they have their own sensors, wiring, and logic. **They do not share components with cruise control**, because if cruise control fails and causes the crash, the safety system must still function. This **independence (segregation)** is the foundational principle of plant safety design.

## 1.3 Why Both Layers Are Mandatory in a Power Plant

A 500 MW thermal power station operates with:
- **Steam pressure**: ~250 bar (super-critical)
- **Furnace temperature**: ~1500 °C
- **Fuel (coal/natural gas)**: Continuously combusted

If the boiler drum runs dry, the tubes melt within **seconds**. If fuel keeps flowing without ignition, it explodes. Therefore, two complementary layers are mandatory:

1. **Continuous Control Layer** — keeps the plant on its *operating envelope* (economic, efficient operation).
2. **Safety Protection Layer** — forces the plant into a *safe state* if the operating envelope is breached (no economic optimization, only protection).

## 1.4 Hierarchical Architecture (Per ISA-95 / IEC 61511)

> [!NOTE]
> **Levels of a Power Plant I&C Architecture:**
> - **Level 0**: Field sensors & actuators (RTDs, thermocouples, transmitters, control valves)
> - **Level 1**: Local control stations & PLCs (burner control, sub-loop control)
> - **Level 2**: Supervisory control (HMI, alarm management, operator interface)
> - **Level 3**: Plant-wide coordination (DCS, historian, optimization)
> - **Level 4**: Enterprise (ERP, plant management)
>
> **Safety systems (SIS, BMS, ESD, F&G)** sit **at Level 1**, but are *isolated* from the DCS both physically (separate I/O cards, separate power supplies) and logically (independent logic solvers).

## 1.5 Key Subsystems Covered in This Module

| Subsystem | Full Form | Primary Purpose |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| DCS | Distributed Control System | Continuous regulatory & supervisory control |
| BMS | Burner Management System | Safe light-off, flame stability, furnace purge |
| SIS | Safety Instrumented System | Risk reduction to acceptable level (SIL-rated) |
| ESD | Emergency Shutdown System | Fast, total plant isolation in emergencies |
| F&G | Fire & Gas Detection | Early detection of fire / toxic-gas release |
| AMS | Alarm Management System | Rationalization, prioritization of operator alarms |

These subsystems form the *backbone* of every modern combined-cycle, super-critical, or nuclear power plant.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Control Loop

A closed-loop control system regulates a **Process Variable (PV)** by comparing it to a **Setpoint (SP)** and driving the **Manipulated Variable (MV)** through a **Controller**.

### 2.1.1 Block Diagram (Standard Single-Loop)

```
       Setpoint (SP)
            │
            ▼  + ───── + ◄─── ERROR (e)
            │           ▲
            │           │
            │      ┌────┴────┐
            │      │ SUMMING│
            │      │  JUNCTION
            │      └────┬────┘
            │           │
            │           ▼
            │      ┌──────────┐
            │      │CONTROLLER│  ← Controller Output (CO)
            │      │   (PID)   │
            │      └────┬─────┘
            │           │
            │           ▼
            │      ┌──────────┐
            │      │ FINAL    │  ← Manipulated Variable (MV)
            │      │CONTROL   │
            │      │ ELEMENT  │
            │      └────┬─────┘
            │           │
            │           ▼
            │      ┌──────────┐
            │      │ PROCESS  │ ──────► Process Variable (PV)
            │      └────┬─────┘
            │           │
            └───────────┘
                  Feedback
```

### 2.1.2 Generic Feedback Loop Algebra

In the Laplace (s-) domain, with **negative feedback**:

$$\text{Controller Output: } CO(s) = G_c(s) \cdot E(s)$$

$$\text{Error Signal: } E(s) = SP(s) - PV(s)$$

$$\text{Process Variable: } PV(s) = G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot CO(s)$$

$$PV(s) = G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_c(s) \cdot \left[ SP(s) - PV(s) \right]$$

Solving for the **Closed-Loop Transfer Function**:

$$\frac{PV(s)}{SP(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s) \cdot H(s)}$$

Where:
- $G_c(s)$ = Controller transfer function
- $G_v(s)$ = Final control element (valve) transfer function
- $G_p(s)$ = Process transfer function
- $H(s)$ = Measurement (transmitter) transfer function (usually = 1 for ideal transmitter)

> [!NOTE]
> **For Unity Feedback ($H(s) = 1$):**
>
> $$\frac{PV(s)}{SP(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_p(s)}$$

### 2.1.3 PID Controller Law (Continuous Form)

A Proportional-Integral-Derivative controller is the workhorse of power plant control:

$$CO(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d\frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

In Laplace domain:

$$G_c(s) = K_p \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s \right]$$

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect | Typical Use in Power Plant |
|-----------|--------|--------|-----------------------------|
| Proportional Gain | $K_p$ | Reduces rise time, increases overshoot | Drum level, pressure trim |
| Integral Time | $T_i$ (s) | Eliminates steady-state error | Boiler feedwater flow |
| Derivative Time | $T_d$ (s) | Dampens oscillation | Steam temperature, furnace pressure |

## 2.2 Types of Control Strategies in Power Plants

### 2.2.1 Feedback Control (Single-Loop)
The PV is measured and fed back. *Most common.* Cannot compensate for disturbances before they affect PV.

### 2.2.2 Feedforward Control
Measures the **disturbance** directly and takes corrective action before PV deviates.

$$\text{Feedforward Controller: } G_{ff}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$$

Where $G_d(s)$ is the disturbance transfer function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Application:** Boiler feedwater control — measure steam flow (disturbance) and adjust feedwater flow *before* drum level drops. Often combined with feedback in a *combined FF+FB* strategy.

### 2.2.3 Cascade Control
Two loops in series: the **outer (master/slave)** controller sets the setpoint of the **inner (secondary)** controller.

```
              ┌──────────── Outer Loop ────────────┐
              │                                       │
   SP ─►(+)──┘                                       │
       ▲  −                                          │
       │                                             │
       │   ┌──── Master Controller ────┐            │
       │   │  e.g., Steam Temp. SP     │            │
       │   └─────────────┬─────────────┘            │
       │                 ▼  Secondary SP            │
       │           (+)─┘                            │
       │           ▲  −                             │
       │           │                                │
       │     ┌─────┴──────── Slave Controller ─────┐│
       │     │ (e.g., Fuel Valve Position)        ││
       │     └──────────────┬─────────────────────┘│
       │                    ▼                       │
       │              ┌──────────┐                 │
       │              │  Process │                 │
       │              └────┬─────┘                 │
       │                   │                       │
       └──────── Secondary PV (Inner)              │
                         │                         │
                         └──── Outer PV (Temp.)────┘
```

**Typical KTU Example:** Steam temperature control
- **Outer**: Temperature controller → sets setpoint of attemperator water flow
- **Inner**: Water flow controller → positions attemperator valve

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade control improves:** (1) Rejection of inner-loop disturbances, (2) Reduces phase lag, (3) Allows higher gain on outer loop.

### 2.2.4 Ratio Control
Holds a fixed ratio between two flows. Used in **fuel-to-air ratio** combustion control:

$$\frac{F_{\text{fuel}}}{F_{\text{air}}} = K_{\text{stoich}}$$

### 2.2.5 Split-Range Control
One controller output drives **multiple valves** in different operating ranges. Example: A level controller may open the *drain valve* at 0–50% output and the *inlet valve* at 50–100% output.

## 2.3 Safety Instrumented System (SIS) — Theory

### 2.3.1 Definition (IEC 61511)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> *"A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) is an implementation of one or more Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) used to achieve or maintain a safe state for the process with respect to a specific hazardous event."*

### 2.3.2 Safety Integrity Level (SIL)

IEC 61508/61511 define **four SILs** based on the **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** for low-demand mode (or **PFH** for high-demand/continuous mode):

| SIL | Low-Demand PFDavg | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF = 1/PFD) | Continuous PFH (per hour) |
|-----|------------------|------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ | 10 to 100 | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 100 to 1000 | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 1000 to 10000 | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 10000 to 100000 | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Power plant boiler trips are typically **SIL 2 or SIL 3**. **SIL 4 is reserved for nuclear reactors** and is the highest safety level. Do NOT confuse SIL with API-RP-754 categories (which classify consequences, not risk reduction).

### 2.3.3 The Safety Lifecycle (IEC 61511)

| Phase | Activity |
|-------|----------|
| 1 | Process & Hazard Analysis (HAZOP) |
| 2 | Allocation of safety functions to protection layers |
| 3 | SIS design (sensor → logic solver → final element) |
| 4 | Installation & commissioning |
| 5 | Validation (proof testing) |
| 6 | Operation & maintenance |
| 7 | Modification |
| 8 | Decommissioning |

### 2.3.4 Architecture Constraint — Redundancy

To achieve higher SIL with simple components, redundancy is added:

| Architecture | Voting | Achievable SIL (with low λ) |
|--------------|--------|------------------------------|
| 1oo1 (1-out-of-1) | Single | Up to SIL 2 |
| 1oo2 (1-out-of-2) | Either trips | Up to SIL 3 |
| 2oo2 (2-out-of-2) | Both must trip | Up to SIL 3 |
| 2oo3 (2-out-of-3) | Any 2 of 3 trip | Up to SIL 3 (highest availability) |

> [!NOTE]
> **2oo3 Voting:** Used in critical burner trips and reactor shutdowns. Tolerant of *one* failed sensor without spurious trip, but trips on *any two* confirming the hazard.

### 2.3.5 Average Probability of Failure on Demand (Simplified)

For a 1oo1 architecture with dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$ and proof test interval $T_i$:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\lambda_D \cdot T_i$$

For a 1oo2 architecture:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo2}} = \frac{1}{3}\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_i^2$$

### 2.3.6 Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}}}$$

The RRF is the factor by which the SIS reduces the frequency of the hazardous event.

## 2.4 Burner Management System (BMS)

The BMS is a *special-purpose SIS* dedicated to **fuel firing safety** in boilers.

### 2.4.1 NFPA 85 Phases

| Phase | Description | Time Allowed |
|-------|-------------|--------------|
| **Purge** | Furnace swept with air to remove combustible gases | 5 furnace volumes @ ≥25% MFC |
| **Light-off** | Pilot ignited, then main burner | Sequence-controlled |
| **Run** | Normal operation; flame continuously monitored | Continuous |
| **Shutdown** | Fuel trip; natural cool-down | Operator/normally closed valves |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permissives** (pre-conditions that must be satisfied before fuel can be admitted):
> - Furnace purge complete (≥ 5 min with ≥ 25% of full air flow)
> - All dampers in correct position
> - No flame detected in furnace
> - No low air flow
> - All dampers proven open/closed via limit switches
> - Master fuel trip not active

### 2.4.2 Flame Detection Methods

| Method | Sensor | Application |
|--------|--------|-------------|
| UV (Ultraviolet) | UV phototube | Gas, light oil flames |
| IR (Infrared) | IR photodiode / PbS cell | Coal, heavy oil flames |
| Visible-light | Photocell | General flames |
| Flame rod (Rectification) | Ionization current | Gas pilots |

## 2.5 Emergency Shutdown (ESD) System

The ESD provides *fast, total isolation* of hazardous inventories:

- **ESD-0 (Unit trip)**: Total unit shutdown — all fuel valves close, all circulation pumps trip, all block valves close.
- **ESD-1 (Process trip)**: Isolates a specific section (e.g., fuel gas manifold).
- **ESD-2 (Local trip)**: Single equipment protection (e.g., turbine lube-oil trip).

### 2.5.1 ESD Design Principles
1. **Fail-safe**: Loss of air, signal, or power → trip
2. **De-energize-to-trip**: Trip by removing power to actuator (solenoid vent)
3. **Independent of DCS**: Separate logic solver, separate I/O
4. **Manual reset only**: After trip, operator must investigate and reset
5. **First-out indication**: Identify which signal initiated the trip

## 2.6 Fire & Gas (F&G) Detection

F&G systems form an independent layer with three functions:
1. **Detect** fire or gas release (detectors)
2. **Alarm** operators and initiate evacuation
3. **Actuate** — close fire dampers, start deluge, isolate fuel

Common detectors:
- **Smoke**: Photoelectric, ionization
- **Heat**: Fixed temperature, rate-of-rise
- **Flame**: IR/UV (similar to BMS)
- **Gas**: Catalytic bead, IR point, ultrasonic
- **Toxic gas**: Electrochemical cells

## 2.7 Alarm Management (ISA 18.2)

The Alarm Management Lifecycle:
1. **Philosophy** — alarm definition, priority levels
2. **Identification** — what alarms are needed
3. **Rationalization** — remove nuisance, set correct priority
4. **Detail Design** — HMI, deadbands, on/off delays
5. **Implementation**
6. **Operation** — metrics: alarms/day/operator should be ~150, peaks <300/h
7. **Maintenance**
8. **Change Management & Audit**

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{PV(s)}{SP(s)} = \dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p H}$ | Closed-loop TF (negative feedback) |
| 2 | $G_c(s) = K_p\!\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s} + T_d s\right]$ | PID controller |
| 3 | $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$ | Ideal feedforward |
| 4 | $\text{PFD}_{avg} = \tfrac{1}{2}\lambda_D T_i$ | 1oo1 avg prob. failure on demand |
| 5 | $\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} = \tfrac{1}{3}\lambda_D^2 T_i^2$ | 1oo2 avg PFD |
| 6 | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}_{avg}}$ | Risk Reduction Factor |
| 7 | $\text{Furnace purge} \geq 5 \text{ volumes @ } \geq 25\% \text{ MFC}$ | NFPA 85 minimum purge |
| 8 | $\dfrac{F_{\text{fuel}}}{F_{\text{air}}} = K_{\text{stoich}}$ | Combustion ratio control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to Remember:**
> **1 furnace volume purge** = furnace volume (m³) / total air flow (m³/min) → purge time in minutes. KTU expects you to compute this for typical furnace volumes.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boiler Drum Level Control**: 3-element (drum level, steam flow, feedwater flow) — a classic cascade+feedforward combo in every utility boiler.
- **Turbine Governor**: Droop + Isochronous speed control, $4\%$ droop standard.
- **Burner Trip**: SIL-3 2oo3 voting of flame scanners + air flow proof switches.
- **Nuclear Plant**: Reactor Protection System (RPS) is **SIL 4**, the only commercial application.
- **Combined Cycle**: Gas turbine BMS and HRSG BMS are independent; coordinated through plant DCS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop Transfer Function for Unity-Feedback PID-Controlled Process

**Scenario (from KTU 2019 Scheme style):**
A steam temperature loop has:
- Process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{(T_1 s + 1)(T_2 s + 1)} = \dfrac{2}{(5s+1)(3s+1)}$
- Final element: $G_v(s) = K_v = 1$
- Transmitter: $H(s) = 1$
- Controller: PI, $G_c(s) = K_p\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_i s}\right) = 0.8\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{10s}\right)$

**Required:** Closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{PV(s)}{SP(s)}$.

### Step 1 — Express $G_c(s)$ as a single fraction

$$G_c(s) = 0.8 \cdot \frac{10s + 1}{10s} = \frac{0.8(10s+1)}{10s} = \frac{8s + 0.8}{10s}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator for clarity:

$$G_c(s) = \frac{0.8(10s+1)}{10s} = \frac{8s + 0.8}{10s}$$

### Step 2 — Forward Path Product $L(s)$

$$L(s) = G_c(s)\cdot G_v(s)\cdot G_p(s) = \frac{8s + 0.8}{10s} \cdot 1 \cdot \frac{2}{(5s+1)(3s+1)}$$

$$L(s) = \frac{2(8s + 0.8)}{10s(5s+1)(3s+1)} = \frac{16s + 1.6}{10s(5s+1)(3s+1)}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Closed-Loop Formula (Unity Feedback)

$$\frac{PV(s)}{SP(s)} = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{16s + 1.6}{10s(5s+1)(3s+1)}}{1 + \dfrac{16s + 1.6}{10s(5s+1)(3s+1)}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $10s(5s+1)(3s+1)$:

$$\frac{PV(s)}{SP(s)} = \frac{16s + 1.6}{10s(5s+1)(3s+1) + (16s + 1.6)}$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Denominator

$$10s(5s+1)(3s+1) = 10s(15s^2 + 8s + 1) = 150s^3 + 80s^2 + 10s$$

Adding $16s + 1.6$:

$$\text{Denominator} = 150s^3 + 80s^2 + 10s + 16s + 1.6 = 150s^3 + 80s^2 + 26s + 1.6$$

### Step 5 — Final Closed-Loop Transfer Function

$$\frac{PV(s)}{SP(s)} = \frac{16s + 1.6}{150s^3 + 80s^2 + 26s + 1.6}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - **Numerator order = 1** (1 zero) — from PI controller zero.
> - **Denominator order = 3** (3 poles) — two from process + one from PI integrator.
> - System is **Type 1** (one integrator in forward path) → **zero steady-state error to a step input**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PFDavg of 1oo2 Architecture

**Problem:** A pressure transmitter has $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour, proof-test interval $T_i = 8760$ h (1 year). Find PFDavg and SIL rating for a 1oo2 system.

### Step 1 — Apply Formula

$$\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{1}{3}\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_i^2$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{1}{3}\,(5\times 10^{-6})^2\,(8760)^2$$

### Step 3 — Compute Each Factor Separately

$$\lambda_D^2 = (5\times 10^{-6})^2 = 25\times 10^{-12} = 2.5\times 10^{-11}$$

$$T_i^2 = (8760)^2 = 76{,}737{,}600$$

$$\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_i^2 = 2.5\times 10^{-11} \times 7.67376\times 10^{7} = 1.9184\times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 4 — Divide by 3

$$\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} = \frac{1.9184\times 10^{-3}}{3} = 6.395\times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 5 — Compare with SIL Bands

| SIL | PFD Range |
|-----|-----------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

$$\text{PFD}_{avg,1oo2} = 6.395\times 10^{-4} \in [10^{-4},\,10^{-3}]$$

**Conclusion: SIL 3 achievable** with this transmitter in 1oo2 architecture (assuming adequate architecture constraint & common-cause-failure coverage per IEC 61511).

> [!NOTE]
> **RRF Check:**
>
> $$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{6.395\times 10^{-4}} \approx 1564 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 10^3 < \text{RRF} < 10^4 \Rightarrow \text{SIL 3}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Furnace Purge Time Calculation

**Problem:** A boiler furnace volume is $V_f = 1500 \text{ m}^3$. Forced-draft fan delivers $Q_{\text{air}} = 600 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$. NFPA 85 requires **5 furnace volumes** at ≥25% of maximum fan capacity.

### Step 1 — Required Volumetric Flow

Air flow at full fan capacity: $Q_{\text{full}} = 600 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$.
At 25% capacity: $Q_{\text{purge}} = 0.25 \times 600 = 150 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$.

### Step 2 — Time for 1 Furnace Volume

$$t_1 = \frac{V_f}{Q_{\text{purge}}} = \frac{1500 \text{ m}^3}{150 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}} = 10 \text{ min}$$

### Step 3 — Time for 5 Furnace Volumes

$$t_{\text{purge}} = 5 \times t_1 = 5 \times 10 = 50 \text{ min}$$

### Step 4 — Validate Permissive

| Permissive | Status |
|------------|--------|
| Air flow ≥ 25% MFC | ✅ ($Q = 600 \geq 150$) |
| ≥ 5 volumes purged | ✅ (purge timer = 50 min) |
| Damper positions verified | Operator action |
| No flame present | UV scanner status |
| Master fuel trip OK | SIS health check |

**Conclusion: Light-off can begin only after 50 min purge** — a hard permissive enforced by the BMS.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Control & Safety Layers

| Attribute | Control Layer (DCS) | Safety Layer (SIS/ESD) |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| **Objective** | Optimize operation | Protect people, plant, environment |
| **Action on failure** | Alarm & continue | Trip / Shutdown |
| **Power-on state** | Energize-to-actuate | **De-energize-to-trip** |
| **Logic solver** | DCS controller | Dedicated, certified safety PLC (SIL-rated) |
| **Response time** | Seconds to minutes | **Milliseconds to seconds** |
| **Independence** | Shared with DCS | Physically & logically separate |
| **Standards** | ISA, IEC 61131 | IEC 61508, IEC 61511, NFPA 85 |
| **Test interval** | Continuous | Periodic proof-test (months to 1 year) |
| **Reset after trip** | Automatic (often) | **Manual only** |
| **Failure philosophy** | Fail-as-is / Fail-open | **Fail-safe (Fail-closed)** |
| **Example** | Drum level PI | Boiler MFT (Master Fuel Trip) |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Drill — Alarm Rate Calculation (ISA 18.2)

**Problem:** A power plant has 800 configured alarms. Operator per shift = 6. Annual operating hours = 8000 h. Acceptable average alarm rate per operator per hour (per ISA 18.2): **~150 alarms/h**. Maximum peak: **300 alarms/h for <5% of the time**.

### Step 1 — Acceptable Daily Alarms per Operator

$$R_{\text{target}} = 150 \text{ alarms/h} \times 8 \text{ h/shift} = 1200 \text{ alarms/shift}$$

### Step 2 — Annual Alarms per Operator

$$R_{\text{year}} = 1200 \times \frac{8000}{8} = 1200 \times 1000 = 1{,}200{,}000 \text{ alarms/yr}$$

### Step 3 — Plant-Wide Annual Alarms (6 operators)

$$R_{\text{plant}} = 1{,}200{,}000 \times 6 = 7.2 \times 10^6 \text{ alarms/yr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the *actual* measured alarm rate is ~10× this, the plant is in **alarm flood**. Causes: improper rationalization, chattering contacts, no dead-band, dual sources. Solution: HAZOP-driven rationalization, shelving, dynamic alarming.

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step — SIL Determination Procedure

| Step | Action | Output |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Identify hazard (HAZOP, FMEA) | Hazard list |
| 2 | Determine consequence severity | Severity category |
| 3 | Estimate event frequency without protection | $f_{np}$ |
| 4 | Define tolerable frequency | $f_{t}$ |
| 5 | Compute required RRF | $\text{RRF} = f_{np}/f_{t}$ |
| 6 | Map RRF → SIL | SIL table |
| 7 | Verify SIS achieves required PFDavg | Verification calculation |

**Example numerical:** Without protection, a furnace explosion frequency is $10^{-2}$/yr. Tolerable is $10^{-5}$/yr.

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{10^{-2}}{10^{-5}} = 10^{3}$$

**Required PFDavg = $10^{-3}$ → SIL 2.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

flowchart TB

    RUN -- ABNORMAL CONDITION --> TRIP
    IGNIT -- FAIL TO LIGHT --> TRIP
