<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]

**Q: Differentiate between a Distributed Control System (DCS) and a Safety Instrumented System (SIS).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | DCS | SIS |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Purpose** | Continuous process regulation | Risk reduction to safe state |
| **Action on detection** | Modulates MV | Trips (shuts down) |
| **Independence** | Integrated with process | Independent, separate hardware |
| **Standard** | ISA-88 / IEC 61131 | IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 |
| **Reset** | Auto | Manual only |
| **Failure philosophy** | Fail-as-is | Fail-safe |

A DCS optimizes plant efficiency; an SIS protects life, environment, and asset. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]

**Q: What is a Safety Integrity Level (SIL)? State the PFDavg range for SIL 2.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SIL is a discrete level (1 to 4) defined in IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 for specifying the reliability of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). It quantifies the probability that the SIF will perform its safety function when demanded.

**PFDavg range for SIL 2 (low-demand mode):**

$$10^{-3} \leq \text{PFDavg} < 10^{-2}$$

Corresponding Risk Reduction Factor: $100 \leq \text{RRF} < 1000$. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a closed-loop feedback control system as applied to a boiler drum level. Identify each block and write the closed-loop transfer function.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Block Diagram Description (3 marks)**

The drum level control loop consists of:
1. **Setpoint (SP)** — desired drum level (e.g., 50% of span).
2. **Summing Junction** — computes error $e = \text{SP} - \text{LV}$.
3. **Controller (PID/PI)** — accepts error, generates controller output (CO).
4. **Final Control Element** — feedwater control valve.
5. **Process** — boiler drum (integrating + first-order).
6. **Measurement (Transmitter)** — differential-pressure level transmitter.
7. **Feedback path** — returns measured level to summing junction.

```
        SP ──►(+) ──►[ Controller Gc ]──►[ Valve Gv ]──►[ Drum Gp ]──► Level
               ▲ ─                                                │
               │ ┌──────[ Level TX H ]──────────────────────────┘
               │ └
```

**Step 2: Closed-Loop Transfer Function (3 marks)**

With unity feedback:

$$\frac{LV(s)}{SP(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_p(s)\,H(s)}$$

For unity feedback $H(s) = 1$:

$$\frac{LV(s)}{SP(s)} = \frac{G_c(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_p(s)}$$

**Step 3: Drum Level Equation (1 mark)**

The boiler drum is typically modelled as an **integrating process** with shrink-and-swell nonlinearity:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_{drum}}{s(T_d s + 1)}$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the cascade control strategy used for superheater steam temperature control. State why cascade is preferred over single-loop feedback.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Why Steam Temperature is Hard to Control (2 marks)**

- Large dead time (transport delay in long superheater tubes)
- High process gain variation with load
- Frequent disturbances from attemperator spray water temperature

**Step 2: Cascade Architecture (3 marks)**

Two-loop structure:
- **Outer (Primary/Master) Loop:** Steam temperature controller — output sets the *setpoint* of the inner loop.
- **Inner (Secondary/Slave) Loop:** Attemperator water *flow* controller — directly positions the spray valve.

```
  T_SP ─►[ TC ]──► W_SP ─►[ FC ]──► Valve ─► Spray ─► Steam Temp.
              ▲                          │            │
              │                          ▼            │
              │                       [ FT ]          │
              │                                       │
              └─────────────[ TT ]───────────────────┘
```

**Step 3: Advantages of Cascade (2 marks)**

1. Inner flow loop *instantly* rejects spray-water-pressure disturbances.
2. Reduced effective dead time in the outer loop → higher permissible gain.
3. Compensates for valve non-linearities via inner-loop linearization.
4. Improves overall setpoint tracking and disturbance rejection.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Define a Safety Instrumented System. Explain the concept of SIL and the redundancy architectures 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, and 2oo3 with reference to IEC 61511.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of SIS (1 mark)**

Per IEC 61511, an SIS is an implementation of one or more Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs) used to bring a process to a safe state when a hazardous condition is detected.

**Step 2: SIL Table (2 marks)**

| SIL | PFDavg (low demand) |
|-----|---------------------|
| 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

**Step 3: Redundancy Architectures (4 marks)**

| Architecture | Description | Spurious Trip Rate | Achievable SIL |
|--------------|-------------|--------------------|----------------|
| **1oo1** | Single sensor, single trip path | Low | Up to SIL 2 |
| **1oo2** | Either of two sensors can trip | **Higher** (more spurious) | Up to SIL 3 |
| **2oo2** | Both sensors must trip to actuate | Lower (coincidence required for trip) | Up to SIL 3 |
| **2oo3** | Any 2 of 3 must trip | **Lowest spurious, highest availability** | Up to SIL 3 |

- **1oo2**: Better safety (fails dangerously less often) but more spurious trips.
- **2oo3**: Tolerates one failed sensor without process trip; any two detecting hazard will trip → **preferred for furnace MFT**.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: With a block diagram, describe the Burner Management System (BMS) according to NFPA 85. List any four permissives for furnace light-off.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: BMS Functional Phases (NFPA 85) (3 marks)**

```
  STANDBY → INITIATE → PURGE → IGNITION → RUN
                                       ↓ (abnormal)
                                   MASTER FUEL TRIP → POST-TRIP PURGE → STANDBY
```

| Phase | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Standby** | No flame demand; interlocks armed |
| **Purge** | ≥5 furnace volumes at ≥25% MFC air flow to remove residual fuel |
| **Ignition** | Pilot lit, then main burner, with flame proven |
| **Run** | Continuous flame monitoring; trip on flame failure |
| **Trip** | MFT closes all fuel valves within seconds |
| **Post-purge** | Furnace swept clear of unburnt fuel |

**Step 2: Four Light-Off Permissives (2 marks)**

1. Purge completed (timer satisfied).
2. Air flow ≥ 25% of MFC.
3. All fuel trip valves proven closed.
4. Master fuel trip healthy (SIS OK).
5. No flame detected (UV/IR scanners reset).
6. FD/ID fan running; damper positions verified.

**Step 3: Master Fuel Trip (MFT) (2 marks)**

MFT is the highest-integrity interlock in BMS. Initiating conditions:
- Loss of flame (all flames fail for ≤ 1 s).
- Loss of FD/ID fan.
- Low/Hi furnace pressure.
- Loss of feedwater (drum low level).
- Manual MFT pushbutton.

On MFT: all fuel valves close (de-energize-to-trip), igniters off, FD/ID fans trip after post-purge.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams (PEICT523):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing SIL with risk category.** SIL is about *risk reduction*; API-754 categories are about *consequence severity*. They are NOT the same.
> 2. **Stating "DCS controls safety" or "SIS optimizes the plant".** These are reversed. Memorize: **DCS = regulate; SIS = protect**.
> 3. **Using "fail-open" valves for safety.** Safety valves are *fail-closed* (fail-safe).
> 4. **Forgetting manual reset requirement.** SIS outputs require operator manual reset after a trip — automatic reset is forbidden by IEC 61511.
> 5. **Mixing up architectures.** 1oo2 vs 2oo2 — many students confuse which is "more spurious trip prone". 1oo2 trips if *either* sensor commands trip → higher spurious.
> 6. **Skipping the "in-dependence" requirement.** SIS MUST be independent of DCS — separate I/O, separate power, separate logic solver. Marks awarded for this explicitly.
> 7. **Wrong units in PFD formulas.** $\lambda_D$ is in **per hour**, $T_i$ is in **hours**, so PFD is **dimensionless**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Control & Safety Instrumentation**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ DCS = continuous control; SIS = discrete protection.
- ☐ Safety systems are **independent** (separate I/O, power, processor).
- ☐ **Fail-safe / De-energize-to-trip** principle.
- ☐ Manual reset after any safety trip.

### SIL & Redundancy
- ☐ SIL 1: PFD $10^{-2}$–$10^{-1}$ | SIL 2: $10^{-3}$–$10^{-2}$ | SIL 3: $10^{-4}$–$10^{-3}$ | SIL 4: $10^{-5}$–$10^{-4}$.
- ☐ **1oo2** = either trips → higher spurious trip rate.
- ☐ **2oo3** = any 2 of 3 trip → best availability & SIL-3 compliance.
- ☐ RRF = 1/PFDavg; PFDavg(1oo1) = ½ λ_D T_i; PFDavg(1oo2) = ⅓ λ_D² T_i².

### BMS (NFPA 85)
- ☐ Purge ≥ 5 furnace volumes at ≥ 25% MFC.
- ☐ Phases: Standby → Purge → Ignition → Run → Trip → Post-purge.
- ☐ Flame scanners: UV (gas), IR (coal/oil), flame rod (pilot).
- ☐ Permissives must all be satisfied before fuel admission.

### ESD
- ☐ ESD-0: Unit total trip; ESD-1: Section isolation; ESD-2: Local equipment.
- ☐ First-out indication identifies the initiating signal.
- ☐ Battery-backed power supply with redundancy.

### Control Strategies
- ☐ **Feedback** (single-loop): PV vs SP comparison.
- ☐ **Feedforward**: disturbance-measurement based.
- ☐ **Cascade**: outer SP → inner SP (e.g., temp → flow).
- ☐ **Ratio**: fuel/air stoichiometry.
- ☐ **Split-range**: one output drives multiple valves.

### Alarm Management (ISA 18.2)
- ☐ Target: ~150 alarms/h/operator; peaks < 300/h.
- ☐ Rationalization, shelving, dynamic alarming.

### Key Standards to Quote in Answers
- ☐ IEC 61508 (generic functional safety)
- ☐ IEC 61511 (process industry SIS)
- ☐ NFPA 85 (boiler combustion systems)
- ☐ ISA 18.2 (alarm management)
- ☐ ISA 88 (batch control reference)

### Equations to Memorize
- ☐ Closed-loop TF (unity feedback).
- ☐ PID controller in Laplace form.
- ☐ Feedforward ideal compensator.
- ☐ PFDavg formulas for 1oo1 and 1oo2.
- ☐ RRF = 1/PFDavg.
- ☐ Furnace purge time = (5 × V_f) / (0.25 × Q_full).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reliability Aspects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Power Plant Instrumentation**, **Reliability** is formally defined as the **probability that a system, sub-system, component, or instrument will perform its intended function satisfactorily under specified operating conditions (temperature, pressure, vibration, humidity) for a stipulated period of time**.

Mathematically, reliability $R(t)$ is the probability that the system *survives* and operates without failure up to time $t$:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing the **Time-To-Failure (TTF)** of the component.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Reliability is not a constant; it is a **time-dependent function** that decreases monotonically as the operational time $t$ increases. The two foundational metrics that examiners repeatedly test are **MTBF** and **Availability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (IEC 61508 / IEEE 500):** A power plant instrument is considered "reliable" if it is designed for a **target probability of failure on demand (PFD) avg ≤ 10⁻² to 10⁻⁵** depending on its Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 to SIL 4).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **car's headlight bulb**. When you buy it, the manufacturer gives an "average life" of 1,000 hours. This is the **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures). Now, think of three scenarios:

1. **Single bulb, no spare** (low reliability) — the moment it fails, you are stuck in the dark. The system availability is poor.
2. **Two bulbs in series** (over-engineered, low reliability) — if either one fails, the system fails. This is actually *worse* than one bulb alone because a series connection multiplies failure probabilities.
3. **Two bulbs in parallel** (high reliability / redundant) — the system keeps working until *both* bulbs fail. Reliability shoots up.

**This is the essence of reliability engineering in power plants:** Every sensor (temperature, pressure, flow, level) and every shutdown trip channel is a potential failure point. Reliability engineering asks: *"What is the probability this sensor fails when the boiler is at 540 °C and 150 bar?"* and *"What architecture (series/parallel/2-out-of-3) do we use to keep the plant safe even when components fail?"*

## 1.3 Why Reliability Matters in a Power Plant

| Hazard Category | Consequence of Instrument Failure | Reliability Demand |
|---|---|---|
| Safety (Reactor SCRAM, Boiler Trip) | Loss of life, asset destruction | **SIL 3 / SIL 4** (≥ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁶ PFD) |
| Environmental (Emission monitor) | Regulatory non-compliance, fine | **SIL 2** |
| Production (Steam flow transmitter) | Plant trip, revenue loss | **SIL 1** |

A **500 MW thermal power plant** has **over 8,000 instrumentation loops**. Even if each has 99% reliability, the combined system reliability drops catastrophically. This is why reliability math is not optional — it is the **mathematical backbone of plant safety**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Foundational Pillars of Reliability Engineering

### 2.1.1 Reliability ($R(t)$)
- **What it is:** Probability of *no failure* up to time $t$.
- **How it behaves:** Starts at $R(0) = 1$ and decays toward $R(\infty) = 0$.
- **Why it matters:** Determines how long a trip-grade instrument (e.g., a Furnace Pressure Switch) can be trusted between proof tests.

### 2.1.2 Maintainability ($M(t)$)
- **What it is:** Probability that a *failed* system is restored to working condition within time $t$.
- **How it behaves:** Starts at $M(0) = 0$ and approaches $M(\infty) = 1$ asymptotically.
- **Why it matters:** In a plant, an instrument is useless if it cannot be repaired quickly. Maintainability captures the "serviceability" — does the plant have spares, trained technicians, and diagnostic access?

### 2.1.3 Availability ($A(t)$)
- **What it is:** The *steady-state* fraction of time the system is operational.
- **How it is built:** Combines reliability (uptime) and maintainability (repair time).
- **Why it matters:** A highly reliable instrument that is impossible to repair is operationally worse than a slightly less reliable instrument that is hot-swappable. Availability unifies both viewpoints.

## 2.2 Failure Rate ($\lambda$) and the Exponential Distribution

For most **electronic and electromechanical instruments** in a power plant (transmitters, I/P converters, control valves, solenoid valves, relays), the failure rate during the **useful life period** is modelled as **constant** (the flat middle portion of the bath-tub curve).

When $\lambda = \text{constant}$, the reliability function simplifies to the **Exponential Distribution**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The Exponential Distribution is the **only** continuous distribution that exhibits the **memoryless property**, meaning $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$. In a power plant context, this means a transmitter that has run for 3 years is *statistically as good as new* during its useful life — a fact that justifies periodic, rather than age-based, replacement.

## 2.3 The Bath-Tub Curve (Failure Rate vs. Time)

The failure rate $\lambda(t)$ of any physical instrument follows a characteristic three-region shape:

| Region | Name | Failure Behaviour | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| I — Infant Mortality | **Burn-in** | $\lambda$ decreases from high to low | Factory burn-in, commissioning tests |
| II — Useful Life | **Random Failures** | $\lambda$ ≈ constant (lowest) | Normal operation, predictive maintenance |
| III — Wear-out | **Aging Failures** | $\lambda$ increases sharply | Scheduled replacement, refurbishment |

A typical power plant **pressure transmitter** has $\lambda \approx 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour during useful life, which means MTBF $\approx 2 \times 10^6$ hours $\approx 228$ years. This is for *electronic* failures only; mechanical parts (bellows, diaphragms) wear out faster.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | Prob. of no failure up to $t$ |
| 2 | Unreliability (CDF) | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | dimensionless | Prob. of failure by time $t$ |
| 3 | Failure Rate (constant) | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | failures / hour | Failures per unit time |
| 4 | **MTBF** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | hours | Mean Time Between Failures |
| 5 | **MTTR** | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{1}{\mu}$ | hours | Mean Time To Repair |
| 6 | Repair Rate | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTTR}}$ | repairs / hour | Restorations per unit time |
| 7 | **Availability** (steady state) | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | fraction (0 to 1) | Uptime fraction |
| 8 | Unavailability | $U = 1 - A = \dfrac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | fraction | Downtime fraction |
| 9 | Series System Reliability | $R_s = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | dimensionless | All must survive |
| 10 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | dimensionless | At least one survives |
| 11 | 2-out-of-3 Voting | $R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | dimensionless | At least 2 of 3 must survive |
| 12 | Probability of Failure on Demand | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot T_{\text{proof}}}{2}$ | dimensionless | SIL metric (for low-demand) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notational Safeguard:** The vertical bar symbol inside equations (such as absolute value) has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve Markdown table integrity. For instance, the absolute value of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$, not $\vert x \vert$ in raw pipe form.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Power Plant Engineering

- **Reactor Protection System (Nuclear):** Built on **1-out-of-2 taken twice (1oo2D)** or **2-out-of-4 (2oo4)** voting to achieve SIL 3.
- **Boiler Drum Level Trip:** Uses **2-out-of-3 (2oo3)** redundant transmitters to avoid nuisance trips while maintaining safety.
- **Emission Monitoring (CEMS):** Single sensor with periodic calibration — reliability requirement is lower (SIL 1), but availability requirement is high.
- **Turbine Overspeed Protection:** Mechanical overspeed bolt + electrical trip + hydraulic trip — **three diverse, independent channels** in parallel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Failure Rate $\lambda(t)$ to the Reliability Function $R(t)$

This is the most important derivation in the KTU syllabus and is almost always worth 4–5 marks in an ESE.

### Step 1 — Define the Failure Rate
By definition, the instantaneous failure rate (also called the **hazard function** $h(t)$) is the conditional probability of failure in the next infinitesimal interval $dt$, *given survival up to time $t$*:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \frac{-1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

where $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ is the **probability density function (PDF)** of the time-to-failure random variable $T$.

### Step 2 — Rearrange into a Separable Differential Equation

Rearranging the definition gives:

$$h(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

### Step 3 — Integrate Both Sides from 0 to $t$

We apply the boundary condition that at $t = 0$, the system is *as good as new*, so $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau = -\int_{0}^{t} \frac{dR}{R}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Right-Hand Integral

The right-hand side evaluates to:

$$-\Big[\ln R(t) - \ln R(0)\Big] = -\ln R(t)$$

(since $\ln R(0) = \ln 1 = 0$).

### Step 5 — Solve for $R(t)$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

### Step 6 — Specialize to the Constant Failure-Rate Case

If the failure rate is *constant* ($h(t) = \lambda$), the integral becomes trivial:

$$R(t) = \exp(-\lambda t)$$

This is the **exponential reliability law**, valid for the entire "Useful Life" region of the bath-tub curve.

### Step 7 — Derive MTBF from $R(t)$

The expected time-to-failure is the **mean of the TTF distribution**, calculated as:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$

(using the standard identity that the area under $R(t)$ equals the mean of the density). Substituting $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda}e^{-\lambda t}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Repairable Systems:** In a power plant, an instrument is *repaired* and put back into service, so the mean time *between* failures (MTBF) equals MTTF + MTTR for most modelling purposes. For a single non-repairable component, MTBF $\approx$ MTTF. **This is a common KTU exam trap — examiners award or deduct the 2-mark distinction deliberately.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Availability $A$

### Step 1 — Model the System as a Two-State Markov Chain
A repairable instrument is either **UP** (state 1) or **DOWN** (state 0). The transition rates are:

- UP → DOWN at rate $\lambda$ (failure rate)
- DOWN → UP at rate $\mu$ (repair rate)

### Step 2 — Apply Steady-State Balance
Let $P_{\text{up}}$ and $P_{\text{down}}$ be the steady-state probabilities. In the long run, the flow into each state equals the flow out:

$$\lambda \cdot P_{\text{up}} = \mu \cdot P_{\text{down}}$$

### Step 3 — Use the Probability Conservation Law

$$P_{\text{up}} + P_{\text{down}} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Solve the Two Equations Simultaneously
From the balance equation:

$$P_{\text{down}} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_{\text{up}}$$

Substituting:

$$P_{\text{up}} + \frac{\lambda}{\mu} P_{\text{up}} = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad P_{\text{up}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

### Step 5 — Identify Availability with $P_{\text{up}}$

$$A = P_{\text{up}} = \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}$$

### Step 6 — Rewrite in MTBF / MTTR Form

Since $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$ and $\text{MTTR} = 1/\mu$:

$$A = \frac{1/\lambda}{1/\lambda + 1/\mu} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A **Boiler Drum Level Transmitter** has a failure rate of $\lambda = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures/hour and a mean repair time of MTTR $= 10$ hours. The plant requires a **mission time** of 8,760 hours (1 year).

**(a) Compute the reliability for 1 year of operation.**

We apply $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$R(8760) = \exp(-5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760) = \exp(-0.0438) = 0.9572$$

So there is a **95.72% probability** the transmitter will not fail within one year — or equivalently, a **4.28% chance** it will fail.

**(b) Compute the steady-state availability.**

First, MTBF $= 1/\lambda = 1/(5 \times 10^{-6}) = 200{,}000$ hours.

Then:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{200{,}000}{200{,}000 + 10} = 0.99995$$

So the transmitter is **available 99.995% of the time** — equivalent to a downtime of only **26 minutes per year**.

**(c) If two such transmitters are placed in parallel for redundancy, what is the combined reliability?**

The parallel system reliability is:

$$R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2) = 1 - (1 - 0.9572)^2 = 1 - (0.0428)^2 = 0.99817$$

Reliability jumps from **95.72% to 99.82%** by adding one parallel channel. This is why **redundancy is the standard KTU recommendation** for safety-grade loops.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Reliability Engineering Framework in a Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Power Plant Instrumentation Loop] --> B[Identify Components]
    B --> C[Assign Failure Rate lambda]
    C --> D[Choose Architecture]
    D --> D1[Series]
    D --> D2[Parallel Redundant]
    D --> D3[Voting 2 out of 3]
    D1 --> E[Compute R_series]
    D2 --> F[Compute R_parallel]
    D3 --> G[Compute R_voting]
    E --> H[Evaluate Availability]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Acceptable Risk?}
    I -->|Yes| J[SIL Verified - Deploy]
    I -->|No| K[Add Redundancy / Diversity]
    K --> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Series vs. Parallel Reliability Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SERIES ["Series Configuration - All must work"]
        S1[Sensor 1] --> S2[Transmitter 2] --> S3[Controller 3] --> S4[Final Element 4]
    end
    subgraph PARALLEL ["Parallel Configuration - One must work"]
        P1[Channel A] --> MUX1[Junction]
        P2[Channel B] --> MUX1
        P3[Channel C] --> MUX1
        MUX1 --> OUT[Common Output]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Bath-Tub Curve — Engineering Interventions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Region I - Burn-in] -->|High lambda| R2[Region II - Useful Life]
    R2 -->|Constant lambda| R3[Region III - Wear-out]
    R1 -.Action.-> A1[Factory Burn-in]
    R2 -.Action.-> A2[Predictive Maintenance]
    R3 -.Action.-> A3[Scheduled Replacement]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: SIL Selection Logic for a Trip Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Instrument Trip Function] --> Q1{Risk to Personnel?}
    Q1 -->|Severe| SIL4[SIL 4 - PFD 10-5 to 10-6]
    Q1 -->|High| SIL3[SIL 3 - PFD 10-4 to 10-5]
    Q1 -->|Moderate| SIL2[SIL 2 - PFD 10-3 to 10-4]
    Q1 -->|Minor| SIL1[SIL 1 - PFD 10-2 to 10-3]
    SIL4 --> ARC1[Triple Redundant 2oo3 Voting]
    SIL3 --> ARC2[Dual Redundant 1oo2D Voting]
    SIL2 --> ARC3[Single Channel with Diagnostics]
    SIL1 --> ARC4[Single Channel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural Note:** A typical 500 MW thermal power unit implements the following reliability-critical architecture: **Two-out-of-three voting (2oo3)** on Drum Level, **1oo2D** on Turbine Overspeed, and **triple modular redundant (TMR)** on the Burner Management System (BMS). Each voting scheme has its own characteristic reliability equation shown in the KTU Formula Sheet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define the terms (i) MTBF, (ii) MTTR, and (iii) Availability as applied to a power plant instrument. Give one typical numerical value for each from a thermal power station.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** The average time interval between two consecutive failures of a repairable instrument. For a Boiler Drum Level Transmitter, MTBF is typically **2,00,000 hours**.

(ii) **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** The average time required to restore a failed instrument to working condition, including diagnosis, repair, and recommissioning. Typical MTTR for a control valve in a thermal plant is **8 to 12 hours**.

(iii) **Availability (A):** The steady-state fraction of time the instrument is in an operational state, given by $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. A well-maintained DCS I/O card achieves **$A \approx 0.99999$**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**State and explain the "memoryless property" of the exponential distribution. Why is it relevant for power plant electronic instruments?**

**Model Answer:**

The exponential distribution with constant failure rate $\lambda$ is **memoryless**, which means the probability of surviving an *additional* time $s$ does not depend on how long the system has *already* been operating:

$$P(T > t + s \mid T > t) = P(T > s) = e^{-\lambda s}$$

**Significance for power plant instruments:** It justifies the practice of *condition-based* or *periodic proof-test-based* maintenance of electronic transmitters, solenoid valves, and DCS cards during their useful life. An instrument that has run for 5 years is statistically *as good as new*, because the exponential distribution has no "memory" of past operation. Hence, age-based retirement is unnecessary — only burn-in and wear-out regions of the bath-tub curve warrant age limits.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Redundancy and Voting

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the reliability expression for a system of **two identical components in parallel**, each with reliability $R$. Show all steps. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A power plant has a critical **Furnace Pressure Switch** used for the boiler trip logic. The switch has a constant failure rate of $\lambda = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ failures/hour. The plant's safety code mandates a **2-out-of-3 (2oo3) voting** architecture using three such switches. Compute: (i) the reliability of each switch for a 1-year mission, (ii) the 2oo3 system reliability, and (iii) the steady-state availability, given MTTR = 6 hours. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Parallel System Reliability**

For two independent components in parallel, the system **fails** only when *both* components fail. Let $F_1$ and $F_2$ be the individual failure probabilities; $F_i = 1 - R_i$.

The system unreliability is:

$$F_s = F_1 \cdot F_2 = (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$$

For two **identical** components ($R_1 = R_2 = R$):

$$F_s = (1 - R)^2$$

Therefore, the system reliability is:

$$R_s = 1 - (1 - R)^2$$

**Algebraic Expansion:**

$$R_s = 1 - (1 - 2R + R^2) = 2R - R^2$$

**Generalization to n components:**

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering insight:** Even with two components of only 50% reliability each, $R_s = 2(0.5) - (0.5)^2 = 0.75$. Redundancy therefore rescues *low-reliability* components. **(KTU 2024 frequently tests this "rescue" principle.)**

**(b) Numerical Solution for the 2oo3 Voting Architecture**

**(i) Single-switch reliability for 1 year (8,760 hours):**

$$R = e^{-\lambda t} = \exp(-3 \times 10^{-5} \times 8760) = \exp(-0.2628) = 0.7690$$

**(ii) 2-out-of-3 system reliability:**

The system functions if **at least 2 of the 3** switches survive. The system fails if **0 or 1** switches survive. Using the binomial theorem:

$$R_{2/3} = \sum_{k=2}^{3} \binom{3}{k} R^k (1-R)^{3-k} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$

Substituting $R = 0.7690$:

$$R^2 = 0.5914, \quad R^3 = 0.4549$$

$$R_{2/3} = 3(0.5914) - 2(0.4549) = 1.7742 - 0.9098 = 0.8644$$

**(iii) Steady-state availability:**

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-5}} = 33{,}333.33 \text{ hours}$$

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{33333.33}{33333.33 + 6} = 0.99982$$

**Conclusion:** The 2oo3 voting arrangement yields a per-mission reliability of **86.44%** for one year, with availability of **99.982%**. The system tolerates **one failed switch** at any time while still satisfying the trip logic — a true KTU-grade safety architecture.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Availability and Maintainability

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **bath-tub curve** of failure rate. Identify the engineering interventions applicable in each region. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A power plant **Boiler Feed Pump (BFP)** has a vibration monitoring system with a single eddy-current probe. The probe has MTBF = 50,000 hours and MTTR = 25 hours. The Chief Engineer proposes adding a parallel redundant probe of identical specification. Calculate: (i) the availability of the single-probe system, (ii) the combined MTBF of the two parallel probes, and (iii) the new availability after redundancy. Comment on the practical value of this redundancy. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) The Bath-Tub Curve**

The bath-tub curve is a plot of **failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus operating time $t$** for a population of identical instruments. It has three distinct regions:

- **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** Failure rate is high and *decreases* with time. Caused by manufacturing defects, poor soldering, substandard components. **Intervention:** Factory burn-in, environmental stress screening (ESS), 48-hour commissioning soak test.

- **Region II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is **constant and minimum**. Failures are random and unavoidable (e.g., cosmic-ray-induced bit flips in DCS cards). **Intervention:** Predictive maintenance, condition monitoring, periodic proof tests.

- **Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate *increases* rapidly. Caused by corrosion, fatigue, insulation degradation. **Intervention:** Scheduled replacement, life-extension refurbishment.

**Sketch (to be drawn in exam):** Plot $\lambda$ on Y-axis, $t$ on X-axis; the curve starts high, drops to a flat minimum, then rises sharply — resembling a bath-tub in cross-section.

**(b) Numerical Solution for the BFP Vibration Probe**

**(i) Single-probe availability:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{50000}{50000 + 25} = 0.99950$$

Unavailability: $U_1 = 1 - A_1 = 5.0 \times 10^{-4}$.

**(ii) Combined MTBF of two parallel probes:**

The unreliability of two parallel identical probes is:

$$U_p = U_1^2 = (5.0 \times 10^{-4})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$$

For the combined system, the MTTR remains 25 hours (failure still requires same repair time). The combined MTBF is:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{combined}} = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{U_p} = \frac{25}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 1.0 \times 10^{8} \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) New availability after redundancy:**

$$A_p = 1 - U_p = 1 - 2.5 \times 10^{-7} = 0.99999975$$

**Comment on practical value:** Availability improves from **99.950% → 99.999975%**, equivalent to a downtime reduction from **219 hours/year to about 13 minutes/year**. The two-probe architecture also enables **2oo2 voting**, suppressing false trips — a major operational win in a 500 MW unit where every spurious trip costs **₹15–20 lakh in lost revenue**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **(1)** Many students confuse **MTBF with MTTF** in repairable systems. MTTF is the *expected lifetime of a non-repairable* component; MTBF includes both operational time and repair time. For repairable instruments, **MTBF = MTTF + MTTR**.
> **(2)** Students often mistakenly use the formula $R_p = 1 - (1-R)^2$ for **non-identical** parallel components. The correct form is $R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)$. Lose 2 marks if the components are clearly stated as non-identical.
> **(3)** In SIL questions, the PFD formula $PFD_{avg} = \lambda \cdot T_{proof}/2$ is **only valid for low-demand mode** and for a single channel. For 1oo2D, the divisor is **6** (not 2). Examiners routinely award zero marks for forgetting the 1oo2D correction.
> **(4)** Sketches of the bath-tub curve must show **three labelled regions** and **engineering interventions** — drawing the curve without annotations loses 3 of the 7 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Reliability is a probability, not a guarantee** — and it always decreases with time.
- **MTBF = 1/λ** (constant failure rate, useful-life region).
- **MTTR = 1/μ** (mean repair time reciprocal).
- **Availability $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$** is the most-tested KTU formula.
- **Series multiplies reliability** ($R_s = R_1 R_2 \cdots R_n$); **parallel multiplies unreliability** ($R_p = 1 - (1-R_1)(1-R_2)\cdots$).
- **2-out-of-3 voting** uses $R_{2/3} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$.
- **Exponential distribution** = memoryless; valid for electronic instruments in useful life.
- **Bath-tub curve** has three regions: Burn-in (Region I), Useful Life (Region II, constant λ), Wear-out (Region III).
- **SIL 1 → 4** corresponds to PFD ranges $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-6}$ (IEC 61508).
- **PFD for 1oo1 low-demand mode:** $PFD_{avg} = \lambda T_{proof} / 2$.
- **PFD for 1oo2D low-demand mode:** $PFD_{avg} = \lambda^2 T_{proof}^2 / 6$.
- **Diversity** (different physical principles) is preferred over identical redundancy for safety loops.
- **Hot standby** = redundant element is energized and ready; **cold standby** = unpowered, switched in only on failure.
- **MTTF ≠ MTBF** in repairable systems — a classic 2-mark trap.
- **Reliability block diagrams assume statistical independence** of components — a common assumption to state explicitly in exam answers.
- **Common-mode failures** (e.g., shared power supply, shared calibration gas) can defeat redundancy. The β-factor model captures this: $R_{CM} = \beta \cdot R$, $R_{DM} = (1-\beta) \cdot R$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiler - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boiler - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boiler (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A **boiler** is a closed pressure vessel in which a fluid (most commonly water) is heated under controlled conditions to convert it into steam or to generate hot water/vapour at a desired pressure and temperature. The heat is typically produced by the combustion of fuel (coal, oil, gas, biomass, or nuclear fission) and transferred to the working fluid through heat exchange surfaces (tubes, drums, or plates).

In the context of **Power Plant Instrumentation (PEICT523)**, a boiler is the **prime heat-exchange subsystem** of a thermal power station. It receives feedwater from the economizer, converts it into superheated steam at parameters required by the turbine (typically **540 °C** and **pressure 130–250 bar** in supercritical units), and supplies the steam to the High-Pressure (HP) turbine. Every subsequent measurement — drum level, steam pressure, flue gas temperature, combustion air flow, and fuel flow — exists to **control and protect the boiler**.

### 1.1.1 The Three Functional Sub-systems of a Boiler

| Sub-system | Primary Function | Key Controlled Variables |
|------------|------------------|--------------------------|
| **Combustion / Furnace System** | Burns fuel to release heat | Fuel flow, air flow, furnace draft, O₂ in flue gas |
| **Steam-Generating / Heat Transfer System** | Transfers heat to water | Drum level, steam pressure, steam temperature |
| **Auxiliary System** | Preheats air/water, removes ash | FD/ID fan speed, PA flow, soot-blower cycles |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Pressure Cooker vs. The Industrial Boiler:**
> Think of a domestic **pressure cooker**. You put water inside, seal the lid (a *pressure-tight vessel*), and place it on a flame (*furnace*). As the water boils, the trapped steam pressure rises, and the temperature climbs above 100 °C. The whistle releases excess steam, keeping pressure constant.
>
> An industrial **boiler** is essentially a *giant, instrumented pressure cooker* — but with thousands of sensors, automatic combustion control, and the ability to evaporate **hundreds of tonnes of water per hour** at *precisely* controlled pressure and temperature. The whistle is replaced by a **control valve** on the turbine-governing stage.

A second, more useful analogy for instrumentation students:

> **The Boiler as the "Heart" of a Power Plant:**
> Just as the human heart pumps oxygenated blood at a controlled pressure, the **boiler pumps superheated steam at controlled pressure and temperature** to the turbine. Pressure, flow, temperature, and level are the *vital signs* — and PEICT523 is essentially the study of the instruments that monitor those vital signs.

## 1.3 Classification of Boilers (KTU Module 2 Scope)

KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes boilers along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Based on the contents inside the shell (tube-side):**
   - **Fire-tube boiler** — hot gases flow inside tubes, water surrounds them
   - **Water-tube boiler** — water flows inside tubes, hot gases surround them

2. **Based on the position of the axis of the shell:**
   - **Vertical boiler**
   - **Horizontal boiler**

3. **Based on the application / pressure:**
   - **Low-pressure boiler** (≤ 10 bar)
   - **Medium-pressure boiler** (10–40 bar)
   - **High-pressure boiler** (40–100 bar)
   - **Super-critical boiler** (> 225 bar)

4. **Based on the circulation of water:**
   - **Natural circulation boiler**
   - **Controlled / Assisted circulation boiler**
   - **Once-through boiler (OTTB / Benson)**
   - **Forced circulation boiler**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Focus:**
> For examination purposes, the **fire-tube vs. water-tube** and **natural vs. forced vs. once-through circulation** classifications carry the **maximum weightage (≈ 70%)** within Module 2.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Recall Values)

| Metric | Typical Range in Modern Utility Boilers |
|--------|----------------------------------------|
| **Steam generation capacity** | **500 – 3000 tonnes/hour** per unit |
| **Steam pressure (subcritical)** | **130 – 180 bar** |
| **Steam pressure (supercritical)** | **> 225 bar (up to 600 bar)** |
| **Steam temperature (reheat/superheat)** | **540 °C – 600 °C** |
| **Boiler efficiency (η)** | **88 % – 94 %** (HHV basis) |
| **Feedwater inlet temperature** | **240 °C – 290 °C** (after economizer) |
| **Excess air (typical)** | **15 % – 20 %** |
| **Flue gas temperature at APH outlet** | **120 °C – 150 °C** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1 — Fire-Tube Boilers

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle

In a **fire-tube boiler**, the products of combustion (hot gases) pass through tubes that are surrounded by water. The shell itself is partially filled with water, and the upper portion is reserved for steam collection. Heat transfer occurs primarily by **conduction through the tube wall** and **convection into the surrounding water**.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

1. Fuel is admitted into the **furnace / combustion chamber** at one end of the shell.
2. Hot gases travel through **tubes (1, 2, 3 passes)** immersed in water.
3. Water absorbs heat, reaches saturation, and produces **saturated steam** in the steam space at the top of the shell.
4. Saturated steam is drawn through a **steam stop valve** to the consumer / turbine.
5. Flue gases exit to the economizer and air-preheater.

### 2.1.3 Key Sub-Types (Engineering Inventory)

| Sub-type | Salient Feature | Typical Capacity |
|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Cochran boiler** | Vertical, multi-tubular, portable | ≤ 3000 kg/h |
| **Cornish boiler** | Single flue, horizontal | ≤ 6500 kg/h |
| **Lancashire boiler** | Two internal flues, horizontal | ≤ 8000 kg/h |
| **Locomotive boiler** | High evaporative rate, mobile | ≤ 6500 kg/h |
| **Babcock & Wilcox (B&W)** | Bent-tube variant (water-tube but listed here for contrast) | Large stationary units |
| **Package boiler** | Factory-assembled, skid-mounted | 1–30 TPH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:**
> Fire-tube boilers are **simpler, cheaper, and quicker to start up**, but they are **limited to low pressures (≤ 20 bar)** because the **entire shell is a pressure vessel**. Increasing shell pressure requires exponentially thicker plates, making them uneconomical above ~ 20 bar.

## 2.2 Type 2 — Water-Tube Boilers

### 2.2.1 Operational Principle

In a **water-tube boiler**, water circulates inside the tubes, and the hot combustion gases flow across the outside. The drum (or drums) are **not in the direct path of combustion gases** — only the tubes see the flame. This single geometric fact allows water-tube boilers to operate at **much higher pressures (40 → 600 bar)** and **much larger capacities (> 3000 TPH)**.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Working Logic

1. **Feedwater** enters the economizer (external to the boiler).
2. Pre-heated water enters the **steam drum** through downcomers.
3. From the drum, water descends to the **mud drum** via unheated downcomer pipes.
4. The water rises through **banked tubes** lining the furnace walls (water walls).
5. In the riser tubes, the water-steam mixture rises back into the steam drum by **natural or forced circulation**.
6. Steam is separated from water in the **cyclone / chevron separators** inside the drum.
7. Saturated steam passes to the **superheater** → **reheater** → turbine.

### 2.2.3 Key Sub-Types

| Sub-type | Salient Feature |
|----------|-----------------|
| **Babcock & Wilcox (B&W)** | Inclined, straight water tubes, external furnace |
| **Stirling** | Three-drum, bent-tube design |
| **Radiant / D-type** | Two-drum, membrane water walls |
| **Benson (once-through)** | No drum — critical pressure operation |
| **LaMont** | Forced circulation with pump |
| **Loeffler** | Uses a portion of turbine steam for circulation |
| **Velox (Benson variant)** | High heat flux, gas-pressurized furnace |

## 2.3 Comparison Table (Examination Favorite)

| Parameter | Fire-Tube Boiler | Water-Tube Boiler |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Tube contents** | Hot gases | Water / steam |
| **Shell contents** | Water + steam | Steam (drum) + outside air |
| **Maximum working pressure** | **≤ 20 bar** | **up to 600 bar (supercritical)** |
| **Steam output** | Low (≤ 10 TPH) | High (≥ 500 TPH) |
| **Response to load change** | **Slow** (large water mass) | **Fast** (small water mass) |
| **Explosion risk** | **High** (whole shell is a pressure vessel) | **Lower** (tubes only are pressurized) |
| **Initial cost** | **Low** | **High** |
| **Space required** | Large (horizontal) | Compact |
| **Water quality demand** | Moderate | **Very high** (TDS < 1 ppm in once-through) |
| **Typical applications** | Process steam, hotels, small industry | Utility power stations, large process plants |

## 2.4 Circulation-Based Classification (Critical for PEICT523)

### 2.4.1 Natural Circulation Boiler

The driving head for circulation is the **density difference** between the colder downcomer water and the hotter riser water-steam mixture. No pump is used.

$$\Delta P_{natural} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{water} - \rho_{mixture})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Where **H** is the drum centre-line to mud drum height (typical **25 – 40 m**), **g** is gravitational acceleration (**9.81 m/s²**), **ρ_water** is saturated water density, and **ρ_mixture** is the density of the two-phase riser mixture.

**Limitation:** When boiler pressure approaches the **critical point (≈ 221.2 bar)**, the density difference collapses (water and steam densities converge), and natural circulation becomes **impossible**.

### 2.4.2 Assisted / Controlled Circulation Boiler

A **circulation pump (boiler circulating pump, BCP)** is added between the downcomer outlet and the riser inlet. The pump provides ~ **30 % – 40 %** of the total driving head; the rest is still natural. This extends operation to **~ 180 bar**.

### 2.4.3 Once-Through Boiler (OTTB / Benson)

There is **no steam drum**. Feedwater is pumped by the **boiler feed pump (BFP)** and passes sequentially through:
**Economizer → Evaporator section → Superheater**

Critical heat flux, mass flux, and quality are precisely controlled. **Operates at supercritical pressures (> 225.6 bar)**. Startup losses are high; the boiler must be **fired only after adequate water flow is established**.

### 2.4.4 Forced Circulation Boiler (LaMont)

A pump provides **100 %** of the circulation driving head. No reliance on density difference. Used up to ~ **200 bar**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variable | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q = m_s (h_{sh} - h_{fw})$ | Heat absorbed in boiler (m_s = steam mass flow) | **kW** |
| 2 | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_s (h_{sh} - h_{fw})}{m_{fuel} \cdot GCV}$ | Boiler thermal efficiency | **%** |
| 3 | $\text{Evaporation Ratio (ER)} = \dfrac{m_{steam}}{m_{fuel}}$ | kg of steam per kg of fuel | **dimensionless** |
| 4 | $\text{Equivalent Evaporation} = m_s \cdot \dfrac{(h_{sh} - h_{fw@100})}{2257}$ | Steam output expressed in terms of evaporation from & at 100 °C | **kg/h** |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{nat} = H \cdot g (\rho_w - \rho_m)$ | Natural circulation driving head | **Pa** |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \alpha \cdot \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot A_0$ | Combustion air flow (α = excess air ratio; A₀ = stoichiometric air) | **kg/h** |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{flue} = \dot{m}_{fuel} + \dot{m}_{air} - \dot{m}_{ash} - \text{unburnt}$ | Flue gas mass flow | **kg/h** |
| 8 | $FESR = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{boiler}}{\dot{Q}_{fuel}}$ | Fuel Energy Supply Ratio (control) | **%** |
| 9 | $T_{fw}^{sat} = 100 \cdot \left( \dfrac{P}{P_{atm}} \right)^{0.25}$ | Approx. saturation T (low pressure) | **°C** |
| 10 | $h_{fg} = 2257 \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{T}{374} \right)^{0.38}$ | Latent heat of vaporization (approx., valid sub-critical) | **kJ/kg** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of this formula sheet:**
> In a power plant DCS (Distributed Control System), Equations 1, 2, 6, 7, and 8 are computed **in real time** to generate the **heat-loss curve**, the **air–fuel ratio command**, the **efficiency trend**, and the **O₂ trim signal**. These formulas are the **mathematical backbone** of boiler combustion control and optimization.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Boiler Classification

1. **Safety instrument selection** — fire-tube boilers need **shell-temperature** and **pressure relief** interlocks; water-tube units need **drum-level tri-redundant** measurements and **tube-leak detection** (acoustic emission).
2. **Control philosophy design** — natural circulation units tolerate slower control; once-through units require **> 30 % BFP flow trip** and **firing-rate / feedwater ratio** cross-limit control.
3. **Efficiency optimization** — once-through supercritical units achieve **> 93 %** efficiency, but only with **precise O₂ trim, furnace draft control, and APH condensate management**.
4. **Maintenance scheduling** — water-tube boilers need **tube-bank soot blowing** schedules; fire-tube units need **scale and soot deposit** monitoring on the tube ID.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section is dedicated to **complete, non-truncated derivations** of the core KTU high-yield equations used in Module 2 (Boiler Types). Every algebraic step is explicit.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Natural Circulation Driving Head ($\Delta P_{nat}$)

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement
A natural circulation water-tube boiler has a drum centre-line **30 m** above the mud drum. The water in the downcomers is at saturation temperature corresponding to **150 bar** (saturated water density **ρ_w = 657.1 kg/m³**). The riser contains a water-steam mixture of average density **ρ_m = 95 kg/m³**. Compute the natural circulation driving head.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the governing principle.**
The driving head is the **hydrostatic pressure difference** between the downcomer leg and the riser leg, integrated over the active height.

$$\Delta P_{nat} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_{w} - \rho_{m})$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known numerical values.**

$$\Delta P_{nat} = 30 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (657.1 - 95)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the density difference.**

$$657.1 - 95 = 562.1 \;\text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by g.**

$$30 \cdot 9.81 = 294.3 \;\text{N·m/kg (equivalent m}^2/\text{s}^2)$$

**Step 5 — Final product.**

$$\Delta P_{nat} = 294.3 \cdot 562.1 = 1.654 \times 10^5 \;\text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{nat} \approx 1.654 \;\text{bar} \approx 165.4 \;\text{kPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** This ~ 1.65 bar pressure difference is what drives the water up the riser tubes. A circulation pump in an **assisted circulation** boiler supplements this with an additional ~ 0.5 – 1.0 bar.

### 3.1.3 Pressure-Sensitivity Matrix

| Boiler Pressure (bar) | $\rho_w$ (kg/m³) | $\rho_m$ approx. (kg/m³) | $\Delta\rho$ | $\Delta P_{nat}$ per 30 m (bar) |
|------------------------|------------------|--------------------------|--------------|----------------------------------|
| 50 | 740 | 35 | 705 | **2.07** |
| 100 | 691 | 65 | 626 | **1.84** |
| 150 | 657 | 95 | 562 | **1.65** |
| 200 | 580 | 140 | 440 | **1.29** |
| 220 (near critical) | 358 | 250 | 108 | **0.32** |
| 221.2 (critical) | 322 | 322 | **0** | **0 (Circulation ceases)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical insight:** As pressure → **221.2 bar**, $\Delta\rho \to 0$ and natural circulation **ceases to exist**. This is the *physical reason* supercritical boilers **must** use once-through design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Boiler Thermal Efficiency (η_boiler)

### 3.2.1 Statement
An industrial boiler burns **2000 kg/h** of coal (GCV = **25,000 kJ/kg**) and generates **18,000 kg/h** of superheated steam at **h_sh = 3200 kJ/kg** from feedwater at **h_fw = 800 kJ/kg**. Compute η_boiler.

### 3.2.2 Derivation Walkthrough

**Step 1 — Define efficiency.**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{\text{Heat absorbed by water/steam}}{\text{Heat released by fuel}}$$

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{\dot{m}_s (h_{sh} - h_{fw})}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot GCV}$$

**Step 2 — Compute enthalpy rise.**

$$h_{sh} - h_{fw} = 3200 - 800 = 2400 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute heat absorbed per hour.**

$$\dot{Q}_{abs} = 18000 \cdot 2400 = 4.32 \times 10^7 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat supplied per hour.**

$$\dot{Q}_{fuel} = 2000 \cdot 25000 = 5.0 \times 10^7 \;\text{kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 — Compute efficiency.**

$$\eta_{boiler} = \frac{4.32 \times 10^7}{5.0 \times 10^7} = 0.864$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{boiler} = 86.4 \;\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering comment:** A η of 86.4 % (HHV basis) is **acceptable for an older subcritical unit**. Modern supercritical units push this to **> 92 %** through economizer + APH + reheat.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Evaporation

### 3.3.1 Statement
Convert the above boiler's output to **"from and at 100 °C"** equivalent evaporation. (Latent heat at 100 °C, h_fg(100°C) = **2257 kJ/kg**.)

### 3.3.2 Derivation

$$\dot{m}_{equiv} = \dot{m}_s \cdot \frac{h_{sh} - h_{fw@100}}{2257}$$

Assuming feedwater at **100 °C** has h_fw@100 = **419 kJ/kg**:

$$h_{sh} - h_{fw@100} = 3200 - 419 = 2781 \;\text{kJ/kg}$$

$$\dot{m}_{equiv} = 18000 \cdot \frac{2781}{2257} = 18000 \cdot 1.232 = 22180 \;\text{kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{equiv} \approx 22.18 \;\text{tonnes/h of "from & at 100 °C" steam}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is "equivalent evaporation" used?**
> It is a **standardized performance benchmark**. A boiler's true output is meaningless without specifying *the conditions* under which the steam was produced. The "from-and-at-100°C" figure allows engineers to **compare two different boilers head-to-head**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stoichiometric Air & Combustion Air Flow

### 3.4.1 Statement
A furnace burns **coal with composition** C = 70 %, H₂ = 5 %, S = 2 %, O₂ = 4 %, ash = 12 %, moisture = 7 % (all by mass). Stoichiometric oxygen required: **2.667·C + 8·H₂ + S – O₂** (kg O₂ per kg fuel, ignoring N₂ in fuel). Air is **23 % O₂ by mass**. Excess air α = **1.20**. Compute combustion air flow per kg of fuel.

### 3.4.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ per kg fuel.**

$$O_2^{st} = 2.667(0.70) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.02) - 0.04$$

$$= 1.867 + 0.40 + 0.02 - 0.04 = 2.247 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric air per kg fuel.**

$$A_0 = \frac{O_2^{st}}{0.23} = \frac{2.247}{0.23} = 9.77 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Actual air with α = 1.20.**

$$A_{actual} = \alpha \cdot A_0 = 1.20 \cdot 9.77 = 11.72 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{A_{actual} \approx 11.72 \;\text{kg air per kg of coal}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PEICT523 link:** This air-flow demand is the **primary setpoint** for the FD (Forced Draft) fan speed controller and the **secondary input** to the cross-limited air-fuel ratio controller.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Heat Loss vs. Excess Air Trade-off (Derivation of Optimal α)

### 3.5.1 Statement
Show that total heat loss is a sum of (a) dry flue gas loss and (b) loss due to incomplete combustion, and that it has a **minimum at α ≈ 1.15 – 1.20**.

### 3.5.2 Derivation

Let:
- $C_{fg}$ = specific heat of flue gas ≈ **1.05 kJ/kg·K**
- $T_{fg}$ = flue gas temperature at APH outlet
- $T_{amb}$ = ambient combustion air temperature
- $A_0$ = stoichiometric air per kg fuel
- $L_C$ = loss due to unburnt carbon (≈ 0 at α > 1.1)

$$L_{total} = \underbrace{A \cdot C_{fg} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{amb})}_{L_1 \; \text{(dry flue gas loss)}} + \underbrace{L_C}_{\text{incomplete comb.}} + \underbrace{\dots}_{L_{moisture}}$$

Now $A = \alpha A_0$, so:

$$L_1 = \alpha A_0 C_{fg} (T_{fg} - T_{amb})$$

**L₁ is a linearly increasing function of α.**

As α → 1, L_C increases sharply (insufficient O₂ for complete combustion).
As α → ∞, L₁ → ∞.

Hence, $L_{total}(\alpha)$ has a **single minimum**. Differentiating and solving:

$$\frac{d L_{total}}{d\alpha} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha_{opt} \approx 1.15 \text{ to } 1.20$$

$$\boxed{\alpha_{opt} \approx 1.18 \;\text{(typical for bituminous coal in PF-fired utility boiler)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **PEICT523 link:** The O₂ trim controller in a DCS continuously drives α toward α_opt by adjusting FD/ID fan speeds based on **in-situ O₂ probe (zirconia-cell) feedback** at the economizer outlet.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix — Quick-Reference KTU Numbers

| Boiler Type | Pressure Class | η Range | Capacity | Drum? | Key Risk |
|-------------|----------------|---------|----------|-------|----------|
| **Cochran (fire-tube)** | Low (≤ 10 bar) | 60 – 70 % | ≤ 3 TPH | Yes (shell) | Shell explosion |
| **Lancashire (fire-tube)** | Low (≤ 16 bar) | 65 – 75 % | ≤ 8 TPH | Yes (shell) | Hot spot on internal flues |
| **Package (fire-tube)** | Low–Medium (≤ 18 bar) | 75 – 82 % | 1 – 30 TPH | Yes | Scale on tube OD |
| **B&W (water-tube)** | High (≤ 100 bar) | 80 – 88 % | 10 – 500 TPH | Yes | Drum-level excursions |
| **Stirling (water-tube)** | High (≤ 100 bar) | 80 – 88 % | 10 – 500 TPH | Yes | Circulation reversal at low load |
| **LaMont (forced)** | High (≤ 200 bar) | 85 – 90 % | 100 – 700 TPH | Yes | BCP failure → tube burnout |
| **Benson (once-through)** | Sub/Super-critical (≤ 600 bar) | **90 – 94 %** | 200 – 3000 TPH | **No** | Flow mismatch at part load |
| **Supercritical OT** | Supercritical (≥ 225 bar) | **92 – 94 %** | 500 – 3000 TPH | **No** | Pseudoboiler transition control |

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between fire-tube and water-tube boilers with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Fire-Tube | Water-Tube |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Hot gases in | **Tubes** | **Shell / furnace space** |
| Water in | Shell | **Tubes** |
| Pressure limit | **≤ 20 bar** | **up to 600 bar** |
| Capacity | Low (≤ 10 TPH) | High (≥ 500 TPH) |
| Examples | Cochran, Lancashire, Locomotive | Babcock & Wilcox, Stirling, Benson |

(1 mark for any two clear, correct differences; 1 mark for examples; 1 mark for neat comparison format.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Why is natural circulation impossible in a supercritical boiler?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Natural circulation depends on the **density difference** $\Delta\rho = \rho_{w} - \rho_{m}$ between the downcomer (cold water) and the riser (hot water–steam mixture).

At the **critical point (P_c = 221.2 bar, T_c = 374.1 °C)**, the densities of saturated liquid and saturated vapour **converge to a single value of 322 kg/m³**. Therefore, $\Delta\rho \to 0$ and the **driving head vanishes**.

Since natural circulation requires a non-zero $\Delta\rho$, a supercritical boiler **must** use either a **forced-circulation pump** (impossible at > 225 bar due to compression work) or, more practically, a **once-through (Benson-type) design** with no drum. Hence, supercritical boilers are **always once-through**.

(1 mark: critical point mention; 1 mark: density convergence; 1 mark: Benson/once-through conclusion.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

#### (a) Classify boilers based on (i) tube content, (ii) circulation, and (iii) pressure. Discuss the working principle of a **natural circulation water-tube boiler** with a neat sketch. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**(i) Tube-content classification (1 mark):**
- Fire-tube: hot gases inside tubes
- Water-tube: water inside tubes

**(ii) Circulation classification (1 mark):**
- Natural, assisted, forced, once-through

**(iii) Pressure classification (1 mark):**
- Low (≤ 10 bar), medium (10–40 bar), high (40–100 bar), supercritical (> 225 bar)

**Working of natural circulation water-tube boiler (4 marks):**
1. Feedwater enters the **economizer** → heated to near-saturation.
2. Water enters the **steam drum** from the top.
3. From the drum, water flows down through **unheated downcomers** to the **mud drum** (or headers at the bottom).
4. The water rises through the **riser tubes** lining the furnace walls, absorbing radiant and convective heat.
5. Density of riser mixture decreases as steam forms; this **density difference** between the cold downcomer and hot riser generates a **natural driving head**:
$$\Delta P_{nat} = H \cdot g \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_m)$$
6. The water–steam mixture re-enters the drum, where **steam is separated** in the cyclone separators and fed to the **superheater**, while saturated water recirculates.

*(Sketch expectation: 2 marks — drum at top, mud drum at bottom, downcomers on the outside, water-wall tubes on the furnace, with arrows for flow direction.)*

---

#### (b) A boiler generates **15,000 kg/h** of steam at **h_sh = 3000 kJ/kg** from feedwater at **h_fw = 700 kJ/kg**, using **1500 kg/h** of coal with **GCV = 24,000 kJ/kg**. Compute (i) the **boiler efficiency**, (ii) the **equivalent evaporation** (from & at 100 °C, h_fg(100°C) = 2257 kJ/kg, h_fw@100 = 419 kJ/kg), and (iii) the **evaporation ratio**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**(i) Boiler efficiency (3 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{\dot{m}_s (h_{sh} - h_{fw})}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot GCV}$$

$$= \frac{15000 \cdot (3000 - 700)}{1500 \cdot 24000} = \frac{15000 \cdot 2300}{3.6 \times 10^7} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^7}{3.6 \times 10^7}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 0.9583 = 95.83 \;\%}$$

*(Note: This is unusually high; the data is illustrative. A more realistic 86–92 % should be expected in real KTU papers — examiners accept the calculated value if the data is as given.)*

**(ii) Equivalent evaporation (2 marks):**

$$\dot{m}_{equiv} = 15000 \cdot \frac{3000 - 419}{2257} = 15000 \cdot \frac{2581}{2257} = 15000 \cdot 1.1436$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{equiv} = 17,154 \;\text{kg/h} \approx 17.15 \;\text{tonnes/h}}$$

**(iii) Evaporation ratio (2 marks):**

$$\text{ER} = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_{fuel}} = \frac{15000}{1500} = 10$$

$$\boxed{\text{ER} = 10 \;\text{kg steam per kg coal}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

#### (a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the **working of a once-through (Benson) boiler**. Discuss why it is preferred for **supercritical operation**. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
```
[Boiler Feed Pump] → [Economizer] → [Evaporator / Transition Section]
→ [Superheater] → [Reheater] → [Turbine]
[Combustion] ↑ supplies heat to all four sections in series.
```

**Working steps (3 marks):**
1. BFP delivers feedwater at **> 225 bar** and **240 – 290 °C** into the economizer.
2. Water progressively absorbs heat; **no distinct phase change drum exists** because the boiler is supercritical.
3. As the fluid passes through the transition zone, the **specific volume rises continuously**; the system transitions smoothly from liquid-like to vapour-like behaviour without a saturated dome.
4. Steam leaves the superheater at **> 600 °C** and is delivered to the HP turbine.
5. Exhaust steam from HP (cold reheat) is returned to the **reheater**, then to IP/LP turbines.

**Why Benson is preferred for supercritical operation (1 mark):**
- Natural circulation fails near the critical point (Δρ → 0).
- A **drum is impossible** to fabricate economically for supercritical pressures.
- A once-through design **eliminates the drum entirely**, allowing continuous, smooth transition through the critical point.

---

#### (b) A pulverized-coal-fired furnace burns coal at **3000 kg/h** with composition **C = 65 %, H₂ = 4 %, S = 1 %, O₂ = 5 %, N₂ = 1 %, ash = 14 %, moisture = 10 %** (mass basis). Stoichiometric oxygen = **2.667·C + 8·H₂ + S – O₂** (kg O₂/kg fuel). Air contains **23 % O₂** by mass. **Excess air α = 1.18**. Compute (i) stoichiometric air per kg fuel, (ii) actual combustion air per kg fuel, and (iii) total flue gas flow per hour (neglect unburnt carbon, assume all moisture and ash leave with flue gas). **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**(i) Stoichiometric O₂ and air per kg fuel (3 marks):**

$$O_2^{st} = 2.667(0.65) + 8(0.04) + 1(0.01) - 0.05$$

$$= 1.7336 + 0.32 + 0.01 - 0.05 = 2.0136 \;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$A_0 = \frac{O_2^{st}}{0.23} = \frac{2.0136}{0.23} = 8.755 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

$$\boxed{A_0 \approx 8.76 \;\text{kg/kg}}$$

**(ii) Actual air per kg fuel (2 marks):**

$$A_{act} = \alpha A_0 = 1.18 \cdot 8.755 = 10.33 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**(iii) Total flue gas flow (2 marks):**

Flue gas = fuel + air (since unburnt ≈ 0)

$$\dot{m}_{flue} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot (1 + A_{act}) = 3000 \cdot (1 + 10.33) = 3000 \cdot 11.33$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{flue} = 33,990 \;\text{kg/h} \approx 34 \;\text{tonnes/h}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic (Module 2 — Boiler Types):**
> 1. **Confusing the *critical* pressure with the *typical supercritical* operating pressure.** Critical P is **221.2 bar**; supercritical units typically operate at **250 – 600 bar**. Writing "225 bar" as critical is a mark-loser.
> 2. **Mixing units in efficiency questions** — kJ/kg vs kcal/kg, kg/h vs TPH. Always state the unit *in the answer line*. Examiners deduct a half-mark for ambiguous units.
> 3. **Drawing a "Babcock & Wilcox" with vertical tubes** — the B&W is *inclined* (45°). Vertical bent-tube designs are Stirling variants. One mark lost for geometry.
> 4. **Skipping the steam drum in the Benson diagram.** The Benson boiler has **NO drum**; many students draw a drum and lose the **once-through** mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract fuel oxygen** in stoichiometric O₂ calculation. Use $2.667 C + 8 H_2 + S - O_2$ — the "-O₂" is the trap.
> 6. **Writing "natural circulation works at 250 bar"** — false. Beyond ~ 180 bar, **assisted** circulation is mandatory, and beyond **221.2 bar**, **once-through** is the only choice.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Boiler Types (Module 2, PEICT523)**

- [x] A **boiler** is a closed pressure vessel that converts feedwater into steam using heat from fuel combustion.
- [x] **Fire-tube boilers:** hot gases inside tubes; water in shell; pressure ≤ 20 bar; examples: Cochran, Lancashire, Locomotive, Package.
- [x] **Water-tube boilers:** water inside tubes; hot gases around them; pressure up to 600 bar; examples: B&W, Stirling, LaMont, Benson.
- [x] **Natural circulation** works only when $\rho_w \neq \rho_m$; driving head $\Delta P = Hg(\rho_w - \rho_m)$.
- [x] **Critical point:** **P_c = 221.2 bar**, **T_c = 374.1 °C** — natural circulation **ceases** here.
- [x] **Assisted circulation** uses a BCP for ~ 30 – 40 % of driving head; suitable up to **180 bar**.
- [x] **Forced circulation (LaMont):** pump provides **100 %** of driving head; suitable up to **200 bar**.
- [x] **Once-through (Benson):** **no drum**; suitable from subcritical to supercritical; **flow and firing rate must be cross-limited** for safety.
- [x] **Boiler efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s (h_{sh} - h_{fw})}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot GCV}$
- [x] **Equivalent evaporation** normalizes steam output to "from-and-at-100 °C" benchmark.
- [x] **Evaporation ratio** = steam per fuel; typical **8 – 12 kg steam/kg coal** in PF-fired units.
- [x] **Optimum excess air** for bituminous coal: **α ≈ 1.15 – 1.20** (balances dry flue gas loss vs. unburnt carbon loss).
- [x] **Stoichiometric air per kg coal (typical):** **7 – 10 kg air/kg fuel**; with α = 1.18, **8 – 12 kg air/kg fuel**.
- [x] Modern utility boiler **efficiency:** **88 – 94 %** (HHV); supercritical + reheating + APH + economizer + ESP.
- [x] **Instrumentation links (PEICT523 focus):** drum-level tri-redundancy, O₂ trim, FD/ID cross-limit, superheat spray attemperation, furnace draft, soot-blower sequencing.
- [x] KTU Module 2 focus areas for exam: **(a) classification, (b) fire-tube vs. water-tube, (c) natural vs. once-through, (d) efficiency calculation, (e) stoichiometric air & excess air.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiler - Turbine Units and Its Range Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boiler - Turbine Units and Its Range Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ MODULE 2: BOILER – TURBINE UNITS AND ITS RANGE SYSTEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
In a modern thermal power station, the **Boiler–Turbine (B–T) Unit** refers to the integrated energy conversion assembly in which the heat released by combustion in the boiler is used to generate superheated steam, which is then expanded through a steam turbine coupled to a generator to produce electrical power. The **Boiler–Turbine Coordinated Control System (B–T CCS)** is the master automation hierarchy that regulates the firing rate (fuel/air flow), feedwater flow, steam temperature, and turbine governor valve opening to ensure that the **net power output** matches the grid demand under all operating regimes — from sub-megawatt start-up to **100% Continuous Maximum Rating (CMR)** of **500 MW** or **660 MW** typical KTU-mapped supercritical units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The B–T unit is the *single largest controlled process* in any thermal station. KTU examiners consistently test the **three modes of load tracking**: Boiler-Following, Turbine-Following, and Coordinated Control.

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Cooker Kitchen" Intuition
Imagine two chefs cooking for a banquet:
- **Chef Boiler** controls the *rate at which curry is produced* (steam flow, pressure, firing rate). He is slow — takes 3–5 minutes to react.
- **Chef Turbine** controls the *rate at which food is served to guests* (power to the grid). He is fast — reacts in 0.5–2 seconds via the governor valve.

If the banquet host (the **grid dispatcher**) suddenly says "Serve faster!":
- *Option 1:* Tell Chef Boiler to cook faster → but guests wait while the curry is being made (**Boiler-Following Mode**, slow).
- *Option 2:* Tell Chef Turbine to open the serving hatch wider → instant response, but the curry pot will empty soon (**Turbine-Following Mode**, fast but unsustainable).
- *Option 3:* Tell *both* chefs in a coordinated way → instant turbine response + steady boiler ramp-up (**Coordinated Control Mode**, the KTU-favoured industrial standard).

This simple analogy underpins the entire module.

### 🔑 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Supercritical 660 MW) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Main Steam Pressure** | **250 kg/cm² (24.5 MPa)** | MPa |
| **Main Steam Temperature** | **540 °C / 565 °C** | °C |
| **Reheat Steam Temperature** | **565 °C** | °C |
| **Boiler Efficiency (η)** | **88–92 %** | % |
| **Turbine Heat Rate** | **1,950–2,050 kcal/kWh** | kcal/kWh |
| **CMR (Continuous Max Rating)** | **500 / 660 / 800 MW** | MW |

> [!NOTE]
> A **superheated steam temperature of 540 °C** is the *thermodynamic sweet spot* — above this, steel creep in the boiler tubes becomes the limiting design factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Boiler–Turbine Energy Conversion Chain

The B–T unit is governed by a strict **causal cascade** of energy states:

1. **Chemical Energy** in coal/dust → released in the furnace.
2. **Thermal Energy** in flue gas → transferred through water walls (radiation) and platen/shield banks (convection).
3. **Internal Energy** of water/steam → latent heat absorption in evaporator + superheat.
4. **Kinetic + Pressure Energy** in the HP/IP/LP turbine stages.
5. **Mechanical Energy** on the turbine shaft → electrical energy at the generator terminals.

Each transition has its own **time constant**:
- Furnace: 2–4 s
- Steam generator (water-steam): 30–60 s
- Superheater: 60–120 s
- Reheater: 120–240 s
- Turbine (governor valve): **0.3–1.0 s** ← *fastest in the chain*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **mismatch in response times** is the *root cause* of why we need three control modes instead of one.

## 2.2 The Three Control Modes — Logic Steps

### 🔷 Mode 1: **Boiler-Following Mode (B–F)**
- **Operating Logic:** Grid demand → Turbine first responds → then Boiler catches up.
- **Use Case:** Large, slow boiler units (subcritical, ≥ 200 MW) with strong grid frequency support requirement.
- **Drawback:** Pressure swing of ±10–15 kg/cm² during load changes → tube metal fatigue.
- **Speed of Response:** Slow (5–10 minutes for full swing).

### 🔷 Mode 2: **Turbine-Following Mode (T–F)**
- **Operating Logic:** Grid demand → Boiler first changes firing rate → then Turbine governor yields.
- **Use Case:** Peak-load plants, cycling duty, hydro-steam combined stations.
- **Advantage:** Stable steam pressure → long boiler life.
- **Drawback:** Slow electrical response (3–6 min for 5 % load step).

### 🔷 Mode 3: **Coordinated Control Mode (CC) — INDUSTRY STANDARD**
- **Operating Logic:** Both actuators receive a *corrected* demand signal derived from the error between **power demand (MWd)** and **actual power (MWa)** plus the **pressure deviation (ΔP)**.
- **Key Idea:** Turbine handles the *fast component* of load error; Boiler handles the *slow* component plus pressure regulation.
- **Block Diagram Triad:** `Load Demand → Summing Junction → PI/PID Controllers → (Boiler + Turbine Actuators)`.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield — appear in almost every Module 2 question paper.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_e = \eta_{B} \cdot \eta_{T} \cdot \dot{m}_{f} \cdot GCV$ | Net electrical power from fuel | **MW** |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{P_e \times 860}{\eta_{T}(h_{1}-h_{2})}$ | Steam mass flow rate from power | **kg/h** |
| 3 | $\Delta h = h_{1}-h_{2} = c_{p}(T_{1}-T_{2}) + L$ | Enthalpy drop (sensible + latent) | **kJ/kg** |
| 4 | $P_{\text{demand}} = P_{\text{base}} + P_{\text{AGC}} + P_{\text{freq}}$ | Composite load demand | **MW** |
| 5 | $e(t) = P_{d}(t) - P_{a}(t) + K_{p}\,\Delta P(t)$ | CC error signal | **MW** |
| 6 | $G_{B}(s) = \dfrac{K_{B}}{(1+\tau_{B}s)}$ | Boiler transfer function (1st-order lag) | — |
| 7 | $G_{T}(s) = \dfrac{K_{T}}{(1+\tau_{T}s)}$ | Turbine transfer function (faster lag) | — |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{P_{\max}-P_{\min}}{P_{\text{nom}}} \times 100$ | Range of a measuring instrument | **%** |
| 9 | $f_{\text{NEMA}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{turndown}}}{R_{\text{range}}}$ | Turndown ratio (NEMA definition) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $B = \dfrac{P_{\max}}{P_{\min}}$ | Turndown ratio (boiler firing) | dimensionless |

> Where: $\dot{m}_{f}$ = fuel flow (kg/s), $GCV$ = Gross Calorific Value (kJ/kg), $c_{p}$ = specific heat (kJ/kg·K), $L$ = latent heat (kJ/kg), $K_{p}$ = pressure bias gain, $\tau_{B},\tau_{T}$ = time constants (s).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering
- **Power plant DCS engineers** use these formulas to tune the master controller during commissioning.
- **NTPC, BHEL, and Reliance Energy** mandate **Coordinated Control** on all supercritical units commissioned after 2015.
- **KTU recruiters (L&T, Tata Power, Schneider Electric)** routinely ask Mode-selection logic interviews.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steam Mass Flow from Power Demand

### **Given:**
- Net electrical output: $P_e$ in kW
- Turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{T}$
- Enthalpy drop: $\Delta h = h_{1}-h_{2}$ in kJ/kg
- Mechanical–electrical efficiency: $\eta_{m} \approx 0.98$

### **Step 1 — Energy Equivalence on the Turbine Shaft**

The mechanical power extracted by the turbine equals the rate of enthalpy change of the steam passing through it.

$$
P_{\text{mech}} = \dot{m}_{s} \cdot \Delta h \cdot \eta_{T}
$$

*Explanation:* Each kilogram of steam gives up $\Delta h$ kJ of enthalpy; only the fraction $\eta_{T}$ of that is converted to useful mechanical work.

### **Step 2 — Convert Mechanical Power to Electrical Power**

$$
P_e = P_{\text{mech}} \cdot \eta_{m} = \dot{m}_{s} \cdot \Delta h \cdot \eta_{T} \cdot \eta_{m}
$$

### **Step 3 — Solve for Mass Flow Rate**

$$
\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{P_e}{\eta_{T} \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot \Delta h}
$$

### **Step 4 — Add the 860 Conversion (kW·h ↔ kcal convention used in India)**

In Indian power-plant practice, the heat rate is often expressed in **kcal/kWh**, so $1 \text{ kW·h} = 860 \text{ kcal}$.

$$
\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{P_{e(\text{kW})} \times 860}{\eta_{T} \cdot (h_{1}-h_{2})}
$$

where $(h_{1}-h_{2})$ is now in **kcal/kg**.

### **Step 5 — Numerical Illustration**

*For a 660 MW unit, $P_e = 660{,}000$ kW, $\eta_{T} = 0.85$, $\Delta h = 300$ kcal/kg:*

$$
\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{660{,}000 \times 860}{0.85 \times 300}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{5.676 \times 10^{8}}{255} = 2.226 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/h}
$$

*Conclusion:* A 660 MW unit needs **~2,226 tonnes of steam per hour** — this is the design steam flow to size HP/IP/LP turbine blading.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Range System Span & Turndown Ratio

### **Definition of Range (KTU 2024 preferred phrasing):**
The **range** of a measurement or control system is the spread between its **lowest** and **highest** operating values that produce a usable, calibrated output.

$$
R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}
$$

### **Definition of Span:**
Span is the **algebraic difference** between the upper-range value (URV) and the lower-range value (LRV).

$$
S = URV - LRV
$$

### **Definition of Turndown Ratio (Boiler context):**
The maximum-to-minimum capacity ratio at which the boiler can operate stably without support fuel (oil).

$$
B = \dfrac{P_{\text{max}}}{P_{\text{min}}}
$$

### **Worked Example — Boiler Range Selection for a 500 MW Unit**

Suppose the boiler must operate from **40 % to 100 %** of CMR:
- $P_{\max} = 500$ MW
- $P_{\min} = 0.40 \times 500 = 200$ MW
- $B = 500 / 200 = \mathbf{2.5}$ (i.e., 2.5 : 1 turndown)
