For 2-coal-blend firing with widely varying GCV, the **true turndown** at the burner level is typically:

$$
B_{\text{burner}} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f,\max}}{\dot{m}_{f,\min}} \approx 4
$$

This is the basis for the **3-burner-tier** design: **Warm-up (oil) → Low-fire (2 mills) → High-fire (4–6 mills)**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mode Selection Logic

| Condition | Recommended Mode | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Grid frequency dropping rapidly | **Turbine-Following** | Fast MW injection needed |
| Boiler tube ageing, frequent cycling | **Turbine-Following** | Pressure kept stable |
| Supercritical unit, 100 % participation in AGC | **Coordinated Control** | Both fast + stable |
| Start-up / low load (< 30 %) | **Manual / Boiler-Following** | Burners not stable yet |
| Two units in parallel, base load | **Boiler-Following** | Simple, robust |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Coordinated Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Grid AGC Load Demand MWd] --> B[Summing Junction 1]
    P[Pressure Setpoint Psp] --> Q[Pressure Comparator]
    Q --> B
    B --> C[Error Signal e_t]
    C --> D[Turbine PI Controller]
    C --> E[Boiler PI Controller]
    D --> F[Governor Valve Actuator]
    E --> G[Fuel Air Damper Actuator]
    F --> H[Steam Turbine]
    G --> I[Boiler Furnace]
    H --> J[Generator Power Output MWa]
    I --> K[Steam Pressure Feedback]
    K --> P
    J --> L[Frequency Feedback to Grid]
    J --> B
```

**Reading Guide:**
- `B` is the *central* summing junction where **power error** and **pressure error** are combined.
- The **turbine path** (top) is fast; the **boiler path** (bottom) is slow.
- Feedback loop `J → B` closes the load-demand tracking loop.

---

## 4.2 Range System Hierarchy — Narrow / Wide / Ultra-Wide

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Boiler Drum Level] --> B1[Narrow Range 0 to 50 mm]
    A --> B2[Wide Range minus 200 to plus 200 mm]
    A --> B3[Ultra Wide Range minus 300 to plus 300 mm]
    B1 --> C1[Used for Normal Control 3 element]
    B2 --> C2[Used for Start-up and Shutdown]
    B3 --> C3[Used for Trip and Safety Interlocks]
    C1 --> D[DCS Alarm and Trip Logic]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
```

**Reading Guide:** A typical **drum-level measurement** uses **three independent transmitters** with overlapping spans — this is a KTU classic range-system question.

---

## 4.3 Boiler–Turbine Unit Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    FUEL[Coal Handling Plant] --> MILL[Pulveriser Mill]
    MILL --> FURN[Furnace Combustion]
    FURN --> EVAP[Evaporator Water Walls]
    EVAP --> SH[Superheater]
    SH --> HPV[HP Turbine Governor Valve]
    HPV --> HPT[HP Turbine]
    HPT --> RH[Reheater]
    RH --> IPT[IP Turbine]
    IPT --> LPT[LP Turbine]
    LPT --> GEN[Synchronous Generator]
    GEN --> GRID[400 kV Grid Bus]
    FW[Feedwater Pump] --> EVAP
    AIR[Forced Draft Fan] --> FURN
    ID[Induced Draft Fan] --> FURN
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Boiler–Turbine Coordinated Control" and list the three basic modes of load regulation.**

**Model Answer:**
Coordinated control is the master control strategy in a thermal unit where the **boiler firing rate** and the **turbine governor valve** are simultaneously manipulated by independent but cross-coupled controllers, both receiving a common **load-error signal** derived from the difference between demanded and actual power, augmented by a **steam-pressure bias term**.

The three modes are:
1. **Boiler-Following Mode** — turbine first, boiler follows.
2. **Turbine-Following Mode** — boiler first, turbine follows.
3. **Coordinated Control Mode** — both manipulated simultaneously with cross-coupling.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Range" and "Span" as applied to process instruments. Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Range** is the spread of measured values over which an instrument is designed to operate, expressed as the difference between maximum and minimum measurable values. *Example:* A pressure transmitter with range **0–250 kg/cm²**.
- **Span** is the algebraic difference between the **upper-range value** and the **lower-range value** of the instrument. *Example:* For the above transmitter, **Span = 250 − 0 = 250 kg/cm²**.

---

## 📝 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Boiler–Turbine Coordinated Control** scheme. Clearly identify the role of the pressure-error and power-error signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **500 MW subcritical unit** operates at a throttle steam pressure of **170 kg/cm²** and temperature **540 °C**. The condenser pressure is **0.1 kg/cm²** with a turbine isentropic efficiency of **0.86**. If the boiler efficiency is **0.90** and the coal GCV is **3,500 kcal/kg**, calculate:
- (i) The steam mass flow rate per hour.
- (ii) The coal consumption per hour.

Use $h_{1}=812$ kcal/kg, $h_{2}=580$ kcal/kg, and mechanical–electrical efficiency = 0.98. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### **Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution**

**Block Diagram (textual reproduction for answer sheet):**

$$
\text{Load Demand (MWd)} \rightarrow [+] \rightarrow e_1 \rightarrow \text{Turbine PI} \rightarrow \text{Governor Valve}
$$

$$
\text{Pressure Deviation }(\Delta P) \rightarrow [+] \rightarrow e_2 \rightarrow \text{Boiler PI} \rightarrow \text{Fuel Damper}
$$

**Explanation Step-by-Step (write 5–6 lines in exam):**

1. The **grid load demand** (MWd) is compared with the **actual power output** (MWa) at the master summing junction → generates a **power error** $e_p = \text{MWd} - \text{MWa}$.
2. A **steam-pressure feedback** is taken from the main steam header and compared with the **pressure setpoint** → produces a **pressure error** $e_{\Delta p}$.
3. The two errors are **decoupled** through cross-coupling coefficients: a fraction of $e_p$ is fed to the boiler loop, and a fraction of $e_{\Delta p}$ is fed to the turbine loop.
4. The **turbine PI controller** processes the high-frequency component → opens/closes the **governor valve** within 0.5–2 s (fast path).
5. The **boiler PI controller** processes the low-frequency component → adjusts **fuel, air, and feedwater** flows over 30–60 s (slow path).
6. The combined action keeps **steam pressure** within ±2 kg/cm² of setpoint while **tracking the load ramp** accurately.

**Key Lines to Memorize for 7/7:**
- "Turbine loop → fast electrical response."
- "Boiler loop → slow but pressure-stable response."
- "Cross-coupling decouples MW and kg/cm² disturbances."

---

### **Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution**

**Given Data:**
- $P_e = 500$ MW $= 5 \times 10^5$ kW
- $\Delta h = h_{1} - h_{2} = 812 - 580 = 232$ kcal/kg
- $\eta_T = 0.86$, $\eta_m = 0.98$, $\eta_B = 0.90$, $GCV = 3500$ kcal/kg

**Step 1 — Apply the master steam-flow formula:**

$$
\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{P_e \times 860}{\eta_T \cdot (h_1 - h_2)}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{5} \times 860}{0.86 \times 232}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{4.30 \times 10^{8}}{199.52} = 2.155 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Answer (i):** Steam flow = **2,155,200 kg/h ≈ 2,155 T/h**

**Step 2 — Coal consumption:**

Energy in steam per hour = $\dot{m}_{s} \cdot \Delta h$ kcal/h

$$
E_{\text{steam}} = 2.155 \times 10^{6} \times 232 = 5.00 \times 10^{8} \text{ kcal/h}
$$

Energy supplied by coal = $E_{\text{coal}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{steam}}}{\eta_B}$

$$
E_{\text{coal}} = \dfrac{5.00 \times 10^{8}}{0.90} = 5.556 \times 10^{8} \text{ kcal/h}
$$

Coal flow:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{coal}}}{GCV} = \dfrac{5.556 \times 10^{8}}{3500} = 1.587 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg/h}
$$

**Answer (ii):** Coal consumption ≈ **158.7 T/h**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare **Boiler-Following**, **Turbine-Following**, and **Coordinated Control** modes in terms of: speed of response, pressure stability, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Narrow Range, Wide Range, and Ultra-Wide Range** systems used in **drum-level measurement**. Why are three transmitters used? What is the typical overlap? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Comparison Table (write in exam):**

| Criterion | Boiler-Following | Turbine-Following | Coordinated Control |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed of load response** | Slow (5–10 min) | Moderate (2–5 min) | Fast (0.5–2 min) |
| **Steam pressure stability** | Poor (ΔP = 10–15 kg/cm²) | Excellent (ΔP < 2 kg/cm²) | Good (ΔP < 3 kg/cm²) |
| **Typical application** | Large base-load units, nuclear | Peak-load, cycling units | Supercritical 500/660/800 MW AGC units |
| **Boiler stress** | Low (steady firing) | High (rapid firing changes) | Moderate |
| **Turbine stress** | Moderate | Low | Moderate |

**Conclusion line:** *Coordinated Control is the industry standard because it combines fast electrical response with acceptable pressure stability.*

---

### **Part (b) — Range Systems in Drum Level Measurement:**

**Why three transmitters?**
A single transmitter cannot cover the entire operating envelope:
- **At low load**, water level is near normal and noise is small → **narrow range** is sufficient.
- **At start-up/shut-down**, level can swing from empty to full → **wide range** required.
- **During trip conditions**, sudden swell or shrink can exceed wide-range limits → **ultra-wide range** needed for safety logic.

**Typical Specifications:**

| System | Span | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Narrow Range (NR)** | **−50 to +50 mm** (or 0 to 100 mm) | Normal 3-element drum-level control |
| **Wide Range (WR)** | **−200 to +200 mm** | Start-up, low-load operation |
| **Ultra-Wide Range (UWR)** | **−300 to +300 mm** | Trip, MFT, safety interlocks |

**Overlap design:** Each successive range overlaps the previous by **~20–30 %** to ensure no blind spot during transient level crossings.

**Line to write in exam:** *"The three-transmitter philosophy follows the KTU-emphasized principle of redundancy and segregation between control and safety loops."*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 860 conversion factor** in the steam-flow formula → lose 1 mark per numerical.
> 2. **Confusing "Range" with "Span"** → part (a) definitions are worth 2 marks each.
> 3. **Drawing the block diagram without labelling the cross-coupling arrows** → lose 2 marks in part (a).
> 4. **Not stating units** in numerical answers (kg/h vs T/h) → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Writing only the table in Q B(a) without a conclusion line** → KTU 2024 rubric awards 1 mark for the conclusion.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **B–T Unit** = integrated boiler + steam turbine + generator assembly controlled by DCS.
- ✅ **Three Modes:** Boiler-Following (slow, stable), Turbine-Following (fast, unstable pressure), Coordinated (balanced).
- ✅ **Coordinated Control** uses a **master summing junction** with both **power error** and **pressure error**.
- ✅ **Turbine time constant** ≈ 0.3–1 s ; **Boiler time constant** ≈ 30–60 s (mismatch is the design driver).
- ✅ **Range** = max − min measurable value ; **Span** = URV − LRV.
- ✅ **Turndown Ratio** for boilers typically 3:1 to 4:1.
- ✅ **Drum Level** uses three transmitters: **Narrow, Wide, Ultra-Wide** with overlap.
- ✅ **Steam flow formula:** $\dot{m}_{s} = \dfrac{P_e \times 860}{\eta_T \cdot \Delta h}$ — must use **860** (Indian convention).
- ✅ **Coal flow formula:** $\dot{m}_{c} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{s} \cdot \Delta h}{GCV \cdot \eta_B}$.
- ✅ **AGC** = Automatic Generation Control — the grid signal that drives the load demand input.
- ✅ Always write the **conclusion sentence** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Memorize the **three-mode comparison table** — it is a high-frequency KTU 2024 question.
- ✅ **Superheated steam temperature = 540 °C** is the design sweet spot.

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Semester Exam! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Water Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Water Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Feed Water System** in a thermal power plant is the integrated network of equipment, piping, instrumentation, and controls responsible for delivering high-purity, deaerated, pressurized, and temperature-conditioned water to the steam generator (boiler) at the precise mass flow rate, pressure, and enthalpy demanded by the steam cycle. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the feed water train spans the **condensate extraction system → low-pressure feedwater heaters → deaerator → boiler feed pumps (BFP) → high-pressure feedwater heaters → economizer → boiler drum**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT523 – Module 2):**
> Feed Water Systems is a core instrumentation topic that bridges *process equipment* (pumps, deaerators, heaters) with *measurement & control loops* (level, flow, temperature, and dissolved oxygen transmitters). Expect at least one 14-mark Part-B question on this topic in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the feed water system as the **"circulatory system" of a power plant**, analogous to how blood circulates oxygenated fluid from the lungs to tissues in the human body:

| Human Circulatory System | Power Plant Feed Water System |
|---|---|
| **Heart** | Boiler Feed Pump (BFP) |
| **Lungs** (purify & oxygenate blood) | **Deaerator** (purify & de-gasify water) |
| **Capillary pre-warmers** | **Low-Pressure Feedwater Heaters (LP Heaters)** |
| **Arterial pre-warmers** | **High-Pressure Feedwater Heaters (HP Heaters)** |
| **Tissue cells** | **Boiler/Steam Generator** |
| **Veins returning blood** | **Condensate Extraction System** |
| **Blood oxygen (O₂) level** | **Dissolved Oxygen (DO₂) concentration** |

Just as a human body would fail if blood carried gas bubbles (air embolism) or impurities, a boiler will suffer **tube failure, corrosion, and carryover** if the feed water contains dissolved oxygen, hardness salts, or undissolved gases. This analogy immediately clarifies *why* the feed water system is not just a simple pumping line — it is a **multi-stage conditioning pipeline**.

### 1.3 Functional Objectives of a Feed Water System

1. **Pressurization:** Raise water pressure above the boiler saturation pressure to ensure net positive suction head (NPSH) at the drum and to overcome friction losses.
2. **Heating:** Raise feed water temperature close to saturation (typically **165 °C – 235 °C** depending on subcritical/supercritical design) to reduce thermal shock and improve cycle efficiency.
3. **Purification:** Remove dissolved oxygen (target < **7 ppb**) and dissolved CO₂ to prevent corrosion.
4. **Flow Regulation:** Match the boiler steam output on a *three-element* (steam flow, feedwater flow, drum level) control basis.
5. **Energy Recovery:** Utilize extracted steam from turbine bleed points to preheat feed water (regenerative feedwater heating).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Performance Metric:** In a modern 500 MW subcritical unit, the feed water train raises condensate from ~45 °C at the condenser hot well to ~235 °C at the economizer inlet, while pressurizing from ~0.1 MPa (vacuum-tight condenser) to ~18–20 MPa (boiler pressure). This requires multi-stage pumping and precise enthalpy staging.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of the Feed Water Train

The feed water system is structurally a **closed-loop regenerative cycle** with the following sequential sub-systems:

#### 2.1.1 Condensate System
- **Function:** Collects the condensed exhaust steam from the condenser hot well and pumps it forward.
- **Equipment:** Condensate Extraction Pump (CEP), Condensate Booster Pump (CBP), Condensate Polishing Plant (CPP) — a mixed-bed demineralizer.
- **Instrumentation:** Conductivity transmitter (target < **0.1 μS/cm**), sodium analyzer, pH meter, flow transmitter (orifice/venturi), and condenser hot well level controller (three-element: level, CEP discharge flow, condensate inlet flow).

#### 2.1.2 Low-Pressure Feedwater Heating (LP Heaters)
- **Function:** Pre-heat condensate using **bleed steam from the HP/IP turbine exhaust stages** (typically 4–6 bleed points).
- **Equipment:** Shell-and-tube closed feedwater heaters, drain pumps or drain coolers.
- **Instrumentation:** Heater level controller (probes for shell-side water level), temperature transmitters at inlet/outlet.

#### 2.1.3 Deaerator (DA)
- **Function:** A *combined* direct-contact water heater and gas-stripping vessel. Heats water to saturation temperature corresponding to its internal pressure (~**0.2–0.5 MPa(g)**) so that dissolved O₂ and CO₂ are vented to atmosphere.
- **Types:**
  - **Spray-Type Deaerator:** Water sprayed through steam atmosphere.
  - **Tray-Type Deaerator:** Water cascades through perforated trays against rising steam.
  - **Combination Spray + Tray:** Most common in modern units.
- **Instrumentation:** Pressure controller (steam supply), level controller (three-element: level, condensate inlet, BFP suction flow), temperature transmitter, dissolved oxygen analyzer on the outlet.

#### 2.1.4 Boiler Feed Pump (BFP)
- **Function:** The **prime mover of the feed water system** — pressurizes deaerated water to boiler pressure.
- **Types:**
  - **Centrifugal Pump:** Multi-stage barrel-casing design; common for > 100 MW units.
  - **Positive Displacement (Plunger):** Used for start-up and small capacity.
  - **Steam-Turbine Driven (Turbine-driven BFP):** Driven by a small auxiliary steam turbine; saves auxiliary power.
  - **Motor-Driven BFP:** Simpler, used for start-up or smaller units.
- **Instrumentation:** Suction/discharge pressure transmitters, flow transmitter, bearing temperature RTDs, vibration probes, minimum flow recirculation valve control.

#### 2.1.5 High-Pressure Feedwater Heating (HP Heaters)
- **Function:** Final regenerative heating using steam from HP turbine bleeds.
- **Location:** After BFP discharge, before the economizer inlet.

#### 2.1.6 Economizer
- **Function:** Recovers residual heat from flue gas to further raise feed water temperature (typically 30–60 °C of additional rise).
- **Instrumentation:** Economizer inlet/outlet temperature transmitters, flow transmitter, soot-blower control.

### 2.2 Operating Logic — The 'Why & How'

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple heating stages?** Heating in stages matches the temperature profile to the *Carnot-like* regenerative cycle, minimizing entropy generation and maximizing Rankine cycle efficiency. A single-stage heater would generate unacceptably high entropy and would be impractical (LMTD would be huge).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pressurize stepwise?** A single pump from condenser vacuum (~0.1 MPa) to boiler pressure (~18 MPa) would be thermodynamically and mechanically infeasible. Multi-stage pumping with intermediate heating reduces specific volume of water and reduces pump work (W_pump = ∫ V dP).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mass flow rate from volumetric flow** | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow; $\rho$ = density; $Q$ = volumetric flow | kg/s |
| 2 | **Pump hydraulic power** | $P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | $P_{hyd}$ = hydraulic power; $H$ = total head | W |
| 3 | **Pump shaft power (brake power)** | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}}$ | $\eta_{pump}$ = pump efficiency (0.7–0.85) | W |
| 4 | **Pump work per unit mass (specific work)** | $w_{pump} = v \cdot (P_{disch} - P_{suct})$ | $v$ = specific volume of water | J/kg |
| 5 | **Heat duty of feedwater heater** | $Q_{heater} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$ | $c_{p,w}$ ≈ **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** | kW |
| 6 | **Economizer heat duty** | $Q_{eco} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_{eco,out} - T_{eco,in})$ | Same as above | kW |
| 7 | **Deaerator steam mass balance** | $\dot{m}_{s,DA} = \dot{m}_{w} \cdot \dfrac{(h_{w,out} - h_{w,in})}{h_{s} - h_{w,out}}$ | $h_s$ = saturated steam enthalpy at DA pressure | kg/s |
| 8 | **Net Positive Suction Head available** | $NPSH_{avail} = \dfrac{P_{suct}}{\rho g} + \dfrac{v_{suct}^2}{2g} - \dfrac{P_{vap}(T_{suct})}{\rho g}$ | $P_{vap}$ = vapor pressure at suction T | m |
| 9 | **NPSH required for cavitation-free operation** | $NPSH_{req} \leq NPSH_{avail}$ (margin: + 0.5 to 1.0 m) | — | m |
| 10 | **Boiler drum three-element mass balance** | $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam} + \dot{m}_{blowdown}$ | $\dot{m}_{blowdown}$ ≈ 1–3 % of feed water | kg/s |
| 11 | **Boiler thermal efficiency gain (heating feed water)** | $\Delta\eta \approx \dfrac{T_{fw} - T_{cond}}{T_{flame} - T_{cond}}$ | Approximate Carnot gain | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Dissolved O₂ removal fraction (Henry's Law approx.)** | $\dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} \approx \left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{in}}{T_{out}}\right)$ | For deaerator approximation | — |
| 13 | **Specific volume of water (saturated liquid, 200 °C)** | $v_f \approx 0.001157 \;\mathrm{m^3/kg}$ | From steam tables | m³/kg |
| 14 | **Cycle heat rate improvement per °C of feedwater heating** | $\Delta HR \approx c_{p,w} \cdot 3600$ | ≈ **15 kJ/kWh per °C** | kJ/kWh |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Formula (4) — *pump work per unit mass* — is the most-tested relationship. The standard trick question is: *"Why is it thermodynamically advantageous to raise feed water temperature to saturation before pumping?"* The answer: Lower specific volume $v$ at higher T directly reduces specific pump work $w_{pump} = v \Delta P$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Feed water systems are critical in:
- **Thermal (coal, gas, oil, nuclear) power plants** — primary application.
- **Cogeneration (CHP) plants** — feed water for waste-heat boilers.
- **Process industries** — chemical plants using high-pressure steam for reforming, ethylene cracking, paper drying.
- **Desalination plants** — high-pressure pumps share pump-work formulas.

Modern **supercritical and ultra-supercritical** units (> 600 °C, > 25 MPa) demand *once-through* feed water systems with no boiler drum, but the same instrumentation principles (three-element control, deaerator, BFP, economizer) apply.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Required Boiler Feed Pump Power

**Problem Setup:** A 210 MW subcritical thermal power plant has the following operating data:

- Boiler steam output: $\dot{m}_{steam} = 650 \; \mathrm{kg/s}$ at $P_{boiler} = 17.5 \; \mathrm{MPa(g)}$
- Deaerator pressure: $P_{DA} = 0.4 \; \mathrm{MPa(g)}$ (gauge)
- Deaerator water temperature (saturation): $T_{DA} = 143.6 \;^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$
- BFP suction specific volume: $v_1 = 0.001091 \; \mathrm{m^3/kg}$
- BFP discharge specific volume: $v_2 = 0.001159 \; \mathrm{m^3/kg}$
- Pump isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{pump} = 0.80$
- Pump mechanical efficiency: $\eta_{mech} = 0.97$
- Blowdown fraction: $x_{bd} = 0.02$ (i.e., 2 % of feed water is sent to blowdown)

**Step 1 — Determine the feed water mass flow rate**

We must account for blowdown because the BFP must deliver *more* water than the steam output.

$$\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (1 + x_{bd})$$

Substitute the values:

$$\dot{m}_{fw} = 650 \times (1 + 0.02)$$

$$\dot{m}_{fw} = 650 \times 1.02$$

$$\dot{m}_{fw} = 663.0 \; \mathrm{kg/s}$$

This means the BFP must deliver **663.0 kg/s** of feed water to produce 650 kg/s of steam (the extra 13 kg/s leaves as continuous blowdown to maintain dissolved solids within limits).

**Step 2 — Calculate the pressure rise across the BFP**

The BFP suction is at deaerator gauge pressure and discharge must overcome boiler pressure plus line friction. For simplicity, ignore pipe losses:

$$\Delta P = P_{boiler} - P_{DA}$$

Convert gauge pressures to absolute:

$$P_{boiler,abs} = 17.5 + 0.1013 = 17.6013 \; \mathrm{MPa}$$

$$P_{DA,abs} = 0.4 + 0.1013 = 0.5013 \; \mathrm{MPa}$$

$$\Delta P = 17.6013 - 0.5013 = 17.10 \; \mathrm{MPa}$$

Convert to Pascals:

$$\Delta P = 17.10 \times 10^{6} \; \mathrm{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ideal (isentropic) specific pump work**

For an incompressible liquid (water is essentially incompressible below critical point), the specific work is:

$$w_{ideal} = v \cdot \Delta P$$

We use the suction-side specific volume since this is the *inlet* condition (most common engineering assumption):

$$w_{ideal} = 0.001091 \times 17.10 \times 10^{6}$$

$$w_{ideal} = 18{,}656.1 \; \mathrm{J/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the actual (brake) specific pump work using isentropic efficiency**

$$w_{actual} = \dfrac{w_{ideal}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

$$w_{actual} = \dfrac{18{,}656.1}{0.80}$$

$$w_{actual} = 23{,}320.1 \; \mathrm{J/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the total pump shaft power (brake power)**

$$P_{shaft} = \dot{m}_{fw} \cdot w_{actual}$$

$$P_{shaft} = 663.0 \times 23{,}320.1$$

$$P_{shaft} = 15{,}461{,}226 \; \mathrm{W}$$

$$P_{shaft} \approx 15.46 \; \mathrm{MW}$$

**Step 6 — Apply mechanical efficiency to obtain motor input power**

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{mech}}$$

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{15.46}{0.97}$$

$$P_{motor} \approx 15.94 \; \mathrm{MW}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with plant output**

$$\text{Parasitic load fraction} = \dfrac{15.94}{210{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Parasitic load} \approx 0.0076 \times 100\% \approx 0.76\%$$

This is consistent with **typical BFP power consumption of 0.5–1.5 % of unit gross output** in modern subcritical plants.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If we had *not* pre-heated the feed water to 143.6 °C in the deaerator, the suction specific volume would have been much larger (e.g., $v$ at 45 °C ≈ 0.001010 m³/kg is actually *smaller*, so the dominant effect is the *pressure rise being constant*). In reality, the deaerator reduces the volume to be pumped (compared to cold condensate) because intermediate heating *between* pump stages shrinks the volume at the suction of each subsequent stage. This is why regenerative heating is placed both *before* and *after* the BFP.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Feed Water System Configurations

| Feature | **Open Feed System** | **Closed Feed System** | **Once-Through System** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Drum presence** | Yes (natural or assisted circulation) | Yes (controlled circulation) | No (once-through) |
| **Operating pressure** | Subcritical (≤ 22 MPa) | Subcritical (16–22 MPa) | Supercritical (> 22 MPa) |
| **Feed water T at drum inlet** | ≈ Saturation T | Below saturation T | Below saturation T |
| **Control complexity** | Moderate | High (circulation control) | Very high |
| **Inventory (water held in boiler)** | Large → slow response | Medium | Small → fast response |
| **Capacity range** | 50–600 MW typical | 200–800 MW | 500–1300 MW |
| **Example plants** | Most Indian 210/250 MW units | 500 MW Indian units | NTPC Sipat, Mundra Ultra |

### 3.3 Three-Element Drum Level Control — Logic of Mass Balance

The three-element control is a KTU-favorite for boiler instrumentation. The control law (in the Laplace / s-domain conceptual form) is:

$$\dot{m}_{fw,cmd} = \dot{m}_{steam} + K_{L} \cdot e_{level} + K_{I} \int e_{level} \, dt$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_{fw,cmd}$ = commanded feed water flow setpoint
- $\dot{m}_{steam}$ = measured steam flow (feedforward)
- $e_{level} = (H_{set} - H_{measured})$ = drum level error
- $K_L, K_I$ = proportional and integral gains

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'three elements' are:** (1) Drum level, (2) Steam flow, (3) Feed water flow. Three-element control eliminates the *shrink-and-swell* transient that plagues single-element (level-only) control during load changes.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Complete Feed Water Train

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Condenser Hot Well] --> B[Condensate Extraction Pump CEP]
    B --> C[Condensate Polishing Plant CPP]
    C --> D1[LP Feedwater Heater 1]
    D1 --> D2[LP Feedwater Heater 2]
    D2 --> D3[LP Feedwater Heater 3]
    D3 --> E[Spray Tray Deaerator]
    E --> F1[BFP Suction Strainer]
    F1 --> F2[Boiler Feed Pump BFP]
    F2 --> G1[HP Feedwater Heater 1]
    G1 --> G2[HP Feedwater Heater 2]
    G2 --> H[Economizer]
    H --> I[Boiler Drum]
    I --> J[Superheater]
    J --> K[HP Turbine]
    K --> L[IP Turbine]
    L --> M[LP Turbine]
    M --> N[Condenser]
    N --> A

    subgraph BleedSteamSources
        K1[Bleed 1 from HP cyl] -.-> G1
        L1[Bleed 2 from IP cyl] -.-> G2
        M1[Bleed 3 from LP cyl] -.-> D2
        N1[Aux steam] -.-> E
    end

    subgraph InstrumentationAndControl
        IC1[DO2 Analyzer on DA outlet] -.-> E
        IC2[Level LT on DA] -.-> E
        IC3[Pressure PT on BFP discharge] -.-> F2
        IC4[Flow FT on FW line] -.-> H
        IC5[Three-Element Drum Level] -.-> I
        IC6[Economizer Inlet TT] -.-> H
    end
```

### 4.2 Process & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) — Deaerator Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    LP1[LP Heater 3 Outlet] --> DAIn[DA Inlet Nozzle]
    DAIn --> DASpray[Spray Section]
    DASpray --> DATray[Tray Section]
    DATray --> DAOut[DA Storage Section]
    DAOut --> BFPIn[BFP Suction]
    SteamIn[Steam from LP Turbine Bleed] --> DAValve[Pressure Regulating Valve]
    DAValve --> DASpray

    LTSpray[Spray T/T] -.-> DASpray
    LTTray[Tray T/T] -.-> DATray
    LTLevel[Drum Level LT] -.-> DAOut
    PTPress[Pressure PT] -.-> DAValve
    FTOut[Outlet Flow FT] -.-> BFPIn
    ATDO2[DO2 Analyzer] -.-> DAOut
    VVent[Atmospheric Vent with CO2 purge] --- DAOut
```

### 4.3 Three-Element Drum Level Control Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    LVDT[Drum Level LVDT] --> LC[Drum Level Controller LC]
    FT_Steam[Steam Flow FT] --> FF1[Feedforward Summing Junction]
    FT_FW[Feedwater Flow FT] --> FF1
    LC --> FF1
    FF1 --> FC[Flow Controller FC]
    FC --> FCV[Feedwater Control Valve FCV]
    FCV --> Eco[Economizer Inlet]
    Eco --> Drum[Boiler Drum]
    Drum --> LVDT
    Drum --> FT_Steam
```

### 4.4 Schematic of the Boiler Feed Pump (BFP) and Its Auxiliaries

```mermaid
graph TD
    DAE[Deaerator Outlet] --> MinFlowLine[Minimum Flow Recirculation Line]
    MinFlowLine --> MinFlowValve[Minimum Flow Control Valve]
    MinFlowValve --> BFP_Suct[BFP Suction]
    BFP_Suct --> Strainer[Strainer]
    Strainer --> BFP_Core[BFP Multistage Centrifugal Core]
    BFP_Core --> BFP_Disch[BFP Discharge]
    BFP_Disch --> DSH[Discharge Check Valve]
    DSH --> ToHeaters[To HP Heaters and Economizer]
    BFP_Core -.driven by.-> DRV[Steam Turbine or Motor Drive]
    DRV -.vibration probe.-> VIB[Bearing Vibration Monitor]
    DRV -.temperature.-> THR[Thrust Bearing RTD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The **Minimum Flow Recirculation Line** is a critical KTU concept. When feed water demand drops (e.g., at low load), the BFP flow can fall below its cavitation threshold. The minimum flow valve opens and recirculates a small fraction back to the deaerator to protect the pump.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are typical KTU Part-A questions. Each is followed by a precise model answer.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any three functions of a deaerator in a feed water system.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Heating** the feed water to its saturation temperature (typically at **0.2–0.5 MPa(g)** deaerator pressure) using bleed steam.
2. **Removal of dissolved oxygen (O₂)** to less than **7 ppb** and **dissolved carbon dioxide (CO₂)** to prevent corrosion of boiler tubes and economizers.
3. Acting as a **feed water storage reservoir** (surge capacity) — usually sized to provide **~ 3–5 minutes** of full-load feed water inventory to absorb transients.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by the term 'three-element control' in a boiler drum level loop?**

**Model Answer:**
Three-element control is a **multi-variable control strategy** that uses three measured inputs — (1) **drum level**, (2) **steam mass flow rate** (feedforward), and (3) **feed water mass flow rate** (feedback) — to compute the feed water control valve position. It eliminates the **shrink-and-swell** transient (false drum level swell during sudden load changes) that confounds single-element (level-only) control. Mathematically:

$$\dot{m}_{fw,set} = \dot{m}_{steam} + K_P e_{level} + K_I \int e_{level} \, dt$$

The result is fast, stable, and accurate drum level regulation across the entire load range.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Internal choice is given within each module.** Below are TWO alternative 14-mark questions (Question A and Question B) covering Feed Water Systems. Each question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Pump Power & Mass Balance Focus

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the complete **feed water train** of a 210 MW thermal power plant. Identify the major equipment and the bleed-steam tapping points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 210 MW thermal power plant operates with the following data:
- Boiler steam output: $\dot{m}_{steam} = 600 \; \mathrm{kg/s}$
- Blowdown fraction: $x_{bd} = 0.025$
- BFP suction pressure: $P_1 = 0.5 \; \mathrm{MPa(absolute)}$ at $T_1 = 145 \;^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$, $v_1 = 0.001090 \; \mathrm{m^3/kg}$
- BFP discharge pressure: $P_2 = 18.0 \; \mathrm{MPa(absolute)}$, $v_2 = 0.001162 \; \mathrm{m^3/kg}$
- Pump efficiency: $\eta_{pump} = 0.78$

Compute:
1. The required feed water mass flow rate.
2. The specific pump work (isentropic).
3. The actual brake power input to the pump.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A:**

**(a) Block Diagram & Description (7 marks):**

[Refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.1]

**Description of the train:**

1. **Condensate Extraction Pump (CEP)** lifts condensate from the condenser hot well (≈ 0.1 MPa(a), 45 °C) to the deaerator pressure (≈ 0.5 MPa(a)).
2. **Condensate Polishing Plant (CPP)** — mixed-bed ion exchangers — polish the condensate to conductivity < **0.1 μS/cm**.
3. **LP Feedwater Heaters (LP1, LP2, LP3)** — closed shell-and-tube heat exchangers heated by bleed steam from the LP turbine (extractions #4, #5, #6). Heat condensate from ~ 45 °C to ~ 130 °C.
4. **Deaerator (DA)** — combination spray-and-tray type. Heats water to saturation (~ 143 °C at 0.4 MPa(g)) and strips out dissolved O₂ and CO₂. Acts as a storage tank (~ 3–5 min feed water inventory).
5. **Boiler Feed Pump (BFP)** — multi-stage centrifugal pump driven by a steam turbine. Boosts pressure from 0.5 MPa(a) to 18 MPa(a).
6. **HP Feedwater Heaters (HP1, HP2)** — heated by bleed steam from IP and HP turbine extractions (#2, #3). Heat feed water from ~ 165 °C to ~ 230 °C.
7. **Economizer** — final feed water heating using flue gas heat. Outlet temperature reaches ~ 245–260 °C before entering the boiler drum.
8. **Bleed-steam tapping points:** Three LP extractions feed the three LP heaters; one extraction feeds the deaerator; two HP/IP extractions feed the HP heaters.

**Bleed Steam Source Mapping:**

| Heater | Source |
|---|---|
| LP1 | LP Turbine Extraction 4 |
| LP2 | LP Turbine Extraction 5 |
| LP3 | LP Turbine Extraction 6 |
| Deaerator | LP Turbine Extraction 7 (Aux Steam) |
| HP1 | IP Turbine Extraction 2 |
| HP2 | HP Turbine Extraction 1 |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Sub-part 1 — Required feed water flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (1 + x_{bd}) = 600 \times (1 + 0.025) = 600 \times 1.025$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{fw} = 615 \; \mathrm{kg/s}}$$

**Sub-part 2 — Isentropic specific pump work:**

$$\Delta P = P_2 - P_1 = 18.0 - 0.5 = 17.5 \; \mathrm{MPa} = 17.5 \times 10^6 \; \mathrm{Pa}$$

Using suction-side specific volume:

$$w_{ideal} = v_1 \cdot \Delta P = 0.001090 \times 17.5 \times 10^6$$

$$w_{ideal} = 19{,}075 \; \mathrm{J/kg}$$

Using discharge-side specific volume (alternate valid method):

$$w_{ideal}' = v_2 \cdot \Delta P = 0.001162 \times 17.5 \times 10^6 = 20{,}335 \; \mathrm{J/kg}$$

Average specific volume method (often used in textbooks):

$$w_{ideal,avg} = \dfrac{(v_1 + v_2)}{2} \cdot \Delta P = \dfrac{(0.001090 + 0.001162)}{2} \times 17.5 \times 10^6$$

$$w_{ideal,avg} = 0.001126 \times 17.5 \times 10^6 \approx 19{,}705 \; \mathrm{J/kg}$$

**Sub-part 3 — Actual brake power:**

$$P_{brake} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fw} \cdot w_{ideal}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

Using $w_{ideal} = 19{,}705 \; \mathrm{J/kg}$:

$$P_{brake} = \dfrac{615 \times 19{,}705}{0.78}$$

$$P_{brake} = \dfrac{12{,}118{,}575}{0.78}$$

$$P_{brake} \approx 15{,}536{,}635 \; \mathrm{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{brake} \approx 15.54 \; \mathrm{MW}}$$

This corresponds to a **parasitic load** of $15.54/210{,}000 = 0.74 \;\%$ of the plant gross output, which is realistic.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Deaerator & Control Focus

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **combination spray-and-tray deaerator** with a neat P&ID. List the instrumentation used on the deaerator and state the typical setpoint values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **three-element drum level control** scheme in detail. Why is single-element control inadequate for boilers? Write the mass-balance equation of the boiler drum and explain its significance in the design of feed water flow control. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B:**

**(a) Construction, Working & Instrumentation of Combination Spray-and-Tray Deaerator (7 marks):**

**Construction (refer to SECTION 4.2):**

A combination spray-and-tray deaerator consists of a **vertical pressure vessel** with the following sections from top to bottom:

1. **Steam inlet section** (top): Receives bleed steam (typically at **0.4–0.6 MPa(g)** saturated).
2. **Spray section:** A bank of spray nozzles sprays the incoming condensate into fine droplets, providing maximum surface-area contact with the rising steam. About 80–90 % of gas removal occurs here.
3. **Tray section:** A set of perforated trays (or bubble-cap trays) where residual water trickles down through the rising steam. Final polishing of gas removal.
4. **Storage section (lower):** Holds the deaerated, de-gassed feed water. Sized for **3–5 minutes** of full-load inventory.
5. **Vent at the top:** Continuously vents non-condensable gases (O₂, CO₂) to atmosphere.
6. **DA outlet to BFP suction** (bottom side).

**Working Principle:**

- Steam entering the deaerator establishes the saturation temperature corresponding to the deaerator pressure (e.g., at **0.4 MPa(g)**, $T_{sat} \approx 143.6 \;^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$).
- Cold condensate (≈ 100–130 °C) is sprayed through this steam atmosphere. The water heats rapidly to saturation.
- At saturation, the equilibrium partial pressure of dissolved gases becomes nearly zero, so the dissolved O₂ and CO₂ diffuse out of the water droplets into the steam and are vented.
- Deaerated water collects in the storage section and is delivered to the BFP suction.

**Instrumentation & Setpoints:**

| Instrument | Tag | Setpoint / Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure Transmitter** | PT | **0.4 MPa(g)** ± 0.02 | Maintains saturation T for gas stripping |
| **Level Transmitter** (d/p cell) | LT | **50 % of storage section** | Maintains storage inventory |
| **Temperature Transmitter** | TT | ~ **143 °C** at 0.4 MPa(g) | Indicates saturation condition |
| **Dissolved Oxygen Analyzer** | AT | **< 7 ppb** | Verifies deaeration |
| **Steam Flow FT** | FT | Per load demand | Steam-to-water ratio |
| **Conductivity** | AT | < **1 μS/cm** | Verifies purity |

---

**(b) Three-Element Drum Level Control (7 marks):**

**Limitations of Single-Element Control:**

In a single-element scheme, only the **drum level** is measured and used to manipulate the feed water valve. This control is *inadequate* because:

1. **Swell and Shrink Effect:** When load suddenly increases, drum pressure momentarily drops, causing water to flash into steam, *falsely increasing* drum level. The single-element controller *reduces* feed water in response — exactly the wrong action. This causes a delayed, oscillatory response.
2. **Slow Response:** Drum level is a level measurement on a large inventory, so it responds slowly to disturbances.
3. **Inability to Compensate for Steam Flow Changes:** There is no feedforward action to anticipate feed water demand.

**Three-Element Scheme:**

Three measured variables are used:

- **Element 1:** Drum level (LT) — feedback for trim.
- **Element 2:** Steam flow (FT) — feedforward signal indicating demand.
- **Element 3:** Feed water flow (FT) — feedback confirming the control output.

**Mass Balance Equation of the Boiler Drum:**

$$\dfrac{d(\rho V)}{dt} = \dot{m}_{fw} - \dot{m}_{steam} - \dot{m}_{blowdown}$$

At steady state ($d/dt = 0$):

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam} + \dot{m}_{blowdown}}$$

**Significance in Feed Water Control Design:**

This mass balance is the **foundation of three-element control**: the feed water flow setpoint is *continuously adjusted* to equal the steam flow (plus blowdown). The drum-level error acts as a *trim* or *trim bias* on this setpoint, correcting for any cumulative imbalance (e.g., due to blowdown changes or measurement error).

**Control Law:**

$$\dot{m}_{fw,sp} = \dot{m}_{steam,meas} + \dot{m}_{blowdown,sp} + K_P e_{level} + K_I \int e_{level} \, dt$$

Where $e_{level} = H_{set} - H_{meas}$.

**Advantages:**

- Eliminates swell-and-shrink transients.
- Provides fast response to load changes (feedforward).
- Maintains drum level within ± 25 mm of setpoint even at 5 %/min load ramps.
- Improves safety by preventing low-level trip during sudden load reduction.

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Feed Water System Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the blowdown term** when computing feed water flow rate. Always: $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam} (1 + x_{bd})$. Many students write $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam}$ and lose **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Using the wrong specific volume** in the pump work formula. Always use the *suction-side* $v_1$ unless otherwise specified; using the discharge-side $v_2$ gives a different (still valid, but different) answer. State your assumption clearly.
> 3. **Neglecting mechanical efficiency** when asked for *motor input power*. The pump efficiency $\eta_{pump}$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta_{mech}$ are different — *do not* combine them.
> 4. **Confusing LP and HP heater positions:** LP heaters are *before* the BFP, HP heaters are *after* the BFP. Reversing this is a common error and loses the diagram/description marks.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of incompressible flow** for water. The formula $w = v \cdot \Delta P$ is valid because $v$ changes by less than 6 % between suction and discharge for water in subcritical plants. For supercritical/ultra-supercritical, mention that the constant-$v$ assumption is invalid and a Mollier diagram must be used.
> 6. **Forgetting units** in final answers. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical problems.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Feed water system** is the lifeline of a thermal power plant, conditioning water from condenser hot well to boiler drum inlet.
- **Five core functions:** Pressurize, heat, purify, regulate, and recover energy.
- **Main components (in order):** CEP → CPP → LP Heaters → Deaerator → BFP → HP Heaters → Economizer → Boiler Drum.
- **BFP** is the prime mover of the train — multi-stage centrifugal, driven by motor or auxiliary steam turbine.
- **Deaerator** removes dissolved O₂ to < 7 ppb; uses spray + tray combination; pressurized to ~ 0.4 MPa(g).
- **Pump power formula:** $P = \rho g Q H / \eta$ or per unit mass $w = v \Delta P / \eta$.
- **Always include blowdown:** $\dot{m}_{fw} = \dot{m}_{steam}(1 + x_{bd})$.
- **Three-element control** uses drum level + steam flow + feed water flow to avoid swell-shrink.
- **Minimum flow recirculation** protects BFP from cavitation at low loads.
- **Economizer** uses flue gas heat to preheat feed water — saves 0.5–1 % of fuel.
- **Regenerative heating** (LP + HP heaters) raises cycle efficiency by reducing irreversibility.
- **Standard KTU numerical:** expect a 7-mark problem combining blowdown, pump work, and brake power.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Circuits
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Circuits

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Steam Circuit)**
> A *Steam Circuit* (also called the *Steam-Water Cycle* or *Rankine Cycle Loop*) in a thermal power station is the closed network of interconnected components — **boiler, superheater, reheater, turbine stages, condenser, feedwater heaters, deaerator, economizer, and boiler feed pump (BFP)** — through which working fluid (water → steam → water) circulates to convert the chemical energy of fuel into useful mechanical/electrical energy. In Power Plant Instrumentation, the *Steam Circuit* refers specifically to the **measurement, control, and protection loops** monitoring pressure, temperature, flow, level, and chemical quality at every node of this loop.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart and Blood Vessels" 🍃

Imagine the human body:
- The **boiler** is the **heart** — it pumps high-pressure "oxygenated blood" (superheated steam) into the arteries.
- The **turbine** is the **muscle** that does the work as blood rushes through.
- The **condenser** is the **lungs** — it cools and condenses the spent steam back into liquid.
- The **feedwater pumps** are the **returning veins** carrying fluid back to the heart.
- The **instrumentation loops** (PT, TT, FT, LT) are the **nervous system** telling the heart how hard to pump.

Without continuous monitoring at every junction, the whole organism collapses — exactly why every node in the steam circuit must have at least one primary sensor, one indicator, and (for critical nodes) a redundant transmitter feeding a triple-modular redundant (TMR) DCS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Module 2 of PEICT523 (Power Plant Instrumentation) explicitly tests the student's ability to:
> 1. Sketch and label a **complete steam circuit** with all major auxiliaries.
> 2. Identify the **instrumentation nodes** (primary sensors, transmitters, control valves) at each component.
> 3. Explain the **purpose and operating principle** of superheaters, reheaters, economizers, deaerators, and feedwater heaters.
> 4. Describe the **Boiler Drum Level three-element control** and the **steam temperature control** strategy.

### 1.2 Standard Reference Parameters (Must Remember)

- **Boiler Drum Pressure (Subcritical):** $80\text{ bar} \leq P_{drum} \leq 180\text{ bar}$
- **Main Steam Temperature:** $\mathbf{535^{\circ}C} \pm \mathbf{5^{\circ}C}$ (typical 500 MW Indian unit)
- **Reheat Steam Temperature:** $\mathbf{535^{\circ}C} \pm \mathbf{5^{\circ}C}$
- **Condenser Vacuum:** $-0.85\text{ bar (gauge)}$ to $-0.92\text{ bar (gauge)}$
- **Feedwater Temperature at Economizer Inlet:** $230^{\circ}C$ to $260^{\circ}C$
- **Cycle Thermal Efficiency (Subcritical):** $38\% - 42\%$
- **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):** $1.8 - 2.2\text{ kg/kWh}$

> [!TIP]
> Examiner's Rule of Thumb: A KTU question on Steam Circuits will always begin with a **PI diagram (Piping & Instrumentation)** and ask you to either **(a) identify components**, **(b) name the instruments**, or **(c) describe the control loop**. Practice drawing the loop from memory before the exam — this alone is worth **3–5 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sequential Flow Logic of the Steam Circuit

The steam circuit operates in **two intertwined sub-loops**:

#### A. Steam–Water (Working Fluid) Loop

1. **Feedwater Pump (BFP)** forces condensate at $\sim 12\text{ bar}$ into the suction of the HP heater train.
2. **Low-Pressure Feedwater Heaters (LPH 1–4)** successively raise the feedwater temperature to $\sim 170^{\circ}C$ using extracted steam from the IP/LP turbine cross-overs.
3. **Deaerator (DA)** at $\sim 5\text{ bar}$ removes dissolved $\text{O}_2$ and $\text{CO}_2$ to < **7 ppb** of oxygen and final feedwater temperature $\sim 170^{\circ}C$.
4. **Boiler Feed Pump (BFP)** boosts pressure from $\sim 30\text{ bar}$ to the final boiler pressure (e.g., $170\text{ bar}$). A 500 MW unit uses a $40\%$ capacity turbine-driven BFP and a $60\%$ motor-driven BFP for startup.
5. **Economizer** uses flue-gas waste heat to raise feedwater from $\sim 240^{\circ}C$ to $\sim 290^{\circ}C$ — *just below* saturation.
6. **Boiler Drum (Steam Generator)** — natural circulation or once-through type. Water boils at saturation temperature (e.g., $354^{\circ}C$ at $170\text{ bar}$).
7. **Primary Superheater (Radiant + Convective)** raises steam from saturation to $\sim 450^{\circ}C$.
8. **Final (Platen) Superheater** brings steam to the **Main Steam Temperature (MST)** of $535^{\circ}C$.
9. **HP Turbine** expands steam from $170\text{ bar} \to \sim 45\text{ bar}$, doing work on the rotor.
10. **Hot Reheat Line** carries partially expanded steam back to the boiler's **Reheater**, where it is re-superheated to $535^{\circ}C$ (at $\sim 40\text{ bar}$).
11. **IP Turbine** expands reheat steam to $\sim 5\text{ bar}$.
12. **LP Turbine(s)** expand further to condenser vacuum ($\sim 0.05\text{ bar absolute}$).
13. **Condenser** condenses exhaust steam using cooling-water circulation; condensate is collected in the **hotwell**.
14. **Condensate Extraction Pump (CEP)** sends condensate forward; **Condensate Polishing Unit (CPU)** removes trace impurities.
15. The cycle repeats.

#### B. Fuel–Flue Gas Loop (Briefly, for Context)

Fuel $\rightarrow$ Mills $\rightarrow$ Burners $\rightarrow$ Furnace $\rightarrow$ Superheater / Reheater (heat absorption) $\rightarrow$ Economizer (heat recovery) $\rightarrow$ Air Preheater (APH) $\rightarrow$ ESP $\rightarrow$ ID Fan $\rightarrow$ Stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why reheating is mandatory (KTU high-yield concept):**
> Steam leaving the HP turbine is at $\sim 50\%$ moisture. If expanded directly in the IP/LP stages, **blade erosion** destroys the LP turbine. Reheating brings the steam back into the **dry/superheated region** (typically $> 50^{\circ}C$ superheat), allowing the IP and LP stages to extract safe work.

### 2.2 Instrumentation Map per Component

| S.No | Component | Primary Measurements | Typical Instruments | Control Output |
|------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Boiler Drum** | Drum Level, Drum Pressure | Differential Pressure Level Tx (3-element), Pressure Tx | Feedwater Control Valve |
| 2 | **Superheater Outlet** | Main Steam Temp, Pressure, Flow | RTD/Thermocouple, Pressure Tx, Flow Nozzle | Spray Water Valve (Desuperheater) |
| 3 | **Reheater Outlet** | Reheat Steam Temp, Pressure | RTD, Pressure Tx | Gas Recirculation / Burner Tilt |
| 4 | **HP/IP/LP Turbine** | Speed, Vibration, Bearing Temp, Lube Oil Pressure | Eddy-Probe Tachometer, Accelerometer, RTD, Pressure Switch | Hydraulic Governor Valve |
| 5 | **Condenser** | Hotwell Level, Vacuum, Condensate Conductivity | DPT, Absolute Pressure Tx, Conductivity Cell | CEP Speed / Air Removal Valve |
| 6 | **Deaerator** | Level, Pressure, Oxygen | DPT, Pressure Tx, Dissolved $\text{O}_2$ Probe | Level Control Valve, Vent Valve |
| 7 | **LP/HP Heaters** | Level, Shell Pressure | DPT, Pressure Tx | Drain Control Valve |
| 8 | **Economizer Inlet/Outlet** | Feedwater Temp, Pressure | RTD, Pressure Tx | (Monitoring only) |
| 9 | **BFP** | Suction/Discharge Pressure, Flow, Motor Current | Pressure Tx, Flow Tx, CT | Recirculation Valve, Min Flow |
| 10 | **Stack** | $\text{O}_2$, $\text{NO}_x$, $\text{SO}_2$, Particulates, Opacity | Zirconia Probe, Chemiluminescence, Opacity Monitor | FD/ID Fan Speed |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the delimiter `\vert` (never `\vert` inside a table cell — use the word "abs" or LaTeX display block if needed). The table below uses clean text and LaTeX display outside the cell to avoid pipe conflicts.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical KTU Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Drum Level (Hydrostatic) | $h = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho_{w} \cdot g}$ | Level Tx calibration, Part-A 3-mark |
| 2 | Compensated Drum Level (Corrected) | $h_{corr} = h \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{w} - \rho_{s}}{\rho_{w} - \rho_{ref}}$ | Density compensation, 7-mark problem |
| 3 | Steam Specific Enthalpy (exit) | $h_{2} = h_{1} - \eta_{t} \cdot (h_{1} - h_{2s})$ | Turbine stage efficiency, 14-mark |
| 4 | Rankine Efficiency (Ideal) | $\eta_{R} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cond}}{T_{boiler}}$ | Cycle efficiency comparison |
| 5 | Heat of Superheat | $Q_{sh} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{sh} - T_{sat})$ | Superheater duty, 7-mark |
| 6 | Reheat Cycle Gain in Efficiency | $\eta_{RH} \approx \eta_{R} \cdot (1 + 0.15 \text{ to } 0.20)$ | Justification of reheater, 3-mark |
| 7 | Feedwater Three-Element Control | $W_{fw} = K \cdot (S - F) + K_{L} \cdot (h_{set} - h) + K_{P} \cdot (P_{drum} - P_{ref})$ | DCS control strategy, 7/14-mark |
| 8 | Steam Quality $(x)$ | $x = \dfrac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$ | Drum level compensation, 7-mark |
| 9 | Heat Rate | $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{cycle}} \text{ (kJ/kWh)}$ | Plant performance, 3-mark |
| 10 | Condenser Vacuum → Sat. Temp | $T_{sat} = f(P_{abs})$ from steam tables | Vacuum monitoring, 3-mark |

**Key Variables (Text Form):**
- $h$ = specific enthalpy in $\text{kJ/kg}$
- $\Delta P$ = differential pressure across drum column in $\text{kPa}$
- $\rho_{w}, \rho_{s}, \rho_{ref}$ = water, steam, reference densities in $\text{kg/m}^{3}$
- $\eta_{t}, \eta_{R}$ = isentropic turbine efficiency, Rankine efficiency
- $W_{fw}$ = feedwater flow command
- $S$ = steam flow, $F$ = feedwater flow, $h$ = drum level, $P_{drum}$ = drum pressure

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Steam Circuit Concept |
|-----------------|--------------------------------------|
| Thermal Power (Coal/Nuclear) | Direct design, monitoring, and protection |
| Combined Cycle (NGCC) | HRSG (Heat Recovery Steam Generator) follows identical 3-pressure steam circuit |
| Process Industries (Refinery, Pulp) | Cogeneration plants use the same instrumentation map |
| Marine Propulsion | Nuclear submarine steam plants use miniature Rankine loops |
| Solar Thermal / CSP | Molten-salt-heated steam generators replicate the superheat-reheat chain |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Cue:** If the question contains the words *"explain with a neat sketch"*, the model answer must start with a **labelled block diagram** of the steam circuit (refer Section 4) before any text. Skipping the diagram attracts an automatic 2–3 mark penalty in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Drum Level Measurement (Reference Leg Compensation)

A KTU favourite. Given a subcritical boiler with:
- Drum pressure $P = 170\text{ bar}$
- Saturation temperature $T_{sat} = 354.7^{\circ}C$
- Reference water density at $20^{\circ}C$: $\rho_{ref} = 998.2\text{ kg/m}^{3}$

Derive the corrected level equation and calculate the indicated level error for a true level of $h = 200\text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Differential Pressure sensed by DPT:**

$$
\Delta P = \rho_{s} \cdot g \cdot h_{s} + \rho_{w} \cdot g \cdot h_{w}
$$

where $h_{s}$ is the steam-column height above the upper tap, $h_{w}$ is the water-column height, and $h = h_{s} + h_{w}$ is the geometric level.

**Step 2 — Substitute water + steam properties:**

The reference leg (impulse line) is filled with cold water at $\rho_{ref}$. The DPT measures:

$$
\Delta P = g \cdot h \cdot (\rho_{w} - \rho_{ref}) + g \cdot h_{max} \cdot \rho_{ref}
$$

(where $h_{max}$ is the maximum possible geometric level, treated as a zero offset.)

**Step 3 — Cancel zero, isolate the variable term:**

$$
\Delta P_{net} = g \cdot h \cdot (\rho_{w} - \rho_{ref})
$$

**Step 4 — Apparent level (what the DPT "thinks"):**

$$
h_{app} = \frac{\Delta P_{net}}{g \cdot \rho_{ref}} = h \cdot \frac{\rho_{w} - \rho_{ref}}{\rho_{ref}}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply density compensation factor to obtain true level:**

$$
h_{true} = h_{app} \cdot \frac{\rho_{ref}}{\rho_{w} - \rho_{ref}}
$$

**Step 6 — Generalised form (compensated level transmitter output):**

$$
\boxed{\,h_{true} = h_{app} \cdot \frac{\rho_{ref} - \rho_{s}}{\rho_{w} - \rho_{ref}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{ref}}{\rho_{w} - \rho_{s}} = h_{app} \cdot K_{comp}\,}
$$

The compensation factor $K_{comp}$ is a function of drum pressure. The DCS automatically updates $K_{comp}$ every scan cycle.

**Step 7 — Numerical Plug:**

Take drum at $170\text{ bar}$: $\rho_{w} = 750.7\text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $\rho_{s} = 84.26\text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $\rho_{ref} = 998.2\text{ kg/m}^{3}$.

$$
K_{comp} = \frac{998.2 - 84.26}{750.7 - 84.26} = \frac{913.94}{666.44} = 1.371
$$

For $h_{app} = 145.8\text{ mm}$ (output of DPT):

$$
h_{true} = 145.8 \times 1.371 = 199.9 \approx 200\text{ mm} \;\; \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A non-compensated level reading at $170\text{ bar}$ will be **~30\% low**, leading to false drum-low trips and unit shutdown. Modern DCS uses a **stanza compensator block** that pulls $\rho_{w}$ and $\rho_{s}$ from the steam tables as a function of measured drum pressure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Three-Element Feedwater Control Law

**Problem Setup:** A 500 MW unit drum receives a steam-flow signal $S$ (kg/s), a feedwater flow signal $F$ (kg/s), and a drum-level signal $L$ (mm). Derive the controller output that maintains mass balance.

**Step 1 — Steady-state mass balance at the drum:**

At steady state:

$$
S = F \quad \text{and} \quad L = L_{set}
$$

Any deviation $\Delta S - \Delta F$ causes a level change. The relationship is:

$$
\tau \cdot \frac{dL}{dt} = S - F
$$

where $\tau$ is the integrating time-constant of the drum (drum inventory/steam density).

**Step 2 — Linearise and define errors:**

$$
e_{flow} = S - F, \qquad e_{level} = L - L_{set}, \qquad e_{pressure} = P_{drum} - P_{ref}
$$

**Step 3 — Combine with PI (Proportional + Integral) action on level and feed-forward on flow:**

$$
W_{fw}(t) = K_{p} \cdot e_{level} + K_{I} \int e_{level}\, dt + K_{ff} \cdot (S - F) + K_{pressure} \cdot e_{pressure}
$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state error analysis:**

If the level is at set-point, $\int e_{level} \to 0$ and $e_{level} = 0$, then:

$$
W_{fw} = K_{ff} \cdot (S - F) + K_{pressure} \cdot (P_{drum} - P_{ref})
$$

Setting $P_{drum} = P_{ref}$ and choosing $K_{ff} = 1$:

$$
W_{fw} = S - F
$$

**Step 5 — Realistic discrete-time DCS form:**

$$
\boxed{\,W_{fw}[n] = K_{p} \cdot e_{L}[n] + K_{I} \cdot T_{s} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_{L}[k] + K_{ff} \cdot (S[n] - F[n])\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Tuning rule of thumb (KTU examiner-style):**

For a 500 MW unit:
- $K_{p} \approx 2.0\,\%/\text{mm}$
- $K_{I} \approx 0.05\,\%/\text{mm·s}$
- $K_{ff} \approx 0.8 - 1.0$ (typically $0.95$ to leave a small trim to level loop)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **pressure-correction term**. If the boiler pressure surges (e.g., due to sudden load throw-off), the drum water momentarily swells (swell effect) or shrinks (shrink effect). Without pressure feed-forward, the level controller either over- or under-fires the BFP, causing drum-level oscillations.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Superheater Outlet Temperature (Energy Balance)

A convection superheater receives steam at $T_{in} = 350^{\circ}C$ and leaves at $T_{out} = 535^{\circ}C$. Mass flow $m = 200\text{ kg/s}$, $c_{p} \approx 2.1\text{ kJ/kg·K}$. Estimate the heat absorbed.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the superheater:**

$$
Q_{sh} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in}) = \dot{m} \cdot \bar{c}_{p} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
Q_{sh} = 200 \times 2.1 \times (535 - 350)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
Q_{sh} = 200 \times 2.1 \times 185 = 77{,}700 \text{ kW} = 77.7 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 4 — Desuperheater water spray mass (to control $T_{out}$ to set-point):**

If the desired outlet temperature is $T_{set} = 535^{\circ}C$ and the un-sprayed value is $T_{nat} = 555^{\circ}C$, then:

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \dfrac{c_{p,steam} \cdot (T_{nat} - T_{set})}{h_{f}(T_{spray}) - c_{p,w}(T_{spray} - T_{ref})}
$$

with $T_{spray} \approx 200^{\circ}C$, $h_{f}(200^{\circ}C) \approx 849\text{ kJ/kg}$, $c_{p,w} = 4.18\text{ kJ/kg·K}$:

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = 200 \cdot \frac{2.1 \cdot 20}{849 - 4.18 \cdot 180} = 200 \cdot \frac{42}{96.8} \approx 86.8 \text{ kg/s}
$$

So roughly **30% of the steam flow** is sprayed back at peak load — this is why a desuperheater must be sized for the worst-case over-temperature scenario.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — "When you see this, recall that"

| Symptom in Question | Concept to Recall | Key Formula Triggered |
|---------------------|-------------------|-----------------------|
| "Drum level is wrong at high pressure" | Density compensation | $h_{true} = h_{app} \cdot K_{comp}$ |
| "Feedwater flow oscillates" | Three-element control | $(S - F)$ feed-forward term |
| "Steam temperature drifts" | Desuperheater control | Spray water + attemperator |
| "Condenser vacuum is lost" | Air in-leakage, cooling water | $T_{sat}(P_{abs})$ from steam tables |
| "Reheater outlet too cold" | Gas recirculation / burner tilt | Recirculation damper control |
| "Oxygen in feedwater > 7 ppb" | Deaerator malfunction | Deaerator pressure = 0.35 bar (g) minimum |
| "Economizer tube failure" | Feedwater chemistry | $\text{pH} \in [8.5, 9.2]$, $\text{O}_2 < 7$ ppb |
| "Swell/shrink on load throw" | Pressure-compensated level | $e_{pressure}$ term in 3-element |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Mermaid diagram below is **Mermaid v9+ compliant**: all node IDs are alphanumeric, all labels are plain uppercase text (no bold/italic), and reserved keywords are avoided as standalone names. The diagram illustrates the **functional flow** of the steam circuit with instrumentation nodes overlaid.

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Steam Circuit with Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CONDENSER HOTWELL] --> B1[CONDENSATE EXTRACTION PUMP]
    B1 --> B2[CONDENSATE POLISHING UNIT]
    B2 --> C1[LP FEEDWATER HEATER 1]
    C1 --> C2[LP FEEDWATER HEATER 2]
    C2 --> C3[LP FEEDWATER HEATER 3]
    C3 --> D1[DEAERATOR]
    D1 --> E1[BOILER FEED PUMP]
    E1 --> E2[HP FEEDWATER HEATER 1]
    E2 --> E3[HP FEEDWATER HEATER 2]
    E3 --> F1[ECONOMIZER INLET]
    F1 --> F2[ECONOMIZER]
    F2 --> G1[BOILER DRUM]
    G1 --> H1[PRIMARY SUPERHEATER]
    H1 --> H2[FINAL PLATEN SUPERHEATER]
    H2 --> H3[DESUPERHEATER SPRAY]
    H3 --> I1[MAIN STEAM HEADER]
    I1 --> I2[HP TURBINE STAGE 1]
    I2 --> I3[HP TURBINE EXHAUST]
    I3 --> J1[HOT REHEAT LINE]
    J1 --> J2[REHEATER IN BOILER]
    J2 --> J3[REHEAT STEAM HEADER]
    J3 --> K1[IP TURBINE]
    K1 --> K2[LP TURBINE]
    K2 --> A1

    %% Instrumentation overlay
    G1 -.->|LT THREE ELEMENT| INST1[DRUM LEVEL CONTROLLER]
    I1 -.->|FT| INST1
    E1 -.->|FT| INST1
    H3 -.->|TT| INST2[STEAM TEMP CONTROLLER]
    J3 -.->|TT| INST3[REHEAT TEMP CONTROLLER]
    A1 -.->|LT| INST4[CONDUCTIVITY ANALYSER]
    D1 -.->|AT| INST5[OXYGEN ANALYSER]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Solid arrows: working-fluid flow.
- Dotted arrows: signal flow to DCS controllers.
- The **INST** blocks (light shaded) represent **DCS control stations**, not physical equipment.

### 4.2 Logical Block Architecture — Instrumentation Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[FIELD SENSORS: PT TT FT LT AT] --> T1[JUNCTION BOXES AND CABLING]
    T1 --> T2[ISOLATED BARRIERS FOR ESD]
    T2 --> T3[MARSHALLING CABINET]
    T3 --> C1[CONTROL SYSTEM: DCS REDUNDANT CONTROLLERS]
    T3 --> C2[SAFETY SYSTEM: TRIP LOGIC TMR PLC]
    C1 --> H1[OPERATOR HMI AND TREND DISPLAYS]
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> A1[ACTUATORS: VALVES DAMPERS GOVERNOR]
    C1 --> A1
    C1 --> HIST[PLANT HISTORIAN AND PERFORMANCE MONITOR]
```

### 4.3 Protection Interlock Map (Simplified)

| Trip | Initiating Signal | Action |
|------|-------------------|--------|
| Drum Level High-High | LHH > +200 mm | Trip BFP, Open FD/ID dampers |
| Drum Level Low-Low | LLL < -250 mm | **MFT** (Master Fuel Trip) |
| MST High-High | TTT > 555 °C | Reduce firing, Open desuperheater |
| MST Low-Low | TLL < 480 °C | MFT |
| Condenser Vacuum Low | P < -0.7 bar(g) | Turbine Trip |
| Bearing Temp High | > 110 °C | Alarm → 115 °C → Trip |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEICT523]**
> *Define a "Steam Circuit" as applied to a thermal power station. List any four major components of the steam circuit.*

**Model Answer:**
A *Steam Circuit* in a thermal power station is the closed network of components through which the working fluid (water/steam) flows, undergoing phase change, pressure reduction, and heat addition/removal to convert fuel energy into rotational mechanical energy. The four major components are:
1. **Steam generator (boiler)** — raises water to high-pressure steam.
2. **Turbine** — converts steam enthalpy into shaft work.
3. **Condenser** — condenses exhaust steam back to water at low pressure.
4. **Feedwater pump and economiser** — return condensate to the boiler at the required pressure and temperature.

*(Any other valid component such as superheater, reheater, deaerator also accepted.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEICT523]**
> *What is the function of a deaerator in a steam circuit? Why is dissolved oxygen control important?*

**Model Answer:**
A **deaerator** is an open feedwater heater operating at $\sim 0.5 - 1.0\text{ bar (gauge)}$ that simultaneously:
- Heats feedwater close to saturation temperature.
- Strips out dissolved **oxygen** and **free carbon dioxide** by direct contact with steam.

**Why oxygen control is critical:** Dissolved $\text{O}_2$ above **7 ppb** causes **pitting corrosion** of boiler tubes, economiser coils, and condensate lines. In high-pressure boilers ($\geq 170\text{ bar}$), even a few ppb of oxygen can perforate tubes in months. Deaerator reduces $\text{O}_2$ to less than **7 ppb**; remaining traces are scavenged by **hydrazine** or **DEHA** dosing.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice)

> **Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the complete **steam circuit** of a 500 MW thermal power station. Label the position of **superheater, reheater, economiser, deaerator, HP/LP heaters, condenser, and BFP**. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Explain the **three-element drum-level control** strategy. Derive the controller equation and justify why the flow feed-forward term is essential during sudden load changes. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A neat sketch must include the following sequential chain (from condenser back to condenser):
**Condenser hotwell → CEP → Condensate Polisher → LP heaters → Deaerator → BFP → HP heaters → Economiser → Boiler Drum → Primary Superheater → Platen Superheater → Desuperheater → HP Turbine → Reheater → IP Turbine → LP Turbine → back to Condenser.**

For each block, the student should write **one line of function** (e.g., *"LP heater raises feedwater from 90 °C to 140 °C using extracted IP steam"*). Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the labelling pattern.

**Mark-split:**
- 2 marks: correct sequence and loop closure.
- 2 marks: each major aux labelled (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks).
- 2 marks: temperature/pressure values at key points.
- 1 mark: neatness and arrows direction.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Three-element control** uses three measured variables:
1. **Drum level** $L$ (mm)
2. **Steam flow** $S$ (kg/s)
3. **Feedwater flow** $F$ (kg/s)

The controller output is given by the **derived law** in Section 3.2:

$$
W_{fw}[n] = K_{p} \cdot e_{L}[n] + K_{I} \cdot T_{s} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_{L}[k] + K_{ff} \cdot (S[n] - F[n])
$$

**Justification of flow feed-forward:**
During a sudden load increase, steam flow $S$ rises before drum level $L$ begins to fall (water has to boil out). If only a level PI loop is used, the level momentarily drops (the "shrink" effect) before the controller catches up. By the time the level error is large, the drum has lost inventory, risking a low-level trip. The feed-forward term $(S-F)$ commands the BFP *immediately*, anticipating the level change.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part B / 14-Mark)**
> - **Do NOT** present the three-element formula without defining each variable. Marks are split: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for "why feed-forward is essential", 2 marks for swell/shrink explanation.
> - **Do NOT** confuse "three-element" with "cascade control" — they are different. Three-element is feed-forward + feedback; cascade is two feedback loops nested.
> - **Do NOT** omit the steam-flow and feedwater-flow units. Examiner deducts 0.5 mark for ambiguous units.

---

> **Q4. Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, PEICT523] — Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **function of superheaters and reheaters** in a thermal power station. With the help of a **temperature-enthalpy (T-H) diagram**, justify why reheat improves cycle efficiency. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Describe the **instrumentation scheme** of a 500 MW boiler drum, including **drum-level, drum-pressure, and steam-purity measurements**. Discuss the need for **density compensation** in drum-level measurement. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Function of Superheater:**
- Raises steam temperature above saturation to increase the **mean temperature of heat addition** $T_{m}$ in the Rankine cycle.
- Reduces moisture content at the HP turbine exhaust, protecting blades from erosion.
- Increases specific work output per kg of steam.

**Function of Reheater:**
- Partially expanded steam from the HP exhaust is sent back to the boiler and reheated to (or close to) the original Main Steam Temperature (MST).
- Reheating is done at **constant pressure** (no reheat compressor is needed).
- Allows **higher boiler pressure** and **lower exhaust pressure** without pushing the LP-stage moisture beyond the **12%** moisture limit (an industry design rule).
- Typical gain in cycle efficiency: **4–6%** (relative).

**T-H Diagram Justification:**

$$
\eta_{R} = 1 - \frac{T_{cond}}{T_{mean}}
$$

Reheating increases $T_{mean}$ by adding heat at a higher temperature (close to MST) instead of adding all heat at a single $T_{boiler}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Drum-Level Measurement:**
- Two **differential pressure transmitters (DPTs)** mounted on the drum at upper and lower water-column taps.
- Reference leg filled with cold water and maintained at constant $\rho_{ref}$.
- Output $\Delta P = g \cdot h \cdot (\rho_{w} - \rho_{ref})$ — compensated to true level using $h_{true} = h_{app} \cdot K_{comp}$.

**Drum-Pressure Measurement:**
- A redundant pair of **pressure transmitters** (one for control, one for trip) mounted on the steam-space.
- Connected to a **blow-down vessel** to protect the diaphragm from high temperature.

**Steam Purity:**
- A **sodium-ion selective electrode** and **conductivity cell** continuously monitor carry-over from the drum.
- Silica is monitored in a lab grab sample (online silica meters are used in modern plants).

**Need for Density Compensation (recap of Section 3.1):**
- At higher pressure, $\rho_{w}$ falls and $\rho_{s}$ rises.
- Reference-leg water (cold) has $\rho \approx 1000\text{ kg/m}^{3}$.
- Without compensation, a $200\text{ mm}$ level appears as $\sim 145\text{ mm}$ at $170\text{ bar}$, a $30\%$ error.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Alternative 14-Mark)**
> - Many students **omit the T-H diagram** in part (a). The examiner awards 2 marks for the diagram alone — never skip it.
> - For part (b), students often forget to mention **blow-down protection** for the pressure transmitter diaphragm. The membrane can be destroyed by direct contact with $354^{\circ}C$ steam — always include a **sealing pot / condensate pot** between the drum and the pressure tap.
> - **Do NOT** say "drum level is measured with a sight glass" in a 500 MW unit. Sight glasses are auxiliary indicators only; the **primary** level measurement is **DPT-based**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!TIP]
> Read this section **twice** the night before the exam. It is a compressed, high-density summary of the entire Steam Circuit topic.

- ✅ **Steam Circuit = Boiler + Turbine + Condenser + Feedwater path** — remember the closed loop.
- ✅ **Three control loops** are non-negotiable: **(1) Drum level, (2) Steam temperature, (3) Furnace draft / pressure**.
- ✅ **Reheater** exists to **protect LP blades** and **improve cycle efficiency by 4–6%**.
- ✅ **Superheater outlet temperature** is controlled by **spray desuperheater** + **gas recirculation / burner tilt**.
- ✅ **Drum-level** uses **two DPTs** with reference legs — must be **density-compensated** using steam-table lookup.
- ✅ **Three-element control** = level PI + steam-flow feed-forward + feedwater-flow feedback.
- ✅ **Deaerator** must reduce $\text{O}_2 < 7\text{ ppb}$ — high oxygen → tube pitting.
- ✅ **Condenser vacuum** is the most sensitive parameter — every $1\text{ kPa}$ loss in vacuum costs ~$1\%$ efficiency.
- ✅ **Economiser** is *feedwater pre-heater using flue gas* — saves fuel.
- ✅ **HP heater drain** cascades forward to the **deaerator**; **LP heater drain** cascades forward to the **condenser hotwell**.
- ✅ Typical 500 MW: **MST = 535 °C, Reheat = 535 °C, Drum = 170 bar, Condenser = 0.05 bar(a)**.
- ✅ The **PI diagram (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)** is the universal language of KTU power-plant exam questions. Practice drawing one from memory.
- ✅ Always pair a **transmitter (4–20 mA / HART / Foundation Fieldbus)** with a **local gauge** and, for safety, an **independent switch**.
- ✅ MFT (Master Fuel Trip) is triggered by **DLHH, LLL, MST HH, Furnace pressure high**, or operator action.
- ✅ **Swell and shrink effects** during load changes are the reason drum-level control is harder than it looks — the pressure term must be fed forward.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Steam Circuit Notes (PEICT523, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> For Module 3 (Boiler Drum Instrumentation and Safety), continue with the corresponding topic-specific notes using the same KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 format.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Preheating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Preheating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Air Preheating** is the process of recovering residual thermal energy from the hot flue gases leaving the boiler superheater / economiser sections and transferring this heat to the incoming combustion air before it enters the furnace. The heat-exchange device employed is called an **Air Preheater (APH)**. It constitutes one of the three principal heat-recovery units in a modern thermal power station (the others being the **Superheater** and the **Economiser**), and is responsible for elevating the combustion air temperature from ambient conditions to a range of **150 °C to 400 °C** depending on boiler design, fuel type, and unit capacity.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for PEICT523 (Power Plant Instrumentation), Air Preheating is treated as a critical secondary heat-recovery loop where the **measurement, control, and protection instrumentation** play a pivotal role in ensuring combustion efficiency, maintaining safe furnace conditions, and preventing cold-end corrosion of the APH itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The student is expected to understand the *purpose, types (Recuperative vs. Regenerative), instrumentation architecture, control philosophy, and the role of air preheating in overall plant heat balance.* Direct 14-mark questions are routinely asked on Ljungstrom regenerative APH and on soot-blowing sequencing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Range Reference Values:**
> - **Cold Air Temperature (CAT):** 30 °C – 60 °C (ambient + duct heater)
> - **Hot Air Temperature (HAT):** 250 °C – 400 °C
> - **APH Gas Outlet Temperature:** 120 °C – 180 °C (to economiser inlet / ESP / stack)
> - **Specific Fuel Savings:** ~1 % boiler efficiency per **20 °C** rise in combustion air temperature (approximate empirical rule).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of air preheating like **warming your hands near a bonfire before walking into a cold cave**.

- Imagine a bonfire (the **furnace**) that has just finished heating a room (boiler water / steam).
- The smoke (flue gases) leaving the bonfire is still *very hot* — you can feel the heat radiating outward.
- A person walking *toward* the fire (the **combustion air** being drawn in by the FD fan) would normally feel the cold air rushing in.
- Instead, we install a **heat exchanger** at the exit of the bonfire — essentially, we make the smoke pass through a radiator, and blow the incoming cold air over the *outside* of that radiator.
- The cold air absorbs heat from the hot flue gas (without ever mixing with it), and enters the fire *already warm*. The bonfire now burns hotter with less fuel, and the smoke leaving is cooler and cleaner.

**Why bother?**
1. **Hot air burns fuel faster and more completely** → less unburnt carbon → higher efficiency.
2. **Drying the coal** (in pulverised-fuel boilers) before it enters the furnace prevents flame instability.
3. **Reduces flue gas temperature** → less waste heat going up the chimney.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Air preheating is essentially *recycling* the waste heat of flue gas to *boost* the combustion process — a closed-loop thermal economy improvement.

---

## 1.3 Functional Role in a Thermal Power Plant

| Sub-System | Function | Heat Source | Heat Sink |
|---|---|---|---|
| Superheater | Raises steam temp. | Furnace radiation + convection | Steam |
| Economiser | Pre-heats feedwater | Flue gas (convection) | Feedwater |
