| **Air Preheater** | **Pre-heats combustion air** | **Flue gas (convection)** | **Combustion air** |
| ESP / Bag Filter | Particulate control | n/a (cleaning) | n/a |

The APH sits at the **cold-end of the gas path** (after the economiser) and the **hot-end of the air path** (before the furnace windbox).

---

## 1.4 Why Air Preheating is Instrumented

The instrumentation of the APH is critical because:

- **Efficiency monitoring** requires accurate measurement of *cold-air temperature*, *hot-air temperature*, and *flue-gas outlet temperature*.
- **Acid corrosion prevention** at the cold end demands *metal-temperature* monitoring (typically maintained above the *acid dew point* — usually **≥ 110 °C** for sulphur-bearing fuels).
- **Rotational safety** of the Ljungstrom type demands monitoring of *bearing temperature*, *motor current*, and *rotation speed*.
- **Soot accumulation** is detected through *differential pressure (ΔP)* across the APH and managed by automated soot-blowing sequences.
- **Trips and interlocks** protect the unit against fire (from unburnt fuel accumulation) and APH failure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Air Preheaters

Air preheaters fall into two broad thermodynamic categories:

### 2.1.1 Recuperative Type (Static / Heat-Exchanger Type)

In a **recuperative APH**, heat transfer occurs *continuously* across a fixed dividing wall between the hot gas stream and the cold air stream. There are **no moving parts** in the heat-transfer region.

**Sub-Types:**

1. **Tubular APH (most common in Indian subcritical units)**
   - Vertical steel tubes with flue gas flowing *inside* (downward) and air flowing *outside* (upward, in cross-flow).
   - Tubes are typically **Ø 40 mm to 50 mm**, made of **mild steel** (hot end) and **enamelled/glass-coated** (cold end) for corrosion resistance.
   - Used in units up to ~210 MW (older installations).

2. **Plate-Type APH**
   - Corrugated plates stacked alternately forming gas and air passages.
   - Compact, high heat-transfer coefficient, but prone to leakage and fouling.

3. **Steam-Coil APH (SCAH)**
   - Uses *extracted steam* instead of flue gas as the heating medium.
   - Used as a *trim heater* to maintain HAT setpoint during low-load operation when flue-gas temperature is insufficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Tubular APH construction, material selection, and acid dew-point corrosion mechanism are **very high-yield topics**.

### 2.1.2 Regenerative Type (Rotating / Ljungstrom)

In a **regenerative APH**, the heat-transfer surface itself is **rotating**. The surface alternately passes through the hot gas stream (where it *absorbs* heat) and then through the cold air stream (where it *releases* heat).

- Invented by Fredrik Ljungstrom (Sweden, 1920s).
- Dominates **modern supercritical and ultra-supercritical units** (>500 MW).
- Rotating element is a **rotor (basket)** filled with corrugated **corrugated metal sheets (heating elements)**.

**Constructional Details:**

- **Rotor:** Cylindrical, slow-rotating (1.5 – 4 RPM), driven by a *primary motor* with a *secondary standby motor*.
- **Sector Plates (Radial Seals):** Divide rotor into **hot sector** (60–70 %), **cold sector** (30–40 %), and a small *transition / leakage* zone.
- **Heating Elements:** Corrugated steel sheets stacked radially; often *duplicated* as *primary* (hot end) and *secondary* (cold end) layers.

**Why Regenerative is Preferred (despite leakage penalty):**
- Compact (⅓ the size of tubular).
- Higher heat-transfer coefficient (gas-side).
- Easier to clean (soot blowers + on-line washing).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic — Recuperative APH

1. **Flue gas** (≈ 350 °C) leaving the economiser enters the APH gas duct at the bottom.
2. Gas flows **upward through the tubes** (in tubular APH).
3. **Cold combustion air** (≈ 40 °C) from the FD fan enters at the bottom of the air casing.
4. Air flows **upward across the tubes** in cross-flow, gaining heat.
5. Heated air exits at **Hot Air Temperature (HAT)** ≈ 280 °C – 350 °C and proceeds to the furnace windbox.
6. Cooled flue gas exits at the top (≈ 130 °C – 160 °C) and proceeds to the ESP / stack.

**Thermal Boundary Conditions:**
$$T_{gas,in} > T_{air,out} > T_{air,in} > T_{gas,out}$$

This ensures feasible heat transfer in the prescribed direction at every point.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic — Ljungstrom Regenerative APH

1. Rotor rotates slowly (e.g., 2 RPM).
2. **Hot sector (60%):** Heating elements pass through hot flue gas → metal temperature rises to ≈ T_gas.
3. Rotor rotates to **cold sector (40%):** heating elements pass through cold air → heat is released to air.
4. Element temperature drops from ≈ T_gas to ≈ T_air over the rotation period.
5. Net effect: heat is transferred *from gas to air* via the *transient thermal storage* of the rotor matrix.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Recuperative APH transfers heat *simultaneously* through a wall; regenerative APH transfers heat *sequentially* via a thermal-storage matrix. The regenerative type inherently has *gas-to-air leakage* (~6–10 %) through seal clearances, slightly reducing thermal efficiency.

---

## 2.4 Acid Dew Point Corrosion (Cold-End Problem)

When sulphur-bearing fuels (coal, oil) are burnt, the flue gas contains **SO₂** and **SO₃**. These combine with moisture:

$$SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4$$

Concentrated sulphuric acid condenses on metal surfaces whose temperature falls **below the acid dew point** (typically **110 °C – 150 °C** for coal with 1–4 % sulphur).

**Consequence:**
- **Severe corrosion** of the cold-end elements of the APH.
- Solution: Use *enamelled steel*, *glass-coated tubes*, or *Corten steel*; maintain cold-end metal temperature **above 110 °C** using *recirculation* or *steam-coil air heaters*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *water dew point* (~45 °C) with *acid dew point* (~110–150 °C). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 2.5 Soot Blowing System

Deposits of fly ash, soot, and unburnt carbon accumulate on the heat-transfer surfaces, reducing the heat-transfer coefficient. **Soot blowers** are retractable high-pressure (typically 30–40 bar) steam or compressed-air lances that periodically traverse the APH.

- **Long retractable soot blowers (LRSB)** for gas-side.
- **Air-side soot blowers** for the cold-end (less common).
- **Sequence:** Programmable in DCS — usually 1–2 blows per shift per APH section.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Heat transferred in APH | $Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{HAT} - T_{CAT})$ | kW | Air-side energy balance |
| 2 | Heat transferred (gas side) | $Q = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out})$ | kW | Gas-side energy balance |
| 3 | Overall effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{HAT} - T_{CAT}}{T_{g,in} - T_{CAT}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.45 – 0.60 |
| 4 | NTU | $NTU = \dfrac{UA}{C_{min}}$ | dimensionless | For design verification |
| 5 | Approx. efficiency gain | $\Delta \eta \approx \dfrac{T_{HAT} - T_{CAT}}{20} \cdot 1\%$ | % | Empirical thumb-rule |
| 6 | Acid dew point (approx.) | $T_{dp} \approx 110 + 10 \cdot S\%$ | °C | S% = sulphur in fuel |
| 7 | Rotor speed | $n = \dfrac{1}{T_{rotation}}$ | RPM | 1.5 – 4 RPM |
| 8 | Leakage (regen.) | $\dot{m}_{leak} \approx (6\% \text{ to } 10\%) \cdot \dot{m}_g$ | kg/s | Through radial seals |
| 9 | Stack loss (Siegert) | $q_2 = K \cdot \dfrac{T_{g,out} - T_{amb}}{CO_2\%}$ | % | For boiler efficiency |
| 10 | Heat capacity rate | $C_{min} = \min(\dot{m}_a c_{p,a},\ \dot{m}_g c_{p,g})$ | kW/K | For $\varepsilon$-NTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention:** All temperatures in **°C** or **K** (interchangeable for differences); mass flow rates in **kg/s**; specific heat in **kJ/kg·K**; heat transfer in **kW** (= kJ/s).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Air preheating is universally employed in:
- Coal-fired, oil-fired, and gas-fired thermal power plants.
- Cement kilns, steel blast furnaces, and process heaters.
- Marine boiler installations.

Modern Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) plants also use **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** secondary APHs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance Across the Air Preheater

**Problem Setup:** A 210 MW coal-fired boiler uses a tubular APH. Given:
- Air mass flow rate, $\dot{m}_a = 200$ kg/s
- Gas mass flow rate, $\dot{m}_g = 210$ kg/s
- $T_{CAT} = 50$ °C, $T_{HAT} = 320$ °C
- $T_{g,in} = 380$ °C
- $c_{p,a} = 1.02$ kJ/kg·K, $c_{p,g} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K

**Find:** (a) Heat transferred, (b) Gas outlet temperature, (c) Effectiveness.

### Step 1 — Air-side Heat Duty

$$Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{HAT} - T_{CAT})$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$Q = 200 \cdot 1.02 \cdot (320 - 50)$$

$$Q = 200 \cdot 1.02 \cdot 270$$

$$Q = 55{,}080 \text{ kW} \approx 55.08 \text{ MW}$$

This is the heat absorbed by the combustion air per second.

### Step 2 — Gas-side Energy Balance

Equating air-side and gas-side heat (neglecting losses):

$$Q = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out})$$

Solving for $T_{g,out}$:

$$T_{g,out} = T_{g,in} - \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g}}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{g,out} = 380 - \dfrac{55{,}080}{210 \cdot 1.05}$$

$$T_{g,out} = 380 - \dfrac{55{,}080}{220.5}$$

$$T_{g,out} = 380 - 249.8$$

$$T_{g,out} \approx 130.2 \text{ °C}$$

This is the APH gas-outlet temperature, which must remain above the acid dew point (≈ 110 °C).

### Step 3 — Effectiveness Calculation

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{HAT} - T_{CAT}}{T_{g,in} - T_{CAT}}$$

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{320 - 50}{380 - 50} = \dfrac{270}{330}$$

$$\varepsilon \approx 0.818$$

> [!NOTE]
> A value of $\varepsilon \approx 0.82$ is unusually high; typical recuperative APHs operate at 0.45–0.60. This indicates either a very large APH (over-sized) or simplified textbook assumptions (neglecting losses). In realistic KTU numericals, $\varepsilon \approx 0.55$ is more representative.

### Step 4 — Implied Efficiency Gain (Empirical)

Using the rule $\Delta \eta \approx \dfrac{\Delta T_{air}}{20} \cdot 1\%$:

$$\Delta \eta \approx \dfrac{320 - 50}{20} \cdot 1\% = \dfrac{270}{20} \cdot 1\%$$

$$\Delta \eta \approx 13.5\%$$

This is a *theoretical maximum gain over a baseline with no preheating*. In practice, part of this gain is offset by additional ID/FD fan power and APH leakage. A more conservative estimate gives **3 % – 5 %** net boiler efficiency improvement for realistic HAT.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Acid Dew Point for Coal with Sulphur

**Given:** Coal sulphur content = 2.5 % by mass.

Using the empirical correlation:

$$T_{dp} \approx 110 + 10 \cdot S\%$$

$$T_{dp} \approx 110 + 10 \cdot 2.5 = 135 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Design Implication:** The APH cold-end metal temperature must be maintained at least **10–15 °C above** $T_{dp}$ to prevent acid condensation → target cold-end metal temperature ≈ **150 °C**. This dictates the minimum $T_{g,out}$ and influences duct heater sizing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Minimum Gas Outlet Temperature (Acid Dew Point Constraint)

The gas leaving the APH should not fall below $T_{dp} + 15$ °C.

From Step 2 of §3.1, the gas outlet temperature is governed by:

$$T_{g,out} = T_{g,in} - \dfrac{\dot{m}_a c_{p,a}(T_{HAT} - T_{CAT})}{\dot{m}_g c_{p,g}}$$

For a fixed HAT, the **only operational lever** to raise $T_{g,out}$ is to **reduce the cold-air inlet temperature** or to **reduce HAT setpoint**.

**Numerical Example:** If sulphur is 3 %:

$$T_{dp} = 110 + 10 \cdot 3 = 140 \text{ °C}$$

Minimum permissible $T_{g,out} = 140 + 15 = 155$ °C.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Effectiveness-NTU for Counter-Flow Recuperative APH

For a counter-flow heat exchanger (tubular APH approximation):

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU \cdot (1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp[-NTU \cdot (1 - C_r)]}$$

where $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ is the heat-capacity ratio.

**Numerical Example:**
- $C_r = 0.9$ (gas and air streams nearly matched in capacity rate)
- $\varepsilon_{target} = 0.55$

Solve for NTU. Using iterative substitution:

Let $\alpha = 1 - C_r = 0.1$.

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - e^{-0.1 \cdot NTU}}{1 - 0.9 \cdot e^{-0.1 \cdot NTU}} = 0.55$$

Let $X = e^{-0.1 \cdot NTU}$. Then:

$$0.55 = \dfrac{1 - X}{1 - 0.9 X}$$

$$0.55(1 - 0.9X) = 1 - X$$

$$0.55 - 0.495 X = 1 - X$$

$$X - 0.495 X = 1 - 0.55$$

$$0.505 X = 0.45$$

$$X = 0.891$$

$$-0.1 \cdot NTU = \ln(0.891) = -0.1154$$

$$NTU = 1.154$$

So the APH requires NTU ≈ 1.15 for 55 % effectiveness — typical design value.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Recuperative vs. Regenerative APH

| Parameter | Recuperative (Tubular) | Regenerative (Ljungstrom) |
|---|---|---|
| Heat-transfer mode | Steady, through wall | Periodic, via matrix storage |
| Size for same duty | Larger | ~ ⅓ the volume |
| Leakage (gas ↔ air) | Negligible | 6 % – 10 % (radial + axial seals) |
| Capital cost | Lower (smaller units) | Higher |
| Suitability for high ash | Limited (fouling) | Excellent |
| Cold-end corrosion | Severe problem | Manageable via sector zoning |
| Typical application | ≤ 210 MW, old plants | ≥ 500 MW, modern units |
| Ease of cleaning | Difficult | Easy (on-line wash possible) |
| Heat-transfer coefficient | Lower | Higher |
| Pressure drop (gas) | Moderate | Slightly higher |
| Moving parts | None | Rotor, drive motor, seals |
| Failure mode | Tube leakage, fouling | Rotor seizure, fire in duct |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Air Preheating System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EconOutlet["Economiser Outlet: Hot Flue Gas ~380 C"] --> APHGasInlet["APH Gas Inlet Duct"]
    APHGasInlet --> APHUnit["AIR PREHEATER Core"]
    APHGasInletT["Temperature Transmitter TG IN"] --> APHUnit
    APHUnit --> APHGasOutlet["APH Gas Outlet ~140 C to ESP/ID Fan"]
    APHGasOutletT["Temperature Transmitter TG OUT"] --> APHGasOutlet
    APHGasOutlet --> IDFan["Induced Draft Fan ID Fan"]
    IDFan --> Stack["Stack Chimney"]

    FDFan["Forced Draft Fan FD Fan"] --> ColdAirDuct["Cold Air Duct ~40 C"]
    ColdAirDuct --> CATTransmitter["Temperature Transmitter T CAT"]
    ColdAirDuct --> APHUnit
    APHUnit --> HotAirDuct["Hot Air Duct ~320 C"]
    HotAirDuct --> HATTransmitter["Temperature Transmitter T HAT"]
    HotAirDuct --> Windbox["Furnace Windbox"]

    APHUnit --> RotorDrive["Rotor Drive Motor Regenerative type"]
    RotorDrive --> RPMMonitor["Speed Sensor RPM"]
    RotorDrive --> BearingTemp["Bearing Temperature Sensors"]

    APHUnit --> SootBlower["Soot Blower Lance"]
    SootBlower --> DPTransmitter["Differential Pressure DP across APH"]

    APHUnit --> DCS["Distributed Control System DCS"]
    DCS --> SootBlower
    DCS --> RotorDrive
    CATTransmitter --> DCS
    HATTransmitter --> DCS
    APHGasOutletT --> DCS
    DPTransmitter --> DCS
    RPMMonitor --> DCS
    BearingTemp --> DCS

    DCS --> OperatorHMI["Operator HMI Workstation"]
    DCS --> TripLogic["Trip Logic: APH Fire / Rotor Stop"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Diagram:**
> - The flow runs from **top** (hot flue gas) → **bottom-right** (stack).
> - The flow runs from **bottom-left** (FD fan, cold air) → **right** (furnace, hot air).
> - All process variables are routed to the **DCS**, which sequences the soot blowers, controls the rotor drive, and triggers trips.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Instrumentation Loops

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Measurement["Measurement Loops"]
        M1["TT-101 Cold Air Temp T_CAT"] --> DCS1["DCS Analog Input"]
        M2["TT-102 Hot Air Temp T_HAT"] --> DCS1
        M3["TT-103 Gas Inlet T_g_in"] --> DCS1
        M4["TT-104 Gas Outlet T_g_out"] --> DCS1
        M5["PT-101 Differential Pressure DP"] --> DCS1
        M6["ST-101 Rotor Speed RPM"] --> DCS1
        M7["TT-105 Bearing Temperature"] --> DCS1
    end

    subgraph Control["Control & Actuation"]
        DCS1 --> Controller["PID Controller in DCS"]
        Controller --> TripLogic2["Trip Logic Block"]
        TripLogic2 --> Output1["SOV Soot Blower Steam Valve"]
        TripLogic2 --> Output2["VFD Rotor Drive Motor"]
        TripLogic2 --> Output3["FD Fan Inlet Damper"]
        TripLogic2 --> Alarm1["Operator Alarm Annunciator"]
    end

    subgraph Protection["Protection Layer"]
        TripLogic2 --> MasterTrip["Master Trip: APH Fire / Rotor Trip"]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Ljungstrom Regenerative APH (Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HotGasStream["Hot Gas Stream from Economiser"]
        HGIn["Flue Gas Inlet ~380 C"] --> HGDown["Hot Sector Top Zone"]
        HGDown --> HGOut["Flue Gas Outlet ~140 C"]
    end

    subgraph Rotor["Rotating Rotor with Heating Elements"]
        R1["Corrugated Steel Sheets Heating Matrix"] --> R2["Radial Sector Plates"]
        R2 --> R3["Central Shaft Driven by Motor"]
    end

    subgraph ColdAirStream["Cold Air Stream from FD Fan"]
        CAIn["Cold Air Inlet ~40 C"] --> CACold["Cold Sector"]
        CACold --> CAOut["Hot Air Outlet ~320 C to Windbox"]
    end

    HotGasStream --> Rotor
    Rotor --> ColdAirStream

    Rotor --> Bearings["Bottom and Top Bearings"]
    Bearings --> DriveMotor["Drive Motor with Gearbox"]
    DriveMotor --> StandbyMotor["Standby Motor Auto-start"]

    HGOut --> ESP["To ESP and ID Fan"]
    CAOut --> Windbox2["To Furnace Windbox"]
```

---

## 4.4 Control Philosophy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Unit on Load"]) --> Monitor["Monitor T_HAT T_CAT T_g_out DP RPM"]
    Monitor --> CompareHAT{"HAT within HAT setpoint plus minus 10 C?"}
    CompareHAT -->|Yes| Normal["Normal Operation"]
    CompareHAT -->|No Low| IncreaseHAT["Reduce Cold Air Inlet Increase FD Damper"]
    CompareHAT -->|No High| DecreaseHAT["Increase Cold Air or Steam Trim"]

    Normal --> SootCheck{"DP across APH greater than setpoint?"}
    SootCheck -->|Yes| FireSoot["Initiate Soot Blowing Sequence"]
    SootCheck -->|No| Monitor

    Monitor --> AcidCheck{"T_g_out less than T_dp plus 15?"}
    AcidCheck -->|Yes| ColdEndAlarm["Cold End Corrosion Alarm"]
    AcidCheck -->|No| Monitor

    Monitor --> FireCheck{"APH Fire Detected via Temperature Spike?"}
    FireCheck -->|Yes| FireTrip["Master Trip APH Steam Soot Blowers ON Fire Water"]
    FireCheck -->|No| Monitor
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Every KTU question on APH control should reference at least three control loops:
> 1. **HAT control loop** (manipulated variable = FD fan damper or steam trim).
> 2. **Soot-blowing sequencing loop** (manipulated variable = ΔP setpoint).
> 3. **Cold-end protection loop** (manipulated variable = air-recirculation damper).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]

**Q: Differentiate between a recuperative air preheater and a regenerative air preheater. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Recuperative APH | Regenerative APH |
|---|---|---|
| Heat-transfer mechanism | Continuous, through fixed wall | Periodic, via rotating matrix |
| Moving parts | None | Rotor, drive motor |
| Leakage | Negligible | 6 – 10 % |
| Size | Larger for same duty | ~ ⅓ smaller |
| **Advantage** | No leakage, simple construction | Compact, easier to clean, higher ash tolerance |

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Correct distinction (mechanism) — 1 mark
- Correct distinction (moving parts/leakage) — 1 mark
- One valid advantage — 1 mark

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: What is acid dew point corrosion in an air preheater? How is it prevented?**

**Model Answer:**
Acid dew-point corrosion occurs when sulphur in the fuel forms **SO₃**, which combines with moisture in the flue gas to form **H₂SO₄**. This acid condenses on the cold-end APH elements whose temperature falls below the acid dew point (~110–150 °C), causing severe corrosion.

**Prevention methods:**
1. Use **enamelled steel** or **glass-coated tubes** at the cold end.
2. Maintain **cold-end metal temperature ≥ T_dp + 15 °C**.
3. Use **air-recirculation** to warm incoming air.
4. Use **steam-coil air heater (SCAH)** for low-load operation.
5. Burn **low-sulphur coal** when feasible.

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition of acid dew-point corrosion — 1 mark
- Cause (SO₃ + H₂O → H₂SO₄) — 1 mark
- Any two prevention methods — 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Ljungstrom regenerative air preheater**. List its main components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the instrumentation and control scheme for a regenerative APH. Discuss the role of the **DCS** in sequencing soot blowers and protecting against fire. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction:**

1. **Rotor (Basket):** A large cylindrical structure (≈ 5–10 m diameter) filled with thin, corrugated **heating elements** made of mild steel (hot end) and Corten/glass-coated steel (cold end). The rotor rotates at **1.5 – 4 RPM** about a vertical shaft.

2. **Casing:** A stationary outer shell divided into three sectors:
   - **Hot Gas Sector** (60–70 % of cross-section): hot flue gas flows upward.
   - **Cold Air Sector** (30–40 %): combustion air flows upward.
   - **Leakage / Transition Sector** (small zone between hot and cold sides).

3. **Sector Plates (Radial Seals):** Stationary plates that prevent gas-to-air leakage. **Axial seals** prevent gas bypass along the rotor circumference.

4. **Drive System:** A **primary motor** with a gearbox, and an **automatic-start standby motor**.

5. **Bearings:** Top and bottom guide and thrust bearings.

**Working:**

- Rotor rotates slowly.
- Heating elements absorb heat from hot flue gas as they pass through the **hot sector**.
- The same elements release heat to cold air as they rotate into the **cold sector**.
- Net effect: heat transfer from flue gas to combustion air.

**Components List (for 1 mark):**
- Rotor, heating elements, casing, sector plates, radial/axial seals, drive motor, standby motor, bearings, soot blowers.

**Marks Breakdown:**
- Construction details (rotor, casing, seals, drive) — 4 marks
- Working principle — 2 marks
- Component list / neat sketch description — 1 mark

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Instrumentation:**

1. **Temperature Transmitters (TT):**
   - TT-101: Cold air temperature (T_CAT)
   - TT-102: Hot air temperature (T_HAT)
   - TT-103: APH gas inlet temperature (T_g,in)
   - TT-104: APH gas outlet temperature (T_g,out)
   - TT-105: Rotor bearing temperature
   - TT-106: APH fire detection (rapid T rise)

2. **Differential Pressure (DP):**
   - Across APH gas path (used for soot accumulation detection)

3. **Speed Sensor (ST):**
   - Rotor RPM monitoring

4. **Control Valves / Actuators:**
   - FD fan inlet damper
   - Steam trim heater control valve
   - Soot-blower isolation valves (SOVs)

**DCS-Based Control Scheme:**

- **HAT Control Loop:** PID controller maintains T_HAT within ± 10 °C of setpoint by adjusting the FD fan inlet damper or SCAH steam valve.
- **Soot Blowing Sequence:** DCS auto-initiates blowing when ΔP exceeds threshold (e.g., 1.5× clean ΔP). Lances traverse the APH on a programmed schedule (e.g., 2 blows/shift).
- **Rotor Protection:** If rotor speed drops to zero or bearing temperature exceeds limit → master trip.
- **Fire Protection:** Sudden T rise in APH (unburnt fuel accumulation) → emergency steam soot blowing + fire-water spray + unit trip.

**Marks Breakdown:**
- Instrumentation list (TT, DP, ST) — 3 marks
- DCS control loops (HAT, soot, fire, rotor) — 3 marks
- Role of DCS — 1 mark

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT confuse** *recuperative* and *regenerative* APH construction — many students describe both interchangeably, leading to **3-mark loss**.
> - For Ljungstrom, students often forget to mention **axial seals** alongside radial seals — **lose 1 mark**.
> - In control questions, students list sensors but **fail to explain the control action** — full marks require both *measurement* and *manipulation*.
> - *Fire detection* and *cold-end corrosion protection* are frequent sub-parts; missing these loses **2 marks**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain the purpose of air preheating in a thermal power plant. Derive the relationship between air preheat and boiler efficiency improvement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coal-fired boiler uses a tubular air preheater with the following data:
- Air mass flow = 250 kg/s, $c_{p,a}$ = 1.02 kJ/kg·K
- Flue gas mass flow = 260 kg/s, $c_{p,g}$ = 1.05 kJ/kg·K
- $T_{CAT}$ = 45 °C, $T_{g,in}$ = 360 °C
- Required effectiveness ε = 0.55

Calculate: (i) Heat transferred, (ii) Hot air temperature, (iii) Gas outlet temperature, (iv) Verify the cold-end metal temperature is above the acid dew point for coal sulphur = 2 %. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Purpose of Air Preheating:**

1. **Improves combustion efficiency** — hot air dries pulverised coal and accelerates flame stability.
2. **Reduces fuel consumption** — raises flame temperature and improves carbon burnout.
3. **Recovers waste heat** from flue gas, improving overall boiler efficiency.
4. **Reduces stack losses** by lowering the flue gas exit temperature.
5. **Prevents flame instability** in pulverised-coal boilers.

**Efficiency Improvement (Empirical Derivation):**

For every **20 °C rise** in combustion-air temperature, boiler efficiency improves by approximately **1 %** (Sievert's rule-of-thumb, refined by ASME).

$$\Delta \eta \approx \dfrac{T_{HAT} - T_{CAT}}{20} \cdot 1\%$$

This is because the air-side enthalpy gain directly substitutes for fuel-side enthalpy that would otherwise be lost in flue gas.

**Marks Breakdown:**
- 4–5 purposes — 4 marks
- Efficiency relation with derivation — 3 marks

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_a = 250$ kg/s, $c_{p,a} = 1.02$ kJ/kg·K
- $\dot{m}_g = 260$ kg/s, $c_{p,g} = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K
- $T_{CAT} = 45$ °C, $T_{g,in} = 360$ °C, $\varepsilon = 0.55$
- Coal S = 2 %

**Step (i): Heat Transferred**

We first find $T_{HAT}$ from the effectiveness equation:

$$T_{HAT} = T_{CAT} + \varepsilon (T_{g,in} - T_{CAT})$$

$$T_{HAT} = 45 + 0.55 (360 - 45)$$

$$T_{HAT} = 45 + 0.55 \cdot 315 = 45 + 173.25 = 218.25 \text{ °C}$$

**Heat transferred (air side):**

$$Q = \dot{m}_a c_{p,a} (T_{HAT} - T_{CAT})$$

$$Q = 250 \cdot 1.02 \cdot (218.25 - 45)$$

$$Q = 255 \cdot 173.25$$

$$Q \approx 44{,}179 \text{ kW} \approx 44.18 \text{ MW}$$

**Step (ii): Hot Air Temperature**

$$T_{HAT} = 218.25 \text{ °C}$$

**Step (iii): Gas Outlet Temperature**

$$T_{g,out} = T_{g,in} - \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g}}$$

$$T_{g,out} = 360 - \dfrac{44{,}179}{260 \cdot 1.05}$$

$$T_{g,out} = 360 - \dfrac{44{,}179}{273}$$

$$T_{g,out} = 360 - 161.8$$

$$T_{g,out} \approx 198.2 \text{ °C}$$

**Step (iv): Acid Dew Point Verification**

For coal sulphur = 2 %:

$$T_{dp} = 110 + 10 \cdot 2 = 130 \text{ °C}$$

Since $T_{g,out} = 198.2 \text{ °C} > T_{dp} + 15 = 145 \text{ °C}$, the cold-end metal temperature is safely above the acid dew point. ✓

**Conclusion:** Operation is safe; corrosion is not a concern under these conditions.

**Marks Breakdown:**
- Correct HAT calculation — 2 marks
- Q calculation — 2 marks
- T_g,out calculation — 1 mark
- Acid dew point verification with comment — 2 marks

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Air Preheater (APH)** is the **third** heat-recovery unit after Superheater and Economiser.
- [x] Two broad types: **Recuperative (Tubular / Plate / SCAH)** and **Regenerative (Ljungstrom)**.
- [x] **Tubular APH** — cold air flows outside vertical tubes; hot flue gas inside. No leakage.
- [x] **Ljungstrom APH** — rotating rotor with corrugated heating elements; 6–10 % leakage is unavoidable.
- [x] Standard range: **T_CAT ≈ 30–60 °C**; **T_HAT ≈ 250–400 °C**.
- [x] **Acid dew point** ≈ **110 + 10·S %**; cold-end metal must be ≥ T_dp + 15 °C.
- [x] **Effectiveness formula**: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{T_{HAT} - T_{CAT}}{T_{g,in} - T_{CAT}}$.
- [x] **Efficiency rule-of-thumb**: 1 % efficiency gain per 20 °C rise in combustion-air temperature.
- [x] Key instrumentation: **TT (CAT, HAT, gas in/out, bearing), DP, ST (rotor RPM), SOVs**.
- [x] Control philosophy: **HAT control loop, Soot-blowing sequencing, Cold-end protection, Fire-trip logic**.
- [x] Soot blowers operate on **steam or compressed air** at 30–40 bar; sequence triggered by ΔP setpoint.
- [x] **Master Trip** on APH: rotor standstill + bearing OT + fire detection → unit trip.
- [x] **Enamelled / Corten / glass-coated steel** used at the cold end for corrosion resistance.
- [x] **Steam-Coil Air Heater (SCAH)** is used as a trim heater during low-load operation.
- [x] **NTU ≈ 1.15** for ε ≈ 0.55 in counter-flow recuperative APH.
- [x] For KTU: emphasise *block diagram + control loops + acid dew point* — these three together guarantee 10+ marks in any 14-mark question.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** Memorise the *formula sheet (§2.6)*, the *comparison matrix (§3.5)*, and be ready to *redraw the Ljungstrom block diagram (§4.3)* from memory. These three assets alone can secure 12+ out of 14 marks on any air-preheating question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** is the mass ratio of air to fuel introduced into the combustion chamber of a power plant boiler, furnace, or internal combustion engine. It is one of the most critical controlled parameters in any thermal power generation system, as it directly determines combustion efficiency, flame stability, pollutant formation (NOx, CO, SO₂, unburnt hydrocarbons), and heat release rate.

$$\text{AFR} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$$

The **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio** (also called the theoretical or chemically correct ratio) is the exact mass of air required to completely burn a unit mass of fuel into fully oxidized products — CO₂, H₂O, and SO₂ — with no residual oxygen.

| Fuel Type | Stoichiometric AFR (by mass) |
|---|---|
| Methane (CH₄) | 17.16 : 1 |
| Gasoline (Octane C₈H₁₈) | 15.13 : 1 |
| Diesel (C₁₂H₂₆) | 14.6 : 1 |
| Kerosene | 14.7 : 1 |
| Natural Gas | 14.5 – 17.4 : 1 |
| Coal (bituminous) | 6 – 10 : 1 |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | 34.3 : 1 |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Cooking Burner

Imagine lighting a domestic LPG stove:
- If the gas knob is opened wide but the air inlet is nearly closed, the flame is **yellow and sooty** — fuel is wasted, smoke forms, and pans get blackened. This is a **rich mixture** (too little air).
- If the air inlet is opened too far, the flame becomes **pale blue, short, and may even blow out** — energy is wasted heating excess nitrogen that escapes up the flue. This is a **lean mixture** (too much air).
- The ideal sweet spot is a **crisp, steady, deep blue cone** — this is the *stoichiometric* zone where every molecule of fuel meets exactly the oxygen it needs.

A 500 MW thermal power station does the same thing — but with **~400 tonnes of coal per hour** and **~4.5 million m³ of air per hour**. A small error of 1% in AFR can waste crores of rupees annually in fuel and environmental penalties.

## 1.3 Key Operational Zones

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical Zones of Air-Fuel Ratio Operation:**
> 1. **Rich Mixture (AFR < Stoichiometric):** Insufficient O₂, produces CO, soot, smoke. Common during startup.
> 2. **Stoichiometric (AFR = Theoretical):** Theoretically complete combustion. Rarely used in practice due to flame instability.
> 3. **Lean Mixture (AFR > Stoichiometric):** Excess O₂ ensures complete burn but lowers flame temperature and efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Equivalence Ratio (φ)** is the reciprocal concept often used by combustion engineers:
> $$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoichiometric}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$$
> - φ < 1 → Lean mixture
> - φ = 1 → Stoichiometric
> - φ > 1 → Rich mixture

## 1.4 Role in Power Plant Instrumentation

In KTU's Power Plant Instrumentation syllabus (Module 2), the Air-Fuel Ratio is the foundation for several control loops:
- **Cross-limited (parabolic) combustion control** — uses AFR as the master variable
- **Oxygen trim control** — uses flue-gas O₂ analyzer feedback (typical setpoint: 2–4% O₂ in flue gas)
- **Boiler efficiency optimization** — AFR directly drives stack loss and incomplete combustion loss
- **Emission monitoring** — Excess air determines NOx formation (thermal and fuel-bound)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry of Complete Combustion

For any fossil fuel, complete combustion follows these elemental reactions:

$$\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2$$

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_2$$

Each element demands a specific mass of oxygen. From atomic weights:
- 12 kg of Carbon needs 32 kg of O₂ → **2.667 kg O₂ per kg C**
- 4 kg of Hydrogen needs 32 kg of O₂ → **8.000 kg O₂ per kg H₂**
- 32 kg of Sulfur needs 32 kg of O₂ → **1.000 kg O₂ per kg S**

## 2.2 The Role of Atmospheric Air

Dry atmospheric air is **23% oxygen and 77% nitrogen by mass** (approximately 21% and 79% by volume). Therefore the mass of air required is:

$$m_{air} = \frac{m_{O_2 \text{ required}}}{0.232}$$

The minimum theoretical air per kg of fuel is:

$$A_0 = \frac{8}{3}\left(\frac{\text{C}}{100}\right) + 8\left(\frac{\text{H}_2}{100}\right) + \frac{\text{S}}{100} \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]}$$

where C, H₂, S are the mass percentages of carbon, hydrogen, and sulfur in the fuel on a dry basis.

## 2.3 Excess Air — The Industrial Reality

In practice, **100% theoretical air never achieves complete combustion** because fuel and air do not mix perfectly in the limited furnace residence time (~2–4 seconds). Engineers therefore supply more than the stoichiometric air.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** **Excess Air (EA)** is the air supplied beyond the theoretical (stoichiometric) requirement, expressed as a percentage of the theoretical air.

$$EA = \frac{A_{actual} - A_0}{A_0} \times 100\%$$

$$A_{actual} = A_0 \left(1 + \frac{EA}{100}\right)$$

**Typical Excess Air Values in Power Plant Boilers:**

| Boiler Type | % Excess Air |
|---|---|
| Pulverized coal, suspension fired | 15 – 25% |
| Stoker-fired (chain grate) | 25 – 50% |
| Oil-fired | 5 – 15% |
| Gas-fired | 5 – 10% |
| Fluidized bed combustion (FBC) | 25 – 40% |

## 2.4 The Link Between Excess Air and Flue-Gas Oxygen

A very useful instrumentation relation — and a high-yield KTU formula — connects excess air to the oxygen percentage in the dry flue gas (assuming no CO, perfect mixing, and negligible unburnt combustibles):

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ (\% in dry flue gas)} = \frac{0.232 \times EA}{1 + 0.232 \times EA} \times 100$$

Equivalently:

$$EA = \frac{0.268 \times \text{O}_2\%}{21 - \text{O}_2\%}$$

**Example KTU-type numerical:** If the flue gas O₂ analyzer reads 3.5%, then:

$$EA = \frac{0.268 \times 3.5}{21 - 3.5} = \frac{0.938}{17.5} = 5.36\%$$

This means only **5.36% excess air** — typical for a well-tuned gas-fired boiler.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Formula Table for Air-Fuel Ratio Problems**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | AFR | $\dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | kg/kg |
| 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ demand | $\dfrac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 3 | Theoretical air $A_0$ | $\dfrac{\frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 4 | Actual air supplied | $A_0\left(1 + \dfrac{EA}{100}\right)$ | kg air / kg fuel |
| 5 | Excess air from O₂ | $\dfrac{0.268 \cdot \text{O}_2\%}{21 - \text{O}_2\%}$ | % |
| 6 | Flue-gas O₂ from EA | $\dfrac{0.232 \cdot EA}{1 + 0.232 \cdot EA}$ | % |
| 7 | Equivalence ratio φ | $\dfrac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | — |
| 8 | Air mass per mole fuel | $n_{fuel} \cdot (4.773 a + 0.5 b + c)$ | kg | 

*In formula 8, for hydrocarbon $C_a H_b O_c$ (gas-phase), 4.773 = 28.97/32 × 4 gives kg air per mole of O₂ reaction.*

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Air-Fuel Ratio directly determines:
- **Thermal efficiency** — every 1% reduction in excess air improves boiler efficiency by ~0.5–0.8%.
- **NOx emissions** — thermal NOx scales exponentially with peak flame temperature; too lean a mixture (excess air) raises flame T, increasing NOx.
- **CO and unburnt carbon** — too rich a mixture (insufficient air) increases CO and soot.
- **Stack heat loss** — excess air carries sensible heat up the chimney as hot N₂.
- **Acid rain precursors** — SO₂ and NOx from incomplete mixing.

A modern Distributed Control System (DCS) in a 500 MW unit maintains flue gas O₂ within ±0.5% of setpoint, translating to millions of rupees in annual fuel savings.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air Requirement for a Coal Sample

This is the **most common KTU numerical problem** — given the ultimate analysis of a coal, find the theoretical air required, the actual air with given excess, and the wet/dry flue gas composition.

**Problem Statement:** A coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
- C = 78%, H₂ = 5%, S = 1.5%, O₂ = 6%, N₂ = 1.5%, Moisture = 3%, Ash = 5%

Find:
1. Theoretical air required per kg of coal
2. Actual air with 30% excess air
3. Mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel
4. Percentage of O₂ in dry flue gas

---

### Step 1: Identify oxygen-consuming and oxygen-supplying elements

Carbon, hydrogen, and sulfur **consume** oxygen during combustion. The oxygen already present in the fuel partially offsets the oxygen needed from air. Nitrogen, moisture, and ash are **inert** (do not participate in combustion).

### Step 2: Calculate oxygen demand from each combustible element

Using the molecular weights (C = 12, H₂ = 2, S = 32, O₂ = 32):

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for Carbon} = \frac{32}{12} \times 0.78 = 2.0800 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for Hydrogen} = \frac{32}{4} \times 0.05 = 0.4000 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for Sulfur} = \frac{32}{32} \times 0.015 = 0.0150 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Total O}_2 \text{ demanded} = 2.0800 + 0.4000 + 0.0150 = 2.4950 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 3: Subtract oxygen already present in the fuel

$$\text{Net O}_2 \text{ required from air} = 2.4950 - 0.06 = 2.4350 \text{ kg O}_2 \text{ per kg coal}$$

### Step 4: Convert to mass of air

Since dry air is 23.2% oxygen by mass:

$$A_0 = \frac{2.4350}{0.232} = 10.495 \text{ kg air per kg coal}$$

### Step 5: Apply the closed-form expression (verification)

Using the master formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{1}{0.232}\left[\frac{8}{3}(C) + 8(H_2) + (S) - (O_2)\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.232}\left[\frac{8}{3}(0.78) + 8(0.05) + (0.015) - (0.06)\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{0.232}\left[2.080 + 0.400 + 0.015 - 0.060\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2.435}{0.232} = 10.495 \text{ kg/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

✅ **Verified.**

### Step 6: Actual air with 30% excess air

$$A_{actual} = A_0 \left(1 + \frac{EA}{100}\right) = 10.495 \times 1.30 = 13.644 \text{ kg air per kg coal}$$

### Step 7: Mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal

Dry flue gas = (CO₂ from C) + (SO₂ from S) + (N₂ from air) + (N₂ from fuel) + (excess O₂)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{CO}_2 \text{ produced} &= \frac{44}{12} \times 0.78 = 2.860 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{SO}_2 \text{ produced} &= \frac{64}{32} \times 0.015 = 0.030 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{N}_2 \text{ from air} &= 13.644 \times 0.768 = 10.478 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{N}_2 \text{ from fuel} &= 0.015 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{Excess O}_2 &= (13.644 - 10.495) \times 0.232 = 3.149 \times 0.232 = 0.731 \text{ kg} \\[4pt]
\text{Total dry flue gas} &= 2.860 + 0.030 + 10.478 + 0.015 + 0.731 \\[4pt]
&= 14.114 \text{ kg per kg coal}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 8: Percentage of O₂ in dry flue gas

$$\text{O}_2\% = \frac{0.731}{14.114} \times 100 = 5.18\%$$

### Step 9: Cross-check with the O₂–EA formula

$$\text{O}_2\% = \frac{0.232 \times 30}{1 + 0.232 \times 30} = \frac{6.96}{7.96} = 87.4\%$$

⚠️ **Wait — that does not match.** This is a *critical KTU pitfall*. The simplified formula assumes the fuel is **pure hydrocarbon with no nitrogen, ash, or moisture**. For a real coal with 6% inherent O₂, 1.5% N₂, 3% moisture, and 5% ash, the simple O₂–EA relation **overestimates** flue-gas O₂. The correct, instrument-grade answer is from the full mass balance above: **5.18% O₂**.

This is why DCS systems must use **mass-balance oxygen trim**, not the simplified empirical formula.

## 3.2 Derivation: AFR for a Gaseous Fuel (Methane CH₄)

**Reaction:**
$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Molecular masses:** CH₄ = 16 g/mol, O₂ = 32 g/mol, Air ≈ 28.97 g/mol

**Oxygen per mole of CH₄:** 2 mol × 32 = 64 g

**Air per mole of CH₄:** $\dfrac{64}{0.232} = 275.86 \text{ g}$

**AFR (mass basis):**
$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{275.86}{16} = 17.24 : 1$$

This matches the well-known value 17.16:1 (small differences due to rounding and exact O₂ fraction in air).

## 3.3 Derivation: AFR for a Liquid Fuel (Diesel, approximated as C₁₂H₂₆)

**Reaction:**
$$\text{C}_{12}\text{H}_{26} + \frac{37}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 12\text{CO}_2 + 13\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Molecular mass of C₁₂H₂₆:** $12(12) + 26(1) = 170 \text{ g/mol}$

**Oxygen required:** $\frac{37}{2} \times 32 = 592 \text{ g}$

**Air required:** $\dfrac{592}{0.232} = 2551.7 \text{ g}$

**AFR (stoichiometric):**
$$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{2551.7}{170} = 15.01 : 1$$

The accepted automotive value of ~14.6:1 reflects the actual diesel composition (C₁₃.₅H₂₅ and aromatics).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Excess Air

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effect of Excess Air on Boiler Performance (qualitative trend, used in KTU short-answer questions)**

| Parameter | Effect of Increasing Excess Air |
|---|---|
| Combustion completeness (CO, soot) | Improves (decreases) |
| Flue gas mass flow | Increases |
| Stack heat loss | Increases |
| Boiler thermal efficiency | Decreases (after an optimum) |
| Flame temperature | Decreases (more N₂ to heat) |
| NOx (thermal) | Decreases (lower flame T) |
| NOx (fuel-bound) | Slight decrease |
| Air-handling fan power | Increases |
| Furnace heat release rate | Decreases slightly |

**There exists an *optimum excess air* (typically 15–20% for coal) that maximizes the trade-off between complete combustion and stack loss.** This is the entire basis of the **Oxygen Trim Control** loop in modern boilers.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — AFR Control Loop in a Pulverized Coal Boiler

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the Air-Fuel Ratio is sensed, controlled, and trimmed in a typical tangentially-fired 500 MW unit. Each node represents either a physical element (mill, FD fan, furnace) or an instrumentation block (transmitter, controller, final control element).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Bunker] --> B[Pulverizer Mill]
    B -->|Pulverized Coal| C[Coal Pipes]
    C --> D[Boiler Furnace]
    E[Ambient Air] --> F[FD Fan]
    F --> G[Air Preheater]
    G -->|Hot Primary Air| C
    G -->|Hot Secondary Air| D
    H[Steam from Turbine] --> I[Feedwater Pump]
    I --> D
    D -->|Hot Flue Gas| J[Air Preheater]
    J --> K[ESP Precipitator]
    K --> L[ID Fan]
    L --> M[Stack]
    N[O2 Analyzer] -->|3-4% O2| P[DCS Controller]
    O[Coal Flow Transmitter] -->|kg per s| P
    P -->|Setpoint to FD Fan| F
    P -->|Setpoint to Mill| B
    P -->|Setpoint to Coal Feeder| A
    D --> Q[Steam Drum]
    Q --> R[Turbine]
    R --> S[Generator]
    S --> T[Power Grid]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **furnace (D)** is the mixing zone where coal and air meet.
- The **O₂ analyzer (N)** samples flue gas post-furnace and provides feedback.
- The **DCS controller (P)** implements a **cross-limited (parabolic) strategy**: it takes the *minimum* of "fuel demand" and "air demand" to ensure neither over-rich nor over-lean operation during transients.
- The final control elements are the **FD fan speed (F)**, **mill feeder (B)**, and **air damper positions**.

## 4.2 Cross-Limited Combustion Control — Block-Level Architecture

The cross-limited (also called *parabolic* or *lead-lag*) control uses two parallel paths for fuel and air setpoints, each constrained by the other. This prevents both black smoke (rich) and flameout (lean) during load changes.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph demand [Master Demand Signal from Boiler Load]
        LD[Load Demand e.g. MW]
    end
    LD --> FC[Function Generator for Fuel Curve]
    LD --> AC[Function Generator for Air Curve]
    FC --> FHS[High Selector for Fuel]
    AC --> ALS[Low Selector for Air]
    FHS --> FCO[Fuel Controller Output]
    ALS --> ACO[Air Controller Output]
    FCO -->|Actuates| FM[Coal Feeder Motor]
    ACO -->|Actuates| AFV[FD Fan Inlet Vanes]
    FM --> FURN[Boiler Furnace]
    AFV --> FURN
    FURN --> O2S[O2 Sensor in Flue Gas]
    O2S --> OT[O2 Trim Controller]
    OT -->|Trim Signal| AC
    FURN --> FS[Flow Sensor Fuel]
    FS --> FT[Fuel Transmitter]
    FT --> FCO
    FURN --> AS[Flow Sensor Air]
    AS --> AT[Air Transmitter]
    AT --> ACO
```

**How it works (KTU explanation):**
1. **Load demand** generates parallel setpoints for fuel and air.
2. **High selector** ensures fuel setpoint never exceeds what the current air flow can support (prevents going rich on increasing load).
3. **Low selector** ensures air setpoint never falls below what the current fuel flow requires (prevents going lean on decreasing load).
4. **O₂ trim** continuously adjusts the air setpoint to maintain ~3% flue gas O₂, rejecting fuel composition variations.

## 4.3 Combustion Stoichiometry Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Composition] --> B[Elemental Analysis C H S O N Ash Moisture]
    B --> C[Oxygen Demand per kg Fuel]
    C --> D[Theoretical Air A0]
    D --> E[Excess Air EA]
    E --> F[Actual Air Supplied]
    F --> G[Flue Gas Composition]
    G --> H[Flue Gas O2 Analyzed]
    H --> I[Closed Loop O2 Trim]
    I -->|Feedback| E
    D --> J[AFR Stoichiometric]
    F --> K[AFR Actual]
    J --> L[Equivalence Ratio phi]
    K --> L
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define the terms: (i) Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio, (ii) Excess Air, and (iii) Equivalence Ratio. State the typical range of excess air used in a pulverized coal-fired boiler.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio:** It is the mass of air theoretically required to burn 1 kg of fuel completely into fully oxidized products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂) with no unburnt fuel and no leftover O₂. For gasoline, the stoichiometric AFR is approximately 15.13:1, and for bituminous coal it ranges from 6:1 to 10:1.

(ii) **Excess Air:** It is the amount of air supplied in excess of the theoretical/stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage of the theoretical air. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$EA = \frac{A_{actual} - A_0}{A_0} \times 100\%$$

(iii) **Equivalence Ratio (φ):** The ratio of the stoichiometric AFR to the actual AFR:

$$\phi = \frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$$

- φ = 1 → stoichiometric
- φ < 1 → lean
- φ > 1 → rich

**Typical excess air for a pulverized coal-fired boiler:** **15% – 25%.**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sample Question]**
**Why is excess air supplied in practical combustion systems? What are the consequences of supplying (a) too little and (b) too much excess air?**

**Model Answer:**

Theoretically, stoichiometric air should suffice for complete combustion. However, in practice, perfect mixing of fuel and air cannot be achieved within the limited furnace residence time (2–4 seconds). Therefore, **excess air is supplied to ensure complete combustion and to compensate for poor fuel-air mixing, fuel-bound nitrogen, and load transients.**

**(a) Consequences of too little excess air (rich mixture, φ > 1):**
- Incomplete combustion → production of **CO, soot, smoke, and unburnt hydrocarbons**
- **Loss of calorific value** — chemical energy escapes up the stack as CO
- **Smoke and opacity violations** — environmental non-compliance
- **Soot deposits** on heat transfer surfaces, reducing heat transfer and risking tube failures
- For a CFBC boiler, may cause bed defluidization

**(b) Consequences of too much excess air (lean mixture, φ < 1):**
- **Large stack heat loss** — excess N₂ (which constitutes 77% of air) carries away sensible heat
- **Reduced boiler efficiency** — typically every 1% increase in excess air above optimum reduces efficiency by 0.5–0.8%
- **Lower flame temperature** — reduces heat transfer by radiation
- **Increased fan power consumption** — more air must be moved
- Eventually, if the mixture becomes too lean, **flame instability and blow-off** can occur

**Optimum excess air** is the value that minimizes the sum of (incomplete combustion loss + stack loss), typically **15–20% for coal, 5–10% for gas, 5–15% for oil.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 70%, H₂ = 5%, S = 1%, O₂ = 8%, N₂ = 1.5%, Moisture = 4%, Ash = 10.5%. Calculate:
  (i) Theoretical air required per kg of coal
  (ii) Actual air required per kg of coal at 25% excess air
  (iii) Mass of dry flue gas per kg of coal
  (iv) Percentage composition of dry flue gas by mass  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **cross-limited (parabolic) combustion control** strategy. How does it prevent both rich and lean combustion during load transients?  **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given Ultimate Analysis (per kg of dry coal, moisture and ash treated as inert for stoichiometry):**

| Component | % by mass |
|---|---|
| Carbon (C) | 70 |
| Hydrogen (H₂) | 5 |
| Sulfur (S) | 1 |
| Oxygen (O₂) | 8 |
| Nitrogen (N₂) | 1.5 |
| Moisture | 4 |
| Ash | 10.5 |

**Step 1: Oxygen required for each combustible element (per kg coal)**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for C} = \frac{32}{12} \times 0.70 = \frac{32 \times 0.70}{12} = 1.8667 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for H}_2 = \frac{32}{4} \times 0.05 = 8 \times 0.05 = 0.4000 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for S} = \frac{32}{32} \times 0.01 = 1 \times 0.01 = 0.0100 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Total O}_2 \text{ demanded} = 1.8667 + 0.4000 + 0.0100 = 2.2767 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Subtract fuel-borne oxygen**

$$\text{Net O}_2 \text{ from air} = 2.2767 - 0.08 = 2.1967 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Convert to theoretical air (per kg coal)**

$$A_0 = \frac{2.1967}{0.232} = 9.468 \text{ kg air / kg coal}$$

**(i) Theoretical air required = 9.468 kg per kg of coal.**

**Step 4: Actual air with 25% excess**

$$A_{actual} = 9.468 \times 1.25 = 11.835 \text{ kg air / kg coal}$$

**(ii) Actual air = 11.835 kg per kg of coal.**

**Step 5: Mass of dry flue gas**

- CO₂ produced: $\dfrac{44}{12} \times 0.70 = 2.5667 \text{ kg}$
- SO₂ produced: $\dfrac{64}{32} \times 0.01 = 0.0200 \text{ kg}$
- N₂ from air: $11.835 \times 0.768 = 9.089 \text{ kg}$
- N₂ from fuel: $0.015 \text{ kg}$
- Excess O₂: $(11.835 - 9.468) \times 0.232 = 2.367 \times 0.232 = 0.549 \text{ kg}$

$$\text{Total dry flue gas} = 2.5667 + 0.0200 + 9.089 + 0.015 + 0.549 = 12.240 \text{ kg per kg coal}$$

**(iii) Dry flue gas mass = 12.24 kg per kg coal.**

**Step 6: Composition of dry flue gas (by mass)**

| Component | Mass (kg) | % by mass |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 2.567 | 20.97% |
| SO₂ | 0.020 | 0.16% |
| N₂ | 9.104 | 74.38% |
| O₂ | 0.549 | 4.49% |
| **Total** | **12.240** | **100.00%** |

**(iv) Dry flue gas composition: CO₂ = 20.97%, SO₂ = 0.16%, N₂ = 74.38%, O₂ = 4.49%.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Cross-Limited (Parabolic) Combustion Control:**

In a boiler, fuel and air must be coordinated at all times — the boiler must never go very rich (smoke, CO) or very lean (flameout, low efficiency). The **cross-limited strategy** uses two parallel setpoint paths, each constrained by the other.

**Key elements:**
1. **Master demand signal** (from load, e.g., MW output or steam flow) generates two parallel setpoints:
   - Fuel setpoint (from a non-linear "fuel curve" — high gain at low load)
   - Air setpoint (from a non-linear "air curve" — parabolic shape, high air at low load)
2. **High Selector** on the fuel path: takes the maximum of (fuel demand, air-flow-derived fuel limit). This means fuel setpoint can rise freely, but is *also limited* by how much air is currently available — preventing the boiler from going rich on increasing load.
3. **Low Selector** on the air path: takes the minimum of (air demand, fuel-flow-derived air limit). This means air setpoint falls freely, but is *also limited* by how much fuel is being burned — preventing the boiler from going lean on decreasing load.
4. **O₂ trim controller** adds a slow trim signal to the air setpoint based on flue gas O₂ measurement, rejecting fuel composition variations.

**Why it prevents rich combustion on load increase:**
- When load increases, the master signal rises. Fuel demand rises first. But the high-selector also receives an "air-limited fuel" signal: $\dfrac{Flow_{air}}{AFR_{required}}$. If air has not yet caught up, the high selector limits the fuel setpoint. Air is given time to ramp up before fuel follows.

**Why it prevents lean combustion on load decrease:**
- When load decreases, the master signal falls. Air demand falls first. But the low-selector also receives a "fuel-limited air" signal: $Flow_{fuel} \times AFR_{required}$. If fuel is still high (mill inertia), the low selector keeps air from falling too fast, preventing a lean excursion.

**Practical effect:** During a load transient, the air-fuel relationship traces a "figure-8" or parabolic pattern in the AFR-vs-time plane — hence the name *parabolic* control.

This strategy is standard in **ABB, Honeywell, Siemens, and Yokogawa DCS** for utility boilers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A furnace burns fuel oil with the following composition by mass: C = 84%, H₂ = 12%, S = 2%, O₂ = 1%, Ash = 1%.
  (i) Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.
  (ii) If 20% excess air is used, find the wet and dry flue gas analysis by mass.
  (iii) The flue gas oxygen analyzer reads 4.2%. Verify whether this matches the 20% excess assumption, and explain any discrepancy.  **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **working principle of a zirconia-cell oxygen analyzer** used for flue gas O₂ measurement. Why is it preferred over a paramagnetic analyzer in hot, dirty flue gas?  **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:** C = 84%, H₂ = 12%, S = 2%, O₂ = 1%, Ash = 1% (per kg fuel)

**Step 1: Oxygen demand**

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for C} = \frac{32}{12} \times 0.84 = 2.2400 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for H}_2 = \frac{32}{4} \times 0.12 = 0.9600 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{O}_2 \text{ for S} = \frac{32}{32} \times 0.02 = 0.0200 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Total O}_2 \text{ demanded} = 2.2400 + 0.9600 + 0.0200 = 3.2200 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Subtract fuel oxygen**

$$\text{Net O}_2 \text{ from air} = 3.2200 - 0.01 = 3.2100 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric air**

$$A_0 = \frac{3.2100}{0.232} = 13.836 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}$$

**(i) Stoichiometric air = 13.836 kg/kg fuel.**

**Step 4: Actual air at 20% excess**

$$A_{actual} = 13.836 \times 1.20 = 16.603 \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**Step 5: Dry flue gas composition**

- CO₂: $\dfrac{44}{12} \times 0.84 = 3.080 \text{ kg}$
- SO₂: $\dfrac{64}{32} \times 0.02 = 0.040 \text{ kg}$
- N₂ from air: $16.603 \times 0.768 = 12.751 \text{ kg}$
- Excess O₂: $2.767 \times 0.232 = 0.642 \text{ kg}$

$$\text{Total dry flue gas} = 3.080 + 0.040 + 12.751 + 0.642 = 16.513 \text{ kg}$$

| Component | Mass (kg) | % by mass |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 3.080 | 18.65% |
| SO₂ | 0.040 | 0.24% |
| N₂ | 12.751 | 77.22% |
| O₂ | 0.642 | 3.89% |
| **Total** | **16.513** | **100.00%** |

**Step 6: Wet flue gas — add water from H₂ combustion**

$$\text{H}_2\text{O produced} = \frac{18}{2} \times 0.12 = 1.080 \text{ kg}$$

$$\text{Wet flue gas} = 16.513 + 1.080 = 17.593 \text{ kg/kg fuel}$$

**(ii) Wet flue gas mass = 17.593 kg/kg fuel; Dry flue gas mass = 16.513 kg/kg fuel** (with composition as tabulated).

**Step 7: Theoretical O₂ at 20% EA**

$$\text{O}_2\% = \frac{0.232 \times 20}{1 + 0.232 \times 20} = \frac{4.64}{5.64} = 82.27\%$$

⚠️ **The simplified empirical formula is for hydrocarbon gases with no inerts**. For a real fuel oil with 1% ash, it gives nonsense. Use the full mass balance.

**Step 8: Compare with actual analyzer reading**

The mass balance gives **3.89% O₂ in dry flue gas** at 20% excess air.
The analyzer reads **4.2%**.

**Discrepancy = 4.2 − 3.89 = 0.31% (about 8% relative error).**

**Possible reasons for discrepancy:**
- **Incomplete combustion** — some carbon forms CO instead of CO₂, freeing extra O₂ that appears in flue gas. If CO is present, O₂ reads higher than the "theoretical CO₂ + O₂" balance predicts.
- **Air infiltration** — leakage into the boiler setting or duct joints adds extra O₂ to the flue gas downstream.
- **Moisture in combustion air** — humid ambient air slightly changes the mass balance (we assumed 23.2% O₂; in humid conditions it drops to ~22.5%, raising the apparent O₂).
- **Analyzer drift / calibration error** — typical zirconia probe drift is ±0.5% O₂ over 6 months.
- **Sampling location** — if the probe is downstream of an air-in-leakage point (e.g., between ESP and ID fan), reading will be high.

**(iii) The 4.2% reading indicates the actual excess air is slightly higher than the design 20%, OR there is some air in-leakage / minor CO formation. The DCS trim should slowly back off FD fan until O₂ returns to the setpoint of 3–4%.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Zirconia-Cell Oxygen Analyzer:**

**Principle:** The analyzer uses a solid-state **zirconium dioxide (ZrO₂)** ceramic tube, stabilized with yttria (Y₂O₃) or calcia (CaO). At high temperature (~600–800°C), ZrO₂ becomes an **oxygen-ion conductor** — O²⁻ ions migrate through the crystal lattice from the high-O₂ side to the low-O₂ side. This ion transport generates an **Nernst EMF** between two platinum electrodes coated on the inner and outer surfaces of the tube:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_{O_2,\,ref}}{P_{O_2,\,meas}}\right)$$

where:
- R = universal gas constant
- T = absolute temperature of the cell
- F = Faraday's constant
- $P_{O_2,\,ref}$ = partial pressure of O₂ on reference side (typically clean ambient air, ~20.9%)
- $P_{O_2,\,meas}$ = partial pressure of O₂ in the flue gas

By measuring E and T, the controller computes $P_{O_2,\,meas}$ and thus the % O₂ in the flue gas.

**Two configurations:**
1. **In-situ (probe-type) zirconia sensor:** The ZrO₂ cell is mounted directly in the flue gas duct. One side is exposed to flue gas, the other to a reference air supply. Cell temperature is maintained by a built-in heater. No sampling line is required.
2. **Sampling-type zirconia analyzer:** A pump draws flue gas through a cooler and filter to a remote cell. Used when flue gas temperature is too high or composition too aggressive for the in-situ probe.

**Why preferred over paramagnetic analyzer in hot, dirty flue gas:**

| Property | Zirconia | Paramagnetic |
|---|---|---|
| Sensitivity to O₂ | Very high (Nernst response) | Moderate (susceptibility-based) |
| Operating temperature | Can be installed in-situ at 600–800°C | Requires cooled, clean sample |
| Response time | < 1 second (in-situ) | 10–30 seconds |
| Sample conditioning | Not required (in-situ) | Requires cooler, filter, pump |
| Interferences | Combustible gases (H₂, CO, CH₄) — burns on Pt electrode, gives false high O₂ | Diaphragm vibrations, shock-sensitive |
| Maintenance | Probe cleaning every 6–12 months | Pump, filter, cooler maintenance frequent |

**In a power plant boiler**, the flue gas is **hot (140–400°C), dusty, moist, and corrosive**. An in-situ zirconia probe is robust, fast, and self-cleaning (high T burns off soot). A paramagnetic analyzer would need a complex sample-conditioning skid, with significant lag and maintenance burden.

Hence, the **zirconia-cell in-situ O₂ analyzer** is the de-facto standard for boiler combustion control trim loops in modern thermal power stations.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Air-Fuel Ratio Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract fuel-borne O₂.** Many students write $A_0 = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S$ divided by 0.232, but forget the **−(O₂ in fuel)** term. This gives an overestimated theoretical air and wrong downstream calculations. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Using the wrong fraction for air.** Use **0.232 (mass basis)** for mass-based calculations and **0.21 (volume basis)** for volume-based problems. Mixing them up is a silent error. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Including moisture and ash in the O₂ demand equation.** These are inerts. Do not add terms for them in the stoichiometric oxygen formula. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 4. **Confusing wet and dry flue gas.** The water from H₂ combustion is part of *wet* flue gas. When the question asks for "dry flue gas analysis," exclude H₂O. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Applying the simplified O₂–EA relation to a real coal.** The relation $\text{O}_2\% = \frac{0.232 \cdot EA}{1 + 0.232 \cdot EA}$ assumes pure hydrocarbon with no fuel-N₂, ash, or moisture. For coals, always do a full mass balance. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 6. **In cross-limited control, mis-stating which selector is high and which is low.** It is *high selector on fuel path* and *low selector on air path* — students often reverse these. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 7. **Unit inconsistency.** Keep kg/kg throughout; do not switch to moles mid-calculation. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Air-Fuel Ratio (Module 2)**
>
> ✅ **AFR** is mass of air ÷ mass of fuel; stoichiometric value depends on fuel.
>
> ✅ **Theoretical air** $A_0 = \frac{1}{0.232}\!\left[\frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_2\right]$ kg/kg fuel (using mass fractions).
>
> ✅ **Excess air** EA = (actual − theoretical)/theoretical × 100%. Typical values: coal 15–25%, oil 5–15%, gas 5–10%.
>
> ✅ **Actual air** = $A_0 (1 + EA/100)$.
>
> ✅ **Dry flue gas** = CO₂ + SO₂ + N₂(from air) + N₂(from fuel) + excess O₂.
>
> ✅ **Wet flue gas** = dry flue gas + H₂O from hydrogen combustion.
>
> ✅ **Empirical link** (hydrocarbon gases only): $EA = \dfrac{0.268 \cdot \text{O}_2\%}{21 - \text{O}_2\%}$.
>
> ✅ **Equivalence ratio** φ = $(A/F)_{stoich} / (A/F)_{actual}$. Lean → φ < 1, Rich → φ > 1.
>
> ✅ **Cross-limited (parabolic) control** uses high-selector on fuel path and low-selector on air path to prevent rich-on-rise and lean-on-fall transients.
>
> ✅ **Oxygen trim** uses a zirconia-cell analyzer in the flue gas to fine-tune the air setpoint to maintain ~2–4% O₂.
>
> ✅ **Too little air** → CO, soot, smoke, incomplete combustion loss.
>
> ✅ **Too much air** → high stack loss, low efficiency, high fan power, lean blow-off risk.
>
> ✅ **Optimum excess air** is a trade-off between combustion completeness and stack heat loss.
>
> ✅ **Zirconia analyzer** uses Nernst EMF on heated ZrO₂; preferred for in-situ flue gas O₂ measurement.
>
> ✅ **Remember** to subtract fuel-borne O₂, exclude moisture/ash from stoichiometry, and report dry flue gas when asked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soot Blowers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Soot Blowers ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) A **Soot Blower** is a mechanical device employed in steam-generating boilers (particularly in thermal power plants) that uses a high-velocity jet of working fluid — typically **superheated steam, compressed air, or water** — to dislodge accumulated deposits of **soot, ash, slag, and fly-ash** from the heat-transfer surfaces of a boiler. These surfaces include the **water-wall tubes, superheater tubes, reheater tubes, economizer coils, and the air-preheater baskets**. Soot blowers are an integral part of the **boiler auxiliary system** and are directly linked to the boiler's **thermal efficiency, availability, and safe operation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The topic falls under *Boiler Instrumentation & Auxiliaries*. Students must focus on the **classification, working principle, location in boiler, media used, and the automatic sequential control system** of soot blowers. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are cooking on a gas stove at home, and a pot of milk is boiling over and spilling onto the burner. The spilled milk gets carbonized (burnt) on the burner, forming a black crust. This crust acts as an **insulator** — it prevents heat from efficiently reaching the pot, and you must scrub it off. The stove now takes longer to boil water and consumes more gas. Now scale this up to a power plant boiler. A typical 500 MW boiler burns ~400 tonnes of coal per hour, producing enormous quantities of fly-ash and unburnt carbon. This carbon sticks to the boiler tubes (the "pots" in our analogy). A **soot blower is like an automated, high-pressure power-washer** that periodically blasts this insulating layer off the tubes, restoring heat transfer. Without it, the boiler would gradually lose efficiency, the tubes would overheat (leading to ruptures), and fuel consumption would rise. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Every 1 mm of soot deposit on the fireside of a boiler tube can increase the flue-gas exit temperature by **15–20 °C**, reducing overall boiler efficiency by **1–2%**. This makes soot blowing economically critical. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Soot blower operating steam pressure:** **1.0 MPa to 4.0 MPa** (typical: 1.5–2.5 MPa) - **Soot blower steam temperature:** **300 °C to 400 °C** - **Jet velocity at nozzle exit:** **200 m/s to 400 m/s** (often supersonic) - **Typical blow duration per lance:** **30 seconds to 90 seconds** - **Cleaning radius of a single nozzle:** **1.5 m to 3.0 m** - **Soot deposit thermal conductivity (κ):** **0.05–0.15 W/m·K** (extremely low — almost as insulating as fiberglass) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Are Soot Blowers Needed? — The Thermodynamic Justification When a fossil fuel is burnt in a boiler, complete combustion is never achieved. The by-products of incomplete combustion include: 1. **Soot** — unburnt carbon particles (mostly in the radiant zone) 2. **Fly-ash** — fine inorganic mineral particles carried by flue gas 3. **Slag** — molten ash that fuses to tube surfaces (furnace walls) These deposits form a **low-thermal-conductivity layer** on the fireside of heat-transfer surfaces. The consequences are: - **Reduced heat flux** (less heat reaches the working fluid) - **Higher flue-gas exit temperature** (energy lost up the stack) - **Increased fuel consumption** for the same steam output - **Local hot spots** on tubes → **tube failure** (rupture → forced outage) - **Corrosion** under the deposit layer (sulfate attack) Soot blowers **mechanically remove these deposits** on a scheduled basis, maintaining the heat-transfer coefficient close to its design value. ### 2.2 Classification of Soot Blowers (Step-by-Step Logic) Soot blowers are classified along **three axes**: (a) by location in the boiler, (b) by mechanical construction, and (c) by operating media. **Step 1 — By Operating Media:** - **Steam soot blowers** (most common — uses boiler's own superheated steam) - **Air soot blowers** (uses compressed air from plant air system) - **Water soot blowers** (high-pressure water lance — used for slag removal in furnace) **Step 2 — By Mechanical Construction & Location:** | Type | Full Name | Location | Lance Travel | |------|-----------|----------|--------------| | **LRSB** | Long Retractable Soot Blower | Superheater, Reheater, Economizer | Enters boiler, traverses, retracts | | **SRSB** | Short Retractable Soot Blower | Water walls, penthouse | Short in-out stroke | | **RSB** | Retractable Soot Blower (generic) | Primary superheater | Medium stroke | | **IK-WB** | Wall Blower (fixed) | Furnace walls | Fixed position, rotating nozzle | - **Air Preheater (APH) Soot Blower** | Uses high-pressure air or steam to clean rotating basket | **Step 3 — By Degree of Automation:** - **Manual soot blowers** (hand-operated valves) - **Semi-automatic** (operator-initiated sequence) - **Fully automatic** (PLC/DCS controlled with timed sequence) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Hot Question:** *"Distinguish between LRSB and SRSB."* > - **LRSB**: Long lance, fully retractable, used for deep-placed surfaces (superheater pendants), travels several meters. > - **SRSB**: Short lance, retractable, used for water walls and furnace zones where the lance does not need to enter deeply. ### 2.3 Working Principle — The Jet Impact Mechanism The soot blower operates on the principle of **high-momentum jet impact**. The sequence is: 1. **Lance insertion** — The lance (a long pipe with nozzles) is pushed into the boiler by a motor-driven carriage. 2. **Valve opening** — A poppet valve opens, admitting superheated steam to the lance. 3. **Steam ejection** — Steam exits through two diametrically opposed nozzles at the lance tip at high velocity. 4. **Rotation** — The lance rotates slowly (typically 6–12 RPM) so the steam jet sweeps a helical path across the tube bank. 5. **Retraction** — After traversing the full length, the steam valve closes and the lance retracts out of the boiler. The **mechanical impact force** of the jet, combined with the **thermal shock** of the hot steam hitting cooler deposits, causes the soot to fracture and fall into the ash hoppers below. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Convention Note:** In all tables below, the vertical bar symbol is rendered as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity. | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Units | Physical Meaning | |---|--------------------|------------|---------------|------------------| | 1 | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (fouled) | $U_f = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{U_c} + \dfrac{\delta_s}{k_s}}$ | W/m²·K | Reduced U when soot layer of thickness $\delta_s$ is present | | 2 | Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (clean) | $U_c$ | W/m²·K | Design value (no deposits) | | 3 | Heat Flux (fouled) | $q_f = U_f \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | W/m² | Heat transferred per unit area with soot | | 4 | Efficiency loss approximation | $\Delta \eta \approx 0.5\% \text{ to } 2\% \text{ per mm of soot}$ | %/mm | Rule-of-thumb for KTU numericals | | 5 | Nozzle exit velocity (from Bernoulli) | $v_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_s - P_b)}{\rho_s}}$ | m/s | Where $P_s$ = steam pressure, $P_b$ = boiler pressure, $\rho_s$ = steam density | | 6 | Steam mass flow per blow | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_s (P_s - P_b)}$ | kg/s | $C_d$ ≈ 0.85–0.95, $A_n$ = nozzle area | | 7 | Soot deposit thermal resistance | $R_s = \dfrac{\delta_s}{k_s}$ | m²·K/W | Insulation value of soot layer | | 8 | Heat loss to stack due to soot | $Q_{loss} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{pg} \cdot \Delta T_g$ | W | Flue-gas sensible heat loss with raised exit temp | | 9 | Cleaning radius | $R_c \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 3.0$ m | m | Effective area swept by one nozzle | | 10 | Lance rotation speed | $N = 6 \text{ to } 12$ RPM | rev/min | Standard KTU value | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Variables to remember for the exam:** > - $\delta_s$ = soot layer thickness (m) — typically 1–5 mm > - $k_s$ = soot thermal conductivity (W/m·K) — typically **0.05–0.15 W/m·K** > - $\Delta T_{lm}$ = log-mean temperature difference (K) ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern 500 MW+ supercritical units, the soot-blowing system is **fully integrated into the DCS (Distributed Control System)**. Each soot blower has its own **SBC (Soot Blower Controller)** that communicates over **fieldbus (Profibus/Modbus)** to the plant DCS. Parameters monitored per soot blower include: - **Steam admission pressure** (PT — pressure transmitter) - **Steam admission temperature** (TT — temperature transmitter) - **Lance position** (limit switches: *in-limit, out-limit*) - **Rotation feedback** (proximity sensor / encoder) - **Valve position** (limit switch on poppet valve) - **Drain valve position** (to prevent water carry-over) The DCS sequences the soot blowers in a **predetermined pattern** to ensure uniform cleaning without over-cooling any localized zone. A typical cycle takes **8–24 hours** for the complete boiler. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Flux Degradation Due to Soot We derive how soot accumulation reduces the heat-transfer rate from the flue gas to the working fluid inside a boiler tube. **Step 1 — Start with the clean-tube heat transfer equation:** For a clean boiler tube, the heat flux $q_c$ (W/m²) is given by the standard overall-heat-transfer equation: $$q_c = U_c \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$ where $U_c$ is the clean overall heat-transfer coefficient and $\Delta T_{lm}$ is the log-mean temperature difference between the flue gas and the steam/water inside the tube. **Step 2 — Recognize the thermal resistance network:** The overall heat transfer coefficient is the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance per unit area. For a clean tube: $$U_c = \frac{1}{R_{total,clean}} = \frac{1}{R_g + R_{w} + R_{s,inside}}$$ Here, $R_g$ is the gas-side convective resistance, $R_w$ is the tube-wall conductive resistance, and $R_{s,inside}$ is the steam-side convective resistance. **Step 3 — Add the soot deposit resistance:** When a soot layer of thickness $\delta_s$ and thermal conductivity $k_s$ deposits on the gas side, an additional resistance appears **in series** with $R_g$: $$R_{soot} = \frac{\delta_s}{k_s}$$ The new (fouled) overall heat-transfer coefficient becomes: $$U_f = \frac{1}{R_{total,fouled}} = \frac{1}{R_g + R_{soot} + R_w + R_{s,inside}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:** Let $R_{other} = R_w + R_{s,inside}$ (the unaffected resistances). Then: $$U_f = \frac{1}{R_g + \dfrac{\delta_s}{k_s} + R_{other}}$$ In terms of the clean coefficient: $$U_f = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{U_c} + \dfrac{\delta_s}{k_s}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the degraded heat flux:** $$q_f = U_f \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_{lm}}{\dfrac{1}{U_c} + \dfrac{\delta_s}{k_s}}$$ **Step 6 — Define the fouling factor $R_f$:** The standard engineering parameter is the **fouling factor**, defined as the additional thermal resistance due to deposits: $$R_f = \frac{\delta_s}{k_s} \quad \left[ \text{units: } \frac{m^2 \cdot K}{W} \right]$$ So the final compact form is: $$U_f = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_f}$$ **Step 7 — Efficiency loss estimate:** The percentage drop in heat flux is: $$\frac{\Delta q}{q_c} = 1 - \frac{q_f}{q_c} = 1 - \frac{U_f}{U_c} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + U_c \cdot R_f}$$ For typical boiler values $U_c \approx 50 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $R_f \approx 0.01 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$: $$\frac{\Delta q}{q_c} = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + 50 \times 0.01} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.5} = 1 - 0.667 = 0.333 \text{ (i.e., 33.3\% drop)}$$ This shows that even a thin (≈1 mm) soot layer with low thermal conductivity causes a massive drop in heat transfer — the **fundamental physical reason** why soot blowing is essential. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Soot Thickness on Heat Transfer We tabulate the percentage drop in heat flux as a function of soot thickness, using $U_c = 50 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $k_s = 0.10 \text{ W/m·K}$. | Soot Thickness $\delta_s$ (mm) | $R_f = \delta_s/k_s$ (m²K/W) | $U_c \cdot R_f$ (dimensionless) | $U_f/U_c$ (ratio) | $\Delta q/q_c$ (% loss) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0.0 (clean) | 0.0000 | 0.00 | 1.000 | 0.0% | | 0.5 | 0.0050 | 0.25 | 0.800 | 20.0% | | 1.0 | 0.0100 | 0.50 | 0.667 | 33.3% | | 2.0 | 0.0200 | 1.00 | 0.500 | 50.0% | | 3.0 | 0.0300 | 1.50 | 0.400 | 60.0% | | 5.0 | 0.0500 | 2.50 | 0.286 | 71.4% | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Notice the **non-linear** nature — the first millimeter of soot causes a 33% loss, while the next millimeter causes only a further 17% (from 33% to 50%). This is because the resistances add in **series**, and diminishing returns set in. The implication for KTU: **frequent, light soot blowing is more efficient than delayed, heavy blowing.** ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) > **Problem:** A boiler superheater has a clean overall heat-transfer coefficient of $U_c = 60 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. After 12 hours of operation, a soot layer of 1.5 mm thickness is deposited on the gas side. Given the thermal conductivity of soot as $k_s = 0.08 \text{ W/m·K}$, calculate: > (a) The fouling factor. > (b) The fouled overall heat-transfer coefficient. > (c) The percentage drop in heat flux if the log-mean temperature difference remains constant at 250 K. **Solution:** **Part (a) — Fouling Factor:** $$R_f = \frac{\delta_s}{k_s} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{0.08 \text{ W/m·K}}$$ $$R_f = 0.01875 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ **Part (b) — Fouled U:** $$U_f = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_f} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{60} + 0.01875}$$ $$\frac{1}{U_c} = 0.01667 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$\frac{1}{U_c} + R_f = 0.01667 + 0.01875 = 0.03542$$ $$U_f = \frac{1}{0.03542} = 28.23 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$ **Part (c) — Percentage Drop in Heat Flux:** $$q_c = U_c \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 60 \times 250 = 15000 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$q_f = U_f \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 28.23 \times 250 = 7057.5 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$\frac{\Delta q}{q_c} = \frac{15000 - 7057.5}{15000} = \frac{7942.5}{15000} = 0.5295$$ $$\boxed{\frac{\Delta q}{q_c} \approx 52.95\%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** Using the ratio method, $U_f/U_c = 28.23/60 = 0.4705$, so the drop is $1 - 0.4705 = 0.5295$ or **52.95%**. ✓ This matches, confirming the calculation. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Soot Blower System The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional architecture** of a soot blower system as it interacts with the boiler, the DCS, and the supporting utilities. This is a high-yield diagram for KTU 14-mark questions on "Explain the soot blower system with a neat block diagram." ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Boiler Furnace
Heat Source] --> B[Furnace Walls
Water Wall Tubes] A --> C[Superheater
Pendant Tubes] A --> D[Reheater
Coil Section] A --> E[Economizer
Coil Bank] A --> F[Air Preheater
Rotating Basket] G[SBC Soot Blower Controller
Local PLC Panel] --> G1[Lance Drive Motor] G --> G2[Poppet Steam Valve] G --> G3[Drain Valve] G --> G4[Rotation Encoder] G --> G5[Limit Switches In and Out] G1 --> H1[Short Retractable
