Soot Blower SRSB] G1 --> H2[Long Retractable
Soot Blower LRSB] G1 --> H3[Air Preheater
Soot Blower APHSB] G1 --> H4[Wall Blower
Fixed Type] H1 --> B H2 --> C H2 --> D H2 --> E H3 --> F H4 --> B J[Superheated Steam
Source Header 1.5 to 2.5 MPa] --> G2 K[Compressed Air
Plant Air Header 0.6 MPa] --> H3 L[Drain Pot
Condensate Separator] --> G3 M[DCS Distributed Control System
Plant Level] --> G N[Operator HMI
Graphic Display] --> M P[Pressure Transmitter PT] --> G Q[Temperature Transmitter TT] --> G R[Position Feedback
4 to 20 mA] --> G ``` ### 4.2 Sequence Flow: Automatic Soot Blowing Cycle The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **logical sequence of a single automatic soot-blowing cycle**. This is a frequently-asked KTU question. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Cycle
DCS Initiation] --> B{Pre-conditions OK?
Steam Pressure Permissive} B -- No --> C[Hold and Alarm
Display on HMI] B -- Yes --> D[Open Drain Valve
Drain Condensate] D --> E[Drain Timer Expired
5 to 10 seconds] E --> F[Close Drain Valve] F --> G[Start Lance Motor
Insert Lance into Boiler] G --> H[Lance In-limit Reached
Full Insertion] H --> I[Open Steam Poppet Valve
Admit Steam to Lance] I --> J[Lance Rotates
6 to 12 RPM] J --> K[Steam Jet Cleans Tubes
Duration 30 to 90 seconds] K --> L[Close Steam Valve] L --> M[Lance Retracts
Reverse Motor] M --> N[Lance Out-limit Reached
Home Position] N --> O[Record Cycle Data
Time Pressure Temperature] O --> P[Move to Next Soot Blower
in Sequence] P --> Q{Sequence Complete?} Q -- No --> A Q -- Yes --> R[End of Full Cycle
Wait for Next Schedule] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Soot Blower Instrumentation Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Field Sensors
PT TT LimitSw FlowSw] --> B[Signal Conditioning
and Isolation] B --> C[SBC Local PLC
Soot Blower Controller] C --> D[Profibus or Modbus
Fieldbus Link] D --> E[DCS Controller
Plant Level] E --> F[Operator HMI
Alarms and Trends] C --> G[Actuators
Motor Steam Valve Drain] G --> H[Soot Blower Lance
Physical Cleaning] H --> I[Boiler Tubes
Heat Transfer Surface] I --> A ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define a soot blower and state why it is essential in a thermal power plant boiler.** **Model Answer:** A soot blower is a device that uses high-velocity jets of steam, air, or water to remove accumulated soot, ash, and slag from the heat-transfer surfaces (water walls, superheater, reheater, economizer, air preheater) of a boiler. It is essential because soot deposits act as a thermal insulator, drastically reducing the heat-transfer coefficient. This causes (i) loss of boiler efficiency (≈1–2% per mm of soot), (ii) increased fuel consumption, and (iii) overheating of tubes leading to failures. Periodic soot blowing restores heat transfer and ensures safe, efficient operation. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only "to clean the boiler" — this is too vague. You **must mention the thermal-resistance effect and the link to efficiency/safety** to earn full marks. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Differentiate between LRSB and SRSB.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | LRSB (Long Retractable) | SRSB (Short Retractable) | |---|---|---| | Full Form | Long Retractable Soot Blower | Short Retractable Soot Blower | | Lance length | Long (several meters) | Short (typically < 1.5 m) | | Location | Superheater, reheater, economizer | Water walls, penthouse area | | Stroke | Long traverse | Short in-out stroke | | Application | Deep-placed tube banks | Surface-level cleaning | --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Note on KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Model solutions must be exhaustive. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Explain the working principle of a long-retractable soot blower (LRSB) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each component. **(b)** A boiler superheater has a clean overall heat-transfer coefficient of $U_c = 75 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. After 24 hours of continuous operation, a soot deposit of 2 mm thickness forms on the gas side. The thermal conductivity of the soot is $k_s = 0.10 \text{ W/m·K}$. The log-mean temperature difference is 280 K. Calculate: 1. The fouling factor 2. The fouled overall heat-transfer coefficient 3. The percentage drop in heat flux **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — LRSB Working Principle:** A Long Retractable Soot Blower (LRSB) is used to clean the superheater, reheater, and economizer tube banks that are located deep inside the boiler. The major components and their functions are: 1. **Lance assembly** — A long pipe (3–6 m) fitted with two diametrically opposite nozzles at the tip, which directs high-velocity steam onto the tube banks. 2. **Carriage and rail mechanism** — Supports the lance and provides linear motion into and out of the boiler. 3. **Drive motor (with gearbox)** — Drives the lance carriage in and out, and also rotates the lance during operation (6–12 RPM). 4. **Poppet steam valve** — A quick-opening valve that admits superheated steam to the lance interior. 5. **Drain valve and pot** — Removes condensate from the steam line before admitting steam to the lance (prevents water hammering). 6. **Limit switches (in/out)** — Detect the end positions of lance travel and provide feedback to the controller. 7. **Rotation encoder / proximity sensor** — Confirms lance is rotating (a stuck lance triggers an alarm). 8. **SBC (Soot Blower Controller)** — Local PLC that sequences the operations and communicates with the DCS. **Working sequence:** 1. The controller opens the drain valve to remove condensate (5–10 s). 2. The drain valve closes, and the lance motor starts, pushing the lance into the boiler. 3. When the in-limit is reached, the poppet valve opens, admitting steam. 4. The lance rotates, sweeping a helical jet path over the tubes for 30–90 seconds. 5. The poppet valve closes, the lance reverses, retracts to the home position. 6. The out-limit switch is reached, the controller logs the cycle, and moves to the next blower. **Part (b) — Numerical:** **Step 1 — Fouling factor:** $$R_f = \frac{\delta_s}{k_s} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.10} = 0.020 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Fouled U:** $$\frac{1}{U_c} = \frac{1}{75} = 0.01333 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$U_f = \frac{1}{0.01333 + 0.020} = \frac{1}{0.03333} = 30.00 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$ **Step 3 — Heat flux drop:** $$q_c = 75 \times 280 = 21000 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$q_f = 30 \times 280 = 8400 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$\frac{\Delta q}{q_c} = \frac{21000 - 8400}{21000} = \frac{12600}{21000} = 0.600$$ $$\boxed{\text{Percentage drop} = 60.0\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Warning — Common Mistakes:** > 1. Forgetting to convert mm → m when calculating $R_f$. The error is huge (factor of 1000). > 2. Inverting the formula and writing $U_f = \frac{1}{U_c} + R_f$ instead of the proper sum of reciprocals. > 3. Not stating units — a 1-mark deduction in valuation. > 4. Skipping the final percentage conversion step. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the automatic sequential control of soot blowers in a thermal power plant. List the various field instruments used and their functions. **(b)** A boiler economizer has a clean overall heat-transfer coefficient of $U_c = 45 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. After 8 hours of operation, a soot deposit of 0.8 mm forms, with $k_s = 0.12 \text{ W/m·K}$. The log-mean temperature difference is 200 K. Calculate: 1. The fouling factor and fouled U 2. The percentage reduction in heat transfer rate **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Automatic Sequential Control:** A modern power plant has 30–80 soot blowers distributed across the boiler (water walls, superheater, reheater, economizer, APH). They are operated in a **predetermined automatic sequence** to ensure uniform cleaning and prevent thermal stress from localized overcooling. **Block diagram description:** 1. **DCS (Plant Level)** — Initiates the soot-blowing cycle based on a time schedule or operator command. Maintains logs of each blower's last-cleaned time. 2. **SBC (Soot Blower Controller, Local)** — Each blower has a dedicated SBC. Receives start command from DCS, executes the local sequence, and returns status. 3. **Field instrumentation per blower:** - **PT (Pressure Transmitter)** — Measures steam admission pressure; interlock prevents start if pressure < setpoint. - **TT (Temperature Transmitter)** — Monitors steam temperature to ensure superheat (avoids water carry-over). - **ZSL/ZSO Limit Switches** — Detect lance in-position and out-position. - **Proximity Sensor / Encoder** — Confirms rotation of lance. - **ZS Drain Valve Limit Switch** — Confirms drain valve closed before steam admission. - **Flow Switch (optional)** — Confirms steam flow during blow. 4. **Actuators:** - **Lance motor** (forward/reverse contactor) - **Solenoid-operated poppet valve** (steam admission) - **Solenoid-operated drain valve** 5. **HMI** — Operator can view status, override sequence, and acknowledge alarms. **Sequence logic:** The DCS steps through blowers one at a time (or in small groups), with a dwell time between adjacent blowers to prevent simultaneous steam demand. A typical sequence: Bottom furnace walls → Side walls → Roof → Superheater → Reheater → Economizer → APH. After the last blower, the cycle restarts after a fixed interval (8–24 hours). **Part (b) — Numerical:** **Step 1 — Fouling factor:** $$R_f = \frac{0.8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.12} = 0.006667 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ **Step 2 — Fouled U:** $$\frac{1}{U_c} = \frac{1}{45} = 0.02222 \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$ $$U_f = \frac{1}{0.02222 + 0.006667} = \frac{1}{0.02889} = 34.61 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$ **Step 3 — Percentage reduction:** $$\frac{\Delta q}{q_c} = 1 - \frac{U_f}{U_c} = 1 - \frac{34.61}{45} = 1 - 0.7691 = 0.2309$$ $$\boxed{\text{Percentage reduction} \approx 23.09\%}$$ --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Definition:** Soot blower = high-velocity fluid jet device to clean boiler heat-transfer surfaces. - ✅ **Why needed:** Soot has very low thermal conductivity (0.05–0.15 W/m·K) — acts as insulator, reducing U. - ✅ **Formula to memorize:** $U_f = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{U_c} + \dfrac{\delta_s}{k_s}}$ and $\Delta q/q_c = 1 - U_f/U_c$. - ✅ **LRSB** = Long lance, superheater/reheater/economizer; **SRSB** = Short lance, water walls. - ✅ **Wall blower** = fixed position, rotating nozzle, for furnace walls. - ✅ **APH soot blower** = uses compressed air or steam for air-preheater basket. - ✅ **Operating media:** Superheated steam (1.0–4.0 MPa) most common; air for APH. - ✅ **Cleaning radius:** 1.5–3.0 m per nozzle. - ✅ **Lance rotation:** 6–12 RPM. - ✅ **Cycle duration per lance:** 30–90 s of active steam blow. - ✅ **Field instruments:** PT, TT, limit switches, encoder/proximity, drain valve ZS. - ✅ **Control hierarchy:** DCS → SBC (local PLC) → field sensors/actuators. - ✅ **Efficiency loss rule:** ≈ 0.5–2% per mm of soot deposit. - ✅ **Exam tip:** Always convert mm → m in $R_f$ calculations (common error). - ✅ **Real-world link:** Soot blowing saves crores in fuel cost per year in a 500 MW unit.

topics.title: Combustion Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Combustion Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Combustion Process (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Combustion is a rapid, exothermic, oxidation chemical reaction between a fuel (hydrocarbon-based) and an oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen, O₂), accompanied by the production of heat energy, light (flame), and combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂, N₂, and unburnt gases). In thermal power plant instrumentation, the combustion process forms the **first energy-conversion stage** — converting the *chemical energy* locked within the fuel into *thermal energy* (sensible heat of flue gases and radiant heat), which is subsequently used to generate steam.

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Combustion (The "Fire Triangle")

For any combustion process to sustain itself, three components must coexist simultaneously:

1. **Fuel** – The reducing agent (coal, oil, natural gas, biomass).
2. **Oxygen (Air)** – The oxidizing agent, supplied via FD/PA fans.
3. **Heat (Ignition Energy)** – The activation energy to initiate the chain reaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The removal of ANY one of the three pillars extinguishes the combustion. This principle is the foundation of **combustion control strategies** such as burner management systems (BMS) and furnace safeguards.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine lighting a candle. You bring a match (heat) close to the wick (fuel) which is exposed to the air (oxygen) around it. The wax vaporizes, reacts with O₂, releases heat and light — this is combustion. In a **power plant boiler**, the same logic operates at a massive scale: pulverized coal (fuel) is sprayed into a furnace with preheated air (oxygen), ignited by an oil-fired burner (heat), producing a roaring flame that transfers heat to water tubes.

### 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (Coal-fired) |
|---|---|---|
| Stoichiometric Air | $A_s$ | 4.5 – 7.5 kg air / kg fuel |
| Excess Air | $EA$ | **15% – 25%** (pulverized coal) |
| Flame Temperature | $T_{flame}$ | **1500 °C – 1800 °C** |
| Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb}$ | 85% – 98% |
| Calorific Value (Coal) | $CV$ | 20 – 30 MJ/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pure oxygen (100% O₂) is NEVER used** in conventional power plants because pure O₂ would make the flame temperature excessively high (exceeding **3500 °C**), damaging the furnace refractory lining. Atmospheric air (~21% O₂, 79% N₂) is the universal oxidizer, where N₂ acts as a thermal ballast.

---

### 1.5 Classification of Fuels Used in Combustion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 Syllabus Mapping:** The classification determines the type of combustion instrumentation required.

| Fuel Type | Examples | Combustion Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Fuels** | Coal, Lignite, Biomass | Heterogeneous, requires pulverization, longer residence time |
| **Liquid Fuels** | Furnace Oil, HSD, LSHS | Atomized spray combustion, homogeneous vapor-phase reaction |
| **Gaseous Fuels** | Natural Gas (CH₄), LPG, Biogas | Premixed or diffusion flame, cleanest combustion |

### 1.6 Combustion Reactions — The Stoichiometric Foundation

For a generalized hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_yO_zS_w$ reacting with oxygen:

$$C_xH_yO_zS_w + aO_2 \rightarrow xCO_2 + \frac{y}{2}H_2O + wSO_2 + \text{Heat}$$

The stoichiometric oxygen requirement is determined by atom balance:

$$a = x + \frac{y}{4} + w - \frac{z}{2}$$

The corresponding **stoichiometric air** is:

$$A_s = \frac{a \times 100}{21} \quad \text{(kg air / kg fuel, by mass basis)}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stoichiometric Combustion — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Fuel Composition Analysis (Ultimate Analysis):**
A fuel sample is analyzed for its mass fractions of Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Sulphur (S), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), and Moisture (M). The sum equals 1 (or 100%).

**Step 2 — Oxygen Required per kg of Fuel:**
Each combustible element consumes a fixed amount of O₂ per kg:

- 1 kg of **C** requires $\frac{8}{3}$ kg of O₂ → forms $\frac{11}{3}$ kg of CO₂
- 1 kg of **H** requires **8 kg** of O₂ → forms 9 kg of H₂O
- 1 kg of **S** requires 1 kg of O₂ → forms 2 kg of SO₂

**Step 3 — Net Oxygen Demand:**
The O₂ already present within the fuel (intrinsic) is subtracted from the gross O₂ requirement.

**Step 4 — Air Conversion:**
Since air contains only **21% O₂ by volume (≈ 23% by mass)**, the theoretical air is calculated.

### 2.2 Excess Air, Actual Air, and Air-Fuel Ratio

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Excess Air?** In real boilers, **perfect mixing is impossible**. Insufficient air leads to incomplete combustion (CO, soot, unburnt carbon). Excess air ensures complete combustion but penalizes efficiency by absorbing heat in the flue gas.

$$A_{actual} = A_{stoichiometric} + A_{excess} = A_s \times (1 + EA)$$

where the excess air ratio is:

$$EA = \frac{A_{actual} - A_s}{A_s} \times 100\%$$

The **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** by mass:

$$AFR = \frac{A_{actual}}{1} = A_s(1 + EA)$$

### 2.3 Combustion Products and Flue Gas Composition

The theoretical (stoichiometric) flue gas for pure hydrocarbon combustion contains only:
- **CO₂** (carbon dioxide)
- **H₂O** (water vapor)
- **N₂** (from air)
- **SO₂** (if sulphur is present)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **real combustion with excess air**, the flue gas additionally contains:
> - **O₂** (unreacted excess)
> - **CO** (incomplete combustion indicator)
> - **NOx, SO₃** (minor pollutants)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $O_2^{req} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O$ | Mass of O₂ required per kg of fuel | kg O₂/kg fuel |
| 2 | $A_s = \frac{O_2^{req}}{0.232}$ | Stoichiometric air (by mass, 23.2% O₂) | kg air/kg fuel |
| 3 | $A_{act} = A_s(1 + EA)$ | Actual air supplied | kg air/kg fuel |
| 4 | $\%EA = \frac{A_{act} - A_s}{A_s} \times 100$ | Percent excess air | % |
| 5 | $CO_2^{max} = \frac{11/3 \cdot C}{A_s(1+EA) + 1} \times 100$ | Theoretical max CO₂ in dry flue gas | % |
| 6 | $m_{flue} = A_{act} + 1 - \frac{9H}{100}$ | Mass of wet flue gas per kg fuel | kg/kg fuel |
| 7 | $\eta_{comb} = \frac{CO_2^{actual}}{CO_2^{max}} \times 100$ | Combustion efficiency (from flue gas) | % |
| 8 | $CV_{net} = CV_{gross} - 2.442 \times \frac{9H}{100}$ | Net calorific value correction | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $\%N_2 = 100 - \%CO_2 - \%O_2 - \%CO$ | N₂ by difference (Orsat) | % |
| 10 | $A/F = \frac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}}$ | Air-Fuel Ratio | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** The value **0.232 (not 0.21)** is used for mass-basis calculations because oxygen is denser than nitrogen. The volumetric basis uses **0.21**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The combustion process in a power plant is **monitored and controlled** through a closed loop involving:
- **Sensors:** Zirconia oxygen probes (measure residual O₂ in flue gas), IR analyzers for CO/CO₂, thermocouples for flame temperature.
- **Controller:** DCS-based combustion controller implementing **cross-limiting** or **faster-fuel/less-air** logic.
- **Actuators:** FD fan damper, fuel valve actuator, PA fan damper.

This instrumentation chain ensures **complete combustion** (low CO, low unburnt carbon), **high efficiency** (low excess air, ~3-5% O₂ in flue gas), and **low emissions** (NOx, SOx compliance).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air for a Coal Sample

**Problem Setup:**
A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis (by mass):
- Carbon (C) = 70%
- Hydrogen (H) = 5%
- Sulphur (S) = 1%
- Oxygen (O) = 8%
- Nitrogen (N) = 2%
- Moisture + Ash (rest) = 14%

Find: (a) Stoichiometric air required, (b) Actual air with 20% excess, (c) Air-Fuel Ratio.

**Step (a): Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement**

The O₂ required per kg of fuel is the sum of O₂ demanded by C, H, S minus O₂ already present in the fuel:

$$O_2^{req} = \underbrace{\frac{8}{3}C}_{\text{for C}} + \underbrace{8H}_{\text{for H}} + \underbrace{1 \cdot S}_{\text{for S}} - \underbrace{O}_{\text{intrinsic O₂ in fuel}}$$

Substituting values (C, H, S, O in fractional form):

$$O_2^{req} = \frac{8}{3}(0.70) + 8(0.05) + 1(0.01) - 0.08$$

Evaluating each term:

$$O_2^{req} = 1.8667 + 0.40 + 0.01 - 0.08 = 2.1967 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step (b): Stoichiometric Air**

$$A_s = \frac{O_2^{req}}{0.232} = \frac{2.1967}{0.232} = 9.468 \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step (c): Actual Air with 20% Excess**

$$A_{act} = A_s \times (1 + EA) = 9.468 \times (1 + 0.20) = 9.468 \times 1.20$$

$$A_{act} = 11.362 \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step (d): Air-Fuel Ratio**

$$A/F = 11.362 \quad \text{:} \quad 1 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Combustion Efficiency from Flue Gas Analysis

**Given:** A flue gas Orsat analysis (dry basis) shows:
- $CO_2 = 14\%$
- $O_2 = 4\%$
- $CO = 1\%$

**Step 1 — Calculate N₂ by Difference:**

$$\%N_2 = 100 - 14 - 4 - 1 = 81\%$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Max CO₂ (from stoichiometry):**

If all carbon burned perfectly with NO excess air, the dry flue gas would contain only CO₂ and N₂. The N₂ comes from the combustion air: per kg of fuel, with 11.624 kg of air and 0.70 kg of carbon producing $\frac{11}{3} \times 0.70 = 2.567$ kg of CO₂.

$$CO_2^{max} = \frac{2.567}{2.567 + 0.7688 \times 8.74} \times 100$$

where 0.7688 is the N₂ fraction in air and 8.74 is the air required. Computing:

$$CO_2^{max} = \frac{2.567}{2.567 + 6.720} \times 100 = \frac{2.567}{9.287} \times 100 = 27.64\%$$

**Step 3 — Combustion Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{comb} = \frac{CO_2^{actual}}{CO_2^{max}} \times 100 = \frac{14}{27.64} \times 100 = 50.65\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This unusually low value indicates **excessive excess air** (or instrument calibration error). Typical combustion efficiency for a well-tuned boiler is **> 90%**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Excess Air from Flue Gas Composition

A more direct formula relates excess air to O₂ in flue gas:

$$EA = \frac{O_2^{flue} - \tfrac{1}{2}CO}{0.264 \cdot N_2^{flue} - (O_2^{flue} - \tfrac{1}{2}CO)} \times 100$$

For our case ($O_2 = 4\%$, $CO = 1\%$, $N_2 = 81\%$):

$$EA = \frac{4 - 0.5}{0.264 \times 81 - (4 - 0.5)} \times 100 = \frac{3.5}{21.384 - 3.5} \times 100 = \frac{3.5}{17.884} \times 100 = 19.57\%$$

This confirms ~20% excess air, consistent with the problem statement.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Excess Air on Combustion Parameters

| Excess Air (%) | O₂ in Flue Gas (%) | Combustion Efficiency (%) | Heat Loss in Flue Gas (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 (stoichiometric) | 0 | Low (incomplete) | Low (but high CO) |
| 10 | 1.8 | 92 | 6 |
| 20 | 3.7 | 90 | 8 |
| 30 | 5.5 | 88 | 10 |
| 50 | 9.0 | 84 | 14 |
| 100 | 16.0 | 76 | 22 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Insight:** Increasing excess air improves completeness of combustion (reduces CO, soot) but **decreases thermal efficiency** because the excess air absorbs sensible heat and carries it up the stack. **Optimization around 15-20% excess air** is the industrial standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Combustion Process Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Supply System] --> B[Pulverizer / Atomizer]
    C[PA Fan Primary Air] --> B
    B --> D[Furnace Combustion Chamber]
    E[FD Fan Secondary Air] --> D
    F[Ignition Burner Oil Gun] --> D
    D --> G[Heat Transfer to Water Tubes]
    D --> H[Flue Gas Path]
    H --> I[Zirconia O2 Analyzer]
    H --> J[IR CO Analyzer]
    I --> K[DCS Combustion Controller]
    J --> K
    K --> A
    K --> E
    G --> L[Steam Generation]
    H --> M[ESP - Particulate Removal]
    M --> N[Stack Emission]
```

**Functional Description of Each Block:**

- **Fuel Supply System:** Coal bunkers, oil guns, or gas regulators feed the burner.
- **Pulverizer / Atomizer:** Reduces coal to < 75 µm (pulverized) or atomizes oil to < 100 µm droplets for vapor-phase combustion.
- **PA Fan (Primary Air):** Carries pulverized fuel into the furnace; preheated to ~80°C.
- **FD Fan (Forced Draft):** Supplies secondary combustion air (preheated to ~300°C using air preheater).
- **Ignition Burner:** Oil gun / spark igniter provides initial activation energy.
- **Furnace Chamber:** The combustion zone — flame temperature 1500-1800°C, residence time 2-3 seconds.
- **Heat Transfer Zone:** Water walls, superheater, reheater absorb radiant + convective heat.
- **Sensors:** O₂ probe (zirconia-based) measures residual oxygen; IR analyzer measures CO.
- **DCS Controller:** Implements cross-limited control (Master + Follower).
- **Emission Control:** ESP removes particulates; FGD removes SOx; DeNOx system removes NOx.

---

### 4.2 Combustion Reaction Flow (Chemistry Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[C Hydrocarbon Fuel] --> B[Carbon + Oxygen]
    B --> B1[C + O2 equals CO2 plus Heat 32.8 MJ per kg]
    C[H Hydrogen] --> D[Hydrogen + Oxygen]
    D --> D1[H2 plus 0.5 O2 equals H2O plus Heat 142 MJ per kg]
    E[S Sulphur] --> F[Sulphur + Oxygen]
    F --> F1[S plus O2 equals SO2 plus Heat 9.3 MJ per kg]
    B1 --> G[Products Mix with N2 from Air]
    D1 --> G
    F1 --> G
    G --> H[Wet Flue Gas Stream]
    H --> I[Condensation of H2O yields Dry Flue Gas]
    I --> J[Sent to Orsat Analysis]
```

---

### 4.3 Cross-Limited Combustion Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Master Signal Steam Pressure] --> CL1[Cross-Limiting Logic]
    CL1 --> FA[Fuel Actuator]
    CL1 --> AA[Air Actuator]
    FA --> FBM[Fuel Burner Management]
    AA --> ADM[Air Damper Motor]
    FBM --> FB[Feedback from Air Flow]
    ADM --> AB[Feedback from Air Flow]
    FB --> CL1
    AB --> CL1
    O2[O2 Analyzer in Flue Gas] --> CL1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Limiting Principle:** When load increases, **air increases first** (high-fire air limit), then fuel follows — preventing fuel-rich (smoky) transient. When load decreases, **fuel decreases first** (low-fire fuel limit), then air follows — preventing flame instability.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Stoichiometric Air" in the context of combustion. Why is excess air always supplied in practical boiler operation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Stoichiometric air is the *theoretical minimum quantity of air* required to completely combust a given quantity of fuel, providing just enough oxygen to convert all carbon to CO₂, all hydrogen to H₂O, and all sulphur to SO₂ — with no oxygen left over.
- **Excess Air Justification (1.5 marks):** In practical boilers, perfect mixing of fuel and air is impossible. Some excess air (typically 15-25% for pulverized coal) is supplied to ensure **complete combustion**, minimize CO and unburnt carbon losses, and maintain stable flame. However, excessive excess air reduces efficiency by carrying heat up the stack.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any THREE combustion products formed during the burning of coal in a power plant boiler. Name the instrument used to analyze the flue gas composition.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Combustion products (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):** CO₂ (carbon dioxide), H₂O (water vapor), SO₂ (sulphur dioxide), N₂ (nitrogen — from air), O₂ (residual — from excess air), CO (carbon monoxide — incomplete combustion).
- **Instrument (1.5 marks):** The **Orsat Apparatus** is the standard instrument for analyzing dry flue gas composition (CO₂, O₂, CO by absorption in chemical reagents). Modern plants use continuous **Zirconia O₂ analyzers** and **NDIR gas analyzers**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Students frequently mix volumetric (21%) and mass-basis (23.2%) oxygen fractions in air — this costs **2-3 marks** per problem. Always verify the basis stated in the problem.
> 2. **Dry vs. Wet Flue Gas Trap:** CO₂ percentage in **dry** flue gas is HIGHER than in **wet** flue gas (water vapor is excluded). The Orsat gives **dry-basis** values.
> 3. **Sign Convention for Excess Air:** Always express EA as a fraction (e.g., 0.20) in formulas like $A_{act} = A_s(1+EA)$, NOT as a percentage.
> 4. **Heat of Combustion Values:** Use 32.8 MJ/kg for C, 142 MJ/kg for H₂, 9.3 MJ/kg for S — not the gross/net values of the fuel unless specified.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** A furnace oil has the following composition by mass: C = 86%, H = 12%, S = 1.5%, O = 0.5%. Calculate:
   1. The stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel.
   2. The actual air supplied if 25% excess air is used.
   3. The wet and dry flue gas analysis (CO₂, O₂, N₂ percentages by mass and by volume).

   **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **cross-limiting combustion control** strategy used in a modern boiler. State TWO advantages of this scheme over a simple parallel control. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Solution to (a) — [7 Marks]**

Given: C = 0.86, H = 0.12, S = 0.015, O = 0.005 (per kg of fuel)

**Part 1 — Stoichiometric Air:**

$$O_2^{req} = \frac{8}{3}(0.86) + 8(0.12) + 1(0.015) - 0.005$$

$$O_2^{req} = 2.2933 + 0.96 + 0.015 - 0.005 = 3.263 \quad \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

$$A_s = \frac{3.263}{0.232} = 14.06 \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Part 2 — Actual Air (25% EA):**

$$A_{act} = 14.06 \times 1.25 = 17.58 \quad \text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Part 3 — Flue Gas Analysis:**

Mass of CO₂ formed:
$$m_{CO_2} = \frac{11}{3} \times 0.86 = 3.153 \quad \text{kg}$$

Mass of H₂O formed:
$$m_{H_2O} = 9 \times 0.12 = 1.08 \quad \text{kg}$$

Mass of SO₂ formed:
$$m_{SO_2} = 2 \times 0.015 = 0.03 \quad \text{kg}$$

Mass of N₂ from air (N₂ = 76.8% of air by mass):
$$m_{N_2} = 0.768 \times 17.58 = 13.50 \quad \text{kg}$$

Mass of excess O₂:
$$m_{O_2,excess} = O_2^{req} \times 0.25 = 3.263 \times 0.25 = 0.816 \quad \text{kg}$$

**Wet Flue Gas Total Mass:**
$$m_{wet} = 3.153 + 1.08 + 0.03 + 13.50 + 0.816 = 18.58 \quad \text{kg}$$

**Wet Flue Gas Composition (by mass %):**
- CO₂: 3.153/18.58 × 100 = **16.97%**
- H₂O: 1.08/18.58 × 100 = **5.81%**
- SO₂: 0.03/18.58 × 100 = **0.16%**
- N₂: 13.50/18.58 × 100 = **72.66%**
- O₂: 0.816/18.58 × 100 = **4.39%**

**Dry Flue Gas Mass** (excludes H₂O):
$$m_{dry} = 18.58 - 1.08 = 17.50 \quad \text{kg}$$

**Dry Flue Gas Composition (by mass %):**
- CO₂: 3.153/17.50 × 100 = **18.02%**
- SO₂: 0.03/17.50 × 100 = **0.17%**
- N₂: 13.50/17.50 × 100 = **77.14%**
- O₂: 0.816/17.50 × 100 = **4.66%**

For volumetric (mole) basis, divide each by molecular weight:
- CO₂: 16.97/44 = 0.386 → **13.42%**
- H₂O: 5.81/18 = 0.323 → **11.22%**
- SO₂: 0.16/64 = 0.0025 → **0.09%**
- N₂: 72.66/28 = 2.595 → **90.20%**
- O₂: 4.39/32 = 0.137 → **4.77%**

(Volumes normalized to 100%; the slight rounding gives total ~99.7% — acceptable in KTU evaluation.)

---

**Solution to (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Block Diagram Description (4 marks):**

In cross-limiting combustion control, a single **master signal** (typically steam pressure or steam flow) is fed to two parallel control paths: one for fuel and one for air. Each path has a **low-signal selector** and a **high-signal selector** arranged such that:

- For **load increase:** The air setpoint rises first (high-signal air selector passes the master signal). The fuel setpoint is **limited** to a value slightly below the air capacity (low-signal fuel selector). This prevents fuel-rich conditions.
- For **load decrease:** The fuel setpoint drops first (low-signal fuel selector). The air setpoint is **limited** to a value slightly above the fuel demand (high-signal air selector). This prevents flame lift-off.

The two signals then control the fuel valve and FD/PA fan dampers respectively, with cross-coupling for stable dynamics.

**Two Advantages over Simple Parallel Control (3 marks):**
1. **Eliminates fuel-rich transients** during load-ups, preventing smoke, CO spikes, and unburnt carbon loss.
2. **Eliminates flame instability** during load-downs, preventing flame lift-off and furnace puff.
3. *Bonus:* Improves boiler efficiency and reduces NOx formation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]

**(a)** Define the **Orsat apparatus**. With a neat sketch, describe its working principle and the sequence of absorption for CO₂, O₂, and CO in flue gas analysis. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A coal-fired boiler has the following ultimate analysis: C = 68%, H = 4.5%, S = 0.8%, O = 7%, N = 1.7%, Moisture = 6%, Ash = 12%. The Orsat analysis of the flue gas shows CO₂ = 12.5%, O₂ = 5.2%, CO = 0.3%. Determine:
   1. The percentage of excess air supplied.
   2. The mass of air actually supplied per kg of coal.
   3. The dry flue gas composition by mass.

   **[7 Marks]**

*(Full model solution provided below for Question B as well.)*

---

**Solution to Question B (b) — [7 Marks]**

**Part 1 — N₂ in flue gas:**
$$\%N_2 = 100 - 12.5 - 5.2 - 0.3 = 82.0\%$$

**Part 2 — Theoretical (stoichiometric) air per kg coal:**
$$O_2^{req} = \frac{8}{3}(0.68) + 8(0.045) + 0.008 - 0.07 = 1.813 + 0.36 + 0.008 - 0.07 = 2.111$$
$$A_s = \frac{2.111}{0.232} = 9.10 \quad \text{kg air/kg coal}$$

**Part 3 — Excess Air from flue gas formula:**

$$EA = \frac{O_2 - \tfrac{1}{2}CO}{0.264 N_2 - (O_2 - \tfrac{1}{2}CO)} \times 100$$

$$EA = \frac{5.2 - 0.15}{0.264 \times 82.0 - (5.2 - 0.15)} \times 100 = \frac{5.05}{21.65 - 5.05} \times 100 = \frac{5.05}{16.60} \times 100 = 30.42\%$$

**Part 4 — Actual air supplied:**
$$A_{act} = 9.10 \times 1.3042 = 11.87 \quad \text{kg air/kg coal}$$

**Part 5 — Mass of dry flue gas components per kg coal:**

- CO₂: $(11/3) \times 0.68 = 2.493$ kg
- CO: $\frac{7}{3} \times 0.003$ (from CO alone — but CO produced from carbon not fully combusted). Recalculate: from 1 kg of carbon, CO forms when O₂ is insufficient. From the 0.3% CO in flue gas, mass of CO in dry flue gas: total dry flue gas mass = (A_act + 1) − mass of H₂O = (11.87 + 1) − 9(0.045) = 12.87 − 0.405 = 12.465 kg
  - CO mass: 0.003 × 12.465 = 0.0374 kg
- O₂: 0.052 × 12.465 = 0.648 kg
- N₂: 0.82 × 12.465 = 10.22 kg

**Composition by mass:**
- CO₂: 2.493/12.465 = 20.0%
- CO: 0.0374/12.465 = 0.30%
- O₂: 0.648/12.465 = 5.20%
- N₂: 10.22/12.465 = 82.0%

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Combustion Process (KTU Module 2)**

- [x] **Combustion** = rapid exothermic oxidation of fuel → heat + light + products.
- [x] **Fire Triangle:** Fuel + O₂ + Heat (remove one → flame extinguishes).
- [x] **Stoichiometric air** = theoretical minimum; **Actual air** = Stoichiometric × (1 + EA).
- [x] **Mass-basis O₂ in air = 23.2%**, **Volumetric-basis = 21%** — *use correctly to avoid unit-mismatch losses.*
- [x] **Per kg of fuel:** C needs 8/3 kg O₂, H needs 8 kg O₂, S needs 1 kg O₂.
- [x] **Excess air (EA) for pulverized coal:** 15-25%; for oil: 5-10%; for gas: 3-5%.
- [x] **Trade-off:** ↑EA → ↓CO (good) but ↑flue gas loss (bad). Optimal EA balances both.
- [x] **Orsat apparatus** = wet-chemical analyzer for **dry** flue gas: CO₂ (KOH) → O₂ (pyrogallol) → CO (cuprous chloride). **N₂ by difference.**
- [x] **Modern instrumentation:** Zirconia O₂ probe (high-temp electrolyte), NDIR analyzers (CO/CO₂/CH₄), paramagnetic O₂ sensor.
- [x] **Combustion efficiency** $\eta = CO_2^{actual}/CO_2^{max} \times 100$ — typical > 90%.
- [x] **Cross-limiting control** prevents both fuel-rich and air-rich transients.
- [x] **Heat values to memorize:** C → 32.8 MJ/kg, H₂ → 142 MJ/kg, S → 9.3 MJ/kg.
- [x] **Emissions priority:** NOx (thermal + fuel-bound) > SOx (from S) > CO (incomplete) > Particulates (unburnt C).
- [x] **NCV = GCV − 2.442 × (9H/100)** — accounts for latent heat of water vapor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Mile Tip for KTU Exam:** Always **state the basis (mass or volume)** explicitly, **show unit conversions**, and **box the final answer** with a clear physical interpretation. KTU examiners award full marks only when the **process logic**, not just the numerical answer, is clearly demonstrated.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Products of Combustion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Products of Combustion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Products of Combustion (KTU 2024 Definition):** The chemical species generated as a result of the exothermic oxidation reaction between a fuel (containing primarily Carbon, Hydrogen, Sulphur, and trace elements) and an oxidizer (typically atmospheric air). These products include heat energy, flue gases (CO₂, CO, H₂O, SO₂, SO₃, NOₓ, O₂, N₂), particulate matter (ash, soot), and unburnt hydrocarbons.

**Stoichiometric Combustion** refers to the *theoretical* or *ideal* combustion process where fuel is burned with **exactly** the chemically required quantity of oxygen, leaving no free oxygen and producing only completely oxidized products (CO₂, H₂O, SO₂).

**Actual Combustion** in real power plants involves *excess air* (typically 10–30% for solid fuels, 5–15% for liquid/gaseous fuels) to ensure complete combustion and prevent the formation of combustible CO and soot.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Campfire with Too Much Wind**
> Imagine lighting a charcoal grill. If you blow on it gently (just enough air), the charcoal burns completely, leaving only white ash and CO₂. If you blow too hard (excess air), the fire gets chilled — you waste heat because the excess air absorbs energy and escapes as warm flue gas. If you barely blow (insufficient air), you get black, smoky charcoal — meaning *incomplete* combustion, producing dangerous CO instead of CO₂.
>
> **Power plants** face this exact balancing act. The "products of combustion" are the gases (and ash) that leave the boiler stack. Instrument engineers measure these gases continuously to:
> 1. **Maximize efficiency** (don't waste fuel or air)
> 2. **Minimize pollution** (reduce NOₓ, SOₓ, CO)
> 3. **Ensure safety** (avoid explosive CO buildup)

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric O₂ requirement for Carbon (C):** **2.667 kg O₂ / kg C**
- **Stoichiometric O₂ requirement for Hydrogen (H₂):** **8.0 kg O₂ / kg H₂**
- **Stoichiometric O₂ requirement for Sulphur (S):** **2.0 kg O₂ / kg S**
- **Molecular weight of dry air:** **28.97 kg/kg-mol** (≈ 29)
- **N₂/O₂ ratio in air (by volume):** **3.773 : 1** (i.e., 79% N₂ and 21% O₂)
- **N₂/O₂ ratio in air (by mass):** **3.31 : 1** (i.e., 76.7% N₂ and 23.3% O₂)
- **Calorific value of Carbon (to CO₂):** **33,850 kJ/kg** (Higher Heating)
- **Calorific value of Carbon (to CO):** **10,200 kJ/kg**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The topic "Products of Combustion" is the **foundation** for flue gas analysis, combustion efficiency calculations, and emissions monitoring — all of which are direct learning outcomes of this module.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Combustion Products

| Product | Chemical Formula | Source | Engineering Significance |
|---------|------------------|--------|--------------------------|
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Complete C combustion | Indicator of complete combustion |
| Carbon Monoxide | CO | Incomplete C combustion | **Toxic**, indicates fuel waste |
| Water Vapour | H₂O | Hydrogen combustion | Major heat carrier; condenses → corrosion |
| Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | Sulphur combustion | Causes acid rain, corrosion |
| Sulphur Trioxide | SO₃ | Catalytic oxidation of SO₂ | Forms sulphuric acid (dew point corrosion) |
| Nitrogen Oxides | NO, NO₂ | High-T fixation of N₂ | Major pollutant (smog, acid rain) |
| Free Oxygen | O₂ | Excess air | Indicator of air-fuel balance |
| Nitrogen | N₂ | Inert from air | Heat absorber (energy loss) |
| Ash / Particulates | — | Mineral content of fuel | Erosion, fouling, pollution |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Chemistry of Combustion — Element by Element

#### 2.1.1 Carbon Oxidation (Two Pathways)

**Pathway 1 — Complete Combustion:**

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + Heat \;(33{,}850 \; kJ/kg \; C)$$

**Pathway 2 — Incomplete Combustion:**

$$2C + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO + Heat \;(10{,}200 \; kJ/kg \; C)$$

The CO formed can further oxidize:

$$2CO + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + Heat$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Power Plants:** If even 1% of carbon converts to CO instead of CO₂, the plant loses roughly **30% of the available heat** from that carbon. This is why boiler operators monitor O₂ and CO in flue gas continuously.

---

#### 2.1.2 Hydrogen Oxidation

$$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + Heat \;(142{,}000 \; kJ/kg \; H_2)$$

The water vapour produced carries away significant latent heat. In a boiler, this is recovered through economizers, air preheaters, and feedwater heating.

---

#### 2.1.3 Sulphur Oxidation

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 + Heat$$

$$2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3 \quad (\text{catalyzed by V}_2O_5 \text{ or high temp})$$

The SO₃ is particularly dangerous because it combines with water vapour in the flue gas to form **H₂SO₄ (sulphuric acid mist)**, causing severe dew-point corrosion in the air preheater and economizer tubes when temperatures drop below the **acid dew point (~150 °C)**.

---

#### 2.1.4 Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ) Formation

**Three mechanisms** (per Zeldovich):

- **Thermal NOₓ:** $N_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO$ (dominant above 1300 °C, follows Arrhenius)
- **Fuel NOₓ:** Nitrogen compounds in fuel oxidize to NO (dominant in coal/oil firing)
- **Prompt NOₓ:** Hydrocarbon radicals react with N₂ (small contribution)

$$N_2 + O \rightarrow NO + N$$
$$N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern power plants use **Low-NOₓ Burners (LNB)** and **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** to control NOₓ, which is the most regulated combustion pollutant.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Air-Fuel Ratio Engineering

**Mass of air theoretically required (O₂ minimum):**

$$O_2^{min} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_2^{fuel} \quad \text{(in kg O₂ / kg fuel)}$$

where C, H₂, S, O₂ are mass fractions of Carbon, Hydrogen, Sulphur, and Oxygen in the fuel (per kg of fuel).

**Stoichiometric Air:**

$$\text{Air}_{stoich} = O_2^{min} \times \frac{100}{23.3} \quad \text{(kg air / kg fuel)}$$

The factor 100/23.3 (= 4.29) accounts for the **23.3% by mass of O₂ in air** (with the remaining 76.7% being N₂).

**Actual Air Supplied:**

$$\text{Air}_{actual} = m \times \text{Air}_{stoich}$$

where $m$ = excess air factor = $1 + \frac{\text{Excess Air \%}}{100}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Excess Air (EA) Definition:** The air supplied *beyond* the stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage of the stoichiometric air.
> $$\text{Excess Air (\%)} = \frac{\text{Actual Air} - \text{Stoichiometric Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}} \times 100$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Min O₂ per kg fuel | $\frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | Stoichiometry |
| 2 | Stoichiometric air | $O_2^{min} \times \frac{100}{23.3}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Combustion design |
| 3 | Excess air % | $\frac{A_{actual} - A_{stoich}}{A_{stoich}} \times 100$ | % | Boiler tuning |
| 4 | Air-Fuel ratio (AFR) | $\frac{\text{Air mass}}{\text{Fuel mass}}$ | dimensionless | Combustion control |
| 5 | Stoichiometric AFR for CH₄ | 17.16:1 | — | Gas fuel benchmark |
| 6 | Stoichiometric AFR for C₈H₁₈ (octane) | 15.13:1 | — | Liquid fuel benchmark |
| 7 | Equivalence ratio $\phi$ | $\frac{(A/F)_{stoich}}{(A/F)_{actual}}$ | dimensionless | Combustion stability |
| 8 | CO₂ % in dry flue gas (max) | $\frac{21}{1 + (\text{AFR stoichiometric} \times \frac{M_{air}}{M_{CO_2}} \times \frac{1}{0.21})}$ | % | Orsat analysis |
| 9 | Combustion Efficiency $\eta_c$ | $\frac{HHV - \text{Losses}}{HHV} \times 100$ | % | Boiler efficiency |
| 10 | Heat loss in dry flue gas | $m_g \cdot c_p \cdot (T_g - T_a)$ | kJ/kg fuel | Efficiency calc |
| 11 | Heat loss due to H₂ → H₂O | $m_{H_2O} \cdot (h_{fg} + c_p \cdot \Delta T)$ | kJ/kg fuel | Latent heat loss |
| 12 | NOₓ formation rate (Zeldovich) | $\propto \exp(-E/RT)$ | ppm | NOₓ prediction |
| 13 | Orsat: % N₂ (by difference) | $100 - (CO_2 + CO + O_2)$ | % | Flue gas analysis |
| 14 | Air supplied (from O₂ in Orsat) | $N_2 \times \frac{21}{79} - O_2$ | % | Orsat interpretation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The "Orsat method" assumes CO₂, CO, O₂, and N₂ are the *only* dry flue gas components. SO₂ is typically absorbed with CO₂ in the Orsat apparatus. This is a frequent exam question.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Products of Combustion framework underpins the **entire emissions monitoring and combustion control industry**. In a modern 500 MW coal-fired plant:

- **Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS)** measure NOₓ, SO₂, CO, O₂, CO₂, particulates, and flow rate — all derived from products of combustion principles.
- **Boiler combustion control loops** (cross-limited O₂ trim) use flue gas O₂% as the feedback signal to optimize excess air in real-time.
- **DeNOₓ and DeSOₓ systems** are designed based on the formation mechanisms of these pollutants.
- **Heat rate optimization** (efficiency) calculations rely on knowing the *quantity* and *temperature* of flue gas products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for a Given Fuel

**Given:** Ultimate analysis of a fuel (mass fractions):
- C = 0.75, H₂ = 0.05, S = 0.02, O₂ = 0.08, N₂ = 0.02, Ash = 0.08 (total = 1.00)

**Goal:** Find the stoichiometric oxygen and air required per kg of fuel.

**Step 1 — Set up the three oxidation reactions (per kg of each element):**

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{32}{12} = 2.667 \; kg \; O_2 / kg \; C$$

$$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{16}{2} = 8.0 \; kg \; O_2 / kg \; H_2$$

$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{32}{32} = 1.0 \; kg \; O_2 / kg \; S$$

**Step 2 — Apply the general formula:**

$$O_2^{min} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_2^{fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values:**

$$O_2^{min} = \frac{8}{3}(0.75) + 8(0.05) + 0.02 - 0.08$$

**Step 4 — Compute term by term:**

$$O_2^{min} = 2.000 + 0.400 + 0.020 - 0.080 = 2.340 \; kg \; O_2 / kg \; fuel$$

**Step 5 — Convert to stoichiometric air (using 23.3% O₂ by mass in air):**

$$\text{Air}_{stoich} = \frac{2.340}{0.233} = 10.04 \; kg \; air / kg \; fuel$$

$$\boxed{\text{Air}_{stoich} \approx 10.04 \; kg \; air / kg \; fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is typical of a bituminous coal (which ranges 8–12 kg air/kg fuel stoichiometric).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Flue Gas Composition from Orsat Analysis

**Orsat analysis** is a volumetric technique where dry flue gas is passed through a series of absorbents:
1. **KOH** → absorbs CO₂ (and SO₂)
2. **Alkaline pyrogallol** → absorbs O₂
3. **Ammoniacal cuprous chloride** → absorbs CO
4. **Remaining gas** → assumed to be N₂

**Given Orsat readings (dry basis, % volume):**
- CO₂ = 12.0%
- O₂ = 4.0%
- CO = 1.0%
- N₂ = 83.0% (by difference)

**Step 1 — Verify sum:**

$$12.0 + 4.0 + 1.0 + 83.0 = 100.0 \; \% \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Determine the actual air supplied.**

Recall that the **N₂ in flue gas comes only from the supplied air** (fuel-bound N₂ is negligible for most coals, and we assume zero fuel-N₂ for this standard analysis).

The N₂/O₂ volume ratio in air is **3.773:1**. So the air associated with the 4.0% O₂ in flue gas contributed:

$$N_2^{from \; air \; reacting \; with \; fuel} = 4.0 \times 3.773 = 15.09\%$$

This 15.09% N₂ travelled with the O₂ that *reacted* with fuel. The **excess N₂** (beyond this) came with the *excess air*:

$$N_2^{excess} = 83.0 - 15.09 = 67.91\%$$

The **excess air** (as % of stoichiometric air) is calculated from the ratio of N₂-excess to N₂-stoichiometric:

$$\text{Excess Air (\%)} = \frac{N_2^{excess}}{N_2^{from \; stoichiometric \; O_2}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Excess Air} = \frac{67.91}{15.09} \times 100 = 449.7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **excessively high** — typical values are 15–50%. Such high excess air indicates either a measurement error or a grossly inefficient boiler. Students must check their calculation logic.

**Step 3 — Re-check with corrected numbers** (assumed CO₂ = 15%, O₂ = 3%, CO = 0.5%, N₂ = 81.5%):

$$N_2^{stoich \; partner} = 3.0 \times 3.773 = 11.32\%$$

$$N_2^{excess} = 81.5 - 11.32 = 70.18\%$$

$$\text{Excess Air} = \frac{70.18}{11.32} \times 100 = 619.97\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the numbers don't sum to 100 or give unrealistic excess air, the question is flawed or you're misinterpreting. Re-verify the readings first.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Combustion Efficiency via Flue Gas Analysis (Siegert Formula)

The **Siegert formula** estimates dry flue gas losses (the dominant boiler loss).

**Given:**
- Flue gas temperature $T_g = 220°C$
- Ambient air temperature $T_a = 30°C$
- CO₂% in dry flue gas = 12%
- Excess air = 30%

**Step 1 — Calculate the specific heat of dry flue gas.**

For dry flue gas (average composition), the approximate specific heat:

$$c_{pg} = 1.005 + 0.0001 \times T_g \quad [kJ/kg\cdot K]$$

$$c_{pg} = 1.005 + 0.0001 \times 220 = 1.027 \; kJ/kg \cdot K$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel.**

For each kg of fuel burned, with stoichiometric air $A_{stoich}$ and excess air factor $m = 1.3$:

The dry flue gas consists of:
- CO₂ from carbon: $\frac{44}{12} \times C = 3.667C$ kg
- SO₂ from sulphur: $\frac{64}{32} \times S = 2S$ kg
- N₂ from air: $0.767 \times m \times A_{stoich}$
- O₂ from excess air: $0.233 \times (m-1) \times A_{stoich}$

**Step 3 — Apply the dry flue gas loss formula:**

$$L_{dry} = m_g \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)$$

For a coal with $A_{stoich} = 10$ kg air/kg fuel:

$$m_g \approx A_{stoich} \cdot m + \text{products} \approx 13 + 2.95 = 15.95 \; kg \; gas / kg \; fuel$$

**Step 4 — Compute the heat loss:**

$$L_{dry} = 15.95 \times 1.027 \times (220 - 30) = 15.95 \times 1.027 \times 190$$

$$L_{dry} = 3111.0 \; kJ / kg \; fuel$$

**Step 5 — Add the moisture loss from H₂ combustion:**

Water from fuel-bound H₂:
$$m_{H_2O} = 9 \times H_2 = 9 \times 0.05 = 0.45 \; kg \; H_2O / kg \; fuel$$

Plus water from combustion of H in air (negligible since atmospheric H₂O is small).

Heat lost in H₂O vapour (latent + sensible):

$$L_{H_2O} = 0.45 \times (2442 + 1.88 \times 190) = 0.45 \times 2799.2 = 1259.6 \; kJ/kg$$

where $2442 \; kJ/kg = h_{fg}$ at 25°C and $1.88 \; kJ/kg\cdot K$ is vapour specific heat.

**Step 6 — Compute combustion efficiency (assuming fuel HHV = 25,000 kJ/kg):**

$$\eta_c = \frac{HHV - L_{dry} - L_{H_2O} - L_{CO}}{HHV} \times 100$$

(neglecting $L_{CO}$ and unburnt carbon for simplicity)

$$\eta_c = \frac{25000 - 3111.0 - 1259.6}{25000} \times 100 = \frac{20629.4}{25000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c \approx 82.52\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a realistic value for a small industrial coal-fired boiler (large utility plants achieve 90%+). The **dominant loss** is dry flue gas sensible heat (63% of the total loss here), which is why economizers and air preheaters are critical.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Excess Air on Combustion

| Excess Air (%) | Stack Temp (°C) | O₂ in Flue Gas (%) | Combustion Efficiency (%) | NOₓ Emissions | Notes |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 5 | 180 | 1.0 | 86 | Low | Risk of CO, soot |
| 10 | 190 | 2.0 | 85 | Low | Best for gaseous fuels |
| 15 | 200 | 3.0 | 84 | Moderate | **Optimal for most boilers** |
| 25 | 215 | 4.0 | 82.5 | Higher | Typical for coal |
| 50 | 250 | 7.5 | 78 | High | Wasteful |
| 100 | 310 | 13.5 | 70 | Very High | Grossly inefficient |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Increasing excess air *reduces* efficiency (due to heated air leaving the stack) but *improves* completeness of combustion (less CO, soot). There is an **optimum** where the marginal gain in combustion completeness equals the marginal loss to stack gas heat. This is what boiler control systems continuously search for.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Combustion System & Products Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fuelIn["FUEL SUPPLY\nCoal or Oil or Gas"] --> mixerA
    airIn["FORCED AIR\nFrom FD Fan"] --> airPreheater
    airPreheater --> mixerA
    mixerA["MIXER\nBurner Assembly"] --> combustor
    combustor["COMBUSTION CHAMBER\nFurnace / Boiler"] --> heatXfer
    heatXfer["HEAT TRANSFER ZONE\nBoiler Tubes"] --> exhaustPath
    exhaustPath["FLUE GAS PATH\nSuperheater, Economizer,\nAir Preheater"] --> emissions
    emissions["EMISSIONS\nCO2, CO, SO2, NOX\nH2O, O2, N2, Ash"] --> stack
    stack["STACK\nAtmosphere"]

    cemsSensor["CEMS SENSORS\nO2 Analyzer, NDIR CO2\nChemiluminescent NOX\nParamagnetic O2"] --> controlRoom
    controlRoom["CONTROL ROOM\nDCS / PLC\nCombustion Optimization"] -. Feedback Control Signal .-> mixerA
    cemsSensor -. Continuous Monitoring .-> exhaustPath
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**

1. **Fuel & Air Inputs:** Fuel and preheated air enter via the mixer.
2. **Combustion:** In the furnace, the products of combustion form — heat is released.
3. **Heat Recovery:** Flue gases pass through heat exchange surfaces (boiler tubes, economizer, air preheater), transferring their heat to water/steam/air.
4. **Emissions Sensing:** Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) measure the *products of combustion* (O₂, CO₂, CO, NOₓ, SO₂) along the exhaust path.
5. **Control Loop:** The measured flue gas composition is fed back to the control room, which adjusts the fuel-air mixture at the mixer to maintain optimal excess air.

---

### 4.2 Process Diagram: Formation Pathways of Combustion Products

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cInput["C in Fuel"] --> path1["Path 1: Complete Oxidation\nC + O2 -> CO2\nReleases 33,850 kJ/kg C"]
    cInput --> path2["Path 2: Incomplete Oxidation\n2C + O2 -> 2CO\nReleases 10,200 kJ/kg C\nLOSS OF 23,650 kJ/kg C"]

    h2Input["H2 in Fuel"] --> path3["H2 Combustion\n2H2 + O2 -> 2H2O\nReleases 142,000 kJ/kg H2"]

    sInput["S in Fuel"] --> path4["S to SO2\nS + O2 -> SO2"]
    path4 --> path5["SO2 to SO3\n2SO2 + O2 -> 2SO3\nCatalyst: V2O5\nForms H2SO4 Dew"]

    n2Air["N2 from Air"] --> path6["Thermal NOX\nN2 + O2 -> 2NO\nAbove 1300 C\nExponential in T"]
    nFuel["N in Fuel"] --> path7["Fuel NOX\nN compounds -> NO\nDominant in coal"]

    o2Excess["Excess O2 from Air"] --> path8["Passes through\nInert heat loss\nIndicator of excess air"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each box represents a *chemical pathway* and *energy consequence*. The product that forms depends on:
> - **Local equivalence ratio** (excess or deficient O₂)
> - **Local temperature** (controls NOₓ, dissociation)
> - **Residence time** (how long gases stay hot)
> - **Catalysts present** (e.g., ash contains Fe, V, which catalyze SO₃ formation)

---

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Excess Air vs Combustion Mode

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Lean
    [*] --> Stoich
    [*] --> Rich

    Lean: LEAN MIXTURE\nExcess Air > 0\nProducts: CO2, O2, N2\nIssue: Heat loss to stack\nBenefit: No CO
    Stoich: STOICHIOMETRIC\nAir = exact requirement\nProducts: CO2, H2O, N2\nTheoretical ideal
    Rich: RICH MIXTURE\nDeficient Air\nProducts: CO, H2, soot, CO2\nIssue: Wasted fuel, smoke\nIssue: Safety hazard

    Lean --> Stoich: Reduce Air
    Stoich --> Rich: Reduce Air Further
    Stoich --> Lean: Add Air
    Rich --> Stoich: Add Air

    Lean --> NOX_Zone: High T promotes NOX
    Rich --> CO_Zone: CO forms and rises
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Real boilers operate *slightly lean* (5–25% excess air) to avoid the CO penalty of rich operation, accepting a small stack loss. This is the **"lean-burn" philosophy** of modern combustion control.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Define "Products of Combustion." List the major gaseous products formed during the complete combustion of a typical hydrocarbon fuel.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Products of combustion are the chemical species — including gases, water vapour, particulates, and heat — generated when a fuel reacts exothermically with an oxidizer (usually air).

**Major gaseous products of complete combustion:**

1. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂):** From the oxidation of carbon content in fuel.
2. **Water Vapour (H₂O):** From the oxidation of hydrogen content in fuel.
3. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂):** From the oxidation of sulphur content in fuel (forms SO₃ in trace amounts).
4. **Nitrogen (N₂):** Passes through unchanged from the combustion air.
5. **Oxygen (O₂):** Present if excess air is supplied.

> The products may also include **incompletely oxidized species** (CO, soot) and **thermal/ fuel NOₓ** under real boiler conditions.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing with brief chemical origin]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
*State the stoichiometric mass of air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of carbon. Show the chemical equation.*

**Model Answer:**

**Chemical Equation:**

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$

**Calculation of oxygen required:**
- Molecular weight of C = 12 kg/kg-mol
- Molecular weight of O₂ = 32 kg/kg-mol
- For 1 kg-mol (12 kg) of C, 1 kg-mol (32 kg) of O₂ is required.
- Therefore, per kg of C:

$$O_2 = \frac{32}{12} = 2.667 \; kg \; O_2 / kg \; C$$

**Stoichiometric air (using 23.3% O₂ by mass in air):**

$$\text{Air}_{stoich} = \frac{2.667}{0.233} = 11.45 \; kg \; air / kg \; C$$

$$\boxed{\text{Stoichiometric air} \approx 11.45 \; kg \; air \; per \; kg \; C}$$

**[3 Marks: 1 for equation, 1 for O₂ calc, 1 for air conversion]**

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

A coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass:
- C = 72%, H₂ = 5%, S = 1.5%, O₂ = 6%, N₂ = 1.5%, Ash = 14%
- (Moisture is neglected in this analysis)

**(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of coal. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** If 40% excess air is supplied, determine:
- (i) Actual air supplied per kg of coal
- (ii) The Orsat analysis (dry flue gas composition) assuming complete combustion

**[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Stoichiometric Air Calculation**

**Step 1: Compute O₂ required per kg of fuel** (using the standard formula):

$$O_2^{min} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_2^{fuel}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (mass fractions in kg/kg fuel):**

$$O_2^{min} = \frac{8}{3}(0.72) + 8(0.05) + 0.015 - 0.06$$

**Step 3: Evaluate:**

$$O_2^{min} = 1.920 + 0.400 + 0.015 - 0.060 = 2.275 \; kg \; O_2 / kg \; fuel$$

**Step 4: Convert to stoichiometric air (dividing by 0.233 = mass fraction of O₂ in air):**

$$\text{Air}_{stoich} = \frac{2.275}{0.233} = 9.764 \; kg \; air / kg \; fuel$$

$$\boxed{\text{Air}_{stoich} \approx 9.76 \; kg \; air / kg \; coal}$$

**Part (b) — Actual Air & Orsat Analysis**

**Step 5: Compute actual air supplied (40% excess, m = 1.40):**

$$\text{Air}_{actual} = 1.40 \times 9.764 = 13.67 \; kg \; air / kg \; fuel$$

**Step 6: Compute dry flue gas components per kg of fuel:**

- **CO₂** (from carbon, 44/12 ratio):
$$m_{CO_2} = \frac{44}{12} \times 0.72 = 2.640 \; kg$$

- **SO₂** (from sulphur, 64/32 ratio):
$$m_{SO_2} = \frac{64}{32} \times 0.015 = 0.030 \; kg$$

- **O₂** (from excess air, 23.3% of excess air):
$$m_{O_2} = 0.233 \times 0.40 \times 9.764 = 0.910 \; kg$$

- **N₂** (from supplied air, 76.7% of total air):
$$m_{N_2} = 0.767 \times 13.67 = 10.485 \; kg$$

**Step 7: Total dry flue gas mass:**

$$m_{total} = 2.640 + 0.030 + 0.910 + 10.485 = 14.065 \; kg \; dry \; flue \; gas / kg \; fuel$$

**Step 8: Convert mass fractions to Orsat (% by volume, ≈ % by mole for ideal gas):**

Mole ratios = mass ratio × (M_total / M_i). Since we need volume % (mole %), we can directly use mass % for typical flue gas (small error) — for accurate KTU answers, use molecular weights:

| Component | Mass (kg) | M (kg/kg-mol) | kmol | Volume % |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|------|----------|
| CO₂ | 2.640 | 44 | 0.0600 | **15.65%** |
| SO₂ | 0.030 | 64 | 0.00047 | 0.12% |
| O₂ | 0.910 | 32 | 0.0284 | 7.41% |
| N₂ | 10.485 | 28 | 0.3745 | 97.65% (incl. N₂ from fuel) |

Wait — let me recompute N₂ more carefully (fuel N₂ = 0.015 kg = 0.015/28 = 0.000536 kmol):

**N₂ in flue gas = 10.485 + 0.015 = 10.500 kg → 10.500/28 = 0.3750 kmol**

Total kmol of dry flue gas = 0.0600 + 0.00047 + 0.0284 + 0.3750 = 0.4639 kmol

| Component | Volume % |
|-----------|----------|
| CO₂ | 12.94% |
| SO₂ | 0.10% |
| O₂ | 6.13% |
| N₂ | 80.83% |
| **Total** | **100%** |

> [!NOTE]
> Orsat apparatus absorbs CO₂ and SO₂ together, so reported CO₂% would be **13.04%** (combined). For a typical KTU answer, treat SO₂ as part of CO₂ reading.

**Final Orsat Analysis (dry basis):**
- CO₂ + SO₂ = **13.0%**
- O₂ = **6.1%**
- N₂ = **80.9%**

$$\boxed{\text{Orsat: CO}_2 = 13.0\%, \; O_2 = 6.1\%, \; N_2 = 80.9\%}$$

**[14 Marks: Part a 7 marks; Part b (i) 2 marks, (ii) 5 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the formation of various products of combustion in a coal-fired boiler. Discuss the factors that influence the formation of NOₓ and SO₃. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** The Orsat analysis of flue gas from a furnace burning coal is:
- CO₂ = 12%, O₂ = 6%, CO = 1%, N₂ = 81%
- Flue gas temperature = 250°C, Ambient temperature = 30°C
- HHV of coal = 28,000 kJ/kg
- Stoichiometric air = 9.5 kg/kg fuel
- Hydrogen content in fuel = 5%

Calculate the **dry flue gas loss**, the **loss due to moisture (H₂O) formation**, and the **combustion efficiency**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Formation of Products of Combustion**

*(Diagram: see SECTION 4, Diagram 4.2 above)*

**Products formed:**

1. **CO₂** — From complete oxidation of C in coal.
2. **CO** — From incomplete oxidation of C (insufficient O₂ or quenching).
3. **H₂O** — From oxidation of H₂ in coal.
4. **SO₂** — From oxidation of S in coal.
5. **SO₃** — From catalytic oxidation of SO₂ (Fe₂O₃ and V₂O₅ in fly ash act as catalysts).
6. **NOₓ** — From three mechanisms (Thermal, Fuel, Prompt — Zeldovich mechanism).
7. **O₂** — From unreacted excess air.
8. **N₂** — Passes through from combustion air.

**Factors influencing NOₓ formation:**
- **Peak flame temperature** (Thermal NOₓ rises exponentially above 1300°C).
- **Excess air level** (more O₂ → more NOₓ).
- **Residence time at high temperature.**
- **Nitrogen content in fuel** (Fuel NOₓ, dominant in coal).
- **Burner design** (staged combustion reduces NOₓ).

**Factors influencing SO₃ formation:**
- **Excess air** (more O₂ available for SO₂ → SO₃).
- **Flame temperature and residence time** (kinetics).
- **Catalytic activity of fly ash** (V₂O₅, Fe₂O₃).
- **Local equivalence ratio** (fuel-rich zones favor SO₃ reduction back to SO₂).

**[7 Marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for product list, 2 for influencing factors]**

**Part (b) — Combustion Efficiency**

**Step 1: Determine excess air from Orsat.**

$$N_2^{stoich \; O_2 \; partner} = O_2 \times 3.773 = 6 \times 3.773 = 22.64\%$$

$$N_2^{excess \; partner} = 81 - 22.64 = 58.36\%$$

$$\text{Excess Air (\%)} = \frac{58.36}{22.64} \times 100 = 257.8\%$$

This is **too high** (unrealistic). Either assume the data has O₂ = 6% and EA = 25% (a typical case), or accept the unrealistic data. For KTU solving, use the data as given but note it.

**Step 2: Calculate dry flue gas per kg of fuel.**

With EA = 25% (m = 1.25):
$$\text{Air}_{actual} = 1.25 \times 9.5 = 11.875 \; kg \; air/kg \; fuel$$

$$N_2 = 0.767 \times 11.875 = 9.108 \; kg$$

$$O_2^{excess} = 0.233 \times 0.25 \times 9.5 = 0.553 \; kg$$

Dry flue gas mass ≈ 9.108 + 0.553 + (CO₂ and CO from fuel) ≈ 11.5 kg

**Step 3: Dry flue gas loss (sensible heat).**

$$L_{dry} = m_g \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)$$

Using $c_{pg} \approx 1.05 \; kJ/kg\cdot K$ for dry flue gas:

$$L_{dry} = 11.5 \times 1.05 \times (250 - 30) = 11.5 \times 1.05 \times 220 = 2656.5 \; kJ/kg$$

**Step 4: Moisture loss (H₂O from fuel).**

Water from fuel H₂:
$$m_{H_2O} = 9 \times H_2 = 9 \times 0.05 = 0.45 \; kg \; H_2O/kg \; fuel$$

$$L_{H_2O} = m_{H_2O} \cdot [h_{fg} + c_{pv}(T_g - T_{ref})]$$

$$L_{H_2O} = 0.45 \times [2442 + 1.88 \times 220] = 0.45 \times 2855.6 = 1285.0 \; kJ/kg$$

**Step 5: Combustion efficiency.**

$$\eta_c = \frac{HHV - L_{dry} - L_{H_2O}}{HHV} \times 100$$

$$\eta_c = \frac{28000 - 2656.5 - 1285.0}{28000} \times 100 = \frac{24058.5}{28000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c \approx 85.9\%}$$

$$\boxed{L_{dry} = 2656.5 \; kJ/kg, \quad L_{H_2O} = 1285.0 \; kJ/kg, \quad \eta_c = 85.9\%}$$

**[14 Marks: Part (a) 7, Part (b) 7 — Part (b) breakdown: Orsat 2, dry loss 2, moisture loss 2, efficiency 1]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in "Products of Combustion" Questions (KTU Board Exam Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Wrong Stoichiometric Air Factor:** Students often use **21% (volume)** instead of **23.3% (mass)** when converting O₂ to air. Always check whether the question asks for kg or Nm³.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Fuel-Bound Oxygen:** When the ultimate analysis includes O₂ (as in biomass, lignite), the formula $O_2^{min} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_2^{fuel}$ must **subtract** the fuel's own oxygen. Skipping this is a **2-mark error** every time.
>
> 3. **Orsat N₂ Miscalculation:** N₂ in Orsat is **always** by difference: $N_2 = 100 - (CO_2 + O_2 + CO)$. A small arithmetic slip here cascades through the entire excess air calculation.
>
> 4. **Using 79/21 instead of 3.773 for N₂/O₂ ratio:** The 3.773 figure is N₂/O₂ *by volume* = 79/21 ≈ 3.773. Some students mistakenly use 3.31 (mass ratio). For Orsat (volumetric), use 3.773.
>
> 5. **Neglecting Moisture Loss:** When asked for "combustion efficiency," including only dry flue gas loss (not moisture loss) inflates the efficiency unrealistically. Always include **both** losses for full marks.
>
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency in HHV:** kJ/kg vs. kCal/kg vs. MJ/tonne — KTU expects **kJ/kg**. A common error is mixing units in the final efficiency formula.
>
> 7. **SO₂ in Orsat:** The Orsat KOH absorber catches **both** CO₂ and SO₂. If asked for "Orsat CO₂ %", the value reported is actually CO₂ + SO₂. Mention this explicitly in answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Products of Combustion (Module 2)**
>
> - [x] **Products of Combustion** = CO₂, CO, H₂O, SO₂, SO₃, NO, NO₂, O₂, N₂, ash, soot.
>
> - [x] **Stoichiometric O₂ Formula:** $O_2^{min} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H_2 + S - O_2^{fuel}$ (kg O₂/kg fuel).
>
> - [x] **Stoichiometric Air Conversion:** Divide O₂ min by **0.233** (mass fraction) → gives kg air/kg fuel.
>
> - [x] **Excess Air Formula:** $EA\% = \frac{N_2 - 3.773 \cdot O_2}{3.773 \cdot O_2} \times 100$ (from Orsat).
>
> - [x] **Orsat Order of Absorption:** KOH (CO₂+SO₂) → Pyrogallol (O₂) → CuCl (CO) → N₂ by difference.
>
> - [x] **Three NOₓ Mechanisms:** Thermal, Fuel, Prompt (Zeldovich). Thermal dominates above 1300°C.
>
> - [x] **SO₃ Source:** Catalytic oxidation of SO₂ by V₂O₅ / Fe₂O₃ in fly ash. Causes acid dew-point corrosion below ~150°C.
>
> - [x] **Combustion Efficiency:** $\eta_c = \frac{HHV - L_{dry} - L_{H_2O} - L_{CO}}{HHV} \times 100$
>
> - [x] **Optimum Excess Air:** 15–25% for coal, 5–15% for oil/gas — balances completeness vs. stack loss.
>
> - [x] **Incomplete Combustion Penalty:** CO formation loses ~70% of carbon's heat value — heavily penalized in efficiency.
>
> - [x] **Real-World Application:** Every measurement in CEMS (O₂, CO₂, CO, NOₓ, SO₂) is a *product of combustion* — making this topic the **backbone** of combustion instrumentation.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Products of Combustion | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEICT523**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 2

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Systems** in a power plant refer to the **integrated assembly of equipment, instrumentation, and control loops** that handle the **receipt, storage, preparation, transportation, conditioning, and metered delivery** of primary and secondary fuels to the combustor/boiler. In instrumentation terms, the *fuel system* is bounded by the **inlet boundary** (e.g., wagon tippler, gas pressure reducing station) and the **burner nozzle** as the outlet boundary.

The fuel subsystem must perform four engineering functions simultaneously:

1. **Supply Assurance** — Provide an uninterrupted, on-demand mass flow of fuel to the boiler at the firing rate demanded by the turbine/grid load.
2. **Quality Conditioning** — Adjust fuel properties (size reduction for coal, viscosity & temperature for oil, pressure & filtration for gas) to acceptable combustion specifications.
3. **Metering & Accounting** — Quantify mass/volume, calorific value, and composition for **billing, efficiency, and environmental compliance**.
4. **Safety Containment** — Prevent explosion, fire, and environmental release through interlocks, firefighting, and emergency shut-off.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Pipeline" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a fuel system as the **kitchen pipeline of a 100-floor hotel**.
> - The **stockyard / tank farm** is the *godown (warehouse)* where raw materials (rice/grains/oil) are unloaded from trucks and stored.
> - **Conveyors / pipelines** are the *staircase carrying* that moves the material to the kitchen.
> - **Pulverizers / heaters** are the *stone-grinder or pre-heater* that prepares the material to a usable form (flour/warm oil).
> - **Flow meters, calorimeters, density meters** are the *measuring cups and weighing scales* used by the chef to keep the recipe right.
> - **Burner management system (BMS)** is the *chef and fire safety officer* — they decide when to light, how much to add, and when to shut down if something burns.

This analogy is extremely useful for KTU viva questions when explaining why we need so many sub-systems in series.

## 1.3 Physical & Process Constants to Remember

- **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** of Indian coal: typically **2,500 – 4,500 kcal/kg**
- **Higher Calorific Value (HCV)** of furnace oil: approximately **10,200 kcal/kg**
- **Density of furnace oil (HFO)** at 15 °C: **0.94 – 0.98 g/cc**
- **LPG specific gravity** (with respect to air): approximately **1.5 – 2.0**
- **Natural gas standard density**: **0.7 – 0.85 kg/Nm³**
- **Standard coal equivalent**: 1 tonne of oil = **1.46 tonnes of coal equivalent (TCE)**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **chain of fuel conversion** — *"Why is bituminous coal preferred over lignite?"* The crisp answer is its **higher calorific value (4,000–5,500 kcal/kg)**, **lower moisture (<10%)**, and **better flame stability** in pulverized-fuel boilers.

## 1.4 Classification of Fuels (Engineering View)

| Fuel Class | Examples | Storage Form | Conditioning Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solid** | Coal, Lignite, Bagasse, Biomass | Stockyard / bunker / silo | Crushing, drying, pulverizing |
| **Liquid** | HFO, LDO, LSHS, Diesel, Naphtha | Day tank + main tank | Heating, pumping, atomization |
| **Gaseous** | Natural gas, LNG, LPG, Biogas | Spherical / bullet vessels | Pressure regulation, odorizing |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Sub-Systems of a Fuel Handling Plant

A KTU module question typically asks *"With a neat block diagram explain the fuel handling system."* The answer must contain the following seven functional blocks arranged in **series flow**:

1. **Receiving / Unloading** — Wagon tippler, truck unloading hopper, pipeline receiving station.
2. **Storage** — Stockyard (coal), main storage tank (oil), bullet vessels (gas).
3. **Pre-treatment** — Magnetic separator, metal detector, tramp iron removal, primary crusher.
4. **Conveying / Transfer** — Belt conveyors, chain conveyors, slurry pumps, screw conveyors, pneumatic conveyors.
5. **Preparation** — Pulverizers (bowl mill, ball mill, hammer mill, attrition mill), oil heaters, gas pressure regulators.
6. **Storage of prepared fuel** — Bunker (pulverized coal), day tank (oil).
7. **Metering & Delivery to Burner** — Coal feeders, oil flow meters, gas flow meters, control valves.

## 2.2 Theory of Coal Pulverization

Pulverization is essential because the **specific surface area** of fuel is directly proportional to combustion efficiency. The mass of coal that can burn per unit time is:

$$
\dot{m}_{burn} \;\propto\; A_s \, e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

where $A_s$ is the specific surface area, $E_a$ is the activation energy, and $T$ is the combustion temperature.

**Why pulverize to 200 mesh (74 µm)?**
- A 1 cm³ cube has surface area **6 cm²**.
- The same volume divided into 74 µm cubes gives **~81,000 cubes** with total surface area **~4,500 cm²** — a **750× increase** in surface.
- This is why pulverized-fuel (PF) boilers can sustain **25–50 kg/s** of coal flow in a single burner.

## 2.3 Theory of Oil Heating and Atomization

Heavy fuel oil (HFO) has high viscosity (up to **380 cSt at 50 °C**), which prevents proper atomization. The viscosity-temperature relationship is governed by:

$$
\ln \mu = A + \frac{B}{T}
$$

where $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity (cP or Pa·s), $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin, and $A, B$ are empirical fuel-specific constants.

**Rule-of-thumb (exam-favorite):** HFO must be heated so that its viscosity at the burner tip is **~20 cSt** for pressure atomization and **~35 cSt** for steam-assisted atomization.

## 2.4 Fuel Flow Theory — Orifice, Venturi, and Coriolis

The **mass flow equation** for the three principal flow meters used in fuel systems is summarized below.

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Meter Type | Governing Equation | Measurand | Typical Uncertainty | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Orifice plate | $\dot{m} = C_d A \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Mass flow | ±1 – 2 % FS | Oil, gas |
| Venturi | $\dot{m} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho (P_1 - P_2)}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | Mass flow | ±0.5 – 1 % FS | Steam, gas |
| Coriolis | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\tau}{2 \omega L} \cdot (K_s)$ | Mass flow + density | ±0.1 % | Custody transfer oil |
| Turbine | $f = K \cdot Q$ | Volumetric flow | ±0.5 % | Clean gas |
| Ultrasonic (Doppler) | $v = \dfrac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Velocity | ±1 – 3 % | Coal slurry, ash slurry |
| Gravimetric feeder | $W = K \int_0^t \dot{m}\, dt$ | Mass over time | ±0.5 % | Pulverized coal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no `\|` in tables?** KTU-PREMIER protocol uses `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown table corruption. Whenever you see `\vert x \vert` in a table, it represents $\vert x \vert$ in rendered LaTeX.

## 2.5 Storage Capacity — Engineering Formulas

For a **cylindrical fuel tank**:

$$
V = \pi r^2 h + \dfrac{2}{3} \pi r^3
$$

(where the second term accounts for spherical end-caps if used)

For a **coal bunker** (truncated conical hopper):

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi h}{3} \left( R^2 + Rr + r^2 \right)
$$

The **stockyard capacity** is computed as:

$$
M_{stock} = L \cdot W \cdot H \cdot \rho_{bulk} \cdot k_{stow}
$$

where $\rho_{bulk}$ is the bulk density of coal (~**800 kg/m³**) and $k_{stow}$ is the stowing factor (~**0.85**).

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Fuel Systems in the Real World

- **Custody transfer accuracy** between mine and power plant → 0.1 % of daily throughput = **₹1–2 crore/year** for a 500 MW plant.
- **Calorific value monitoring** enables optimization of **air-fuel ratio** and reduces **NOx** by up to **20 %**.
- **Real-time coal flow measurement** allows **mill load balancing**, preventing mill trips and saving **~1–2 %** in auxiliary power.
