# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Orifice Plate Mass Flow Equation

We derive the canonical KTU exam formula for an orifice plate fuel flow meter.

**Step 1 — Mechanical Energy Balance (Bernoulli between upstream point 1 and throat point 2):**

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_1^2}{2} = \dfrac{P_2}{\rho} + \dfrac{v_2^2}{2}
$$

Rearranging,

$$
v_2^2 - v_1^2 = \dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}
$$

**Step 2 — Continuity Equation:**

$$
A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v_1 = v_2 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting into the Bernoulli result:**

$$
v_2^2 \left( 1 - \left(\dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 \right) = \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}
$$

Solving for $v_2$,

$$
v_2 = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by area and discharge coefficient:**

The ideal flow rate is multiplied by a discharge coefficient $C_d$ to account for vena contracta and viscous losses:

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \dfrac{C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

This is the **final KTU-grade orifice mass flow equation**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners love this derivation because it tests **three** outcomes simultaneously: Bernoulli, continuity, and the role of $C_d$.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem — Coal Bunker Sizing

A 210 MW thermal power plant has a per-hour coal consumption of 110 tonnes. The bunker capacity must be designed to hold **8 hours of boiler load at MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating)**. Bulk density of coal is 800 kg/m³ and stowing factor is 0.85. The bunker is a cylinder of diameter 6 m with a conical bottom of 4 m height ending in a 1.2 m radius outlet. Compute the **required height of the cylindrical section**.

**Step 1 — Total coal mass needed:**

$$
M = 110 \times 8 = 880 \; \text{tonnes} = 8.8 \times 10^5 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Account for stowing factor:**

$$
M_{stored} = \dfrac{M}{k_{stow}} = \dfrac{8.8 \times 10^5}{0.85} = 1.035 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent coal volume:**

$$
V_{total} = \dfrac{M_{stored}}{\rho_{bulk}} = \dfrac{1.035 \times 10^6}{800} = 1294 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 4 — Volume of conical hopper section:**

Using the truncated cone formula with $R = 6$ m, $r = 1.2$ m, $h = 4$ m:

$$
V_{cone} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 4}{3} (6^2 + 6 \cdot 1.2 + 1.2^2)
$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:

$$
6^2 = 36, \quad 6 \cdot 1.2 = 7.2, \quad 1.2^2 = 1.44
$$

$$
36 + 7.2 + 1.44 = 44.64
$$

$$
V_{cone} = \dfrac{4 \pi \cdot 44.64}{3} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 44.64}{3} = 186.97 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Required cylindrical section volume:**

$$
V_{cyl} = V_{total} - V_{cone} = 1294 - 187 = 1107 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 6 — Cylinder height from $V = \pi r^2 h$:**

$$
h = \dfrac{V_{cyl}}{\pi r^2} = \dfrac{1107}{\pi \cdot 36} = \dfrac{1107}{113.1} = 9.79 \; \text{m}
$$

**Result:** The cylindrical section of the bunker must be **approximately 9.8 m tall**, giving a total bunker height of **~13.8 m**.

> [!TIP]
> KTU pro-tip: Always state the **design assumption** explicitly — e.g., *"8 hours storage at MCR including stowing factor"*. Examiners reward explicit assumptions with full marks.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem — Orifice Sizing for Furnace Oil

An orifice plate is used to measure furnace oil flow of 4 m³/hr. The orifice diameter is 30 mm in a 100 mm pipe. Density of oil is 950 kg/m³. If a manometer reads 200 mm of mercury ($\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600$ kg/m³), determine the **discharge coefficient** $C_d$.

**Step 1 — Convert differential pressure:**

$$
\Delta P = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h = 13600 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.2 = 26{,}683 \; \text{Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the diameters ratio $\beta$:**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} = \dfrac{30}{100} = 0.3, \quad \beta^4 = 0.3^4 = 0.0081
$$

**Step 3 — Throat area:**

$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.03)^2}{4} = 7.07 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass flow from ideal equation:**

$$
\dot{m}_{ideal} = \dfrac{A_2 \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
\sqrt{2 \cdot 950 \cdot 26683} = \sqrt{5.07 \times 10^7} = 7120
$$

Denominator:

$$
\sqrt{1 - 0.0081} = \sqrt{0.9919} = 0.9959
$$

Therefore:

$$
\dot{m}_{ideal} = \dfrac{7.07 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 7120}{0.9959} = 5.055 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Actual mass flow from the volumetric data:**

$$
\dot{m}_{actual} = 4 \, \dfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{hr}} \cdot 950 \, \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{3600} = 1.056 \; \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 6 — Discharge coefficient:**

$$
C_d = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{actual}}{\dot{m}_{ideal}} = \dfrac{1.056}{5.055} = 0.209
$$

**Sanity check:** $C_d$ is normally in the range **0.6 – 0.65** for a clean orifice — our answer of **0.21** indicates the manometer reading is unrealistically small for this flow, or that the orifice is **grossly undersized** (suggesting the manometer would actually read **~3 m of Hg** at this flow).

This kind of "result-must-pass-sanity-check" reasoning is exactly what KTU examiners reward as **engineering judgement**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the various types of fuel used in power plants. Discuss the advantages of pulverized coal firing over stoker firing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Solid fuels — coal, lignite, peat, biomass, bagasse.
2. Liquid fuels — furnace oil (HFO), light diesel oil (LDO), low sulphur heavy stock (LSHS).
3. Gaseous fuels — natural gas, LPG, biogas, blast furnace gas.

**Advantages of pulverized coal (PC) firing over stoker firing (2 marks):**

- **Faster combustion rate** due to enormous increase in surface area (~750×), so PC boilers can accept **larger coal particles and lower grade coal**.
- **Higher capacity** — PC boilers are built up to **1000 MW unit** while stokers are limited to **~10 MW**.
- **Better load-following** — PC mills can be cut in/out as load changes; stokers have poor turn-down ratio.
- **Lower excess air** (~15 % for PC vs 30 – 50 % for stokers), improving **boiler efficiency by 2 – 4 %**.
- **Cleaner combustion** — complete burnout reduces CO, smoke, and unburnt carbon in ash.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the role of a magnetic separator in a coal handling plant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **magnetic separator** is installed **after the wagon tippler and before the primary crusher** in a coal handling plant. It uses either **permanent magnets (rare-earth NdFeB)** or **electromagnets** to remove **ferrous tramp iron** (broken belt fasteners, hammers, nuts, bolts, wire pieces) that may have entered the coal supply chain.

- **Prevents crusher damage** — tramp iron entering the crusher can break teeth, hammers, and rolls, causing costly downtime.
- **Prevents conveyor belt damage** — sharp ferrous pieces can rip the belt cover.
- **Prevents pulverizer fires** — sparks from impact of metal on metal in the bowl mill can ignite coal dust.

Thus, the magnetic separator is a **critical protective device** and is treated as a **Class-A safety interlock** in modern coal handling plant instrumentation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **coal handling plant** of a thermal power station. List the **major instrumentation** at each stage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **mass flow equation for an orifice meter** and explain why a **differential pressure transmitter (DPT)** is preferred over a single pressure gauge. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Coal Handling Plant

The coal handling plant (CHP) executes seven sequential operations:

**Stage 1 — Unloading:** Coal is received via railway wagons, trucks, or ships. **Wagon tipplers** rotate the wagon through 160° to dump coal into a receiving hopper. Instrumentation: **level switches (rotary paddle)** in the hopper, **limit switches** on tippler, **load cell** on wagon weight.

**Stage 2 — Primary Crushing & Magnetic Separation:** Jaw or gyratory crushers reduce size to **100 – 150 mm**. Magnetic separators (overhead self-cleaning type) are installed. Instrumentation: **vibration sensors on crusher bearings**, **motor current monitor**, **magnetic separator temperature sensor**.

**Stage 3 — Stockpile Storage:** Coal is stacked in stockyard via **stacker** and reclaimed via **reclaim hoppers** and **scraper reclaimers**. Instrumentation: **radar / ultrasonic level sensors**, **coal inventory management software (weight integration)**.

**Stage 4 — Secondary Crushing:** A secondary crusher or **vibrating screen** reduces coal to **20 – 25 mm** before bunkers.

**Stage 5 — Pulverization:** Bowl mills / ball mills pulverize coal to **200 mesh (74 µm)**. Instrumentation: **mill differential pressure (DP across classifier)**, **mill motor current**, **mill outlet temperature**, **mill vibration probes**, **primary air flow**.

**Stage 6 — Coal Feeding to Burner:** Gravimetric feeders meter pulverized coal. Instrumentation: **weightometer (load cell on feeder)**, **belt speed sensor (tachogenerator)**, **coal flow transmitter (4–20 mA)**.

**Stage 7 — Ash Removal:** Bottom ash to ash pond; fly ash to ESP / silo. Instrumentation: **ash level sensors**, **ash slurry density meters**, **bucket elevator load cells**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — Orifice Mass Flow Equation

See the full derivation in **Section 3.1** above. The key result is:

$$
\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}}
$$

**Why DPT is preferred (3 marks):**

1. **Noise immunity** — $\dot{m} \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$, so a small change in $P_1$ or $P_2$ alone (caused by pump pulsation, header fluctuation) would corrupt a single-gauge reading. DPT measures $\Delta P$ directly, canceling **common-mode noise**.
2. **Higher accuracy** — A DPT has accuracy of **±0.075 % of span** versus ±1 % for a single gauge.
3. **Built-in square root extractor** — modern DPTs (e.g., Rosemount 3051CD) linearize the square root internally, providing a **4–20 mA linear flow signal**.
4. **HART / Fieldbus capability** — enables remote calibration and diagnostics, satisfying modern DCS-based fuel control requirements.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **furnace oil supply system** of a thermal power plant with a block diagram. Discuss the **function of each instrument** in the system. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a sketch, explain the **working of a gravimetric coal feeder**. Derive the **expression for mass flow rate** in terms of belt load and belt speed. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a) — Furnace Oil Supply System

The HFO supply system delivers atomized, viscosity-corrected oil to the boiler burner. A typical sequence is:

1. **Main storage tank (500 – 2000 kL capacity)** — Filled by tankers, equipped with **level transmitters (radar type)**, **temperature sensors**, and **high-high level alarms**.
2. **Transfer pumps (2 × 100 % duty, one running + one standby)** — Centrifugal pumps taking suction from the main tank. Instrumentation: **suction pressure gauge**, **discharge pressure transmitter**, **pump motor current**, **vibration sensor**.
3. **Duplex strainer** — Two basket-type strainers in parallel; one online, one standby. **Differential pressure switch** across the strainer indicates clogging.
4. **Oil heater (steam-coil or electric)** — Heats oil from ambient to **90 – 110 °C**. **Temperature controller (TC)** with **RTD/thermocouple** in a closed loop operates the steam control valve.
5. **Daily service tank (50 – 100 kL)** — Buffers flow and provides suction head. **Level switch (LSH, LSL)**, **temperature transmitter**.
6. **Boiler feed pump (gear pump / screw pump)** — Generates **40 – 60 bar** pressure for atomization.
7. **Viscosity control loop** — **Capillary viscometer** or **falling-body viscometer** measures viscosity; controller modulates steam to heater to maintain **20 cSt at burner tip**.
8. **Mass flow meter (Coriolis preferred for custody transfer accuracy)** — Output fed to **flow controller (FC)** which positions the **flow control valve (FCV)**.
9. **Atomizing medium** — For oil firing, **steam-assisted atomizer** is common. The steam-to-oil ratio is maintained at **0.4 – 0.6 kg steam / kg oil**.

**Instruments Summary Table:**

| Tag | Instrument | Function |
|---|---|---|
| LIT | Level Indicator Transmitter | Tank inventory monitoring |
| TT | Temperature Transmitter | Heater outlet control |
| FT | Flow Transmitter | Oil mass flow to burner |
| VT | Viscosity Transmitter | Heater temperature control |
| PDT | Differential Pressure Transmitter | Strainer clogging, pump head |
| FCV | Flow Control Valve | Modulating oil flow to burner |
| SDV | Shut Down Valve | Emergency trip valve |

#### Model Solution for (b) — Gravimetric Coal Feeder

A gravimetric feeder consists of a **short conveyor belt** of length $L$ with **load cells (weighing idlers)** at the midpoint and a **speed sensor (tachogenerator / encoder)** at the drive pulley.

**Derivation of mass flow rate:**

Let:
- $W$ = total mass on the weighing idler (kg)
- $L$ = length of the weighing idler section (m)
- $v$ = belt speed (m/s)

**Step 1 — Mass per unit length of belt:**

$$
w = \dfrac{W}{L} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Mass passing any cross-section per second** (i.e., mass flow rate):

$$
\dot{m} = w \cdot v = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{L} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Total mass delivered in time $t$:**

$$
M = \dot{m} \cdot t = \dfrac{W \cdot v \cdot t}{L}
$$

**Step 4 — For gravimetric control loop:** The load cell gives $W$, the speed sensor gives $v$, and the controller computes $\dot{m}$ continuously. The controller output is fed to the **variable-speed drive** of the belt motor to maintain the setpoint mass flow demanded by the **boiler master controller (BMC)**.

**Why gravimetric over volumetric feeders?**

- Volumetric feeders (screw-type) are affected by **coal bulk density variations** (which can change by ±15 % with moisture).
- Gravimetric feeders compensate for these variations and hold mass flow accuracy within **±0.5 %**, making them the **industry standard for utility boilers** above 50 MW capacity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "pulverizer" with "crusher"** — crusher is for **10–100 mm**, pulverizer is for **<0.1 mm** (200 mesh). Mixing these loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong $C_d$ formula for orifice** — writing $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gH}$ (water) instead of $\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_d A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{2\rho\Delta P}$ (gas/fluid). Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet** — always write **[kg/s, kg/m³, Pa, m, m²]**. Half-mark deduction per missing unit.
> 4. **Not labeling Mermaid / block diagrams** — KTU deducts up to **2 marks** for an unlabeled block diagram in a 7-mark question.
> 5. **Missing the stowing factor** in coal bunker sizing — losing 1 mark.
> 6. **Not mentioning safety interlocks (SDV, flame scanner, BMS)** in fuel system descriptions — 1–2 mark loss for "safety blind spot".
> 7. **Mixing up HFO and LDO** — HFO = Heavy Fuel Oil, LDO = Light Diesel Oil. They have different viscosities (380 cSt vs 9 cSt) and different heating requirements.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Coal handling** = Unload → Stockyard → Crusher → Conveyors → Bunker → Pulverizer → Feeder → Burner.
- [ ] **Oil handling** = Main tank → Pump → Strainer → Heater → Day tank → Pump → Viscosity control → Atomization.
- [ ] **Gas handling** = Main → SDV → Filter → Regulator → SDV → Vent → BGCV → Burner (Triple block + bleed).
- [ ] **Pulverizer types**: Bowl mill (BHEL XRP-803), Ball mill, Hammer mill, Attrition mill — know which is for which boiler.
- [ ] **Key orifices**: $D = 30$–$50$ mm in 100–200 mm pipe, $\beta \approx 0.5$–$0.7$ for best accuracy.
- [ ] **Gravimetric feeder equation**: $\dot{m} = (W/L) \cdot v$, accuracy ±0.5 %.
- [ ] **Coriolis meter** = preferred for **custody transfer** because it measures both mass and density.
- [ ] **HFO heating**: target viscosity 20 cSt at burner tip; heating temperature ~90 – 110 °C.
- [ ] **Coal bunker** = 6 – 8 hours of MCR storage, stowing factor 0.85, bulk density ~800 kg/m³.
- [ ] **Magnetic separator** = mandatory after wagon tippler, **Class-A interlock**.
- [ ] **BMS (Burner Management System)** must be **SIL-2 / SIL-3 certified** for fuel safety per **IEC 61508**.
- [ ] **Key ratios to remember**: Steam-to-oil atomization ratio = **0.4 – 0.6 kg/kg**; Air-to-fuel ratio for coal = **6 – 8 kg/kg** stoichiometric.
- [ ] **Pneumatic conveying velocity** for pulverized coal = **18 – 25 m/s** (above saltation, below pipe erosion).
- [ ] **ESP (Electrostatic Precipitator)** efficiency target = **>99.9 %** for fly ash collection.
- [ ] **Specific fuel consumption** for 1 kWh = ~0.5 kg coal equivalent (Indian grid average).
- [ ] **Standard conversion**: 1 TOE = 1.46 TCE = 7.4 BOE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treatment of Flue Gases
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Treatment of Flue Gases

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flue Gas Treatment (FGT)**, also termed **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)** in its narrower sense, is the ensemble of **post-combustion pollution control engineering processes** applied to the exhaust stream of fossil-fuel-fired boilers in thermal power plants. The objective is to **reduce the concentration of regulated atmospheric emissions** — primarily **Sulphur Oxides (SOx)**, **Nitrogen Oxides (NOx)**, **Particulate Matter (PM)**, **Heavy Metals (Hg, As, Se)**, and **Greenhouse Gases (CO₂, N₂O)** — to levels compliant with the statutory norms prescribed by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC), Government of India**, and the international **WHO (World Health Organization) / USEPA** ambient air quality standards.

The flue gas exiting the **Economizer** and **Air-Pre-Heater (APH)** typically has a temperature of **120 °C to 180 °C** and contains:

- **SO₂**: 400 – 2500 ppm (depending on sulphur content of coal, 0.3 % to 1.5 % by mass)
- **NOx** (NO + NO₂): 200 – 800 ppm
- **Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅ + PM₁₀)**: 5 000 – 20 000 mg/Nm³ (uncontrolled)
- **CO₂**: 10 – 15 % by volume
- **Hg (Elemental + Oxidized)**: 1 – 20 µg/Nm³
- **HCl, HF**: trace halogen acids

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Patient on Life Support"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a thermal power plant boiler as a human body burning food for energy.** Just as the body produces metabolic waste (CO₂, urea) that must be filtered by the kidneys and exhaled by the lungs, the boiler produces combustion waste (SO₂, NOx, ash, CO₂) that must be "filtered" before being released to the atmosphere.
>
> - **SO₂** is the "toxic urea" → removed by a "kidney" (the **Scrubber / FGD system**)
> - **NOx** is the "excess oxidants" → neutralized by a "liver" (the **DeNOx / SCR system**)
> - **Particulates (fly ash)** are the "solid debris" → captured by a "sieve" (the **ESP / Bag Filter**)
> - **CO₂** is the unavoidable "exhaled breath" → captured (and buried) by a "gas mask" (the **CCUS system**)

Without this "renal/lung" treatment, the plant would be poisoning its own surroundings — just like untreated metabolic waste causes uremia in the body.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Modern Flue Gas Treatment

| Pillar | Target Pollutant | Primary Technology | Indian Regulatory Limit (New Plants, 2015+) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Desulfurization** | SO₂ | Wet Limestone FGD, Dry CFB-FGD | $\le 100\ \text{mg/Nm}^3$ |
| **Denitrification (DeNOx)** | NO + NO₂ | Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) | $\le 100\ \text{mg/Nm}^3$ |
| **Particulate Control** | Fly Ash (PM) | ESP, Hybrid ESP, Baghouse | $\le 30\ \text{mg/Nm}^3$ |

The standard KTU 2024 scheme treats each pillar as a sub-topic, and students must be able to draw the **Process & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** for each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Modern Flue Gas Train

The order in which the flue gas treatment units are arranged along the gas path is **not arbitrary** — it is dictated by **gas temperature windows** required for optimum operation of each subsystem:

$$
\boxed{
T_{\text{APH exit}} \ (\approx 130^\circ\text{C})
\ \rightarrow\ \text{SCR (DeNOx)}
\ \rightarrow\ \text{Air Pre-Heater}
\ \rightarrow\ \text{ESP (Particulate Control)}
\ \rightarrow\ \text{FGD (Desulfurization)}
\ \rightarrow\ \text{Wet Stack}
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SCR comes BEFORE the APH (Hot-Side / High-Dust Arrangement)?**
> Because the SCR catalyst requires a **high operating temperature of 300 °C – 420 °C** to maintain its catalytic activity, and the APH cools the gas from ~400 °C to ~130 °C. Placing SCR after the APH would require **re-heating the gas using a costly Gas-Gas Heater (GGH)** — economically unviable.

## 2.2 Detailed Subsystem Analysis

### 2.2.1 Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)

#### A. Wet Limestone Forced-Oxidation (LSFO) FGD — The Industry Workhorse

**Operating Principle:** A counter-current spray tower where limestone slurry (CaCO₃ in water, ~10 – 15 % w/w) absorbs SO₂:

$$
\text{SO}_2 + \text{CaCO}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \ \longrightarrow\ \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}\ (\text{Gypsum}) + \text{CO}_2
$$

**Key Process Parameters:**

- **Liquid-to-Gas Ratio (L/G):** 8 – 15 L/m³
- **Slurry pH:** 5.0 – 6.0 (controlled by oxidation air and limestone feed)
- **Slurry Chloride concentration:** < 8 000 ppm (above this → corrosion)
- **Inlet SO₂:** up to 2 500 ppm → Outlet: < 50 ppm
- **Removal Efficiency:** **95 % – 98 %**

#### B. Dry / Semi-Dry FGD (CFB — Circulating Fluidized Bed)

Used in space-constrained plants and for lower-sulphur coals. Lime (Ca(OH)₂) is injected dry into a reactor where it reacts with SO₂ in the presence of humidified air:

$$
\text{SO}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \ \longrightarrow\ \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- Removal efficiency: **80 % – 90 %** (single stage)
- No wastewater → suitable for **drought-prone regions** like Rajasthan.

#### C. Regenerable FGD

Used for **high-sulphur fuels** where gypsum market is poor. Example: **Wellman-Lord Process** using Sodium Sulphite:

$$
\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \ \longrightarrow\ 2\,\text{NaHSO}_3
$$

The bisulphite is then thermally regenerated to release **concentrated SO₂ (used to make Sulphuric Acid — saleable byproduct)**.

### 2.2.2 Denitrification — NOx Reduction

#### A. Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)

Ammonia (NH₃) is injected into the flue gas over a **V₂O₅ / WO₃ / TiO₂** catalyst:

$$
4\,\text{NO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \ \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst, } 350^\circ\text{C}}\ 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

For NO₂ (the "fast SCR" reaction, kinetically favoured at lower temperatures):

$$
\text{NO} + \text{NO}_2 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \ \longrightarrow\ 2\,\text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

- **NH₃ Slip** (unreacted ammonia) must be kept **< 2 ppm** to prevent downstream deposition and visible plume.
- **Removal efficiency:** 80 % – 95 %.

#### B. Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction (SNCR)

Ammonia or urea is injected directly into the furnace at **850 °C – 1100 °C**. No catalyst, lower cost, but **only 30 % – 60 % efficiency**.

#### C. Low-NOx Burners (Primary Measure)

Reduces **thermal NOx** formation by **staging the combustion air** — fuel-rich zone first, then fuel-lean burnout zone. Reduces NOx at source by 30 % – 50 %.

### 2.2.3 Particulate Control — Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)

- **Charging:** Discharge electrodes at **– 40 kV to – 70 kV DC** corona-wire discharge.
- **Migration velocity:** Charged particles migrate to grounded collecting plates under Coulomb force.
- **Collection efficiency** is given by the **Deutsch-Anderson Equation** (derived in §3):

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{A \cdot w}{Q}\right)
$$

where $w$ = effective migration velocity, $A$ = total collecting plate area, $Q$ = gas volumetric flow.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (coal) | $\text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{11.6\,\text{C} + 34.8\,(\text{H} - \text{O}/8) + 4.35\,\text{S}}{1}$ | kg air / kg fuel; **C, H, O, S** in mass fractions |
| 2 | Stoichiometric O₂ required | $\text{O}_{2,\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{32\,\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{32\,\text{H}_2}{4} + \text{S} - \text{O}_2$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel |
| 3 | Excess Air Ratio | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}}$ | dimensionless; typical: **1.15 – 1.25** |
| 4 | SO₂ Mass Generated (from S) | $\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot w_S$ | kg/s; $w_S$ = mass fraction of S in fuel |
| 5 | FGD Removal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{FGD}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} - C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}} \times 100\,\%$ | $C$ = SO₂ concentration in mg/Nm³ |
| 6 | Deutsch-Anderson ESP Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{ESP}} = 1 - \exp(-A\,w\,/\,Q)$ | dimensionless; $w \in [0.05,\ 0.20]$ m/s |
| 7 | Ammonia Stoichiometry (SCR) | $\text{NSR} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{NH}_3,\text{in}}}{\dot{n}_{\text{NOx},\text{in}}}$ | Normalized Stoichiometric Ratio; **0.8 – 1.0** |
| 8 | Ca/S Molar Ratio (Wet FGD) | $\dfrac{\text{mol CaCO}_3}{\text{mol S}_{\text{in fuel}}} = 1.03 - 1.10$ | 3 % – 10 % stoichiometric excess |
| 9 | Lime Stoichiometry (Dry FGD) | $\text{Ca/S} = 1.3 - 1.8$ | higher ratio needed (lower reactivity) |
| 10 | Liquid-to-Gas Ratio (Wet FGD) | $\text{L/G} = 8 - 15$ | L of slurry per m³ of gas |
| 11 | Specific Collector Area (SCA) of ESP | $\text{SCA} = A/Q$ | m² / (m³/s); typical **40 – 80** |
| 12 | Particulate Penetration | $P_t = 1 - \eta = \exp(-A w / Q)$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Flue Gas Volume (STP, dry) | $V_g = \left(\dfrac{11\,\text{C}}{12} + \dfrac{9\,\text{H}}{2} + \dfrac{\text{N}_2}{28}\right) \times 22.4$ | Nm³ / kg fuel |
| 14 | NO₂ Formation (Zeldovich Thermal) | $\dfrac{d[\text{NO}]}{dt} = 6 \cdot 10^{16}\,T^{-0.5}\,\exp(-69\,150/T)\,[\text{O}][\text{N}_2]$ | mol/cm³·s |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **ESP and FGD together** enable Indian coal-fired plants (e.g., NTPC Vindhyachal, 4 760 MW) to operate at **SO₂ < 100 mg/Nm³ and PM < 30 mg/Nm³** — the strictest in Asia.
> - The **Gypsum byproduct** from wet FGD is of **pharma-grade purity (> 95 % CaSO₄·2H₂O)** and is sold to cement and plaster-of-paris industries, recovering ~30 % of FGD operating cost.
> - **CCUS (Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage)** is now mandatory for **all new thermal plants in the UK (since 2021)** and under the **EU ETS Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM)** from 2026.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deutsch-Anderson Equation for ESP Collection Efficiency

**Statement:** *The fractional collection efficiency of an electrostatic precipitator is given by* $\eta = 1 - \exp(-A w / Q)$.

**Starting Assumption:** Each gas streamline passes through the ESP with a constant **migration velocity** $w$ (m/s) toward the collecting plate. The concentration of particles $n(x)$ at distance $x$ from the inlet decreases as particles drift toward the plates.

**Step 1 — Define the collection rate:**
Consider a thin slab of gas of thickness $\mathrm{d}x$ moving through the ESP. The rate at which particles are removed is proportional to the local concentration:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}n}{\mathrm{d}t} = -\frac{n \cdot w}{L}
$$

where $L$ = characteristic half-spacing between plates.

**Step 2 — Convert to spatial derivative:**
Since the gas flows at velocity $u$ along the ESP length $\ell$:

$$
\mathrm{d}t = \frac{\mathrm{d}x}{u}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}n}{\mathrm{d}x} = -\frac{n \cdot w}{L \cdot u}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from inlet to outlet:**

$$
\int_{n_{\text{in}}}^{n_{\text{out}}} \frac{\mathrm{d}n}{n} = -\frac{w}{L\,u}\int_{0}^{\ell}\mathrm{d}x
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the integrals:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{n_{\text{out}}}{n_{\text{in}}}\right) = -\frac{w\,\ell}{L\,u}
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of collecting area and gas flow:**
Total plate area $A = b \cdot \ell$ (where $b$ = total plate width), and volumetric flow rate $Q = u \cdot b \cdot L$. Therefore:

$$
\frac{w\,\ell}{L\,u} = \frac{w \cdot A}{Q}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{n_{\text{out}}}{n_{\text{in}}}\right) = -\frac{w\,A}{Q}
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for collection efficiency:**

$$
\eta = 1 - \frac{n_{\text{out}}}{n_{\text{in}}} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{w\,A}{Q}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\ \eta_{\text{ESP}} = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{A\,w}{Q}\right)\ }
$$

**Numerical Validation — Worked Example (KTU-style):**
A 500 MW unit has an ESP with $A = 50\,000\ \text{m}^2$ of collecting area, gas flow $Q = 1\,000\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$, and effective migration velocity $w = 0.12\ \text{m/s}$. Compute the efficiency.

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{50\,000 \times 0.12}{1\,000}\right) = 1 - \exp(-6)
$$

$$
\exp(-6) = 0.00248
$$

$$
\eta = 1 - 0.00248 = 0.99752 \approx 99.75\,\%
$$

> The outlet concentration is therefore $\le 0.25\,\%$ of inlet → compliant with **PM $\le$ 30 mg/Nm³** for inlet loading up to **12 000 mg/Nm³**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for Coal Combustion

**Combustion Reactions:**

$$
\text{C} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{CO}_2
\qquad
\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}
\qquad
\text{S} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{SO}_2
$$

**Step 1 — Per kg of fuel:**

For carbon ($C$ kg):
$$
\text{O}_2\ \text{required} = \frac{32}{12}\,C = 2.667\,C\ \text{kg}
$$

For hydrogen ($H$ kg):
$$
\text{O}_2\ \text{required} = \frac{32}{4}\,H = 8.000\,H\ \text{kg}
$$

For sulphur ($S$ kg):
$$
\text{O}_2\ \text{required} = \frac{32}{32}\,S = 1.000\,S\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Net oxygen (subtract oxygen already in fuel):**

$$
\text{O}_{2,\text{net}} = \frac{8}{3}\,C + 8\,H + S - O_{\text{fuel}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mass of air (air contains 23.2 % O₂ by mass):**

$$
\text{Air}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{1}{0.232}\!\left(\frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O_{\text{fuel}}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\ \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} = 11.5\,C + 34.5\,H + 4.31\,S - 4.31\,O_{\text{fuel}}\ \ }
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Mass of SO₂ Emitted per kg of Coal

**Step 1 — Molar masses:** S = 32, SO₂ = 64.

**Step 2 — Mass ratio conversion:**
Each kg of sulphur in coal produces $\dfrac{64}{32} = 2$ kg of SO₂.

**Step 3 — General expression:**

$$
\boxed{\ \dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 2\,w_S \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}}\ \ }
$$

**Worked Example:** A 1 000 MW plant burns coal at **3.5 kg/kWh** = 3 500 kg/h (for a 1 000 MW unit this is actually 350 t/h — recalibrated for 500 MW). With $w_S = 0.7\,\%$ and thermal efficiency 38 %:
Coal flow $\dot{m}_f = 1.6\ \text{kg/kWh} \times 5 \times 10^5\ \text{kW} = 8 \times 10^5\ \text{kg/h}$ → 800 t/h.

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = 2 \times 0.007 \times 800 = 11.2\ \text{t/h} = 268.8\ \text{t/day}
$$

After FGD at 95 % efficiency:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2,\text{stack}} = 11.2 \times 0.05 = 0.56\ \text{t/h}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Calcium-to-Sulphur (Ca/S) Stoichiometry for Wet FGD

**Step 1 — Molar balance:** 1 mol S in fuel → 1 mol SO₂ → 1 mol CaSO₃ (intermediate) → 1 mol CaSO₄·2H₂O (gypsum).

**Step 2 — Theoretical Ca needed:** 1 mol CaCO₃ per mol S. Molar masses: CaCO₃ = 100, S = 32.

**Step 3 — Theoretical mass ratio:**

$$
\left(\frac{\text{CaCO}_3}{\text{S}}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{100}{32} = 3.125
$$

**Step 4 — Real-world ratio with 5 % excess:**

$$
\boxed{\ \frac{\text{Ca}}{\text{S}} = 1.05 \times \frac{100}{32} = 3.28\ \text{kg CaCO}_3\ /\ \text{kg S}\ }
$$

**Step 5 — Limestone consumption for a 500 MW plant:**

If $w_S = 0.7\,\%$, coal flow = 200 t/h:
$$
\text{Sulphur in coal} = 0.007 \times 200 = 1.4\ \text{t/h}
$$
$$
\text{Limestone} = 3.28 \times 1.4 = 4.59\ \text{t/h (pure)}
$$
With limestone purity = 90 %:
$$
\text{Raw limestone feed} = 4.59 / 0.9 = 5.1\ \text{t/h}
$$
$$
\text{Gypsum produced} = \frac{172}{32} \times 1.4 = 7.525\ \text{t/h}\ (\approx 180\ \text{t/day})
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Flue Gas Treatment Process Flow (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    boiler1[Coal Fired Boiler Furnace] --> aph1[Air Preheater APH - Hot Side 350C]
    aph1 --> scr1[SCR DeNOx Reactor with V2O5 WO3 TiO2 Catalyst]
    scr1 --> aph2[APH Cold Side 130C - Reheat by Gas Gas Heater GGH]
    aph2 --> esp1[Electrostatic Precipitator ESP - 4 Fields]
    esp1 --> idfan1[Induced Draft Fan IDF]
    idfan1 --> gghIn[GGH Inlet Side - Hot Clean Gas to FGD]
    gghIn --> fgdscrub[Wet Limestone FGD Spray Tower Absorber]
    fgdscrub --> gypsump[Gypsum Dewatering - Centrifuge to 10 percent Moisture]
    fgpsump2[Gyp Stockpile Byproduct]
    fgdscrub --> gghOut[GGH Outlet Side - Reheat Flue Gas to 80C]
    gghOut --> wetstack1[Wet Stack - Exit to Atmosphere at 80C]
    
    bo3[Limestone Crusher and Ball Mill] --> slurr1[Slurry Preparation Tank 15 percent w w]
    slurr1 --> fgdscrub
    ox3[Oxidation Air Blower] --> fgdscrub
    am1[Aqueous Ammonia Storage Tank 25 percent NH3] --> evap1[Ammonia Vaporizer]
    evap1 --> ainj[Ammonia Injection Grid AIG]
    ainj --> scr1
    pt1[Differential Pressure Transmitter] --> esp1
    pt2[Opacity Monitor PM Detector] --> wetstack1
    so2a[SO2 Analyser UV DOAS] --> wetstack1
    noxa[NOx Analyser Chemiluminescence] --> wetstack1
    oxya[O2 Analyser Paramagnetic] --> wetstack1
    dcs1[Plant DCS PLC] -.Control Signal.-> fgdscrub
    dcs1 -.Control Signal.-> scr1
    dcs1 -.Control Signal.-> esp1
    dcs1 -.Telemetry.-> cems1[CEMS Continuous Emissions Monitoring System]
    cems1 -.Reports to CPCB.-> regulator1[CPCB Regulatory Authority]
```

## 4.2 Subsystem-Level Block Diagram — Wet Limestone FGD

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inletgas1[Hot Flue Gas Inlet at 130C] --> quench1[Quench Zone - Cooling to 65C via Recirculated Scrubbing Liquor]
    quench1 --> absorbtower1[Absorber Tower - 3 Levels of Spray Nozzles]
    absorbtower1 --> mistel1[Mist Eliminator - 2 Stage Chevron Type]
    mistel1 --> outletgas1[Clean Saturated Gas Outlet to GGH]
    slurrpump1[Slurry Recirculation Pump Centrifugal 250 kW] --> absorbtower1
    limestone1[Limestone Slurry Feed Tank] --> slurrpump1
    oxblow1[Oxidation Air Blower] --> oxvessel1[Oxidation Tank at Base of Absorber]
    absorbtower1 --> oxvessel1
    oxvessel1 --> gypsumdewater1[Gypsum Dewatering Hydrocyclone plus Centrifuge]
    gypsumdewater1 --> gypsum1[Gypsum Pile Byproduct]
    phprobe1[Online pH Probe in Oxidation Tank] --> slurrpump1
    denprobe1[Density Probe in Slurry Tank] --> limestone1
    lvlprobe1[Level Transmitter in Absorber Sump] --> slurrpump1
```

## 4.3 SCR Reactor Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    aig1[Ammonia Injection Grid 7 Pipe Array] --> catalyst1[Catalyst Layer 1 - Honeycomb V2O5]
    catalyst1 --> catalyst2[Catalyst Layer 2 - Redundancy for Low Load Operation]
    catalyst2 --> so3layer1[SO3 Oxidation Layer - Limits SO3 Formation]
    so3layer1 --> aighotgas1[Hot Flue Gas 350 to 420C Exit to APH]
    nh3slip1[NH3 Slip Analyzer Tunable Diode Laser TDL] --> aig1
    nox1[NOx Analyzer at Outlet] --> aig1
    temp1[Temperature Probe at Inlet] --> aig1
    soot1[Soot Blower Steam Lance] --> catalyst1
```

## 4.4 ESP Internal Schematic (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inletairstack1[Untreated Flue Gas Inlet] --> distplate1[Gas Distribution Plates - Ensure Uniform Velocity]
    distplate1 --> chrgfield1[Charging Field - 60 kV Corona Discharge Wires]
    chrgfield1 --> collfield1[Collection Field - Grounded Plates 400 mm Spacing]
    collfield1 --> rappfield1[Rapping System - Impulse Hammers Every 5 Minutes]
    rappfield1 --> hoppr1[Hopper Bottom - 45 Degree Slope]
    hoppr1 --> ashsluic1[Ash Sluicing Conveyor]
    collfield1 --> outlet1[Clean Gas Outlet to IDF Fan]
    tr1[High Voltage Transformer Rectifier T R Set] --> chrgfield1
    cur1[Current Density Sensor] --> tr1
    volt1[Voltage Sensor] --> tr1
    opp1[Optical Opacity Monitor] --> outlet1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model]`
**Define the term "Flue Gas Desulfurization". With a neat sketch, describe the working of a Wet Limestone FGD system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD) is the process of removing **sulphur dioxide (SO₂)** from the flue gas exhaust of a fossil-fuel-fired boiler before it is discharged to the atmosphere through the stack.

In a **Wet Limestone FGD** system (the most widely deployed technology in Indian coal-fired power plants), the hot flue gas enters an **absorber / spray tower** where it comes into contact with a counter-current spray of finely ground **limestone slurry (CaCO₃ + H₂O)** at 10–15 % concentration. The slurry absorbs the SO₂ through the following overall reaction:

$$
\text{SO}_2 + \text{CaCO}_3 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{CO}_2
$$

The product **calcium sulphate dihydrate (gypsum)** is collected at the bottom of the absorber, dewatered by centrifuge, and sold as a commercial byproduct to the cement industry. The cleaned flue gas, now saturated with water vapour, passes through a **mist eliminator** and is reheated to ~80 °C in a **Gas-Gas Heater (GGH)** before discharge through the **wet stack**. Removal efficiency of **95 % – 98 %** is routinely achieved.

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model]`
**Compare SCR and SNCR methods of NOx reduction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) | SNCR (Selective Non-Catalytic Reduction) |
|---|---|---|
| **Reagent** | Ammonia (NH₃) or urea | Ammonia or urea |
| **Catalyst** | V₂O₅ / WO₃ / TiO₂ catalyst required | **No catalyst** |
| **Operating Temperature** | 300 °C – 420 °C | 850 °C – 1 100 °C (in furnace) |
| **Removal Efficiency** | 80 % – 95 % | 30 % – 60 % |
| **NH₃ Slip** | < 2 ppm | Higher (5 – 10 ppm) |
| **Capital Cost** | High (due to catalyst + reactor) | Low |
| **Operating Cost** | Moderate (catalyst replacement every 24 000 h) | Moderate (high reagent consumption) |
| **Placement** | After the furnace, before APH | Inside the furnace |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Describe with a neat block diagram the working of an Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP). Derive the Deutsch-Anderson equation for ESP efficiency.** `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Construction & Working (4 marks):**
An Electrostatic Precipitator consists of two sets of electrodes housed in a casing:
1. **Discharge electrodes (corona wires):** Thin tungsten wires stretched between frames, maintained at a **negative DC potential of 40 kV – 70 kV**. They ionize the surrounding gas, generating **negative ions and free electrons**.
2. **Collecting electrodes (plates):** Large grounded metal plates spaced 200–400 mm apart from the wires.

**Working Stages:**
1. **Ionization / Charging stage:** Electrons emitted by corona discharge attach to dust particles, giving them a **net negative charge**.
2. **Migration / Collection stage:** Charged particles are driven toward the grounded plates by the **Coulomb force**, where they accumulate as a layer.
3. **Rapping / Cleaning stage:** Mechanical hammers (rapper system) periodically strike the plates to dislodge the dust into hoppers below.

**Derivation of Deutsch-Anderson Equation (3 marks):**
*(Steps 1–7 as derived in §3.1 of this document.)*

Starting from the differential mass balance:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}n}{\mathrm{d}t} = -\frac{n \cdot w}{L}
$$

Converting to spatial form using $u\,\mathrm{d}t = \mathrm{d}x$:

$$
\frac{\mathrm{d}n}{n} = -\frac{w}{L\,u}\,\mathrm{d}x
$$

Integrating along the ESP length $\ell$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{n_{\text{out}}}{n_{\text{in}}}\right) = -\frac{w\,\ell}{L\,u}
$$

Substituting $A = b\ell$ and $Q = u\,b\,L$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{n_{\text{out}}}{n_{\text{in}}}\right) = -\frac{w\,A}{Q}
$$

Final result:

$$
\boxed{\ \eta = 1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{w\,A}{Q}\right)\ }
$$

---

**`(b)` A thermal power plant burns coal with sulphur content 1.2 % at a rate of 200 tonnes/hour. The plant uses a wet limestone FGD with 96 % removal efficiency and a Ca/S molar ratio of 1.05. Calculate:**
- `(i)` Mass of SO₂ produced per hour
- `(ii)` Mass of SO₂ emitted per hour after FGD
- `(iii)` Daily limestone requirement (purity 92 %)
- `(iv)` Daily gypsum produced `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Sulphur mass fraction in coal: $w_S = 1.2\,\% = 0.012$
- Coal flow rate: $\dot{m}_f = 200\ \text{t/h}$
- FGD removal efficiency: $\eta_{\text{FGD}} = 96\,\%$
- Ca/S molar ratio: 1.05
- Limestone purity: 92 %
- Molar mass of CaCO₃ = 100 kg/kmol; S = 32 kg/kmol; CaSO₄·2H₂O = 172 kg/kmol

**Step (i) — Mass of SO₂ produced:**
Sulphur flow = $0.012 \times 200 = 2.4\ \text{t/h}$

Each kg of S → 2 kg of SO₂ (since 32 → 64):

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2,\text{gen}} = 2 \times 2.4 = 4.8\ \text{t/h}
$$

**Step (ii) — SO₂ emitted after FGD:**
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2,\text{stack}} = 4.8 \times (1 - 0.96) = 4.8 \times 0.04 = 0.192\ \text{t/h}
$$

**Step (iii) — Daily limestone requirement:**
Sulphur daily = $2.4 \times 24 = 57.6\ \text{t/day}$
Pure CaCO₃ (stoichiometric) = $\dfrac{100}{32} \times 57.6 = 180\ \text{t/day}$
With 1.05 molar excess:

$$
\text{Pure CaCO}_3 = 180 \times 1.05 = 189\ \text{t/day}
$$

Accounting for 92 % purity:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{limestone,raw}} = \frac{189}{0.92} = 205.43\ \text{t/day}
$$

**Step (iv) — Daily gypsum produced:**
$$
\text{Gypsum} = \frac{172}{32} \times 57.6 \times 0.96 \ (\text{assuming 96 % conversion}) = 297.5\ \text{t/day}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** forget the factor of **2** when converting sulphur mass to SO₂ mass. (Many students write $\dot{m}_{\text{SO}_2} = \dot{m}_S$ — this loses 1 mark.)
> 2. The **Ca/S molar ratio of 1.05 means 5 % stoichiometric excess** — apply it AFTER the stoichiometric ratio, NOT before.
> 3. **Gypsum yield is reduced by the FGD efficiency** (96 % in this case). The other 4 % of S leaves as unreacted SO₂ and soluble sulphites in the wastewater.
> 4. Always express limestone on a **per-day basis** when the question asks "daily requirement" — partial credit is lost if you leave it in t/h.

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks)

**`(a)` With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR) system for NOx control. State the operating temperature window and discuss the significance of NH₃ slip.** `(7 marks)`

**Model Solution:**

**Description (4 marks):**
The SCR system is installed **between the economizer outlet and the air pre-heater (APH)** where the flue gas temperature is in the range of **300 °C – 420 °C** — the optimum window for the V₂O₅ / WO₃ / TiO₂ catalyst.

Aqueous ammonia (typically 25 % NH₃ in water) is vaporized and injected into the flue gas through an **Ammonia Injection Grid (AIG)** — a network of stainless-steel pipes with calibrated nozzles. The ammonia-gas mixture then passes through the **catalyst honeycomb modules**, where the following reactions occur:

$$
4\,\text{NO} + 4\,\text{NH}_3 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 4\,\text{N}_2 + 6\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

For NO₂ (the **fast SCR** reaction):

$$
\text{NO} + \text{NO}_2 + 2\,\text{NH}_3 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{N}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Operating Temperature Window (2 marks):**
- **Below 300 °C:** Catalyst activity drops sharply, ammonia salts (ammonium bisulphate, NH₄HSO₄) condense and **plug the catalyst pores** (catalyst poisoning).
- **Above 420 °C:** Catalyst undergoes **thermal sintering** → permanent loss of active surface area → irreversible deactivation.
- **Optimum:** 350 °C ± 20 °C.

**NH₃ Slip (1 mark):**
The "ammonia slip" is the unreacted ammonia that exits the SCR reactor into the downstream FGD. It must be kept **< 2 ppm** because:
1. Excess NH₃ reacts with SO₃ to form **ammonium bisulphate** — a sticky, corrosive deposit that fouls the APH and ESP.
2. Visible **blue/white plume** forms at the stack due to ammonia condensation.
3. Causes **fish kill** if deposited in nearby water bodies.

> Continuous NH₃ slip monitoring is done using a **Tunable Diode Laser (TDL) analyser** at the SCR outlet.

---

**`(b)` Define the term "Excess Air". A coal-fired boiler uses coal with the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 65 %, H = 5 %, S = 1.5 %, N = 1.5 %, O = 8 %, Ash = 19 %. Calculate:** `(7 marks)`
- `(i)` Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel
- `(ii)` Theoretical CO₂ % in dry flue gas
- `(iii)` If 20 % excess air is used, find the actual O₂ % in dry flue gas

**Model Solution:**

**Given (per kg of fuel):**
C = 0.65, H = 0.05, S = 0.015, O_fuel = 0.08

**Definition (1 mark):**
Excess air is the **air supplied beyond the stoichiometric requirement**, expressed as a percentage or fraction. It is necessary for **complete combustion** and to prevent the formation of CO, soot and unburnt hydrocarbons.

**Step (i) — Stoichiometric O₂:**
$$
\text{O}_{2,\text{stoich}} = \frac{8}{3}C + 8H + S - O_{\text{fuel}}
$$

$$
= \frac{8}{3}(0.65) + 8(0.05) + 0.015 - 0.08
$$

$$
= 1.7333 + 0.40 + 0.015 - 0.08 = 2.0683\ \text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}
$$

**Step (ii) — CO₂ % in dry flue gas:**
At stoichiometric condition (no excess air), the dry flue gas contains:

- CO₂ from carbon: $\dfrac{44}{12} \times 0.65 = 2.383\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$
- SO₂ from sulphur: $\dfrac{64}{32} \times 0.015 = 0.030\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$
- N₂ from air (3.76 mol N₂ per mol O₂): $\dfrac{77}{23}\times 2.0683 = 6.924\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$

Total dry flue gas mass = $2.383 + 0.030 + 6.924 = 9.337\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$

$$
\text{CO}_2\,\% \text{(by mass, dry)} = \frac{2.383}{9.337} \times 100 = 25.52\,\%
$$

$$
\text{CO}_2\,\% \text{(by volume)} = \frac{0.65/12}{0.65/12 + 0.015/32 + 6.924/28} \times 100 = 16.5\,\%
$$

**Step (iii) — O₂ % with 20 % excess air:**
O₂ supplied = $1.20 \times 2.0683 = 2.482\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$
O₂ unused = $0.20 \times 2.0683 = 0.4137\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$
Total N₂ = $6.924 \times 1.20 = 8.309\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$ (since extra air carries extra N₂)

Total dry flue gas = $2.383 + 0.030 + 0.4137 + 8.309 = 11.135\ \text{kg/kg fuel}$

$$
\text{O}_2\,\% \text{(by volume, dry)} = \frac{0.4137/32}{2.383/44 + 0.030/64 + 0.4137/32 + 8.309/28} \times 100
$$

$$
= \frac{0.01293}{0.05416 + 0.00047 + 0.01293 + 0.29675} \times 100 = \frac{0.01293}{0.36431} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\ \text{O}_2 = 3.55\,\% \text{(dry basis)}\ }
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always subtract** the oxygen already present in the fuel ($O_{\text{fuel}}$). Forgetting this is the **most common mistake** in coal combustion problems.
> 2. The molecular weight of air is **28.97 (≈ 29)**, NOT 28 (N₂ alone). Use **3.76** (or 79/21) for the molar N₂:O₂ ratio of air.
> 3. For "% in dry flue gas", the **water vapour (H₂O from hydrogen combustion) must be excluded** from the denominator. Neglecting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Excess air percentage and excess air ratio are different.** 20 % excess air corresponds to an excess air ratio $\varepsilon = 1.20$, not 0.20.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- [ ] **FGD** removes **SO₂**; **SCR** removes **NOx**; **ESP/Bag Filter** removes **Particulate Matter** — these are the **three pillars** of flue gas treatment.
- [ ] The order on the gas path: **SCR → APH → ESP → FGD → Stack**.
- [ ] **SCR** uses **V₂O₅/WO₃/TiO₂** catalyst at **300 °C – 420 °C**.
- [ ] **SNCR** has no catalyst but only **30 % – 60 %** efficiency (vs SCR's **80 – 95 %**).
- [ ] **Wet Limestone FGD** uses **CaCO₃** slurry, produces **gypsum** as byproduct (saleable).
- [ ] **Ca/S molar ratio** for wet FGD: **1.03 – 1.10**; for dry CFB: **1.3 – 1.8**.
- [ ] **Deutsch-Anderson equation** for ESP: $\eta = 1 - \exp(-Aw/Q)$.
- [ ] **NH₃ slip** in SCR must be **< 2 ppm** to avoid downstream fouling.
- [ ] **Mass of SO₂ produced = 2 × (mass of S in fuel)** (since 64/32 = 2).
- [ ] **Stoichiometric air formula** (per kg fuel): $11.5C + 34.5H + 4.31S - 4.31O_{\text{fuel}}$.
- [ ] **Excess air** of 15 – 25 % is typical in coal-fired boilers.
- [ ] **Wet stack** operates at ~80 °C after GGH reheat; **Dry stack** at ~130 °C (no FGD).
- [ ] **CEMS** (Continuous Emission Monitoring System) reports to **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** every 15 minutes.
- [ ] **CFB-FGD (Circulating Fluidized Bed)** is preferred for **low-sulphur Indian coal** and where **water is scarce**.
- [ ] **Gypsum purity** in wet FGD is > 95 % — saleable to **cement, POP, and pharma** industries.
- [ ] **Key analyser types** for CEMS:
  - **SO₂** → UV DOAS analyser
  - **NOx** → Chemiluminescence analyser
  - **O₂** → Paramagnetic / Zirconia cell
  - **PM (Opacity)** → Transmissometer (in-stack or extractive)
  - **Hg** → Cold-vapour Atomic Absorption (CVAAS)
  - **Flow** → Thermal mass flow meter or Pitot traverse

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Turbine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Steam Turbine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Steam Turbine** is a prime mover that converts the thermal energy of high-pressure, high-temperature superheated steam into mechanical work (rotational kinetic energy) by means of the momentum and pressure changes of the steam flowing through a series of stationary nozzles and rotating blades mounted on a shaft. The converted mechanical work drives the electrical generator coupled to the turbine rotor in a thermal power plant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEICT523 / Module 2):**
> A steam turbine, in the context of power plant instrumentation, is treated as a **process object** whose **speed, power output, vibration, axial displacement, bearing temperature, lube oil pressure, and condenser vacuum** must be continuously measured, controlled, and protected using appropriate transducers, governors, and Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The "Windmill on Steroids" Model**
>
> Imagine a **windmill** that turns because wind (fluid) strikes its blades. Now imagine you replace the wind with a much more powerful, high-velocity, high-pressure jet of steam fired precisely through shaped nozzles. The blades absorb the kinetic energy, spin a shaft, and that shaft drives a generator producing electricity.
>
> - **Nozzle** = the "hose" that accelerates and directs the steam (converts pressure energy → kinetic energy).
> - **Moving Blades** = the "fan blades" that catch the steam jet and convert its kinetic energy → mechanical rotation.
> - **Governor** = the "cruise control" of the turbine, holding the shaft speed constant despite changing electrical load.
> - **Condenser** = the "exhaust pipe" that creates a vacuum at the turbine exit, sucking more steam through, just like a vacuum cleaner pulls air.

The steam essentially does a **"U-turn"** through the curved blade passages, transferring almost all of its momentum (and in reaction turbines, pressure thrust) to the rotor.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following are the **standard metrics** monitored in KTU-aligned power plant instrumentation:

- **Shaft Speed (N)**: 3000 RPM (50 Hz system) or 3600 RPM (60 Hz system) — measured using magnetic pickup / proximity probes.
- **Inlet Steam Pressure**: typically **160 – 250 bar** (supercritical units up to 600 bar).
- **Inlet Steam Temperature**: typically **535 °C – 600 °C** (modern ultrasupercritical).
- **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)**: typically **2.0 – 2.5 kg/kWh** for a modern unit.
- **Condenser Vacuum**: typically **0.05 – 0.10 bar absolute (a)** — corresponding to **−0.90 to −0.95 bar gauge**.
- **Stage Pressure Ratio**: 1.2 – 1.5 (typical for 50% reaction blades).
- **Blade Speed (u)**: 150 – 400 m/s depending on stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these constants matter for instrumentation:** Every one of the above parameters requires at least one dedicated sensor (pressure transmitter, RTD/Thermocouple, eddy-current probe, vacuum transducer). The accuracy and response time of these sensors directly determine the plant's MW output, heat rate, and unit trip reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Steam Turbines

### 2.1.1 Impulse Turbine
- Steam expands **only in the stationary nozzles**. Pressure remains **constant** across the moving blades.
- The entire pressure energy of steam is converted to **kinetic energy** in the nozzle.
- High steam velocity is the only driver of blade rotation.

### 2.1.2 Reaction Turbine
- Steam expands **progressively in both the stationary (fixed) and moving blades**.
- Approximately **50 %** of the pressure drop occurs in the moving blades (Parsons design).
- Blade passages are shaped as **convergent nozzles**.
- The relative velocity at exit is greater than at inlet — the "reaction" creates a reactive thrust on the blades.

### 2.1.3 Compounded Impulse Turbines
- **Pressure Compounding (Rateau):** Multiple pressure stages, each with its own nozzle + blade pair. Steam pressure drops step-wise.
- **Velocity Compounding (Curtis):** One nozzle + multiple rows of moving blades separated by fixed (guide) blades. Velocity drops step-wise.

## 2.2 Governing of Steam Turbines — The "Why & How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of Governing:** The turbine shaft speed must remain **constant** at the synchronous value (3000 RPM for 50 Hz) irrespective of the **electrical load** on the alternator. As load increases, steam flow must increase; as load decreases, steam flow must decrease.

### 2.2.1 Throttle Governing (Lanz Method)
- A single control valve (throttle valve) at the turbine inlet regulates the **steam pressure** delivered to the first stage.
- Simple, robust, but suffers from **throttling losses** at partial loads.
- **How it works:** Governor flyweights sense speed change → moves pilot valve → moves hydraulic power piston → repositions throttle valve → changes steam mass flow.

### 2.2.2 Nozzle Governing (Meyer's Method)
- A series of **2 to 6 control valves** feed steam to a **group of nozzles** arranged circumferentially.
- Valves are opened **progressively (sequentially)** so that as each valve opens, more nozzles come into action.
- **Advantage:** Better partial-load efficiency because the nozzles in operation always run at **full (design) pressure** — only the **number of active nozzles** changes.
- **Instrumentation implication:** Each valve position is monitored by an LVDT; valve sequencing is governed by a hydraulic or electro-hydraulic (EHC) servo system.

### 2.2.3 Bypass Governing
- Steam is admitted at **intermediate stages** during start-up or part-load, bypassing the HP section.
- Used mainly during **low-load operation** to avoid HP cylinder blade windage heating.

## 2.3 Critical Instrumentation Parameters for Steam Turbine

The following table summarises the **prime measurements** that every KTU-2024 student must be able to recall. These are the **physical quantities the instruments sense** on a steam turbine.

| S.No | Measured Variable | Sensor / Transducer | Location | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Shaft Speed (RPM) | Magnetic Pickup / Proximity Probe | Thrust bearing collar | 0 – 3600 RPM |
| 2 | Vibration (Displacement) | Eddy-Current Probe (Seismic) | Bearing housings (X & Y axes) | 0 – 200 µm pk-pk |
| 3 | Axial Thrust Position | Eddy-Current Probe (non-contact) | Thrust bearing | −0.5 to +0.5 mm |
| 4 | Bearing Metal Temperature | RTD (Pt-100) | Babbitt metal of each bearing | 0 – 120 °C |
| 5 | Bearing Drain Oil Temp | RTD / Thermocouple | Bearing oil drain line | 0 – 110 °C |
| 6 | Lube Oil Pressure | Bourdon / Strain-Gauge Transmitter | Lube oil manifold | 0 – 5 bar |
| 7 | HP/LP Cylinder Differential Expansion | LVDT | Cylinder-to-rotor | 0 – 25 mm |
| 8 | Cylinder-to-Cylinder Expansion | LVDT | HP-LP crossover | 0 – 50 mm |
| 9 | Main Steam Pressure | Strain-Gauge / Piezo Transmitter | Main steam header | 0 – 300 bar |
| 10 | Main Steam Temperature | Type-K Thermocouple | Main steam line | 0 – 600 °C |
| 11 | Condenser Vacuum | Capacitance / Piezo Vacuum Transmitter | Condenser shell | 0 – 1 bar abs |
| 12 | Governor Valve Position | LVDT / Rotary Encoder | Each control valve stem | 0 – 100 % |
| 13 | Steam Flow (Feedwater + Main) | Vortex / Venturi / Annubar | Main steam line | 0 – 2000 TPH |
| 14 | Rotor Eccentricity (Slow Roll) | Eddy-Current Probe | Journal | 0 – 200 µm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question (Module 2):**
> *"List any eight measurements performed on a steam turbine rotor and state the type of sensor used for each."*
> → The above 14 rows are the **answer bank** — pick 8 and elaborate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table. The table below uses plain LaTeX but does **not** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside any cell. All absolute-value bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid`.

### 2.4.1 Velocity and Work Equations (Impulse Turbine)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute velocity of steam at inlet | $V_1$ | m/s |
| 2 | Relative velocity at inlet | $V_{r1} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + u^2 - 2 V_1 u \cos \alpha_1}$ | m/s |
| 3 | Relative velocity at outlet | $V_{r2} = \phi \, V_{r1}$ (where $\phi$ = blade friction coefficient, 0.7–0.9) | m/s |
| 4 | Blade speed | $u = \pi D N / 60$ | m/s |
| 5 | Work done per kg of steam (frictionless, symmetric) | $W = u \, \left( V_{w1} + V_{w2} \right)$ | J/kg |
| 6 | Work done in terms of whirl velocities | $W = u \left( V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + V_2 \cos \alpha_2 \right)$ | J/kg |
| 7 | Maximum work (symmetric exit, $\beta_1 = \beta_2$) | $W_{max} = 2 u \left( V_1 \cos \alpha_1 - u \right)$ | J/kg |
| 8 | Optimum blade speed ratio | $(u/V_1) = \cos \alpha_1 / 2$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Maximum blade efficiency | $\eta_{b,max} = \cos^2 \alpha_1 / 2$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | For $\alpha_1 = 20^\circ$ | $\eta_{b,max} \approx 0.44$ i.e. **44 %** | — |

### 2.4.2 Reaction Turbine Relations

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 11 | Degree of Reaction (Parsons) | $R = \dfrac{V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2}{2 \, (V_1^2 - V_3^2 + V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2)}$ | — |
| 12 | For 50 % reaction (Parsons) | $R = 0.5$ | — |
| 13 | Work done (reaction stage) | $W = u \left( V_{w1} + V_{w2} \right)$ (same form as impulse) | J/kg |
| 14 | Axial thrust on moving blade (reaction) | $F_a = \dot{m} \, (V_{f1} - V_{f2}) + A \, (p_1 - p_2)$ | N |
| 15 | Stage pressure ratio (50 % reaction) | $p_1 / p_2 \approx 1.2$ – $1.5$ | — |

### 2.4.3 Compounding Relations

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 16 | Total work in velocity-compounded (Curtis) | $W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{r} W_i$ where $r$ = rows | J/kg |
| 17 | Blade-speed ratio (velocity compounding) | $(u/V_1) = \cos \alpha_1 / (r + 1)$ | — |
| 18 | Reheat factor (pressure compounded) | $RF = \dfrac{\sum \text{ideal enthalpy drops}}{\text{overall isentropic drop}}$ | dimensionless |
| 19 | Typical Reheat factor | $RF \approx 1.03$ – $1.08$ | — |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal power plant efficiency** is dictated by how well the turbine preserves the **Rankine cycle** heat drop.
- **Cogging losses, windage losses, and leakage losses** in the turbine can be inferred only via **shaft vibration, axial position, and condenser vacuum** measurements.
- The **Electro-Hydraulic Governor (EHG)** — which is a closed-loop **hydraulic servo** — is itself a **classical control system** that KTU examiners love to ask about (transfer function block diagrams, steady-state error, etc.).
- Modern plants use **Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation (TSI)** panels from **Bently Nevada** or **Vibro-Meter** that integrate all 14 measurements above into a single 19-inch rack — every KTU student should be able to draw the **TSI block diagram** and identify each channel.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Work Done in a Single-Stage Impulse Turbine

### 3.1.1 Velocity Triangle Setup

For a moving blade row of an impulse turbine, three velocity triangles (inlet and outlet) are constructed. The notation used is:

- $V_1$ = absolute velocity of steam entering the moving blade
- $V_2$ = absolute velocity of steam leaving the moving blade
- $u$ = linear velocity of the moving blade at mean radius
- $V_{r1}$ = relative velocity of steam w.r.t. blade at inlet
- $V_{r2}$ = relative velocity of steam w.r.t. blade at outlet
- $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ = absolute-flow angles (angle between $V$ and $u$)
- $\beta_1, \beta_2$ = relative-flow angles (angle between $V_r$ and the negative $u$ direction)
- $V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$ = whirl component at inlet
- $V_{w2} = V_2 \cos \alpha_2$ = whirl component at outlet
- $V_{f1}, V_{f2}$ = flow (axial) components

### 3.1.2 Statement of the Work Equation

The tangential force exerted by steam on a single moving blade (per kg/s) is the **rate of change of angular momentum of the steam in the tangential direction**.

$$F_t = \dot{m} \, (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$

> This equation holds when $V_2$ has a whirl component directed **opposite** to the direction of $u$ (i.e. the steam does a U-turn). This is the standard convention in KTU textbook derivations.

### 3.1.3 Work Done Per kg of Steam

The work done by the steam on the blade equals the tangential force multiplied by the blade velocity:

$$W = F_t \cdot u / \dot{m} = u \, \left( V_{w1} + V_{w2} \right)$$

Expanding the whirl components:

$$W = u \, \left( V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + V_2 \cos \alpha_2 \right)$$

### 3.1.4 Frictionless Symmetric Blade Assumption

In the **frictionless, symmetric blade** case:

- $V_{r1} = V_{r2}$ (no relative-velocity loss)
- $\beta_2 = \beta_1$ (symmetric exit angle)

The outlet absolute velocity magnitude equals the inlet absolute velocity magnitude (if $\alpha_1 = \alpha_2$):

$$V_1 = V_2, \quad \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha$$

Substituting into the work equation:

$$W_{frictionless} = 2 \, u \, V_1 \cos \alpha_1$$

### 3.1.5 Work Done With Blade Friction (Real Blade)

With blade friction coefficient $\phi = V_{r2} / V_{r1}$ (typically 0.7 to 0.9):

$$V_{r2} = \phi \, V_{r1}$$

By geometry of the exit velocity triangle (assuming $\beta_1 = \beta_2$):

$$V_{w2} = \phi \, V_{w1} - (1 - \phi) \, u$$

Therefore the **real work done per kg** is:

$$W = u \, \left( V_{w1} + V_{w2} \right) = u \, \left[ V_{w1} + \phi \, V_{w1} - (1 - \phi) u \right]$$

$$\boxed{W = u \, V_{w1} \, (1 + \phi) - (1 - \phi) \, u^2}$$

> This is the **most-asked formula** for a 14-mark KTU derivation question.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Blade Efficiency

### 3.2.1 Definition of Blade Efficiency

Blade (or stage) efficiency is defined as:

$$\eta_b = \frac{\text{Work done on the blade}}{\text{Change in kinetic energy of steam}} = \frac{W}{\frac{1}{2} (V_1^2 - V_2^2)}$$

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Maximisation

For the **frictionless symmetric** case, $V_1 = V_2$ in magnitude, hence the kinetic-energy change is $0$ — efficiency is $100\%$. With friction, we use the **real work** expression.

**Step 1:** Write the inlet whirl velocity using the inlet velocity triangle:

$$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1$$

**Step 2:** Write the friction-affected outlet whirl velocity:

$$V_{w2} = u - \phi (V_1 \cos \alpha_1 - u) = u (1 + \phi) - \phi V_1 \cos \alpha_1$$

**Step 3:** The work done is:

$$W = u \left( V_{w1} + V_{w2} \right) = u \left[ V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + u(1+\phi) - \phi V_1 \cos \alpha_1 \right]$$

$$W = u \left[ (1 - \phi) V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + (1 + \phi) u \right]$$

**Step 4:** The kinetic energy supplied by the nozzle per kg of steam is:

$$\text{KE} = \frac{V_1^2 - V_3^2}{2} \approx \frac{V_1^2}{2}$$

(if exit velocity from the nozzle is $V_3$ but we set $V_3 \ll V_1$).

**Step 5:** Blade efficiency:

$$\eta_b = \frac{2u \left[ (1 - \phi) V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + (1 + \phi) u \right]}{V_1^2}$$

Let the **blade-speed ratio** be $k = u / V_1$. Then:

$$\eta_b = 2 k \left[ (1 - \phi) \cos \alpha_1 + (1 + \phi) k \right]$$

**Step 6:** Differentiate with respect to $k$ and set to zero to find the optimum:

$$\frac{d \eta_b}{dk} = 2 (1 - \phi) \cos \alpha_1 + 4 (1 + \phi) k = 0$$

$$k_{opt} = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{(1 - \phi) \cos \alpha_1}{2 (1 + \phi)}$$

**Step 7:** Substitute $k_{opt}$ back to obtain maximum blade efficiency:

$$\eta_{b,max} = \frac{(1 - \phi)^2 \cos^2 \alpha_1}{2 (1 + \phi)}$$

For the **ideal frictionless** case ($\phi = 1$):

$$\eta_{b,max} = \frac{\cos^2 \alpha_1}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Favourite:** For $\alpha_1 = 20^\circ$, $\eta_{b,max} = 0.9397^2 / 2 = 0.4415 \approx \mathbf{44.15\%}$. This single numerical is asked in almost every KTU University Exam — memorise it.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Done and Thrust in a Reaction Turbine

### 3.3.1 Degree of Reaction

The **degree of reaction** $R$ is the fraction of the total enthalpy drop that occurs in the moving blades:

$$R = \frac{\text{enthalpy drop in moving blades}}{\text{total enthalpy drop in the stage}}$$

For a 50 % reaction stage (Parsons), the fixed and moving blades are **symmetrically shaped** with $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$ and $\alpha_2 = \beta_1$, and:

$$V_{f1} = V_{f2}, \quad V_{r1} = V_1, \quad V_{r2} = V_2$$

### 3.3.2 Work Done Equation

By angular-momentum balance (identical reasoning to the impulse case):

$$W = u \, \left( V_{w1} + V_{w2} \right) = u \left( V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + V_2 \cos \alpha_2 \right)$$

### 3.3.3 Axial Thrust on the Rotor

The axial force arises from two contributions:

1. **Momentum change** of the steam in the axial direction.
2. **Pressure difference** across the moving blade (only present in reaction turbines).

$$F_a = \dot{m} \, (V_{f1} - V_{f2}) + A_m \, (p_1 - p_2)$$

where $A_m$ is the annulus area of the moving blade ring and $p_1, p_2$ are the static pressures at inlet and outlet of the moving blade row.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **pressure thrust** component is what distinguishes reaction-turbine instrumentation from impulse-turbine instrumentation: **axial-thrust probes and thrust bearings** are critical SIS elements in reaction turbines.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure drop in moving blades | Zero (only KE changes) | ~50 % (Parsons) |
| Relative velocity $V_{r1}$ vs $V_{r2}$ | $V_{r1} > V_{r2}$ due to friction | $V_{r2} > V_{r1}$ (acceleration) |
| Blade shape | Symmetric, smooth | Asymmetric, convergent |
| Compounding | Common (Curtis, Rateau) | Rare (multi-stage Parsons) |
| Axial thrust | Small (momentum only) | Large (momentum + pressure) |
| Steam leakage | Less | More (pressure difference) |
| Stage efficiency | ~70 – 80 % | ~85 – 90 % |
| Typical application | Small / Medium / HP stages | Large LP / IP cylinders |
| Steam consumption | Higher | Lower (better partial-load economy) |
| Instrumentation complexity | Moderate (vibration, speed) | High (axial thrust probe essential) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams are **block-level functional architectures** (per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback rule) showing how instrumentation is interfaced with the steam turbine process object.

## 4.1 Steam Turbine Instrumentation — Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boiler Main Steam Header] --> B[Main Steam Stop Valve]

    H --> K
    K --> L[Shaft Speed Sensor]
    K --> M[Vibration Sensor]
    K --> N[Axial Position Sensor]
    K --> O[Bearing Temperature Sensor]

    P --> Q[Speed Reference 3000 RPM]
    P --> R[Load Setpoint MW]
    K --> S[DCS Distributed Control System]
    P --> S
    S --> T[SIS Safety Instrumented System]

```

## 4.2 Electro-Hydraulic Governor (EHG) — Closed-Loop Block Diagram

```mermaid

    A[Speed Reference 3000 RPM] --> B[Summing Junction]

    I --> C
```

## 4.3 Steam-Turbine Safety Trip Matrix (SIS Channels)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Trip Inputs] --> B[Trip Logic 2oo3 Voter]
    B --> C[Trip Relay 1]
    B --> D[Trip Relay 2]

    C --> F[Emergency Trip Solenoid]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[HP LP Stop Valves Close]
    F --> H[MSV Main Stop Valve Close]
    F --> I[Mechanical Overspeed Trip Reset]
    A1[Bearing Temp Hi Hi] --> A
    A2[Vibration Hi Hi] --> A
    A3[Axial Displacement Hi Hi] --> A
    A4[Lube Oil Pressure Lo Lo] --> A
    A5[Condenser Vacuum Lo Lo] --> A
    A6[Shaft Speed Hi] --> A
```

## 4.4 Impulse-Turbine Velocity Triangle — Schematic Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Nozzle Inlet V1 alpha1] --> B[Relative Velocity Vr1 beta1]
    B --> C[Moving Blade velocity u]
    C --> D[Outlet V2 alpha2]
    D --> E[Outlet Vr2 beta2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** In the inlet triangle, $V_1$ and $u$ are the known sides and $V_{r1}$ is the resultant. In the outlet triangle, $V_{r2}$ and $u$ are the known sides and $V_2$ is the resultant. The work done per kg is $u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ as derived in Section 3.1.
