# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 marks)

**Differentiate between impulse and reaction steam turbines.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Impulse Turbine | Reaction Turbine |
|---|---|---|
| Pressure drop in moving blades | **Zero** (pressure constant) | **~50 %** (Parsons design) |
| Steam velocity change in blades | Decreases due to friction | Increases (acceleration) |
| Relative velocity ratio | $V_{r2} < V_{r1}$ | $V_{r2} > V_{r1}$ |
| Blade shape | Symmetric, smooth | Asymmetric, convergent nozzle |
| Axial thrust on rotor | Small | Large (pressure + momentum) |
| Steam leakage | Less | More, requires sealing |
| Typical application | HP, Curtis stages | LP, multi-stage Parsons |
| Number of stages for same pressure drop | Fewer (large pressure drop per stage) | More (small pressure drop per stage) |

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 marks)

**What is the function of a steam turbine governor? Name the three methods of governing.**

**Model Answer:**

The **governor** is a feedback control device that automatically regulates the steam flow to the turbine to maintain **constant shaft speed** (3000 RPM) irrespective of variations in electrical load. It compares actual speed with a reference and repositions the control valves via a hydraulic servo.

The **three methods of governing** are:

1. **Throttle governing** (Lanz method) — single throttle valve regulates HP inlet pressure.
2. **Nozzle governing** (Meyer's method) — multiple control valves progressively admit steam to groups of nozzles.
3. **Bypass governing** — steam admitted to intermediate stages during low-load / start-up.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** With the help of velocity triangles, derive the expression for **work done per kg of steam** in a single-stage impulse turbine, taking friction into account. Also state the condition for **maximum blade efficiency** and hence obtain the expression for it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> The complete derivation is reproduced from Section 3.1 and 3.2 of these notes.

**Step 1:** From inlet velocity triangle, the relative velocity at inlet is obtained by vector subtraction:

$$V_{r1} = \sqrt{V_1^2 + u^2 - 2 V_1 u \cos \alpha_1}$$

**Step 2:** Apply angular-momentum theorem to steam flowing over the blade. The tangential force per kg/s is the change in tangential momentum:

$$F_t = V_{w1} + V_{w2}$$

**Step 3:** Work done per kg is the tangential force times blade speed:

$$W = u \, (V_{w1} + V_{w2}) = u \, (V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + V_2 \cos \alpha_2)$$

**Step 4:** With blade friction coefficient $\phi = V_{r2}/V_{r1}$ and symmetric exit ($\beta_1 = \beta_2$):

$$V_{w2} = u(1 + \phi) - \phi V_1 \cos \alpha_1$$

**Step 5:** Substituting:

$$W = u [V_1 \cos \alpha_1 (1 - \phi) + u(1 + \phi)]$$

$$\boxed{W = (1 - \phi) u V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + (1 + \phi) u^2}$$

**Step 6:** Blade efficiency is work done divided by kinetic energy supplied:

$$\eta_b = \frac{2 W}{V_1^2} = 2 k [(1 - \phi) \cos \alpha_1 + (1 + \phi) k]$$

**Step 7:** Differentiate w.r.t. $k = u/V_1$ and equate to zero:

$$\frac{d \eta_b}{dk} = 0 \Rightarrow k_{opt} = \frac{u}{V_1} = \frac{(1 - \phi) \cos \alpha_1}{2(1 + \phi)}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute back:

$$\eta_{b,max} = \frac{(1 - \phi)^2 \cos^2 \alpha_1}{2(1 + \phi)}$$

For $\phi = 1$ (frictionless): $\eta_{b,max} = \cos^2 \alpha_1 / 2$. For $\alpha_1 = 20°$, this gives **44.15 %**.

**(b)** A single-stage impulse turbine has a blade mean diameter of **1.2 m** and runs at **3000 RPM**. The steam issues from the nozzle at a velocity of **350 m/s** at an angle of **22°** to the plane of rotation. The blade friction coefficient is **0.85** and the blades are symmetric ($\beta_1 = \beta_2$). Calculate:
   (i) Blade speed
   (ii) Work done per kg of steam
   (iii) Blade efficiency
   (iv) Axial thrust on the blades if the steam flow rate is **15 kg/s** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Blade speed:**

$$u = \frac{\pi D N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 1.2 \times 3000}{60} = 60 \pi = 188.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Inlet whirl velocity:**

$$V_{w1} = V_1 \cos \alpha_1 = 350 \times \cos 22° = 350 \times 0.9272 = 324.5 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Work done (with friction):**

$$W = (1 - \phi) u V_1 \cos \alpha_1 + (1 + \phi) u^2$$

$$W = (1 - 0.85)(188.5)(324.5) + (1 + 0.85)(188.5)^2$$

$$W = 0.15 \times 188.5 \times 324.5 + 1.85 \times 35543.5$$

$$W = 9175 + 65755 = 74930 \text{ J/kg} \approx 74.93 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Blade efficiency:**

$$\eta_b = \frac{2 W}{V_1^2} = \frac{2 \times 74930}{350^2} = \frac{149860}{122500} = 0.7095$$

$$\eta_b \approx 70.95\%$$

**Step 5 — Axial thrust:**

The flow (axial) velocity is:

$$V_{f1} = V_1 \sin \alpha_1 = 350 \times \sin 22° = 350 \times 0.3746 = 131.1 \text{ m/s}$$

For a symmetric exit, $V_{f2} = V_{f1}$ (no flow-velocity change):

$$F_a = \dot{m} (V_{f1} - V_{f2}) = 0$$

**Therefore axial thrust is zero in this case** (only for a truly symmetric blade, as assumed).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A(b):**
> Students commonly **forget to apply the friction coefficient** when computing $V_{w2}$. Many compute only $V_{w2} = -V_{w1}$ (frictionless case) and get $W = 2 u V_{w1} = 122.4$ kJ/kg, which is **~63 % higher** than the correct value. Always check: *"friction given? → use $\phi$."*
> Also, students often **erroneously compute axial thrust** as $F_a = \dot{m} V_{f1}$ ignoring the cancellation. For symmetric blades, $V_{f1} = V_{f2}$, so $F_a = 0$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **Electro-Hydraulic Governor (EHG)** system used in a modern steam turbine. Explain the role of the LVDT feedback and the PID controller. State **two trip parameters** that would cause an SIS-initiated emergency shutdown. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **EHG** is a closed-loop electro-hydraulic servo system that regulates the steam inlet valve position to maintain constant shaft speed. Its components are:

1. **Speed Sensor (Magnetic Pickup / Proximity Probe):** Produces a frequency proportional to shaft RPM (e.g. 3000 Hz at 3000 RPM if there are 60 teeth).
2. **Speed Reference Setting:** Operator / DCS sets the desired speed (3000 RPM).
3. **Summing Junction (Error Detector):** Compares actual and reference speeds.
4. **PID Controller (Electronic):** Generates a corrective signal based on Proportional, Integral, Derivative actions.
5. **Servo Amplifier:** Boosts the controller output to drive the hydraulic actuator.
6. **Hydraulic Power Cylinder:** Physically repositions the governor valve stem.
7. **LVDT Position Feedback:** The Linear Variable Differential Transformer is mounted on the valve stem and provides a voltage proportional to the **actual valve position**. This negative feedback ensures the valve reaches the desired position accurately and improves the dynamic response of the loop.
8. **Governor Valve:** Admits steam to the first-stage nozzles.

**Block diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.

**Two SIS trip parameters** that would initiate an emergency turbine trip:

- **Lubricating-oil pressure Lo-Lo** (e.g. < 0.8 bar at bearing manifold).
- **Shaft vibration Hi-Hi** (e.g. > 200 µm peak-to-peak at any bearing).
- *Also acceptable:* Axial displacement Hi-Hi, Bearing-metal temperature Hi-Hi, Overspeed (> 110 % of rated), Condenser vacuum Lo-Lo.

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **50 % reaction (Parsons) steam turbine stage**. Derive the **degree of reaction** and show that the work done per kg of steam equals $u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A 50 % reaction stage consists of a **fixed blade row (nozzle guide vane)** followed by a **moving blade row** of identical convergent shape, mounted on the rotor. The fixed blades and moving blades together form the complete stage.

**Working:** High-pressure steam enters the fixed blades, where ~50 % of the enthalpy drop occurs and the steam is accelerated. The accelerated steam then enters the moving blades, where the remaining 50 % of the enthalpy drop takes place. The pressure drop across the moving blades creates a **reaction thrust** on the rotor.

**Degree of Reaction derivation:**

By definition:

$$R = \frac{\Delta h_{moving}}{\Delta h_{total}} = \frac{(V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2)/2}{(V_1^2 - V_3^2 + V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2)/2}$$

For symmetric blade shapes ($V_{f1} = V_{f2}$, $\alpha_1 = \beta_2$):

From the velocity-triangle geometry, $V_1 = V_{r2}$ and $V_3 = V_{r1}$, and so:

$$R = \frac{V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2}{V_{r2}^2 - V_{r1}^2} = 0.5 \text{ i.e. } 50\%$$

**Work done per kg:**

Applying angular-momentum theorem to steam in the moving row:

$$F_t = \dot{m} (V_{w1} + V_{w2}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad W = u (V_{w1} + V_{w2})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> 1. In part (a), students frequently **omit the LVDT feedback loop** in the EHG block diagram. Without this negative feedback the loop is open-loop and the valve would never settle. **Always show the LVDT feedback line** returning to the summing junction.
> 2. In part (b), students often confuse the **absolute** velocity triangles with the **relative** velocity triangles when deriving $R$. Remember: fixed blades have only **absolute** velocities, moving blades have both **relative and absolute** velocities. The reaction is computed using the **relative** velocity change in the moving row.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORISE BEFORE EVERY KTU EXAM:**

- [x] Work done in impulse turbine: $W = u(V_{w1} + V_{w2})$ — same form as reaction.
- [x] Optimum blade-speed ratio: $k = u/V_1 = (1-\phi)\cos\alpha_1 / [2(1+\phi)]$.
- [x] Max blade efficiency (frictionless): $\eta_{b,max} = \cos^2 \alpha_1 / 2 = 44.15\%$ for $\alpha_1 = 20°$.
- [x] Degree of reaction for 50 % reaction: $R = 0.5$ (Parsons).
- [x] Three governing methods: **Throttle (Lanz), Nozzle (Meyer), Bypass** — know the advantage of nozzle governing (better partial-load efficiency).
- [x] Fourteen TSI parameters and their sensors — at minimum: **shaft speed (mag pickup), vibration (eddy current), axial position (eddy current), bearing temp (RTD), lube oil pressure (Bourdon), condenser vacuum (capacitance), differential expansion (LVDT), valve position (LVDT)**.
- [x] TSI stands for **Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation** — vendors: Bently Nevada, Vibro-Meter, GE Bently.
- [x] SIS trip philosophy: **2-out-of-3 (2oo3) voting** for overspeed and critical trips (per IEC 61508/61511 SIL-3).
- [x] EHG = closed-loop servo: **Speed ref → Error → PID → Servo amp → Hydraulic cylinder → Governor valve → LVDT feedback**.
- [x] Reheat factor (pressure compounded): $RF = \Sigma(\text{stage ideal drops}) / \text{overall isentropic drop}$, typically **1.03 – 1.08**.
- [x] Specific steam consumption (SSC) of a modern subcritical plant: **~2.0 – 2.5 kg/kWh**.
- [x] Synchronous speed: **3000 RPM (50 Hz, 2-pole)** — remember $N_s = 120 f / P$.

> **End of Module 2 — Steam Turbine Notes | PEICT523 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alternator
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Alternator**, formally known as a **Synchronous Generator**, is a rotating electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms mechanical input power (delivered by a prime mover such as a steam turbine, gas turbine, hydro turbine, or diesel engine) into three-phase alternating electrical output power. It operates on the fundamental principle of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, wherein a relative motion between a magnetic flux field and a stationary armature winding induces an electromotive force (EMF) governed by the rotational speed and the magnetic flux linkage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** "An alternator is an AC electrical generator that converts mechanical energy to electrical energy in the form of alternating current. In power plants, alternators are coupled to prime movers (turbines) and operate at synchronous speed to deliver power at a constant grid frequency of **50 Hz** (India) or **60 Hz** (USA)."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Wheel and Pipe System"**

Imagine a large circular water wheel connected to a system of pipes. As the wheel rotates, water is pushed out through three pipes arranged at **120°** angular spacing, each delivering water in synchronized, alternating pulses. 

Now, replace the water with **electrons** and the wheel with the **alternator rotor**. The mechanical energy from the turbine spins the rotor (which has electromagnets or permanent magnets), and as the magnetic field cuts across stationary copper coils (stator windings), it pushes electrons through the external circuit in an alternating fashion.

| Water Wheel Analogy | Alternator Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Rotating wheel | **Rotor** | Carries magnetic field; driven by prime mover |
| Water flow | **Electron flow (current)** | Generated AC output |
| Three pipes | **3-Phase stator windings** | Deliver 3-phase power at 120° phase displacement |
| Pump (driving force) | **Prime Mover (Turbine)** | Mechanical input energy source |
| Pressure regulator | **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)** | Maintains constant terminal voltage |

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

- **Synchronous Speed** $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ — measured in **RPM** (Revolutions Per Minute)
- **Standard Grid Frequency (India):** $f = \mathbf{50\ Hz}$
- **Standard Grid Frequency (USA):** $f = \mathbf{60\ Hz}$
- **Number of Poles:** $P$ — must be an **even integer** (2, 4, 6, 8, ..., 32)
- **Power Output Range:** From **kW** (small diesel gensets) to **1500 MVA** (large turbo-alternators)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In all KTU problems, unless otherwise specified, always assume **f = 50 Hz**, **3-phase**, **Y-connected stator winding**, and a **field winding on the rotor** (typical cylindrical rotor for high-speed turbo-alternators).

### 1.4 Role of an Alternator in a Power Plant

In a thermal power plant, the **alternator forms the heart of the energy conversion chain**. The complete energy path is:

$$\text{Coal} \rightarrow \text{Steam} \rightarrow \text{Turbine (Mechanical)} \rightarrow \text{Alternator (Electrical)} \rightarrow \text{Transformer} \rightarrow \text{Grid}$$

Without the alternator, the mechanical rotational energy produced by the turbine cannot be transmitted as usable electrical energy to consumers. Hence, the **efficiency, voltage stability, frequency stability, and synchronization capability** of the alternator directly determine the **quality and reliability** of the entire power plant.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of an alternator can be broken down into six structured logical stages:

1. **Mechanical Input Reception:** The prime mover (steam/hydro/gas turbine) supplies rotational mechanical energy to the alternator shaft.
2. **Magnetic Field Excitation:** A **DC excitation current** ($I_f$) is supplied to the rotor field winding via slip rings and brushes (or brushless exciter). This produces a **main magnetic flux** ($\Phi$) in the rotor poles.
3. **Relative Motion Generation:** The rotor rotates at synchronous speed $N_s$, producing a **rotating magnetic field** that physically sweeps past the stationary stator conductors.
4. **Flux Cutting Action:** According to Faraday's Law, the rate of change of flux linkage through each stator coil induces an EMF: $e = -N\dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$.
5. **EMF Waveform Generation:** Since the flux variation with rotor angle is sinusoidal in nature (due to distributed windings and chamfered/slotted pole faces), the induced EMF is **sinusoidal**.
6. **Three-Phase Power Output:** Three identical stator windings displaced by **120° electrical** generate three sinusoidal EMFs: $e_a$, $e_b$, $e_c$, forming a balanced 3-phase system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Synchronous"?** The rotor speed is *locked* to the grid frequency. It cannot rotate at any arbitrary speed — it must rotate at exactly $N_s = 120f/P$. This is why it's called a **synchronous machine**.

### 2.2 Constructional Details

| Component | Location | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stator Core** | Stationary outer frame | Laminated silicon steel | Reduces eddy current losses; houses slots |
| **Stator Winding** | Embedded in stator slots | Copper conductors (insulated) | Where EMF is induced; arranged in 3 phases |
| **Rotor (Cylindrical)** | Rotating inner body | Solid forged steel | Used for high-speed (1500/3000 RPM) turbo-alternators |
| **Rotor (Salient Pole)** | Rotating inner body | Laminated steel with pole shoes | Used for low-speed (hydro) alternators |
| **Slip Rings & Brushes** | On rotor shaft | Carbon brushes, brass rings | Feed DC excitation to rotor |
| **Cooling System** | Embedded in frame | Hydrogen, water, or air | Removes heat from windings and core |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these 12 formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Alternator problem.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Synchronous Speed** | $N_s = \dfrac{120f}{P}$ | $f$ in Hz, $P$ = number of poles |
| 2 | **Frequency of Induced EMF** | $f = \dfrac{P \cdot N_s}{120}$ | $N_s$ in RPM |
| 3 | **EMF per Phase (Flux)** | $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, K_p \, f \, \Phi \, T_{ph}$ | $K_w$ = winding factor, $K_p$ = pitch factor |
| 4 | **Pitch Factor** | $K_p = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | $\alpha$ = short-pitch angle (electrical degrees) |
| 5 | **Distribution Factor** | $K_d = \dfrac{\sin(m\beta/2)}{m \sin(\beta/2)}$ | $m$ = slots/pole/phase, $\beta$ = slot angle |
| 6 | **Winding Factor** | $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$ | Typically 0.95 – 0.98 |
| 7 | **Synchronous Impedance** | $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$ | $R_a$ = armature resistance, $X_s$ = synchronous reactance (Ω/phase) |
| 8 | **Synchronous Reactance** | $X_s = X_a + X_l$ | $X_a$ = armature reaction reactance, $X_l$ = leakage reactance |
| 9 | **Open-Circuit EMF** | $E_0 = V + I_a (R_a + jX_s)$ | Phasor sum (per phase) |
| 10 | **Voltage Regulation** | $\%VR = \dfrac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$ | $E_0$ = no-load EMF, $V$ = full-load terminal voltage |
| 11 | **Three-Phase Output Power** | $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \, \cos\phi$ | $V_L$ = line voltage, $I_L$ = line current, $\cos\phi$ = power factor |
| 12 | **Power Factor (Angle)** | $\cos\phi = \dfrac{R_a}{Z_s}$ | For cylindrical rotor with purely resistive loading |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Substitution Note for Tables:** When writing phasor equations, avoid using the `|` character inside markdown table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. For example, write $\vert Z_s \vert$ or $\mid Z_s \mid$, not $\vert Z_s \vert$ directly inside a table row to prevent Markdown parser conflicts.

### 2.4 EMF Equation — Rigorous Derivation Foundation

The EMF induced in a single conductor is:

$$e_{conductor} = B \cdot l \cdot v$$

For a full-pitched, concentrated winding with $T_{ph}$ turns per phase, the total flux cut per revolution equals $2\Phi$ per turn (once entering pole, once leaving pole). The average EMF is therefore:

$$E_{avg} = 2 \Phi \cdot f \cdot T_{ph}$$

Multiplying by the form factor $\dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.11$ for a pure sine wave, and incorporating $K_w$:

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, f \, \Phi \, T_{ph}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** This equation is the *design cornerstone* of every alternator. By varying $\Phi$ (via field excitation), the terminal voltage is regulated. The AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator) directly exploits this principle.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation: EMF Equation of an Alternator

**Given:**
- $P$ = number of poles
- $N_s$ = synchronous speed in RPM
- $\Phi$ = flux per pole (in Webers, Wb)
- $T_{ph}$ = number of turns per phase
- $K_w$ = winding factor ($K_p \cdot K_d$)

**Find:** RMS EMF per phase, $E_{ph}$

---

**Step 1: Convert rotational speed to frequency**

The rotor completes $N_s$ revolutions per minute. Each revolution contains $P/2$ complete flux cycles (since each pole pair = 1 cycle). Therefore:

$$f = \frac{P \cdot N_s}{2 \cdot 60} = \frac{P \cdot N_s}{120} \quad [\text{Hz}]$$

**Step 2: Time period of one cycle**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} \quad [\text{seconds}]$$

**Step 3: Flux cut by one conductor per revolution**

As the rotor sweeps past one stator conductor, the flux linking the conductor changes from $+\Phi$ to $-\Phi$ over one pole-pair. The total flux cut per revolution = $2\Phi$ (one pole entering + one pole leaving).

**Step 4: Flux cut per second**

Flux cut per second per conductor = $\text{(Flux per revolution)} \times \text{(Revolutions per second)}$

$$\frac{d\Phi_{total}}{dt} = 2\Phi \cdot \frac{N_s}{60} = 2\Phi f \quad [\text{Wb/s}]$$

**Step 5: Average EMF in one conductor**

By Faraday's Law, the average EMF induced per conductor is:

$$e_{avg, conductor} = \frac{d\Phi_{total}}{dt} = 2\Phi f \quad [\text{V}]$$

**Step 6: Average EMF for the entire phase winding**

Each phase has $T_{ph}$ turns. If all turns were full-pitched and concentrated, the average EMF per phase would be:

$$E_{avg, phase} = 2\Phi f T_{ph}$$

**Step 7: Apply the form factor (RMS conversion)**

For a pure sinusoidal waveform, the RMS value is related to the average value by the form factor $K_f = \dfrac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} = 1.11$. Therefore:

$$E_{rms, ideal} = 1.11 \times 2\Phi f T_{ph} = 2.22 \Phi f T_{ph}$$

**Step 8: Incorporate the Winding Factor $K_w$**

In a practical alternator, the windings are:
- **Distributed** across multiple slots (reduces EMF slightly → factor $K_d < 1$)
- **Short-pitched / Chording** (reduces EMF slightly → factor $K_p < 1$)

The combined reduction is captured by $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$, typically ranging from **0.95 to 0.98**.

Multiplying Step 7 by $K_w$:

$$E_{rms, actual} = 2.22 \cdot K_w \cdot \Phi \cdot f \cdot T_{ph}$$

**Step 9: Final EMF Equation**

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 2.22 \, K_w \, \Phi \, f \, T_{ph} \quad [\text{V/phase}]}$$

$$\boxed{E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, \Phi \, f \, T_{ph} \quad [\text{V/phase}]}$$

The form $4.44$ is obtained by substituting $2.22 = 4.44/2$, where the factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for the numerical relationship between average and peak (rather than average and RMS) used in the formal derivation.

---

### 3.2 Voltage Regulation — Phasor Analysis & Numerical Derivation

**Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem Pattern):**

> A 3-phase, Y-connected, 1500 kVA, 6600 V alternator has armature resistance $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$/phase and synchronous reactance $X_s = 5\ \Omega$/phase. At full-load, the line current is **131.2 A** at **0.8 power factor lagging**. Calculate the no-load EMF and the percentage voltage regulation.

**Step 1: Compute Phase Voltage and Phase Current**

For a Y-connection:
$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{6600}{\sqrt{3}} = 3810.5 \quad [\text{V}]$$

$$I_{ph} = I_L = 131.2 \quad [\text{A}] \quad (\text{since Y-connected})$$

**Step 2: Compute Synchronous Impedance Magnitude**

$$Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2} = \sqrt{(0.5)^2 + (5)^2}$$

$$Z_s = \sqrt{0.25 + 25} = \sqrt{25.25}$$

$$Z_s = 5.025 \quad [\Omega/\text{phase}]$$

**Step 3: Compute Power Factor Angle**

$$\cos\phi = 0.8 \Rightarrow \phi = \cos^{-1}(0.8) = 36.87° \quad [\text{lagging}]$$

$$\sin\phi = 0.6$$

**Step 4: Construct the Phasor Equation**

The KVL for one phase of the alternator is:

$$\vec{E_0} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$$

Taking $\vec{V}$ as the reference phasor along the positive real axis:

$$\vec{V} = V_{ph} \angle 0° = 3810.5 + j0 \quad [\text{V}]$$

Since the load is **lagging**, the current phasor lags the voltage:

$$\vec{I_a} = I_{ph} \angle -\phi = 131.2 \angle -36.87°$$

**Step 5: Compute Voltage Drop Across Impedance**

In rectangular form:
$$\vec{I_a} = 131.2 (\cos(-36.87°) + j\sin(-36.87°))$$
$$\vec{I_a} = 131.2 (0.8 - j0.6)$$
$$\vec{I_a} = 104.96 - j78.72 \quad [\text{A}]$$

Now multiply by $(R_a + jX_s) = (0.5 + j5)$:

$$\vec{I_a} \cdot (R_a + jX_s) = (104.96 - j78.72)(0.5 + j5)$$

Expanding:

$$= 104.96 \times 0.5 + 104.96 \times j5 - j78.72 \times 0.5 - j78.72 \times j5$$

$$= 52.48 + j524.8 - j39.36 - j^2 \cdot 393.6$$

Since $j^2 = -1$:

$$= 52.48 + j524.8 - j39.36 + 393.6$$

$$= (52.48 + 393.6) + j(524.8 - 39.36)$$

$$= 446.08 + j485.44 \quad [\text{V}]$$

**Step 6: Compute the No-Load EMF Phasor**

$$\vec{E_0} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$$

$$\vec{E_0} = (3810.5 + 0j) + (446.08 + j485.44)$$

$$\vec{E_0} = 4256.58 + j485.44 \quad [\text{V}]$$

**Step 7: Compute the Magnitude of $E_0$**

$$E_0 = \sqrt{(4256.58)^2 + (485.44)^2}$$

$$E_0 = \sqrt{18,118,316.8 + 235,651.9}$$

$$E_0 = \sqrt{18,353,968.7}$$

$$\boxed{E_0 \approx 4284.16 \quad [\text{V/phase}]}$$

**Step 8: Compute Percentage Voltage Regulation**

$$\%VR = \frac{E_0 - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100$$

$$\%VR = \frac{4284.16 - 3810.5}{3810.5} \times 100$$

$$\%VR = \frac{473.66}{3810.5} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%VR \approx 12.43\%}$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Alternator Performance at Different Power Factors

For a cylindrical rotor alternator with $\cos\phi = 1$ (purely resistive load), the regulation is positive. As the load becomes more inductive (lagging), the regulation worsens. For leading power factor (capacitive), the regulation can become **negative** (i.e., terminal voltage rises on load).

| Load Power Factor | Power Factor Angle $\phi$ | Effect on Terminal Voltage | Regulation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Unity ($\cos\phi = 1$) | $0°$ | Slight drop due to $I_a R_a$ | Small positive |
| 0.8 lagging (typical inductive) | $36.87°$ | Significant drop | Positive (~10–15%) |
| 0.5 lagging (highly inductive) | $60°$ | Large drop | Highly positive |
| Zero lagging (pure inductive) | $90°$ | Maximum drop | Maximum positive |
| Zero leading (pure capacitive) | $-90°$ | Maximum rise | Maximum negative |
| 0.8 leading (capacitive) | $-36.87°$ | Terminal voltage rises | Negative |
| Unity leading | $0°$ | Same as unity lagging | Slight positive |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define an alternator. Why is it called a "synchronous" machine?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **alternator** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that converts mechanical input energy (from a prime mover such as a turbine) into three-phase alternating electrical output energy, operating on Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction.

It is called a "**synchronous**" machine because the rotor rotates at a **fixed speed locked to the supply frequency**, governed by the relation $N_s = 120f/P$. The rotor cannot rotate at any arbitrary speed — it must rotate in synchronism with the rotating magnetic field produced by the stator or with the grid frequency to which it is connected.

**(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: principle, 1 mark: synchronous justification)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

List any **three** constructional differences between a **cylindrical rotor alternator** and a **salient pole alternator**.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Feature | Cylindrical Rotor | Salient Pole Rotor |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | High speed (1500 / 3000 RPM) | Low speed (100–500 RPM) |
| Application | Steam turbines, gas turbines | Hydro turbines, diesel engines |
| Construction | Smooth, uniform air gap | Projecting poles with non-uniform air gap |
| Mechanical strength | High (forged steel) | Moderate (laminated pole pieces) |
| Number of poles | 2 or 4 | 4 to 32 or more |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the EMF equation of a single-phase alternator. Clearly state the role of the winding factor $K_w$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, Y-connected alternator has the following ratings and test data:

- Rated apparent power: $S = 1500\ \text{kVA}$
- Line voltage: $V_L = 6600\ \text{V}$
- Frequency: $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$
- Number of poles: $P = 4$
- Turns per phase: $T_{ph} = 200$
- Flux per pole: $\Phi = 0.1\ \text{Wb}$
- Winding factor: $K_w = 0.96$

Calculate:

(i) The synchronous speed $N_s$ in RPM.  
(ii) The induced EMF per phase $E_{ph}$.  
(iii) The line EMF $E_L$.  

(7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EMF Derivation (7 marks):**

The complete step-by-step derivation has been presented in **Section 3.1** of these notes. For the answer script, students should write:

- **Step 1:** Express frequency $f = \dfrac{PN_s}{120}$
- **Step 2:** State flux cut per conductor per second = $2\Phi f$
- **Step 3:** Apply Faraday's Law: $e_{avg} = 2\Phi f$ per conductor
- **Step 4:** For $T_{ph}$ turns: $E_{avg,phase} = 2\Phi f T_{ph}$
- **Step 5:** Apply form factor $K_f = 1.11$ for RMS conversion
- **Step 6:** Multiply by winding factor $K_w$

**Final equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, \Phi \, f \, T_{ph}$

The **winding factor** $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$ accounts for:
- $K_p$ (pitch factor): Reduction due to short-pitched coils
- $K_d$ (distribution factor): Reduction due to distributed winding across multiple slots

Typical value: $0.95 \le K_w \le 0.98$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Synchronous Speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{120f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500 \quad [\text{RPM}]$$

**(ii) EMF per Phase:**

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, \Phi \, f \, T_{ph}$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 0.96 \times 0.1 \times 50 \times 200$$

$$E_{ph} = 4.44 \times 0.96 \times 1000$$

$$E_{ph} = 4262.4 \quad [\text{V/phase}]$$

**(iii) Line EMF (Y-connection):**

$$E_L = \sqrt{3} \times E_{ph} = 1.732 \times 4262.4$$

$$E_L \approx 7382.5 \quad [\text{V}]$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023] (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **synchronous reactance** $X_s$ and derive the expression for **voltage regulation** of an alternator using the synchronous impedance method (EMF method). (7 marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, 5 MVA, 11 kV, Y-connected alternator has armature resistance $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega/\text{phase}$ and synchronous reactance $X_s = 4\ \Omega/\text{phase}$. At a full load of **0.8 power factor lagging**, calculate:

(i) The synchronous impedance per phase.
(ii) The no-load EMF per phase.
(iii) The percentage voltage regulation.

(7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

**Synchronous Reactance $X_s$:** It is the **fictitious reactance** used to represent the combined effect of:
1. **Leakage reactance** ($X_l$) — due to flux that links the armature but not the main field
2. **Armature reaction reactance** ($X_a$) — due to the demagnetizing effect of armature flux on the main field

$$X_s = X_l + X_a$$

**Voltage Regulation using EMF Method (Synchronous Impedance Method):**

The phasor equation per phase:
$$\vec{E_0} = \vec{V} + \vec{I_a}(R_a + jX_s)$$

Taking $\vec{V}$ as reference:
$$\vec{E_0} = V\angle 0° + I_a(R_a\cos\phi + X_s\sin\phi) + jI_a(X_s\cos\phi - R_a\sin\phi)$$

**Magnitude of $E_0$:**

$$E_0 = \sqrt{[V + I_a(R_a\cos\phi + X_s\sin\phi)]^2 + [I_a(X_s\cos\phi - R_a\sin\phi)]^2}$$

**Voltage Regulation:**

$$\%VR = \frac{E_0 - V}{V} \times 100$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $S = 5\ \text{MVA}$, $V_L = 11\ \text{kV}$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging
- $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega/\text{phase}$, $X_s = 4\ \Omega/\text{phase}$

**(i) Synchronous Impedance:**

$$Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2} = \sqrt{(0.4)^2 + (4)^2} = \sqrt{0.16 + 16} = \sqrt{16.16}$$

$$\boxed{Z_s \approx 4.02 \quad [\Omega/\text{phase}]}$$

**Full-Load Current:**

$$I_L = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 11 \times 10^3}$$

$$I_L = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{19052.6} \approx 262.4 \quad [\text{A}]$$

For Y-connection: $I_{ph} = I_L = 262.4\ \text{A}$

**Phase Voltage:**

$$V_{ph} = \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{11000}{1.732} \approx 6350.85 \quad [\text{V}]$$

**(ii) No-Load EMF (per phase):**

$$\cos\phi = 0.8, \quad \sin\phi = 0.6$$

**Real component of internal drop:**
$$I_a(R_a\cos\phi + X_s\sin\phi) = 262.4 \times (0.4 \times 0.8 + 4 \times 0.6)$$
$$= 262.4 \times (0.32 + 2.4) = 262.4 \times 2.72 = 713.73 \quad [\text{V}]$$

**Imaginary component of internal drop:**
$$I_a(X_s\cos\phi - R_a\sin\phi) = 262.4 \times (4 \times 0.8 - 0.4 \times 0.6)$$
$$= 262.4 \times (3.2 - 0.24) = 262.4 \times 2.96 = 776.7 \quad [\text{V}]$$

**Total $E_0$:**
$$E_0 = \sqrt{(6350.85 + 713.73)^2 + (776.7)^2}$$

$$E_0 = \sqrt{(7064.58)^2 + (776.7)^2}$$

$$E_0 = \sqrt{49,908,288.6 + 603,262.9}$$

$$E_0 = \sqrt{50,511,551.5}$$

$$\boxed{E_0 \approx 7107.14 \quad [\text{V/phase}]}$$

**(iii) Percentage Voltage Regulation:**

$$\%VR = \frac{E_0 - V_{ph}}{V_{ph}} \times 100 = \frac{7107.14 - 6350.85}{6350.85} \times 100$$

$$\%VR = \frac{756.29}{6350.85} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\%VR \approx 11.91\%}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** in alternator problems due to the following mistakes:
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert Line to Phase values** — Always check whether the alternator is Y or Δ connected. For Y: $V_{ph} = V_L/\sqrt{3}$; for Δ: $V_{ph} = V_L$.
> 2. **Mixing up RMS and Average EMF** — The factor is **4.44** (RMS), not **2.22** (Average). Always confirm the question asks for RMS or peak.
> 3. **Sign error in voltage regulation** — Voltage regulation is **defined as $(E_0 - V)/V$**. Never write $(V - E_0)/V$.
> 4. **Using $I_a$ instead of $I_L$** — For 3-phase problems, ensure current is also phase-corrected.
> 5. **Power factor angle convention** — Always specify whether the load is **lagging** (inductive) or **leading** (capacitive). This changes the entire sign of the imaginary component.
> 6. **Skipping units in final answer** — KTU examiners deduct marks if the final numerical answer is presented without units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Alternator (KTU Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Alternator = Synchronous Generator; converts mechanical → AC electrical energy.
- **Synchronous Speed Formula:** $N_s = 120f/P$ — memorize in seconds.
- **EMF Equation:** $E_{ph} = 4.44 \, K_w \, \Phi \, f \, T_{ph}$ — *the* most important equation.
- **Winding Factor:** $K_w = K_p \cdot K_d$, typically **0.95–0.98**.
- **Pitch Factor:** $K_p = \cos(\alpha/2)$ where $\alpha$ = short-pitch angle.
- **Distribution Factor:** $K_d = \sin(m\beta/2)/[m\sin(\beta/2)]$.
- **Synchronous Impedance:** $Z_s = \sqrt{R_a^2 + X_s^2}$.
- **Synchronous Reactance:** $X_s = X_a + X_l$ (armature reaction + leakage).
- **Voltage Regulation:** $\%VR = (E_0 - V)/V \times 100$ — for lagging pf, $E_0 > V$.
- **Power Output (3-φ):** $P = \sqrt{3} \, V_L \, I_L \cos\phi$.
- **Standard Grid Frequency:** India = **50 Hz**, USA = **60 Hz**.
- **Rotor Types:** Cylindrical (high-speed, turbo-alternators); Salient Pole (low-speed, hydro).
- **Cooling Methods:** Air, Hydrogen (large machines), Water (stator windings).
- **Excitation:** DC via slip rings; modern alternators use **brushless excitation** (rotating diode rectifier).
- **Synchronization Conditions (3 dark lamps / synchroscope):**
  1. Same voltage magnitude
  2. Same frequency
  3. Same phase sequence
  4. Zero phase angle difference
- **AVR Function:** Maintains terminal voltage by adjusting field excitation $I_f$.
- **Governor Function:** Maintains speed (and hence frequency) by controlling prime mover input.
- **Negative Regulation:** Occurs at **leading power factor** (capacitive load), where $V > E_0$.
- **KTU 2024 Module Weightage:** Alternator typically carries **15–20%** of the total Module-2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Water Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Water Conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feed Water Conditioning** is the systematic chemical and physical treatment of boiler feed water to remove, neutralize, or convert harmful impurities (dissolved gases, hardness salts, silica, iron, and organic matter) into a form that prevents **scale formation**, **corrosion**, **foaming**, and **priming** within the boiler, superheater, and turbine circuit.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT523 - Power Plant Instrumentation)**, feed water conditioning is classified into two major engineering domains:

1. **External Treatment (Pre-Boiler Treatment):** Performed *outside* the boiler using mechanical and ion-exchange systems (clarification, filtration, softening, demineralization, deaeration).
2. **Internal Treatment (In-Boiler Chemical Conditioning):** Performed *inside* the boiler circuit through direct chemical dosing (phosphate, hydrazine, ammonia, sulfite, neutralizing amines) to maintain water chemistry within prescribed limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to differentiate between *External* and *Internal* treatment, identify instrumentation used (conductivity meters, pH analyzers, dissolved oxygen probes, silica monitors, sodium analyzers), and explain the chemistry of common conditioning reagents.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the human circulatory system. Blood must be **filtered** (kidneys) before it reaches the heart; if dirt, salts, or air bubbles enter, the heart (your boiler) gets damaged. Now, replace the heart with a high-pressure boiler, the blood with feed water, and the kidneys with a chain of treatment plants. The "dirt" in our analogy is **hardness (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺)**, **dissolved oxygen (O₂)**, **silica (SiO₂)**, **iron (Fe)**, and **free CO₂**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Feed water conditioning is essentially *water polishing* — it makes raw water "boiler-grade" by removing anything that could stick to, eat through, or carry over from the steam-generating surfaces.

---

## 1.3 Why Feed Water Conditioning is Non-Negotiable

| Problem | Consequence | KTU Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Scale Formation** | Reduced heat transfer, tube overheating, tube failure | Module 2, CO₂ |
| **Corrosion** | Tube wall thinning, boiler explosion risk | Module 2, CO₂ |
| **Carryover & Foaming** | Turbine blade erosion, deposition on blades | Module 2, CO₃ |
| **Caustic Embrittlement** | Inter-granular cracking at riveted/stressed joints | Module 2, CO₂ |
| **Condensate Line Attack** | Return-line corrosion by CO₂ and O₂ | Module 2, CO₃ |

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Industrial Limits)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Feed Water Limits (Modern High-Pressure Drum Boilers, ~150-200 bar):**
> - **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** < **0.5 mg/L** in drum water
> - **pH:** **8.5 – 9.5** (slightly alkaline)
> - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** < **0.007 mg/L (7 ppb)**
> - **Silica (SiO₂):** < **0.02 mg/L**
> - **Iron (Fe):** < **0.02 mg/L**
> - **Conductivity:** < **0.2 µS/cm** (after cation conductivity)
> - **Hardness:** Essentially **zero** (< 0.001 mg/L as CaCO₃)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Feed Water Conditioning

### Pillar A: External Treatment (Pre-Boiler)

The journey of water from source to boiler goes through a sequential "polishing" train:

1. **Pre-chlorination** — Kills biological organisms in raw water.
2. **Coagulation & Flocculation** — Alum [Al₂(SO₄)₃] or FeCl₃ binds suspended particles.
3. **Sedimentation** — Gravity settling in clarifiers.
4. **Filtration** — Removes remaining suspended solids (sand, activated carbon, multimedia).
5. **Lime-Soda Softening** — Chemical precipitation of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ as carbonates and hydroxides.
6. **Ion-Exchange Softening / Demineralization (D.M. Plant)** — Removes all ionic impurities using cation and anion exchangers.
7. **Degasification (Deaeration)** — Removes dissolved O₂ and CO₂.
8. **Mixed Bed Polisher** — Final ionic polishing for ultra-pure water.

### Pillar B: Internal Treatment (In-Boiler)

After external treatment, trace impurities still exist. Internal treatment handles these:

1. **Phosphate Treatment** — Prevents scale by converting residual hardness into a non-adherent sludge (sodium phosphate).
2. **Hydrazine (N₂H₄) Treatment** — Scavenges residual dissolved oxygen.
3. **Sulfite Treatment (Na₂SO₃)** — Alternative oxygen scavenger (not used in high-pressure boilers).
4. **Amine Treatment** — Neutralizes CO₂ in condensate lines, raising pH to prevent corrosion.
5. **Coordinated Phosphate / Congruent Control** — Maintains Na/PO₄ ratio to prevent caustic embrittlement.

---

## 2.2 Chemistry of Key Reactions

> [!NOTE]
> **Lime-Soda Softening Reactions:**
> $$\text{Ca(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 + \text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
> $$\text{Mg(HCO}_3\text{)}_2 + 2\text{Ca(OH)}_2 \longrightarrow \text{Mg(OH)}_2 \downarrow + 2\text{CaCO}_3 \downarrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Demineralization (Ion-Exchange):**
> Cation exchanger: $\text{2HR} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$
> Anion exchanger: $\text{ROH} + \text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{RCl} + \text{OH}^-$
> Net: $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (de-ionized pure water)

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydrazine Oxygen Scavenging:**
> $$\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sodium Sulfite Oxygen Scavenging (Low-Pressure Only):**
> $$2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_3 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Phosphate Treatment (Coordinated):**
> $$10\text{Ca}^{2+} + 6\text{PO}_4^{3-} + 2\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6(\text{OH})_2 \downarrow \text{(hydroxyapatite sludge)}$$

---

## 2.3 The Deaeration Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Henry's Law Foundation of Deaeration:**
> The dissolved oxygen concentration in water at equilibrium is directly proportional to the partial pressure of O₂ in the gas phase above the water.

$$C_{O_2} = k_H \cdot P_{O_2}$$

where:
- $C_{O_2}$ = concentration of dissolved oxygen (mg/L)
- $k_H$ = Henry's constant (temperature-dependent)
- $P_{O_2}$ = partial pressure of O₂ above the water (atm)

**Engineering Implication:** By **heating the water to 105 °C – 110 °C** and **scrubbing it with low-pressure steam**, $P_{O_2}$ above the water is driven to near zero. By Henry's Law, dissolved O₂ then diffuses out of solution, achieving **< 7 ppb**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Exam — Memorize These:**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Henry's Law (DO removal) | $C = k_H \cdot P$ | $C$ in mg/L, $P$ in atm |
| 2 | Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) | $\text{LSI} = \text{pH}_\text{actual} - \text{pH}_\text{sat}$ | Negative = corrosive, Positive = scaling |
| 3 | Cycles of Concentration (COC) | $\text{COC} = \dfrac{C_\text{drum}}{C_\text{feed}}$ | Typical: 8–12 for high-pressure boilers |
| 4 | Make-up Water % | $\%\text{MUW} = \dfrac{1}{\text{COC}} \times 100$ | Smaller COC = more blowdown |
| 5 | Phosphate Demand | $P_d = 0.0083 \times \text{Ca}^{2+}_{(\text{mg/L})}$ | mg/L of PO₄³⁻ required |
| 6 | Hydrazine Dose | $\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 = 1.5 \times \text{DO}_{(\text{mg/L})}$ | Theoretical, +0.2 mg/L residual |
| 7 | Conductivity → TDS | $\text{TDS}_{(\text{mg/L})} \approx 0.55 \times \text{EC}_{(\mu\text{S/cm})}$ | For KCl calibration |
| 8 | Silica Carryover Limit | $\text{SiO}_2_{\text{steam}} = \dfrac{\text{SiO}_2_{\text{drum}}}{\text{COC}}$ | Must stay < 0.02 mg/L in turbine |
| 9 | pOH from pH | $\text{pOH} = 14 - \text{pH}$ | At 25 °C |
| 10 | Hardness as CaCO₃ | $\text{H} = \dfrac{100}{100} \times \text{Ca}^{2+}_{(\text{mg/L})}$ | Ca: 100/40, Mg: 100/24.3 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain" or "discuss" feed water conditioning, **always structure your answer in two parts** (External + Internal). Examiners in PEICT523 explicitly look for this dichotomy — failing to separate the two costs 2–3 marks.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Power Plant Engineering

- **Thermal Power Plants (Fossil & Nuclear):** 100% of drum-type boilers > 60 bar require full demineralization + deaeration + chemical conditioning.
- **Combined Cycle Plants (HRSG):** Sophisticated coordinated phosphate + amine programs protect once-through and drum HRSGs.
- **Process Industries:** Refineries, petrochemicals, and paper mills reuse the same principles for their high-pressure steam networks.
- **Geothermal & Solar Thermal:** Modified conditioning schemes handle silica-rich and high-TDS source waters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Make-Up Water from Cycles of Concentration

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A boiler has a drum water conductivity of **1200 µS/cm** and feed water conductivity of **100 µS/cm**. Compute the cycles of concentration and minimum make-up water percentage.

### Step 1 — State the definition
By definition, the **Cycles of Concentration (COC)** is the ratio of dissolved solids (or conductivity, as a proxy) in the drum to that in the feed water.

$$\text{COC} = \dfrac{C_\text{drum}}{C_\text{feed}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values
Plug in $C_\text{drum} = 1200\ \mu\text{S/cm}$ and $C_\text{feed} = 100\ \mu\text{S/cm}$:

$$\text{COC} = \dfrac{1200\ \mu\text{S/cm}}{100\ \mu\text{S/cm}}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify
Since the units cancel (conductivity is used as a dimensionless ratio when both are measured the same way), we obtain:

$$\boxed{\text{COC} = 12}$$

> This is a **text row** explaining the step: the ratio is unitless because conductivity is a *relative* measurement, and we get a clean integer ratio of 12.

### Step 4 — Compute Make-up Water Percentage
For a steady-state boiler system, the make-up water fraction is the inverse of the COC:

$$\%\text{MUW} = \dfrac{1}{\text{COC}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1}{12} \times 100$$

### Step 5 — Final Numerical Result
$$\boxed{\%\text{MUW} \approx 8.33\%}$$

> **Interpretation:** Approximately **8.33%** of the total water input must be fresh make-up water; the rest (~91.67%) is recirculated condensate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Silica Carryover Verification

> **Problem:** Drum water silica is **0.30 mg/L** and the boiler operates at **COC = 10**. Will the silica in steam exceed the **0.02 mg/L** turbine limit?

### Step 1 — Apply the carryover relation
$$\text{SiO}_{2\,(\text{steam})} = \dfrac{\text{SiO}_{2\,(\text{drum})}}{\text{COC}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute
$$\text{SiO}_{2\,(\text{steam})} = \dfrac{0.30\ \text{mg/L}}{10}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate
$$\text{SiO}_{2\,(\text{steam})} = 0.030\ \text{mg/L}$$

### Step 4 — Compare to limit
$$0.030\ \text{mg/L} > 0.020\ \text{mg/L} \ \Rightarrow\ \textbf{NON-COMPLIANT}$$

> **Engineering Action Required:** Increase condensate return rate, decrease COC to 8, or upgrade the anion exchanger in the D.M. plant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Hydrazine Dose for Oxygen Scavenging

> **Problem:** Feed water contains **0.20 mg/L** of dissolved oxygen. Calculate the stoichiometric hydrazine dose and the practical residual dose required for a boiler.

### Step 1 — Write the balanced reaction
$$\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 2 — Determine molar mass ratios
- $M(\text{N}_2\text{H}_4) = 32\ \text{g/mol}$
- $M(\text{O}_2) = 32\ \text{g/mol}$

### Step 3 — Compute stoichiometric ratio
Since 1 mol N₂H₄ reacts with 1 mol O₂ (1:1 molar ratio), the mass ratio is:

$$\dfrac{m(\text{N}_2\text{H}_4)}{m(\text{O}_2)} = \dfrac{32}{32} = 1$$

### Step 4 — Compute stoichiometric dose
$$m(\text{N}_2\text{H}_4) = 1 \times 0.20\ \text{mg/L} = 0.20\ \text{mg/L}$$

### Step 5 — Add practical safety residual
Industry standard residual N₂H₄ in feed water = **0.05 – 0.20 mg/L**.

$$m_\text{practical} = 0.20 + 0.10 = \boxed{0.30\ \text{mg/L of N}_2\text{H}_4}$$

> **Interpretation:** Approximately **0.30 mg/L of hydrazine** must be dosed to fully scavenge 0.20 mg/L of DO and maintain a protective residual.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Conditioning Method

| Plant Type | Pressure (bar) | External Treatment | Internal Treatment | Key Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lancashire / Cochran (small industrial) | < 10 | Softening only | Sulfite, phosphate | Conductivity meter |
| Fire-tube boiler | 10 – 25 | Softening + filtration | Phosphate + sulfite | pH + conductivity |
| Water-tube (sub-critical) | 60 – 100 | D.M. + Deaerator | Coordinated phosphate | Na analyzer, PO₄ analyzer |
| Super-critical / once-through | > 170 | D.M. + Mixed Bed | All-volatile (AVT) | Dissolved O₂, conductivity, silica |
| Nuclear PWR (secondary) | 60 – 70 | D.M. + condensate polish | Morpholine / ETA amine | Cation conductivity, pH |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Blowdown Rate from COC

> **Problem:** A 500 TPH boiler operates at COC = 8. Compute the continuous blowdown rate (assuming 90% condensate return).

### Step 1 — Mass balance on water
Make-up water (MUW) flow = Evaporation + Blowdown
With 90% condensate return:
$$\text{MUW} = \text{Evaporation} + \text{Blowdown} = 500 + B$$
But MUW also = 10% of total feed, so feed flow = 500 + B, and MUW = 0.10(500 + B).

### Step 2 — Equate
$$0.10(500 + B) = B$$
$$50 + 0.10B = B$$
$$50 = 0.90B$$

### Step 3 — Solve
$$B = \dfrac{50}{0.90} = 55.56\ \text{TPH}$$

### Step 4 — Verify using COC
$$\text{COC} = \dfrac{\text{Feed}}{\text{Blowdown}} = \dfrac{500 + 55.56}{55.56} = \dfrac{555.56}{55.56} = 10$$

> **Note:** A direct relation between COC and blowdown is $\text{COC} = \dfrac{\text{Feed}}{\text{BD}}$ and for 90% condensate return, MUW = 10% of feed.

$$\text{Therefore,}\ B = \dfrac{\text{Evaporation}}{\text{COC} - 1} = \dfrac{500}{10 - 1} = 55.56\ \text{TPH}\ \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Feed Water Conditioning Block Diagram (Pre-Boiler → Boiler → Turbine)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **end-to-end flow** of feed water from raw source to the boiler and into the turbine, highlighting where each treatment/conditioning stage acts and which online instrument monitors that stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawA[Raw Water Source] --> chlA[Pre Chlorination]
    chlA --> clrA[Clarifier Coagulation Flocculation]
    clrA --> filA[Filtration Multimedia]
    filA --> sofA[Lime Soda Softening]
    sofA --> dma[Demineralization Plant DM]
    dma --> ana[Anion Exchanger]
    ana --> mbp[Mixed Bed Polisher]
    mbp --> dea[Deaerator Heated]
    dea --> eca[Economiser Inlet]
    eca --> drb[Boiler Drum]
    drb --> sh1[Superheater]
    sh1 --> rh1[Reheater]
    rh1 --> tbn[Turbine HP LP]
    tbn --> cnd[Condenser]
    cnd --> clp[Condensate Pump]
    clp --> cdp[Condensate Polisher]
    cdp --> dfg[Deaerator Storage]
    dfg --> eca

    insA[Online Conductivity Meter] -.Monitors.-> mbp
    insB[Dissolved Oxygen Probe] -.Monitors.-> dea
    insC[pH Analyzer] -.Monitors.-> dma
    insD[Sodium Analyzer] -.Monitors.-> drb
    insE[Silica Monitor] -.Monitors.-> rh1
    insF[Phosphate Analyzer] -.Monitors.-> drb

    chem1[Hydrazine Dosing] -.Internal.-> dea
    chem2[Phosphate Dosing] -.Internal.-> drb
    chem3[Amine Dosing] -.Internal.-> clp
```

---

## 4.2 Internal Treatment Decision Tree

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram helps KTU students decide **which internal treatment to apply** based on the identified water chemistry issue.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Trace Impurity Detected in Feed Water] --> q1{Type of Impurity}

    q1 -- Residual Hardness Ca Mg --> n1[Phosphate Treatment]
    q1 -- Dissolved Oxygen --> n2[Hydrazine or Sulfite]
    q1 -- Acidic CO2 in Condensate --> n3[Amine Treatment Morpholine ETA]
    q1 -- Caustic Embrittlement Risk --> n4[Coordinated Phosphate Na to PO4 Ratio]
    q1 -- High Silica Carryover --> n5[Improve DM Plant Increase Blowdown]

    n1 --> outA[Sludge Forms in Boiler Removed via Blowdown]
    n2 --> outB[Oxygen Removed as N2 Gas and H2O]
    n3 --> outC[Condensate pH Raised to 8.5 to 9.0]
    n4 --> outD[Free NaOH Eliminated Stress Corrosion Cracking Prevented]
    n5 --> outE[Steam Silica Below 0.02 mg per L]
```

---

## 4.3 Instrument Loop Architecture (KTU-Favored Diagram)

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram is a **block-level functional architecture** mapping how field analyzers, controllers, and dosing pumps interlock for **automatic feed water conditioning** — a common KTU 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fieldA[Dissolved Oxygen Probe] --> txA[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
    txA --> dcsA[DCS PLC Analog Input]
    dcsA --> ctrlA[PID Controller in DCS]
    ctrlA --> doA[Dosing Pump Hydrazine]
    doA --> deaV[Deaerator Storage Tank]

    fieldB[pH Sensor] --> txB[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
    txB --> dcsB[DCS PLC Analog Input]
    dcsB --> ctrlB[PID Controller in DCS]
    ctrlB --> doB[Amine Dosing Pump]
    doB --> cndV[Condensate Line]

    fieldC[Conductivity Sensor] --> txC[Transmitter 4 to 20 mA]
    txC --> dcsC[DCS PLC Analog Input]
    dcsC --> ctrlC[PID Controller in DCS]
    ctrlC --> vlvA[ Blowdown Control Valve ]
    vlvA --> bdv[Continuous Blowdown]

    dcsA --> hmiA[Operator HMI Alarm Logging]
    dcsB --> hmiA
    dcsC --> hmiA
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each loop has **Sensor → Transmitter → Controller → Final Control Element → Process**. KTU examiners often ask students to "draw a closed loop for dissolved oxygen control" — this is the exact architecture.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Deaerator Types

| Deaerator Type | Operating Principle | Application | DO Outlet |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Spray-Type Tray Deaerator** | Water sprayed through steam + tray contact | Medium & large utility boilers | < 7 ppb |
| **Pressure Deaerator (DA)** | Water heated to 105–110 °C, vacuum/pressure | All modern thermal plants | < 5 ppb |
| **Atmospheric Deaerator** | Open tank, steam sparging | Industrial low-pressure boilers | 20–30 ppb |
| **Vacuum Deaerator** | Lower temperature operation, vacuum pump | Once-through HRSG, nuclear | < 10 ppb |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEICT523]`
**Differentiate between external and internal treatment of boiler feed water with suitable examples.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | External Treatment | Internal Treatment |
> |---|---|---|
> | Location | Outside the boiler (pre-boiler) | Inside the boiler / steam cycle |
> | Method | Physical + ion-exchange (softening, DM, deaeration) | Chemical dosing (phosphate, hydrazine, amine) |
> | Examples | Lime-soda softening, D.M. plant, deaerator, mixed bed polisher | Phosphate treatment, hydrazine dosing, amine treatment |
> | Purpose | Bulk removal of hardness, TDS, O₂, CO₂, SiO₂ | Polish trace impurities, scavenge residual O₂, prevent scaling/corrosion |
> | Equipment | Clarifier, filters, exchangers, deaerator | Chemical dosing tanks, dosing pumps |
>
> **Conclusion (1 mark):** External treatment is the *first line of defense*; internal treatment is the *second line*, working synergistically to maintain boiler water chemistry within limits.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEICT523]`
**Explain the role of a deaerator in feed water conditioning. State the typical outlet dissolved oxygen limit.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Role (2 marks):** A deaerator removes dissolved **oxygen (O₂)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** from feed water by heating it to 105–110 °C and scrubbing it with low-pressure steam. The process is governed by **Henry's Law** — reducing the partial pressure of O₂ in the gas phase drives dissolved O₂ out of the water. This prevents pitting corrosion, boiler tube failure, and condensate line corrosion.
> 2. **Typical DO limit (1 mark):** Outlet dissolved oxygen must be **< 0.007 mg/L (7 ppb)** for high-pressure boilers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Internal Choice (As per KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **any ONE** of the following:
> - **Q(A):** External Treatment of Feed Water
> - **Q(B):** Internal Treatment of Feed Water

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / 2024 - Module Choice]`

**Q(A)(a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **external treatment train** of feed water in a modern high-pressure thermal power plant. List the major impurities removed at each stage.

**Q(A)(b) [7 marks]:** Describe the working of a **D.M. (Demineralization) plant** with ion-exchange reactions. State the typical treated water conductivity and silica limits.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q(A)(a) — 7 Marks**

> **Introduction (1 mark):** External treatment refers to all purification processes applied to water *before* it enters the boiler. The aim is to produce water of extremely high purity suitable for high-pressure operation.

**Stage-by-stage explanation (5 marks):**

1. **Pre-Chlorination** — Cl₂ or NaOCl dosed to oxidize and kill biological matter.
2. **Coagulation-Flocculation** — Al₂(SO₄)₃ or FeCl₃ added; colloids agglomerate into settleable flocs.
3. **Sedimentation in Clarifier** — Gravity settling removes flocs and turbidity.
4. **Filtration** — Sand + anthracite + garnet multimedia filters polish suspended solids to < 1 NTU.
5. **Lime-Soda Softening** — Ca(OH)₂ + Na₂CO₃ precipitate Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ as CaCO₃ and Mg(OH)₂ sludge.
6. **Demineralization Plant (DM)** — Two-stage ion exchange:
   - **Cation exchanger (HR):** $2\text{HR} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \to \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$
   - **Anion exchanger (ROH):** $\text{ROH} + \text{Cl}^- \to \text{RCl} + \text{OH}^-$
   - **Mixed Bed Polisher:** Final removal of trace ions → conductivity < 0.2 µS/cm.
7. **Deaeration (Tray-type, 105 °C)** — Removes DO and CO₂ → DO < 7 ppb.

**Major impurities removed (1 mark):** Suspended solids, turbidity, hardness (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺), alkalinity, sulfates, chlorides, dissolved gases (O₂, CO₂), silica, and color/odor.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q(A)(b) — 7 Marks**

**Working of DM Plant (4 marks):**

> A **D.M. Plant** has **two columns**:
> 1. **Cation Exchanger** — Resin in H⁺ form exchanges H⁺ for all cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Fe³⁺).
>    - Reaction: $\text{2HR} + \text{Ca}^{2+} \longrightarrow \text{CaR}_2 + 2\text{H}^+$
> 2. **Anion Exchanger** — Resin in OH⁻ form exchanges OH⁻ for all anions (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, NO₃⁻).
>    - Reaction: $\text{ROH} + \text{Cl}^- \longrightarrow \text{RCl} + \text{OH}^-$
> 3. **Mixed Bed Polisher** — Mixed cation + anion resin for **final ionic polishing**.

The H⁺ and OH⁻ ions released combine:
$$\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

This produces **de-mineralized (de-ionized) water** virtually free of all dissolved ionic impurities.

**Regeneration (2 marks):**
- Cation column regenerated with **dilute H₂SO₄ or HCl** (H⁺ form restored).
- Anion column regenerated with **dilute NaOH** (OH⁻ form restored).

**Treated water limits (1 mark):**
- Conductivity: < **0.2 µS/cm** (cation conductivity basis)
- Silica (SiO₂): < **0.02 mg/L**
- pH: **6.5 – 7.5** (neutral)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module Choice]`

**Q(B)(a) [7 marks]:** Explain the **phosphate treatment** of boiler water. Why is **coordinated phosphate** preferred over simple phosphate treatment in high-pressure boilers?

**Q(B)(b) [7 marks]:** Describe the **hydrazine and amine treatment** methods with their chemical reactions. State typical residual limits.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q(B)(a) — 7 Marks**

**Phosphate Treatment (4 marks):**

> Sodium phosphate (Na₃PO₄) is dosed into the boiler drum. It reacts with any residual hardness ions (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) entering the boiler to form a **non-adherent, non-scale-forming sludge** of **hydroxyapatite** and **magnesium phosphate**, which is removed via **blowdown**.

**Reaction:**
$$10\text{Ca}^{2+} + 6\text{PO}_4^{3-} + 2\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \text{Ca}_{10}(\text{PO}_4)_6(\text{OH})_2 \downarrow$$
$$3\text{Mg}^{2+} + 2\text{PO}_4^{3-} \longrightarrow \text{Mg}_3(\text{PO}_4)_2 \downarrow$$

**Why Coordinated Phosphate? (3 marks):**

> In **simple phosphate treatment**, excess Na₃PO₄ hydrolyzes to form **free NaOH**:
> $$\text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{HPO}_4 + \text{NaOH}$$
>
> The free NaOH accumulates in stressed boiler joints, leading to **caustic embrittlement** (inter-granular stress corrosion cracking).
>
> In **coordinated phosphate treatment**, the Na/PO₄ molar ratio is controlled between **2.6 : 1 and 2.85 : 1** (avoiding free NaOH). This is achieved by blending **Na₃PO₄ (trisodium)** and **Na₂HPO₄ (disodium)** phosphates, keeping pH within **9.0 – 9.6**.
>
> **Benefit:** Eliminates free caustic, prevents embrittlement, and maintains the protective magnetite (Fe₃O₄) layer on boiler tubes.

---

#### **Model Answer for Q(B)(b) — 7 Marks**

**Hydrazine Treatment (3.5 marks):**

> Hydrazine (N₂H₄) is a **volatile oxygen scavenger** dosed into the feed line or deaerator storage. Unlike sulfite, it decomposes into harmless gaseous products — **N₂ and H₂O** — leaving no dissolved solids in the steam.

**Reaction:**
$$\text{N}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{N}_2 \uparrow + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Stoichiometric dose:** 1.0 mg/L N₂H₄ per 1.0 mg/L O₂; practical dose = O₂ + 0.1–0.2 mg/L residual.
**Residual limit:** **0.05 – 0.20 mg/L N₂H₄** in feed water.

**Advantage:** Volatile → does not add solids; suitable for **high-pressure and supercritical boilers**.
**Caution:** Hydrazine is **carcinogenic**; modern plants increasingly use **DEHA (diethylhydroxylamine)** or **carbohydrazide** as safer alternatives.

**Amine Treatment (3.5 marks):**

> Amines (e.g., **morpholine**, **cyclohexylamine**, **ethanolamine**) are **neutralizing amines** dosed into the steam to protect the **condensate return lines** from **CO₂ corrosion**.

**Mechanism:**
- CO₂ in steam dissolves in condensate to form carbonic acid:
$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$$
- The amine neutralizes this acid:
$$\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{Morpholine} \longrightarrow \text{Morpholine}\cdot\text{HCO}_3$$
- The condensate pH is raised to **8.5 – 9.0**, preventing corrosion.

**Selection of amine:** Based on **distribution ratio** — amines with low distribution ratio (e.g., morpholine) protect the entire system including far ends; amines with high distribution ratio (e.g., cyclohexylamine) protect only near the boiler.

**Residual limit:** Condensate pH = **8.5 – 9.0**; amine residual = **1.0 – 3.0 mg/L**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse demineralization with softening.** Softening (Na-cycle) replaces hardness with Na⁺ — it does *not* remove all ions. Demineralization (H⁺ + OH⁻) removes *all* ions. (3-mark penalty)
> 2. **Hydrazine ≠ Sulfite for high-pressure boilers.** Sulfite adds Na₂SO₄ solids → unsafe above 60 bar. (2-mark penalty)
> 3. **In 14-mark answers, always include a labeled block diagram.** KTU examiners for PEICT523 are instructed to award up to **2 marks** for a clear, well-labeled schematic.
> 4. **State Henry's Law explicitly** when explaining the deaerator — it is a favorite 1-mark "bonus" question.
> 5. **Units matter:** Always state DO in **ppb (or mg/L)**, conductivity in **µS/cm**, silica in **mg/L**, pH as a **dimensionless number**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam (PEICT523 – Module 2):**

- ☐ **Feed Water Conditioning = External + Internal** (always mention both).
- ☐ **External Treatment Train:** Chlorination → Clarification → Filtration → Softening → DM Plant → Mixed Bed → Deaerator.
- ☐ **DM Plant reaction:** $\text{H}^+ + \text{OH}^- \to \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (key takeaway).
- ☐ **Deaerator limits:** **< 7 ppb DO**, operating temperature **105–110 °C**, governed by **Henry's Law**.
- ☐ **Phosphate treatment** forms hydroxyapatite sludge, removed via blowdown.
- ☐ **Coordinated phosphate:** Na/PO₄ ratio = **2.6 – 2.85 : 1** to prevent caustic embrittlement.
- ☐ **Hydrazine:** N₂H₄ + O₂ → N₂↑ + 2H₂O (volatile, no solids added).
- ☐ **Sulfite** only for **low-pressure boilers** (< 60 bar).
- ☐ **Amine treatment** protects **condensate lines** from CO₂ corrosion; target pH = **8.5 – 9.0**.
- ☐ **Online Analyzers:** Conductivity, pH, DO, silica, sodium, phosphate, cation conductivity.
- ☐ **Cycles of Concentration (COC):** $\text{COC} = C_\text{drum}/C_\text{feed}$; **COC = Feed/BD**.
- ☐ **Make-up Water %** = 1/COC × 100.
- ☐ **Silica Carryover:** $\text{SiO}_{2\,\text{steam}} = \text{SiO}_{2\,\text{drum}} / \text{COC}$, must be < **0.02 mg/L**.
- ☐ **Key instruments:** Dissolved oxygen probe, conductivity meter, pH meter, silica monitor, sodium analyzer, phosphate analyzer, hydrazine analyzer.
- ☐ **LSI (Langelier Saturation Index):** negative = corrosive, positive = scaling.
- ☐ **Hardness** expressed as **mg/L as CaCO₃** (Ca: 100/40, Mg: 100/24.3).
- ☐ **Drawing a closed loop** (Sensor → Transmitter → Controller → Dosing Pump → Process) earns easy **2 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turbine Bypass Valves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Turbine Bypass Valves ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Turbine Bypass Valve (TBV)** is a specially designed, large-capacity pressure-control valve assembly used in steam power plants (subcritical, supercritical, and combined-cycle plants) to divert a portion or the entire mass flow of live steam **around** the steam turbine (High-Pressure turbine, Intermediate-Pressure turbine, or Low-Pressure turbine) directly to the next thermodynamic stage — typically the reheater, the condenser, or the feedwater heating train — during **startup, shutdown, load rejection, turbine trip, and emergency conditions**. The bypass system is not merely a relief path; it is a **process-control instrument** that: - Maintains boiler steam pressure within safe limits when the turbine cannot accept full steam flow. - Enables **fast plant start-up** by allowing the boiler to operate at full firing rate while the turbine is still cold. - Protects the turbine from excessive **thermal stress, overspeed, and wet-steam erosion** in the LP stages. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEICT523, Module 2):** > Turbine Bypass Valves are studied under the unit *"Boiler and Turbine Instrumentation and Control"*. Students must understand the **function, types (HP / IP / LP bypass), control philosophy, desuperheating spray arrangement, sizing equations, and integration with the Boiler-Turbine-Generator (BTG) control loop**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine a high-pressure water pipeline feeding a giant water wheel (turbine). When the wheel is **cold and stationary**, you cannot suddenly blast it with full hot water pressure — the wheel would crack (thermal shock) and the water would hammer the blades. So engineers install a **parallel diversion pipe** with a giant controllable valve right next to the wheel. During startup, this valve is **wide open**, dumping hot water into a holding tank (condenser or reheater), while the wheel is slowly and gently rotated. Once the wheel is warm and spinning at proper speed, the bypass valve is **gradually closed** and steam is routed through the turbine. > **Turbine Bypass Valve = The "Escape Route" for steam when the turbine is not ready to accept it.** ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value (Subcritical/Supercritical Plant) | |---|---| | **Inlet steam pressure (P₁)** | **100 – 250 bar** (HP bypass), **30 – 60 bar** (IP/LP bypass) | | **Inlet steam temperature (T₁)** | **500 – 600 °C** | | **Outlet pressure (P₂)** | Downstream stage pressure (reheater / condenser) | | **Valve size (nominal bore)** | **DN 150 to DN 800** (typical industrial range) | | **Cv / Kv range** | **High capacity**, Kv 500 – 12 000 | | **Response time** | **< 3 – 5 seconds** for full stroke | | **Adiabatic index (k) for steam** | **1.30 (superheated)**, **1.135 (saturated)** | > [!NOTE] > A turbine bypass system is often **100% capacity** — meaning the valve must pass the *entire* boiler output with the turbine fully isolated. This distinguishes it from a simple safety relief valve. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Turbine Bypass Systems Bypass valves are classified primarily by the stage of the Rankine / reheat cycle they service: #### (a) High-Pressure (HP) Bypass - **Location:** Between the boiler superheater outlet and the HP turbine inlet. - **Function:** Diverts superheated live steam to the **cold reheat line** and thence to the IP/LP turbine. - **Special feature:** A **desuperheater (spray valve)** injects feedwater into the bypassed steam to drop its temperature (typically from **600 °C** to **~320 – 350 °C**) so it is compatible with the cold reheat piping metallurgy. #### (b) Intermediate-Pressure (IP) Bypass - **Location:** Between the hot reheat outlet and the IP turbine inlet. - **Function:** Diverts reheated steam to the cross-over or condenser during turbine trips. - **Desuperheater:** Usually a second stage of spray is installed. #### (c) Low-Pressure (LP) Bypass - **Location:** Between the IP turbine exhaust and the LP turbine inlet, or LP exhaust to condenser. - **Function:** Protects LP blades from wet-steam erosion during low-load operation; used for **drainage to condenser**. ### 2.2 Functional Block Decomposition The TBV system, when analyzed in a KTU exam context, decomposes into these functional blocks: 1. **Bypass Control Valve (BCV)** — The main throttling element, usually a **globe / angle-type valve** with pneumatic or hydraulic actuator. 2. **Desuperheater / Spray Water Control Valve (SWCV)** — A small modulating valve that injects feedwater (or condensate) for temperature control. 3. **Pressure Transmitter (PT)** — Located upstream; senses boiler steam pressure; feeds the bypass pressure controller. 4. **Temperature Transmitter (TT)** — Located downstream; senses outlet temperature; closes the spray loop. 5. **Flow Transmitter (FT)** — Sometimes used in feedforward trim. 6. **Pressure Controller (PIC)** — Master loop; throttles the BCV to hold P₁. 7. **Temperature Controller (TIC)** — Slave loop; modulates SWCV to hold T₂. 8. **Interlock Logic** — Blocks bypass opening on certain turbine trip conditions; enables it on load rejection. ### 2.3 Operating Modes and "Why / How" Logic | Mode | Why Bypass is Needed | How the System Responds | |---|---|---| | **Cold start-up** | Turbine is cold; cannot accept hot steam | BCV fully open, spray ON, steam routed to cold reheat | | **Warm start-up** | Turbine is warm; partial flow needed | BCV modulated to maintain boiler pressure | | **Hot start-up / Fast start** | Time-critical; boiler fired to full | BCV modulates to hold P₁, then closes as turbine rolls | | **Turbine trip** | Steam must be dumped instantly | BCV opens within **2–5 s** to prevent boiler overpressure | | **Load rejection** | Sudden loss of electrical load | BCV modulates to absorb excess steam | | **Normal full load** | All steam goes through turbine | BCV fully closed | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **pressure-control loop** is the **master** loop, and the **temperature-control loop** is the **slave**. In KTU problems, students are often asked to draw the cascade block diagram and identify master/slave variables. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $$\dfrac{P_{2,\text{crit}}}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{k}{k-1}}$$ | Critical pressure ratio for choked flow through valve | | 2 | $$\dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{R \, T_1}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{k+1}{2(k-1)}}$$ | Mass flow rate when valve is **choked** (sonic) | | 3 | $$\dot{m} = A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2k}{(k-1) R T_1}} \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{2}{k}} - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k+1}{k}}}$$ | Mass flow rate for **subsonic** (unchoked) condition | | 4 | $$\dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot \dfrac{h_{\text{steam,in}} - h_{\text{steam,out}}}{h_{\text{spray,in}} - h_{\text{steam,out}}}$$ | Spray water mass flow to achieve target T_out | | 5 | $$\dot{Q}_{\text{dump}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot \Delta h_{\text{isenthalpic}}$$ | Heat energy dumped to condenser per unit time | | 6 | $$\tau_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{\rho \, c_p \, V}{h A_s}$$ | First-order thermal time constant of thick-walled HP piping (relevant to bypass sizing) | | 7 | $$C_v = \dot{m} \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{\text{SG}}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}$$ | Valve flow coefficient (US units) — used in sizing | > Where: > - $A$ = valve throat area > - $P_1, P_2$ = upstream and downstream pressure > - $T_1$ = upstream absolute temperature > - $k$ = specific heat ratio > - $R$ = specific gas constant > - $h$ = specific enthalpy > - $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ > [!WARNING] > In the formula table above, vertical bars in tables have been replaced with horizontal layout to **avoid breaking the KTU markdown compiler**. Use `\vert` for absolute-value notation when copying to LaTeX. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern combined-cycle gas turbine (CCGT) plants with Heat Recovery Steam Generators (HRSGs), the **HP and IP bypass systems are essential** because: - The HRSG is a once-through unit; the turbine must ramp up slowly to match the steam demand. - A single HRSG can deliver **300 – 800 t/h** of steam; without bypass, the boiler would trip on overpressure within seconds of a turbine trip. - The bypass is a **commercial necessity** for grid-frequency response (FCB — Fast Cut Back capability). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Pressure Ratio Through a Bypass Valve **Premise:** Steam flowing through the converging nozzle of a bypass valve reaches sonic velocity at the throat when the downstream pressure falls below a critical value. **Step 1 — Starting from steady-flow energy equation** (reversible, adiabatic, neglecting inlet kinetic energy): $$ h_1 + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} $$ Since inlet velocity is negligible: $$ h_1 - h_2 = \dfrac{V_2^2}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Express enthalpies for an ideal gas (steam approximated as ideal for high-T throttling):** $$ h_1 - h_2 = c_p \, (T_1 - T_2) $$ **Step 3 — Isentropic relation between temperatures and pressures:** $$ \dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} $$ **Step 4 — Sonic condition at the throat: Mach number = 1, so:** $$ V_2 = \sqrt{k \, R \, T_2} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into the energy equation:** $$ c_p \, T_1 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}}\right] = \dfrac{k R T_2}{2} $$ **Step 6 — Replace $T_2$ using Step 3 and use $c_p = \dfrac{k R}{k-1}$:** $$ \dfrac{k R}{k-1} \, T_1 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}}\right] = \dfrac{k R}{2} \, T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} $$ **Step 7 — Cancel $k R T_1$ from both sides:** $$ \dfrac{1}{k-1} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}}\right] = \dfrac{1}{2} \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} $$ **Step 8 — Let $x = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}}$, then:** $$ \dfrac{1 - x}{k-1} = \dfrac{x}{2} $$ **Step 9 — Solve algebraically for $x$:** $$ 2(1 - x) = x(k-1) $$ $$ 2 - 2x = kx - x $$ $$ 2 = kx + x = x(k+1) $$ $$ x = \dfrac{2}{k+1} $$ **Step 10 — Recover $P_2 / P_1$:** $$ \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)_{\text{crit}}^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} = \dfrac{2}{k+1} $$ Raising both sides to the power $\dfrac{k}{k-1}$: $$ \boxed{\;\left(\dfrac{P_{2,\text{crit}}}{P_1}\right) = \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{k}{k-1}}\;} $$ **Numerical evaluation:** For superheated steam, $k = 1.30$: $$ \dfrac{P_{2,\text{crit}}}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.30}\right)^{\tfrac{1.30}{0.30}} = (0.8696)^{4.333} = 0.546 $$ For saturated (wet) steam, $k = 1.135$: $$ \dfrac{P_{2,\text{crit}}}{P_1} = (0.9091)^{8.41} = 0.577 $$ > **Interpretation:** For a 100 bar HP steam line, the throat reaches sonic velocity once the downstream pressure drops below **54.6 bar**. Below this, further reduction in $P_2$ does **not increase** mass flow — the valve is **choked**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Choked Mass Flow Through the Bypass Valve **Step 1 — Continuity equation at the throat:** $$ \dot{m} = \rho_2 \, A \, V_2 $$ **Step 2 — Use isentropic relations to express $\rho_2$ and $V_2$ in terms of stagnation (upstream) state:** $$ \rho_2 = \rho_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{k}}, \qquad V_2 = \sqrt{k R T_2} $$ **Step 3 — Apply sonic condition $P_2 = P_{2,\text{crit}}$:** $$ V_{\text{crit}} = \sqrt{k R T_1 \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)} $$ **Step 4 — Throat density:** $$ \rho_{\text{crit}} = \rho_1 \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{k}} $$ **Step 5 — Use ideal-gas law $\rho_1 = P_1 / (R T_1)$:** $$ \rho_{\text{crit}} = \dfrac{P_1}{R T_1} \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{k}} $$ **Step 6 — Multiply density × velocity × area:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = A \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{R T_1} \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{k}} \cdot \sqrt{k R T_1 \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)} $$ **Step 7 — Group terms:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{R T_1}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{k}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{2}} $$ **Step 8 — Combine exponents:** $$ \dfrac{1}{k} + \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{2 + k}{2k} = \dfrac{k+1}{2k} $$ So the exponent term becomes: $$ \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\tfrac{k+1}{2k}} $$ **Step 9 — Final result:** $$ \boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{R \, T_1}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{k+1}{2(k-1)}}\;} $$ **Step 10 — Numerical check (industrial example):** Given: $A = 0.05 \text{ m}^2$, $P_1 = 100 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$, $T_1 = 838 \text{ K}$ (565 °C), $k = 1.30$, $R = 461.5 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ for steam. $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = 0.05 \times 10^7 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.30}{461.5 \times 838}} \times (0.8696)^{1.917} $$ $$
