\sqrt{\dfrac{1.30}{386\,737}} = \sqrt{3.36 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.834 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ (0.8696)^{1.917} = 0.785 $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = 0.05 \times 10^7 \times 1.834 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.785 = 720 \text{ kg/s} $$ Converting: $720 \times 3600 = 2.59 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/h} \approx 2592 \text{ t/h}$. This is consistent with a 500 MW supercritical unit's full steam output (which is around **1900 – 2400 t/h**), confirming the **100% bypass capacity** design rule. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spray Water Mass Flow for Desuperheater **Premise:** An enthalpy balance across the desuperheater station must hold at steady state. **Step 1 — Mass balance:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{out}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} + \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} $$ **Step 2 — Enthalpy balance (no heat loss, mixing):** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \cdot h_{\text{out}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot h_{\text{steam}} + \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} \cdot h_{\text{spray}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:** $$ (\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} + \dot{m}_{\text{spray}}) \cdot h_{\text{out}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot h_{\text{steam}} + \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} \cdot h_{\text{spray}} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $\dot{m}_{\text{spray}}$:** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot (h_{\text{steam}} - h_{\text{out}}) = \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} \cdot (h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{spray}}) $$ **Step 5 — Solve for spray mass flow:** $$ \boxed{\;\dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot \dfrac{h_{\text{steam}} - h_{\text{out}}}{h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{spray}}}\;} $$ **Numerical example:** $\dot{m}_{\text{steam}} = 700 \text{ kg/s}$, $h_{\text{steam}} = 3570 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{\text{out}} = 3050 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (target), $h_{\text{spray}} = 800 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (feedwater at 180 °C): $$ \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = 700 \times \dfrac{3570 - 3050}{3050 - 800} = 700 \times \dfrac{520}{2250} = 700 \times 0.231 = 161.7 \text{ kg/s} $$ Thus about **23.1%** of the main steam flow must be sprayed as feedwater to achieve the target outlet enthalpy. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Operating Conditions | Condition | $P_1$ (bar) | $P_2$ (bar) | $P_2/P_1$ | Flow regime | Control action | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Cold start, full open | 100 | 25 | 0.25 | **Choked** | BCV 100%, SWCV active | | Warm start, 50% | 120 | 55 | 0.46 | Subsonic | BCV 50%, SWCV active | | Normal full load | 150 | 150 (turbine) | ≈1.0 | BCV closed | BCV 0% | | Trip dump | 165 | 25 | 0.15 | Choked | BCV opens in **3 s** | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture — HP Bypass System in a Supercritical Plant ```mermaid flowchart LR BOILER[Boiler Drum / Once-Through
Steam Generator] PT1["Pressure Transmitter PT-1
Upstream Steam"] BCV["Bypass Control Valve BCV
Main Throttling Element"] SWCV["Spray Water Control Valve
SWCV - Desuperheater"] PT2["Pressure Transmitter PT-2
Outlet"] TT1["Temperature Transmitter TT-1
Outlet to Reheat"] COLDREHEAT["Cold Reheat Line
to IP/LP Turbine"] PIC["Pressure Controller PIC
Master Loop"] TIC["Temperature Controller TIC
Slave Loop Cascade"] SPRAY["Feedwater Spray
Injection Nozzle"] TURBINE[HP Turbine Inlet] BOILER --> PT1 PT1 --> PIC PIC --> BCV BCV --> SPRAY SPRAY --> SWCV SWCV --> TT1 TT1 --> TIC TIC --> SWCV BCV --> PT2 PT2 --> COLDREHEAT COLDREHEAT --> TURBINE ``` ### 4.2 HP + IP Bypass Combined Architecture (Combined-Cycle / Supercritical) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HOT["Hot Side - Boiler / HRSG"] B[Boiler / HRSG Evaporator] SH[Superheater] RH1[Reheater 1 Cold Side] RH2[Reheater 2 Hot Side] end subgraph HPSIDE["HP Bypass Loop"] HPBV[HP Bypass Valve] HPSPRAY[HP Desuperheater Spray] end subgraph TOUROP["Turbine"] HPT[HP Turbine] IPT[IP Turbine] LPT[LP Turbine] GEN[Generator] end subgraph IPSIDE["IP Bypass Loop"] IPBV[IP Bypass Valve] IPSPRAY[IP Desuperheater Spray] end subgraph COLDSIDE["Condenser / Feedwater"] COND[Condenser] FW[Feedwater Pump] end B --> SH --> HPBV HPBV --> HPSPRAY --> RH1 SH --> HPT HPT --> RH2 RH2 --> IPBV IPBV --> IPSPRAY --> IPT RH2 --> IPT IPT --> LPT --> GEN LPT --> COND --> FW --> B FW --> HPSPRAY FW --> IPSPRAY ``` ### 4.3 Cascade Control Loop — Master / Slave Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Pressure Setpoint] PV1[Upstream Pressure P1] ERR1[Error e1 = SP - PV1] PIC1[PIC Master Controller] TSP[Temperature Setpoint - Slave SP] PV2[Outlet Temperature T2] ERR2[Error e2 = TSP - PV2] TIC1[TIC Slave Controller] BCVOUT[BCV Position Command] SWCVOUT[SWCV Position Command] SP --> ERR1 PV1 --> ERR1 ERR1 --> PIC1 PIC1 --> TSP TSP --> ERR2 PV2 --> ERR2 ERR2 --> TIC1 TIC1 --> SWCVOUT PIC1 --> BCVOUT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The **Pressure Controller (PIC)** is the *outer / master* loop. Its output becomes the **setpoint of the Temperature Controller (TIC)**, which is the *inner / slave* loop. The PIC moves the **Bypass Control Valve (BCV)**; the TIC moves the **Spray Water Control Valve (SWCV)**. This cascade is the standard KTU question for Module 2. ### 4.4 Trip / Interlock Logic Block ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Turbine Trip Signal] T2[Load Rejection > 40 percent] T3[Boiler Overpressure PT High] T4[Fast Cut Back FCB Demand] AND1{Any Trip TRUE?} A1[Open BCV in 3 seconds] A2[Open SWCV in 2 seconds] A3[Isolate Main Steam Trip Valve] A4[Signal to Condenser Dump Valves] T1 --> AND1 T2 --> AND1 T3 --> AND1 T4 --> AND1 AND1 -- YES --> A1 AND1 -- YES --> A2 AND1 -- YES --> A3 AND1 -- YES --> A4 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define a Turbine Bypass Valve and state TWO situations where it becomes essential. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A Turbine Bypass Valve (TBV) is a large-capacity, modulating pressure-control valve that diverts steam around the steam turbine directly to the next thermodynamic stage (such as the cold reheat line, IP/LP turbine, or condenser), maintaining boiler steam pressure within safe limits. Two essential situations: 1. **Turbine trip / load rejection** — When the turbine suddenly disconnects from the grid, the boiler continues to generate steam. The TBV opens rapidly (within **2–5 seconds**) to dump the entire steam flow to the condenser, preventing boiler overpressure and safety-valve lifting. 2. **Cold / warm start-up** — When the turbine rotors and casings are still cold, full steam flow would cause severe thermal-stress damage. The TBV stays fully open, allowing the boiler to be fired to full load while the turbine is gradually warmed and rolled. **Word limit: ~80 words. Marks: 3 (1 for definition, 1 each for situations).** --- #### Question 2: What is the role of the desuperheater in an HP bypass system? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The desuperheater is a **temperature-control subsystem** consisting of a spray water control valve (SWCV) and a spray nozzle located downstream of the HP bypass valve. Its role is to **inject feedwater (or condensate) into the bypassed superheated steam** to reduce its temperature from the boiler outlet level (typically **500 – 600 °C**) to a value compatible with the cold-reheat piping metallurgy (typically **320 – 350 °C**). This protects the cold-reheat tubes from **thermal fatigue and creep failure** during start-up when they are at low temperature. The desuperheater also provides **modulating temperature control** in cascade with the pressure-control loop, ensuring the bypassed steam enthalpy matches downstream requirements. **Word limit: ~80 words. Marks: 3.** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **HP turbine bypass system** of a 500 MW subcritical boiler. Describe the **cascade control strategy** (pressure master, temperature slave) used to coordinate the bypass control valve and the desuperheater spray valve. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A supercritical boiler generates **1800 t/h** of steam at **250 bar, 600 °C** entering the HP turbine. During a turbine trip, the HP bypass valve opens fully, dumping steam to the cold reheat line at **40 bar**. The desuperheater spray water is feedwater at **180 °C** and the target outlet temperature is **320 °C**. Calculate: (i) The critical pressure ratio and comment on the flow regime. (ii) The mass flow rate of spray water required (in **t/h**). (iii) The maximum steam mass flow through the bypass valve if the throat diameter is **400 mm** and the discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.95$. Use steam tables: at 250 bar, 600 °C → $h_1 \approx 3584$ kJ/kg; at 40 bar, 320 °C → $h_2 \approx 3045$ kJ/kg; feedwater at 180 °C → $h_{fw} \approx 763$ kJ/kg. Take $k = 1.30$, $R = 461.5$ J/(kg·K). **\[7 marks\]** --- ### Model Solution — Question A #### (a) HP Bypass System Block Diagram and Cascade Control Strategy **Working Principle (4 marks):** - During a turbine trip, the HP bypass valve (BCV) must open rapidly to redirect all the live steam from the boiler superheater to the cold reheat line, from where it eventually reaches the IP/LP turbine and condenser. - The bypassed steam temperature is far higher than the cold reheat pipe can tolerate; therefore a **desuperheater** sprays feedwater to bring the temperature down to a safe value. - The control objective is to keep boiler pressure stable (so the boiler does not trip on overpressure) **and** to keep the cold-reheat temperature within metallurgical limits. **Cascade Control Strategy (3 marks):** - The **Pressure Controller (PIC)** is the **master (outer) loop**. Its input is the deviation between the boiler steam pressure setpoint (e.g., 250 bar) and the measured upstream pressure $P_1$. Its output is a control signal that drives the BCV. - The **Temperature Controller (TIC)** is the **slave (inner) loop**. Its setpoint is the output of the PIC (or a fixed value like 320 °C, depending on design). Its input is the deviation between setpoint and measured outlet temperature $T_2$. Its output drives the SWCV. - The cascade architecture ensures that **any pressure disturbance** (e.g., sudden load rejection) is corrected first by the **fast inner temperature loop** modulating the spray, while the **outer pressure loop** slowly repositions the main BCV. **Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid block.)* --- #### (b) Numerical Solutions **(i) Critical pressure ratio and flow regime (2 marks):** $$ \left(\dfrac{P_{2,\text{crit}}}{P_1}\right) = \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{k}{k-1}} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.30}\right)^{\tfrac{1.30}{0.30}} = (0.8696)^{4.333} = 0.546 $$ Therefore: $$ P_{2,\text{crit}} = 0.546 \times 250 = 136.5 \text{ bar} $$ The actual downstream pressure is **40 bar**, which is **less than 136.5 bar**. Therefore, the flow is **choked (sonic)** at the throat. **(ii) Spray water mass flow (2.5 marks):** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \times \dfrac{h_{\text{steam}} - h_{\text{out}}}{h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{spray}}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = 1800 \times \dfrac{3584 - 3045}{3045 - 763} = 1800 \times \dfrac{539}{2282} = 1800 \times 0.2362 = 425.2 \text{ t/h} $$ **(iii) Maximum steam mass flow through the bypass (2.5 marks):** Throat area: $$ A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.4)^2 = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2 $$ Choked mass flow: $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{R T_1}} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{\!\tfrac{k+1}{2(k-1)}} $$ Where $P_1 = 250 \times 10^5 = 2.5 \times 10^7$ Pa, $T_1 = 600 + 273 = 873$ K. $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = 0.95 \times 0.1257 \times 2.5 \times 10^7 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.30}{461.5 \times 873}} \times (0.8696)^{1.917} $$ $$ \sqrt{\dfrac{1.30}{402\,830}} = \sqrt{3.227 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.796 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ (0.8696)^{1.917} = 0.785 $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = 0.95 \times 0.1257 \times 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 1.796 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.785 $$ $$ = 0.95 \times 0.1257 \times 2.5 \times 10^7 \times 1.41 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ = 4209 \text{ kg/s} = 15\,152 \text{ t/h} $$ Since the actual boiler output is **1800 t/h**, the valve has **plenty of margin** (8.4× capacity). The system is not capacity-limited; it is **response-time limited**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / May 2023]` **(a)** Differentiate clearly between **HP bypass, IP bypass, and LP bypass** systems. For each, state: location in the steam cycle, the typical inlet/outlet pressures, the need (or no need) for desuperheating, and the primary operating scenario. **\[7 marks\]** **(b)** A 210 MW thermal power plant has a single HP bypass valve sized to pass **100% of boiler MCR flow**. The boiler MCR is **750 t/h** at **140 bar, 540 °C**. The bypass discharges to the cold reheat line at **25 bar**. The desuperheater cools the steam to **300 °C**. Compute: (i) The choked-flow mass capacity of the valve if the throat diameter is **300 mm**, $C_d = 0.92$, and $k = 1.30$. (ii) Verify whether this valve can actually pass the full MCR. (iii) The mass of spray water needed (in **t/h**) if feedwater is at **160 °C**. Steam data: at 140 bar, 540 °C → $h_1 \approx 3433$ kJ/kg; at 25 bar, 300 °C → $h_2 \approx 3008$ kJ/kg; at 25 bar, 160 °C → $h_{fw} \approx 675$ kJ/kg. **\[7 marks\]** --- ### Model Solution — Question B #### (a) Comparison of HP, IP, and LP Bypass Systems | Feature | HP Bypass | IP Bypass | LP Bypass | |---|---|---|---| | Location | Boiler superheater outlet → cold reheat line | Hot reheat outlet → IP turbine inlet bypass | LP exhaust → condenser (drainage) | | Inlet pressure (typical) | 100 – 250 bar | 30 – 60 bar | 0.1 – 5 bar (vacuum to few bar) | | Inlet temperature | 500 – 600 °C | 500 – 600 °C (reheated) | 80 – 250 °C (saturated / wet) | | Need for desuperheater | **Yes, mandatory** (large ΔT) | **Yes** (smaller ΔT) | **No** (low T, no superheat) | | Primary scenario | Cold/warm start-up, turbine trip, FCB | Reheat protection on turbine trip | LP blade erosion protection, low-load dump | | Capacity (% of MCR) | 100% | 100% | 30 – 70% (smaller bypass) | **Key distinguishing points (for marks):** - HP bypass **always** requires a desuperheater because the cold reheat pipe is at much lower temperature; thermal shock would crack it. - IP bypass may or may not require a desuperheater depending on the reheat pipe metallurgy and target outlet temperature. - LP bypass typically discharges directly to the **condenser**, so desuperheating is not needed; it is mainly a **drainage / wetness control** system. **Marks split: 7 (1 for table framework, 1 for each row's correct content, 1 for primary scenario).** --- #### (b) Numerical Solutions **(i) Choked-flow mass capacity (2.5 marks):** $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.3)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$ $P_1 = 140 \times 10^5 = 1.4 \times 10^7$ Pa $T_1 = 540 + 273 = 813$ K $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = 0.92 \times 0.0707 \times 1.4 \times 10^7 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1.30}{461.5 \times 813}} \times (0.8696)^{1.917} $$ $$ \sqrt{\dfrac{1.30}{375\,200}} = \sqrt{3.466 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.862 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = 0.92 \times 0.0707 \times 1.4 \times 10^7 \times 1.862 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.785 $$ $$ = 0.92 \times 0.0707 \times 1.4 \times 10^7 \times 1.46 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ = 1329 \text{ kg/s} = 4784 \text{ t/h} $$ **(ii) Verification (1.5 marks):** The choked capacity is **4784 t/h**, which is far greater than the MCR of **750 t/h**. Ratio: $4784 / 750 = 6.38$. Therefore, the valve **can comfortably pass 100% MCR**. The design is **not mass-flow limited**; it is **temperature- and response-time limited**. (In practice, the 100% capacity refers to the valve's *normal* throttled range, while choked capacity is the *absolute maximum* theoretical value.) **(iii) Spray water mass (3 marks):** $$ \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \times \dfrac{h_{\text{steam}} - h_{\text{out}}}{h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{spray}}} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = 750 \times \dfrac{3433 - 3008}{3008 - 675} = 750 \times \dfrac{425}{2333} = 750 \times 0.1822 = 136.6 \text{ t/h} $$ So about **18.2%** of the steam mass is sprayed to achieve the temperature reduction. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting the isentropic ideal-gas assumption:** In choked-flow problems, students often directly apply the formula without deriving the critical-pressure condition. The examiner expects the **value of the critical ratio (0.546 for superheated steam)** to be stated explicitly, with a comment on the **choked / unchoked** regime. > 2. **Unit confusion in mass flow:** Use **SI units (kg/s)** consistently inside formulas, then convert to **t/h** only at the end. Mixing MPa with bar, or kg/s with t/h mid-calculation, is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**. > 3. **Wrong enthalpy values from steam tables:** Steam-table readings vary with edition. State the **assumed values** clearly at the start. Examiners allow ±2% tolerance. > 4. **Confusing HP and IP bypass:** In Part B, students often write "HP bypass" when the question asks for IP bypass. The **location, pressure level, and need for desuperheater** are the giveaway. > 5. **Cascade loop direction:** The **master** is the **slower outer loop** (pressure), and the **slave** is the **faster inner loop** (temperature). Reversing this is a 2-mark error. > 6. **Neglecting $C_d$:** The discharge coefficient (typically 0.92 – 0.98) **must be multiplied** with the theoretical mass flow. Forgetting it loses 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Turbine Bypass Valves (PEICT523, Module 2)** - [ ] **Definition:** TBV = control valve that diverts steam around the turbine to downstream stage (reheat/condenser) during transient operation. - [ ] **Three types:** HP bypass (high pressure, requires desuperheater), IP bypass (reheat protection, often with desuperheater), LP bypass (condenser drainage, no desuperheater). - [ ] **Capacity rule:** 100% of boiler MCR must be passable by HP and IP bypass combined; 30 – 70% for LP bypass. - [ ] **Desuperheater:** Spray water valve (SWCV) that injects feedwater to drop steam T from 600 °C to ~320 °C for cold-reheat compatibility. - [ ] **Cascade control:** PIC (pressure) = master outer loop; TIC (temperature) = slave inner loop. The PIC drives BCV; TIC drives SWCV. - [ ] **Critical pressure ratio:** $(P_2/P_1)_{\text{crit}} = (2/(k+1))^{k/(k-1)}$ → 0.546 for superheated steam (k=1.3), 0.577 for saturated (k=1.135). - [ ] **Choked flow regime:** Once $P_2/P_1 < 0.546$, flow is sonic and choked — no further increase with lower $P_2$. - [ ] **Trip response:** Bypass valve must open in **2 – 5 seconds** to prevent boiler overpressure on turbine trip. - [ ] **Key formula — Choked mass flow:** $\dot{m}_{\text{choked}} = C_d \cdot A \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{k/(R T_1)} \cdot (2/(k+1))^{(k+1)/[2(k-1)]}$. - [ ] **Spray water formula:** $\dot{m}_{\text{spray}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \cdot (h_{\text{steam}} - h_{\text{out}}) / (h_{\text{out}} - h_{\text{spray}})$. - [ ] **Applications:** Cold/warm/hot start-up, load rejection, turbine trip, FCB (Fast Cut Back), grid-frequency response. - [ ] **Components to remember:** BCV, SWCV, PT, TT, PIC, TIC, interlock logic, spray nozzle, dump valves to condenser. - [ ] **KTU 2024 exam tip:** Always draw the **cascade block diagram** in cascade-control questions — examiners award 2–3 marks just for a clear diagram. ---

topics.title: Details of Boiler Processes in Combined Cycle Power Plant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Details of Boiler Processes in Combined Cycle Power Plant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **Combined Cycle Power Plant (CCPP)**, the "boiler" is technically termed a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)**. It is a highly specialised, water-tube heat exchanger that captures waste heat from the hot exhaust gases of an upstream **Gas Turbine (GT)** to produce superheated steam, which is then expanded through a downstream **Steam Turbine (ST)** to generate additional electrical power.

$$ \text{GT Exhaust (Flue Gas)} \xrightarrow{\text{Heat Transfer}} \text{HRSG} \xrightarrow{\text{Steam Generation}} \text{ST} \rightarrow \text{Generator} $$

The combined electrical output of the GT and the ST yields a **Net Plant Efficiency** in the range of **55–62 % (LHV basis)**, which is significantly higher than the 33–38 % achievable with a stand-alone open-cycle gas turbine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The boiler in a CCPP is NOT a conventional fuel-fired boiler. It is a *waste-heat recovery unit* whose primary heat source is the gas turbine exhaust (typically 540 °C – 620 °C). Supplementary firing may be added but is not the primary energy source.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a car engine. When petrol burns inside the engine, only about **30–35 %** of the energy moves the car; the rest escapes as hot exhaust gas, which simply dissipates into the atmosphere. A CCPP does exactly the opposite — it **traps the wasted exhaust heat** from a gas turbine and uses it to boil water, drive a steam turbine, and squeeze out an extra 25–30 % of electricity from the *same* fuel input.

The **HRSG** is the "trap" — a tall, finned-tube tower where:
- **Cold water enters at the bottom** (economiser section).
- It progressively heats up, boils (evaporator/drum), and becomes superheated steam (superheater section).
- **Hot flue gas enters at the top** and flows *downward* (counter-flow), giving up its heat progressively.
- **Cold exhaust gas exits at the bottom** (ID fan / stack), typically at 90–120 °C.

> [!NOTE]
> The HRSG is essentially a **giant counter-flow heat exchanger** in which a fluid changes phase. The instrumentation challenge is to monitor and control several temperatures, pressures, drum levels, and flow rates simultaneously across multiple pressure levels.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value (CCPP) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Gas Turbine Exhaust Temperature | **540 – 620** | °C |
| HRSG Stack Temperature | **90 – 120** | °C |
| HP Steam Pressure | **60 – 130** | bar (a) |
| IP Steam Pressure | **10 – 30** | bar (a) |
| LP Steam Pressure | **3 – 6** | bar (a) |
| HP Superheated Steam Temp | **500 – 565** | °C |
| Net Plant Efficiency (LHV) | **55 – 62** | % |
| Specific Work Output | **1700 – 2100** | kJ/kWh |
| HRSG Approach Point | **5 – 15** | °C |
| HRSG Pinch Point | **5 – 25** | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Approach Point** = (Saturation temperature − Feedwater outlet temperature) at evaporator inlet.  
> **Pinch Point** = (Gas temperature − Steam saturation temperature) at the evaporator inlet zone.  
> Both are **critical design KPIs** that define HRSG thermodynamic performance and physical size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an HRSG — Pressure Levels

A modern CCPP HRSG is classified by the **number of independent steam pressure circuits** it contains. Each pressure level has its own economiser, drum (for evaporative circuits) or once-through coil, and superheater.

### 2.1.1 Single-Pressure HRSG

- One steam circuit at a single pressure level (typically 30 – 60 bar).
- **Limitation:** Pinch point temperature difference at the evaporator inlet becomes very small when gas temperature approaches saturation, causing either:
  - Excessive evaporator surface area, or
  - High stack temperature (efficiency loss).
- Used only in **small/older** CCPPs.

### 2.1.2 Dual-Pressure HRSG

- Two pressure levels: **High Pressure (HP)** + **Low Pressure (LP)**.
- LP circuit extracts heat from the **cooler sections** of the gas path (after the HP evaporator), reducing pinch-point losses and allowing lower stack temperatures (**85 – 100 °C**).
- **Most common configuration** for modern CCPPs (250 – 400 MW class).

### 2.1.3 Triple-Pressure HRSG

- Three levels: HP + IP + LP.
- Also includes a **reheater** (steam leaving the HP turbine is re-superheated in the HRSG before entering the IP/LP turbine).
- Used in **large, base-load CCPPs** (>400 MW) where plant efficiency must exceed **60 %**.
- Pinch point is virtually eliminated → very low stack temperature (**80 – 95 °C**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why more pressure levels?** As the gas cools, its available temperature differential shrinks. By introducing a lower-pressure circuit that boils at a *lower* saturation temperature, the gas can continue to give up heat efficiently. This is the fundamental thermodynamic justification for the cascade architecture.

## 2.2 Internal Heat Transfer Zones (Gas Path — Top to Bottom)

The gas-side flow is from top to bottom; the water/steam flow is from bottom to top (counter-flow).

| Zone (Gas Flow) | Function | Instrumentation Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **Supplementary Firing Duct** (optional) | Raises gas temp from ~540 °C to ~700 °C using a small duct burner | Combustion airflow, fuel flow, O₂ trim |
| **Superheater** | Raises saturated steam → superheated steam (500 – 565 °C) | Inlet/outlet T, attemperator spray flow |
| **Reheater** (if present) | Reheats partially expanded HP exhaust steam | Reheat T control |
| **Evaporator (Boiler Bank)** | Phase change: water → saturated steam | Drum level, drum P, circulation flow |
| **Economiser** | Pre-heats feedwater using residual gas heat | Feedwater inlet/outlet T, flow |

## 2.3 Control Loops in an HRSG — Process Instrumentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following control loops are **examination favourites** for the KTU 2024 scheme PEICT523 paper.

### (a) Drum Level Control (Three-Element Control)

The HP/IP evaporator drum level is controlled by a **three-element controller** that receives:
1. **Drum level** (compensated for density/swell, using differential pressure DP cells).
2. **Steam flow** (from the superheater outlet, e.g., orifice or V-cone meter).
3. **Feedwater flow** (from the economiser inlet, e.g., Magmeter / Venturi).

$$ \text{Output (Valve position)} = f\bigl(\text{Level}_{error},\ \Delta \text{Steam flow},\ \Delta \text{Feedwater flow}\bigr) $$

- The **level** corrects long-term drift.
- The **flow match (steam − feedwater)** corrects short-term disturbances (swell/shrink effect).
- This decouples the level from the rapid density changes that occur during load changes.

### (b) Steam Temperature Control — Attemperator

Superheated steam temperature is controlled by an **attemperator (desuperheater)**, which sprays deaerated feedwater into the superheater stages to absorb excess enthalpy.

$$ T_{SH,out} = T_{SH,set} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{spray} = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt $$

- Sensors: Type-K or Type-N **thermocouples** at superheater outlet, arranged in a *multiple-of-three* (MOS) voting configuration (e.g., 2-out-of-3 voting) for high availability.
- Spray water temperature is held constant via a dedicated control valve.

### (c) Combustion Control (Supplementary Firing Only)

When the HRSG has a duct burner:
- **Fuel flow** is set by the steam pressure setpoint.
- **Combustion air** is set to maintain a constant **excess air ratio** (typically 2–5 % O₂ in flue gas).
- **O₂ trim** uses a **zirconia oxygen probe** in the flue gas duct to fine-tune the air damper.

$$ \lambda = \frac{\text{Actual Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}} \quad ; \quad \text{O}_2\% = \frac{21( \lambda - 1)}{\lambda} \times 100 $$

### (d) Furnace Draft / Pressure Control

HRSG casing pressure is maintained at a slight **negative pressure (–25 to –75 mmH₂O)** by an **Induced Draft (ID) fan** with a pressure transmitter in the convection section.

### (e) Feedwater Control

A **feedwater pump** (motor- or turbine-driven) is controlled by the **feedwater regulating valve** in series with the three-element drum level controller. Economiser inlet flow is measured by a **venturi** or **annular flowmeter**.

### (f) Continuous Emissions Monitoring (CEMS)

- **NOx** — Chemiluminescence or NDIR analyser.
- **SO₂** — NDIR analyser.
- **CO** — NDIR analyser.
- **O₂** — Zirconia or paramagnetic analyser.
- **Particulates (Opacity)** — Opacimeter (transmissometer).
- **Stack flow** — Thermal mass flow or pitot array.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g}\, (T_{g,in} - T_{g,out})$ | Heat absorbed by HRSG from gas | kW |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_s\,(h_{out} - h_{in})$ | Heat gained by steam/water side | kW |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{Q}{h_{sh} - h_{fw}}$ | Steam generation rate | kg/s |
| 4 | $\eta_{HRSG} = \dfrac{T_{g,in} - T_{g,stack}}{T_{g,in} - T_{ref}}$ | HRSG thermal efficiency (effectiveness basis) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Overall heat transfer (LMTD method) | kW |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | °C / K |
| 7 | $\text{Approach} = T_{sat} - T_{fw,eco,out}$ | Approach point temperature | °C |
| 8 | $\text{Pinch} = T_{g,evap,in} - T_{sat}$ | Pinch point temperature | °C |
| 9 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}}$ | Excess air ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{O}_2\% = \dfrac{21(\lambda - 1)}{\lambda}$ | O₂ in dry flue gas (perfect combustion) | % |
| 11 | $P_{drum} = P_{sh} + \Delta P_{tubes}$ | Drum pressure setpoint | bar |
| 12 | $\Delta h_{fw} = c_{p,fw} \cdot \Delta T_{eco}$ | Feedwater enthalpy gain in economiser | kJ/kg |
| 13 | $\dot{W}_{ST} = \dot{m}_s \cdot \Delta h_{turbine} \cdot \eta_{iso}$ | Steam turbine power output | kW |
| 14 | $\eta_{plant,net} = \dfrac{P_{GT} + P_{ST} - P_{aux}}{ \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV}$ | Net CCPP efficiency | dimensionless |
| 15 | $T_{approach} \geq 5\,°C$ | HRSG design constraint (no feedwater boiling in economiser) | — |
| 16 | $T_{pinch} \geq 5\,°C$ | HRSG design constraint (positive heat transfer at evaporator inlet) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Syntax Note for KTU Answer Booklet:** In written exams, never use the `|` symbol for absolute value (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) inside tabular text — it breaks markdown table rendering. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ for proper LaTeX rendering.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operator Safety:** Drum level, furnace pressure, and combustion O₂ control prevent boiler implosion, tube dry-out (causing rupture), and explosive unburnt-fuel accumulation.
- **Plant Efficiency Optimisation:** Maintaining the lowest possible stack temperature (without acid dew-point corrosion) maximises heat recovery. The pinch-point design directly governs the cost-vs-efficiency trade-off.
- **Emissions Compliance:** CEMS data is logged continuously for **CPCB / KSPCB** regulatory reporting.
- **Asset Life:** Steam temperature excursions > 10 °C above design accelerate creep-fatigue damage in HP/IP superheater tubes. Tight attemperator control adds years to tube life.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — HRSG Steam Generation Rate

**Given (typical dual-pressure CCPP problem statement):**
- Gas flow rate $\dot{m}_g = 280$ kg/s
- Gas specific heat $c_{p,g} = 1.05$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Gas inlet temperature $T_{g,in} = 580$ °C = **853 K**
- Gas stack temperature $T_{g,stack} = 110$ °C = **383 K**
- HP superheated steam enthalpy $h_{sh} = 3375$ kJ/kg (at 80 bar, 500 °C)
- HP feedwater enthalpy $h_{fw} = 1200$ kJ/kg (at 80 bar, 220 °C)
- IP superheated steam enthalpy $h_{sh,IP} = 2900$ kJ/kg
- IP feedwater enthalpy $h_{fw,IP} = 600$ kJ/kg
- IP steam mass fraction: 15 % of total HRSG output
- Stack loss: **5 %** of Q absorbed

**Step 1 — Heat released by gas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{gas} &= \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_{g,in} - T_{g,stack}) \\
        &= 280 \times 1.05 \times (580 - 110) \\
        &= 280 \times 1.05 \times 470 \\
        &= 280 \times 493.5 \\
        &= 138{,}180 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Net heat available to water/steam (subtracting stack-radiative loss):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} &= 0.95 \times 138{,}180 \\
        &= 131{,}271 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — HP steam enthalpy rise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h_{HP} &= h_{sh,HP} - h_{fw,HP} \\
              &= 3375 - 1200 \\
              &= 2175 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — IP steam enthalpy rise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h_{IP} &= h_{sh,IP} - h_{fw,IP} \\
              &= 2900 - 600 \\
              &= 2300 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total steam output (mass balance).** Let $\dot{m}_{HP}$ be the HP steam flow. Then $\dot{m}_{IP} = 0.15\,\dot{m}_{HP} / 0.85 = 0.1765\,\dot{m}_{HP}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{net} &= \dot{m}_{HP} \cdot \Delta h_{HP} + \dot{m}_{IP} \cdot \Delta h_{IP} \\
131{,}271 &= \dot{m}_{HP} \cdot 2175 + 0.1765\,\dot{m}_{HP} \cdot 2300 \\
131{,}271 &= \dot{m}_{HP} \cdot (2175 + 405.95) \\
131{,}271 &= \dot{m}_{HP} \cdot 2580.95 \\
\dot{m}_{HP} &= \dfrac{131{,}271}{2580.95} \\
\dot{m}_{HP} &\approx 50.86 \text{ kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — IP steam flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{IP} = 0.1765 \times 50.86 \approx 8.98 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 7 — Total steam:**

$$
\dot{m}_{total} = 50.86 + 8.98 \approx 59.84 \text{ kg/s} \quad (\approx 215.4 \text{ t/h})
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 280 kg/s exhaust stream at 580 °C can produce nearly **60 kg/s** of high-quality steam — sufficient to drive a 100–120 MW ST unit alongside a 150 MW GT, yielding a combined output of ~250–270 MW.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Excess Air Ratio from Flue Gas O₂

A zirconia probe in the HRSG stack reads **3.5 % O₂** (dry basis). Find the excess air ratio $\lambda$ and the actual air–fuel ratio by mass.

**Step 1 — Use the textbook relation for O₂ in dry flue gas:**

$$
\text{O}_2\% = \dfrac{21(\lambda - 1)}{\lambda} \times 100
$$

Wait — this standard relation assumes **zero O₂ in the fuel** and the standard 21 % O₂ in air. For most gaseous fuels (natural gas) it is accurate.

**Step 2 — Rearrange and solve:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
3.5 &= \dfrac{21(\lambda - 1)}{\lambda} \\
3.5\,\lambda &= 21\,\lambda - 21 \\
21 - 3.5\,\lambda &= 21 \cdot \lambda \cdot (1/1) - 3.5 \cdot \lambda \\
21 &= 21\,\lambda - 3.5\,\lambda \\
21 &= 17.5\,\lambda \\
\lambda &= \dfrac{21}{17.5} \\
\lambda &= 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Air–fuel ratio (for natural gas, stoichiometric AF ≈ 17.2 kg air / kg fuel):**

$$
AF_{actual} = \lambda \times AF_{stoich} = 1.20 \times 17.2 = 20.64 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
$$

**Step 4 — Combustion efficiency check.** A λ of 1.20 corresponds to ~3–5 % dry-gas heat loss for natural gas — acceptable for HRSG supplementary firing.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pinch Point and Approach Point Verification

**Given a dual-pressure HRSG:**
- HP drum pressure = 60 bar → saturation temperature $T_{sat,HP} = 275.6$ °C
- HP economiser outlet (feedwater entering evaporator) = 268 °C
- HP evaporator inlet gas temperature = 295 °C

**Approach Point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Approach}_{HP} &= T_{sat,HP} - T_{fw,evap,in} \\
                     &= 275.6 - 268 \\
                     &= 7.6\,°C \quad \checkmark \text{ (within 5–15 °C design range)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pinch Point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pinch}_{HP} &= T_{g,evap,in} - T_{sat,HP} \\
                   &= 295 - 275.6 \\
                   &= 19.4\,°C \quad \checkmark \text{ (within 5–25 °C design range)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** A *positive* pinch point is mandatory. If pinch ≤ 0, the gas cannot transfer heat to the water at that location — evaporator surface area becomes infinite, and the HRSG is thermodynamically infeasible.

---
