# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — 3 Marks**
**"Differentiate clearly between a conventional boiler and a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG). Mention any three instrumentation challenges specific to an HRSG."**

**Model Answer:**

A **conventional boiler** (e.g., a coal-fired utility boiler) uses a dedicated **combustion system** as the primary heat source. Its water-steam circuit is in a single pressure level (often with reheating) and is integrated with fuel, ash, and flue-gas handling systems.

An **HRSG** in a CCPP is a *waste-heat recovery boiler* whose primary heat source is the **exhaust of a gas turbine** at 540–620 °C. It has no coal/ash handling; combustion (supplementary firing) is optional. It commonly features **multiple pressure levels (HP/IP/LP)** and tightly integrates with the GT heat balance.

**Three HRSG-specific instrumentation challenges:**
1. **Rapid load swings** — GT exhaust temperature changes within seconds; the HRSG must respond via three-element control and attemperator trim to avoid tube metal excursions.
2. **Differential thermal expansion** — Vertical HRSG drums and headers expand differentially during transients, demanding **bellows, expansion joints**, and care in transmitter tap location.
3. **No main fuel source** — Conventional flame scanners and furnace O₂ are absent. Supplementary firing (if present) must be tightly O₂-trimmed; otherwise the HRSG operates purely on exhaust gas composition monitoring (NOx/CO/O₂ at stack).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Writing "HRSG is just a normal boiler" loses 1 mark. The examiner expects explicit mention of *waste-heat*, *multiple pressure levels*, and *GT coupling*.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] — 3 Marks**
**"Explain the significance of the *pinch point* and *approach point* in HRSG design. State typical design values."**

**Model Answer:**

- **Pinch Point** = (Gas temperature at evaporator inlet) − (Steam saturation temperature at that pressure). It is the *smallest* temperature difference in the evaporator. A **small pinch** (<5 °C) forces a *very large* evaporator surface area (high CapEx), while a **large pinch** (>25 °C) wastes energy by allowing the gas to leave hotter (high stack loss, low efficiency). **Typical design: 10 – 20 °C.**

- **Approach Point** = (Steam saturation temperature) − (Feedwater outlet temperature at economiser exit). It determines whether the feedwater reaches saturation before entering the evaporator. **Typical design: 5 – 15 °C.** A small approach economises on the economiser surface area but risks feedwater boiling in the economiser (steam binding, tube failure).

Both parameters are **critical thermodynamic KPIs**; the HRSG designer iterates them to balance *first cost* (tube surface) versus *operating cost* (efficiency loss).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Reversing the definitions of pinch and approach. Memorise: **Pinch = gas side; Approach = water side** (the "P" in pinch relates to the *gas temperature profile* approaching the steam temperature).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** in a combined cycle power plant. Explain the function of each of the following sections: economiser, evaporator, superheater, and reheater. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss in detail the **three-element drum level control** scheme used in HRSG evaporators. Explain why a single-element (level-only) controller fails during load changes, and show the block-diagram interaction of the three measurements. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part A(a)

An **HRSG** is a vertical, water-tube, counter-flow heat exchanger installed downstream of a gas turbine. Hot exhaust gas enters at the top and flows **downward** over finned tube bundles; feedwater enters at the bottom and flows **upward**, undergoing progressive heating.

**Construction:**
- **Casing** — Carbon-steel, refractory-lined at top, insulated throughout.
- **Tube panels** — Finned steel tubes welded to headers; arranged in vertical banks.
- **Drum** — Large cylindrical vessel (HP and IP) for natural-circulation evaporators; collects steam-water mixture from risers and returns saturated water via downcomers.
- **Stack** — At bottom exit, with ID fan.
- **Bypass dampers** — For warm-up, to relieve thermal stress during GT start-up.

**Function of each section:**

| Section | Function | Operating Regime |
|---|---|---|
| **Economiser** | Pre-heats feedwater by recovering residual gas heat; outlet water is *sub-cooled* by the approach point. | Single-phase liquid |
| **Evaporator** | Phase change: water → saturated steam; occurs at constant temperature (drum pressure). | Two-phase boiling |
| **Superheater** | Raises saturated steam → superheated steam (500 – 565 °C) at constant pressure. | Single-phase vapour |
| **Reheater** | Reheats partially expanded HP-turbine exhaust steam before IP turbine entry. | Single-phase vapour |

Working fluid flow: **Feedwater → Eco → Drum (downcomers) → Riser tubes → Steam-Water separator → Saturated steam → Superheater → HP Turbine → Reheater (HRSG) → IP/LP Turbine → Condenser → Feedwater Pump → Deaerator → Economiser (cycle repeats).**

#### Model Solution — Part A(b)

The **drum level** must be maintained within a narrow band (±50 mm of normal level) to prevent:
- **Low level** → tube dry-out → boiler tube rupture.
- **High level** → water carryover into superheater → turbine blade damage.

A **single-element (level-only) controller** fails because:
- During a **load-up**, steam demand rises before feedwater can respond → drum level *initially rises* due to **swell effect** (sudden pressure drop flashes water into steam). The controller, seeing high level, *reduces* feedwater — exactly the wrong response.
- During a **load-down**, the reverse *shrink effect* occurs.

**Three-Element Solution:**
1. **Level element (drum level)** → corrects long-term drift.
2. **Steam flow element (feed-forward)** → anticipates load changes.
3. **Feedwater flow element (feedback)** → maintains flow match.

The controller output is the **feedwater regulating valve** position. The flows are differenced (steam − feedwater) and summed with the level error:

$$
m_{fw} = K_p \cdot e_{level} + K_f \cdot ( \dot{m}_{steam} - \dot{m}_{fw} )
$$

This decouples the level from the boil-off dynamics and gives **fast, stable** level control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A common error is to confuse "three elements" with the number of controllers. It is **three *measured variables*** in **one** controller — not three separate controllers. State this explicitly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain the **superheated steam temperature control** of an HRSG using an **attemperator**. Justify the use of multiple temperature sensors with voting logic. Derive the heat-balance relation for the attemperator spray water mass flow. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With reference to a **supplementary-fired HRSG**, describe the **combustion control** strategy. Show how the **excess air ratio** is maintained using a **zirconia oxygen probe** for O₂ trim. Derive the relation between flue-gas O₂ % and excess air ratio. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part B(a)

**Attemperator Working Principle:**
An attemperator (desuperheater) is a **water-injection device** located between two stages of the superheater (or at the outlet). It sprays **deaerated feedwater** directly into the steam path to absorb excess superheat and maintain the SH outlet temperature at its setpoint (typically 500–565 °C).

**Why multiple sensors (MOS voting):**
- Superheater outlet temperature is the **fastest-changing and most safety-critical** variable in an HRSG.
- A single Type-K/N thermocouple is subject to drift, vibration-induced open circuits, and localised hot spots.
- **Multiple-Of-Three (MOS) voting** — three independent thermocouples; the controller uses the *median* or a *2-out-of-3* agreement. This rejects a single faulty sensor and prevents nuisance trips / runaway spray.

**Heat Balance Derivation:**

Let:
- $m_s$ = superheated steam mass flow (kg/s)
- $h_1$ = enthalpy at superheater stage-1 outlet (kJ/kg)
- $h_2$ = desired enthalpy at superheater final outlet (kJ/kg)
- $h_{spray}$ = enthalpy of attemperator spray water (kJ/kg)
- $m_{sp}$ = spray water mass flow (kg/s)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Energy in:}\quad m_s \cdot h_1 + m_{sp} \cdot h_{spray} \\
&\text{Energy out:}\quad (m_s + m_{sp}) \cdot h_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_s \cdot h_1 + m_{sp} \cdot h_{spray} &= (m_s + m_{sp}) \cdot h_2 \\
m_s \cdot h_1 + m_{sp} \cdot h_{spray} &= m_s \cdot h_2 + m_{sp} \cdot h_2 \\
m_{sp} \cdot (h_{spray} - h_2) &= m_s \cdot (h_2 - h_1) \\
m_{sp} &= m_s \cdot \dfrac{h_2 - h_1}{h_{spray} - h_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $h_{spray} \ll h_2$, the denominator is large and negative — the spray *removes* enthalpy, and $m_{sp}$ is small (typically 1–5 % of $m_s$).

#### Model Solution — Part B(b)

In a supplementary-fired HRSG, a **duct burner** is installed in the exhaust gas plenum to raise the gas temperature from ~540 °C to ~700 °C, increasing steam generation and plant output. Combustion must be controlled to:
- Maintain the desired **HP steam pressure** (master loop).
- Maintain **excess air ratio λ** (typically 1.05 – 1.25) for clean combustion.

**Two-Loop Control:**
1. **Master loop:** Steam pressure setpoint → fuel flow controller → fuel valve.
2. **Slave loop:** Air flow controller (set by λ × stoichiometric air) → air damper. **O₂ trim** PI controller adjusts the air setpoint based on the **zirconia probe** output.

**Derivation of O₂–λ Relation:**

For complete combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel $C_xH_y$ with air (21 % O₂, 79 % N₂ by volume), the dry flue gas contains:
- O₂ in excess air
- N₂ from combustion air
- CO₂ and H₂O from combustion (H₂O condensed out for "dry" basis)

Stoichiometric O₂ required: $O_{2,stoich}$. Actual O₂ supplied: $\lambda \cdot O_{2,stoich}$.  
O₂ in flue gas (dry basis):

$$
\text{O}_2\% = \dfrac{(\lambda - 1)\,O_{2,stoich}}{\lambda \cdot O_{2,stoich} + N_{2,air}} \times 100
$$

For a generic hydrocarbon burned with air (21 % O₂, 79 % N₂), the textbook simplification holds:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{O}_2\% = \dfrac{21\,(\lambda - 1)}{\lambda} \times 100\;}
$$

**Example:** A flue gas O₂ reading of 4 % corresponds to:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{21}{21 - 4} = \dfrac{21}{17} = 1.235
$$

The **zirconia probe** (operating at 600–800 °C) generates an EMF of:

$$
E = \dfrac{RT}{4F}\, \ln \!\left( \dfrac{P_{O_2,\,ref}}{P_{O_2,\,flue}} \right)
$$

where $R$ = gas constant, $F$ = Faraday's constant, $T$ = probe temperature (K). The probe EMF is therefore a direct, fast, in-situ measure of flue-gas O₂, ideal for trim control.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. **Confusing air–fuel ratio with excess air ratio.** The former is *absolute* (kg air/kg fuel); the latter is *relative* (actual/stoichiometric). Mark loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Zirconia probe location** matters. It must be in the *convection pass* (after the burner, before the stack) where gas temperature is 300–600 °C — the probe's operating range. Placement in the stack (too cold) ruins accuracy.
> 3. **Forgetting the 21% basis** in the O₂–λ relation — a common algebraic slip.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this as your last-night KTU exam revision sheet.**

- ✅ **HRSG ≠ conventional boiler.** It is a *waste-heat recovery* unit; GT exhaust is the primary heat source.
- ✅ **Counter-flow architecture** — gas flows *down*, water/steam flows *up*. Always.
- ✅ **Pinch point** = gas-side minimum ΔT at evaporator inlet. **Design: 10–20 °C.** Must be **positive**.
- ✅ **Approach point** = water-side ΔT at economiser outlet. **Design: 5–15 °C.** Limits feedwater in-economiser boiling.
- ✅ **Pressure levels** — Single < Dual < Triple. More levels → lower stack temperature → higher efficiency → higher CapEx.
- ✅ **Three-element drum level** = Level + Steam Flow + Feedwater Flow → Feedwater Valve. Single-element fails during swell/shrink.
- ✅ **Attemperator** = desuperheater. Sprays feedwater into SH stages. **MOS voting** (2oo3) on SH-outlet T/Cs.
- ✅ **Combustion control** = Master (steam pressure → fuel) + Slave (air flow) + O₂ trim (zirconia probe).
- ✅ **Excess air relation:** $\text{O}_2\% = \dfrac{21(\lambda-1)}{\lambda}\times 100$. For natural gas, **λ ≈ 1.10–1.25**.
- ✅ **CEMS** monitors NOx, SO₂, CO, O₂, opacity, flow. Mandatory for KSPCB/CPCB compliance.
- ✅ **Furnace draft** maintained at **–25 to –75 mmH₂O** by ID fan + pressure transmitter.
- ✅ **CCPP net efficiency** is 55–62 % (LHV), roughly 1.7× a stand-alone GT or ST plant.
- ✅ **Supplementary firing** raises GT exhaust from 540 °C to ~700 °C; uses a small duct burner; tightly O₂-trimmed.
- ✅ **HP/IP/LP pressures** typically 80–130 / 15–30 / 3–6 bar; steam temperatures 500–565 °C.
- ✅ **Exam trick:** Whenever a numerical is asked — *first* write the energy balance, *then* the mass/enthalpy, *then* substitute. Show units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Burner Tilting and Bypass Damper
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Burner Tilting and Bypass Damper

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Burner Tilting — Formal Definition

**Burner Tilting** (also referred to as *Tilting Burners* or *Register Tilting Mechanism*) is a sophisticated combustion control technique predominantly deployed in **tangentially-fired (corner-fired) utility boilers** of thermal power stations. The mechanism allows the entire burner assembly (register + nozzle) to be physically rotated about a horizontal pivot axis at the boiler corner, typically within a tilt range of **±30°** from the horizontal datum (with **+20° upward** and **−20° downward** being the most common operational envelope).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Burner tilting is a steam temperature control strategy wherein the inclination angle of the burner nozzles (mounted at the four corners of the furnace) is continuously varied to shift the flame envelope, thereby redistributing the radiative and convective heat fluxes between the furnace water walls, the superheater, and the reheater sections.*

### 1.2 Bypass Damper — Formal Definition

A **Bypass Damper** is a flow-control device installed in the **flue gas ducting** system of a boiler. It creates an alternate (parallel) path for the hot combustion gases, allowing a controlled fraction of the flue gas to **bypass** the convective heat transfer surface (typically the **Reheater** or the **Economiser**). The percentage of opening of the damper modulates the residence time and contact area of the hot gas with the heat exchange tubes, providing a robust, large-range means of regulating **Reheat Steam Temperature (RHST)** without water/steam-side injection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** While *Burner Tilting* primarily affects the **Furnace Heat Absorption Pattern (radiative zone)**, the *Bypass Damper* manipulates the **Convective Heat Transfer Rate** in the back-pass. Both systems are typically deployed **simultaneously** in modern supercritical units for cascade control of steam temperatures.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hand-Cranked Torchlight" and the "Kitchen Chimney Damper"

To make these two concepts immediately intuitive to a first-time learner, let us use two everyday analogies:

**Analogy A — Burner Tilting = Tilting a High-Power Flashlight in a Dark Hall**

Imagine you are standing in a long dark corridor holding a high-power flashlight (the burner flame). If you point the flashlight **straight ahead (horizontal)**, the bright spot of light (the flame envelope) lands on the **middle wall** — corresponding to the superheater absorbing the maximum heat, yielding normal steam temperature.

If you now **tilt the flashlight slightly upward**, the bright spot slides onto the **far wall near the ceiling** (the reheater and final superheater region). Suddenly, the temperature in those downstream areas rises because they are receiving more concentrated radiative energy. Conversely, **tilting the flashlight downward** shifts the hot spot toward the **floor** (furnace bottom / slag tap region), causing the upper regions to cool down.

The burner tilting mechanism works on exactly this principle: the **angle of the flame vector** is changed, which in turn changes **which part of the furnace enclosure receives the peak heat flux**.

**Analogy B — Bypass Damper = The Damper Valve in Your Kitchen's Smoke Exhaust**

Consider the metallic sliding plate (*damper*) in your kitchen's chimney exhaust pipe. When you **close** the damper, all smoke must pass through the grease filter (heat exchanger surface), heating it up intensely. When you **partially open** the damper, some smoke escapes through the alternate path *without* touching the filter, so the filter receives less hot gas and cools down.

In a power plant, the **bypass damper** plays the identical role for the **reheater tubes**. Opening the bypass damper allows flue gas to circumvent the reheater, dropping the reheat steam temperature; closing it forces all the gas through the reheater, raising the reheat temperature.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *Tilt the flame → move the heat zone (radiative control). Open the damper → divert the heat carrier (convective control).*

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants, Standard Metrics, and Typical Ranges

| Parameter | Typical Industrial Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Burner tilt angle (Up) | **+15° to +30°** | degrees (°) |
| Burner tilt angle (Down) | **−15° to −30°** | degrees (°) |
| Actuator hydraulic pressure | **160 – 250 bar** | bar |
| Bypass damper position (0%) | Fully closed — 100% gas through HX | % opening |
| Bypass damper position (100%) | Fully open — max bypass | % opening |
| Steam temp. sensitivity to tilt | **~1.5 to 2.5 °C per degree of tilt** | °C/° |
| Typical RHST control band | **±5 °C** of setpoint (e.g., 540 °C) | °C |
| Damper response time | **5 to 30 seconds** (full stroke) | s |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Burner Tilting — Detailed Operational Logic

The burner tilting mechanism in a corner-fired boiler consists of **four burners** mounted at the four corners of the furnace, all tilted by the **same angle** (either in unison or grouped in pairs) to maintain a stable, centrally-positioned fireball. The following logic steps describe the operational physics:

### Step 1 — The Tangential Fireball Geometry
In a tangentially-fired boiler, the four corner-mounted burners fire jets that are **tangent to an imaginary circle** inscribed in the furnace cross-section. These jets create a slow-rotating, doughnut-shaped fireball (the *fireball* or *flame envelope*) at the geometric centre of the furnace.

### Step 2 — Tilting Displaces the Fireball Centroid
When all four burners tilt **upward by an angle θ**, the geometry of the impingement shifts. The geometric centroid of the fireball moves **upward and away** from the furnace centre, **toward the furnace exit (nose region)**. The fireball is essentially pulled "up the chimney."

### Step 3 — Heat Flux Redistribution
The radiant heat flux distribution `q"(x,y,z)` across the furnace walls changes as a function of the tilt angle `θ`:

$$q''(x, y, z, \theta) = \int_{V_{fire}(\theta)} \frac{\kappa \cdot T_{flame}^{4}(\theta)}{\pi r^{2}} \, dV$$

where:
- `V_fire(θ)` = fireball volume, which is a **function of the tilt angle** θ
- `κ` = absorption coefficient of the flue gas (function of partial pressure of CO₂, H₂O)
- `T_flame(θ)` = adiabatic flame temperature (weakly dependent on θ)
- `r` = distance from a fireball element to the wall element

### Step 4 — Steam Temperature Response
A simplified but exam-friendly relationship between tilt angle and steam temperature is:

$$\Delta T_{steam} = K_{tilt} \cdot \theta$$

where:
- `K_tilt` = gain constant, typically **1.5 – 2.5 °C/°** of tilt
- `θ` = tilt angle in degrees (positive = upward)
- `ΔT_steam` = change in superheater/reheater outlet temperature

### Step 5 — Why Upward Tilt Raises Steam Temperature
When burners tilt up:
1. The fireball moves **closer to the superheater and reheater** (located in the back-pass / nose region).
2. **Radiative heat flux to water walls decreases** (since the fireball is now further from them).
3. **Convective heat flux to superheater/reheater increases** (gas temperature entering the back-pass is higher).
4. The boiler's steam output gets **hotter**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Burner Tilting:**
> *Tilt UP → Steam temperature INCREASES.*
> *Tilt DOWN → Steam temperature DECREASES.*
> This rule holds for both the superheater and reheater circuits.

### Step 6 — Practical Effect on NOₓ Emissions
Tilting burners **downward** increases the **residence time** of combustion gases in the **lower, cooler** zones of the furnace, promoting **staged combustion** and reducing **thermal NOₓ** formation. This makes burner tilting a **dual-purpose control**: *steam temperature regulation + NOₓ mitigation*.

---

## 2.2 Bypass Damper — Detailed Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Physical Construction
A bypass damper is essentially a **multi-louver (louvre) or rotary disc** assembly installed in a duct that **branches off from the main flue gas path** and **rejoins it downstream** of the heat exchanger it is meant to bypass. The damper modulates the cross-sectional flow area of this parallel branch.

### Step 2 — Flow Splitting
The hot flue gas leaving the furnace has a total mass flow rate `ṁ_total`. At the bifurcation point, it splits as:

$$\dot{m}_{total} = \dot{m}_{HX}(\alpha) + \dot{m}_{bypass}(\alpha)$$

where `α` is the fractional opening of the bypass damper (0 ≤ α ≤ 1). The split follows the **parallel-resistance (hydraulic) model**:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{HX}}{\dot{m}_{bypass}} = \frac{C_{d, HX} \cdot A_{HX}}{C_{d, bypass} \cdot A_{bypass}(\alpha)} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{HX}}{\Delta P_{bypass}(\alpha)}}$$

where `C_d` = discharge coefficient, `A` = effective flow area, and `ΔP` = pressure drop across each branch.

### Step 3 — Heat Duty of the Heat Exchanger
The thermal duty `Q_HX` absorbed by the reheater is:

$$Q_{HX}(\alpha) = \dot{m}_{HX}(\alpha) \cdot c_{p,gas} \cdot (T_{gas,in} - T_{gas,out, HX}(\alpha))$$

When `α` increases (damper opens), `ṁ_HX` decreases, and consequently `Q_HX` decreases → **RHST drops**.

### Step 4 — Closed-Loop Control Signal
The position command `α(t)` is generated by a **PI / PID controller** that uses the error between the measured reheat steam temperature `T_RH(t)` and its setpoint `T_RH,sp`:

$$\alpha(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau$$

where `e(t) = T_RH,sp − T_RH(t)`.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the topic "Burner Tilting and Bypass Damper" that a KTU student is expected to memorize for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Formula / Relationship | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta T_{steam} = K_{tilt} \cdot \theta$ | Steam temperature change vs. tilt angle | °C, °, °C/° |
| 2 | $q''(x,y,z,\theta) = \int \frac{\kappa T_{flame}^{4}}{\pi r^{2}} dV$ | Radiative flux to furnace wall | W/m² |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{total} = \dot{m}_{HX} + \dot{m}_{bypass}$ | Flue gas mass conservation at bifurcation | kg/s |
| 4 | $\dot{Q}_{HX} = \dot{m}_{HX} \cdot c_{p,gas} \cdot \Delta T_{gas}$ | Convective heat duty in HX | kW, kJ/kg, K |
| 5 | $\alpha(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e \, dt$ | PI controller output for damper | % opening |
| 6 | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{\dot{Q}_{loss}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV}$ | Combustion efficiency (tilt affects losses) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $NO_x \propto e^{-E_a / R \cdot T_{flame, max}}$ | Zeldovich NOₓ formation kinetics | ppm |
| 8 | $\tau_{residence} = \frac{V_{furnace}(\theta)}{\dot{V}_{gas}}$ | Gas residence time in furnace | s |
| 9 | $T_{flame, max} \approx T_{ad} \cdot (1 - \chi_{rad})$ | Peak flame temp (radiation loss factor) | K |
| 10 | $K_{tilt} \approx 1.5 - 2.5$ | Industrial gain constant for tilt | °C/° |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Burner tilting and bypass dampers are not merely textbook curiosities. They are **mission-critical** in the operation of:

1. **Supercritical and Ultra-Supercritical Boilers** (e.g., 660 MW, 800 MW, 1000 MW units) — where strict RHST control (±5 °C) is mandatory to prevent creep failure of austenitic reheat tubes.
2. **Combined-Cycle Heat Recovery Steam Generators (HRSGs)** — bypass dampers are essential for **duct firing** and **load-following**.
3. **CFB (Circulating Fluidized Bed) Boilers** — secondary air register tilting is used for **in-bed temperature profile control**.
4. **NOₓ Compliance Units** — burner tilting is a primary mechanism for meeting **MoEF/Euro VI emission norms** without SCR/SNCR hardware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tilt Angle vs. Steam Temperature Sensitivity

We begin with the fundamental **Stefan-Boltzmann law** for a participating flue gas medium. The net radiative heat transfer to the superheater tubes located at the furnace exit plane is given by the **illuminated fraction concept** used in zone modelling.

### Step 1 — Define the View Factor Geometry
For a tilted fireball whose centroid has been displaced by a vertical distance `δz` from the furnace geometric centre, the view factor `F_{fire → SH}` from the fireball to the superheater plane is:

$$F_{fire \to SH}(\theta) = F_{0} + \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} \cdot \delta z(\theta) + \frac{1}{2} \frac{\partial^{2} F}{\partial z^{2}} \cdot \delta z^{2}(\theta) + \dots$$

### Step 2 — Relate the Vertical Displacement to Tilt Angle
For a furnace of half-width `W` and corner burner pivot at the same height as the fireball centre, the geometric displacement of the fireball centroid is:

$$\delta z(\theta) = L_{arm} \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

where `L_arm` is the effective moment arm of the burner jet (typically 60–80% of the furnace half-width `W`).

### Step 3 — Combine into a Single Equation
Substituting back into the Stefan-Boltzmann net heat flux equation:

$$q_{SH}(\theta) = \sigma \cdot \epsilon_{eff} \cdot T_{flame}^{4} \cdot \left[ F_0 + \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} L_{arm} \tan(\theta) \right]$$

### Step 4 — Linearise for Small Angles
Since the operational tilt range is small (|θ| < 30°), we use the Taylor expansion $\tan(\theta) \approx \theta$ (in radians):

$$q_{SH}(\theta) \approx \sigma \epsilon_{eff} T_{flame}^{4} \left[ F_0 + \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} L_{arm} \theta \right]$$

### Step 5 — Derive the Steam Temperature Sensitivity
Using the energy balance on the superheater, the outlet steam temperature change is:

$$\Delta T_{SH} = \frac{q_{SH}(\theta) - q_{SH}(0)}{\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot c_{p,steam}}$$

Substituting and simplifying:

$$\Delta T_{SH} = \frac{\sigma \epsilon_{eff} T_{flame}^{4} \cdot \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} \cdot L_{arm}}{\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot c_{p,steam}} \cdot \theta$$

### Step 6 — Identify the Gain Constant
Defining the constant of proportionality as `K_tilt`:

$$K_{tilt} = \frac{\sigma \epsilon_{eff} T_{flame}^{4} \cdot \frac{\partial F}{\partial z} \cdot L_{arm}}{\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot c_{p,steam}}$$

The final compact form is:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{steam} = K_{tilt} \cdot \theta}$$

This justifies the empirical rule of thumb (1.5–2.5 °C per degree of tilt) used in DCS engineering.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bypass Damper Flow Splitting Ratio

The flue gas bifurcation at the bypass damper is governed by **Bernoulli's equation** in the two parallel branches.

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli Across the Heat Exchanger Branch
For the gas flowing through the heat exchanger tubes (a typical finned tube bank):

$$\Delta P_{HX} = \frac{f_{HX} \cdot L_{HX}}{D_{h, HX}} \cdot \frac{\rho_{gas} v_{HX}^{2}}{2} + K_{entry} \cdot \frac{\rho_{gas} v_{HX}^{2}}{2} + K_{exit} \cdot \frac{\rho_{gas} v_{HX}^{2}}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli Across the Bypass Branch
The bypass branch has a much lower pressure drop (typically 0.5–2 mbar vs. 8–15 mbar for the HX). The dominant term is the **damper characteristic**:

$$\Delta P_{bypass}(\alpha) = \frac{1}{C_{d}^{2}(\alpha)} \cdot \frac{\rho_{gas} v_{bypass}^{2}}{2}$$

where the discharge coefficient `C_d(α)` is a strong function of the damper opening fraction α.

### Step 3 — Equate the Pressure Drops (Parallel Branches)
By the **node pressure equality** at the bifurcation:

$$\Delta P_{HX} = \Delta P_{bypass}(\alpha) = \Delta P_{system}$$

### Step 4 — Express Velocities in Terms of Mass Flow Rates
$$v_{HX} = \frac{\dot{m}_{HX}}{\rho_{gas} A_{HX}}, \quad v_{bypass} = \frac{\dot{m}_{bypass}}{\rho_{gas} A_{bypass}(\alpha)}$$

### Step 5 — Combine and Solve for the Mass Flow Split
After substitution and algebraic manipulation (omitted here for brevity, but yielding the standard orifice-flow solution), we obtain:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{bypass}}{\dot{m}_{HX}} = A_{bypass}(\alpha) \cdot C_{d}(\alpha) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{K_{HX,total}}}$$

where $K_{HX,total}$ is the lumped resistance coefficient of the heat exchanger branch.

### Step 6 — Final Compact Form for the Bypass Fraction
Defining the bypass fraction as $\beta = \dot{m}_{bypass} / \dot{m}_{total}$:

$$\beta(\alpha) = \frac{A_{bypass}(\alpha) \cdot C_{d}(\alpha)}{\sqrt{K_{HX,total}} + A_{bypass}(\alpha) \cdot C_{d}(\alpha)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** When `α → 0` (damper closed), $\beta \to 0$ and all gas passes through the HX. When `α → 1` (fully open), $\beta \to 1$ and most gas bypasses the HX. The exact shape of the $\beta(\alpha)$ curve is **non-linear** and is typically mapped empirically during commissioning.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Burner Tilting vs. Bypass Damper

| Attribute | Burner Tilting | Bypass Damper |
|---|---|---|
| **Control Zone** | Radiative (furnace) | Convective (back-pass) |
| **Speed of Response** | Slow (15 – 60 s) | Moderate (5 – 30 s) |
| **Range of Influence** | Both SH & RHST | Primarily RHST |
| **Effect on NOₓ** | Significant (downward tilt = low NOₓ) | Minimal direct effect |
| **Mechanical Complexity** | High (hydraulic actuators, pivot bearings) | Moderate (damper drive, linkages) |
| **Maintenance Burden** | High (seals, hydraulic oil) | Moderate (blade wear, linkage) |
| **Effect on Efficiency** | Slight degradation at extreme tilts | η drops when bypass is high (energy dumped) |
| **Suitable for Cycling Duty** | Excellent (modern units) | Good but limited by thermal stress |
| **Cost of Implementation** | High (≈ ₹1–3 Cr per unit) | Moderate (≈ ₹0.5–1.5 Cr per unit) |
| **Primary Sensor Feedback** | SH/RH steam temp., furnace temp. | RH steam temp., gas temp. at HX outlet |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 500 MW tangentially-fired boiler has a burner tilt mechanism with `K_tilt = 2.0 °C/°`. The superheater outlet temperature setpoint is **540 °C**, and the measured temperature is **528 °C**. Calculate the required upward tilt angle.

### Step 1 — Identify the Error
The required temperature rise is:

$$\Delta T_{steam} = T_{sp} - T_{measured} = 540 - 528 = 12 \, °C$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Sensitivity Formula
$$\theta = \frac{\Delta T_{steam}}{K_{tilt}} = \frac{12}{2.0} = 6°$$

### Step 3 — State the Final Answer
**Answer:** The burners must be tilted **upward by 6°** to raise the SH outlet temperature by 12 °C.

### Step 4 — Cross-Check the Result
A 6° upward tilt is well within the industrial range (|θ| < 30°), so the control action is feasible and safe. The final SH outlet temperature is expected to settle at **540 °C ± 2 °C** after the transient dies out.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always express the **direction of tilt (up/down)** explicitly in your answer, along with the numerical magnitude. Marks are frequently deducted when students only write the number without the directional qualifier.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Integrated Steam Temperature Control System

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **complete control loop** integrating the burner tilting mechanism and the bypass damper, with their respective sensor and actuator interfaces, in a modern Distributed Control System (DCS).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Main Steam Temperature Sensor SH_T]:::sensor
    B[Reheat Steam Temperature Sensor RH_T]:::sensor
    C[DCS Controller SteamTemp_PID]:::controller
    D[Tilt Actuator Hydraulic Pump]:::actuator
    E[Burner Tilting Mechanism]:::physical
    F[Reheat Bypass Damper Drive]:::actuator
    G[Flue Gas Bypass Damper]:::physical
    H[Furnace Heat Flux Zone]:::zone
    I[Superheater Convective Bank]:::zone
    J[Reheater Convective Bank]:::zone
    K[Operator HMI Panel]:::hmi

    A -->|4-20 mA Signal| C
    B -->|4-20 mA Signal| C
    K -->|Manual Setpoint 540 deg C| C

    C -->|0-100 percent Command| D
    C -->|0-100 percent Command| F

    D -->|Hydraulic Oil 250 bar| E
    E -->|Rotates Burner Register| H

    F -->|Motor Drive Signal| G
    G -->|Modulates Bypass Duct Flow| J

    H -->|Radiative Heat Transfer| I
    H -->|Radiative Heat Transfer| J
    I -->|Convective Heat Transfer| A
    J -->|Convective Heat Transfer| B

    classDef sensor fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef actuator fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef physical fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef zone fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef hmi fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Figure 4.1 — Integrated architecture showing how DCS-issued commands propagate through the hydraulic and damper drive systems to influence the furnace heat flux and back-pass convective heat transfer, with temperature sensors closing the feedback loop.**

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Effect of Tilt Direction on Flame Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Normal["NORMAL TILT 0 deg"]
        N1[BURNER]:::b
        N2[FIREBALL AT FURNACE CENTRE]:::flame
        N3[SH OUTLET 540 deg C]:::out
        N1 -->|Horizontal Jets| N2
        N2 -->|Radiative Flux| N3
    end

    subgraph TiltUp["TILT UP plus 20 deg"]
        U1[BURNER TILTED UP]:::b
        U2[FIREBALL DRIFTED UP]:::flameHot
        U3[SH OUTLET 570 deg C]:::outHot
        U1 -->|Upward Jets| U2
        U2 -->|More Flux on Back Pass| U3
    end

    subgraph TiltDown["TILT DOWN minus 20 deg"]
        D1[BURNER TILTED DOWN]:::b
        D2[FIREBALL DRIFTED DOWN]:::flameCool
        D3[SH OUTLET 505 deg C]:::outCool
        D1 -->|Downward Jets| D2
        D2 -->|Less Flux on Back Pass| D3
    end

    classDef b fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flame fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flameHot fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef flameCool fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outHot fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef outCool fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Figure 4.2 — Schematic illustrating how the burner tilt angle physically displaces the fireball centroid, which in turn changes the superheater outlet steam temperature.**

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bypass Damper Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FURNACE EXIT 1100 deg C]:::furnace
    B[BIFURCAION POINT]:::node
    C[REHEATER BANK 540 deg C design]:::reheater
    D[BYPASS DUCT with DAMPER]:::bypass
    E[AIR PREHEATER ENTRY 380 deg C]:::aph
    F[REHEAT STEAM OUTLET]:::steam
    G[BYPASS GAS STREAM]:::bypassStream

    A --> B
    B -->|Flow m_HX| C
    B -->|Flow m_bypass alpha| D
    C -->|Heated Steam| F
    D -->|Hot Bypass Gas| G
    C --> E
    D --> E

    classDef furnace fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef node fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reheater fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bypass fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef aph fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef steam fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bypassStream fill:#FFC0CB,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Figure 4.3 — Flue gas bifurcation at the bypass damper, illustrating how a fraction of hot gas can be diverted away from the reheater to regulate RHST.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The two streams (through the reheater and through the bypass) eventually merge at the **Air Pre-Heater (APH) inlet**. From a control standpoint, the bypass damper therefore **trades off RHST for APH inlet temperature** — a key consideration in seasonal efficiency planning.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Burner Tilting. Explain with a neat sketch how tilting the burners upward affects the superheater outlet steam temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

Burner tilting is a steam temperature control technique used in tangentially-fired (corner-fired) boilers wherein the burner assemblies mounted at the four corners of the furnace are rotated about a horizontal pivot axis to vary the inclination angle, typically within ±30° from the horizontal.

When the burners are tilted **upward**, the geometry of the jet impingement shifts such that the **fireball centroid** migrates from the furnace geometric centre towards the **furnace exit / nose region**. As a result:

1. The radiative heat flux to the furnace water walls **decreases** (fireball is now further from the lower walls).
2. The radiative and convective heat flux to the superheater and reheater (located in the back-pass) **increases**.
3. Consequently, the **superheater outlet steam temperature rises**.

Quantitatively, the relationship is:

$$\Delta T_{steam} = K_{tilt} \cdot \theta$$

with $K_{tilt} \approx 1.5 - 2.5 \, °C/°$. An upward tilt of +10° therefore raises the SH outlet temperature by approximately **15–25 °C**.

**Keywords for 3 marks:** Definition (1) + Tilt-up mechanism (1) + Effect on SH temperature with formula (1).

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**What is a bypass damper? State its primary application and one advantage over water/steam injection for reheat temperature control.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bypass damper** is a flow-control device (louvre or rotary disc type) installed in a parallel branch of the flue gas ductwork downstream of the furnace. It modulates the fraction of hot combustion gases that bypass the convective heat exchanger (typically the reheater), thereby controlling the heat duty absorbed by the reheater.

**Primary application:** Regulation of **Reheat Steam Temperature (RHST)** in utility boilers, particularly during partial load operation where the RHST tends to rise undesirably.

**Advantage over attemperation (water/steam injection):**
- Bypass damper control is a **dry method** — it does not inject water into the reheat circuit, preserving the **reheat cycle efficiency** (no thermal penalty from the injected water heating and evaporating).
- It offers a **wider control range** with **faster response** and is more suitable for **frequent load cycling** in modern supercritical units.

**Keywords for 3 marks:** Definition (1) + Application (1) + One advantage (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Explain in detail the **construction, working principle, and control strategy** of the burner tilting mechanism used in a 500 MW tangentially-fired boiler. Include a labeled schematic in your answer.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Construction (3 marks):**
- **Four corner-mounted burner registers** (each comprising a wind box, secondary air nozzles, and oil/coal igniters) pivoted on a **horizontal hinge** at the front/rear of the boiler corner.
- **Hydraulic actuators** (typically 4 nos., one per corner) connected through **lever arms** to the registers. Operating pressure: **160 – 250 bar**.
- **Position feedback transducers** (LVDTs or rotary encoders) that provide real-time tilt angle to the DCS.
- **Synchronization linkages** ensuring all four corners tilt by the same angle to maintain fireball symmetry.
- A **hydraulic power unit (HPU)** with electric motor, oil reservoir, and servo valves.

**(ii) Working Principle (2 marks):**
When a tilt command is issued by the DCS (e.g., +10°), the servo valves direct high-pressure hydraulic oil to the actuators. The actuators extend, rotating the burner registers about the pivot. The jet trajectory changes, displacing the fireball centroid vertically. The new fireball position alters the heat flux distribution, which is detected by the SH/RH outlet temperature sensors, completing the closed loop.

**(iii) Control Strategy (2 marks):**
The DCS uses a **cascade control** structure:
- **Master loop (slow):** SH outlet temperature → setpoint for the tilt position.
- **Slave loop (fast):** Tilt position LVDT → hydraulic servo valve command.
- Typical **PI tuning**: $K_p \approx 0.5$ to $2.0 \, \%/°C$ and $K_i \approx 0.05$ to $0.2 \, \%/(°C \cdot s)$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
With the help of a suitable diagram, describe the **bypass damper system** for reheat steam temperature control. Derive the relationship between the damper opening and the heat duty of the reheater.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Diagram (3 marks):**
A schematic showing the **furnace exit → bifurcation → reheater bank + bypass duct (with damper) → merger → APH inlet**. (Reference Figure 4.3 above.)

**(ii) Working Description (2 marks):**
- At the bifurcation point, the flue gas stream splits into two parallel paths: one through the reheater, the other through the bypass duct.
- The bypass damper modulates the flow area `A_bypass(α)` and the discharge coefficient `C_d(α)`.
- The fraction of gas passing through the reheater is:

$$\dot{m}_{HX}(\alpha) = \frac{\dot{m}_{total}}{1 + A_{bypass}(\alpha) C_{d}(\alpha) / \sqrt{K_{HX,total}}}$$

- As `α` increases (damper opens), $\dot{m}_{HX}$ decreases → less heat is transferred to the reheat steam → **RHST drops**.

**(iii) Heat Duty Derivation (2 marks):**
The heat duty of the reheater is:

$$Q_{HX}(\alpha) = \dot{m}_{HX}(\alpha) \cdot c_{p,gas} \cdot (T_{gas,in} - T_{gas,out, HX}(\alpha))$$

This shows that $Q_{HX}$ is a **monotonically decreasing function of α**. Differentiating with respect to α:

$$\frac{dQ_{HX}}{d\alpha} = -\dot{m}_{total} \cdot c_{p,gas} \cdot (T_{gas,in} - T_{gas,out, HX}) \cdot \frac{d\beta(\alpha)}{d\alpha} < 0$$

confirming the inverse relationship. The RHST is then given by the energy balance on the steam side:

$$T_{RH,out} = T_{RH,in} + \frac{Q_{HX}(\alpha)}{\dot{m}_{RH,steam} \cdot c_{p,steam}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
Compare the **burner tilting mechanism** and the **bypass damper** as two competing methods of steam temperature control. Tabulate your answer covering at least **six points of comparison**.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Comparison Criterion | Burner Tilting | Bypass Damper |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Zone of Control | Radiative (furnace) | Convective (back-pass) |
| 2 | Steam circuits affected | Both SH and RHST | Primarily RHST |
| 3 | Response Time | Slow (15–60 s) | Moderate (5–30 s) |
| 4 | Range of Control | ±30° (≈ ±45–75 °C) | 0–100% (≈ ±50–80 °C) |
| 5 | Effect on NOₓ | Significant (downward tilt → low NOₓ) | Marginal |
| 6 | Effect on Boiler Efficiency | Slight loss at extreme tilts | Loss due to wasted gas heat |
| 7 | Mechanical Complexity | High (hydraulic) | Moderate (motor + linkage) |
| 8 | Cost | Higher (₹1–3 Cr) | Lower (₹0.5–1.5 Cr) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** In modern supercritical units, both systems are deployed in a **complementary** fashion — burner tilting handles the **coarse, slow control** of the radiative zone, while the bypass damper handles the **fine, fast trim** of the convective RHST. Together, they form an integrated **cascade trim system**.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
A 210 MW tangentially-fired boiler uses a burner tilting mechanism with $K_{tilt} = 2.2 \, °C/°$. The current tilt angle is **+5° (up)** and the measured SH outlet temperature is **538 °C** (setpoint: **545 °C**). Calculate:
(i) The additional tilt angle required to achieve the setpoint.
(ii) The final tilt angle (including direction).

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Calculation of additional tilt (4 marks):**

Temperature deficit to be corrected:

$$\Delta T_{steam} = 545 - 538 = +7 \, °C$$

Required change in tilt angle:

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{\Delta T_{steam}}{K_{tilt}} = \frac{+7}{2.2} = +3.18°$$

(Positive sign indicates **upward** tilt is required.)

**(ii) Final tilt angle (3 marks):**

$$\theta_{final} = \theta_{current} + \Delta \theta = (+5°) + (+3.18°) = +8.18°$$

**Final Answer:** The burners must be tilted to **+8.18° (upward)** from the horizontal datum to bring the SH outlet temperature to the setpoint of 545 °C. This is well within the industrial operational range (|θ| < 30°), so the action is feasible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Direction Omission:** Many students write only "tilt = 8.18°" without specifying **upward (+)** or **downward (−)**. This costs **1 mark minimum**.
> 2. **Sign Convention Error:** A temperature deficit (T_sp > T_measured) demands **upward tilt**, not downward. Negative tilt for positive error is a **fatal conceptual error**.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always use **degrees (°)** for tilt angle, not **radians**, unless the formula explicitly requires radians. The empirical $K_{tilt}$ values are in **°C per degree**, not per radian.
> 4. **Formula Misapplication:** Some students confuse the burner tilt formula with the bypass damper flow formula. Be clear on which control variable you are calculating.
> 5. **Missing Assumptions:** Always state the **range of validity** of the linearised formula (|θ| < 30°).
> 6. **Skipping the Conclusion:** Always end with a **feasibility check** (is the calculated θ within the operational range?). Marks are awarded for engineering judgement, not just computation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and review it 30 minutes before the exam.**

### Key Concepts
- [x] **Burner Tilting** = rotating the corner-mounted burners (typical range: **±30°**) to shift the fireball centroid.
- [x] **Bypass Damper** = modulating a parallel flue gas path to control convective heat transfer to the reheater.
- [x] Both are **steam temperature control** mechanisms, but they operate in **different zones** (radiative vs. convective).

### Golden Rules
- [x] **Tilt UP** → **Steam Temperature RISES** (fireball moves toward back-pass).
- [x] **Tilt DOWN** → **Steam Temperature FALLS** (fireball moves toward water walls).
- [x] **Damper OPEN** → **RHST FALLS** (less gas through reheater).
- [x] **Damper CLOSE** → **RHST RISES** (all gas forced through reheater).

### Key Formula
- [x] $\Delta T_{steam} = K_{tilt} \cdot \theta$ where $K_{tilt} \approx 1.5$ to $2.5 \, °C/°$.

### Typical Industrial Ranges (Memory Anchors)
- [x] Burner tilt range: **±15° to ±30°**
- [x] Hydraulic actuator pressure: **160 – 250 bar**
- [x] Damper full stroke time: **5 – 30 seconds**
- [x] RHST control band: **±5 °C of setpoint (typically 540 °C)**

### Pros & Cons to Remember
- [x] Burner tilting also helps **reduce NOₓ** when tilted downward (staged combustion effect).
- [x] Bypass damper is a **dry control method** — superior to attemperation for efficiency.
- [x] Both systems together provide **coarse-fine (cascade) trim** of SH and RHST.

### Common Exam Traps
- [x] Always specify the **direction (up/down)** in your tilt angle answer.
- [x] Always verify the calculated angle is **within the feasible range**.
- [x] Do not confuse the **radiative control (tilt)** with the **convective control (damper)**.

### One-Line Definitions (for 2-mark sub-questions)
- [x] *Burner Tilting:* "A combustion control technique that varies the inclination angle of the corner burners to shift the fireball centroid and thereby control steam temperature."
- [x] *Bypass Damper:* "A flue-gas flow modulation device that creates a parallel bypass path to the convective heat exchanger, used primarily for reheat steam temperature control."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Temperature Measurement in Boilers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Metal Temperature Measurement in Boilers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** *Metal Temperature Measurement in Boilers* refers to the instrumentation techniques employed to continuously monitor the surface and bulk metal temperatures of critical pressure-bearing components in a boiler system — including water walls, screen tubes, superheater (SH) and reheater (RH) tubes, headers, and economizer inlets — to prevent metallurgical failure, ensure operational safety, and optimize thermal efficiency.

The measurement is performed using **contact-type temperature sensors** (primarily Mineral-Insulated Metal-Sheathed — MIMS — thermocouples) that are mechanically, thermally, or metallurgically bonded to the parent tube/shell so that the sensor junction tracks the **true metal temperature (TMT)** as closely as possible.

### Critical Monitoring Locations in a Modern Utility Boiler

| Location | Typical Metal Temp Range (°C) | Criticality |
|---|---|---|
| Furnace Water-Wall Tubes | 350 – 550 | High (membrane panel integrity) |
| Screen Tubes (Platen) | 450 – 600 | Medium |
| Superheater Inlet (SH I) | 450 – 580 | High (first stage) |
| Superheater Outlet / Final SH | 540 – 620 | **Very High** (creep zone) |
| Reheater Hot Leg | 540 – 600 | **Very High** (creep zone) |
| Primary Reheater Inlet | 330 – 450 | High |
| Economizer Inlet / Outlet | 250 – 360 | Medium |
| Steam Headers | 450 – 600 | High (thermal stress) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fever Thermometer" of a Boiler

Imagine a human body running a high fever. The doctor does not just measure the air around the patient — she presses a thermometer **against the skin or under the tongue** to capture the *internal* core temperature. A boiler behaves identically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** The *steam* inside a tube is like a "patient's breath," and the *flame* outside is like the "ambient room." Neither tells you the *true* state of the "patient's body" (the metal tube wall). If the metal temperature exceeds its metallurgical design limit (the "fatal fever"), the tube **creeps, swells, ruptures, or bursts catastrophically** — even if the steam thermometer reads perfectly normal. Therefore, we must **clamp, weld, or peen** a sensor *directly* onto the metal itself.

## 1.3 Why Metal Temperature Is Measured — The "Why" Engineer's Checklist

- **Creep Prevention:** At sustained metal temperatures above **0.4 × T<sub>melting</sub> (in Kelvin)**, ferritic steels undergo *time-dependent plastic deformation* called **creep**. For **T91** tubes, this limit is ~600 °C; for **SS304H**, ~650 °C; for **Inconel 617**, ~750 °C.
- **Tube Burst Protection:** A 1 % wall-thickness loss combined with a 25 °C metal-temperature excursion can halve tube life.
- **Thermal Stress & Cycling:** During start-up, shutdown, and load swings, the differential between ID and OD wall temperatures produces **axial and circumferential thermal stresses**.
- **Fouling & Slagging Detection:** Localized hot spots on water-wall tubes indicate **slag buildup, refractory loss, or flame impingement**.
- **Process Optimization (Attemperation Control):** Spray-water attemperators use superheater outlet metal temperature feedback to throttle desuperheating spray.
- **Lifetime Assessment:** Continuous metal temperature data feeds into the **ASME Section I / Section VIII** remaining-life calculations and **EPRI** damage models.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The sensor selected for boiler metal-temperature measurement is **almost always a MIMS (Mineral-Insulated Metal-Sheathed) thermocouple**, *not* an RTD, because (a) it is robust, (b) it can be bent and formed into the tube contour, (c) it survives vibration, and (d) it has an upper use limit of **1260 °C** for Type K and **1300 °C** for Type N (per **IEC 60584**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sensor Architecture — Anatomy of a MIMS Thermocouple

A MIMS (Mineral-Insulated Metal-Sheathed) thermocouple consists of:

1. **Hot (Measuring) Junction** — formed by welding the two dissimilar thermoelements (e.g., Chromel–Alumel for Type K, Nicrosil–Nisil for Type N, Chromel–Constantan for Type E).
2. **Thermoelements** — two wires of specified alloy, standard sizes 0.5 mm, 1.0 mm, 1.5 mm, 3.0 mm diameter.
3. **Mineral Insulation** — densely packed **Magnesium Oxide (MgO, ~96 % purity)** or **Alumina (Al<sub>2</sub>O<sub>3</sub>)** powder that provides electrical isolation and mechanical support.
4. **Metal Sheath** — typically **SS316**, **SS310**, **Inconel 600**, **Inconel 625**, or **Haynes 230** depending on environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The sheath selection is the **single most critical design decision** in boiler metal-temperature work. **Inconel 600** is the workhorse for superheater/reheater zones (oxidation-resistant up to 1150 °C in clean flue gas). **SS310** is acceptable for water-wall zones. **Plain carbon-steel sheaths must never be used** above 550 °C because they oxidize and decarburize rapidly.

## 2.2 Installation Methods — Bonding the Sensor to the Tube

| Method | Description | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Peening** | Sensor tip arc-welded/pinned into a 6 mm hole drilled into the tube wall, then hammer-peened flush | Best thermal response (τ<sub>63</sub> ≈ 0.8 s) | Requires tube to be drained; intrusive |
| **Weld Pad (Tig-welded boss)** | A small pad is TIG-welded to the tube; the TC is then either peened into the pad or strapped | Reversible; low intrusion | Adds 1–2 °C thermal resistance |
| **Cap Strap / Band Clamp** | Sensor held against tube by SS band & thermal-paste interface | Easy retrofit | Worst lag (τ<sub>63</sub> ≈ 5–8 s) |
| **Pad-Welded with Thermocouple Integral** | TC sheath is itself continuously TIG-welded to the tube over a 25–50 mm length | Best for high-temperature cycling zones | Permanent; not replaceable |
| **Bayonet / Spring-Loaded** | TC inserted into a welded socket with thermal compound | Field-replaceable | Air-gap error |

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Response Time (Time Constant):** The **first-order thermal time constant τ** is the time taken for the sensor output to reach **63.2 %** of a step change. For a peened MIMS TC of 1.5 mm OD, **τ ≈ 0.8 – 1.5 s** in still air; for a 3 mm clamped TC, **τ ≈ 3 – 8 s**. Per **IEC 60515**, this is the metric used to grade the sensor's "speed class."

## 2.3 Heat-Transfer at the Bonded Junction — The Fundamental Physics

When the sensor is bonded to the tube, three resistances dominate the heat-flow path:

1. **Conduction along the thermocouple sheath** (from the welded pad to the cold end) — a *heat-sink* effect that **lowers** the apparent reading.
2. **Conduction through the gas/flue side** (the environment).
3. **Conduction through the tube metal itself**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **largest source of error** in metal-temperature measurement is **stem conduction (fin-effect) error** — the thermocouple sheath acts as a *fin* that conducts heat away from the hot junction into the colder remote junction. This error can be **2 °C to 8 °C** if the immersion depth is inadequate.

### Immersion-Depth Rule (Mandatory KTU Concept)

A practical rule-of-thumb is:

$$L_{immersion} \;\geq\; 10 \times d_{sheath}$$

where **L<sub>immersion</sub>** is the depth of TC inserted *into* the hot zone (along the tube) and **d<sub>sheath</sub>** is the outer diameter of the sheath. For a 3 mm OD TC, the pad/strap must extend at least **30 mm** along the tube contour from the hot junction. Failing this rule introduces stem-conduction error of:

$$\Delta T_{stem} \;\approx\; \frac{T_{tube} - T_{ambient}}{\cosh\!\left(\dfrac{L_{immersion}}{d_{sheath}}\right)}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **E<sub>ab</sub>** | Seebeck EMF | $E_{ab} = \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_{ab}(T)\,dT$ | mV / V | Fundamental TC equation |
| **τ** | Thermal time constant | $T(t) = T_{final} - (T_{final}-T_{initial})\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | s | First-order lag |
| **τ<sub>63</sub>** | Time to 63.2 % step | $\tau_{63} = \tau$ | s | IEC 60515 standard |
| **L/d ratio** | Immersion rule | $L \geq 10\,d$ | — | Prevents stem-conduction error |
| **ΔT<sub>stem</sub>** | Stem-conduction error | $\Delta T_{stem} \approx \dfrac{\Delta T_{0}}{\cosh(L/d)}$ | °C | Negligible for $L/d > 20$ |
| **T<sub>creep</sub>** | Creep onset | $T_{creep} \approx 0.4\,T_{melting}$ | K (Kelvin) | Metallurgical limit |
| **R<sub>insul</sub>** | Insulation resistance | $R \geq 100\,\text{M}\Omega$ at 50 V | Ω | Min per IEC 61515 |
| **R<sub>t</sub>(T)** | RTD resistance (PT100) | $R_t = R_0\,[1 + A\,T + B\,T^2 + C\,(T-100)\,T^3]$ | Ω | Callendar–Van Dusen |
| **ITS-90** | Temperature Standard | Polynomial reference function | °C | Mandatory for thermocouples |
| **ΔT<sub>max</sub>** | Allowable overshoot | $\Delta T_{max} = T_{design} - T_{alarm}$ | °C | Trip margin |

> [!WARNING]
> In the above table, the absolute value `|x|` is **deliberately replaced** with explicit difference notation `(T − T_ref)` because the pipe character would break the Markdown table parser. In LaTeX math contexts, you may freely use $\vert x \vert$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Generation:** Provides the input signal to **Boiler Protection Systems (BPS)** and **Drum-Level-Trip interlock chains**.
- **Predictive Maintenance:** Feeds **EPRI's Boiler Tube Failure (BTF) Database** and **Remaining Life Assessment (RLA)** models.
- **Process Industries:** Used in **ethylene-cracker furnace coils, ammonia primary reformers, hydrocracker reactors** — anywhere the metal temperature is the limiting life factor.
- **Emission Compliance:** Metal-temperature monitoring of **waste-heat boiler** sections in process gas plants enables cleanest combustion zone tuning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Seebeck EMF Equation for a Thermocouple

The fundamental governing law for any thermocouple is the **Seebeck Effect**. The derivation proceeds from thermodynamic principles.

### Step 1: Define the absolute Seebeck coefficient

The absolute Seebeck coefficient $S_a(T)$ of a single material $a$ is defined as the open-circuit voltage produced per unit temperature difference, assuming one junction is at absolute zero (0 K):

$$S_a(T) = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{V_a}{\Delta T} \quad \left[\dfrac{\mu V}{K}\right]$$

**Interpretation:** $S_a(T)$ is an *intrinsic* material property; for Chromel it is positive, for Alumel it is slightly positive, but for Constantan it is negative — which is why Chromel–Constantan (Type E) gives ~68 µV/°C, the highest of the standard thermocouples.

### Step 2: Combine two dissimilar materials

When a junction is formed between two conductors $a$ and $b$ held at temperatures $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold, reference), the net EMF observed at the open ends is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{ab}(T_1, T_2) &= \int_{T_2}^{T_1} S_a(T)\,dT - \int_{T_2}^{T_1} S_b(T)\,dT \\
&= \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \left[S_a(T) - S_b(T)\right] dT \\
&= \int_{T_2}^{T_1} S_{ab}(T)\,dT
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The total voltage equals the difference between the *absolute* Seebeck voltages generated in each leg, which collapses into the **relative Seebeck coefficient** $S_{ab}(T)$ — a tabulated function (e.g., per **ITS-90**).

### Step 3: Apply the reference (cold) junction

In practice, $T_2 \neq 0\,\text{K}$ but equals the **Reference Junction Temperature** $T_{ref}$ — usually the terminal-block temperature or a software-compensated ice-point equivalent. The measurement equation becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{measured} &= \int_{T_{ref}}^{T_{hot}} S_{ab}(T)\,dT \\
T_{hot} &= f^{-1}\!\left(E_{measured} + \int_{0}^{T_{ref}} S_{ab}(T)\,dT \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The instrument (or DCS) solves the inverse function to recover the hot-junction temperature.

## 3.2 Derivation — Stem-Conduction Error (The "Fin Effect")

Consider a cylindrical thermocouple sheath of diameter $d$ immersed a depth $L$ into a hot surface at temperature $T_s$ while the ambient is $T_\infty$. Heat flowing out of the hot junction by conduction through the sheath obeys the 1-D fin equation.

### Step 1: Set up the fin differential equation

The temperature profile $T(x)$ along the sheath from the hot tip ($x = 0$) to the cold end ($x = L$) satisfies:

$$
\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2\,\theta = 0
$$

where $\theta(x) = T(x) - T_\infty$ and the **fin parameter** is:

$$m = \sqrt{\dfrac{h\,P}{k\,A}}$$

Here $h$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient, $P$ = perimeter ($\pi d$), $A$ = cross-section ($\pi d^2/4$), and $k$ = thermal conductivity of the sheath.

### Step 2: Solve the homogeneous ODE

The general solution is:

$$
\theta(x) = C_1\,e^{m x} + C_2\,e^{-m x}
$$

### Step 3: Apply boundary conditions

- **BC 1:** At the cold end, $x = L$: $T(L) = T_\infty \;\Rightarrow\; \theta(L) = 0$.
- **BC 2:** At the hot tip, $x = 0$: $T(0) = T_{s,actual}$ (true surface).

Applying BC 1:

$$
0 = C_1\,e^{m L} + C_2\,e^{-m L} \;\Rightarrow\; C_1 = -C_2\,e^{-2 m L}
$$

### Step 4: Reduce to hyperbolic form

Substituting back:

$$
\theta(x) = C_2\!\left(e^{-mx} - e^{-2mL}\,e^{mx}\right) = C'_2 \sinh\!\left[m(L - x)\right]
$$

Hence the temperature profile is:

$$
T(x) = T_\infty + C'_2\,\sinh\!\left[m(L - x)\right]
$$

### Step 5: Find the apparent tip temperature

At $x = 0$, the *apparent* tip temperature $T_{tip,app}$ differs from the actual surface temperature by the **fin-effect error**:

$$
\Delta T_{stem} = T_{tip,app} - T_{s,actual} = -\theta(0)\!\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\cosh(mL)}\right]
$$

In its most widely cited form (assuming perfect thermal contact at the tip):

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta T_{stem} \;\approx\; (T_{s} - T_\infty)\!\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\cosh(mL)}\right]\;}
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

- As $m L \to \infty$, $\cosh(m L) \to \infty$ and $\Delta T_{stem} \to 0$ — *deep immersion eliminates the error*.
- For a 3 mm OD Inconel sheath with $k \approx 14$ W/m·K, $h \approx 50$ W/m²·K (still air), the fin parameter is $m \approx 110\,\text{m}^{-1}$, requiring $L \geq 30$ mm to keep $\Delta T_{stem} < 1\,°\text{C}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Thermocouple Type

| Parameter | Type K (Chromel–Alumel) | Type N (Nicrosil–Nisil) | Type E (Chromel–Constantan) | Type R/S (Pt–PtRh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max useful range** | –200 to **+1260 °C** | –200 to **+1300 °C** | –200 to **+900 °C** | 0 to **+1480 °C** |
| **Sensitivity @ 600 °C** | ~43 µV/°C | ~38 µV/°C | ~67 µV/°C | ~10 µV/°C |
| **Oxidation resistance** | Good (suffers "green rot" in low O₂) | Excellent (no green rot) | Good | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Best boiler use** | Water walls, screen tubes, headers | **Superheater, reheater (preferred)** | High-precision lab | Reheater / SH in ultrasupercritical |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever a question mentions a "**reheater outlet in a 600 °C ultrasupercritical boiler**," the correct answer for sensor selection is **Type N (Nicrosil–Nisil) thermocouple with Inconel 600 sheath** because of (a) superior resistance to "green-rot" sulphur attack at low oxygen partial pressure, and (b) long-term drift stability at >1100 °C.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Trip-Margin Calculation

**Problem:** A superheater outlet has a design metal temperature of **620 °C**. The creep-onset limit of the alloy is **0.4 × T<sub>melting</sub> = 0.4 × (1400 + 273) K = 669 K** — wait, we must use **0.4 of the *absolute* melting temperature in Kelvin**. Recompute:

- T<sub>melting</sub> = 1400 °C = 1673 K
- 0.4 × 1673 K = 669 K = **396 °C** — this is too low, so the "0.4 rule" is for *creep strain onset* in ferritic steels at short duration. For austenitic SS304H, the **design limit** per **ASME Section I** is **705 °C / 1300 °F**.

A more useful exam-style number is the **trip margin** $\Delta T_{margin}$:

$$
\Delta T_{margin} = T_{trip} - T_{normal}
$$

If normal operating metal temperature is **600 °C** and the trip is set at **625 °C**, the margin is:

$$
\Delta T_{margin} = 625 - 600 = 25\,°\text{C}
$$

The **alarm-to-trip delay** $t_{delay}$ should satisfy the **creep-rupture law** (Larson–Miller):

$$
LMP = \dfrac{T_K}{1000}\!\left[15 + \log_{10}(t_{hours})\right]
$$

Solving for $t$ at $T = 625\,°\text{C} = 898\,\text{K}$ and assuming $LMP_{allow} = 24$ (typical for design life of 100,000 h):

$$
\begin{aligned}
24 &= \dfrac{898}{1000}\,[15 + \log_{10} t] \\[4pt]
26.726 &= 15 + \log_{10} t \\[4pt]
\log_{10} t &= 11.726 \\[4pt]
t &= 5.32 \times 10^{11}\text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** At 625 °C, the tube will not rupture within plant lifetime, so the **trip is conservatively set 20–30 °C below the metallurgical limit** to leave measurement uncertainty margin.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**List any three reasons why metal temperature is measured directly in a boiler rather than relying on steam-side temperature.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Metallurgical protection — creep & rupture:** Tube material (e.g., SS304H, T91) loses strength rapidly above its design metal temperature (~600 °C for ferritics). Steam temperature may read "normal" while local hot spots on the tube wall exceed the creep limit, leading to **tube burst** (1 mark).
2. **Lag-free response:** Steam temperature inside a tube is a *bulk* measurement, heavily damped by convection. Metal temperature responds within **~0.8 s** (peened TC) to actual heat-flux changes, enabling **fast trip action** (1 mark).
3. **Detection of localized anomalies:** Slag build-up, refractory loss, flame impingement, and tube-side fouling produce **localized hot spots** on the metal that a steam RTD cannot resolve but a bonded TC can (1 mark).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define "stem-conduction error" in a metal-temperature thermocouple and state the practical rule to minimize it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stem-conduction error is the **temperature difference between the *actual* metal surface temperature and the *apparent* temperature indicated by the thermocouple hot junction**, caused by **heat flowing along the thermocouple sheath** (which acts as a fin) from the hot tip to the colder remote end (1 mark).

**Practical rule:** The immersion length $L$ of the thermocouple along the hot surface must satisfy the **"Ten-Times-Diameter" rule** (2 marks):

$$L \;\geq\; 10 \times d_{sheath}$$

For a 3 mm OD MIMS TC, $L \geq 30$ mm; for a 6 mm OD, $L \geq 60$ mm.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q. (a) Explain the construction and working of a Mineral-Insulated Metal-Sheathed (MIMS) thermocouple used for boiler metal-temperature measurement. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A MIMS thermocouple (per **IEC 60515 / IEC 61515**) is a robust, hermetically sealed temperature sensor consisting of the following parts (2 marks):

1. **Thermoelements** — two dissimilar-alloy wires (e.g., **Chromel (+) and Alumel (–)** for Type K, or **Nicrosil (+) and Nisil (–)** for Type N). Standard diameters: 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 3.0 mm.
2. **Hot Junction** — formed by **capacitor-discharge welding** of the two thermoelements at one end.
3. **Mineral Insulation** — densely compacted **Magnesium Oxide (MgO, ~96 % purity)** powder surrounding the wires. It provides (a) electrical isolation between conductors and sheath, and (b) mechanical support against vibration (1 mark).
4. **Metal Sheath** — a seamless tube of **Inconel 600**, **SS316**, or **SS310** that is mechanically swaged and drawn to compress the MgO. It is hermetically sealed at the cold end by a moisture-resistant seal.
5. **Cold-End Termination** — potted with epoxy, fitted with a terminal head, or supplied as flying leads.

**Working Principle:** Based on the **Seebeck Effect**, the temperature difference between the welded hot junction (on the boiler tube) and the cold junction (at the instrument terminals) generates a **millivolt EMF** proportional to the temperature difference (1 mark). The EMF is linearized per the **ITS-90** polynomial look-up tables stored in the DCS (1 mark).

**Installation in boilers:** The hot tip is **peened** into a small hole drilled in the tube, or **TIG-welded** to a small boss. The sheath is then strapped/welded along the tube contour for at least **L = 10d** to prevent stem-conduction error (1 mark).

**Why MIMS is preferred in boilers:** (a) can be bent to conform to tube curvature; (b) withstands vibration and mechanical shock; (c) hermetically sealed — immune to moisture ingress; (d) long-term stability; (e) available in miniature 0.5 mm OD for minimal thermal-mass (1 mark).

---

**Q. (b) A boiler reheater outlet is operated at 595 °C design metal temperature with Type N thermocouples (Inconel 600 sheath, 3 mm OD). Calculate (i) the minimum permissible immersion length to limit stem-conduction error to < 1 °C, and (ii) the corresponding Seebeck EMF at 595 °C reference 0 °C. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Immersion Length Calculation (3 marks):**

The fin-effect stem-conduction error is given by:

$$
\Delta T_{stem} = (T_s - T_\infty)\!\left[1 - \dfrac{1}{\cosh(mL)}\right]
$$

For a 3 mm OD Inconel 600 sheath:
- $k_{Inconel} \approx 14\,\text{W/m·K}$
- $h \approx 50\,\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$ (still-air ambient on the "cold" end side)
- $P = \pi d = \pi \times 0.003 = 9.42 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$
- $A = \pi d^2/4 = 7.07 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2$

$$
m = \sqrt{\dfrac{hP}{kA}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{50 \times 9.42 \times 10^{-3}}{14 \times 7.07 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{4757} \approx 68.98\,\text{m}^{-1}
$$

Set $\Delta T_{stem} \leq 1\,°\text{C}$, with $(T_s - T_\infty) \approx 570\,°\text{C}$ (assuming $T_\infty = 25\,°\text{C}$):

$$
\cosh(mL) \geq \dfrac{570}{570 - 1} = 1.00176
$$

For small arguments, $\cosh(x) \approx 1 + x^2/2$, so:

$$
1 + \dfrac{(mL)^2}{2} \geq 1.00176 \;\Rightarrow\; (mL)^2 \geq 0.00351
$$

$$
L \geq \dfrac{\sqrt{0.00351}}{68.98} \approx 8.58 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.858\,\text{mm}
$$

This is far below the **"10-d rule"** of $L \geq 30$ mm, so the practical binding constraint is the **immersion rule**, not the math (1 mark):

$$
\boxed{L_{min} = 30\,\text{mm} \;\;(\text{per the Ten-Diameter rule})}
$$

**(ii) Seebeck EMF at 595 °C, Reference 0 °C (4 marks):**

For a Type N (Nicrosil–Nisil) thermocouple per **ITS-90 / IEC 60584**, the polynomial from 0 °C to 1300 °C is tabulated. At 595 °C, the inverse polynomial gives an EMF of approximately **20.50 mV** (1 mark).

The full forward polynomial for 0 °C ≤ T ≤ 1300 °C (Type N, per NIST ITS-90) yields (selected terms):

$$
E(T) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} a_i\,T^i \quad [\mu\text{V}]
$$

Using the standard tabulated coefficients and evaluating at $T = 595\,°\text{C}$ (1 mark):

- $a_0 = 0$
- $a_1 = 2.61534 \times 10^{1}$
- $a_2 = 1.09276 \times 10^{-2}$
- $a_3 = -4.58789 \times 10^{-6}$
- $a_4 = 2.26528 \times 10^{-9}$
- $a_5 = -5.80710 \times 10^{-13}$
- $a_6 = 6.32248 \times 10^{-17}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(595) &= 26.1534 \times 595 + 0.010928 \times 595^2 - 4.5879\times 10^{-6}\times 595^3 \\
&\quad + 2.2653\times 10^{-9}\times 595^4 - 5.807\times 10^{-13}\times 595^5 \\
&\quad + 6.322\times 10^{-17}\times 595^6 \\
&\approx 15561.3 + 3869.6 - 965.7 + 284.7 - 69.4 + 5.2 \\
&\approx 18685\,\mu\text{V} \;\approx\; 18.69\,\text{mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The polynomial is a fit to the **NIST Type-N reference table**; values can also be looked up directly in NIST ITS-90 tables for verification. Tabulated table values typically cite $E_{N}(595\,°\text{C}) \approx 18.69$ mV (1 mark).

**Cold-junction compensation** (if reference is at 25 °C and not 0 °C):

$$
E_{hot,0°C} = E_{hot,T_{ref}} + E_{T_{ref},0°C} = 18.69 + 0.991 \approx 19.68\,\text{mV}
$$

where $E_{25°C} \approx 0.991$ mV for Type N (1 mark).

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]

**Q. (a) Discuss the various installation methods for bonding thermocouples to boiler tubes, comparing their advantages, disadvantages, and typical applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The method of bonding the thermocouple to the boiler tube determines the **measurement accuracy, response time, replaceability, and long-term reliability**. Five methods are widely used in KTU-relevant power plant practice (1 mark):

**1. Peening (1.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** A 1.5 – 2 mm deep hole is drilled (or arc-air gouged) into the tube wall; the TC tip is inserted and then **mechanically hammered ("peened")** so that the sheath material plastically deforms and locks the tip in intimate metallic contact with the tube.
- **Advantages:** Best thermal contact → **τ₆₃ ≈ 0.8 s**; very low measurement error; immune to vibration.
- **Disadvantages:** Tube must be drained and cooled; sensor is non-replaceable; small stress concentration.
- **Application:** Final superheater, reheater, and other high-criticality permanent locations.

**2. TIG-Welded Boss / Pad (1.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** A small austenitic pad (~15 × 8 × 3 mm) is TIG-welded onto the tube surface; the TC is either peened into the pad or strapped.
- **Advantages:** Reversible — the boss can be removed and re-welded; the tube itself is not drilled.
- **Disadvantages:** Adds ~1 °C thermal resistance; pad can fall off if weld quality is poor.
- **Application:** Water walls, screen tubes, headers, platen superheaters.

**3. Continuous Pad Welding (1.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** The TC sheath is continuously TIG-welded along a 25–50 mm length directly onto the tube.
- **Advantages:** Excellent thermal coupling along a long immersion length — virtually eliminates stem-conduction error.
- **Disadvantages:** Permanent; not field-replaceable; requires skilled welders.
- **Application:** High-temperature cycling zones where stem-conduction error must be minimized.

**4. Cap Strap / Band Clamp (1.5 marks):**
- **Procedure:** The TC tip is held against the tube by a stainless-steel band, with high-temperature thermal compound (e.g., Omega Thermocouple Cement) at the interface.
- **Advantages:** Quick retrofit; reversible.
- **Disadvantages:** Worst response time (τ₆₃ ≈ 5–8 s); thermal-paste dries out.
- **Application:** Economizer, low-temperature headers, temporary monitoring.

**5. Bayonet / Spring-Loaded Socket (1 mark):**
- **Procedure:** A socket is welded to the tube; the TC is spring-loaded against the socket bottom.
- **Advantages:** Field-replaceable.
- **Disadvantages:** Air-gap error (2–3 °C).
- **Application:** Drum-level, attemperator spray pipes, low-temperature auxiliary lines.

---

**Q. (b) A superheater tube is constructed of SS304H with design metal temperature 620 °C and tube OD 38 mm × wall thickness 6 mm. The creep-rupture strength at 100,000 h is 100 MPa. The Larson-Miller parameter (LMP) for this alloy is given by LMP = 23 at 100,000 h. Estimate the maximum allowable operating time at 650 °C and at 580 °C, and comment on the significance for the trip setting. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Larson-Miller Parameter** is the standard time-temperature parameter for creep life prediction:

$$
LMP = \dfrac{T_K}{1000}\left[15 + \log_{10}(t_{hours})\right]
$$

where $T_K = T_{°C} + 273.15$.

**Case 1: At 650 °C (3 marks):**

$T_K = 650 + 273.15 = 923.15\,\text{K}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
23 &= \dfrac{923.15}{1000}\!\left[15 + \log_{10} t\right] \\[4pt]
\log_{10} t + 15 &= \dfrac{23 \times 1000}{923.15} = 24.916 \\[4pt]
\log_{10} t &= 9.916 \\[4pt]
t &= 10^{9.916} \approx 8.24 \times 10^{9}\,\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2: At 580 °C (3 marks):**

$T_K = 580 + 273.15 = 853.15\,\text{K}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
23 &= \dfrac{853.15}{1000}\!\left[15 + \log_{10} t\right] \\[4pt]
\log_{10} t + 15 &= \dfrac{23 \times 1000}{853.15} = 26.961 \\[4pt]
\log_{10} t &= 11.961 \\[4pt]
t &= 10^{11.961} \approx 9.15 \times 10^{11}\,\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Trip-Setting Significance (1 mark):**

A 30 °C increase from 580 °C → 650 °C reduces creep life by **a factor of ~111** (from $9.15 \times 10^{11}$ to $8.24 \times 10^{9}$ hours). Therefore, the **Hi-Hi metal-temperature trip** must be set with a tight margin (typically **20–30 °C below** the design temperature) so that the tube never operates in the catastrophic-creep regime.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating "steam temperature" with "metal temperature":** Many students answer metal-temperature questions using *steam-side* RTD data. KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this confusion. Always distinguish: *T<sub>steam</sub> ≠ T<sub>metal</sub>*.
> 2. **Forgetting stem-conduction error:** When asked about TC installation on a tube, at least **1–2 marks** are reserved for the immersion-depth rule $L \geq 10d$. Skipping this is a common, costly omission.
> 3. **Using Type K on a 1300 °C reheater:** Type K suffers from "**green rot**" (chromium oxidation in low-O₂ reducing flue gas) above 1000 °C, causing rapid drift. Always specify **Type N with Inconel 600 sheath** for high-temperature boiler zones.
> 4. **Forgetting Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** A bare EMF reading is meaningless without a referenced CJC. The exam often awards **1 mark** for the CJC line item.
