```mermaid
    Q0[Start: Volumetric Flow Reading Qact] --> Q1{Is fluid ideal gas?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is pressure constant?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Use steam tables directly]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Compute KT = sqrt Tactual over Tstd]
    Q4 --> Q5[Compute Qstd = Qact times KT]
    Q2 -- No --> Q6[Use combined KPT correction]
    Q6 --> Q7[KPT = sqrt of Pstd Tactual over Pactual Tstd]
    Q7 --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q8[Display Corrected Flow]
    Q3 --> Q8
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Apr 2024]

**Define the temperature correction factor for air flow measurement. Why is it a square-root function?**

**Model Answer:**
The temperature correction factor **K_T** is a dimensionless number used to convert a flowmeter's indicated volumetric flow rate from the actual operating temperature to a reference (standard) temperature. It is defined as:

$$K_T \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{T_{actual}}{T_{std}}}$$

It is a **square-root function** because differential-pressure flowmeters derive flow from √(ΔP/ρ), and density ρ varies inversely with absolute temperature T. Therefore, the indicated volumetric flow scales with √T, and the correction must also take the square-root form to properly compensate. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for the why-√T explanation)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Aug 2023]

**List any three types of pressure-measuring instruments used for steam in a power plant and state one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Bourdon tube pressure gauge** — Used for local indication of boiler drum pressure (up to 200 bar). **(1 mark)**
2. **Diaphragm-type differential pressure transmitter** — Used for measuring steam flow across orifice plates in the main steam line. **(1 mark)**
3. **Strain-gauge pressure transducer** — Used as a precision sensor feeding the turbine-inlet-pressure trip circuit. **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the temperature correction factor for the volumetric flow rate of air measured by an orifice meter. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flowmeter reads **2500 Nm³/h** of air at an operating temperature of **80 °C**. The standard reference temperature is **25 °C**. Determine the **actual volumetric flow rate in m³/h** at the operating condition. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

1. **Assumption list** (1 mark):
   - Air behaves as an ideal gas.
   - Absolute pressure remains constant between operating and standard states.
   - Orifice discharge coefficient C_d is temperature-invariant.
   - Steady-state, one-dimensional flow.

2. **Step 1** — Bernoulli + continuity across orifice:
$$Q_{actual} \;=\; C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho_{actual}}}$$
**(1 mark)**

3. **Step 2** — Standard-condition expression:
$$Q_{std} \;=\; C_d \, A_2 \, \sqrt{\frac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho_{std}}}$$
**(1 mark)**

4. **Step 3** — Ratio:
$$\frac{Q_{std}}{Q_{actual}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{actual}}{\rho_{std}}}$$
**(1 mark)**

5. **Step 4** — Ideal gas substitution $\rho = P/(R\,T)$:
$$K_T \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{T_{actual}}{T_{std}}}$$
**(2 marks)**

6. **Step 5** — Conclusion: Q_std = Q_actual × K_T. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1** — Convert temperatures to Kelvin: **(1 mark)**
$$T_{actual} = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15 \; \text{K}$$
$$T_{std} = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 2** — Compute K_T: **(2 marks)**
$$K_T = \sqrt{\frac{353.15}{298.15}} = \sqrt{1.1845} = 1.0884$$

**Step 3** — Invert correction to get actual flow: **(2 marks)**
$$Q_{actual} = \frac{Q_{std}}{K_T} = \frac{2500}{1.0884} = 2296.9 \; \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4** — Conclusion with units and statement: **(2 marks)**
The actual operating flow rate is **≈ 2297 m³/h**, which is lower than the standard reading because hot air is less dense.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge** for steam pressure measurement. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working of a **thermocouple-based temperature measurement system** for superheated steam with reference-junction compensation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (4 marks):**
- C-shaped (or helical) elastic metal tube of elliptical cross-section, fixed at one end and free at the other.
- Free end connected via mechanical linkage, gear sector, and pinion to a pointer moving over a calibrated dial.
- For high pressures, a **helical Bourdon** is used to reduce size; for very high pressures, a **multiturn spiral**.
- Material: typically **Phosphor bronze** (low P) or **Stainless steel / Inconel** (high P steam).

**Working (3 marks):**
- Internal steam pressure tends to straighten the curved tube, producing a small angular displacement.
- This displacement is amplified by the linkage and converted into pointer rotation.
- Since Δx ∝ P (derived above), the dial can be linearly calibrated in pressure units.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Thermocouple Principle (3 marks):**
- Two dissimilar wires (e.g., Chromel and Alumel for Type-K) joined at a hot junction.
- Hot junction placed in thermowell immersed in steam.
- Seebeck EMF E = f(T_hot − T_cold) is generated and measured.

**Reference Junction Compensation (3 marks):**
- The cold (reference) junction is not at 0 °C in practice.
- A separate temperature sensor (often a silicon diode or PT100) measures T_ref.
- The EMF equivalent of T_ref is computed from the thermocouple polynomial tables.
- The total measured EMF = E(T_hot, T_ref) is added to E(T_ref, 0 °C) to recover the true E(T_hot, 0 °C) value.
- Lookup table or polynomial then converts this back to T_hot in °C.

**Signal Path (1 mark):**
Thermowell → Hot junction → Compensating cable → Isothermal block → Amplifier → ADC → DCS display in °C.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin** when applying K_T — this is the #1 mistake and costs 2–3 marks per question.
> 2. **Using Q_std directly without dividing by K_T** when asked for actual flow — read the question carefully: "actual" means divide, "standard" means multiply.
> 3. **Confusing mass flow and volumetric flow** — mass flow needs NO temperature correction (only volumetric does).
> 4. **Applying the ideal-gas correction to wet/saturated steam** — saturated steam does NOT obey PV = nRT, so steam tables are mandatory.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention cold-junction compensation** when describing thermocouple systems — it is a 2-mark item that KTU examiners specifically look for.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Correction factor K_T = √(T_actual / T_std)** is valid only for **ideal gases at constant pressure**.
- For **combined P–T correction**: $K_{PT} = \sqrt{P_{std} \, T_{actual} / (P_{actual} \, T_{std})}$.
- **Mass flow is temperature-invariant**; only volumetric flow needs correction.
- Always use **Kelvin** in K_T formulas — never °C.
- **Bourdon tube**: Δx ∝ P; deflection is proportional to internal pressure.
- **Thermocouple for steam**: Type K most common; **must include cold-junction compensation**.
- **PT100 RTD**: Resistance varies as R = R₀(1 + AT + BT²); used for air preheater and economizer inlet.
- **Thermowell** is mandatory to protect sensors from high-velocity steam erosion.
- **Three-valve manifold** is used to safely isolate DP transmitters from live steam lines.
- **Steam tables** are mandatory for saturated or near-saturated steam — never substitute ideal-gas law blindly.
- Standard reference: **T_std = 25 °C = 298.15 K**, **P_std = 101.325 kPa**.
- R for air = **287 J/(kg·K)**, R for steam = **461.5 J/(kg·K)**.
- A 5 °C temperature error in steam can shift turbine-efficiency calculations by **0.4–0.6 %**.
- The discharge coefficient C_d typically ranges from **0.6 to 0.65** for sharp-edged orifice plates.
- Differential-pressure flowmeters always require a **square-root extractor** in the signal chain.
- For **higher accuracy in steam flow**, **flow nozzles** are preferred over orifice plates because their C_d remains more stable over wider Reynolds-number ranges.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoke and Dust Monitor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smoke and Dust Monitor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Smoke and Dust Monitors** (also called *Particulate Matter Monitors* or *Stack Emission Monitors*) are continuous emission monitoring systems (CEMS) used in power plants to measure the concentration of suspended particulate matter (PM) and gaseous smoke opacity in flue gas streams discharged through chimneys/stacks.

As per the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** of India and **EPA (Environmental Protection Agency)** standards, every thermal power plant must continuously monitor:
- **Particulate Matter (PM)**: Solid/liquid particles suspended in flue gas, measured in **mg/Nm³** (milligrams per Normal cubic meter).
- **Opacity**: The degree to which flue gas obscures vision, measured in **percentage (%)**.
- **PM2.5** and **PM10**: Particulates with aerodynamic diameter $\le 2.5\ \mu m$ and $\le 10\ \mu m$ respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Hazy Sunset Test":**
> Imagine you are looking at the setting sun on a polluted evening. The sun looks dimmer and redder because dust and smoke particles in the atmosphere **absorb** and **scatter** the sunlight. A smoke monitor works on exactly the same principle!
> 
> - **Opacity Monitor** → Like wearing sunglasses; it measures *how much light is blocked* by the smoke (similar to a hazy sun).
> - **Dust Monitor (PM Analyzer)** → Like weighing the dust collected on a filter paper after passing air through it (gravimetric), or measuring how a beta-ray beam weakens when it passes through dust-laden gas.

## 1.3 Why Is It Needed in Power Plants?

| Pollutant | Source in Power Plant | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Fly Ash (PM) | Incomplete combustion of coal | Respiratory diseases, silicosis |
| **SO₂** | Sulfur in coal | Acid rain, bronchitis |
| **NOₓ** | High-temperature combustion | Smog, ozone depletion |
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion | Toxic (binds with hemoglobin) |
| **Particulate Lead, Hg** | Trace elements in coal | Neurological damage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to explain the *working principle, transducer mechanism, and signal conditioning* of smoke/dust monitors used downstream of **Electrostatic Precipitators (ESP)** and **Bag Filters** in thermal power plants.

## 1.4 Classification of Smoke and Dust Monitors

$$\text{Smoke/Dust Monitors} \begin{cases} \text{Optical Methods} \begin{cases} \text{Opacity / Transmissometer} \\ \text{Light Scattering Monitor} \end{cases} \\ \text{Radiometric Methods} \begin{cases} \text{Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor} \\ \text{Gamma-ray Monitor} \end{cases} \\ \text{Electrostatic / Triboelectric Methods} \\ \text{Gravimetric Methods (Manual/Reference)} \end{cases}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Opacity Monitor (Smoke Density Monitor / Transmissometer)

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A **transmissometer** projects a beam of visible (or infrared) light across the stack/chimney. A photodetector on the opposite side measures the **intensity of transmitted light**. The reduction in intensity is proportional to smoke/dust concentration.

### 2.1.2 Physical Law — Beer-Lambert Law

The attenuation of light through a particulate-laden medium follows the **Beer-Lambert Law**:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha L C}$$

Where:
- $I$ = transmitted light intensity (W/m² or lux)
- $I_0$ = incident light intensity
- $\alpha$ = specific extinction coefficient (m²/kg)
- $L$ = path length through the medium (m)
- $C$ = particulate concentration (kg/m³)

### 2.1.3 Opacity vs. Transmittance

**Transmittance** is the ratio:

$$T = \frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha L C}$$

**Opacity** is the fraction of light *blocked*:

$$\text{Opacity} = 1 - T = 1 - e^{-\alpha L C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam problems, opacity is often expressed as a **percentage**:
> $$\text{Opacity (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$$

### 2.1.4 Why "Why" and "How" — Engineering Insight
- **Why use optical method?** → Non-contact, real-time, suitable for hot, corrosive flue gas.
- **How to handle fouling of optical windows?** → Use **air-purge blowers** that continuously blow clean air across the windows to prevent dust deposition.
- **How is the light source chosen?** → Often modulated LED (e.g., 880 nm IR) to reject ambient light interference; some use **double-pass** configuration (reflector on opposite side) to double the path length across narrow stacks.

## 2.2 Light Scattering Dust Monitor (Forward Scattering Type)

### 2.2.1 Principle
When a particle is illuminated by a light beam, it scatters light in all directions. The **forward-scattered intensity** ($I_s$) is proportional to the **volume of particles** in the sensing zone.

> **Mie Theory** (for particles comparable to wavelength):
> The scattering intensity depends on the *size parameter* $x = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda}$ where $d$ = particle diameter, $\lambda$ = wavelength.

For a monodisperse aerosol:
$$I_s = I_0 \cdot N \cdot \sigma_s$$

Where:
- $N$ = number density of particles (particles/m³)
- $\sigma_s$ = scattering cross-section (m²)

## 2.3 Beta Attenuation Mass Monitor (Most Accurate PM Method)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle
The flue gas is drawn through a **filter tape** (glass fiber or cellulose). Particulates are deposited on the tape. A **beta radiation source** (typically **¹⁴C** or **⁸⁵Kr**) is passed through the loaded tape.

Beta rays are attenuated according to the **mass of deposited particulate**, *independent of particle size, color, refractive index, or chemical composition* — making it the most reliable method.

### 2.3.2 Mathematical Foundation
Beta attenuation follows an exponential law (analogous to X-ray absorption):

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_m \rho L}$$

Where:
- $I$ = transmitted beta intensity (counts)
- $I_0$ = incident beta intensity (counts)
- $\mu_m$ = mass attenuation coefficient (m²/kg) — for ¹⁴C at 0.15 MeV, $\mu_m \approx 0.25$ m²/g
- $\rho$ = density of deposited dust (kg/m³)
- $L$ = thickness of deposit (m)

The **mass per unit area** of deposit $m_A = \rho L$, therefore:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_m m_A}$$

## 2.4 Triboelectric Dust Monitor

### 2.4.1 Principle
When dust particles strike a **metallic probe** (rod or electrode) inserted in the flue gas duct, they transfer a small **electric charge** (triboelectric effect). The resulting AC current signal is proportional to the dust concentration.

$$Q \propto m_{\text{dust}} \cdot v_{\text{dust}}$$

Where $Q$ is the charge transferred, $m_{\text{dust}}$ is the mass of dust, and $v_{\text{dust}}$ is the particle velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Used for **broken bag detection** in bag-house filters and **ESP performance** monitoring. Output is in **mg/m³**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every high-yield formula** expected in KTU 2024 ESE for this topic.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Value / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Beer-Lambert Law | $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha L C}$ | $I$: intensity (W/m²); $C$: concentration (kg/m³); $L$: m | Foundation of opacity monitor |
| 2 | Transmittance | $T = I / I_0 = e^{-\alpha L C}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le T \le 1$ |
| 3 | Opacity (%) | $\text{Opa} = (1 - T) \times 100$ | dimensionless, in % | CPCB limit: $\le 20\%$ (24-hr avg) |
| 4 | Beta Attenuation | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu_m m_A}$ | $m_A$: mass/area (g/m²); $\mu_m$: m²/g | Independent of particle type |
| 5 | Dust Mass from Beta | $m_A = -\frac{1}{\mu_m} \ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | g/m² | Direct measure of PM |
| 6 | PM Concentration | $C_{\text{PM}} = \frac{m_A \cdot A_{\text{filter}}}{V_{\text{sampled}}}$ | mg/m³ | $A$: filter area; $V$: sampled volume |
| 7 | Particle Size Parameter (Mie) | $x = \pi d / \lambda$ | dimensionless | $x \approx 1$: Mie regime |
| 8 | Rayleigh Criterion | $I_s \propto d^6 / \lambda^4$ | scattered intensity | Valid for $d \ll \lambda$ |
| 9 | Triboelectric Charge | $Q = k \cdot C_{\text{dust}} \cdot v$ | Coulombs | Used in broken-bag detectors |
| 10 | Stoke's Law (settling) | $v_t = \frac{(\rho_p - \rho_f) g d^2}{18 \mu}$ | m/s | Used in isokinetic sampling |
| 11 | Normalization to STP | $C_N = C \cdot \frac{P}{101.325} \cdot \frac{273.15}{T}$ | mg/Nm³ | CPCB reporting standard |
| 12 | Sampling Flowrate | $Q_s = v_{\text{iso}} \cdot A_{\text{nozzle}}$ | m³/s | Isokinetic sampling |

> **Units to memorize:** `mg/Nm³` (milligrams per normal cubic meter), `ppm` (parts per million), `µg/m³` (micrograms per cubic meter), `%` opacity, `Bq` (Becquerel for radioactive source).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Sensor Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Stack monitoring at chimney exit | Opacity transmissometer | Regulatory compliance |
| ESP outlet | Triboelectric / light scattering | ESP efficiency verification |
| Bag-house outlet | Triboelectric | Broken-bag detection (alarm) |
| Ambient air quality | Beta attenuation (PM), light scattering | Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station (CAAQMS) |
| Coal-handling plants | Gravimetric (manual) | Reference method |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Beer-Lambert Law for Opacity Monitor

> **Starting assumption:** Consider a thin slice of particulate-laden gas of thickness $dx$ at distance $x$ from the light source. The reduction in light intensity $dI$ is proportional to:
> 1. The current intensity $I(x)$
> 2. The thickness $dx$
> 3. The concentration of particles $C$

### Step 1: Differential Form

The infinitesimal change in intensity across slice $dx$:

$$dI = -\alpha \cdot C \cdot I(x) \cdot dx$$

Where $\alpha$ is the *specific extinction coefficient* (also called the *mass extinction coefficient*) and the **negative sign** indicates intensity decreases.

### Step 2: Separation of Variables

Rearranging to separate $I$ and $x$:

$$\frac{dI}{I(x)} = -\alpha \cdot C \cdot dx$$

### Step 3: Integration Over Path Length

Integrate both sides from the source ($x=0$, $I=I_0$) to the detector ($x=L$, $I=I$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha C \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

The left side is $\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = \ln(I/I_0)$, and the right side is $-\alpha C L$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha C L$$

### Step 4: Exponentiation

Exponentiating both sides:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha C L}$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law** for the opacity monitor.

### Step 5: Opacity Definition

By definition:

$$\text{Opacity} = 1 - \frac{I}{I_0} = 1 - e^{-\alpha C L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Opacity is a *non-linear* function of concentration. Doubling the path length (using a reflector) or doubling the concentration **does not double the opacity** — it must be computed via the exponential.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Particulate Mass from Beta Attenuation

### Step 1: Beta Attenuation Equation

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_m m_A}$$

where $m_A = \rho L$ is the *areal density* (mass per unit area) of the deposited dust.

### Step 2: Solve for $m_A$

Divide both sides by $I_0$:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu_m m_A}$$

Take the natural logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu_m m_A$$

Multiply by $-1$:

$$\mu_m m_A = -\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

### Step 3: Isolate $m_A$

$$m_A = \frac{1}{\mu_m} \ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$

### Step 4: Convert to PM Concentration

If the sampled gas volume is $V_s$ (in m³, normalized to STP) and the deposit is spread over a filter of area $A_f$ (in m²), the PM concentration in the flue gas is:

$$C_{\text{PM}} = \frac{m_A \cdot A_f}{V_s} \quad \text{(in mg/m³)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: $m_A$ is in g/m², $A_f$ in m², so numerator is in grams. Multiply by $10^3$ to get mg.

### Step 5: Sample Numerical Evaluation

> **Example:** Suppose $I_0 = 10000$ counts, $I = 8200$ counts, $\mu_m = 0.25\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$, $A_f = 5\ \text{cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^2$, $V_s = 1.0\ \text{m}^3$ at STP.

**Step 5a:** Compute the log ratio:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{10000}{8200}\right) = \ln(1.2195) \approx 0.1985$$

**Step 5b:** Compute $m_A$:

$$m_A = \frac{0.1985}{0.25} = 0.794\ \text{g/m}^2$$

**Step 5c:** Compute total dust mass on filter:

$$m_{\text{dust}} = m_A \cdot A_f = 0.794 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 3.97 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{g} = 0.397\ \text{mg}$$

**Step 5d:** Compute PM concentration:

$$C_{\text{PM}} = \frac{0.397\ \text{mg}}{1.0\ \text{m}^3} = 0.397\ \text{mg/m}^3 \approx 0.4\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

> This is well within CPCB limits (50 mg/Nm³ for new thermal plants, 100 mg/Nm³ for older ones).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Opacity from Known Particulate Concentration

> **Example (reverse problem):** A stack has path length $L = 4$ m, $\alpha = 0.05\ \text{m}^2/\text{g}$, and $C = 800\ \text{mg/m}^3 = 0.8\ \text{g/m}^3 = 8 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{kg/m}^3$.

### Step 1: Compute the exponent

$$\alpha C L = 0.05 \times 0.8 \times 4 = 0.16$$

### Step 2: Compute transmittance

$$T = e^{-\alpha C L} = e^{-0.16} \approx 0.8521$$

### Step 3: Compute opacity

$$\text{Opacity} = 1 - T = 1 - 0.8521 = 0.1479 \approx 14.8\%$$

> This is just under the typical 20% CPCB limit, indicating acceptable operation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Normalization of Flue Gas Concentration to STP

Flue gas temperature is typically 150–200 °C; the concentration must be reported at **0 °C, 1 atm (STP)**.

Using the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, for the same number of moles:

$$\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$$

Thus the corrected (normal) concentration:

$$C_N = C_{\text{meas}} \times \frac{P_{\text{meas}}}{P_N} \times \frac{T_N}{T_{\text{meas}}}$$

With $P_N = 101.325$ kPa, $T_N = 273.15$ K:

$$C_N = C_{\text{meas}} \times \frac{P_{\text{meas}}}{101.325} \times \frac{273.15}{T_{\text{meas}}}$$

### Worked Example

Measured at stack: $C_{\text{meas}} = 250$ mg/m³, $T_{\text{meas}} = 180\ ^\circ\text{C} = 453.15$ K, $P_{\text{meas}} = 100$ kPa.

$$C_N = 250 \times \frac{100}{101.325} \times \frac{273.15}{453.15}$$

$$C_N = 250 \times 0.9869 \times 0.6028 = 148.7\ \text{mg/Nm}^3$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix helps KTU students quickly compare instruments during exams.

| Property | Opacity Monitor | Light Scattering | Beta Attenuation | Triboelectric |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output** | % Opacity | Relative mg/m³ | Absolute mg/m³ | Relative mg/m³ |
| **Accuracy** | ±2% opacity | ±10% | ±5% (reference-grade) | ±20% (qualitative) |
| **Calibration** | Not required | Requires dust standard | Absolute (no calibration needed) | Drift-prone |
| **Particle type** | Independent | Depends on size & RI | **Independent** | Depends on triboelectric series |
| **Real-time** | Yes | Yes | Slight delay (filter cycle) | Yes |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | Very low |
| **Use in Power Plant** | Stack outlet | ESP outlet | Continuous Ambient Monitoring | Bag filter / ESP outlet |
| **KTU Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since physical optical hardware cannot be drawn in pure text/Mermaid, the following block-level functional architectures are provided. These diagrams are KTU-board-exam-ready for *block diagram* questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Similar]

**Q: Define opacity of flue gas. How is it related to transmittance? Mention the working principle of an opacity monitor used in thermal power plant stacks.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Definition (1 mark):** Opacity is the degree to which smoke and dust particles in flue gas prevent the transmission of light, expressed as a percentage. It is mathematically defined as:
> $$\text{Opacity} = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\ \%$$
> 
> **Relation (1 mark):** Transmittance $T = I/I_0$, so $\text{Opacity} = (1 - T) \times 100\%$.
> 
> **Principle (1 mark):** An opacity monitor (transmissometer) projects a beam of modulated LED/laser light across the stack. A photodetector on the opposite side measures transmitted intensity $I$. Air-purge blowers keep optical windows clean. The output is calibrated in % opacity.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Similar]

**Q: List any three methods of particulate matter monitoring in thermal power plants. Compare their advantages.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 
> **Three methods:**
> 1. **Opacity / Transmissometer** — measures % opacity across stack using light attenuation (Beer-Lambert).
> 2. **Beta Attenuation Monitor** — measures absolute mass of PM deposited on filter tape via beta ray absorption; reference-grade accuracy, independent of particle properties.
> 3. **Triboelectric Monitor** — measures AC charge generated when dust strikes a probe; used for broken-bag detection.
> 
> **Comparison (1 mark):** Beta attenuation is most accurate but expensive; opacity monitors are simple and cheap; triboelectric is qualitative but excellent for leak detection.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain with neat block diagram the working of a beta attenuation particulate matter monitor used for continuous emission monitoring. Derive the expression used to compute the PM mass per unit area.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: A beta attenuation monitor records an initial count $I_0 = 8000$ counts per minute. After sampling 1.5 m³ of flue gas (at STP) on a filter of area 6 cm², the count rate drops to $I = 6500$ counts per minute. If $\mu_m = 0.26$ m²/g, calculate (i) the mass per unit area of deposit, (ii) the total dust collected, and (iii) the PM concentration in mg/Nm³.**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer Section 4.2 above — sampling probe → heated line → filter tape → beta source → detector → counter → microcontroller → display/output.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
- A constant beta radiation source (¹⁴C, 0.156 MeV) is positioned on one side of a filter tape, and a **Geiger-Müller (GM) tube** on the other.
- Flue gas is drawn isokinetically through the filter; particulates deposit on the tape.
- Initially, the beta count rate $I_0$ is measured with a clean tape.
- After a sampling cycle, the count rate $I$ is measured. The reduction is due only to the deposited mass.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
The beta attenuation follows:

$$I = I_0 e^{-\mu_m m_A}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \mu_m m_A$$

Therefore:

$$m_A = \frac{1}{\mu_m}\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) \quad \text{(in g/m²)}$$

> This is the **fundamental equation** of beta attenuation PM monitoring. It is independent of particle size, color, refractive index, or chemical composition — making it a reference-grade measurement.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $I_0 = 8000$ cpm, $I = 6500$ cpm, $V_s = 1.5$ m³, $A_f = 6\ \text{cm}^2 = 6 \times 10^{-4}$ m², $\mu_m = 0.26$ m²/g.

**Step (i): Mass per unit area (2 marks)**

$$m_A = \frac{1}{0.26}\ln\left(\frac{8000}{6500}\right)$$

Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{8000}{6500} = 1.2308$$

Compute the natural log:

$$\ln(1.2308) \approx 0.2076$$

Therefore:

$$m_A = \frac{0.2076}{0.26} = 0.7985\ \text{g/m}^2 \approx 0.80\ \text{g/m}^2$$

**Step (ii): Total dust collected (2 marks)**

$$m_{\text{dust}} = m_A \cdot A_f = 0.7985 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$m_{\text{dust}} = 4.791 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{g} = 0.479\ \text{mg}$$

**Step (iii): PM concentration in mg/Nm³ (3 marks)**

$$C_{\text{PM}} = \frac{m_{\text{dust}}}{V_s} = \frac{0.479\ \text{mg}}{1.5\ \text{m}^3}$$

$$C_{\text{PM}} = 0.319\ \text{mg/Nm}^3$$

> **Conclusion (0 marks, optional remark):** This is well below CPCB limit (50 mg/Nm³ for new plants), indicating excellent PM control by the upstream ESP.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an opacity monitor (transmissometer) used in a thermal power plant stack. State the Beer-Lambert law and discuss how the path length affects opacity measurement.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: The flue gas in a stack has particulate concentration of 600 mg/m³. The opacity monitor has a path length of 5 m. If the mass extinction coefficient is $\alpha = 0.06$ m²/g, calculate (i) the transmittance, (ii) the percentage opacity, and (iii) comment on whether the plant meets the CPCB limit of 20% opacity.**

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer Section 4.1 — LED source → optics → flue gas path → receiving optics → photodetector → amplifier → display. Add air-purge for window cleaning.

**Working (2 marks):**
A modulated **LED source** (often IR at 880 nm) projects a parallel beam across the stack. A photodetector measures the transmitted intensity $I$. Modulation at a fixed frequency (e.g., 500 Hz) eliminates ambient light interference. The ratio $I/I_0$ is computed continuously to give % opacity.

**Beer-Lambert Law (1 mark):**

$$I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$$

> **Path length effect (1 mark):** Increasing $L$ (by reflector) *exponentially* increases opacity, improving sensitivity. For low-concentration gases, double-pass geometry doubles the path length and dramatically increases measurement resolution.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $C = 600\ \text{mg/m}^3 = 0.6\ \text{g/m}^3$, $L = 5$ m, $\alpha = 0.06$ m²/g.

**Step (i): Transmittance (2 marks)**

First compute $\alpha C L$:

$$\alpha C L = 0.06 \times 0.6 \times 5 = 0.18$$

Then:

$$T = e^{-0.18} = 0.8353 \approx 83.53\%$$

**Step (ii): Percentage opacity (3 marks)**

$$\text{Opacity (\%)} = (1 - T) \times 100 = (1 - 0.8353) \times 100 = 16.47\%$$

**Step (iii): Compliance (2 marks)**

> The measured opacity is **16.47%**, which is **below the CPCB limit of 20%**.
> 
> Therefore, the plant **complies** with the regulatory standard. However, the margin is only ~3.5%, suggesting the ESP may need preventive maintenance soon to avoid future violations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–2 marks):** Always convert $mg/m^3$ to $g/m^3$ (or $kg/m^3$) before plugging into the Beer-Lambert equation. $\alpha$ has units of m²/**g** or m²/**kg** — keep consistent.
> 2. **Inversion of $I/I_0$ (–1 mark):** Some students write $I_0/I$ inside the log; remember: mass comes from $\ln(I_0/I)$, *not* $\ln(I/I_0)$.
> 3. **Forgetting area in PM calculation (–2 marks):** The beta equation gives *mass per unit area*. You must multiply by filter area to get total mass, then divide by sampled volume.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram (–2 to –3 marks):** KTU 2024 scheme mandates a *neat block diagram* for full marks in 7-mark sub-questions. Always draw one.
> 5. **Confusing opacity with concentration:** Opacity is a *percentage of light blocked*; it is *not* the same as PM concentration. Different particle sizes/colors give different opacities for the same mass.
> 6. **Neglecting normalization (–1 mark):** Always convert measured concentration to *mg/Nm³* (standard temperature 273.15 K, pressure 101.325 kPa) before comparing to CPCB limits.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3: Smoke and Dust Monitor**

- **Smoke/Dust monitors** are part of **CEMS (Continuous Emission Monitoring System)** mandated by CPCB for thermal power plants.
- **Four main types:** Opacity (Transmissometer), Light Scattering, Beta Attenuation, Triboelectric.
- **Opacity** = $(1 - I/I_0) \times 100\%$, based on **Beer-Lambert Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$.
- **Beta attenuation** is the **reference-grade** method; output is **absolute PM mass** $m_A = (1/\mu_m)\ln(I_0/I)$.
- **¹⁴C** (0.156 MeV) and **⁸⁵Kr** (0.687 MeV) are commonly used beta sources.
- **Geiger-Müller (GM) tube** detects beta rays.
- **Light scattering** uses **Mie theory**; forward-scattered intensity is proportional to particle volume.
- **Triboelectric** sensors detect charge transfer when dust strikes a probe — used for **broken-bag detection**.
- **Air-purge blowers** on transmissometer windows prevent fouling.
- **Isokinetic sampling** is critical to obtain a representative sample (Stoke's law-based probe design).
- **CPCB limit** for new thermal plants: **50 mg/Nm³ PM**, **≤ 20% opacity** (24-hr average).
- **Reporting unit:** Always convert to **mg/Nm³** at **273.15 K, 101.325 kPa**.
- **Real-world placement:** opacity at *stack exit*, triboelectric at *ESP outlet*, beta attenuation in *CAAQMS*, light scattering in *workplace dust monitors*.
- **Key advantage of beta method:** Independent of particle size, color, refractive index, and chemical composition.
- **Key advantage of opacity method:** Non-intrusive, real-time, low cost.
- **Key advantage of triboelectric method:** Inexpensive, ideal for alarm applications in bag filters.
- **Mermaid block diagrams** for each monitor type are expected in 7-mark sub-questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flame Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flame Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flame Monitoring** is the continuous, real-time measurement and verification of the presence, quality, and stability of a flame inside a boiler furnace or combustion chamber, accomplished through the detection of one or more intrinsic physical properties of the flame (conductivity, temperature, radiation, or ionization). It forms the sensory backbone of the **Burner Management System (BMS)** and is mandated by safety codes (e.g., NFPA 85, ISA 84) to prevent **puff explosions**, **unburnt fuel accumulation**, and **furnace implosions**.

In a KTU Power Plant Instrumentation context, flame monitoring is treated as a **process-safety instrumentation loop** rather than a simple indicating measurement — its output drives trip logic, fuel shut-off valves (FSOVs), and master fuel trips (MFTs).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Night Watchman of the Boiler**
> Imagine a giant industrial furnace as a campfire, and the operator must be absolutely sure that the fire is *actually burning* before continuously feeding it with fuel oil or gas. A flame monitor acts like a highly specialized **night watchman** standing at the furnace door with a special set of eyes and ears — he doesn't just *look* for fire, he senses its heat, its light, its crackle (ionization), and its electrical hum. The instant the watchman shouts "**The fire is out!**", an automatic valve slams shut cutting off the fuel supply, preventing a gas leak and a catastrophic explosion.

## 1.3 Physical Properties of a Flame (Sensed Parameters)

A combustion flame is not just "hot air with light" — it is a **partially ionized plasma** that simultaneously exhibits four measurable properties:

| # | Property | Physical Origin | What the Sensor "Sees" |
|---|----------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Electrical Conductivity / Ionization** | High-temperature thermal ionization of combustion gases (Na⁺, K⁺, free e⁻) | A small rectified DC current (µA) between two electrodes immersed in the flame |
| 2 | **Temperature** | Exothermic oxidation reaching **1500–2500 K** | IR radiation or thermocouple EMF |
| 3 | **Radiation (Emissivity)** | Black-body/grey-body emission from soot particles, CO₂, H₂O vapor | Visible light, UV photons, IR photons |
| 4 | **Acoustic / Pressure Fluctuations** | Turbulent combustion noise | Dynamic pressure pulses (rarely used) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key constant for the module:** The **Stefan–Boltzmann constant** is **σ = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴**, governing the total radiant energy emitted per unit area of the flame. The **elementary charge** **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** governs flame ionization current.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Flame Monitoring

Boiler furnaces store enormous potential energy. If the flame is extinguished while fuel continues to flow, the unburnt fuel mixes with incoming air and forms an explosive cloud. A subsequent **delayed ignition** produces a **puff (furnace explosion)**, capable of destroying the unit. Flame monitoring thus provides:

1. **Proof of Ignition** — Confirms the pilot/main burner has actually lit within a defined trial-for-ignition period (typically **3 to 10 seconds**).
2. **Continuous Proof of Combustion** — Verifies the flame remains stable during firing.
3. **Trip Initiation Signal** — Generates a hardwired trip to the BMS upon flame failure (FF).

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Flame Sensing (Step-by-Step)

1. **Optical/electrical interface** — The flame property (UV, IR, visible, or ionized current) is coupled to a transducer via a sight tube, fibre-optic cable, or direct electrode immersion.
2. **Transduction** — Photons strike a photocathode / PbS cell / Si photodiode, or ions conduct current across a flame rod gap.
3. **Signal Conditioning** — The raw µA or mV signal is amplified, filtered (to reject background furnace glow), and compared against a **flame-on threshold**.
4. **Discrimination** — The scanner electronics distinguish *real flame flicker* (typically **6–25 Hz modulation**) from background radiation.
5. **Binary Output** — A relay / 4–20 mA / contact closure is delivered to the BMS PLC or hardwired trip relay.

## 2.3 Classification of Flame Monitoring Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four canonical KTU-classified flame detection technologies** — these are the *core sub-topics* the examiner will test:

### A. Flame Rod (Ionization) Detector
- A single **flame rod electrode** (insulated except at the tip) and the **burner body** form the two electrodes.
- The **flame rectifies** the applied AC (typically 250 V AC, 50/60 Hz) into a small DC current (typically **1–25 µA**).
- Operating Principle: **Saha ionization** — at high temperature, gas atoms lose electrons: $M \rightleftharpoons M^+ + e^-$
- Best suited for **gas-fired** and **oil-fired** burners.

### B. UV (Ultraviolet) Flame Scanner
- Uses a **gas-filled photocell** (e.g., filled with Argon, Iodine) or a solid-state **UV silicon carbide (SiC) photodiode**.
- Sensitive to wavelengths below **~300 nm** (UV-A and UV-B).
- Responds to the **OH\*** and **CO\*** chemiluminescent radicals of the flame.
- **Cannot "see" through" the refractory once the burner fires** — self-discriminating against hot refractory background.
- Best for **gas and light oil** burners.

### C. IR (Infrared) Flame Scanner
- Uses a **lead sulphide (PbS)** or **indium gallium arsenide (InGaAs)** photoconductive cell.
- Sensitive to wavelengths of **800–2400 nm**.
- Detects IR emission from **hot soot particles** and **CO₂ / H₂O** bands.
- Best for **coal-fired** and **heavy oil** burners (coal flames have weak UV but strong IR).

### D. Visible-Light Photocell Scanner
- Uses a **cadmium sulphide (CdS)** or **silicon photodiode**.
- Detects flickering visible flame.
- Older technology, used in small package boilers.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every formula** required to solve KTU examination questions on flame monitoring. Memorize the boundary conditions and SI units — KTU examiners often award partial credit for correctly stating the constants.

| # | Formula / Law | Mathematical Form | What It Models | SI Units |
|---|---------------|-------------------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** (total flame radiation) | $E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$ | Total radiant exitance from a flame of emissivity $\varepsilon$ at temperature $T$ | W·m⁻² |
| 2 | **Saha Ionization Equation** (degree of ionization) | $\frac{n_i \, n_e}{n_n} = \frac{2}{\lambda^{3}} \, \frac{g_i \, g_e}{g_n} \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{\chi}{k_B T}\right)$ | Ratio of ionized to neutral atoms in flame plasma | Dimensionless |
| 3 | **Photoelectric Equation** (UV/IR photon energy) | $E_{ph} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a single photon striking the photocathode | Joules (or eV) |
| 4 | **Ionization Current** (flame rod) | $I = \frac{V_{flame}}{R_{flame}} = n_e \, e \, A \, v_d$ | DC current rectified by flame between rod and burner | Amperes (µA typical) |
| 5 | **Wien's Displacement Law** (peak flame emission wavelength) | $\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}, \quad b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | Wavelength of maximum flame radiation | metres |
| 6 | **Flame Conductivity** (plasma model) | $\sigma_f = \frac{n_e \, e^{2} \, \tau}{m_e}$ | Electrical conductivity of partially ionized flame gas | S·m⁻¹ |
| 7 | **Flame Flicker Frequency** | $f_{flame} \approx 6 \text{ to } 25$ Hz | Discriminator band-pass centre | Hz |
| 8 | **Signal-to-Noise Threshold** | $\text{SNR} = \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \geq 3$ (typical) | Reliable flame "ON" detection | Dimensionless |

**Key Constants for Substitutions (in SI units):**

| Constant | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Stefan–Boltzmann | $\sigma$ | $5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| Speed of light | $c$ | $2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m·s⁻¹ |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J·K⁻¹ |
| Wien's displacement | $b$ | $2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K |
| Elementary charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| Electron mass | $m_e$ | $9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When writing absolute values inside markdown tables, **never** use the pipe character `\vert` literally. Use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking the table parser.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In thermal power stations, flame monitoring directly enables:

- **Compliance with NFPA 85 / IBR (Indian Boiler Regulations)** — mandatory flame-failure trip.
- **Permissives in BMS** — the burner cannot be admitted to firing without proven flame.
- **Commissioning & Tuning** — flame scanners are adjusted to their **minimum reliable signal** during light-off to avoid "false-flame" trips.
- **Life Extension** — early detection of flame instability prevents refractory damage and water-wall tube failures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Flame Radiant Exitance Using Stefan–Boltzmann Law

**Problem Statement:** A pulverized-coal flame has an effective emissivity of $\varepsilon = 0.85$ and a luminous zone temperature of $T = 1873$ K. Compute the total radiant exitance sensed by an IR flame scanner.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing law.**
For a flame treated as a grey body in thermal equilibrium, the total hemispherical emissive power is given by the Stefan–Boltzmann law:

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}, \quad \varepsilon = 0.85, \quad T = 1873 \ \text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $T^{4}$.**

$$T^{4} = (1873)^{4} = 1.231 \times 10^{13} \ \text{K}^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the three factors.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= (0.85) \times (5.670 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.231 \times 10^{13}) \\
  &= 0.85 \times 6.980 \times 10^{5} \\
  &= 5.933 \times 10^{5} \ \text{W·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Express in standard engineering form.**

$$E \approx 5.93 \times 10^{5} \ \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E \approx 593 \ \text{kW·m}^{-2}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the *total* radiant flux density at the flame surface. A PbS IR cell with a lens of 5 cm² will intercept $E \times A \approx 30$ W of optical power, which is comfortably above the noise floor.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Emission Wavelength (Wien's Displacement Law)

**Problem Statement:** For the same coal flame at $T = 1873$ K, determine the wavelength at which emission peaks. Justify the choice of an **IR** scanner over a **UV** scanner.

**Step 1 — State Wien's displacement law.**

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T}, \qquad b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m·K}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{max} &= \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1873} \\
              &= 1.547 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to nanometres.**

$$\lambda_{max} = 1547 \ \text{nm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret and justify scanner choice.**

This peak lies deep in the **near-IR** band, *not* in the UV. Hence a **PbS / InGaAs IR scanner** is the appropriate choice for a coal flame — a UV detector would be insensitive to the bulk of the emission.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Flame Rod Ionization Current

**Problem Statement:** A flame rod immersed in a gas flame has an effective plasma resistance of $R_{flame} = 4.0 \times 10^{7}$ Ω. An AC potential of $V_{rms} = 250$ V is applied between the rod tip and the grounded burner. Estimate the DC ionization current rectified by the flame.

**Step 1 — Convert AC RMS to peak.**

$$V_{peak} = V_{rms} \times \sqrt{2} = 250 \times 1.414 = 353.6 \ \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Use Ohm's law for the plasma path.**

$$I = \frac{V_{peak}}{R_{flame}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$I = \frac{353.6}{4.0 \times 10^{7}} = 8.84 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to microamperes.**

$$I \approx 8.84 \ \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with flame-on threshold.**

A typical flame monitor's "ON" threshold is $\geq 1$ µA. The computed 8.84 µA is well above threshold, confirming reliable flame detection.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Photoelectric Response of a UV Scanner

**Problem Statement:** A UV scanner's photocathode is illuminated by flame photons of wavelength $\lambda = 250$ nm. Calculate the energy of each photon in (a) joules and (b) electron-volts.

**Step 1 — Apply the photoelectric equation.**

$$E_{ph} = h \nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute in SI units.**

$$E_{ph} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(2.998 \times 10^{8})}{250 \times 10^{-9}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$hc = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8} = 1.986 \times 10^{-25} \ \text{J·m}$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $\lambda$.**

$$E_{ph} = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 7.944 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{J}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to electron-volts.**

$$E_{ph} = \frac{7.944 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 4.96 \ \text{eV}$$

**Interpretation:** UV photons in a flame carry roughly 5 eV — more than enough to overcome the work function of common photocathodes (e.g., Cs-Sb, work function ~2 eV), ensuring reliable UV photocurrent.

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Flame Rod (Ionization) | UV Scanner | IR Scanner | Visible-Light Scanner |
|-----------|------------------------|------------|------------|------------------------|
| **Sensed Property** | Ionized current (µA) | UV photons ($<300$ nm) | IR photons ($800$–$2400$ nm) | Visible flicker ($400$–$700$ nm) |
| **Active / Passive** | Active (powered electrode) | Passive | Passive | Passive |
| **Best Fuel Match** | Gas, light oil | Gas, light oil | Coal, heavy oil | Gas |
| **Hot Refractory Rejection** | Excellent | **Excellent** (intrinsic) | Moderate (needs filtering) | Poor |
| **Cross-Sensitivity** | Ignitor spark | Arc welder light | Sunlight, refractory | Sunlight |
| **Typical Signal** | 1–25 µA | Photocurrent nA–µA | mV across photoresistor | kΩ change |
| **Response Time** | < 100 ms | < 50 ms | < 100 ms | < 200 ms |
| **Maintenance** | Rod cleaning | Lens cleaning, gas refill | Lens cleaning | Lens cleaning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Flame Monitoring Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Boiler Furnace / Combustion Chamber] --> B[Flame Property Emission]
    B --> C1[UV Photons]
    B --> C2[IR Photons]
    B --> C3[Ionized Plasma Path]
    B --> C4[Visible Light Flicker]

    C1 --> D1[UV Scanner Module]
    C2 --> D2[IR Scanner Module]
    C3 --> D3[Flame Rod Module]
    C4 --> D4[Photocell Module]

    D1 --> E[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F[Flicker Discriminator 6 to 25 Hz Band Pass]
    F --> G[Threshold Comparator]
    G --> H{Flame ON Decision}
    H -->|YES| I1[Permissive Signal to BMS]
    H -->|NO| I2[Flame Failure Trip Signal]
    I2 --> J[Master Fuel Trip MFT Relay]
    J --> K[Fuel Shut Off Valves Close]
    K --> L[Safety Lockout and Alarm to Operator]
    I1 --> M[Continue Normal Firing]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Burner Management System (BMS) Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Start Permissive Check] --> P1[Fuel Valve Closed Confirmation]
    P1 --> P2[Airflow Established Confirmation]
    P2 --> P3[Purge Complete 5 Minutes with all Flaps Open]
    P3 --> P4[Igniter Energized]
    P4 --> P5[Open Pilot Fuel Valve]
    P5 --> P6{Flame Detected within 10 s Trial for Ignition}
    P6 -->|YES| P7[Open Main Fuel Valve]
    P6 -->|NO| P8[Lockout and Alarm]
    P7 --> P9{Continuous Flame Proof}
    P9 -->|YES| P10[Normal Run]
    P9 -->|NO| P11[Initiate MFT Master Fuel Trip]
    P11 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: UV Scanner Internal Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Furnace Flame UV Emission] --> S2[Sight Tube with Cooling Air]
    S2 --> S3[Quartz Lens UV Transparent]
    S3 --> S4[Gas Filled UV Photocell Tube]
    S4 --> S5[Photocurrent Generated]
    S5 --> S6[Load Resistor Converts to Voltage]
    S6 --> S7[Amplifier and Discriminator]
    S7 --> S8[Relay Output Flame ON or OFF]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why quartz and not glass?** Ordinary glass absorbs UV below ~350 nm. A UV scanner requires a **quartz** or **UV-grade sapphire** window to transmit the short-wavelength flame radiation to the photocathode.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: Flame Rod Circuit Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[250 V AC Supply] --> A2[Series Resistor R1]
    A2 --> A3[Flame Rod Electrode]
    A3 -->|Arc through plasma| A4[Grounded Burner Body]
    A4 --> A5[Return to Supply Neutral]
    A3 --> A6[Tap Point to Flame Amplifier]
    A6 --> A7[Rectifier and DC Amplifier]
    A7 --> A8[Trip Relay]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four physical properties of a flame that are used for flame monitoring in a power plant boiler. Name the corresponding detector type for each property.**

**Model Answer (4 points × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

| # | Property | Detector Type |
|---|----------|----------------|
| 1 | Electrical conductivity / ionization | Flame rod (ionization probe) |
| 2 | Ultraviolet radiation ($\lambda < 300$ nm) | UV scanner with gas-filled photocell |
| 3 | Infrared radiation ($800 < \lambda < 2400$ nm) | IR scanner with PbS or InGaAs cell |
| 4 | Visible-light flicker | Photocell (CdS) scanner |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "temperature" as a flame property but cannot name a corresponding detector. Always pair property ↔ detector. **Zero marks** will be awarded for a mismatched pair.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Why is an IR flame scanner preferred over a UV scanner for a pulverized-coal-fired boiler?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pulverized-coal flame radiates predominantly in the **infrared** band (peak emission $\lambda_{max} \approx 1.5$ µm by Wien's law) due to incandescent soot particles and CO₂/H₂O vibrational bands. UV emission from coal flames is comparatively weak. Furthermore, coal flames have a very hot, bright refractory background that emits UV strongly; a UV scanner cannot easily discriminate the flame from this background, whereas an IR scanner filters the modulated flame flicker (6–25 Hz) from the steady refractory glow. Therefore, IR scanners give **higher signal-to-noise ratio and better reliability** in coal-fired units.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you will be offered a Module-level internal choice. **Both** alternative questions are solved below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Set A]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **UV flame scanner** used in an oil-fired boiler furnace. Discuss how it discriminates between a real flame and the background hot refractory. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gas-fired boiler furnace has a flame whose luminous zone has an emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.78$ and temperature $T = 2100$ K. Calculate **(i)** the total radiant exitance and **(ii)** the peak emission wavelength. Identify the most suitable flame scanner (UV or IR) and justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — UV Flame Scanner Working (7 marks):**

**Working principle (3 marks):**
A UV flame scanner consists of:
1. A **sight tube** with cooling-air sweep.
2. A **quartz lens** (transparent to UV) at the inboard end.
3. A **gas-filled UV-sensitive photocell** (tube) at the outboard end — typically filled with a low-work-function gas mixture that ionizes on UV photon impact.
4. A load resistor, amplifier, and discriminator circuit.
5. A relay output contact.

UV photons emitted by the flame's OH\* and CO\* chemiluminescent radicals (wavelengths 200–300 nm) pass through the sight tube and lens, strike the photocathode, and generate a **photocurrent** proportional to flame intensity.

**Refractory discrimination (3 marks):**
- A hot refractory glows brightly but emits predominantly in the **infrared and visible** spectrum, not in the UV below 300 nm.
- The UV tube is **intrinsically blind** to refractory glow because its gas fill and window material reject longer wavelengths.
- Additionally, the amplifier includes a **flicker discriminator** tuned to 6–25 Hz — the natural modulation frequency of a turbulent flame — rejecting the **steady** background.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** See Section 4.3 of these notes.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total radiant exitance (3 marks):**

Apply Stefan–Boltzmann law:

$$E = \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}$$

Substitute $\varepsilon = 0.78$, $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴, $T = 2100$ K:

$$T^{4} = (2100)^{4} = 1.9448 \times 10^{13} \ \text{K}^{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= 0.78 \times 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.9448 \times 10^{13} \\
  &= 0.78 \times 1.1027 \times 10^{6} \\
  &= 8.601 \times 10^{5} \ \text{W·m}^{-2} \\
  &\approx 860 \ \text{kW·m}^{-2}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Peak emission wavelength (2 marks):**

Apply Wien's displacement law:

$$\lambda_{max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2100} = 1.38 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m} = 1380 \ \text{nm}$$

**Scanner choice (2 marks):**
The peak wavelength is **1380 nm**, which lies in the **near-infrared**. Although the flame also emits strongly in UV from chemiluminescent radicals, the **total radiated power peak** is in IR. For an **oil-fired** boiler, however, the UV signature is strong and clean (oil flames are luminous but produce sharp OH\* bands), and UV scanners intrinsically reject refractory glow. **A UV scanner is the most suitable** for this oil-fired application, with an IR scanner as a backup. If the question is interpreted strictly by the *peak* wavelength, an **IR scanner** (PbS or InGaAs centred near 1.4 µm) would be optimal.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Unit Mistakes:** A very common error is computing $T^4$ as $(2.1)^4 \times 10^4$ or similar — the **4th power of 2100** is $1.9448 \times 10^{13}$, not $1.94 \times 10^{9}$. Always cube-and-multiply systematically. **2 marks lost per wrong unit.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 Set B]`

**(a)** Explain the **flame rod (ionization) method** of flame detection. Derive the expression for the ionization current and state the typical magnitude of flame current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Burner Management System (BMS)** and its sequence of operations. List any four flame-failure trip conditions that initiate a **Master Fuel Trip (MFT)**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Flame Rod / Ionization Method (7 marks):**

**Construction (2 marks):**
A **flame rod** is a single high-temperature-resistant metal electrode (typically Inconel or Kanthal) sheathed in a high-alumina ceramic insulator, with only the tip exposed. It is mounted such that the tip projects into the flame zone, with the **burner body (or a second electrode)** acting as the return path.

**Working principle (3 marks):**
An AC potential of about **250 V, 50/60 Hz** is applied between the flame rod and the grounded burner. The high-temperature combustion plasma contains **free electrons and positive ions** (Na⁺, K⁺) produced by Saha ionization. The flame conducts electricity in a **non-ohmic, rectifying manner** because:

- The rod is small (small emitting area) and the burner is large — so current flows only in one half-cycle.
- The result is a **rectified DC microampere-level current** (typically **1–25 µA**).

**Derivation of ionization current (1 mark):**

$$I = \frac{V_{flame}}{R_{flame}} = n_e \, e \, A \, v_d$$

where $n_e$ is the electron number density, $e$ the elementary charge, $A$ the effective cross-section, and $v_d$ the electron drift velocity.

**Typical magnitude (1 mark):**
Healthy gas/oil flame → **5–25 µA**; weak flame → **1–3 µA**; no flame → **< 0.5 µA** (trip threshold).

---

**Part (b) — BMS and MFT Conditions (7 marks):**

**Burner Management System definition (2 marks):**
The BMS is a **safety-critical control and interlock system** that manages the safe start-up, operation, and shut-down of one or more burners. It enforces the firing sequence, validates permissives, monitors flame, and initiates protective trips. BMS is implemented using **redundant PLCs, hardwired relays, and SIL-rated logic** (per IEC 61508 / IEC 61511).

**Sequence of operations (3 marks):**
1. **Pre-start checks** — fuel pressure, air flow, cooling water, FD/ID fans.
2. **Purge** — 5 minutes (or volume-based) of air flow with all fuel valves proven closed.
3. **Light-off** — igniter ON → pilot fuel valve open → trial for ignition (3–10 s).
4. **Main fuel admission** — only after pilot flame is proven.
5. **Run** — continuous flame monitoring.
6. **Normal shutdown** — fuel valves close, fans run for cool-down.
7. **Emergency shutdown (MFT)** — immediate fuel isolation, lockout, alarm.

**Four flame-failure / MFT conditions (2 marks):**
1. **Loss of flame (LOF)** — scanner signal falls below threshold for > 1–2 s.
2. **Loss of all flames** in multi-burner furnaces.
3. **Loss of airflow** or FD/ID fan trip.
4. **High furnace pressure** (implosion / puff prevention).
5. **Fuel pressure high/low** deviations.
6. **Atomizing steam failure** (oil-fired).
7. **Manual MFT push-button** operated.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — BMS ≠ Combustion Control:** Many students confuse **BMS** (safety, trip logic) with **Combustion Control System (CCS)** (O₂ trim, fuel–air ratio). The BMS is a **safety system**; the CCS optimizes efficiency. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for conflating the two. **2 marks lost** for this confusion.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — print this and revise the night before the exam.**

- [ ] Flame monitoring is a **safety-instrumented function (SIF)** within the **Burner Management System (BMS)**; its primary goal is **explosion prevention**, not measurement.
- [ ] The **four sensed flame properties** are: ionization, UV, IR, and visible flicker — each exploits a different physical phenomenon.
- [ ] **Flame rod** → senses ionized plasma → active method → best for **gas/oil**; gives **1–25 µA** DC.
- [ ] **UV scanner** → senses $\lambda < 300$ nm → **intrinsically rejects hot refractory** → best for **gas/oil**.
- [ ] **IR scanner** → senses $\lambda = 800$–$2400$ nm → best for **coal/heavy oil**; needs flicker discrimination to reject refractory.
- [ ] **Stefan–Boltzmann law** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ governs total flame radiation; use $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.
- [ ] **Wien's law** $\lambda_{max} = b/T$, with $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K, locates the peak emission.
- [ ] **Photoelectric** photon energy $E = hc/\lambda$; for UV at 250 nm, $E \approx 5$ eV.
- [ ] **Flicker frequency band** is 6–25 Hz — this is what the discriminator band-pass filter is tuned to.
- [ ] **MFT (Master Fuel Trip)** is the ultimate protective action: it closes all fuel valves instantly and locks out the unit.
- [ ] **Trial-for-ignition** is typically **3 to 10 s**; absence of flame here = lockout.
- [ ] **Purge time** of 5 minutes (or 5 furnace volumes) is mandatory before light-off to clear unburnt fuel.
- [ ] Use $\lvert x \rvert$ (not `|x|`) in any markdown table cell containing mathematical notation.
- [ ] Always pair **property ↔ detector** in 3-mark questions; unmatched pairs score zero.
- [ ] **BMS ≠ CCS** — safety vs. efficiency — do not conflate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smoke Density Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Smoke Density Measurement ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Smoke Density** is defined as the degree of opacity (or blackness) of the flue gas stream exhausted from a boiler stack, expressed as a percentage of light extinction caused by suspended particulate matter (soot, fly-ash, unburnt hydrocarbons) when a collimated light beam is transmitted through the gas column. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context, **Smoke Density Measurement (SDM)** is the process of quantitatively determining the particulate loading and optical opacity of stack emissions to monitor combustion efficiency and ensure compliance with environmental emission norms. The two key optical parameters used in power plant instrumentation are: - **Opacity (O):** The percentage of incident light that is prevented from passing through the gas stream. Expressed as: $$O = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\,\%$$ - **Optical Density (D):** The logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of incident to transmitted light intensity. $$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The standard reference wavelength used in industrial smoke density monitors is **540 nm (green light)** as recommended by the **International Commission on Illumination (CIE)** because the human eye is most sensitive to this wavelength and particulate scattering is wavelength-dependent. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine standing on a highway on a foggy morning. As the fog becomes thicker, you can see less and less of the car headlights approaching you. The light from the headlamps is *extincted* (reduced) by the water droplets suspended in the air. **Smoke density measurement works on exactly the same principle.** A beam of light is projected across the flue gas duct, and a photodetector on the opposite side measures how much of that light "survives" the journey. The more smoke particles (soot, fly-ash) present, the darker the smoke, and the less light reaches the detector. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of pouring dark coffee into a transparent glass. A single drop of coffee allows most light to pass. Add ten drops, and the glass becomes nearly opaque. The "darkness" you perceive is precisely what a smoke density meter measures — except the instrument does it electronically and continuously. ### 1.3 Industrial Significance | Parameter | Standard Reference | |---|---| | **Stack Opacity Limit (Coal Plants)** | **< 20%** (CPCB / EPA norms) | | **Reference Wavelength** | **540 nm** | | **Optical Path Length** | Typically **0.5 m to 6 m** | | **Ringelmann Scale** | 0 (white) to 5 (black) | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle: The Optical Foundation Smoke density measurement rests on the **Beer-Lambert Law of light absorption**, which states that the attenuation of a light beam passing through a particulate medium is an exponential function of the particulate concentration and the optical path length. The operational logic is broken into the following structured steps: - **Step 1 — Light Projection:** A stabilized, collimated light source (typically a tungsten-halogen lamp or LED) emits a light beam of intensity $I_0$ across the flue gas duct. - **Step 2 — Interaction with Particulates:** As the beam traverses the smoke, photons are absorbed and scattered by soot particles, fly-ash, and unburnt carbon particles. - **Step 3 — Attenuation:** The transmitted intensity $I$ reaching the photodetector is reduced according to the Beer-Lambert exponential law. - **Step 4 — Photodetection:** A photodiode or photomultiplier tube converts the attenuated light intensity into a proportional electrical current. - **Step 5 — Signal Conditioning:** The electronic unit computes opacity, optical density, and equivalent Ringelmann number, then displays the value and triggers alarms if limits are breached. > [!IMPORTANT] > The 'Why' Behind It: Combustion inefficiency directly produces visible smoke. By continuously monitoring smoke density, plant operators can detect **maladjusted burners, low excess air, incomplete combustion, and inefficient soot-blowing schedules** in real time. ### 2.2 Methods of Smoke Density Measurement #### A. Ringelmann Smoke Chart Method (Manual / Visual) - A series of five gridded charts shaded from **0% (white)** to **100% (black)** in 20% steps. - An observer compares the stack plume against the chart at a standardized distance. - Largely obsolete in modern plants — replaced by electronic instruments. - Subjective, weather-dependent, and operator-fatigue affected. #### B. Transmissometer (Light Extinction / Transmission Method) - Measures the **transmitted** light intensity $I$ through a known path length $L$. - The most widely used method in modern thermal power stations. - Operates in situ (probe inserted into the duct) or cross-duct (source and detector on opposite walls). #### C. Light Scattering Method - Measures light scattered at 90° or forward angles by particulates. - Best suited for low-to-medium dust concentrations. - Used in electrostatic precipitator outlet monitoring. #### D. Light Reflection Method - A combined source-detector probe measures light reflected back from particulates. - Single-sided installation — useful where cross-duct mounting is not feasible. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha L}$ | Beer-Lambert Law (transmitted intensity) | W/m² | | 2 | $O = \left(1 - \dfrac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$ | Opacity percentage | % | | 3 | $D = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Optical (Absorbance) Density | dimensionless | | 4 | $D = \dfrac{\alpha L}{\ln(10)} \approx 0.4343\,\alpha L$ | Optical density in terms of extinction coefficient | dimensionless | | 5 | $\alpha = -\dfrac{1}{L}\ln\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Extinction coefficient | m⁻¹ | | 6 | $R = \dfrac{O}{20}$ | Ringelmann number (0 to 5) | dimensionless | | 7 | $C = \dfrac{\alpha}{k_m}$ | Particulate mass concentration (using specific extinction) | mg/m³ | Where: - $I_0$ = incident light intensity - $I$ = transmitted light intensity - $L$ = optical path length through the flue gas (in metres) - $\alpha$ = extinction coefficient (depends on particle size, refractive index, wavelength) - $k_m$ = specific mass extinction coefficient (m²/g) - $C$ = particulate mass concentration - $R$ = Ringelmann number (used in environmental compliance) > [!NOTE] > The boundary conditions for the Beer-Lambert Law: it is valid only for **monochromatic light, dilute suspensions, and uniform particle distribution**. At very high dust loadings (extinction > 95%), the relationship becomes non-linear due to multiple scattering. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Boiler Combustion Optimization:** Real-time smoke density feedback adjusts fuel-air ratios to achieve optimal excess air (~15–20%). - **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory for CPCB (India), EPA (USA), and IEA emission norms. - **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) / Bag Filter Performance Monitoring:** Detects broken bags, rapping failures, and ash-handling issues. - **Soot Blower Scheduling:** Triggers soot-blowing cycles only when smoke density crosses a threshold — saves steam and reduces tube erosion. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Transmitted Intensity Relationship (Beer-Lambert Law) Let us consider an elemental slice of flue gas of thickness $dx$ at a distance $x$ from the light source. The fractional reduction in intensity as the beam traverses this slice is proportional to the slice thickness and the local extinction coefficient $\alpha$. The differential form of the attenuation law is: $$\frac{dI}{dx} = -\alpha \cdot I$$ The negative sign indicates that intensity **decreases** as the beam propagates through the medium. **Step 1:** Separate variables to prepare for integration. $$\frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \, dx$$ **Step 2:** Integrate both sides from the source end ($x = 0$, $I = I_0$) to the detector end ($x = L$, $I = I$). $$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\alpha \int_{0}^{L} dx$$ **Step 3:** Evaluate the integrals. The left side is a natural logarithm; the right side is a simple linear function. $$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\alpha L$$ **Step 4:** Apply logarithm rules to combine. $$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\alpha L$$ **Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides to recover the intensity ratio. $$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\alpha L}$$ **Step 6:** Rearrange to obtain the final Beer-Lambert expression for transmitted intensity. $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha L}$$ This is the **fundamental equation** upon which all transmissometric smoke density meters operate. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: From Photodetector Current to Opacity > A cross-duct smoke density monitor is installed across a boiler flue gas duct. The optical path length is $L = 4\,\text{m}$. The photodetector registers a current of $3.8\,\mu\text{A}$, while the reference (zero-smoke) current is $I_0 = 10.0\,\mu\text{A}$. Calculate: > 1. The opacity of the flue gas > 2. The optical density > 3. The extinction coefficient **Given Data:** - $I_0 = 10.0\,\mu\text{A}$ - $I = 3.8\,\mu\text{A}$ - $L = 4\,\text{m}$ --- **Part (a): Opacity Calculation** Using the opacity formula from the cheat sheet: $$O = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100$$ Substituting values: $$O = \left(1 - \frac{3.8}{10.0}\right) \times 100$$ $$O = (1 - 0.38) \times 100 = 0.62 \times 100$$ $$\boxed{O = 62\,\%}$$ The flue gas opacity is **62%**, which is significantly above the CPCB regulatory limit of 20% — the plant operator should be alerted. --- **Part (b): Optical Density Calculation** Using the absorbance formula: $$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right)$$ Substituting values: $$D = \log_{10}\left(\frac{10.0}{3.8}\right) = \log_{10}(2.6316)$$ $$\boxed{D = 0.4202}$$ The optical density is **0.4202** (dimensionless). --- **Part (c): Extinction Coefficient Calculation** Starting from the Beer-Lambert law solved for $\alpha$: $$\alpha = -\frac{1}{L}\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right)$$ Substituting values: $$\alpha = -\frac{1}{4}\ln\left(\frac{3.8}{10.0}\right) = -\frac{1}{4}\ln(0.38)$$ Computing the natural logarithm: $\ln(0.38) = -0.9676$. $$\alpha = -\frac{1}{4}(-0.9676) = \frac{0.9676}{4}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha = 0.2419\,\text{m}^{-1}}$$ The extinction coefficient is **0.2419 m⁻¹**. --- **Part (d — Bonus): Particulate Mass Concentration** If the specific mass extinction coefficient is $k_m = 0.05\,\text{m}^2/\text{g}$: $$C = \frac{\alpha}{k_m} = \frac{0.2419}{0.05} = 4.838\,\text{g/m}^3$$ $$\boxed{C \approx 4.84\,\text{g/m}^3}$$ This corresponds to a heavily loaded flue gas stream — typically seen in poorly tuned chain-grate stoker furnaces. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Method Comparison | Feature | Ringelmann Chart | Transmissometer | Light Scattering | Light Reflection | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Principle** | Visual comparison | Light extinction | Angular scattering | Back-scatter | | **Accuracy** | Low (~ ±10%) | High (~ ±2%) | Medium (~ ±5%) | Medium (~ ±5%) | | **Path Length** | Visual (open stack) | 0.5–6 m | 0.1–1 m | 0.05–0.3 m | | **Installation** | None | Cross-duct or in-situ | Single-side | Single-side | | **Output** | Discrete (0–5) | Continuous analog/digital | Continuous | Continuous | | **Best For** | Compliance audits | Continuous monitoring | ESP outlet | Tight spaces | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cross-Duct Transmissometer ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Lamp PSU
Stabilized DC] --> B[Light Source
Tungsten Halogen 540 nm] B --> C[Collimating Optics
Biconvex Lens] C --> D[Flue Gas Duct
Optical Path L] D --> E[Focusing Optics
Biconvex Lens] E --> F[Photodetector
Silicon Photodiode] F --> G[Transimpedance
Amplifier] G --> H[Microcontroller
ADC + DSP] H --> I[Display Unit
LCD Percentage] H --> J[Alarm Relay
Threshold Comparator] H --> K[4-20 mA Output
To DCS / PLC] H --> L[RS485 Modbus
Communication] M[Reference Detector
Beam Stability Check] --> H N[Auto Zero / Span
Calibration Module] --> H ``` ### 4.2 Schematic Flow of Signal Processing in SDM ```mermaid flowchart TD S0[Stack Flue Gas] --> S1[Light Source Emits
Intensity I0] S1 --> S2[Particulate Attenuation
Beer Lambert Law] S2 --> S3[Photodetector Captures
Intensity I] S3 --> S4[Current to Voltage
Conversion] S4 --> S5[Amplification and
Noise Filtering] S5 --> S6[Analog to Digital
Conversion] S6 --> S7[Computation Unit
Calculates Opacity and Density] S7 --> S8{Threshold
Check} S8 -- Above Limit --> S9[Activate Alarm
and Trip Signal] S8 -- Below Limit --> S10[Normal Display
and Logging] S11[Auto Calibration
Using Reference Cell] --> S7 ``` ### 4.3 Component-Level Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Transmitter Side T1[Power Supply Unit] --> T2[Lamp Driver Circuit] T2 --> T3[Collimated Light Source] end subgraph Receiver Side R1[Photodetector] --> R2[Pre Amplifier Stage] R2 --> R3[Band Pass Filter 540 nm] R3 --> R4[Main Amplifier] end T3 -->|Beam Through Stack| R1 R4 --> R5[Signal Processor and Display] R5 --> R6[Control Room DCS] R5 --> R7[Data Logger and Historian] R5 --> R8[Alarm Annunciator Panel] ``` ### 4.4 Key Operating Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD P0[Power On] --> P1[Self Diagnostics
Lamp and Detector Health] P1 --> P2[Auto Zero Calibration
Using Clean Air Reference] P2 --> P3[Normal Operation Mode
Continuous Monitoring] P3 --> P4{Opacity Reading
Available} P4 --> P5[Convert to Ringelmann
and Mass Concentration] P5 --> P6[Update Display
and DCS Output] P6 --> P7{Reading Above
Threshold} P7 -- Yes --> P8[Trigger Alarm
and Log Event] P7 -- No --> P3 P8 --> P9[Periodic Auto Span
Calibration Check] P9 --> P3 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define smoke density and opacity. State the standard reference wavelength used in industrial smoke density monitors.* **Model Answer:** **Smoke density** is a measure of the particulate loading in flue gas, quantified by the optical attenuation of a light beam passing through the gas column. It is an indicator of combustion completeness and emission cleanliness. **Opacity** is the percentage of incident light that fails to be transmitted through the flue gas stream due to absorption and scattering by particulates. $$\text{Opacity} = \left(1 - \frac{I}{I_0}\right) \times 100\,\%$$ The standard reference wavelength used is **540 nm (green light)**, as recommended by the **CIE**, since human visual sensitivity peaks in this region and particulate scattering effects are reproducible. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the Beer-Lambert Law as applied to smoke density measurement. What is the role of the extinction coefficient?* **Model Answer:** The Beer-Lambert Law applied to smoke density measurement states that the transmitted light intensity $I$ decays exponentially with the optical path length $L$ through the flue gas: $$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha L}$$ Where $\alpha$ is the **extinction coefficient** (m⁻¹), which characterizes the combined effect of absorption and scattering per unit length of the gas column. It depends on particle size distribution, refractive index, wavelength of light, and particulate concentration. The extinction coefficient is a key parameter for converting optical measurements into mass concentration of particulates. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) --- #### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q.A. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a cross-duct transmissometer type smoke density monitor. Discuss the role of the auto-zero and auto-span calibration features. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle:** A cross-duct transmissometer measures the attenuation of a collimated light beam (typically 540 nm) as it traverses the flue gas duct between a transmitter unit and a receiver unit mounted on opposite walls of the stack. **Block Diagram:** The system consists of the following functional blocks: 1. **Light Source Assembly:** A stabilized tungsten-halogen lamp or LED emits a collimated beam of constant intensity $I_0$. The lamp is driven by a constant-current PSU to eliminate intensity drift. 2. **Collimating Optics:** A biconvex lens system shapes the diverging light into a parallel beam of small divergence. 3. **Optical Path:** The beam crosses the flue gas duct of length $L$ (typically 1–6 m).
