Power plant DCS typically targets **SIL 2 / SIL 3** for burner management (BMS) and **turbine overspeed protection**. ## 2.7 Real-World Utility In modern Indian power stations (NTPC, Adani Power, Tata Power), the DCS is the **single point of truth** for operating the entire **Boiler-Turbine-Generator (BTG)** island. **Unit coordinated control** — where boiler firing rate, turbine valve position, and generator MW output are jointly optimised — is impossible without a tightly integrated DCS. The **250 MW + 500 MW + 660 MW + 800 MW supercritical units** commissioned post-2010 in India (as per CEA norms) **mandatorily** use DCS for normal operation; manual control is only a backup. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Availability of Dual-Redundant DCS Controller **Given:** - Single controller MTBF = $\text{MTBF}_s = 100{,}000$ hours. - MTTR (time to swap hot-standby module) = $\text{MTTR}_s = 4$ hours. **Find:** Availability of the parallel (dual-redundant) configuration. ### Step 1 — Single-Channel Availability By definition: $$ A_s = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}_s}{\text{MTBF}_s + \text{MTTR}_s} $$ Substituting the values: $$ A_s = \dfrac{100{,}000}{100{,}000 + 4} $$ $$ A_s = \dfrac{100{,}000}{100{,}004} \approx 0.99996 $$ This corresponds to **"Four-Nines" availability** (≈ 21 minutes downtime/year for a single controller). ### Step 2 — Unavailability of Single Channel $$ U_s = 1 - A_s = 1 - 0.99996 = 4 \times 10^{-5} $$ ### Step 3 — Probability Both Channels Fail Simultaneously For parallel redundancy, the system fails **only if both channels fail at the same time**. The unavailabilities multiply (assuming independence): $$ U_{\text{system}} = U_s \times U_s = (4 \times 10^{-5})^2 $$ $$ U_{\text{system}} = 16 \times 10^{-10} = 1.6 \times 10^{-9} $$ ### Step 4 — System Availability $$ A_{\text{system}} = 1 - U_{\text{system}} = 1 - 1.6 \times 10^{-9} $$ $$ A_{\text{system}} \approx 0.9999999984 $$ This corresponds to **≈ 5 seconds of downtime per year** — nearly **"Six-Nines"** availability. ### Step 5 — Improvement Factor $$ \text{Improvement} = \dfrac{A_{\text{system}}}{A_s} = \dfrac{0.9999999984}{0.99996} \approx 1.00004 $$ While the absolute numbers look similar, the **unavailability has dropped by 4 orders of magnitude** — the figure that truly matters for plant safety. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Sampling Rate for Boiler Drum Level Control The **drum level** of a 500 MW boiler oscillates due to "swell and shrink" effects with a **natural frequency** of approximately $f_{\text{max}} = 0.5\ \text{Hz}$. ### Step 1 — Apply Nyquist–Shannon Theorem To reconstruct the drum-level signal without aliasing: $$ f_{\text{sample}} \ge 2 \cdot f_{\text{max}} $$ $$ f_{\text{sample}} \ge 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0\ \text{Hz} $$ ### Step 2 — Apply Engineering Safety Factor (× 5 to × 10) In practice, DCS samples at 5–10× Nyquist: $$ f_{\text{sample, DCS}} = 10 \times f_{\text{Nyquist}} = 10\ \text{Hz} $$ ### Step 3 — Corresponding Scan Period $$ T_{\text{scan}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{sample}}} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.1\ \text{s} = 100\ \text{ms} $$ This 100 ms scan is the **industry standard** for fast boiler loops. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU High-Frequency Examination Topics | S.No | Concept | Engineering Principle | Typical KTU Question Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Purdue Levels 0–4 | Plant hierarchy standard | "Explain the 5 levels of DCS in a thermal power plant" | | 2 | Bumpless Transfer | Continuity of control during redundancy switch | "Why is bumpless transfer mandatory for boiler drum level?" | | 3 | SIL / IEC 61508 | Functional safety | "Calculate SIL for given PFD" | | 4 | HART vs Fieldbus | Smart instrumentation | Compare signal types | | 5 | Burner Management System (BMS) | SIS built on DCS | "Role of BMS in DCS architecture" | | 6 | OPC UA | Interoperability | "How does OPC UA enable multi-vendor DCS integration?" | | 7 | Historian & Trend | Time-series database | "Why is 1-second historian resolution inadequate for bearing vibration?" | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — ISA-95 / Purdue DCS Architecture for a Thermal Power Plant ```mermaid graph TB subgraph L4["LEVEL 4 — ENTERPRISE"] ERP["ERP / SAP / Oracle
Financial, Procurement, HR
Regulatory Reporting CEA / CERC"] end subgraph L3["LEVEL 3 — OPERATIONS / PLANT INFORMATION"] PI["Plant Information Server
Performance KPIs
Heat Rate, Aux Consumption"] MMS["Maintenance Mgmt System
Condition Monitoring
Predictive Analytics"] end subgraph L2["LEVEL 2 — SUPERVISORY"] OS1["Operator Station 1
HMI Mimic, Trends, Alarms"] OS2["Operator Station 2
Shift Engineer View"] ES["Engineering Station
Configuration and Tuning"] HIS["Historian Server
Time-Series Database"] ALM["Alarm Management
ISA 18.2 Compliant"] end subgraph L1["LEVEL 1 — BASIC CONTROL"] CTRL1["Redundant Controller A
Boiler Control Loops
PID Cascade Feedforward"] CTRL2["Redundant Controller B
Turbine Governor AVR"] CTRL3["Redundant Controller C
Balance of Plant BOP"] IOA["I O Subsystem AI AO DI DO
Hot Swappable"] end subgraph L0["LEVEL 0 — FIELD"] SEN["Sensors
PT TT FT LT Vibration"] ACT["Actuators
Control Valves Dampers Drives"] end NET1["Plant Ethernet Backbone
TCP IP Redundant"] NET2["Fieldbus Network
Foundation Fieldbus HART"] ERP --- NET1 PI --- NET1 MMS --- NET1 NET1 --- OS1 NET1 --- OS2 NET1 --- ES NET1 --- HIS NET1 --- ALM OS1 --- CTRL1 OS2 --- CTRL2 ES --- CTRL3 CTRL1 --- IOA CTRL2 --- IOA CTRL3 --- IOA IOA --- NET2 NET2 --- SEN NET2 --- ACT ``` > [!NOTE] > The arrows above represent **logical data flow**, not just physical cabling. **Level 4 ↔ Level 0 is unidirectional command flow (top-down) and telemetry flow (bottom-up)**. **Horizontal flow** between controllers (e.g., CTRL1 ↔ CTRL2) carries **interlock and coordination signals** like the unit master / boiler follow / turbine follow mode logic. ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Dual-Redundant Controller with Bumpless Transfer ```mermaid graph LR PROC["Process Variable
e.g., Drum Level"] PT["Pressure Transmitter 4-20 mA"] PC["Primary Controller
PID Block"] BC["Backup Controller
PID Block Standby"] SW["Auto Transfer Switch
Bumpless Logic"] CV["Control Valve Actuator"] HMI["Operator HMI
Status Indication"] PROC --> PT PT --> PC PT --> BC PC --> SW BC --> SW SW --> CV PC -.->|"Heartbeat Sync"| BC PC -->|"Diagnostics OK"| HMI BC -->|"Diagnostics OK"| HMI ``` **Operation:** - The Primary and Backup controllers continuously exchange **heartbeat signals** and **track each other's output (PV, SP, MV)**. - If Primary fails (no heartbeat for > 200 ms), the **Auto Transfer Switch** routes Backup's output to the valve **without any bump**, and the operator HMI displays the fault. ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — DCS Communication Network Topology ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CN["CONTROL NETWORK - REDUNDANT ETHERNET"] SW1["Network Switch A
Primary Ring"] SW2["Network Switch B
Secondary Ring"] end OS1["Operator Station 1"] --- SW1 OS2["Operator Station 2"] --- SW2 ENG["Engineering Station"] --- SW1 ENG --- SW2 HIS["Historian"] --- SW1 HIS --- SW2 CTRL1["Controller 1"] --- SW1 CTRL1 --- SW2 CTRL2["Controller 2"] --- SW1 CTRL2 --- SW2 SW1 <-->|"Ring Protocol PRP HSR"| SW2 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a Distributed Control System (DCS). List any four advantages of DCS over conventional centralized control. **Model Answer:** A **Distributed Control System (DCS)** is a digitally networked control architecture in which the regulatory, sequential, and supervisory control functions are distributed across multiple geographically dispersed intelligent controllers, all interconnected via a high-speed redundant communication network. **Four Advantages over Centralised Control:** 1. **Higher Reliability** — Failure of one controller does not shut down the entire plant (fault containment). 2. **Reduced Cabling** — Fieldbus networks drastically reduce point-to-point copper wiring. 3. **Scalability** — New control loops / I/O can be added by plugging modules into existing racks. 4. **Centralised Supervision with Distributed Control** — Operators view the whole plant on HMI, while control decisions are taken locally (faster response). --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by "Bumpless Transfer" in a redundant DCS controller? **Model Answer:** **Bumpless Transfer** is the automatic, smooth handover of control from a **primary (active)** controller to a **standby (backup)** controller — or vice versa — such that the **manipulated variable (valve output) does not experience any step change** during the switchover. It is achieved by continuously **tracking the SP, PV, and MV** between the two controllers. Bumpless transfer is mandatory for critical loops like **boiler drum level, furnace pressure, and turbine speed** to avoid process upsets that could otherwise lead to a unit trip. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Distributed Control System (DCS)** used in a modern thermal power plant. Describe each level of the **Purdue / ISA-95 model** in detail, highlighting the role of DCS in the **Boiler–Turbine–Generator (BTG)** coordinated control. #### Part (a) — DCS Architecture Diagram and Level Description (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** A modern thermal power plant DCS follows the **5-level Purdue Reference Model**: | Level | Function | Typical Hardware in Power Plant | |---|---|---| | **Level 0** | Field I/O — Sensors & Actuators | PT, TT, FT, LT; control valves, damper drives, soot blower motors | | **Level 1** | Basic / Regulatory Control | Redundant controllers (ABB AC 800M / Honeywell C300 / Siemens AS 410) executing PID loops | | **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | Operator Stations with HMI mimics, Alarm logs, Trends, Engineering Workstations, Historian | | **Level 3** | Operations Management | Plant Information Server, Performance Calculator, Maintenance Scheduler | | **Level 4** | Enterprise | ERP (SAP), Regulatory reporting to CEA/CERC, Fuel management | Refer to **Section 4.1** for the corresponding block diagram. **Role in BTG Coordinated Control:** - The **Boiler Follows (BF)**, **Turbine Follows (TF)**, and **Coordinated Control (CC)** modes are implemented in the DCS. - The boiler master controller modulates **fuel flow, FD/ID fan speeds, and feedwater** to track load demand. - The turbine master controller modulates **HP/IP/LP valve positions** in synchronisation with the boiler. - DCS integrates **AVR (Automatic Voltage Regulator)**, **Governor**, and **Synchroniser** via standard interfaces (Modbus/OPC). #### Part (b) — Redundancy, Bumpless Transfer & Communication (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Redundancy Strategy:** - **Controllers** — Hot redundant (1oo2D or 2oo3 voting) for critical loops. - **I/O Cards** — Redundant pairs with signal-line diagnostics. - **Network** — Redundant Ethernet ring (PRP/HSR), redundant power supplies. - **Servers** — Hot standby historian and alarm servers. **Bumpless Transfer Mechanism:** Both controllers (Primary A and Backup B) compute the same PID output. B continuously tracks A's setpoint, process variable, and output. When A fails (heartbeat timeout > 200 ms), B's output is routed to the actuator with **zero step change**. **Communication:** - **Level 0–1:** HART / Foundation Fieldbus. - **Level 1–2:** Proprietary deterministic Ethernet (e.g., Honeywell FTE, Siemens Profinet IRT). - **Level 2–4:** TCP/IP, OPC UA for vendor-neutral exchange. **Conclusion:** The combination of redundancy, bumpless transfer, and a layered network ensures that the DCS achieves **> 99.999% availability**, meeting the **"Five Nines" reliability** demanded by 24×7 power generation. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** Explain the difference between **DCS, PLC, and SCADA** systems. Discuss in detail the role of DCS in implementing **Burner Management System (BMS)** and **Turbine Control** in a 500 MW thermal power unit. #### Part (a) — DCS vs PLC vs SCADA (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** | Parameter | DCS | PLC | SCADA | |---|---|---|---| | Architecture | Distributed controllers with central supervision | Standalone / networked | Master-slave telemetry | | Loop type | Continuous (PID-heavy) | Discrete / Sequential | Monitoring-heavy | | Redundancy | Built-in (controllers, I/O, network) | Add-on (optional) | Limited | | Typical Vendor | ABB 800xA, Siemens PCS 7 | Siemens S7, Rockwell | WinCC, iFIX | | Best Suited | BTG island, refinery | Coal handling, packaging | Hydro telemetry, water distribution | **Key insight for KTU:** DCS is preferred over PLC for **continuous regulatory control** (boiler drum level, furnace pressure) because of its **inherent redundancy** and **integrated PID blocks**. PLC is preferred for **discrete interlocks** (conveyor interlocks, valve sequencing). SCADA is typically a **supervisory layer over PLC/DCS** for remote sites. #### Part (b) — BMS and Turbine Control via DCS (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** **Burner Management System (BMS):** - Implemented as a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** within the DCS, typically **SIL 2 / SIL 3** certified per IEC 61508. - Performs: - **Pre-ignition purge** (≥ 4 air changes of furnace volume). - **Flame scanning** using UV / IR detectors. - **Fuel trip** (master fuel trip MFT) on flame failure, low furnace pressure, loss of FD fan. - **Permissive interlock logic** ensuring safe light-off sequence. - DCS records all trips with **Sequence of Events (SOE)** timestamping at **1 ms resolution** for post-trip analysis. **Turbine Control via DCS:** - **Speed/Load Governor:** PID controller modulates HP valve opening to maintain speed (3000 rpm for 50 Hz) and load setpoint. - **Turbine Stress Controller:** Computes **stress indices** (e.g., LDC — Life Duration Counter) and limits acceleration rate to protect rotor. - **Auto Synchronisation:** DCS receives bus voltage, frequency, and phase from the synchroniser and issues **close breaker** command when slip < 0.1 Hz and phase angle < ±10°. - **Overspeed Trip:** Independent mechanical + DCS-based electronic trip at **110% rated speed (3300 rpm)** — SIL 3 integrity. **Conclusion:** In a 500 MW unit, BMS and Turbine Control are **non-negotiable functions** of the DCS, with redundancy and SIL classification ensuring that no single failure can cause a hazardous event. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Writing "DCS is just an advanced PLC"** — DCS and PLC have *fundamentally different* design philosophies. Marks will be cut if not differentiated. > 2. **Confusing HMI with DCS** — HMI is **only Level 2**. The actual control happens at Level 1. > 3. **Forgetting Bumpless Transfer** — Almost every "redundancy" question expects this term. Omitting it = lose 2 marks. > 4. **Mixing up Purdue Levels** — A common error is placing the historian at Level 1 instead of Level 2. > 5. **No units in availability calculation** — Always mention **"Five Nines" = 99.999%** as a qualitative check. > 6. **Ignoring IEC 61508 / SIL** — Functional safety is a high-weight topic in 2024 scheme papers. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **DCS = Distributed (controllers) + Centralised (supervision)** — never the other way around. - ✅ **Purdue Levels: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4** — Field → Control → Supervisory → Operations → Enterprise. - ✅ **Bumpless Transfer** is the *signature feature* of DCS redundancy. - ✅ **Redundancy math:** $A_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - A_1)(1 - A_2)$ — a favourite KTU numerical. - ✅ **Nyquist:** Sample at **≥ 2 × max signal frequency**; engineering practice uses **5×–10×** for boiler loops. - ✅ **SIL** for BMS / Turbine Overspeed = **SIL 2 / SIL 3**. - ✅ **Fieldbus = Level 0–1**; **Ethernet = Level 1–4**. - ✅ **OPC UA** is the open standard for multi-vendor interoperability. - ✅ **Historian** stores every tag at **1-second (or faster) resolution** — vital for performance analysis. - ✅ **Power Plant DCS vendors:** ABB 800xA, Honeywell Experion, Siemens PCS 7, Emerson DeltaV, Yokogawa CENTUM VP. - ✅ **BMS functions:** Purge → Ignite → Modulate → Trip; **MFT (Master Fuel Trip)** is the ultimate safety action. - ✅ **Turbine control modes:** **Turbine Follows (TF), Boiler Follows (BF), Coordinated Control (CC).** - ✅ **Auto-synch parameters:** Voltage diff ≤ 5%, Frequency diff ≤ 0.2 Hz, Phase angle ≤ ±10°.

topics.title: Interlocks in Boiler Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interlocks in Boiler Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Interlocks in Boiler Operation:** A boiler interlock is a **logically structured safety-protection system** comprising sensors, logic solvers, and final control elements that automatically initiate corrective or trip actions to prevent hazardous operating conditions. It enforces a defined sequence of **permissive conditions** during start-up, steady-state operation, and shut-down, ensuring that no unsafe state transition can occur in the boiler–turbine–generator (BTG) train.

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, interlocks are classified under the broader umbrella of **Process Safety & Burner Management Systems (BMS)** and are treated as an outcome of Course Outcome CO3: *"Apply instrumentation concepts for safe and efficient operation of thermal power plant auxiliaries."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Railway Signalman" Viewpoint

Imagine a railway signalman controlling a train's entry into a station.

- The **train (boiler)** cannot move forward unless **all track switches (permissives)** are aligned correctly.
- If the signalman detects a **broken rail (fault condition)**, he immediately **trips the signal to red** and prevents the train from proceeding.
- The signalman follows a strict **checklist (interlock logic)** before authorising movement.

This is exactly how a boiler interlock works: it is a **digital or relay-based "signalman"** that continuously evaluates whether the boiler's operating envelope is safe and only permits a state change (e.g., light-off, load increase) when **every defined permissive condition** is satisfied. A violation of any condition forces an **automatic trip**, mirroring the red signal.

## 1.3 Why Interlocks Are Mandatory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR-1950)** and the **KTU Power Plant Instrumentation syllabus**, every boiler above a rated evaporation capacity must implement a certified interlock chain covering at minimum: drum level, furnace pressure, fuel supply, air supply, and flame monitoring. The **standard trip response time** for critical interlocks is mandated to be **≤ 3 seconds**.

---

## 1.4 KTU-Categorised Classification of Boiler Interlocks

| S.No | Category | Function | Typical Initiation |
|:----:|:---------|:---------|:-------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical Interlock** | Prevents physical actuation of valves/switches out of sequence | Cam, key, latch arrangement |
| 2 | **Electrical Interlock** | Hard-wired relay-based permissive logic | Auxiliary contacts of contactors |
| 3 | **Process / Safety Interlock** | Initiated by process variables crossing trip limits | Drum level, pressure, temperature, flame sensors |
| 4 | **Operational / Sequential Interlock** | Enforces a defined start-up / shut-down sequence | PLC / DCS logic |
| 5 | **Software / Logic Interlock** | Implemented in Burner Management System (BMS) firmware | IEC 61131-3 languages (FBD, LD) |

---

## 1.5 The Three Foundational Principles of Interlocking

> [!NOTE]
> **Principle 1 – Permissiveness:** A subsequent operation is *allowed only if* all prior permissive conditions read TRUE.
>
> **Principle 2 – Fail-Safe Operation:** Any sensor, wire-break, or power-loss event must drive the system to its **safest state** (typically a trip).
>
> **Principle 3 – First-Out Indication:** The control system must record and display **which interlock caused the trip first** to aid post-trip diagnostics.

These three principles form the **evaluation rubric** used by KTU examiners for full-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of a Boiler Interlock Loop

A boiler interlock is **not a single device** but a five-stage closed loop. Understanding this layered architecture is essential for both KTU theory and numerical analysis.

### Stage 1 — Sensing Layer
- **Drum Level Transmitter (LT)** — typically a **differential-pressure (DP) cell** with a **±150 mm WC** calibrated span.
- **Furnace Pressure Transmitter (PT)** — range typically **−50 mm WC to +50 mm WC**.
- **Flame Scanner (FT/FS)** — UV or IR photodiode, response **≤ 1 s**.
- **Fuel Pressure / Temperature Transmitters (PT, TT)** — for oil/gas burners.

### Stage 2 — Logic Solver Layer
This is where the **permissive logic** is evaluated. In legacy plants, this is realised with **electromechanical relays wired in series (AND logic) or parallel (OR logic)**. In modern stations, it is implemented inside a **Safety PLC (SIL-2 / SIL-3 rated per IEC 61508)**.

### Stage 3 — Final Control Layer
- **Solenoid Valves** on fuel lines.
- **Inlet Guide Vane (IGV) actuators** on FD/ID fans.
- **Master Trip Relays** (also called **Master Fuel Trip (MFT) relays**).

### Stage 4 — Actuation Layer
The physical shutdown — fuel shut-off, fan tripping, generator breaker opening — happens here.

### Stage 5 — Annunciation & First-Out Layer
Beacon lamps, hooters, and DCS mimic screens display the **trip cause**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Master Fuel Trip (MFT)** is the *highest-level* interlock in any boiler. Once MFT is initiated, **all fuel inputs** (oil guns, gas valves, coal feeders) are commanded to close in less than **3 seconds**, and no reset is permitted until the operator clears all sub-trip causes.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula, boundary condition, and standard metric the KTU 2024 examiner expects for this topic. *(Note: vertical bars in equations use `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.)*

| # | Formula / Standard | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|:-:|:-------------------|:--------|:---------------------|
| 1 | $T_{trip} = T_{process} - T_{delay}$ | Effective trip time equals process deviation time minus sensor/logic delay | **0.5 – 3 s** |
| 2 | $\Delta P_{drum} = \rho_{w}\, g\, \Delta h$ | Differential pressure across a drum level column | $\rho_{w} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3,\ g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 3 | $V_{LP\,air} = 1.05 \times V_{stoich}$ | Combustion air flow set-point (excess-air factor) | **5 – 15 % excess** |
| 4 | $SIL = f(PFD_{avg})$ | Safety Integrity Level mapped to average Probability of Failure on Demand | SIL-1: $10^{-2}\!-\!10^{-1}$, SIL-4: $10^{-5}\!-\!10^{-4}$ |
| 5 | $\text{MFT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \quad \text{(OR-logic)}$ | Master Fuel Trip activates if **any** sub-trip $C_i$ asserts | Boolean summation |
| 6 | $P_{purge} \geq 30\,\text{mm WC}$ for $t \geq 5\,\text{min}$ | Pre-ignition purge must achieve this pressure-time integral | Standard furnace purge spec |
| 7 | $F_{flame} = \frac{I_{signal}}{I_{refuse}}$ | Flame stability factor (signal-to-noise ratio) | $F_{flame} \geq 1.5$ (healthy) |
| 8 | $t_{reset} \geq 30\,\text{s}$ | Minimum cool-down / post-trip dwell before reset is accepted | Boiler-specific |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Boiler interlocks are the cornerstone of **personnel safety**, **equipment protection**, and **regulatory compliance** in every coal-, oil-, or gas-fired power station. Beyond the textbook, the same logic-engineering principles appear in:

- **Nuclear reactor protection systems** (where trip logic is dual-redundant and SIL-4).
- **Offshore oil & gas** platform emergency shutdown (ESD) systems.
- **Refinery furnace burners** in petrochemical complexes.
- **Combined-cycle gas turbine (CCGT)** start-up sequencing.

Thus, mastery of this topic directly aligns the KTU student with **Industry 4.0 instrumentation roles** in NTPC, BHEL, Tata Power, and global EPC contractors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Drum-Level Trip Differential Pressure

The most analytically important KTU numerical on this topic concerns converting a **physical drum level set-point** into a **DP transmitter set-point**.

### Given Data
- Drum centre-line height above transmitter: $h_{ref} = 500\,\text{mm}$
- High-level trip set-point: $h_{trip} = +75\,\text{mm}$ above centre-line
- Low-level trip set-point: $h_{low} = -100\,\text{mm}$ below centre-line
- Water density: $\rho_{w} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Saturated-steam density (cold-leg): $\rho_{s} = 1.0\,\text{kg/m}^3$
- Acceleration due to gravity: $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$

### Step 1 — Establish the Reference Differential Pressure

The wet-leg DP cell is referenced against the steam space (negligible density). Therefore,

$$
\Delta P_{ref} = (\rho_{w} - \rho_{s})\, g\, h_{ref}
$$

Substituting values,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{ref} &= (1000 - 1.0) \times 9.81 \times 0.500 \\
&= 999 \times 9.81 \times 0.500 \\
&= 4899.55\,\text{Pa} \\
&\approx 50.0\,\text{mm WC} \quad \big(\text{since } 1\,\text{mm WC} \approx 9.81\,\text{Pa}\big)
\end{aligned}
$$

> The reference pressure corresponds to the drum being exactly half-full at the transmitter taps.

### Step 2 — Compute Trip-Level Differential Pressures

For the **high-level trip**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{high} &= \Delta P_{ref} + (\rho_{w} - \rho_{s})\, g\, h_{trip} \\
&= 4899.55 + (999)(9.81)(0.075) \\
&= 4899.55 + 734.55 \\
&= 5634.10\,\text{Pa} \\
&\approx 57.44\,\text{mm WC}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **low-level trip**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{low} &= \Delta P_{ref} - (\rho_{w} - \rho_{s})\, g\, h_{low} \\
&= 4899.55 - (999)(9.81)(0.100) \\
&= 4899.55 - 980.22 \\
&= 3919.33\,\text{Pa} \\
&\approx 39.95\,\text{mm WC}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Define the Trip Window

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_{low} \;\leq\; \Delta P_{measured} \;\leq\; \Delta P_{high}\;}
$$

$$
39.95\,\text{mm WC} \;\leq\; \Delta P_{measured} \;\leq\; 57.44\,\text{mm WC}
$$

If the measured DP falls **below 39.95 mm WC** → **LOW-WATER-LEVEL TRIP** (boiler is at risk of exposing furnace tubes).  
If the measured DP rises **above 57.44 mm WC** → **HIGH-WATER-LEVEL TRIP** (risk of carryover into superheater).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Furnace Purge Time & Volume Exchange

The pre-ignition purge interlock requires that the furnace volume be exchanged with a minimum of **5 air changes** before any fuel valve is permitted to open.

### Step 1 — Furnace Volume Expression

$$
V_{furnace} = L \times W \times H
$$

where $L$, $W$, $H$ are the internal length, width, and height of the combustion chamber.

### Step 2 — Required Purge Volume

$$
V_{purge} = N \times V_{furnace}
$$

with $N = 5$ (KTU standard) and **air-change constant** $= \mathbf{5}$.

### Step 3 — Purge Duration

$$
t_{purge} = \frac{V_{purge}}{Q_{FD\,fan}}
$$

### Worked Numerical

Assume a $10\,\text{m} \times 8\,\text{m} \times 15\,\text{m}$ furnace, with FD fan rated at $Q = 30\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{furnace} &= 10 \times 8 \times 15 = 1200\,\text{m}^3 \\
V_{purge} &= 5 \times 1200 = 6000\,\text{m}^3 \\
t_{purge} &= \frac{6000}{30} = 200\,\text{s} \approx 3.33\,\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the KTU code requires **at least 5 minutes** of purge with positive furnace pressure $\geq 30\,\text{mm WC}$, the **dominant constraint becomes the time, not the volume**. Therefore the operator must purge for $t_{purge,\,enforced} = 5\,\text{min}$ even if volumetric exchange is achieved earlier.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Boiler Interlocks & Their Logic

| Interlock Name | Sensor | Trip Condition | Logic Gate | Final Action | KTU Mark Weight |
|:---------------|:-------|:---------------|:-----------|:-------------|:---------------:|
| Low Drum Level | Drum LT | $\Delta P < 39.95\,\text{mm WC}$ | AND (2oo3) | MFT + Audio Alarm | **High** |
| High Drum Level | Drum LT | $\Delta P > 57.44\,\text{mm WC}$ | AND (1oo2) | Blowdown valve open + alarm | Medium |
| High Furnace Pressure | Furnace PT | $P > +25\,\text{mm WC}$ | OR | FD/ID fan trip + MFT | **High** |
| Low Furnace Pressure | Furnace PT | $P < -30\,\text{mm WC}$ | OR | Alarm only | Low |
| Loss of Flame | Flame Scanner | $F_{flame} < 1.0$ for $1\,\text{s}$ | OR (per burner) | Individual burner trip → MFT | **High** |
| Low Fuel Oil Pressure | Oil PT | $P < 5\,\text{kg/cm}^2$ | AND with TT | Oil gun trip | Medium |
| High Fuel Gas Pressure | Gas PT | $P > 4\,\text{kg/cm}^2$ | OR | Gas valve trip | Medium |
| FD Fan Failure | Fan aux. contactor | Current < 20 % rated | OR | MFT (if running) | Medium |
| Air-Flow Failure | Air-flow switch | $\Delta P < 5\,\text{mm WC}$ | OR | MFT | **High** |
| Purge Complete | Pressure + Timer | $\Delta P \geq 30\,\text{mm WC}$ AND $t \geq 5\,\text{min}$ | AND | Permit ignition | **High** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards Applied
- All node IDs are purely alphanumeric and **letter-prefixed** (e.g., `nodeA`, `logicB`).
- No reserved keywords used as standalone node names.
- Labels contain raw uppercase text only — no markdown bold, italics, or HTML.

---

## 4.2 Master Fuel Trip (MFT) Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["DRUM LEVEL LOW"] --> logicA
    nodeB["FURNACE PRESSURE HIGH"] --> logicA
    nodeC["LOSS OF FLAME"] --> logicA
    nodeD["FD FAN FAILURE"] --> logicA
    nodeE["PURGE NOT COMPLETE"] --> logicA
    nodeF["LOW FUEL PRESSURE"] --> logicA
    nodeG["EMERGENCY PB PRESSED"] --> logicA

    logicA{"OR LOGIC ANY TRUE"}
    logicA --> nodeH["MASTER FUEL TRIP RELAY ENERGISES"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["ALL FUEL VALVES CLOSE"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["FD AND ID FAN TRIP"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["GENERATOR BREAKER OPEN"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["HOOTER AND BEACON ON"]
    nodeH --> nodeM["FIRST OUT ANNUNCIATION"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every sensor on the left feeds an **OR-logic gate**. If **any one** asserts TRUE, the central Master Fuel Trip relay energises, simultaneously closing fuel valves, tripping fans, opening the breaker, and raising an alarm.

---

## 4.3 Boiler Start-Up Permissive Sequence (Interlock Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA["BOILER COLD START"] --> startB["CHECK FD FAN AVAILABLE"]
    startB -->|NOT READY| startC["ALARM FD FAN FAULT"]
    startB -->|READY| startD["START FD FAN"]
    startD --> startE["VERIFY AIRFLOW DP GT 5 MM WC"]
    startE -->|NO| startF["AIRFLOW FAILURE TRIP"]
    startE -->|YES| startG["INITIATE FURNACE PURGE 5 MIN"]
    startG --> startH["VERIFY FURNACE PRESSURE GT 30 MM WC"]
    startH -->|NO| startF
    startH -->|YES| startI["PURGE COMPLETE PERMISSIVE TRUE"]
    startI --> startJ["CHECK DRUM LEVEL IN BAND"]
    startJ -->|OUT OF BAND| startK["DRUM LEVEL TRIP"]
    startJ -->|IN BAND| startL["PERMIT IGNITION OIL GUN 1"]
    startL --> startM["VERIFY FLAME PRESENT 5 SEC"]
    startM -->|NO| startN["FLAME FAILURE TRIP"]
    startM -->|YES| startO["PERMIT NEXT BURNER SEQUENCE"]
    startO --> startP["BOILER ON LOAD"]
```

> This **state-transition diagram** is the canonical KTU visual aid for an interlock explainer. Every transition is gated by a *permissive* that must read TRUE.

---

## 4.4 Trip Logic Architecture — 2-out-of-3 Voting

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensA["DRUM LEVEL SENSOR 1"] --> voteA
    sensB["DRUM LEVEL SENSOR 2"] --> voteA
    sensC["DRUM LEVEL SENSOR 3"] --> voteA
    voteA{"2 OUT OF 3 VOTING LOGIC"}
    voteA -->|TWO OR MORE LOW| outA["INITIATE LOW WATER TRIP"]
    voteA -->|LESS THAN TWO| outB["NO TRIP ALLOW OPERATION"]
```

> **Note for KTU students:** The **2oo3 voting** architecture is the **IEC 61508 SIL-2** standard, providing tolerance against any *single* sensor failure without compromising safety. This is a frequent 14-mark sub-part.

---

## 4.5 Component-Level Block Schematic of an Interlock Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA["BOILER PROCESS VARIABLE"] --> instA["PRIMARY SENSOR"]
    instA --> instB["TRANSMITTER 4 TO 20 MA"]
    instB --> instC["SAFETY PLC INPUT MODULE"]
    instC --> logicB{"PERMISSIVE AND TRIP LOGIC IEC 61131 3"}
    logicB -->|TRIP TRUE| instD["OUTPUT MODULE RELAY"]
    instD --> instE["MASTER FUEL TRIP RELAY"]
    instE --> actA["FUEL SOLENOID VALVES CLOSE"]
    logicB -->|NORMAL| instF["DCS DISPLAY AND CONTROL"]
    instF --> opA["OPERATOR HUMAN MACHINE INTERFACE"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a boiler interlock. List any four common interlocks implemented in a modern utility boiler.**

**Model Answer:**
A boiler interlock is a logically structured safety system that automatically initiates protective action when a process variable deviates beyond its safe operating envelope, enforcing a defined permissive sequence for start-up, operation, and shut-down.

Four common interlocks are:
1. **Drum-level low / high interlock** — protects against tube uncovering and water carryover.
2. **Furnace-pressure high interlock** — prevents furnace explosion.
3. **Flame-failure interlock** — initiates MFT when flame is lost.
4. **Purge-complete interlock** — permits ignition only after a 5-minute air purge.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is Master Fuel Trip (MFT)? State the standard response time mandated by IBR.**

**Model Answer:**
The Master Fuel Trip is the highest-priority boiler safety interlock that simultaneously commands **closure of all fuel valves**, **tripping of FD and ID fans**, and **opening of the generator breaker** upon assertion of any critical trip condition. As per **Indian Boiler Regulations (IBR-1950)** and modern BMS standards, the **standard MFT response time is ≤ 3 seconds**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` — 14 Marks

**a)** Explain the architecture and operating philosophy of a Burner Management System (BMS). Describe how the Master Fuel Trip (MFT) is implemented and the role of first-out annunciation. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **furnace-purge interlock** including the logic for purge initiation, the requirement of positive furnace pressure, and the standard purge-time calculation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### (a) Burner Management System (BMS) Architecture — 7 Marks

The BMS is a **dedicated, SIL-rated safety sub-system** of the DCS that exclusively handles burner start-up, monitoring, and shut-down logic. Its architecture has four functional layers:

| Layer | Function | Hardware |
|:------|:---------|:---------|
| 1. Sensing | Flame, pressure, temperature, flow | UV/IR scanners, RTDs, PTs, flow switches |
| 2. Logic Solver | Permissive evaluation, voting | Safety PLC (IEC 61508 SIL-2/3) |
| 3. Actuation | Fuel valves, dampers, contactors | Solenoid valves, relays, actuators |
| 4. Annunciation | Operator feedback | HMI screens, beacons, hooters |

**Operating philosophy:**
- The BMS enforces **fail-safe** behaviour: loss of any signal is treated as a **trip** rather than a permissive.
- Every burner has its **own individual safety chain** (start-permissive, flame-stability check, trip logic).
- The **MFT** is a global trip that aggregates **all** sub-trip signals through a **2oo3 voting** logic at the boiler level.
- **First-out annunciation** captures the *chronologically first* trip cause so that root-cause analysis can be performed rapidly.

The MFT relay is **normally energised** (fail-safe). On any trip, the relay **de-energises**, dropping out all downstream fuel valves.

---

#### (b) Furnace-Purge Interlock — 7 Marks

The **purge interlock** ensures that the furnace contains no residual fuel-air mixture before ignition. Its sequence is:

1. **Precondition:** FD fan must be running, and all fuel valves must be proven **closed** (limit-switch feedback).
2. **Purge initiation:** Operator presses "Start Purge" on the HMI.
3. **Air-flow verification:** Differential pressure across the air preheater must read $\Delta P \geq 5\,\text{mm WC}$.
4. **Time-pressure integral:** Furnace pressure must remain $\geq 30\,\text{mm WC}$ for a continuous duration of **5 minutes**.
5. **Permissive issued:** Only after all conditions hold, the BMS issues the "Purge Complete" flag, which then **permits** the first oil gun to be lit.

**Purge-time calculation:**

$$
t_{purge} = \frac{N \cdot V_{furnace}}{Q_{FD\,fan}}
$$

with $N = 5$ air-changes. For the numerical example derived in Section 3.2, $t_{purge} \geq 200\,\text{s}$. However, **the regulatory minimum of 5 minutes is binding**, so the enforced purge time is $t_{enforced} = \max(t_{calculated},\; 300\,\text{s})$.

A neat diagram of the purge sequence is provided in **Section 4.3** of these notes.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` — 14 Marks

**a)** Discuss in detail the **drum-level interlock** for a 210 MW utility boiler. Derive the differential-pressure set-points for low-level and high-level trips given the relevant drum geometry. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Explain the **flame-failure interlock**. With reference to IEC 61508, describe how a 2-out-of-3 sensor voting logic provides SIL-2 protection. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### (a) Drum-Level Interlock — 7 Marks

The **drum-level interlock** protects the boiler tubes from overheating (low-level) and the superheater from water carryover (high-level). The derivation follows the procedure shown in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

Given: $\rho_w = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_s = 1.0\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $h_{ref} = 500\,\text{mm}$, $h_{high} = +75\,\text{mm}$, $h_{low} = -100\,\text{mm}$.

**Reference DP:**

$$
\Delta P_{ref} = (\rho_w - \rho_s)\, g\, h_{ref} = (999)(9.81)(0.500) = 4899.55\,\text{Pa} \approx 50.0\,\text{mm WC}
$$

**High-level DP:**

$$
\Delta P_{high} = \Delta P_{ref} + (999)(9.81)(0.075) = 5634.10\,\text{Pa} \approx 57.44\,\text{mm WC}
$$

**Low-level DP:**

$$
\Delta P_{low} = \Delta P_{ref} - (999)(9.81)(0.100) = 3919.33\,\text{Pa} \approx 39.95\,\text{mm WC}
$$

Therefore the trip window is $39.95 \leq \Delta P_{measured} \leq 57.44\,\text{mm WC}$.

The interlock is implemented with **three independent drum-level transmitters** arranged in a **2oo3 voting** architecture, with the trip output fed to the MFT relay.

---

#### (b) Flame-Failure Interlock and SIL-2 Voting — 7 Marks

The flame-failure interlock uses **flame scanners** (UV, IR, or visible-light photodiodes) to detect combustion. The scanner output is a **4–20 mA** signal proportional to flame intensity.

**Logic:**
- For each burner, a healthy flame is defined as $F_{flame} \geq 1.5$ (signal-to-noise ratio above 1.5) sustained for at least **5 seconds**.
- If $F_{flame} < 1.0$ for **1 second**, the burner is declared failed and **tripped individually**.
- If two or more adjacent burners fail, the **boiler-level MFT** is initiated.

**SIL-2 Voting (per IEC 61508):**
Three flame scanners per burner are mounted at 120° apart. Their outputs feed a **2-out-of-3 majority voter**:

| Voter Output | Action |
|:-------------|:-------|
| 2 or 3 scanners report healthy flame | Declare flame **PRESENT** |
| 2 or 3 scanners report flame failure | Declare flame **LOST** → trip |
| 1 healthy, 2 failed | Trip (fail-safe majority) |
| Sensor disagreement (1 healthy, 1 borderline, 1 failed) | Annunciate, but follow majority |

This architecture achieves an **average Probability of Failure on Demand** between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-1}$, which is the SIL-2 range, satisfying IEC 61508 for burner-management applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in Boiler Interlock answers:**
> 1. **Confusing MFT with individual burner trip.** MFT is a *global* trip; many students erroneously list MFT as a single-burner interlock. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Omitting the fail-safe principle.** Any answer that does not mention *de-energise-to-trip* loses a guaranteed mark.
> 3. **Skipping the 5-minute purge time constant.** Numerical answers without the IBR-mandated minimum are treated as incomplete.
> 4. **Ignoring first-out annunciation.** Examiners specifically look for the term *first-out* in MFT answers.
> 5. **Mixing up 1oo2 vs 2oo2 voting.** Use **1oo2** for "either trips" and **2oo2** for "both must trip". Get this wrong and the entire logic chain becomes invalid.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Interlock** = safety logic that enforces permissive sequences and automatic trip actions.
- **Master Fuel Trip (MFT)** is the highest-priority trip; its response time is **≤ 3 seconds**.
- **Three principles**: permissiveness, fail-safe operation, first-out indication.
- **Drum-level trip window** for the worked example is **39.95 – 57.44 mm WC**.
- **Furnace purge** requires $\Delta P \geq 30\,\text{mm WC}$ for $t \geq 5\,\text{min}$ before ignition is permitted.
- **Five-volume air changes** is the engineering minimum, but **5 minutes is the regulatory minimum** — whichever is larger applies.
- **Flame-failure interlock** uses UV/IR scanners with $F_{flame} \geq 1.5$ as the healthy threshold.
- **2oo3 voting** is the SIL-2 standard for critical boiler trips per **IEC 61508**.
- **IBR-1950** mandates certified interlock chains for all boilers above the prescribed capacity.
- **Modern BMS** is implemented in **Safety PLCs** using **IEC 61131-3** languages (FBD, Ladder).
- Always remember: **fail-safe = de-energise to trip**; never design for energise-to-trip.
- Annunciation must include **first-out** identification for every trip event.
- Reset is permitted only after **all trip causes are cleared** and a minimum cool-down of **30 s** has elapsed.
- The **master relay** is normally energised; loss of power itself constitutes a trip — a deliberate fail-safe feature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cooling System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a thermal power plant, only about **30–40%** of the heat energy released by fuel combustion in the boiler is converted into useful electrical energy. The remaining **60–70%** of the energy *must* be rejected to the environment to maintain the Rankine cycle's efficiency and to condense the exhaust steam from the turbine back into water for re-use in the boiler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cooling System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A cooling system in a power plant is an integrated assembly of heat exchangers, cooling towers, water circulation pumps, piping networks, and instrumentation devices whose primary function is to **reject waste process heat** from the condenser (and auxiliary plant equipment) to a heat sink — typically the atmosphere, a river, a lake, or the sea — while maintaining precise temperature, flow, level, and chemistry parameters required for the **thermal efficiency** and **safe operation** of the power cycle.

The cooling system therefore forms the **ultimate heat sink** of the entire thermodynamic cycle. Without it, the condenser pressure would rise, turbine back-pressure would increase, and the plant's net power output would collapse.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Human Body" of a Power Plant

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the cooling system as the **sweating mechanism of a human body**.
>
> - When you exercise, your body generates excess heat. To maintain a steady internal temperature of 37 °C, sweat glands release moisture on the skin. As this moisture evaporates, it absorbs latent heat and cools you down.
> - Similarly, in a power plant, the boiler-turbine-condenser system is the "body." The **cooling tower** is the "sweat gland," and the **circulating cooling water** is the "sweat." The fan/air draft is the "moving air" that accelerates evaporation.
> - The **instruments** (RTDs, flow meters, level switches) are the body's *nervous system*, telling the control room whether cooling is adequate or if there's a "fever" (overheating).

If sweat stops, the body overheats and fails. Likewise, if the cooling system fails, the plant must **trip** (emergency shutdown) within minutes to prevent catastrophic turbine or condenser damage.

## 1.3 The Role of Cooling System in the Power Cycle

The cooling system is the **terminal component** of the Rankine cycle:

$$\text{Boiler} \rightarrow \text{Turbine} \rightarrow \text{Condenser (Cooling System)} \rightarrow \text{Pump} \rightarrow \text{Boiler}$$

The condenser is a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** where:

1. Exhaust steam from the LP (Low-Pressure) turbine enters the shell side.
2. Cool circulating water passes through thousands of tubes.
3. Steam condenses on the outer surface of these tubes, releasing **latent heat of vaporization** (~ **2260 kJ/kg** for water at 1 atm).
4. The condensate is collected and pumped back to the deaerator/boiler.
5. The heated cooling water is sent to the **cooling tower**, where it rejects heat to the atmosphere.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics in Cooling Systems

The following standard parameters are used to characterize cooling system performance in KTU 2024 assessments:

| Parameter | Typical Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Condenser Vacuum** | 700 – 740 mm Hg | Indicates effectiveness of heat removal |
| **CW Inlet Temperature** | 25 °C – 32 °C | Temperature of water entering condenser |
| **CW Outlet Temperature** | 35 °C – 42 °C | Heated water leaving condenser |
| **Condenser Back Pressure** | 0.05 – 0.10 bar (abs) | Steam pressure inside condenser |
| **Cooling Tower Range** | 8 °C – 14 °C | T_hot − T_cold_water |
| **Cooling Tower Approach** | 3 °C – 6 °C | T_cold_water − T_wet_bulb |
| **L/G Ratio** | 0.8 – 1.5 | Liquid-to-Air mass flow ratio |
| **Cycle of Concentration (COC)** | 3 – 6 | Ratio of dissolved solids |

---

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Always remember the relationship **"Range ≥ Approach"**. A well-designed cooling tower typically has **Range ≈ 1.5 × Approach** to 2 × Approach. Memorize this for the 3-mark short-answer section.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Power Plant Cooling Systems

Cooling systems in thermal and nuclear power plants are broadly classified into three families. The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3) emphasizes the instrumentation of all three.

### A. Once-Through (Open-Loop) Cooling System

- Cooling water is drawn from a large natural source (river, lake, sea).
- The water passes **once** through the condenser and is discharged back to the source.
- Requires an enormous volume of water (e.g., a 500 MW plant needs ≈ **60,000 m³/hr**).
- **Advantage:** Simple design, no cooling tower required.
- **Disadvantage:** Strict environmental regulations on thermal discharge; causes thermal pollution; not feasible in arid regions.

### B. Closed-Loop (Recirculating) Wet Cooling System

- Cooling water is recirculated between the **condenser** and the **cooling tower**.
- Heat is rejected to the atmosphere primarily by **evaporation** in the cooling tower.
- Requires only **2–5%** of the make-up water compared to once-through systems.
- **Most widely used** in modern power plants.

### C. Dry (Air-Cooled) Cooling System

- Uses **finned tube heat exchangers (Air-Cooled Condensers — ACC)** and large axial fans.
- No water consumption; ideal for water-scarce regions.
- **Disadvantage:** Higher turbine back-pressure → lower plant efficiency.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a Mechanical Draft Cooling Tower

The **induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower** is the most common type in modern KTU-referenced power plants.

### Major Subsystems (Instrumentation Perspective)

1. **Water Distribution System:** Spray nozzles, splash bars, fill (packing) — supports maximum air-water contact area.
2. **Air Movement System:** Axial fans (induced draft) at the tower top, with dampers and vibration sensors.
3. **Heat Transfer Media:** PVC film fill or splash fill.
4. **Drift Eliminators:** Capture entrained water droplets (typical drift loss < **0.005%** of circulating flow).
5. **Collection Basin (Cold Well):** Collects cooled water at the bottom; houses the level transmitter and the CW pump suction.
6. **Make-Up Water System:** Replaces evaporative loss; controlled by level switches in the basin.
7. **Blowdown System:** Bleeds out concentrated water to control **Cycle of Concentration**.

## 2.3 The Three Fundamental Performance Parameters

### (i) Cooling Tower Range (ΔT)

The **range** is the temperature difference between the water entering the tower (hot) and the water leaving the tower (cold).

$$\text{Range} = T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,out}$$

- A larger range ⇒ greater heat rejection ⇒ smaller water flow rate.
- Typical range for a power plant cooling tower: **8 °C to 14 °C**.

### (ii) Cooling Tower Approach

The **approach** is the difference between the cold water leaving temperature and the **ambient wet-bulb temperature**.

$$\text{Approach} = T_{cold,out} - T_{wb}$$

- A *smaller* approach ⇒ closer to thermodynamic ideal ⇒ more efficient (and expensive) tower.
- The approach can never be **negative** (you cannot cool water below the wet-bulb temperature using only evaporative cooling).
- Typical approach: **3 °C to 6 °C**.

### (iii) Effectiveness (ε)

$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,out}}{T_{hot,in} - T_{wb}} = \frac{\text{Range}}{\text{Range} + \text{Approach}}$$

- A well-designed tower has ε ≈ **0.65 – 0.85**.

## 2.4 Heat and Mass Transfer Model (Merkel Equation Foundation)

The fundamental energy balance of a cooling tower (per unit time) is:

$$Q = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, (T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,out}) = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T_{range}$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_w$ = mass flow rate of circulating water (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = specific heat of water = **4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- $Q$ = heat rejected (kW)

The evaporation loss per unit time is:

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{\dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T_{range}}{h_{fg}}$$

Where $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization of water ≈ **2450 kJ/kg** at typical cooling tower conditions.

**Evaporation loss (in % of circulating flow):**

$$\text{Evaporation \%} \approx 0.00085 \times \Delta T_{range} \times 100$$

For $\Delta T = 10$ °C ⇒ Evaporation ≈ **0.85%** of circulation rate.

## 2.5 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master cheat sheet** for cooling system problems. Memorize these equations and their units.

| # | Formula | Description | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Total heat rejected (kW) | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{evap} = Q / h_{fg}$ | Evaporation rate (kg/s) | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $\text{Evap \%} = 0.00085 \cdot \Delta T$ | % of CW flow lost as evaporation | ★★★★ |
| 4 | $\text{Range} = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$ | Tower range (°C) | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | $\text{Approach} = T_{cold} - T_{wb}$ | Tower approach (°C) | ★★★★★ |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \text{Range}/(\text{Range}+\text{Approach})$ | Tower effectiveness | ★★★★ |
| 7 | $L/G = \dot{m}_w / \dot{m}_{air}$ | Liquid-to-Air mass ratio | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $\text{COC} = C_{cw} / C_{mu}$ | Cycle of Concentration | ★★★ |
| 9 | $\dot{m}_{bd} = \dot{m}_{evap} / (\text{COC}-1)$ | Blowdown rate | ★★★ |
| 10 | $T_{cond} = T_{cw,out} + \text{TTD}$ | Condenser saturation temp | ★★★★ |

Where **TTD (Terminal Temperature Difference)** is typically **3 °C to 5 °C** in a clean condenser.

## 2.6 Instrumentation of Cooling Systems

The KTU 2024 syllabus places strong emphasis on the **instrumentation** of the cooling system loop. The following instruments are mandatory in a modern power plant:

| Measurement | Primary Sensor | Typical Location | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Condenser CW Inlet Temp** | RTD (Pt-100) | CW pump discharge header | 4–20 mA |
| **Condenser CW Outlet Temp** | RTD (Pt-100) | Condenser outlet header | 4–20 mA |
| **CW Flow** | Orifice + DPT or Magnetic Flowmeter | CW common return line | 4–20 mA |
| **Condenser Hotwell Level** | Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT) | Hotwell | 4–20 mA |
| **Cooling Tower Basin Level** | Ultrasonic / DPT | Cold well basin | 4–20 mA + Switches |
| **Fan Vibration** | Accelerometer (piezoelectric) | Fan motor bearings | 4–20 mA / mV/g |
| **Fan Motor Current** | CT + Transducer | Motor starter | 4–20 mA |
| **Make-up Water Flow** | Magnetic flowmeter | Make-up line | 4–20 mA |
| **CW Conductivity** | Conductivity cell | CW return line | 4–20 mA |
| **Blowdown Valve Position** | LVDT / Potentiometer | Blowdown control valve | 4–20 mA |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Magnetic Flowmeters for CW?** Cooling water is conductive but contains no hydrocarbons; magnetic flowmeters give **no pressure drop** and are unaffected by suspended solids or scaling — ideal for this application.

## 2.7 Control Loops in a Cooling System

### Loop 1: Cold Water Temperature Control
- **Controlled Variable:** CW outlet temperature from tower.
- **Manipulated Variable:** Fan blade pitch angle or fan speed (VFD).
- **Sensor:** RTD in cold well.
- **Controller:** PID.

### Loop 2: Basin Level Control (Make-Up Water)
- **Controlled Variable:** Basin water level.
- **Manipulated Variable:** Make-up water control valve.
- **Strategy:** Level transmitter modulates the valve. Low-low level switch initiates **plant alarm**; high-high level switch opens the **overflow weir**.

### Loop 3: Blowdown Conductivity Control
- **Controlled Variable:** Conductivity in the CW return line.
- **Manipulated Variable:** Blowdown control valve.
- **Set Point:** Corresponds to a Cycle of Concentration of typically 4–5.

### Loop 4: Condenser Hotwell Level Control
- **Controlled Variable:** Condensate level in hotwell.
- **Manipulated Variable:** Condensate extraction pump (CEP) speed or valve.
- **Criticality:** Low level → air ingress into CEP → cavitation; high level → flooding of condenser.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Evaporation, Make-Up, and Blowdown Water Balance

Consider a **steady-state** cooling tower system. Let:

- $\dot{m}_w$ = circulating water flow rate (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_{evap}$ = water lost by evaporation (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_{bd}$ = blowdown water flow (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_{mu}$ = make-up water flow rate (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_{drift}$ = drift (windage) loss — typically negligible for our analysis

**Step 1: Mass Balance on Dissolved Solids (e.g., Total Dissolved Solids, TDS)**

At steady state, the rate of TDS entering the system equals the rate leaving via blowdown (make-up brings fresh TDS, drift is negligible, evaporation leaves solids behind).

$$C_{mu} \cdot \dot{m}_{mu} = C_{cw} \cdot \dot{m}_{bd}$$

Where:
- $C_{mu}$ = TDS concentration of make-up water (ppm)
- $C_{cw}$ = TDS concentration in circulating water (ppm)

**Step 2: Definition of Cycle of Concentration (COC)**

The COC is the ratio of TDS in circulating water to TDS in make-up water:

$$\text{COC} = \frac{C_{cw}}{C_{mu}}$$

**Step 3: Substitution**

Rearranging Step 1:

$$\frac{\dot{m}_{bd}}{\dot{m}_{mu}} = \frac{C_{mu}}{C_{cw}} = \frac{1}{\text{COC}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{bd} = \frac{\dot{m}_{mu}}{\text{COC}}$$

**Step 4: Overall Mass Balance (water)**

At steady state, the water entering the system = water leaving:

$$\dot{m}_{mu} = \dot{m}_{evap} + \dot{m}_{bd}$$

**Step 5: Solve for Blowdown**

Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$\dot{m}_{mu} = \dot{m}_{evap} + \frac{\dot{m}_{mu}}{\text{COC}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{mu} \left(1 - \frac{1}{\text{COC}}\right) = \dot{m}_{evap}$$

$$\dot{m}_{mu} \cdot \frac{\text{COC} - 1}{\text{COC}} = \dot{m}_{evap}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{mu} = \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot \frac{\text{COC}}{\text{COC} - 1}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{bd} = \frac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{\text{COC} - 1}}$$

These two boxed equations are **the most heavily tested KTU formula** for cooling water chemistry questions.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem

**Problem Statement:**

A 210 MW thermal power plant uses a closed-loop cooling system with the following data:

- CW flow rate: $\dot{m}_w = 12{,}000$ m³/hr
- CW inlet temperature to tower (hot): 42 °C
- CW outlet temperature from tower (cold): 30 °C
- Ambient wet-bulb temperature: 26 °C
- Latent heat of vaporization: $h_{fg} = 2400$ kJ/kg
- Specific heat of water: $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/kg·K
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- Make-up water TDS: $C_{mu} = 250$ ppm
- Cycle of Concentration: 4

**Find:**
1. Total heat rejected in the tower
2. Effectiveness of the tower
3. Evaporation loss (kg/hr and % of CW flow)
4. Make-up water required (m³/hr)
5. Blowdown required (m³/hr)

---

**Solution:**

**Given Data Conversion:**

$$\dot{m}_w = 12{,}000 \, \frac{m^3}{hr} \times 1000 \, \frac{kg}{m^3} = 1.2 \times 10^7 \, kg/hr$$

Convert to kg/s (divide by 3600):

$$\dot{m}_w = \frac{1.2 \times 10^7}{3600} \approx 3333.33 \, kg/s$$

**Part 1: Heat Rejected (Q)**

$$Q = \dot{m}_w \, c_p \, \Delta T_{range}$$

$$\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold} = 42 - 30 = 12 \, °C$$

$$Q = 3333.33 \times 4.186 \times 12$$

$$Q = 3333.33 \times 50.232$$

$$Q \approx 167{,}440 \, kW \approx 167.44 \, MW$$

**Part 2: Effectiveness (ε)**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Range}}{\text{Range} + \text{Approach}}$$

$$\text{Approach} = T_{cold} - T_{wb} = 30 - 26 = 4 \, °C$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{12}{12 + 4} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75$$

So the cooling tower is **75% effective** — typical of a well-designed induced-draft tower.

**Part 3: Evaporation Loss**

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{167{,}440}{2400} \approx 69.77 \, kg/s$$

Convert to kg/hr:

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = 69.77 \times 3600 \approx 251{,}160 \, kg/hr$$

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = 251.16 \, m^3/hr$$

As a percentage of CW flow:

$$\text{Evap \%} = \frac{251.16}{12{,}000} \times 100 \approx 2.09\%$$

**Cross-check using shortcut formula:**

$$\text{Evap \%} = 0.00085 \times \Delta T = 0.00085 \times 12 = 0.0102 = 1.02\%$$

> The two methods give slightly different results because the shortcut formula uses $h_{fg}$ at 30 °C and assumes 1.02% directly. The exact calculation gives 2.09% because we used the actual $Q$ derived from the flow rate, which is the **exact thermodynamic** approach. The shortcut is a quick estimator.

**Part 4: Make-Up Water**

$$\dot{m}_{mu} = \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot \frac{\text{COC}}{\text{COC} - 1}$$

$$\dot{m}_{mu} = 251.16 \times \frac{4}{4 - 1} = 251.16 \times \frac{4}{3}$$

$$\dot{m}_{mu} \approx 334.88 \, m^3/hr$$

**Part 5: Blowdown**

$$\dot{m}_{bd} = \frac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{\text{COC} - 1} = \frac{251.16}{3} \approx 83.72 \, m^3/hr$$

**Verification (mass balance):**

$$\dot{m}_{evap} + \dot{m}_{bd} = 251.16 + 83.72 = 334.88 \, m^3/hr = \dot{m}_{mu} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Heat rejected, Q | **167.44 MW** |
| Effectiveness, ε | **0.75 (75%)** |
| Evaporation | **251.16 m³/hr** |
| Make-up water | **334.88 m³/hr** |
| Blowdown | **83.72 m³/hr** |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Cooling Tower Types

| Parameter | Natural Draft | Forced Draft | Induced Draft Counter-flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air movement | Density difference | Forced by fan at bottom | Fan at top |
| Tower height | Very tall (~150 m) | Short | Medium |
| Approach (°C) | 3 – 5 | 5 – 7 | 3 – 5 |
| Power consumption | Very low | Moderate | High (large fans) |
| Capital cost | Highest | Lowest | Moderate |
| Drift loss | 0.005% | 0.02% | 0.005% |
| Typical application | Large nuclear/thermal | Small industrial | Most modern thermal |
| KTU exam frequency | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |

## 3.4 Derivation: Merkel Equation (NTU Method) — Conceptual Outline

The rigorous design of a cooling tower uses the **Merkel equation**, which combines heat and mass transfer:

$$\frac{dh}{dT_w} = \frac{L \cdot c_p}{G} \cdot \frac{(h_{w} - h)}{(h_s - h_w)}$$

Where:
- $h$ = enthalpy of air (kJ/kg)
- $h_w$ = enthalpy of air-water mixture at water temperature
- $h_s$ = saturated air enthalpy at water temperature
- $L$ = water mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $G$ = air mass flow rate (kg/s)

Integrating from cold water exit to hot water inlet gives the **Merkel Integral (M)**:

$$\int_{T_{cw}}^{T_{hw}} \frac{c_p \, dT_w}{h_s - h_w} = \frac{L}{G}$$

The **NTU (Number of Transfer Units)** is:

$$\text{NTU} = \int_{T_{cw}}^{T_{hw}} \frac{c_p \, dT_w}{h_s - h_w} \approx \frac{c_p \, \Delta T_{range}}{h_s - h_w}$$

This integral is solved numerically (Simpson's rule) using **psychrometric chart** data for saturated air enthalpy at each incremental water temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Simplification:** For Module 3 problems, the Merkel integral is *not* required numerically. You are expected to know its **physical meaning** (combined heat + mass transfer model) and the **L/G ratio** that results from it.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Recirculating Cooling System — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Boiler\nSteam Generator] -->|Superheated Steam| B[HP Turbine\nHigh Pressure]
    B --> C[IP Turbine\nIntermediate Pressure]
    C --> D[LP Turbine\nLow Pressure]
    D -->|Exhaust Steam| E[Condenser\nShell and Tube HX]
    E -->|Condensate| F[Condensate Extraction Pump CEP]
    F --> G[Deaerator]
    G -->|Feedwater| A
    
    E <-->|Cooling Water| H[Cooling Tower\nInduced Draft]
    H -->|Cold Water| I[Cooling Water Pump CWP]
    I -->|High Pressure CW| E
    
    H -->|Make-up Water| J[Make-up Water System]
    H -->|Blowdown| K[Blowdown Treatment Plant]
    
    L[Atmospheric Air Intake] -->|Forced Inlet| H
    H -->|Warm Moist Air| M[Atmospheric Discharge via Drift Eliminators]
    
    N1[RTD Pt-100 - CW Inlet] -.-> E
    N2[RTD Pt-100 - CW Outlet] -.-> E
    N3[Magnetic Flowmeter - CW Flow] -.-> I
    N4[Level Transmitter - Hotwell] -.-> F
    N5[Level Transmitter - Basin] -.-> H
    N6[Conductivity Sensor] -.-> K
    N7[Accelerometer Fan Vibration] -.-> H
    N8[DCS PLC PID Controller] -.-> N1
    N8 -.-> N2
    N8 -.-> N3
    N8 -.-> N4
    N8 -.-> N5
    N8 -.-> N6
    N8 -.-> N7
```

## 4.2 Induced-Draft Counter-Flow Cooling Tower — Cross-Sectional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TD_TOP[Top of Tower]
        FAN[Axial Fan with Gear Reducer]
        DRIFT[Drift Eliminators]
        MOTOR[3-Phase AC Motor with VFD]
    end
    
    subgraph TD_MID[Middle Section - Fill Zone]
        SPRAY[Hot Water Distribution Pipe and Spray Nozzles]
        FILL[PVC Film Fill Packing]
        SPLASH[Splash Bars]
    end
    
    subgraph TD_BOT[Bottom Section - Basin]
        BASIN[Collection Basin Cold Well]
        LVLTX[Level Transmitter Ultrasonic]
        SUMP[CW Pump Sump Strainer]
        BDV[Blowdown Control Valve with LVDT]
        MUV[Make-up Water Control Valve]
    end
    
    HOT[Hot Water from Condenser 42 C] --> SPRAY
    SPRAY --> FILL
    FILL --> SPLASH
    SPLASH --> BASIN
    BASIN --> SUMP
    SUMP --> PUMP[CW Pump to Condenser]
    
    AIR[Ambient Air Inlet Louver Side] -->|Induced Flow| FILL
    FILL -->|Evaporative Cooling| DRIFT
    DRIFT --> FAN
    FAN -->|Warm Moist Air| EXH[Atmospheric Exhaust]
    
    MOTOR -.->|Drives| FAN
    LVLTX -.->|Signal to DCS| MUV
    BDV -.->|Modulates on Conductivity| BASIN
```

## 4.3 Cooling Water Chemistry — Cycle of Concentration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MU[Make-up Water\nC_mu = 250 ppm] -->|Sumps| SYS
    SYS[Cooling Tower + CW Loop\nC_cw = 1000 ppm\nCOC = 4]
    SYS -->|Evaporation Loss\n2.09 percent\nNo Solids Exit| ATM[Atmosphere]
    SYS -->|Blowdown Loss\n0.7 percent\nSolids Exit| BD[Blowdown Tank]
    SYS -->|Drift Loss\n0.005 percent| ATM
    BD -->|Treated and Discharged| ENV[Environment]
    
    CTRL[Conductivity Sensor in CW Return] --> CTRL2[Blowdown Controller]
    CTRL2 -->|Modulates| BD
```

## 4.4 Control Loop Hierarchy for Cooling System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 0 - Field Sensors\nRTD, DPT, Magmeter, Accelerometer]
    L2[Level 1 - Control Room DCS PLC PID]
    L3[Level 2 - Supervisory SCADA HMI]
    L4[Level 3 - Plant Management MIS]
    
    L1 -->|4-20 mA HART| L2
    L2 -->|Ethernet OPC| L3
    L3 -->|Database Reports| L4
    
    L2 --> C1[Loop 1 Fan Speed for CW Temp]
    L2 --> C2[Loop 2 Make-up Valve for Basin Level]
    L2 --> C3[Loop 3 Blowdown Valve for Conductivity]
    L2 --> C4[Loop 4 CEP Speed for Hotwell Level]
```

## 4.5 Heat Exchanger — Condenser Cross-Section Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHELL[Shell Side - Steam]
    S1[Exhaust Steam Entry from LP Turbine] --> S2[Steam Distribution Zone]
    S2 --> S3[Tube Bundle Region - Latent Heat Release]
    S3 --> S4[Condensate Collection at Bottom]
    S4 --> S5[Condensate to CEP]
    end
    
    subgraph TUBE[Tube Side - Cooling Water]
    T1[Hot CW Outlet to Tower] --> T2[Upper Water Box]
    T2 --> T3[Thousands of Titanium / SS Tubes]
    T3 --> T4[Lower Water Box]
    T4 --> T5[Cold CW Inlet from Tower]
    end
    
    S3 -.->|Heat Transfer through Tube Wall| T3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the terms "Range" and "Approach" as applied to a cooling tower. Why is approach always greater than zero for a wet cooling tower?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Range** is defined as the difference between the temperature of hot water entering the cooling tower and the cold water leaving the cooling tower:
> $$\text{Range} = T_{hot,in} - T_{cold,out}$$
>
> **Approach** is defined as the difference between the cold water leaving temperature and the **ambient wet-bulb temperature** of the surrounding air:
> $$\text{Approach} = T_{cold,out} - T_{wb}$$
>
> The approach is always greater than zero (i.e., $T_{cold,out} > T_{wb}$) because in a wet cooling tower, heat rejection occurs primarily by **evaporation** of water, and the lowest temperature to which water can be cooled by evaporation is the **wet-bulb temperature** of the air. By the second law of thermodynamics, the water cannot be cooled below the air's wet-bulb temperature, so the approach is always strictly positive. **(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "Cycle of Concentration" (COC) in a cooling water system? State its typical operating range.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Cycle of Concentration (COC)** is defined as the ratio of the concentration of dissolved solids (TDS or chloride) in the **circulating cooling water** to the concentration of the same parameter in the **make-up water**:
> $$\text{COC} = \frac{C_{cw}}{C_{mu}}$$
>
> The COC quantifies how many times the make-up water has been concentrated through evaporation before being bled out as blowdown. A higher COC indicates less make-up and blowdown water consumption but increases the risk of scaling and corrosion.
>
> **Typical operating range:** COC = **3 to 6** for most power plant cooling water systems. **(3 marks: 1.5 + 1 + 0.5)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **closed-loop recirculating cooling water system** of a thermal power plant, showing the condenser, cooling tower, CW pump, make-up water and blowdown lines. Label the major instrumentation points. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 500 MW thermal power plant uses a cooling tower with the following data:
- CW flow rate: 18,000 m³/hr
- CW inlet (hot) temperature: 44 °C
- CW outlet (cold) temperature: 31 °C
- Ambient wet-bulb temperature: 27 °C
- Specific heat of water: 4.186 kJ/kg·K
- Latent heat of vaporization: 2400 kJ/kg

Calculate:
1. Range, approach, and effectiveness of the tower
2. Total heat rejected in MW
3. Evaporation loss in m³/hr
4. If COC = 5, find make-up water and blowdown in m³/hr **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Explanation (7 marks)**

The closed-loop cooling system consists of:

1. **Condenser (Shell and Tube HX):** Receives exhaust steam from the LP turbine. Steam condenses on the outside of the tubes, releasing latent heat to the cooling water flowing inside the tubes.
2. **Cooling Water Pump (CWP):** Circulates CW from the cooling tower to the condenser. Usually 2 × 100% or 3 × 50% pumps with one as standby.
3. **Cooling Tower (Induced Draft):** Rejects heat from the hot water to the atmosphere by evaporation and convection.
4. **Make-up Water System:** Compensates for evaporation, drift, and blowdown losses; typically drawn from a clarifier.
5. **Blowdown System:** Maintains COC by bleeding out concentrated water.

**Instrumentation Labels (must be drawn in the diagram):**
- RTD at CW inlet to condenser (T₁)
- RTD at CW outlet from condenser (T₂)
- Magnetic flowmeter on CW return line (F)
- DPT in condenser hotwell (L₁)
- Ultrasonic level transmitter in cooling tower basin (L₂)
- Conductivity sensor in CW return (C)
- Vibration sensor on cooling tower fan (V)
- Make-up and blowdown control valves (MV₁, MV₂)

**Award breakdown:** Diagram: 3 marks, Explanation: 2 marks, Instrumentation: 2 marks.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Range, Approach, Effectiveness (2 marks)**

$$\text{Range} = T_{hot} - T_{cold} = 44 - 31 = 13 \, °C$$

$$\text{Approach} = T_{cold} - T_{wb} = 31 - 27 = 4 \, °C$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Range}}{\text{Range} + \text{Approach}} = \frac{13}{13 + 4} = \frac{13}{17} \approx 0.765$$

**Step 2: Heat Rejected (2 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_w = 18{,}000 \times 1000 = 1.8 \times 10^7 \, kg/hr = 5000 \, kg/s$$

$$Q = 5000 \times 4.186 \times 13 = 5000 \times 54.418 = 272{,}090 \, kW$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 272.09 \, MW}$$

**Step 3: Evaporation Loss (1.5 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}} = \frac{272{,}090}{2400} = 113.37 \, kg/s$$

Convert to m³/hr: $\dot{m}_{evap} = 113.37 \times 3.6 = 408.13 \, m^3/hr$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{evap} \approx 408.13 \, m^3/hr}$$

**Step 4: Make-up and Blowdown (1.5 marks)**

$$\dot{m}_{mu} = \dot{m}_{evap} \times \frac{COC}{COC - 1} = 408.13 \times \frac{5}{4} = 510.16 \, m^3/hr$$

$$\dot{m}_{bd} = \frac{\dot{m}_{evap}}{COC - 1} = \frac{408.13}{4} = 102.04 \, m^3/hr$$

**Verification:** $408.13 + 102.04 = 510.17 \approx 510.16$ ✓

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare the **once-through**, **closed-loop wet**, and **dry cooling** systems used in thermal power plants. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each, and identify the type most commonly used in modern thermal plants. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe with a sketch the construction and working of an **induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower**. Explain the role of **(i) drift eliminators, (ii) fill, (iii) fans, and (iv) blowdown system.** **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 marks)**

| System | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| **Once-Through** | Simple design, no cooling tower | Massive water consumption; thermal pollution of water body |
| **Closed-Loop Wet** | Low water consumption (~3% of once-through); proven technology | Higher capital cost; plume visible; requires water treatment |
| **Dry (ACC)** | Zero water consumption; ideal for arid regions | Higher turbine back-pressure; 5–8% lower plant efficiency; very large footprint |

**Most commonly used in modern thermal power plants:** The **closed-loop wet recirculating system with induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower** is the industry standard. **(Award 7 marks: 3 for table + 2 for explanation + 2 for conclusion)**

---

**(b) Induced-Draft Counter-Flow Cooling Tower (7 marks)**

**Construction (Sketch Required):**
- A large rectangular or hyperbolic structure made of RCC (Reinforced Cement Concrete) with FRP (Fiber Reinforced Plastic) lining.
- Bottom: **Collection basin (cold well)** with sump strainer.
- Middle: **Spray nozzles + fill packing (PVC film)**.
- Top: **Axial fan + drift eliminators** housed in a fan deck.

**Working:**
1. Hot water from the condenser is pumped to the top of the tower and distributed via **spray nozzles** over the fill.
2. Water falls as a thin film through the fill, while ambient air is drawn **upward** (counter-flow) by the induced-draft fan.
3. Direct contact between air and water causes **evaporation** of a small fraction of water, which absorbs latent heat and cools the bulk water.
4. Cooled water collects in the basin and is recirculated by the CW pump.

**Roles (4 marks total — 1 mark each):**

- **(i) Drift Eliminators:** These are zig-zag shaped PVC or polypropylene plates at the top of the fill. They capture entrained water droplets from the rising moist air, reducing **drift loss** to <0.005% of circulation rate. They prevent water wastage and environmental salt drift in coastal plants.

- **(ii) Fill (Packing):** The fill is the primary heat-and-mass-transfer surface. It breaks the falling water into thin films or small droplets to maximize the **air-water interfacial area**. Film fill (sheets of corrugated PVC) is preferred for high-capacity towers; splash fill (wood or PVC bars) for lower-capacity industrial towers.

- **(iii) Fans:** Large axial-flow fans driven by 3-phase induction motors via right-angle gear reducers. They create the **induced draft** that pulls air upward through the tower. Modern fans use **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** control to modulate air flow and maintain cold water temperature setpoint.

- **(iv) Blowdown System:** A continuous or intermittent water bleed controlled by a **conductivity sensor + PID + control valve**. It removes concentrated cooling water (high TDS) to prevent scaling, fouling, and microbiological growth inside the condenser tubes. The blowdown rate is calculated as $\dot{m}_{bd} = \dot{m}_{evap} / (COC - 1)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Range and Approach:** Many students reverse these. Remember — *Range = how much water cools; Approach = how close to ideal*.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert m³/hr to kg/s** before applying $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$. Always check units first.
> 3. **Confusing COC with cycles per hour:** COC is a *ratio of concentrations*, not a frequency.
> 4. **Neglecting the difference between wet-bulb and dry-bulb temperature:** The approach uses the **wet-bulb temperature**, not the dry-bulb.
> 5. **In block diagrams, forgetting to show the control valves** on make-up and blowdown lines — this is a 1-mark loss many students suffer.
> 6. **Not stating units explicitly in numerical answers** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam!)**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ Cooling system = the heat sink of the Rankine cycle; rejects 60–70% of fuel energy.
- ☐ Three families: Once-Through, Closed-Loop Wet, Dry (ACC).
- ☐ Most common in power plants: **Induced-Draft Counter-Flow Wet Cooling Tower**.

### Performance Triad
- ☐ **Range = T_hot − T_cold** (typically 8–14 °C)
- ☐ **Approach = T_cold − T_wb** (typically 3–6 °C, always > 0)
- ☐ **Effectiveness ε = Range / (Range + Approach)** (typically 0.65–0.85)

### Water Chemistry
- ☐ **COC = C_cw / C_mu** (typically 3–6)
- ☐ **Evaporation loss ≈ 0.00085 × ΔT** % of CW flow
- ☐ **Make-up water = Evaporation × [COC / (COC − 1)]**
- ☐ **Blowdown = Evaporation / (COC − 1)**

### Energy Equation
- ☐ **Q = ṁ_w × c_p × ΔT** (kW)
- ☐ **c_p of water = 4.186 kJ/kg·K**
- ☐ **h_fg ≈ 2400 kJ/kg** at cooling tower conditions

### Instrumentation Map
- ☐ **CW Temperature:** RTD Pt-100 at condenser inlet and outlet.
- ☐ **CW Flow:** Magnetic flowmeter (no pressure drop, no moving parts).
- ☐ **Hotwell Level:** Differential Pressure Transmitter (DPT) — critical for CEP cavitation protection.
- ☐ **Basin Level:** Ultrasonic or DPT — controls make-up water valve.
- ☐ **Conductivity:** Online conductivity cell — controls blowdown valve.
- ☐ **Fan Vibration:** Piezoelectric accelerometer — predictive maintenance.
- ☐ **Make-up Flow:** Magnetic flowmeter on make-up line.

### Control Loops
- ☐ **Loop 1:** Cold water temperature → Fan speed (VFD)
- ☐ **Loop 2:** Basin level → Make-up valve
- ☐ **Loop 3:** Conductivity → Blowdown valve
- ☐ **Loop 4:** Hotwell level → Condensate Extraction Pump (CEP)

### Condenser Link
- ☐ **T_cond = T_cw_out + TTD** (TTD = 3–5 °C in clean condenser)
- ☐ Higher CW temperature → Higher condenser pressure → Lower turbine efficiency.

### Common Numerical Traps
- ☐ Always convert m³/hr to kg/s for $Q$ calculation.
- ☐ Approach uses **wet-bulb** temperature, not dry-bulb.
- ☐ Blowdown ≠ drift; they are separate loss mechanisms.
- ☐ Cooling tower cannot cool water below wet-bulb temperature.
- ☐ Hyper-natural-draft towers are tall (≈ 150 m) but consume zero fan power.

### KTU 2024 Module-3 Vocabulary You Must Know
- ☐ Merkel Integral (NTU method for cooling tower design)
- ☐ L/G ratio (Liquid-to-Air mass flow ratio)
- ☐ Drift eliminators, fill, splash bars
- ☐ Cycle of Concentration (COC) and TDS
- ☐ Make-up, blowdown, evaporation, drift
- ☐ Psychrometric chart, wet-bulb, dry-bulb, relative humidity

> **End of Section 5 — You are now fully prepared for any KTU 2024 Module 3 Cooling System question!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controls in Boiler: Feed Water Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Controls in Boiler: Feed Water Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Feed Water Control** is a critical closed-loop regulatory control strategy employed in steam-generating boiler systems to maintain the **water level inside the steam drum** at a precisely defined set-point, while simultaneously ensuring that the **mass flow rate of feedwater entering the drum exactly matches the mass flow rate of steam leaving the drum** under steady-state operating conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the feed water control loop is classified as a **multi-variable regulatory control problem** because the drum level is influenced not by a single process variable, but by the interaction of three primary measurements: **drum level, steam flow, and feedwater flow**. Accordingly, the control architecture is implemented in three progressive levels of sophistication:

| Control Strategy | Measured Variables | Controlled Variable |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
