| **Single-Element Control** | Drum Level (h) | Feedwater Valve |
| **Two-Element Control** | Drum Level (h) + Steam Flow (Wₛ) | Feedwater Valve |
| **Three-Element Control** | Drum Level (h) + Steam Flow (Wₛ) + Feedwater Flow (W_f) | Feedwater Valve / Pump Speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The student must be able to *derive the mass-balance equation* for the boiler drum, *explain the shrink-and-swell phenomenon*, and *compare single, two, and three-element control* schemes with their respective block diagrams. Marks are typically awarded for distinguishing the response characteristics of each scheme.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Bucket" Model

Imagine you are filling a **bucket with a tap** (feedwater inlet) while simultaneously **scooping water out with a cup** (steam leaving the boiler). Your **only job** is to keep the water in the bucket at a fixed marked level (drum level setpoint).

- **The challenge:** If someone suddenly scoops faster (load increases → more steam demand), the water level falls. If they slow down, the level rises.
- **Single-element control:** You simply *watch the water level* and open/close the tap accordingly. **Problem:** When you suddenly start scooping faster, the water level momentarily *rises* (because the increased bubbling/swirling pushes water up) before it actually falls — confusing your eyes. This is the **"swell" effect**.
- **Two-element control:** You also *feel the rate at which water is being scooped out* (steam flow), and preemptively open the tap *before* the level drops.
- **Three-element control:** You additionally *measure the exact flow coming out of the tap* (feedwater flow), guaranteeing that inflow exactly equals outflow at all times. This is the **most accurate** and **industry-preferred** strategy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Metrics** (used throughout power plant control design):
> - Typical drum level setpoint: **± 50 mm** of geometric centerline.
> - Feedwater flow measurement: **Orifice plate / Venturi** with differential pressure transmitter.
> - Steam flow measurement: **Nozzle / Annular averaging Pitot tube**.
> - Controller action: **Reverse-acting PI / PID** with anti-reset windup.

## 1.3 Why Feed Water Control is Critical

Failure of feed water control leads to:

1. **Low water level** → exposing boiler tubes → tube overheating → **tube rupture** (catastrophic).
2. **High water level** → water carryover into superheater/turbine → **turbine blade erosion**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The phrase *"shrink and swell"* is a high-frequency 3-mark question. A student who simply states *"level changes"* without naming the phenomenon and explaining the cause (density change of saturated water) loses full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physical Principle — Mass Balance

The boiler drum, treated as a **control volume**, obeys the classical conservation-of-mass equation:

$$\text{Accumulation} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow}$$

For the steam drum:

- **Inflow** = Feedwater flow (W_f) + any continuous blowdown (W_b)
- **Outflow** = Steam flow (Wₛ) + any blowdown (W_b)

In **steady state**, the level remains constant:

$$W_f = W_s \quad \text{(neglecting blowdown)}$$

In **dynamic/transient** state, the level varies because the *density* of water inside the drum changes due to the formation of steam bubbles (swelling) or condensation of bubbles (shrinkage).

## 2.2 The Shrink and Swell Phenomenon — Step-by-Step Logic

### 2.2.1 What is "Swell"?
When steam demand **increases suddenly** (load step-up):
1. Steam bubbles in the drum-water mixture are **pulled out faster** than they can form.
2. The volume fraction of steam in the drum **temporarily increases** because pressure momentarily dips, flashing more water into steam.
3. The **bulk density** of the water-steam mixture **decreases**.
4. The drum level **rises** (visually) even though the actual mass of water has *decreased*.

### 2.2.2 What is "Shrink"?
When steam demand **decreases suddenly** (load step-down):
1. Steam bubbles persist in the drum.
2. The pressure rises; steam bubbles **condense** back into water.
3. The bulk density **increases**.
4. The drum level **falls** (visually) even though the actual mass of water has *increased*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Shrink and swell cause the drum level to move in the **opposite direction** of the true mass of water during the first 30–90 seconds of a load transient. This is what makes single-element control behave poorly.

## 2.3 Three Control Architectures — Structural Logic

### 2.3.1 Single-Element Control
- **Only** drum level (h) is measured.
- Controller output drives the feedwater control valve.
- **Drawback:** Cannot distinguish between level change due to true mass change and level change due to swell/shrink. Proportional action causes **false response** during transients.

### 2.3.2 Two-Element Control
- Drum level (h) + Steam flow (Wₛ).
- Steam flow is used as a **feedforward** signal: when steam demand rises, the feedwater valve is opened *before* the level actually falls.
- The level controller output acts as a **trim/correction** to the feedforward action.
- **Drawback:** Does not measure feedwater flow directly; assumes the feedwater valve acts perfectly. If the valve sticks or feedwater pressure varies, errors occur.

### 2.3.3 Three-Element Control
- Drum level (h) + Steam flow (Wₛ) + Feedwater flow (W_f).
- The **steam flow and feedwater flow** are subtracted in a flow-balancing loop (W_f - W_s → 0 in steady state).
- This **differential flow error** drives the valve directly.
- The level signal provides a **slow trim** to correct any long-term offset.
- **Advantage:** Fastest response, most accurate, used in **all large utility boilers (> 50 MW)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Relation | Symbol Description | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $W_f = W_s$ (steady state) | Mass flow balance | Setpoint tracking |
| 2 | $A \dfrac{dh}{dt} = W_f - W_s$ | Drum level dynamics (ideal, no swell) | Linearized drum model |
| 3 | $h_{measured} = h_{mass} + h_{swell}$ | Drum level decomposition | Understanding swell |
| 4 | $K_{sw} = \dfrac{\Delta h_{swell}}{\Delta P_{drum}}$ | Swell gain (m/mmHg) | Transfer function |
| 5 | $W_f (s) = \left[ K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_i s} \right) \right] E(s)$ | PI controller action | Controller tuning |
| 6 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ | Drum level process TF | First-order model |
| 7 | $G_v(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$ | Feedwater valve TF | Valve dynamics |
| 8 | $G_f(s) = \dfrac{K_f}{\tau_f s + 1}$ | Flow transmitter TF | Sensor dynamics |
| 9 | $T_{sw} \approx 30 \text{ to } 90 \text{ s}$ | Swell time constant | Empirical |
| 10 | $\text{Offset} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p K_c K_v}$ | Steady-state offset | Tuning verification |

> [!NOTE]
> All symbols: $A$ = drum cross-sectional area (m²), $h$ = drum level (m), $W$ = mass flow (kg/s), $K$ = gain, $\tau$ = time constant (s), $s$ = Laplace variable, $E(s)$ = error signal, $K_c$ = controller gain, $\tau_i$ = integral time.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Utility thermal power stations (200–800 MW):** Three-element control is **mandatory** as per ASME and IBR (Indian Boiler Regulations) codes.
- **Process industry boilers:** Two-element control is common in oil refineries and chemical plants.
- **Small package boilers (< 5 TPH):** Single-element control is acceptable due to slow dynamics.
- **Combined cycle / HRSG:** Three-element with feedforward from gas turbine load signal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Drum Mass-Balance Equation

### 3.1.1 Ideal Drum (No Swell Effect)

**Step 1 — Identify the control volume.**
The steam drum is treated as a cylindrical tank of cross-sectional area $A$ (m²). Let $h$ be the water level measured from a reference datum.

**Step 2 — Write the general mass balance.**

$$\text{Rate of accumulation of mass} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of measurable variables.**

- Inflow mass rate = $W_f$ (feedwater flow, kg/s)
- Outflow mass rate = $W_s$ (steam flow, kg/s)
- Accumulation = $A \rho \dfrac{dh}{dt}$ (assuming incompressible water, $\rho$ = constant density)

**Step 4 — Form the differential equation.**

$$A \rho \frac{dh}{dt} = W_f - W_s$$

**Step 5 — Divide by $\rho A$ to obtain level dynamics.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{1}{A \rho}(W_f - W_s) = \frac{1}{C}(W_f - W_s)$$

where $C = A \rho$ is the drum capacitance (kg/m).

**Step 6 — In steady state, $\dfrac{dh}{dt} = 0$:**

$$W_f = W_s \quad \text{(Mass Conservation Condition)}$$

### 3.1.2 Drum with Swell Effect

**Step 1 — Decompose the measured level.**

$$h_{measured}(t) = h_{mass}(t) + h_{swell}(t)$$

where $h_{mass}$ is the level due to true water mass, and $h_{swell}$ is the apparent level change due to density variation (bubble volume change).

**Step 2 — Relate swell to drum pressure deviation.**

A step change in steam demand causes a transient drum pressure change $\Delta P_{drum}(t)$. This pressure change forces bubbles to expand or collapse:

$$h_{swell}(t) = K_{sw} \cdot \Delta P_{drum}(t) \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/T_{sw}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Pressure dynamics (assuming choked steam flow).**

When steam flow demand steps up by $\Delta W_s$, the drum pressure drops:

$$\frac{dP_{drum}}{dt} = -\frac{1}{C_p}(W_s - W_f) + \text{other disturbances}$$

**Step 4 — Net drum level equation (linearized).**

$$A \frac{dh_{measured}}{dt} = W_f - W_s + A \cdot K_{sw} \frac{d(\Delta P)}{dt}$$

This is the **classical Astrom-Bell drum level model** (1966), the foundation of modern feed water control design.

## 3.2 Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function (Three-Element Control)

### 3.2.1 Block Diagram Algebra

**Step 1 — Define transfer functions.**

- Process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$
- Valve: $G_v(s) = \dfrac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$
- Flow sensor: $G_f(s) = \dfrac{K_f}{\tau_f s + 1}$
- Level sensor: $G_h(s) = K_h$
- Level controller: $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_i s} \right)$

**Step 2 — Inner flow loop (feedforward + feedback).**

The flow-balancing signal is $E_f(s) = W_s(s) - W_f(s)$ (with appropriate sign convention). The inner loop has forward path $G_v G_f$ and unity feedback.

**Step 3 — Inner closed-loop transfer function.**

$$T_{inner}(s) = \frac{G_v(s) G_f(s)}{1 + G_v(s) G_f(s)} \approx 1 \quad \text{(when loop gain is high)}$$

**Step 4 — Outer level loop.**

The outer loop is a conventional feedback of measured level $h$ against setpoint $h_{sp}$, with $T_{inner}(s)$ in the forward path:

$$\frac{h(s)}{h_{sp}(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) T_{inner}(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) T_{inner}(s) G_p(s) G_h(s)}$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state offset.**

Using the final value theorem for a unit step change in $h_{sp}$:

$$\text{Offset} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{s} - \frac{h(s)}{h_{sp}(s)} \cdot \frac{1}{s} \right]$$

Substituting PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_i s} \right)$:

$$\text{Offset} = 0 \quad \text{(integral action eliminates steady-state error)}$$

This proves that the **three-element control with PI action gives zero offset** in steady state.

## 3.3 Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A boiler drum has cross-sectional area $A = 5 \text{ m}^2$. Initially, feedwater flow $W_f = 20 \text{ kg/s}$ and steam flow $W_s = 20 \text{ kg/s}$. Suddenly, steam demand increases to $W_s = 30 \text{ kg/s}$. Assuming no controller action (open loop), calculate the rate of fall of drum level. Water density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

### 3.3.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the mass balance equation.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{W_f - W_s}{A \rho}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{20 - 30}{(5)(1000)} = \frac{-10}{5000} = -0.002 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Interpret the result.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = -2 \text{ mm/s} = -120 \text{ mm/min}$$

This means that, in the absence of control, the drum level would fall at 2 mm per second, leading to unsafe low-level conditions within minutes.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Single-Element | Two-Element | Three-Element |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of measurements | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| Response to load step | Slow, oscillates | Fast | Fastest |
| Effect of swell/shrink | Strongly affects | Partially compensated | Fully compensated |
| Sensitivity to feedwater pressure variation | Yes | Yes | No (measured) |
| Sensitivity to valve nonlinearity | Yes | Yes | No (measured) |
| Steady-state offset (with PI) | Zero | Zero | Zero |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Typical application | Small boilers < 5 TPH | Medium 5–50 TPH | Large utility boilers > 50 MW |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single-Element Feed Water Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1[Setpoint Drum Level] --> SUM1((+))
    H1[Drum Level Transmitter h] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> ERR1[Error Signal e t]
    ERR1 --> PID1[PI Controller Kc tau i]
    PID1 --> VLV1[Feedwater Control Valve]
    VLV1 --> DRM1[Steam Drum Process]
    DRM1 --> H1
    classDef ctrl fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    class PID1,VLV1,SP1,SUM1,ERR1,H1 ctrl
    class DRM1 proc
```

```mermaid
    FFSUM --> VLV2[Feedwater Valve]
```

## 4.5 Signal-Flow Graph Interpretation

In three-element control, the inner flow loop is **fast** (bandwidth 1–2 Hz), while the outer level loop is **slow** (bandwidth 0.01–0.05 Hz). This **time-scale separation** is the engineering reason why the cascade structure works robustly.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define the terms "shrink" and "swell" with reference to boiler drum level measurement. Why is this phenomenon important for feed water control design?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Shrink:** When the steam demand from the boiler suddenly **decreases**, the drum pressure momentarily **rises**, causing the steam bubbles suspended in the drum water to **condense**. This increases the bulk density of the water-steam mixture, causing the **apparent drum level to fall** even though the actual mass of water has increased.
>
> **Swell:** When the steam demand suddenly **increases**, the drum pressure momentarily **falls**, flashing more water into steam bubbles. The bubble volume expands, **decreasing the bulk density**, and the apparent drum level **rises** even though the actual mass of water has decreased.
>
> **Importance:** Shrink and swell cause the drum level signal to move in the **opposite direction** of the true water mass during the first 30–90 seconds of a load transient. A controller relying only on the level signal (single-element control) will react **incorrectly** during this period. Two-element and three-element control schemes use steam-flow feedforward to overcome this limitation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: List the three elements measured in three-element feed water control. State the function of each element in the control loop.**

**Model Answer:**

> The three elements are:
> 1. **Drum Level (h):** Provides the **outer feedback signal** representing the controlled variable. A slow-acting PI controller uses this signal to correct any long-term offset between feedwater and steam flow.
> 2. **Steam Flow (Wₛ):** Used as a **feedforward signal**. When load changes, the steam flow signal changes first, and the controller preemptively adjusts the feedwater valve to match, eliminating the swell-induced delay.
> 3. **Feedwater Flow (W_f):** Used in the **inner flow-balancing loop**. The difference (W_f − Wₛ) drives the flow controller to maintain the mass balance. This loop compensates for disturbances in feedwater pressure and valve nonlinearity. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a three-element feed water control system for a boiler drum. Derive the mass-balance equation of the drum and explain how shrink and swell phenomena affect the choice of control strategy. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Block Diagram and Operation (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

The three-element feed water control system uses **three measurements** to control **one manipulated variable** (feedwater valve position). The block diagram consists of:

1. **Outer Loop (Level Loop):** Setpoint drum level $h_{sp}$ is compared with measured level $h$. The error is processed by a **PI controller** (slow integral action, τ_i ≈ 100–300 s) whose output is the **trim signal** $u_{trim}$.

2. **Inner Loop (Flow-Balancing Loop):** The measured steam flow $W_s$ is subtracted from the measured feedwater flow $W_f$. The difference $(W_f - W_s)$ is the **flow error**. A **fast P or PI controller** (bandwidth 5–10× higher than outer loop) acts on this error.

3. **Signal Summation:** The trim signal $u_{trim}$ from the outer loop is **added** to the feedforward signal from steam flow to generate the **valve command signal**. The valve adjusts the feedwater flow to bring the flow error to zero and simultaneously maintain the drum level at setpoint.

**Operation:** In steady state, $W_f = W_s$ and $h = h_{sp}$. When load increases, $W_s$ rises first; the inner loop opens the valve to increase $W_f$ rapidly, preventing level swell from confusing the controller. The outer level loop provides slow trim to maintain accuracy. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Mass-Balance Derivation and Shrink-Swell Analysis (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Drum Mass Balance (Ideal):**
Treating the steam drum as a control volume of cross-sectional area $A$ and water density $\rho$:

$$A \rho \frac{dh}{dt} = W_f - W_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{W_f - W_s}{A\rho}$$

In steady state, $\frac{dh}{dt} = 0$, hence $W_f = W_s$.

**Step 2 — Including Swell Effect:**

$$h_{measured}(t) = h_{mass}(t) + K_{sw} \cdot \Delta P_{drum}(t) \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/T_{sw}}\right)$$

where $K_{sw}$ is the swell gain and $T_{sw} \approx 30$–$90$ s is the swell time constant.

**Step 3 — Effect on Control Strategy:**
During a load step-up, $h_{measured}$ momentarily rises (swell) even as $W_f < W_s$. A single-element controller interprets this as "level is high" and **closes the feedwater valve** — exactly the wrong action. This is why two-element and three-element control with steam-flow feedforward are mandatory for boilers that experience frequent load changes.

**Step 4 — Justification of Three-Element Choice:**
Three-element control **decouples** the level signal from the fast transient response by relegating it to a slow trim role, while the flow signals handle the fast dynamics. This eliminates the swell-induced false reaction. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> - **−1 mark:** Drawing block diagram without labeling the three signals (h, W_s, W_f) explicitly.
> - **−2 marks:** Forgetting the **integral term** in the outer level controller when claiming "zero offset."
> - **−2 marks:** Confusing "swell" with "shrink" definitions, or stating that the level change is real rather than apparent.
> - **−1 mark:** Writing $W_f = W_s$ as the *only* equation without deriving the differential form.
> - **−1 mark:** Omitting units in numerical substitution.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: Compare single-element, two-element, and three-element feed water control schemes. For a boiler drum with cross-sectional area $A = 4 \text{ m}^2$, feedwater flow $W_f = 25 \text{ kg/s}$, and steam flow $W_s = 35 \text{ kg/s}$ (open loop), calculate the rate of change of drum level. Water density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$. Justify which control scheme is most suitable for a 200 MW utility boiler. (14 marks)**

#### (a) Comparison of the Three Control Schemes (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Single-Element | Two-Element | Three-Element |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Measured variables** | Drum level h | h + W_s | h + W_s + W_f |
| **Controller type** | Single PI | PI level + feedforward | Cascade: outer PI + inner P/PI |
| **Response to swell** | Poor (false reaction) | Good (feedforward) | Excellent |
| **Sensitivity to feedwater pressure disturbance** | High | High | Low (measured) |
| **Steady-state offset** | Zero (with PI) | Zero (with PI) | Zero (with PI) |
| **Speed of response** | Slow | Fast | Fastest |
| **Cost and complexity** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Recommended application** | < 5 TPH | 5–50 TPH | > 50 TPH (utility) |

**Justification for 200 MW utility boiler:** A 200 MW unit experiences frequent load swings (typically 3–5% per minute ramp). The boiler drum is large (≈ 8 m diameter), giving significant swell time constants. The steam flow is high (≈ 200 kg/s), and any imbalance even for 10 seconds would cause tube overheating. Therefore, **three-element control is the only acceptable scheme** for this application. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Numerical Calculation of Rate of Level Change (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant equation.**

For an open-loop boiler drum (no control), the mass balance gives:

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{W_f - W_s}{A \rho}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{25 - 35}{(4)(1000)} = \frac{-10}{4000} = -0.0025 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to more intuitive units.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = -2.5 \text{ mm/s} = -150 \text{ mm/min} = -9 \text{ m/h}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

In the absence of control, the drum level falls at 2.5 mm per second. For a typical alarm level at −100 mm from setpoint, the alarm would be reached in **40 seconds**. The boiler would trip on low-level protection within approximately **1–2 minutes**, leading to an emergency shutdown.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

This calculation clearly demonstrates the **necessity of an automatic closed-loop control system**. For a 200 MW boiler, only **three-element control** provides the necessary speed, accuracy, and disturbance rejection to prevent such a scenario. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> - **−2 marks:** Not converting the final answer into mm/min or mm/s (students often leave it in m/s, which is difficult to interpret practically).
> - **−1 mark:** Forgetting to include the sign (negative indicates level falling).
> - **−2 marks:** Generic comparison without quantitative values or specific application context.
> - **−1 mark:** Not explicitly stating why three-element control is chosen for the given 200 MW unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **Feed water control** maintains drum level AND enforces $W_f = W_s$ in steady state.
- [ ] **Three control strategies:** single-element (h), two-element (h + W_s), three-element (h + W_s + W_f).
- [ ] **Shrink:** level falls transiently when load decreases (bubbles condense, density increases).
- [ ] **Swell:** level rises transiently when load increases (bubbles expand, density decreases).
- [ ] **Mass balance equation:** $A \rho \dfrac{dh}{dt} = W_f - W_s$.
- [ ] **Swell time constant** $T_{sw} \approx 30$–$90$ s (memorize the range).
- [ ] **Single-element control** is suitable only for small boilers (< 5 TPH).
- [ ] **Two-element control** uses steam flow as **feedforward**.
- [ ] **Three-element control** uses **cascade** structure: outer level loop (slow) + inner flow loop (fast).
- [ ] **Three-element control** is mandatory for utility boilers > 50 MW.
- [ ] **Integral action** in the level controller eliminates steady-state offset.
- [ ] **Failure of feed water control** causes tube overheating (low level) or turbine erosion (high level).
- [ ] **Astrom-Bell drum model** (1966) is the mathematical foundation: includes swell as additive disturbance to level signal.
- [ ] **Swell gain** $K_{sw}$ depends on drum geometry and operating pressure.
- [ ] **Time-scale separation:** inner flow loop is 5–10× faster than outer level loop.
- [ ] Standard measurement instruments: **differential pressure** level transmitter (or guided-wave radar), **orifice plate** for feedwater flow, **annular Pitot** or **nozzle** for steam flow.
- [ ] Controller tuning typical values: $K_c = 0.5$–$2.0$ for outer loop, $K_c = 1.0$–$3.0$ for inner flow loop; $\tau_i = 60$–$300$ s for level controller.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soot Blowing Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Soot Blowing Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Soot Blowing Operation** is the controlled, sequential removal of ash, slag, and unburnt carbonaceous deposits (collectively termed *soot* or *fouling*) from the heat-absorbing surfaces of a boiler — primarily the furnace walls, superheater tubes, reheater tubes, economizer coils, and air preheater elements — using a high-velocity jet of blowing medium (steam, compressed air, or water) ejected through **soot blowers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT523 – Module 4):** Soot blowing is studied under *boiler instrumentation and auxiliary control systems*. The instrumentation angle focuses on **pressure transmitters, temperature sensors, position feedback devices, sequence controllers, and DCS-based automation** of soot-blowing networks.

In a pulverized-coal-fired boiler, the combustion of fuel produces **flue gas** laden with:

| Component | Typical Origin |
|---|---|
| Fly ash (sintered/particulate) | Incompletely fused mineral matter |
| Unburnt carbon (soot) | Incomplete combustion of coal volatiles |
| Molten slag (liquid phase) | Silica + Al₂O₃ + Fe₂O₃ eutectics above **1200 °C** |
| Vapour-phase alkali sulphates | Na₂SO₄, K₂SO₄ condensing at **550–650 °C** |

These deposits accumulate on tube banks, degrading heat-transfer coefficients and causing tube overheating.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine blowing the lint filter on a clothes dryer with a reversed vacuum-cleaner nozzle. Just as lint clogs the dryer filter and restricts airflow (forcing the heater to work harder), soot clings to boiler tubes and forces the furnace to burn more fuel to deliver the same steam output. A **soot blower is essentially a robotic high-pressure "jet wash"** that travels inside the boiler and puffs away this dirt at programmed intervals.

If you *don't* blow the soot:
- Heat transfer drops
- Tube metal temperature rises (risk of bursting)
- Fuel consumption rises
- Emissions rise
- The boiler derates (cannot deliver rated MW)

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Steam soot-blowing pressure** at the blower inlet: **1.0 – 3.5 MPa (10 – 35 bar)**
> - **Steam mass flow per blower pulse:** 0.3 – 1.5 kg/s (depending on tube spacing)
> - **Blowing-medium velocity at nozzle exit:** 300 – 600 m/s (supersonic in some designs)
> - **Time-on per position (pulse duration):** 5 – 30 seconds
> - **Blower traverse rate (retract type):** 0.1 – 0.3 m/s
> - **Critical fouling factor threshold (R<sub>f</sub>):** 0.0002 – 0.001 m²·K/W
> - **Allowable tube metal temperature rise above saturation:** 25 – 50 K (limit per IBR)

The **fouling factor (R<sub>f</sub>)** is the thermal resistance added by soot, defined as:

$$R_f = \frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} - \frac{1}{U_{\text{fouled}}}$$

where $U$ is the overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K). When $R_f$ exceeds the threshold, the soot-blowing sequence is triggered.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" of Soot Formation — Formation Mechanism

Coal combustion produces fouling through three distinct mechanisms:

1. **Slagging (high-temperature, > 1200 °C):** Molten ash droplets adhere to furnace walls and pendent tubes. Forms glassy, brittle deposits.
2. **Fouling (mid-temperature, 600 – 1000 °C):** Sticky, partially sintered ash on superheater and reheater tubes. Most problematic region.
3. **Cold-end deposition (< 400 °C):** Fine particulates and condensation of H₂SO₄ on economizer and air-preheater surfaces. Causes corrosion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the instrumentation focus?** Soot deposition cannot be measured *directly* on a live boiler. Therefore, **proxy variables** — steam temperature, flue-gas temperature at economizer exit, furnace draft, and tube-skin thermocouples — are measured and used by the **DCS soot-blowing sequencer** to determine *when* and *where* to blow.

## 2.2 Types of Soot Blowing Media — Comparative Analysis

| Medium | Pressure Range | Use Case | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Superheated Steam** | 1.0 – 3.5 MPa | Primary choice for all zones | Dry, hot, no corrosion | Drains boiler output; needs superheat to avoid quenching |
| **Compressed Air** | 0.8 – 1.5 MPa | Air preheater region, economizer | No steam loss; cold operation possible | Less thermal cleaning energy; moisture risk |
| **Water (Hot)** | 2.0 – 4.0 MPa | Emergency / severe slag removal | Strong impact; vapor shock | Tube thermal shock; de-rating; explosion risk |

## 2.3 Classification of Soot Blowers (KTU High-Yield)

| Blower Type | Location | Mounting | Travel | Media |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Long Retractable (LR)** | Furnace walls, platen superheater | Wall-mounted | 3 – 12 m | Steam |
| **Short Retractable (SR)** | Pendant superheater, reheater | Tube-bank-inserted | 0.5 – 3 m | Steam |
| **Fixed Rotary (FR)** | Reheater banks, economizer headers | Welded in place; rotates 360° | Fixed; rotating head | Steam |
| **Wall Blower (WB)** | Water-wall furnace | Inserted through wall; non-travel | Short stroke | Steam |
| **Rotary Air Preheater Soot Blower (RAPH-SB)** | APH sector | Tracked carriage | Travels with rotor | Steam / air |
| **Soot Blower Lance with Popping Valves** | Various | Multi-nozzle lance | Retracting | Steam |
| **Shot Soot Blower** | Slag-tap furnaces | Pneumatic | — | Steel shot + air |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fouling Factor | $R_f = \dfrac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} - \dfrac{1}{U_{\text{fouled}}}$ | m²·K/W | Trigger metric for soot blowing |
| 2 | Heat Duty Drop | $\Delta Q = U_{\text{clean}} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{clean}} - U_{\text{fouled}} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{\text{fouled}}$ | W | Energy penalty due to soot |
| 3 | Tube Skin Temperature Rise | $\Delta T_{mt} = \dfrac{q \cdot s}{k} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f \cdot k}{s}\right)$ | K | $q$ = heat flux, $s$ = tube wall thickness, $k$ = tube metal conductivity |
| 4 | Nozzle Exit Jet Velocity | $v_{exit} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma-1} \cdot R \cdot T_0 \cdot \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{p_{exit}}{p_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}$ | m/s | Isentropic expansion of steam/air |
| 5 | Mass Flow per Nozzle | $\dot{m} = C_d \cdot A_n \cdot p_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2\gamma}{(\gamma-1)RT_0}\left[\left(\dfrac{p_b}{p_0}\right)^{2/\gamma} - \left(\dfrac{p_b}{p_0}\right)^{(\gamma+1)/\gamma}\right]}$ | kg/s | $C_d \approx 0.85$ – $0.95$ |
| 6 | Blower Energy Impact (Steam-side) | $\Delta m_{steam} = \dot{m}_{blow} \cdot t_{pulse} \cdot N_{blowers}$ | kg | Mass of steam bled per cycle |
| 7 | Boiler Efficiency Penalty | $\Delta \eta \approx \dfrac{\Delta T_{fg} \cdot \dot{m}_{fg}}{Q_{fuel}}$ | — | $Q_{fuel}$ is fuel calorific input |
| 8 | Pulse Duration Criterion | $t_{pulse} \geq \dfrac{\rho_{dep} \cdot \delta_{dep}}{\dot{m}_{blow} \cdot v_{jet}}$ | s | Empirical cleaning duration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of the pipe `|` character in any exam answer table to avoid formatting breaks in digital evaluation portals.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Soot-blowing systems are mission-critical in:

- **Utility thermal power plants** (coal, lignite, biomass) — 200 MW to 800 MW units
- **Cogeneration / combined-cycle HRSGs** — Heat Recovery Steam Generators
- **Process industry boilers** — petrochemical, pulp & paper, sugar mills
- **Waste-to-energy plants** — high-fouling refuse-derived fuel (RDF) combustion
- **Marine boilers** — compact, often hand-blown or short retractable

The instrumentation suite (transmitters, position sensors, valves, and DCS sequencer) typically represents **0.3 – 0.8 %** of total boiler capital cost but recovers **2 – 5 %** of fuel efficiency losses.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Tube Skin Temperature Rise due to Soot Deposition

**Statement:** Show that a soot layer of fouling factor $R_f$ causes the tube metal temperature to rise by an amount proportional to $R_f$.

**Step 1 — Set up the thermal resistance network.**

A clean boiler tube carrying steam at saturation temperature $T_{sat}$ has its outside surface at temperature $T_{w,clean}$, separated from the flue gas at $T_{fg}$ by an overall resistance $\frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}}$. The heat flux is:

$$q_{\text{clean}} = U_{\text{clean}} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{sat})$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the fouling layer.**

The soot layer adds an extra resistance $R_f$ in series on the gas side. The new overall coefficient becomes:

$$\frac{1}{U_{\text{fouled}}} = \frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} + R_f$$

Rearranging the definition of $R_f$:

$$R_f = \frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} - \frac{1}{U_{\text{fouled}}}$$

**Step 3 — Express the new heat flux.**

$$q_{\text{fouled}} = U_{\text{fouled}} \cdot (T_{fg} - T_{sat})$$

**Step 4 — Tube metal temperature drop relation.**

The temperature drop across the tube metal is:

$$\Delta T_{mt} = \frac{q \cdot s}{k}$$

where $s$ is tube wall thickness and $k$ is the thermal conductivity of tube steel (≈ 45 W/m·K).

**Step 5 — Compare clean and fouled cases.**

Taking the difference and substituting $q_{\text{fouled}} = q_{\text{clean}} - \Delta q$ (since with constant gas temperature, fouling reduces $q$):

$$\Delta T_{mt,\text{rise}} = \frac{(q_{\text{clean}} - q_{\text{fouled}}) \cdot s}{k}$$

This yields the engineer-friendly form:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{mt} = \frac{q \cdot s}{k} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f \cdot k}{s}\right)}$$

**Conversion logic:** This equation shows that as the fouling factor $R_f$ increases, the tube metal temperature rises. Once $\Delta T_{mt}$ exceeds the **25 – 50 K** IBR limit, soot blowing is mandated. The boiler DCS uses *tube-skin thermocouples* (Type-K, mineral-insulated) to measure this rise in real time.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nozzle Exit Velocity (Steam Soot Blower)

**Statement:** Derive the isentropic exit velocity of steam from a soot-blower nozzle.

**Step 1 — Start with energy conservation across the nozzle.**

For an adiabatic, isentropic nozzle, the steady-flow energy equation gives:

$$\frac{v_{exit}^2}{2} = h_0 - h_{exit}$$

where $h_0$ is the stagnation enthalpy upstream and $h_{exit}$ is the exit static enthalpy.

**Step 2 — Apply the isentropic relation for an ideal gas.**

For an ideal gas with specific heat ratio $\gamma$ and gas constant $R$:

$$h_0 - h_{exit} = c_p \cdot (T_0 - T_{exit})$$

where $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$.

**Step 3 — Use the isentropic temperature–pressure relation.**

$$\frac{T_{exit}}{T_0} = \left(\frac{p_{exit}}{p_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve for $v_{exit}$.**

$$v_{exit}^2 = 2 c_p T_0 \left[1 - \left(\frac{p_{exit}}{p_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$$

$$\boxed{v_{exit} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \gamma}{\gamma - 1} \, R \, T_0 \left[1 - \left(\frac{p_{exit}}{p_0}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]}}$$

**Conversion logic:** For steam soot blowers, $\gamma$ is taken as 1.3 (superheated steam) and $R = 461.5$ J/kg·K. With $p_0 = 3$ MPa, $T_0 = 600$ K, and choked-flow $p_{exit}/p_0 = 0.546$ (for $\gamma = 1.3$):

$$v_{exit} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 1.3}{0.3} \cdot 461.5 \cdot 600 \cdot (1 - 0.546)} \approx 512 \; \text{m/s}$$

This confirms why nozzle jets are effective — the **500 m/s impact** mechanically shears soft deposits and the **temperature differential** thermally shocks brittle slag.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Boiler Efficiency Penalty from a Single Soot-Blower Steam Bleed

**Statement:** A boiler of 250 MW capacity has a furnace gas flow of 600 kg/s. A soot-blowing cycle draws 0.6 kg/s of superheated steam for 25 minutes. Estimate the efficiency penalty.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant data.**

- Furnace gas mass flow: $\dot{m}_{fg} = 600$ kg/s
- Typical flue-gas specific heat: $c_{p,fg} \approx 1.05$ kJ/kg·K
- Soot-blowing steam mass: $\Delta m_{steam} = 0.6 \times (25 \times 60) = 900$ kg per cycle
- Equivalent steam thermal energy at 3 MPa, 600 K: $h \approx 3686$ kJ/kg
- Energy bled: $E_{blow} = 900 \times 3686 = 3.317 \times 10^6$ kJ per cycle
- Coal calorific value: $GCV = 25{,}000$ kJ/kg
- Boiler thermal efficiency baseline: $\eta = 0.38$

**Step 2 — Equivalent fuel penalty.**

$$m_{\text{fuel,equiv}} = \frac{E_{blow}}{GCV \cdot \eta} = \frac{3.317 \times 10^6}{25{,}000 \times 0.38} \approx 349 \; \text{kg coal per cycle}$$

**Step 3 — Annualise (assuming 4 cycles per day, 330 days).**

$$m_{\text{fuel,annual}} = 349 \times 4 \times 330 \approx 460{,}000 \; \text{kg/year} = 460 \; \text{tonnes/year}$$

**Conversion logic:** This 460 t/year penalty is **recovered many times over** by the efficiency gain from keeping heat-transfer surfaces clean — typically a *1.5 – 3 %* gain in boiler efficiency, worth **₹ 2 – 5 crores/year** in fuel savings for an Indian 250 MW plant.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Soot Blower Operation Sequence Logic

The **DCS sequencer** executes the following logic in a typical 14-step retractable blower cycle:

| Step | Action | Sensor / Transmitter | Duration | Interlock |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Position sensor reads "fully retracted" | Proximity / LVDT | < 0.5 s | Boiler load > 60 % MCR |
| 2 | Open inlet steam isolation valve | Limit switch | 1 – 2 s | Steam pressure > 2.0 MPa |
| 3 | Open pop valve (blow pulse 1) | Pressure transmitter | 5 – 8 s | Furnace draft OK |
| 4 | Lance extends by 0.3 m | LVDT position | 2 s | Travel torque < limit |
| 5 | Pulse 2, 3, 4 ... | Pressure | 5 – 8 s | Tube-skin temp monitored |
| 6 | Lance retracts | LVDT | 2 s | End of travel limit switch |
| 7 | Close inlet valve | Limit switch | 1 s | Retract confirm |
| 8 | Drain trap cycle | Float / conductivity | 10 s | Drain temperature > 150 °C |
| 9 | Move to next blower in sequence | DCS index | — | — |
| 10 | Log parameters (time, pressure, tube ΔT) | DCS historian | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** KTU examiners love to ask "list the interlocks in a soot-blowing system." Memorize the table above.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Soot Blower Instrumentation & Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    B --> C{Blower Zone Selection}
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hot Flue Gas T_fg approx 1100 C] --> R1
    R1[R_gas_side convective resistance] --> R2

    R4[Steam-side convective resistance R_steam] --> B[Steam T_sat approx 540 C]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Define soot blowing. List any four types of soot blowers used in thermal power plant boilers.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Soot blowing** is the process of removing accumulated ash, slag, and unburnt carbon deposits from boiler heat-transfer surfaces by jetting high-pressure steam or air through specially designed soot blowers, in order to maintain heat-transfer efficiency and prevent tube overheating.

**Four types of soot blowers:**
1. Long Retractable (LR) soot blower
2. Short Retractable (SR) soot blower
3. Fixed Rotary (FR) soot blower
4. Rotary Air Preheater Soot (RAPH) blower

*(Acceptable: Wall blower, Shot soot blower, IR — infrared — for APH region.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**What is the function of a pop valve in a soot-blower assembly?**

**Model Answer:**

A **pop valve** is a quick-opening, quick-closing valve installed in series with the soot-blower lance. Its function is to:

1. Provide an **on/off pulse** of blowing medium to the lance nozzle.
2. Generate a **sharp, high-velocity jet** by rapidly releasing compressed steam/air.
3. Allow **blow-pulse timing control** from the DCS.
4. Provide **leak-tight isolation** in the closed state to prevent steam loss.

Two pop valves are used in series to meet **double-block-and-bleed** safety requirements per IBR.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Soot Blower Instrumentation & DCS

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the instrumentation and control system used for automatic soot-blowing operation in a 250 MW coal-fired boiler. List the sensors, actuators, and control logic.**

**Model Solution:**

The automatic soot-blowing system uses a **DCS-based sequencer** integrating the following:

**Sensors (Field Layer):**
1. **Steam pressure transmitter (PT)** at blower inlet: range 0 – 5 MPa, HART-enabled, with smart diagnostics. Critical interlock: blowing inhibited if pressure < 1.5 MPa.
2. **Steam temperature transmitter (TT)**: Type-K sheath thermocouple, ensures superheat > 200 °C to avoid water carryover.
3. **Lance position feedback (LVDT)**: 0 – 12 m stroke, 4 – 20 mA output, ±0.5 % linearity.
4. **Tube-skin thermocouples**: Mineral-insulated Type-K, 1.5 mm sheath, on critical superheater tubes.
5. **Furnace draft transmitter**: ±50 mbar range, for furnace pressure interlock.
6. **Lance motor torque limiter**: shear-pin / load-cell type.

**Actuators:**
1. **Motor-Operated Valve (MOV)** — inlet steam isolation.
2. **Pop valves** — pneumatic-actuated quick-open valves.
3. **Lance drive motor** — 3-phase induction, 0.75 kW, with VFD for variable traverse.
4. **Drain trap solenoid**.

**Control Logic (DCS):**

The sequencer runs a **state machine** with the following logic gates:

- **Trigger logic:** Boiler load > 60 % MCR *AND* time since last cycle > setpoint (4 – 8 hours) *OR* tube-skin ΔT > 25 K above baseline.
- **Permission interlocks:** Master fuel ON, no flame failure, drum level normal, FD/ID fans running, no soot-blower in maintenance lockout.
- **Sequential blow:** Zone-by-zone blow pattern (furnace → platen → SH → RH → economizer → APH).
- **Adaptive control:** Pulse duration adjusted based on real-time tube-skin temperature drop after each pulse.

**Communication:** Foundation Fieldbus / PROFIBUS PA to the DCS; data logged in OSIsoft PI historian.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Describe the step-by-step sequence of operation of a Long Retractable (LR) soot blower with the help of a timing diagram.**

**Model Solution:**

A typical LR soot-blower cycle (≈ 6 minutes) is:

| Phase | Time (s) | Action | Valve States |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Idle** | 0 – 10 | Position sensor confirms full retract; DCS checks interlocks. | All valves closed. |
| **Arm** | 10 – 20 | Open inlet MOV slowly to prevent water hammer. | MOV opens; pop valves closed. |
| **Advance & Pulse 1** | 20 – 40 | Lance extends 0.3 m; pop valve 1 opens for 5 s. | Pop 1 pulses. |
| **Pulse 2 – 6** | 40 – 90 | Lance advances stepwise; each step opens pop valve 2 for 5 s. | Pop 2 pulses 5 times. |
| **Hold at far end** | 90 – 110 | Lance at maximum penetration; one long pulse for slag removal. | Pop 2 latched open. |
| **Retract & Pulse** | 110 – 180 | Lance retracts stepwise; pop valve pulses in reverse for back-blow. | Pop valves pulse during retract. |
| **Close & Drain** | 180 – 220 | Close inlet MOV; drain trap opens; lance fully retracts. | All valves close; drain opens. |
| **Standby** | 220 – 360 | Cool-down, position reset, ready for next cycle. | All closed. |

> **Total per blower:** 6 minutes; **24 LR blowers** in a 250 MW boiler = **2.4 hours** for a full sequence.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Types, Media & Performance

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Compare the different types of soot blowers used in a thermal power plant boiler. State the zone of application for each.**

**Model Solution:**

| S. No. | Type | Description | Zone of Application | Blowing Medium |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Long Retractable (LR)** | Wall-mounted, multi-nozzle lance travels 3 – 12 m into furnace | Furnace walls, platen superheater, final superheater | Steam (1.5 – 3.5 MPa) |
| 2 | **Short Retractable (SR)** | Compact lance travels 0.5 – 3 m | Pendant superheater, primary reheater | Steam |
| 3 | **Fixed Rotary (FR)** | Welded in place, head rotates 360° | Reheater banks, economizer inlet | Steam |
| 4 | **Wall Blower (WB)** | Short-stroke, inserted through furnace wall | Furnace water walls (slag zones) | Steam |
| 5 | **RAPH Soot Blower** | Tracked carriage travels through APH sector | Air preheater (hot, cold end) | Steam / air |
| 6 | **Shot Soot Blower** | Pneumatic ejection of steel shot | Slag-tap furnace floors | Steel shot + air |
| 7 | **Soot Blower with Popping Valves** | Multi-nozzle lance with timed pop-valve pulses | All zones with timed pulses | Steam |

**Selection criteria:**
- Zone temperature (above or below 540 °C — determines whether to use superheated steam or air).
- Tube spacing and geometry.
- Required cleaning frequency.
- Access for lance retraction.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss the effects of soot accumulation on boiler performance. How does instrumentation help in optimizing the soot-blowing schedule?**

**Model Solution:**

**Effects of soot accumulation:**

1. **Reduction in heat-transfer coefficient:** $U$ may fall by 30 – 50 % in severely fouled superheaters, raising the fouling factor $R_f$.
2. **Increased flue-gas exit temperature:** $\Delta T_{fg,exit}$ rises 20 – 60 K, directly lowering boiler efficiency:

$$\Delta \eta = \frac{\dot{m}_{fg} \cdot c_{p,fg} \cdot \Delta T_{fg,exit}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100\%$$

For a 250 MW unit, a 30 K exit-gas rise corresponds to ≈ 0.8 % efficiency loss = ₹ 5 – 7 crores/year.

3. **Tube overheating:** Local tube-skin temperature may exceed the creep limit of T22 / T91 steel (≈ 600 °C), risking rupture.
4. **Increased draft loss:** Ash plugs in APH raise gas-side pressure drop, forcing ID/FD fans to draw more power (+ 200 – 500 kW).
5. **Load derating:** Operators may be forced to throttle the unit to keep tube temperatures within IBR limits.
6. **Emissions rise:** Incomplete heat release increases unburnt carbon and CO emissions.

**Role of instrumentation in optimization:**

| Instrument | Function |
|---|---|
| Flue-gas exit temperature TT | Primary trigger for soot-blow sequence |
| Tube-skin TCs | Real-time tube health monitoring |
| Furnace draft PT | Detects APH fouling pressure drop |
| Steam flow + temperature + drum pressure | Computes boiler efficiency live; correlates to fouling |
| Lance position LVDT + torque | Detects lance jams, schedule maintenance |
| Hopper level / ash flow | Indicates post-blow ash removal |
| Acoustic / IR cameras (advanced) | Direct deposit imaging (research) |

A **Model-Predictive Controller (MPC)** in modern DCS can schedule each blower *individually* based on these signals, achieving **2 – 3 % efficiency gain** over fixed-time cycling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Soot-Blowing Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating soot blowers with soot particles** — students often describe "blowing soot" instead of "blowing deposits *off* tubes."
> 2. **Omitting interlocks** — a 14-mark question that doesn't list *at least 4 interlocks* (steam pressure, furnace draft, boiler load, lance position) loses **3 – 4 marks** easily.
> 3. **Mixing up LR / SR / FR** — these are easily confused. Use the mnemonic: **L**ong = **L**arge furnace; **S**hort = **S**mall mid-section; **F**ixed Rotary = **F**or economizer.
> 4. **Forgetting units** — KTU mandates SI units. Always write **MPa (not bar)** and **m/s** (not km/h) in formulas.
> 5. **Skipping safety** — failing to mention **double pop valve** and **drain trap** costs marks in instrumentation questions.
> 6. **Hand-drawn diagrams are accepted** — but **block diagrams (as in Section 4 of these notes) earn 1 – 2 extra marks** for clarity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Soot blowing** = controlled removal of ash/slag/unburnt carbon from boiler heat-transfer surfaces using high-velocity jets.
- ✅ Primary blowing medium = **superheated steam** at **1 – 3.5 MPa**, with jet velocity of **300 – 600 m/s**.
- ✅ Six major blower types: **LR, SR, FR, WB, RAPH, Shot**.
- ✅ **Fouling factor** $R_f$ is the key trigger metric — defined as $R_f = \frac{1}{U_{\text{clean}}} - \frac{1}{U_{\text{fouled}}}$.
- ✅ **Tube-skin thermocouples** are the safety backbone — IBR allows only **25 – 50 K** rise above saturation.
- ✅ **Interlocks** (memorize): steam pressure ≥ 1.5 MPa, furnace draft OK, boiler load ≥ 60 % MCR, lance retracted, no flame failure, no maintenance lockout.
- ✅ **Double pop valve** + **drain trap** = mandatory safety architecture.
- ✅ **Sequencer logic** is a DCS state machine — most KTU 14-mark questions test this.
- ✅ Efficiency penalty from soot = **1.5 – 3 %** — instrumentation recovers this via adaptive scheduling.
- ✅ **RAPH soot blowers** travel on tracks *with* the APH rotor (special case — not stationary).
- ✅ **Emerging tech:** IR cameras, acoustic emission sensors, AI-based MPC for predictive soot-blow scheduling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision:** If you remember nothing else, remember — **Steam, Pressure 1–3.5 MPa, Lance travels, Pop valve pulses, DCS sequencer triggers on tube-skin ΔT.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam Temperature Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steam Temperature Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steam Temperature Control** is the closed-loop regulatory process employed in thermal power plants to maintain the temperature of superheated and reheated steam leaving the boiler within a tight permissible band (typically **540 °C ± 5 °C** for modern subcritical units and **565 °C ± 5 °C** for supercritical units) so as to maximise Rankine cycle efficiency while protecting the high-pressure (HP) and intermediate-pressure (IP) turbine blades from thermal stress and creep failure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic falls under the *Boiler Instrumentation* cluster, and the steam temperature is treated as a **Process Variable (PV)** in a cascade or feedforward control configuration, where the **Controlled Variable (CV)** is steam temperature and the **Manipulated Variable (MV)** is typically the spray water flow (attemperation) or flue gas flow (recirculation/bypass).

## 1.2 Why Steam Temperature Control is Critical

The temperature of steam entering the HP turbine inlet is one of the most critical operating parameters in a thermal power plant because:

- **Thermal Efficiency**: Cycle efficiency rises almost linearly with live-steam temperature (Carnot factor).
- **Material Limits**: Modern ferritic steel tubes (T91, T92) and austenitic superheaters (TP347H) have a **maximum allowable creep stress** that bounds the upper temperature.
- **Turbine Blade Life**: A 10 °C excess can cut blade life by ~50 % (Larsen–Miller rule).
- **Load Following**: During load ramps, superheater dynamics are slow (τ ≈ 30–90 s) and non-linear, demanding sophisticated control.

> [!NOTE]
> **Permissible Steam Temperature Limits (KTU Reference Values):**
> - Subcritical Unit (500 MW): **535 °C to 545 °C**
> - Supercritical Unit (660/800 MW): **560 °C to 570 °C**
> - Temperature swing permitted per minute: **≤ 2 °C/min** during normal load changes.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shower Mixer" Intuition

Imagine taking a hot shower. The water heater (boiler) sends out steam-like, scalding water (superheated steam). If it is too hot, you instinctively turn the **cold-water knob** to mix cooler water and bring the temperature to a comfortable level. The body senses the temperature (sensor), the brain decides how much cold water to add (controller), and the hand turns the knob (final control element).

A **Spray Attemperator** does exactly the same thing:
- The "hot stream" = superheated steam
- The "cold-water knob" = spray water injected directly into the steam
- The "skin" sensing the temperature = RTD / thermocouple at the SH outlet
- The "brain" = PID / cascade DCS controller

This simple analogy is the foundation of the most widely deployed method of steam temperature control in modern power stations: **Spray Attemperation**.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific heat of superheated steam | $C_{ps}$ | **2.1** | kJ/kg·K |
| Latent heat of water at attemperator pressure | $h_{fg}$ | **2000 – 2200** | kJ/kg |
| Density of spray water | $\rho_w$ | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Spray water pressure | $P_w$ | **15 – 40** | bar(g) |
| Spray water temperature | $T_w$ | **150 – 220** | °C |
| Spray droplet size | $d_p$ | **50 – 200** | µm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Sequence — Six Logical Steps

1. **Steam Generation**: Saturated steam leaves the boiler drum (for subcritical) or steam separator (for once-through) at the saturation temperature corresponding to drum pressure (e.g., **at 180 bar, T_sat ≈ 357 °C**).
2. **Primary Superheating**: Steam flows through the **Primary Superheater (PSH)** in the radiant zone. Radiant heat transfer raises its temperature, but the rate of rise is *self-limiting* (steam absorbs more with rising temperature, lowering metal-wall temperature).
3. **Inter-stage Attemperation Point**: An **Attemperator** is placed between PSH and **Final Superheater (FSH)**. This is the injection point for spray water.
4. **Spray Water Injection**: High-pressure deaerator water (or feedwater from the cascade) is atomised into fine droplets, which evaporate almost instantaneously, absorbing the **latent heat of vaporisation** from the steam.
5. **Final Superheating**: The desuperheated steam is reheated in the FSH to the final desired temperature.
6. **Reheat Section**: Steam expanded partially in the HP turbine returns to the **Reheater (RH)** for reheating. The RH outlet temperature is independently controlled (no attemperator on RH is allowed, because reheat steam is at low pressure and any water carryover is catastrophic — so RH control uses flue gas methods).

## 2.2 Methods of Steam Temperature Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Standard Methods (KTU Board-Favourite List):**
> 1. **Spray Attemperation** (most common for SH)
> 2. **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)**
> 3. **Gas Bypass / Stack Damper**
> 4. **Tilting Burners**
> 5. **Excess Air / Combustion Control**
> 6. **Combined / Cascade Control**

### 2.2.1 Spray Attemperation
- Cold water (deaerator storage or HP feedwater) is atomised into the steam line.
- Fastest response, located in convective pass.
- Limited by the *mass of water* available and the *spray nozzle atomisation quality*.

### 2.2.2 Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)
- A portion of the hot flue gas (post-economiser, ~300–400 °C) is drawn by an FGR fan and re-injected into the furnace.
- Increases the gas-side mass flow, lowering peak furnace temperature, but raises convective pass gas temperature → useful for *reheater* control.
- Slow, large time constant.

### 2.2.3 Gas Bypass / Stack Damper
- A fraction of flue gas is diverted around the superheater section, reducing heat transfer.
- Economically simple, but causes back-pressure and reduces efficiency.

### 2.2.4 Tilting Burners
- Burner nozzles are pivoted up or down (±20°–30°).
- Tilting up → flame moves away from SH → SH temperature drops.
- Tilting down → flame impinges on SH → SH temperature rises.
- Effective for both SH and RH simultaneously.

### 2.2.5 Excess Air Control
- Increasing excess air lowers flame temperature (more N₂ ballast), reducing SH outlet T.
- Used only as a *trim* signal because of efficiency penalty.

### 2.2.6 Combined / Cascade Control
- **Spray attemperation** as fast inner loop for SH.
- **Burner tilt / FGR** as slow outer loop for RH.
- Modern plants use **cascade control** with two temperature transmitters at different points along the SH.

## 2.3 Why and How — The "Why" Behind Attemperation

> **Why not just let the boiler naturally produce the right temperature?**
> Because boiler load changes (100 % → 50 %) cause the superheater heat flux to change, but the steam mass flow drops even more, leading to a *temperature rise* at part load. Without an active trim mechanism, the temperature would either exceed material limits (high load) or fall below the design point (low load).

> **How does attemperation solve it?**
> The enthalpy of the water-spray is far lower than that of the steam at the injection point. The mass of water required is small, but the latent-heat absorption is enormous, allowing fast and precise trimming.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat absorbed by steam in superheater | kW (kJ/s) |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} \, (T_1 - T_2)}{h_{g,\,P} - h_{w,\,T_w}}$ | Spray water mass flow (desuperheater) | kg/s |
| 3 | $T_{out} = T_{in} - \dfrac{\dot{m}_w (h_{g,P} - h_{w,T_w})}{\dot{m}_s \, C_{ps}}$ | Final steam temperature after attemperation | °C |
| 4 | $NTU = \dfrac{U \, A}{\dot{m}_s \, C_{ps}}$ | Number of transfer units (superheater) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-NTU}$ | Effectiveness of superheater heat exchanger | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\tau_{SH} = \dfrac{M_s \, C_{ps}}{U \, A}$ | Superheater thermal time constant | seconds |
| 7 | $\eta_{cycle} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cond}}{T_{steam}}$ | Carnot approximation of cycle efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $K_p$ (process) = $\dfrac{\partial T_{out}}{\partial \dot{m}_w}$ | Process gain of attemperator loop | °C·s/kg |
| 9 | $\sigma_{creep} = A \cdot e^{-B/T}$ | Larsen–Miller creep relation (qualitative) | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation (2) is the *primary sizing equation* used by Boiler Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) like BHEL, L&T-MHI, and Doosan to specify the spray water control valve (CV) Cv rating. Equation (6) is critical for tuning the **cascade master/secondary loop** in DCS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Energy Balance for a Spray Attemperator

Let:
- $\dot{m}_s$ = steam mass flow rate entering the attemperator (kg/s)
- $\dot{m}_w$ = spray water mass flow rate injected (kg/s)
- $T_1$ = steam temperature upstream of attemperator (°C)
- $T_2$ = steam temperature downstream of attemperator (°C)
- $C_{ps}$ = average specific heat of superheated steam (kJ/kg·K)
- $h_{g,P}$ = enthalpy of steam at attemperator pressure, saturated (kJ/kg)
- $h_{w,T_w}$ = enthalpy of spray water at injection temperature $T_w$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_{w,2}$ = enthalpy of liquid water at $T_2$ (≈ saturated liquid at attemperator P)

**Step 1** — Write the steady-state energy balance on the attemperator control volume (no heat loss to surroundings assumed; adiabatic mixing):

$$
\dot{m}_s \, h_{s,1} + \dot{m}_w \, h_{w,T_w} = (\dot{m}_s + \dot{m}_w) \, h_{s,2}
$$

where $h_{s,1} = C_{ps} T_1$ and $h_{s,2} = C_{ps} T_2$ for the superheated steam.

**Step 2** — Expand the outlet enthalpy of the steam-water mixture. The spray water evaporates fully, becoming part of the steam stream. So the total enthalpy of the mixture is:

$$
(\dot{m}_s + \dot{m}_w) \, h_{s,2} = \dot{m}_s \, h_{g,P} - \dot{m}_s \, (h_{g,P} - h_{w,2}) + \dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} (T_2 - T_{sat,P}) + \dot{m}_w \, h_{g,P}
$$

However, for a clean derivation, we consider that the *only* energy required by the water spray is the latent heat of vaporisation plus sensible heating from $T_w$ to $T_{sat,P}$. Thus, the heat absorbed by the spray is:

$$
Q_{absorbed} = \dot{m}_w \, (h_{g,P} - h_{w,T_w})
$$

**Step 3** — This heat is provided by cooling the steam from $T_1$ to $T_2$:

$$
\dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} (T_1 - T_2) = \dot{m}_w \, (h_{g,P} - h_{w,T_w})
$$

**Step 4** — Solve for spray water mass flow rate $\dot{m}_w$:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} (T_1 - T_2)}{h_{g,P} - h_{w,T_w}}
$$

**Step 5** — Solve for downstream temperature $T_2$:

$$
T_2 = T_1 - \dfrac{\dot{m}_w \, (h_{g,P} - h_{w,T_w})}{\dot{m}_s \, C_{ps}}
$$

**Step 6** — Numerical example. Let:
- $\dot{m}_s$ = **500 kg/s**
- $C_{ps}$ = **2.1 kJ/kg·K**
- $T_1$ = **520 °C** (pre-attemperator)
- $T_2$ target = **480 °C** (post-attemperator)
- $h_{g,P}$ at 30 bar = **2804 kJ/kg**
- $h_{w,T_w}$ at $T_w$ = 200 °C = **849 kJ/kg**

Then:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{500 \times 2.1 \times (520 - 480)}{2804 - 849} = \dfrac{500 \times 2.1 \times 40}{1955} = \dfrac{42000}{1955}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w \approx 21.48 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

This means that to desuperheat **500 kg/s** of steam by **40 °C**, only about **21.5 kg/s** (≈ **4.3 %** of steam mass) of spray water is needed — confirming attemperation is a *low-mass, high-energy* technique.

## 3.2 Derivation — Superheater Thermal Time Constant

A superheater is a once-through convective heat exchanger. The thermal time constant governs how fast the outlet temperature responds to disturbances (firing rate, flow, spray).

**Step 1** — Energy stored in the tube metal + steam inventory:

$$
E = M_{metal} \, C_{p,metal} \, \bar{T}_{metal} + M_s \, C_{ps} \, \bar{T}_s
$$

**Step 2** — Power input from flue gas (convective heat transfer):

$$
\dot{Q}_{in} = U \, A \, (T_{gas} - T_s)
$$

**Step 3** — Energy balance (lumped capacitance — valid for tube metal-dominated dynamics):

$$
M_{metal} \, C_{p,metal} \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} = U A (T_{gas} - T_s) - \dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

**Step 4** — For control-design purposes, linearise around the operating point. The first-order time constant is:

$$
\tau_{SH} = \dfrac{M_{metal} \, C_{p,metal}}{U \, A}
$$

**Step 5** — A typical 500 MW unit has $M_{metal} \approx 80\,000$ kg, $C_{p,metal}$ = **0.5 kJ/kg·K**, $U A \approx 2000$ kW/K. Hence:

$$
\tau_{SH} = \dfrac{80000 \times 0.5}{2000} = 20 \;\text{s}
$$

This is why SH temperature control needs a **cascade strategy** — the slow master (T_out) is assisted by a fast secondary (T_mid).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Method

| Method | Response Time | Range of Control | Cost | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Spray Attemperation | Fast (3–10 s) | ± 30 °C | Low | SH (Primary) |
| FGR | Slow (60–120 s) | ± 50 °C | High (FGR fan) | RH |
| Gas Bypass | Slow (90 s) | ± 20 °C | Low | SH Trim |
| Tilting Burner | Medium (20–40 s) | ± 40 °C | Medium | SH + RH |
| Excess Air | Fast (5 s) | ± 10 °C | Negligible | Trim only |
| Cascade (T1 + T2) | Fast (5 s) | ± 50 °C | Medium | SH (Best Practice) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
