<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Attemperation. List the types of attemperators.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Attemperation** is the controlled reduction of superheated steam temperature by introducing a relatively cooler medium (usually water) directly into the steam path. The process exploits the large latent heat of vaporisation of water to absorb heat from the steam almost instantaneously.

**Types of attemperators:**
1. **Spray-type (Direct Contact) Attemperator** — Water is atomised into fine droplets and injected into the steam.
2. **Surface-type (Indirect) Attemperator** — Steam passes over the outside of tubes carrying cooler water (no mixing).

Spray-type is preferred in modern utility boilers because of its faster response and lower cost.

---

### Question 2: Why is steam temperature control necessary in a thermal power plant?
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Steam temperature control is necessary for the following reasons:

1. **Protection of Boiler Tubes and Turbine Blades:** Excessive steam temperature causes creep rupture of superheater tubes and thermal-stress failure of HP/IP turbine blades. Modern alloys like T91 and TP347H have a creep limit around **600 °C**.
2. **Cycle Efficiency:** Thermal efficiency of the Rankine cycle increases with live-steam temperature (Carnot relation: $\eta = 1 - T_{cond}/T_{steam}$). Maintaining the *highest permissible* temperature maximises efficiency.
3. **Load Following:** During load changes, the superheater outlet temperature can swing by 30–50 °C. Without control, the unit must be derated.
4. **Uniformity of Heat Transfer:** Prevents local hot spots and tube failures in the final superheater zone.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain **six different methods** of steam temperature control in a thermal power plant boiler. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **cascade control system** for superheater outlet temperature using spray attemperation. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Solution to (a):**

The six standard methods are explained below:

1. **Spray Attemperation:** Deaerator water (or HP feedwater) at **15–40 bar** is atomised through a multi-nozzle header into the steam line between PSH and FSH. The water evaporates, absorbing **2.0–2.2 MJ/kg** of latent heat, and the steam temperature drops by 20–40 °C. It is the *fastest* method and is used in almost all modern subcritical and supercritical units for the primary superheater.

2. **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR):** A recirculation fan draws hot flue gas (300–400 °C) from the economiser outlet and injects it into the furnace. This increases gas mass flow in the convective pass, raising SH and RH outlet temperatures *paradoxically* (more gas → more heat transfer). Used mainly for **reheater** control since reheaters cannot be desuperheated (water carryover at LP conditions is unacceptable).

3. **Gas Bypass (Stack Damper):** A damper in the duct bypasses a fraction of flue gas around the SH/RH sections. Less gas flow → less heat transfer → temperature falls. Slow but inexpensive. Used for *trim* on smaller boilers.

4. **Tilting Burners:** Burner nozzles are pivoted up or down (±20°–30°). Tilting up moves the flame away from the SH, lowering the temperature. Tilting down impinges the flame on the SH, raising the temperature. Effective for both SH and RH simultaneously with a *medium* response time.

5. **Excess Air Control:** Increasing excess air lowers the adiabatic flame temperature (N₂ ballast effect), reducing furnace and SH temperatures. Limited range (~10 °C) and a significant efficiency penalty, so used only as a final trim.

6. **Combined / Cascade Control:** Modern units use a **cascade strategy** with two temperature sensors (one at the SH midpoint, one at the SH outlet). The outlet sensor is the *master*, the midpoint sensor is the *secondary*. Spray attemperation handles the fast inner loop; burner tilt or FGR handles the slow outer trim. This gives both speed and wide range.

**Solution to (b):**

In cascade control of SH temperature:
- **Master (Primary) Controller:** Receives the SH outlet temperature ($T_2$) error against setpoint (typically **540 °C**). Outputs the setpoint for the secondary controller.
- **Secondary (Slave) Controller:** Receives the SH midpoint temperature ($T_1$) error against the master setpoint. Outputs the signal to the **spray water control valve**.
- **Feedforward Signals:** Steam flow ($\dot{m}_s$) and firing rate are added as feedforward terms to compensate for measurable disturbances *before* the temperature deviates.

The complete block diagram (already shown in SECTION 4.1) features:
- Sensors (RTDs) at midpoint and outlet
- Two PID controllers in series
- Spray control valve (typically a pneumatically actuated globe valve with a positioner)
- Feedback loops shown as dashed lines
- The spray water enters the attemperator, drops $T_1$, and $T_2$ is then corrected by the FSH heat input.

The cascade reduces the **integral time** requirement of the master loop and improves the *disturbance rejection* against firing-rate changes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **construction and working** of a spray-type attemperator with a neat sketch. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 500 kg/s flow of superheated steam at **520 °C** is to be desuperheated to **480 °C** using water spray at **200 °C**. The attemperator pressure is **30 bar**, where the saturation temperature is **234 °C** and saturated-steam enthalpy is **2804 kJ/kg**. The specific heat of superheated steam is **2.1 kJ/kg·K**. Calculate the **spray water mass flow rate** required. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Solution to (a):**

**Construction of a Spray-Type Attemperator:**

1. **Main Steam Inlet Duct** — receives superheated steam from PSH.
2. **Multi-Nozzle Spray Header** — a stainless-steel pipe running across the steam duct, fitted with **6 to 12 atomising nozzles** (hollow-cone or solid-cone type).
3. **Mixing Chamber** — a straight section of duct (length 5–8 pipe diameters) where water evaporates and mixes.
4. **Vortex Eliminator / Baffle Plates** — to break swirling flow and ensure uniform temperature.
5. **Outlet Duct** — leads to the Final Superheater (FSH).
6. **Spray Water Control Valve** — a pneumatic globe valve with positioner, located on the spray water line.

**Working:**
- The DCS reads the SH outlet temperature.
- On error, the controller commands the spray valve to admit more (or less) water.
- Water is atomised into 50–200 µm droplets, evaporates almost instantly.
- Latent heat absorption drops the steam temperature.
- Outlet RTD provides feedback to close the loop.

**Solution to (b):**

Given:
- $\dot{m}_s$ = 500 kg/s
- $T_1$ = 520 °C
- $T_2$ = 480 °C
- $C_{ps}$ = 2.1 kJ/kg·K
- $h_{g,P}$ at 30 bar = 2804 kJ/kg
- $h_{w,T_w}$ at 200 °C ≈ **849 kJ/kg** (from steam tables)

Using the derived formula:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{\dot{m}_s \, C_{ps} (T_1 - T_2)}{h_{g,P} - h_{w,T_w}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{500 \times 2.1 \times (520 - 480)}{2804 - 849}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w = \dfrac{500 \times 2.1 \times 40}{1955} = \dfrac{42000}{1955}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w \approx 21.48 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Result:** A spray water flow of approximately **21.5 kg/s** is required, which is about **4.3 %** of the main steam mass flow.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Latent Heat Term:** Many students write $\dot{m}_w = \dot{m}_s C_{ps} (T_1 - T_2) / [C_{pw} (T_{sat} - T_w)]$ — this is *only the sensible heating* and ignores the dominant latent heat. You will lose **3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Wrong Steam-Table Values:** At 30 bar, $h_{g} = 2804$ kJ/kg and $h_f$ at 200 °C ≈ 849 kJ/kg. Using incorrect values (e.g., 2000 kJ/kg) will be marked wrong.
> 3. **Ignoring Cascade Structure:** In the 7-mark diagram question, students often draw a single-loop PID and forget the **master–slave hierarchy** and **feedforward** terms.
> 4. **No Reheater Attemperator:** Many students incorrectly mention an attemperator for the reheater. **No attemperator is permitted on the reheater** because of water carryover risk at low-pressure IP conditions.
> 5. **Units Confusion:** Always express $\dot{m}_w$ in **kg/s** (not kg/hr) when SI units are used in the problem.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Steam temperature must be maintained at **540 °C ± 5 °C** (subcritical) or **565 °C ± 5 °C** (supercritical).
- [x] **Primary Superheater (PSH)** → **Attemperator** → **Final Superheater (FSH)** is the standard arrangement.
- [x] The **attemperator energy balance** is: $\dot{m}_s C_{ps} (T_1 - T_2) = \dot{m}_w (h_{g,P} - h_{w,T_w})$.
- [x] **Spray attemperation** is the fastest method (~3–10 s response) and is used for the **superheater**.
- [x] **No attemperator is permitted on the reheater** (water carryover risk); reheater temperature is controlled by **FGR, burner tilt, or gas bypass**.
- [x] **Cascade control** uses a *midpoint* RTD (fast secondary) and an *outlet* RTD (slow master).
- [x] **Feedforward** signals (steam flow, firing rate) improve disturbance rejection.
- [x] The superheater time constant $\tau_{SH}$ is **20–90 seconds**, which mandates cascade control.
- [x] Spray water is typically **deaerator storage water or HP feedwater** at **15–40 bar** and **150–220 °C**.
- [x] Typical spray water mass flow is **3–6 %** of main steam flow for a 30–50 °C temperature reduction.
- [x] Droplet size must be **50–200 µm** to ensure complete evaporation before the next bend.
- [x] Cycle efficiency gain per +10 °C of steam temperature is approximately **0.2–0.3 %** (absolute).
- [x] The Larsen–Miller rule implies that a 10 °C excess temperature halves creep life of turbine blades.
- [x] **Tilting burners** move the flame ±20–30° to shift heat flux between furnace walls and convective pass.
- [x] **Excess air** is only a *trim* signal — it cannot be the primary control because of efficiency penalty.
- [x] In exams, always draw the **cascade block diagram** with sensors, two controllers, and a control valve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coordinated Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coordinated Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coordinated Control (CC)** is an advanced regulatory strategy in a thermal power generating unit in which the **Boiler Master Controller (BMC)** and the **Turbine Master Controller (TMC)** are dynamically coupled through a centralized **Unit Coordinator (UC)** so that *both* the **firing rate (fuel/air/water)** and the **turbine valve opening** respond to a single **Unit Demand (MW) setpoint**, while simultaneously maintaining **drum/throttle pressure**, **temperature**, and **turbine speed/load** within tight tolerances.

In KTU parlance, coordinated control is the *integrated automation scheme* that treats the **boiler + turbine** as one coupled plant (a *single* MIMO — Multi-Input, Multi-Output — system) instead of two independent control loops. It is the **highest level** of control hierarchy in a fossil-fired unit, sitting above the individual sub-loop controllers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a two-seater **bicycle built for two (a tandem)** where both riders must pedal in sync to move forward smoothly. If the front rider (boiler) pedals too fast, the chain (steam) over-stretches; if the back rider (turbine) pedals too fast, the chain goes slack and the system stalls.

- The **Unit Load Demand (ULD)** = the speed at which the bicycle must travel.
- The **BMC (Boiler Master)** = the front rider generating energy.
- The **TMC (Turbine Master)** = the back rider using the energy.
- The **Unit Coordinator** = the voice of the team captain telling both riders *what to do and when*, balancing energy generation and consumption.

If they both respond to the same instruction at the right moment (i.e., **coordinated control**), the tandem glides smoothly. That is precisely what a power plant does: the energy produced by the boiler must equal the energy consumed by the turbine at every instant.

### 1.3 Why Coordinated Control is Needed

> [!NOTE]
> - A modern 210/500/660/800 MW unit must respond to **grid frequency deviations** in *seconds*.
> - Boiler dynamics are inherently **slow** (10's of seconds to minutes due to fuel combustion, water-wall thermal inertia).
> - Turbine dynamics are inherently **fast** (milliseconds — only valve lag).
> - A **conflict** arises if these are controlled independently: a sudden load demand will cause **drum pressure collapse** (boiler lag) or **over-pressurization** (turbine lag).
> - Coordinated control resolves this by **cross-coupling** the two controllers, so the *fast* element (turbine) handles *fast* transients and the *slow* element (boiler) is pre-emptively readied.

### 1.4 Key Metrics & Constants (KTU Board Emphasis)

| Metric | Typical Value | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Unit ramp rate | **1–3 %/min** (utility), up to **5 %/min** (governor duty) | $\Delta P / \Delta t$ |
| Frequency bias setting | **4–5 %** droop | $R$ |
| Drum pressure dead-band | **±0.5 %** of rated MCR | $\Delta P_{dr}$ |
| Throttle pressure setpoint | **16.67 MPa** (subcritical) / **25 MPa** (supercritical) | $P_{th}$ |
| Energy storage time constant | **3–8 s** (drum) | $\tau_{dr}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Balance Principle — Foundation of Coordinated Control

The control of a boiler–turbine unit is fundamentally a problem of **matching the rate of steam generation to the rate of steam consumption**.

Let:
- $D_B$ = steam flow produced by the boiler (kg/s)
- $D_T$ = steam flow consumed by the turbine (kg/s)
- $P_d$ = drum pressure (MPa)
- $V$ = total stored steam volume in the drum & risers (m³)
- $\rho_s$ = steam density (kg/m³)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

The **unsteady mass-balance** on the water-steam circuit is:

$$D_B(t) - D_T(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\rho_s V\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** If $D_B > D_T$ → pressure **rises**; if $D_B < D_T$ → pressure **falls**. The drum acts as an **integrating element** (a storage tank).

In differential form (assuming $V$ constant and using chain rule):

$$D_B - D_T = V \cdot \frac{d\rho_s}{dt} = V \cdot \frac{\partial \rho_s}{\partial P_d}\bigg|_T \cdot \frac{dP_d}{dt} + V \cdot \frac{\partial \rho_s}{\partial T}\bigg|_{P_d} \cdot \frac{dT}{dt}$$

For a **saturated drum** (temperature slaved to pressure via saturation curve), the second term is implicitly absorbed; the **effective storage coefficient** is:

$$C_d = V \cdot \frac{\partial \rho_s}{\partial P_d}\bigg|_{\text{sat}}$$

So the simplified working equation becomes:

$$D_B - D_T = C_d \cdot \frac{dP_d}{dt}$$

### 2.2 The Three Operating Modes (KTU Highly-Tested)

| Mode | Who Acts First? | Why? | KTU Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Boiler-Following (BF)** | Turbine responds to load demand first, boiler catches up | Protects pressure | **Slow load pickup** — limited ramp rate (≈ 1 %/min) |
| **Turbine-Following (TF)** | Boiler responds to load demand first, turbine holds pressure | Faster pressure recovery | **Boiler lag still slows load** — pressure transient is sharp |
| **Coordinated Control (CC)** | **Both** respond simultaneously via cross-coupling | Best of both worlds | **Complex tuning**; requires good model |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When asked "Why not use only BF or TF?", answer: *"BF and TF are decoupled loops optimized for *one* variable (load *or* pressure). A modern unit must meet **grid frequency codes** which require both load **and** pressure to be tightly regulated — only CC can deliver this."*

### 2.3 Architecture of a Coordinated Control System

A typical modern CC loop has these layers:

1. **Unit Load Demand (ULD)** — input from AGC (Automatic Generation Control) or operator.
2. **Unit Coordinator (UC)** — computes individual demands for boiler and turbine.
3. **Boiler Master Controller (BMC)** — drives fuel, FD/ID fan, feedwater, air, superheater spray.
4. **Turbine Master Controller (TMC)** — drives HP/IP/LP governor valves.
5. **Cross-coupling terms** — *feed-forward* signals from TMC to BMC (and vice-versa).

The energy balance equation translated to control law is:

$$\boxed{\;\mu_B(s) = K_B \cdot \left[\,e_{load}(s) + \alpha \cdot P_{th}(s) - \beta \cdot P_{act}(s)\,\right]\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mu_T(s) = K_T \cdot \left[\,e_{load}(s) - \gamma \cdot P_{th}(s) + \delta \cdot P_{act}(s)\,\right]\;}$$

where:
- $\mu_B$, $\mu_T$ = control valve signals to boiler and turbine
- $e_{load} = P_{dem} - P_{act}$ = load error
- $P_{th}$, $P_{act}$ = throttle pressure demand vs. actual
- $K_B$, $K_T$ = controller gains
- $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ = **cross-coupling coefficients** (tuning knobs)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D_B - D_T = C_d \dfrac{dP_d}{dt}$ | Mass/energy balance on drum | kg/s, MPa, s |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{\Delta f / f_{nom}}{\Delta P / P_{rated}}$ | **Droop / speed regulation** | % / % |
| 3 | $P = D_T \cdot (h_1 - h_2) \cdot \eta_T$ | Turbine power (Rankine form) | MW, kg/s, kJ/kg |
| 4 | $K_{uf} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{act}}{\Delta P_{dem}}$ | **Unit factor** (CC tracking) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_s}{D_T}$ | Steam storage time constant | s |
| 6 | $e_{p} = P_{th}^{sp} - P_{th}^{act}$ | Throttle pressure error | MPa |
| 7 | $e_{L} = P_{dem} - P_{act}$ | Load error | MW |
| 8 | $\mu_{B}^{ff} = K_{ff} \cdot \dfrac{dP_{dem}}{dt}$ | Feed-forward signal to boiler | % |
| 9 | $\Delta f = -K_{gov} \cdot \Delta P_v$ | Governor speed equation | Hz, % |
| 10 | $S_{cc} = \dfrac{P_{act}^{final} - P_{act}^{initial}}{P_{dem}^{step} - P_{act}^{initial}}$ | **Coordinated-control step-response** ratio | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Note:** All setpoint/actual pairs use the convention **{quantity}^{sp}** = setpoint and **{quantity}^{act}** = actual measurement, as standardized in KTU valuation keys.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Grid compliance:** Indian Electricity Grid Code (IEGC) mandates that each 500 MW+ unit must demonstrate ≥ 3 %/min ramp under CC.
- **Plant efficiency:** A well-tuned CC reduces excess air, stabilizes steam temperature, and improves heat rate by **0.5–1.5 %**.
- **Equipment life:** Smooth pressure profiles reduce thermal-cycling fatigue in headers, reducing LP rotor cracking risk.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Drum-Pressure Transfer Function (Linearized)

> **Goal:** Show that the boiler–turbine plant has an **integrating** behaviour between flow mismatch and drum pressure.

**Step 1:** Start from the continuity equation for the drum (control volume $V$):

$$D_B(t) - D_T(t) = \frac{dm_s}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Express stored mass as $m_s = \rho_s(P_d) \cdot V$ (drum volume is fixed; steam density is a function of saturation pressure $P_d$).

$$D_B - D_T = V \cdot \frac{d\rho_s}{dP_d} \cdot \frac{dP_d}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Define the **drum-storage coefficient**:

$$C_d \;=\; V \cdot \frac{d\rho_s}{dP_d}\bigg|_{\text{sat}}$$

For saturated water/steam in a typical 210 MW unit, $V \approx 80$ m³ and $d\rho_s/dP_d \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-2}$ kg/(m³·MPa). Therefore:

$$C_d \approx 80 \times 1.3 \times 10^{-2} = 1.04 \; \text{kg/(s·MPa)} \quad (\text{after unit conversion})$$

A more practical, KTU-accepted expression uses volumetric storage per MPa:

$$C_d \;=\; V \cdot \frac{\partial \rho_s}{\partial P}\bigg|_{\text{sat}} \;\; \text{[kg·s}^{-1}\text{·MPa}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$D_B(s) - D_T(s) = C_d \cdot s \cdot P_d(s)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $P_d(s)$:

$$P_d(s) = \frac{1}{C_d \cdot s} \cdot \left[\,D_B(s) - D_T(s)\,\right]$$

This is the **plant transfer function** for drum pressure w.r.t. flow mismatch — a **pure integrator** (1/s). In a real boiler, transport lag $e^{-\tau_d s}$ and combustion lag $\frac{1}{\tau_f s + 1}$ modify it:

$$G_{plant}(s) = \frac{1}{C_d \cdot s} \cdot \frac{e^{-\tau_d s}}{(\tau_f s + 1)(\tau_w s + 1)}$$

**Step 6:** Add the **turbine** transfer function. Turbine power is roughly proportional to steam flow at (approximately) constant enthalpy drop, so:

$$\frac{P_{act}(s)}{D_T(s)} = K_T \quad \text{(steady-state proportionality)}$$

But valve dynamics give a lag of about 0.1–0.3 s:

$$G_{T}(s) = \frac{K_T}{\tau_v s + 1}$$

**Step 7:** Combine. The **open-loop** relationship between a demand change $\Delta P_{dem}$ and actual MW output is:

$$P_{act}(s) = \frac{K_T}{(\tau_v s + 1)} \cdot D_T(s)$$

But $D_T$ is itself a function of the throttle pressure times the CV (control valve) coefficient:

$$D_T = K_{cv} \cdot \sqrt{P_{th} - P_{cond}} \cdot \mu_v$$

For the *linearized* model (small perturbations around an operating point $P_{th}^*$):

$$\Delta D_T = \left.\frac{\partial D_T}{\partial P_{th}}\right|_* \cdot \Delta P_{th} + \left.\frac{\partial D_T}{\partial \mu_v}\right|_* \cdot \Delta \mu_v$$

Let $K_1 = \partial D_T / \partial P_{th}$ and $K_2 = \partial D_T / \partial \mu_v$. Then:

$$\Delta D_T(s) = K_1 \cdot \Delta P_{th}(s) + K_2 \cdot \Delta \mu_v(s)$$

**Step 8:** Substitute into the energy balance:

$$C_d \cdot s \cdot \Delta P_d(s) = \Delta D_B(s) - K_1 \cdot \Delta P_d(s) - K_2 \cdot \Delta \mu_v(s)$$

**Step 9:** Solve for $\Delta P_d$:

$$\Delta P_d(s) = \frac{\Delta D_B(s) - K_2 \cdot \Delta \mu_v(s)}{C_d \cdot s + K_1}$$

This is a **first-order lag with an integrator offset** (the $K_1$ in the denominator stabilizes it). The plant's open-loop transfer function is therefore:

$$G_P(s) = \frac{\Delta P_d(s)}{\Delta \mu_v(s)} = \frac{-K_2}{C_d \cdot s + K_1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** The boiler acts as a **first-order lag with a dominant integrator**, while the turbine acts as a **fast first-order element**. This *dynamic mismatch* is the fundamental reason coordinated control is necessary — and why its tuning involves a **PI controller on the slow side (boiler)** and a **P/PID controller on the fast side (turbine)**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Tuning Strategy

| Controller | Input (PV) | Output (MV) | Controller Type | Tuning Range (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **BMC** | $e_L + \alpha \cdot e_p$ (load + pressure errors) | Fuel, FD/ID, FW, Air | PI or PID | $K_p$: 0.5–2.0; $T_i$: 30–90 s |
| **TMC** | $e_L - \gamma \cdot e_p$ (load – pressure errors) | HP/IP valve demand | P + lead-lag | $K_p$: 1.0–3.0; lead = 0.5–2 s |
| **UC** | ULD + frequency bias | Individual demands | Lead-lag + gain | $K$: 0.8–1.2; $T_l$: 5–20 s |

The **cross-coupling coefficients** $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ are *tuned* so that under a step load demand:
- The throttle pressure transient is minimized (BMC's pre-emptive action).
- The load ramp matches the demand curve exactly (TMC's fast response).
- No **inverse response** (turbine initial pick-up causes pressure dip that *increases* before boiler recovers) is observed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coordinated Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    AGC[AGC Grid Signal MW] --> UC
    OPR[Operator Setpoint MW] --> UC
    FREQ[Frequency Bias Hz] --> UC
    UC[Unit Coordinator UC]
    PACT[Actual Power MW] --> UC
    PTH_ACT[Throttle Pressure MPa] --> UC
    PTH_SP[Throttle Pressure Setpoint MPa] --> UC
    UC -->|Boiler Demand percent| BMC
    UC -->|Turbine Demand percent| TMC
    BM[Boiler Plant: Fuel Air Feedwater] -->|Steam flow kg per s| DRUM
    DRUM[Drum and Superheater] -->|Throttle Steam| TMC
    TMC -->|Valve Opening percent| TBV
    TBV[HP IP LP Turbine Valves] --> GEN
    GEN[Generator Unit MW] --> PACT
    GEN -->|Step up Transformer| GRID[HV Grid]
    BMC -.Feed Forward cross coupling.-> TMC
    TMC -.Feed Back to BMC.-> BMC
    DRUM -.Pressure Feedback MPa.-> PTH_ACT
    PTH_ACT -.Pressure Error.-> UC
    PTH_SP -.Pressure Setpoint.-> UC
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Response to a Load Step

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Grid as Grid / AGC
    participant UC as Unit Coordinator
    participant BMC as Boiler Master
    participant TMC as Turbine Master
    participant Plant as Boiler-Turbine Plant
    Grid->>UC: Step +5 percent Load
    UC->>TMC: Instant turbine demand (fast)
    UC->>BMC: Pre-emptive boiler demand (slow + feed-forward)
    TMC->>Plant: HP valve opens by delta mu
    Plant-->>UC: Pressure dips (e_p negative)
    Plant-->>UC: Power rises (e_L decreases)
    UC->>TMC: Reduce demand as pressure recovers
    UC->>BMC: Maintain demand for ramp
    BMC->>Plant: Increase fuel and air
    Plant-->>UC: Pressure returns to setpoint
    Plant-->>UC: Power matches new demand
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Inputs: ULD Frequency Bias] --> PROC[Unit Coordinator Processor]
    PROC --> SPLIT[Demand Splitter]
    SPLIT -->|X percent| BPATH[Boiler Path Slow]
    SPLIT -->|Y percent| TPATH[Turbine Path Fast]
    BPATH -->|PI tuning| BCTRL[Boiler Master Controller]
    TPATH -->|P plus lead tuning| TCTRL[Turbine Master Controller]
    BCTRL -->|MV percent| BACTUATOR[Fuel Valve Air Damper FD ID Feedwater Valve]
    TCTRL -->|MV percent| TACTUATOR[HP IP LP Governor Valves]
    BACTUATOR --> BPLANT[Boiler Process Drum Furnace]
    TACTUATOR --> TPLANT[Turbine Generator]
    BPLANT --> COUPLE[Steam Flow Coupling]
    TPLANT --> COUPLE
    COUPLE -->|Pressure MW signals| FB[Feedback Sensors]
    FB --> PROC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the architecture diagram, **solid arrows** are primary control flows; **dotted arrows** are cross-coupling / feed-forward signals. In the sequence diagram, time flows *top-to-bottom*, and each actor's *lifeline* is implicit in the vertical lines.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define coordinated control in a thermal power plant. Why is it preferred over independent boiler and turbine control?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Coordinated control is an integrated control strategy in which the **Boiler Master Controller (BMC)** and **Turbine Master Controller (TMC)** are coupled through a **Unit Coordinator** so that both respond to a single **Unit Load Demand (ULD)**, regulating **load** and **throttle pressure** simultaneously.
- **(1 mark)** It is preferred because: (i) it eliminates the conflict between the *fast* turbine and *slow* boiler, (ii) it improves **ramp rate** to comply with grid codes (3–5 %/min), and (iii) it minimizes **throttle-pressure deviation** during transients.
- **(1 mark)** Independent control would cause either *slow load pickup* (boiler-following) or *large pressure swings* (turbine-following).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the energy-balance equation governing boiler–turbine coordination. Explain the physical meaning of each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(2 marks)** $D_B - D_T = C_d \cdot \dfrac{dP_d}{dt}$
- **(1 mark)** $D_B$ = steam flow produced by the boiler (kg/s); $D_T$ = steam flow consumed by the turbine (kg/s); the LHS is the **mass-flow mismatch**. The RHS shows that any mismatch integrates into a change in **drum pressure** $P_d$, with $C_d$ being the **drum storage coefficient** (kg·s⁻¹·MPa⁻¹). If $D_B > D_T$ the drum pressure rises; if $D_B < D_T$ it falls — the drum behaves as an integrator.

---

### Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE Module Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a coordinated control system** for a 210 MW drum-type thermal unit. Label all major loops and feedback signals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **drum-pressure transfer function** $G_P(s) = \dfrac{\Delta P_d(s)}{\Delta \mu_v(s)}$ starting from the energy-balance equation. Discuss the role of the **drum storage coefficient** $C_d$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block diagram (4 marks) + explanation (3 marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.1** above (the Mermaid architecture). Draw the following hierarchical blocks (KTU expects hand-drawn equivalent in answer sheet):

- **Level 1:** Unit Load Demand (ULD) source — AGC/operator.
- **Level 2:** Unit Coordinator — generates *Boiler Demand* and *Turbine Demand*.
- **Level 3:** Master Controllers — BMC (drives fuel, FD/ID, feedwater, air) and TMC (drives governor valves).
- **Level 4:** Plant — boiler, drum, superheater, turbine, generator.
- **Feedbacks:** $P_{act}$ (MW), $P_{th}^{act}$ (MPa), $T_{sh}$ (°C), drum level.
- **Cross-couplings:** Feed-forward from TMC→BMC (so boiler pre-positions fuel) and from BMC→TMC (so turbine valves track pressure).

**Explanation points (3 marks):**
1. The UC computes the *split* of total demand between boiler and turbine using the principle of energy conservation.
2. Cross-coupling compensates for the **dynamic mismatch** — slow boiler vs. fast turbine.
3. Multiple *inner loops* (fuel flow, air flow, drum level) handle the local control; CC is the *outermost* loop.

**(b) Derivation (5 marks) + discussion (2 marks):**

- Start from $D_B - D_T = C_d \cdot \dfrac{dP_d}{dt}$ — **(1 mark)**
- Substitute $D_T = K_1 \Delta P_d + K_2 \Delta \mu_v$ (linearized turbine model) — **(1 mark)**
- Substitute $D_B = G_B(s) \cdot \Delta \mu_B(s)$ (boiler fuel-to-steam dynamics) and combine — **(1 mark)**
- Solve for $\Delta P_d(s) / \Delta \mu_v(s)$:

$$G_P(s) = \frac{-K_2}{C_d \cdot s + K_1 + G_B(s)}$$  — **(2 marks)**

- **Discussion (2 marks):** $C_d$ is the *energy storage capacity* of the drum; a larger $C_d$ means the drum can buffer larger flow mismatches without large pressure changes (good for transients), but also means *slower* pressure recovery. Typical $C_d$ values are 0.5–2.0 kg·s⁻¹·MPa⁻¹ depending on drum volume and operating pressure.

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks]**
**(a)** Compare **Boiler-Following**, **Turbine-Following**, and **Coordinated Control** modes. For each, state (i) which element acts first, (ii) the ramp-rate capability, and (iii) the typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 MW unit is operating at 400 MW. A step demand of +50 MW is applied. The throttle pressure setpoint is 16.67 MPa and the actual pressure drops to 15.80 MPa momentarily. Calculate **(i)** the load error $e_L$ in MW, **(ii)** the throttle pressure error $e_p$ in MPa, and **(iii)** the mass-flow mismatch $D_B - D_T$ in kg/s, given $C_d = 1.0$ kg·s⁻¹·MPa⁻¹ and that the pressure reaches its minimum in 8 s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison table (5 marks) + applications (2 marks):**

| Feature | Boiler-Following | Turbine-Following | Coordinated |
|---|---|---|---|
| First responder | Turbine | Boiler | Both |
| Ramp rate | **Slow (≈ 1 %/min)** | **Moderate (≈ 2 %/min)** | **Fast (3–5 %/min)** |
| Pressure deviation | Large (poor) | Moderate | **Small (best)** |
| Frequency response | Poor | Fair | **Excellent** |
| Typical use | Base-load, old units | Mid-merit | **Grid-code compliant, modern units** |

**(b) Numerical solution:**

- **(i) Load error (2 marks):**  
  $e_L = P_{dem} - P_{act} = (400 + 50) - 400 = \mathbf{+50 \text{ MW}}$ (at the instant of the step).

- **(ii) Pressure error (2 marks):**  
  $e_p = P_{th}^{sp} - P_{th}^{act} = 16.67 - 15.80 = \mathbf{+0.87 \text{ MPa}}$.

- **(iii) Mass-flow mismatch (3 marks):**  
  Using $D_B - D_T = C_d \cdot \dfrac{dP_d}{dt}$:  
  The pressure drops from 16.67 → 15.80 MPa, a change $\Delta P_d = -0.87$ MPa in 8 s.  
  Average rate: $\dfrac{dP_d}{dt} \approx \dfrac{-0.87}{8} = -0.10875$ MPa/s.  
  Therefore:  
  $$D_B - D_T = 1.0 \times (-0.10875) = \mathbf{-0.10875 \text{ kg/s}}$$  
  (The negative sign confirms the turbine is consuming more steam than the boiler is generating at that instant — exactly the condition coordinated control must correct.)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Coordinated-Control Questions (KTU Examiner Notes):**
> 1. **Confusing the sign of $e_p$:** It is $P_{th}^{sp} - P_{th}^{act}$ (setpoint minus actual). Writing it the other way loses 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting units in the storage coefficient:** $C_d$ is **not** dimensionless — it carries kg·s⁻¹·MPa⁻¹. Examiners specifically check this.
> 3. **Mixing up BF and TF:** Boiler-Following means the **turbine** leads (because the boiler sets the steam flow, and the turbine follows the *resulting* pressure). A surprising number of students get this reversed.
> 4. **Skipping cross-coupling in the diagram:** A CC block diagram *without* the dotted feed-forward arrows from TMC→BMC and BMC→TMC is incomplete; expect a **2-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Treating the drum as a simple lag:** The drum's dominant behaviour is **integrating**, not first-order. The transfer function has a $1/s$ term — failing to identify this loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coordinated control = BMC + TMC, coupled by a Unit Coordinator**, both driven by a common ULD.
- **Energy balance:** $D_B - D_T = C_d \cdot \dfrac{dP_d}{dt}$ — drum is the integrating element.
- **Three modes:** Boiler-Following (slow), Turbine-Following (mid), Coordinated (fast + pressure-stable).
- **Drum plant TF:** first-order lag with integrator offset: $G_P(s) = \dfrac{-K_2}{C_d s + K_1 + G_B(s)}$.
- **Turbine plant TF:** first-order, $\tau_v \approx 0.1$–$0.3$ s.
- **Cross-coupling coefficients** $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ are the *tuning knobs* of CC.
- **Key metrics to memorize:** ramp rate (3–5 %/min), droop (4–5 %), drum-pressure dead-band (±0.5 %).
- **Sign convention:** $e_L = P_{dem} - P_{act}$; $e_p = P_{th}^{sp} - P_{th}^{act}$.
- **Block diagram must show:** ULD → UC → {BMC, TMC} → {Boiler, Turbine} → plant, with cross-coupling arrows and feedbacks of MW, MPa, °C.
- **Grid code driver:** Indian Electricity Grid Code mandates ≥ 3 %/min ramp for ≥ 500 MW units — only CC can deliver this.
- **Real-world impact:** A well-tuned CC improves plant heat rate by **0.5–1.5 %** and significantly extends equipment life.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion Control - Main Pressure Air/Fuel Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Combustion Control - Main Pressure Air/Fuel Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Combustion Control** in a steam power plant is the coordinated regulation of three fundamental mass/energy streams feeding the boiler furnace — **fuel**, **combustion air**, and **flue gas (draft)** — to maintain the boiler's steam output at the desired pressure and flow set-point, while simultaneously maintaining safe, efficient, and environmentally compliant combustion.

Within this overarching strategy, the **Main Pressure Air/Fuel Ratio Control** (also known as the **Boiler-Following Steam Pressure Air/Fuel Ratio Control** or **Fuel/Air-Cross-Limiting Control**) is the specific sub-system that:

1. Uses the **steam header pressure (or boiler steam drum pressure)** as the master **demand variable**.
2. Modulates the **fuel flow** (via the burner/feeder actuator) in direct proportion to the steam demand.
3. Simultaneously modulates the **combustion air flow** (via the FD fan damper/vane) in a **predefined stoichiometric ratio** with the fuel — the so-called **Air/Fuel (A/F) ratio**.
4. Employs **cross-limiting** (lead/lag logic) to ensure that air always *leads* fuel during load increases and fuel always *leads* air during load decreases, thereby preventing:
   - **Fuel-rich transient pockets** (smoke, CO, soot, explosion risk)
   - **Air-rich transient pockets** (heat loss, NOₓ, stack heat loss).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Combustion Control):**
> The Main Pressure A/F Ratio Control is the **primary combustion control loop** required for any pulverised-fuel, oil-fired, or gas-fired utility boiler. Mastery of this loop, the **excess air / O₂ trim loop**, and the **draft (FD/ID) control loop** together constitutes the *core 14-mark KTU Module-4 question*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Carburettor-on-a-Boiler" Picture

Imagine driving a car on a hilly road. Your right foot on the accelerator is the **fuel**, and the air entering through the throttle body is the **air**. To climb a hill smoothly:

- You press the accelerator (more fuel demand).
- The throttle opens (more air demand) — but **not by the exact same amount**; the air has to be slightly **ahead** (lead) so that the fuel always finds oxygen to burn, never forming unburned pockets.
- When you take your foot off (load decrease), the fuel goes down first and air follows slightly later — again, to flush residual fuel.

Now replace the "hill" with the **turbine's steam demand** (the electrical load on the grid), and the "accelerator pedal" with the **steam header pressure error**. The same logic — but in tonnes/hour, not millilitres — is what your boiler's combustion controller is doing **60 times every second**.

## 1.3 The Three Interlocked Loops of Boiler Combustion Control

| Loop | Primary Measurement | Final Control Element | Control Goal |
|:-----|:--------------------|:----------------------|:-------------|
| **1. Fuel Flow Loop** | Steam pressure (header/drum) | Fuel valve / burner damper / feeder speed | Match fuel rate to steam demand |
| **2. Air Flow Loop** | Steam pressure + fuel flow (via A/F ratio) | FD-fan inlet vane / FD-fan speed | Provide stoichiometric + excess air |
| **3. Flue-Gas / Draft Loop** | Furnace pressure (negative draft) | ID-fan inlet damper / speed | Maintain −10 to −25 mmH₂O furnace draft |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **Main-Pressure A/F Ratio Control**, the steam header pressure acts as the *single master demand signal* that simultaneously cascades to both the fuel controller and the air controller via the A/F ratio station — hence the name "main pressure" control.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Steam Pressure is the Master Demand Signal

In a drum-type boiler, the mass of water/steam inside the drum acts as a **thermal capacitor**. If the turbine takes out more steam than the boiler is making:

1. Drum pressure drops.
2. This pressure drop is the *first and fastest* indicator of load increase.
3. The pressure controller (PI/PID) outputs a **higher set-point** to the fuel flow controller.
4. The fuel valve opens, furnace heat release rises, steam generation rises, pressure recovers.

Because pressure responds in **seconds**, while steam flow measurement has unavoidable transport delays (superheater piping lag), pressure is the **fastest closed-loop feedback** for combustion control. This is the fundamental justification for the *Main Pressure* strategy.

## 2.2 The Air/Fuel Ratio — Stoichiometric and Excess

For complete combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel **CₘHₙSₚO_q**, the *theoretical* (stoichiometric) oxygen requirement is:

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = m + \frac{n}{4} + p - \frac{q}{2} \quad \text{[moles of } O_2 \text{ per mole of fuel]}$$

The corresponding **theoretical air requirement** is (since dry air = 21% O₂ + 79% N₂ by volume):

$$A_{\text{theo}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{theo}}}{0.21} \quad \text{[moles of air per mole of fuel]}$$

In practice, **more than stoichiometric air** is supplied to ensure complete combustion — this is the **excess air**, typically **15–25 %** for oil/gas and **20–35 %** for pulverised coal:

$$\% \text{Excess Air} = EA = \frac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_{\text{theo}}}{A_{\text{theo}}} \times 100$$

$$\lambda = \frac{A_{\text{actual}}}{A_{\text{theo}}} = 1 + \frac{EA}{100} \quad \text{(equivalence-ratio reciprocal, a.k.a. air ratio)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The combustion controller aims to keep $\lambda \approx 1.15$ to $1.25$ (15–25 % excess air). Too low → incomplete combustion, smoke, CO, fuel waste. Too high → stack heat loss, NOₓ formation, fan power waste.

## 2.3 The Need for Cross-Limiting (Lead/Lag)

A naive, parallel fuel and air controller would have both loops start moving simultaneously. The problem:

- During a **load-up transient**, the fuel valve and air damper both start opening at the same rate.
- Due to different actuator speeds (fuel valves are often faster than dampers, or vice-versa), one may temporarily run ahead of the other.
- If **fuel leads air** during load-up → momentary fuel-rich flame → puff, smoke, even explosion.
- If **air leads fuel** during load-down → momentary air-rich cold flame → flame instability, possible extinction.

**Cross-limiting** solves this by *dynamically swapping* which loop is the slave:

| Transient Direction | Lead | Lag | Reason |
|:--------------------|:-----|:----|:-------|
| **Load increasing** | **Air** opens first (high-limit on fuel) | Fuel follows after air is available | Prevents fuel-rich condition |
| **Load decreasing** | **Fuel** closes first (low-limit on air) | Air follows after fuel is reduced | Prevents air-flush quenching |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Notes |
|:-:|:--------------------|:--------|:---------------------|:------|
| 1 | Theoretical O₂ | $O_{2,\text{theo}} = m + n/4 + p - q/2$ | mol O₂ / mol fuel | For hydrocarbon CₘHₙSₚO_q |
| 2 | Theoretical air | $A_{\text{theo}} = O_{2,\text{theo}} / 0.21$ | mol air / mol fuel | 21% O₂ in dry air |
| 3 | Excess air % | $EA = (A_{\text{actual}} - A_{\text{theo}})/A_{\text{theo}} \times 100$ | 15 – 35 % | Coal > oil > gas |
| 4 | Air ratio | $\lambda = 1 + EA/100$ | 1.15 – 1.35 | Reciprocal of equivalence ratio $\phi$ |
| 5 | Stack heat loss | $Q_{\text{stack}} = m_{\text{fg}} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{stack}} - T_{\text{ref}})$ | kJ/s | ↑ as $EA$ ↑ |
| 6 | Furnace draft | $P_{\text{furnace}} = -10$ to $-25$ mmH₂O | mmH₂O or Pa | Slight negative to contain flame |
| 7 | Steam pressure set-point | $P_{\text{sp}} = P_{\text{header,nom}}$ | bar (e.g., 135 bar for sub-critical) | Master controller output |
| 8 | Lead-limit on fuel | $F_{\text{max}} = \alpha \cdot A_{\text{measured}}$ | kg/s | $\alpha$ = inverse of A/F ratio |
| 9 | Lag-limit on air | $A_{\text{min}} = (1/\alpha) \cdot F_{\text{measured}}$ | kg/s | Air cannot go below fuel demand |
| 10 | Combustion efficiency (heat-loss method) | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = 100 - (L_{\text{stack}} + L_{\text{moist}} + L_{\text{CO}} + L_{\text{rad}})$ | % | Siegert formula |

> [!NOTE]
> In the **KTU board exam**, questions on this topic frequently ask you to (a) draw the block diagram with cross-limiting, (b) explain the lead/lag logic, (c) derive the air requirement from fuel composition, and (d) comment on the role of O₂ trim.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The Main-Pressure A/F Ratio Control is the **heart of every utility boiler** worldwide — from a 210 MW Kayamkulam (NTPC) oil-fired unit to a 800 MW ultra-supercritical coal unit at Tirupati (APGENCO). Without it, neither load following on the grid nor statutory emission norms (NOₓ, CO, particulates) can be met. Modern DCS implementations (ABB 800xA, Siemens SPPA-T3000, Honeywell Experion) package these loops as "**CombustionMaster**" or "**BoilerMaster**" function blocks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Air for a Typical Furnace Oil

Consider a typical heavy furnace oil (HFO) used in Indian power stations with the empirical formula approximated as **CH₁.₆S₀.₀₂O₀.₀₂** (carbon-hydrogen-sulphur-oxygen). One kilogram-mole of this fuel has **m = 1**, **n = 1.6**, **p = 0.02**, **q = 0.02**.

### Step 1 — Theoretical O₂ requirement per mole of fuel

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = m + \frac{n}{4} + p - \frac{q}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = 1 + \frac{1.6}{4} + 0.02 - \frac{0.02}{2}$$

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = 1 + 0.4 + 0.02 - 0.01$$

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = 1.41 \quad \text{mol O}_2 \text{ per mol of fuel}$$

> Step 1 explanation: This is the standard stoichiometric equation for a generic hydrocarbon CₘHₙSₚO_q. The fuel is first *de-oxygenated* (subtract q/2 to remove intrinsic O), then the remaining C, H, and S are oxidised to CO₂, H₂O, and SO₂ respectively.

### Step 2 — Theoretical air per mole of fuel

$$A_{\text{theo}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{theo}}}{0.21} = \frac{1.41}{0.21}$$

$$A_{\text{theo}} = 6.714 \quad \text{mol air per mol of fuel}$$

> Step 2 explanation: Dry air contains 21 % O₂ by volume (and 79 % N₂ + trace gases). To deliver 1.41 moles of O₂, you need 1.41 / 0.21 = 6.714 moles of air.

### Step 3 — Convert to mass basis

Molar mass of fuel ≈ $12(1) + 1(1.6) + 32(0.02) + 16(0.02) = 13.52$ kg/kg-mol.
Molar mass of air ≈ 28.97 kg/kg-mol.

$$\frac{A_{\text{theo, mass}}}{\text{kg fuel}} = 6.714 \times \frac{28.97}{13.52} = 14.39 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

### Step 4 — With 20 % excess air

$$A_{\text{actual}} = 1.20 \times A_{\text{theo}} = 1.20 \times 6.714 = 8.057 \quad \text{mol air / mol fuel}$$

Or in mass:

$$A_{\text{actual, mass}} = 1.20 \times 14.39 = 17.27 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}$$

> Step 4 explanation: The boiler is supplied with 20 % more air than stoichiometrically required to guarantee complete combustion. This 20 % buffer is a designer choice — oil-fired boilers operate efficiently at ~15-20 % EA, while coal-fired boilers need ~25-35 %.

## 3.2 Derivation: Air/Fuel Ratio Setpoint at Different Loads

Let the **boiler steam output** vary from 60 % to 100 % MCR (Maximum Continuous Rating). The A/F ratio *setpoint* is generally held **constant** in modern boilers (constant $\lambda$ philosophy), but in some older boilers it is varied as:

$$\lambda(\dot{m}_{\text{steam}}) = \lambda_0 \cdot \left[ 1 + k \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{steam}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{MCR}}}\right) \right]$$

where $k \approx 0.05$–$0.10$ is the *low-load enrichment factor* used to keep the flame stable at low loads. The Main-Pressure controller multiplies the fuel demand by $\lambda$ to compute the air demand.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Cross-Limiting on Transient Safety

| Scenario | Without Cross-Limit (Naive) | With Cross-Limit (Main-Pressure A/F) |
|:---------|:----------------------------|:-------------------------------------|
| Sudden 5 % load ↑ | Fuel ↑ first by ~0.5 s, then air ↑ → momentary black smoke, possible puff | Air ↑ immediately; fuel capped by $F_{\max} = \alpha \cdot A_{\text{meas}}$ until air catches up |
| Sudden 5 % load ↓ | Air ↓ first → cold-flame extinction for ~1 s, CO spike | Fuel ↓ immediately; air capped by $A_{\min} = F_{\text{meas}} / \alpha$ |
| FD-fan surge | Air overshoots → fuel controller unaware, $\lambda$ drifts up → NOₓ, stack loss | High-limit block on air re-asserts fuel slave → A/F ratio restored |
| Fuel valve sticking | Air overshoots unburned fuel pocket → next instant explosion risk | Low-limit block on air; flame monitor trips burner (separate safety interlock) |

## 3.4 Derivation: Why Furnace Draft (FD/ID) Must be Co-Controlled

The furnace pressure is governed by the **mass balance** of air in and flue gas out. For steady state:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{air, in}} \approx \dot{m}_{\text{flue gas, out}}$$

Transiently, the ID-fan damper is driven by a separate PI controller whose **PV** is the furnace pressure transducer (−10 to −25 mmH₂O). Its **CV** is the ID-fan inlet vane. The FD and ID controllers are *de-coupled* via the furnace pressure loop so that increasing FD air does not blow flame out of the furnace (which would happen at $P_{\text{furnace}} \geq +5$ mmH₂O).

For a steady-state furnace pressure $P_f$ in mmH₂O:

$$\Delta P_{\text{furnace}} = P_{\text{furnace}} - P_{\text{outside}} = K_1 \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{air}} - K_2 \cdot \dot{m}_{\text{flue gas}}$$

where $K_1$ and $K_2$ are aerodynamic conductance constants. The ID controller tunes $\dot{m}_{\text{flue gas}}$ to keep the right-hand side equal to the set-point (negative).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU exam, when a question asks for "combustion control block diagram", you MUST include all three loops (fuel, air, draft) and the cross-limit logic — a single-loop diagram will earn only partial credit.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State the role of excess air in boiler combustion. Why is excess air kept around 15–25 % for oil-fired boilers?**

**Model Answer:**

Excess air is the quantity of air supplied **beyond the stoichiometric requirement** for complete combustion of the fuel. Its role is to:

1. Guarantee complete combustion of all fuel particles (no CO, no soot).
2. Provide a buffer against A/F mismatch during transients.
3. Keep the flame temperature below the ash-fusion point (for coal).

For oil-fired boilers, **15–25 % excess air** is maintained because:
- Oil atomises and vaporises easily → near-complete combustion achievable at low EA.
- Lower EA → higher flame temperature → better efficiency.
- Too little EA (<10 %) → risk of unburnt hydrocarbon smoke and CO.
- Too much EA (>30 %) → excessive NOₓ formation and stack heat loss.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "cross-limiting" in boiler combustion control and explain its necessity.**

**Model Answer:**

Cross-limiting is a logic technique applied to the fuel and air controllers such that during a **load-up transient**, the **air loop leads** (high-limits the fuel demand), and during a **load-down transient**, the **fuel loop leads** (low-limits the air demand).

**Necessity:**

- Without cross-limiting, fuel-rich pockets form during load-ups → smoke, CO, possible puff/explosion.
- Without cross-limiting, air-rich quenching occurs during load-downs → flame instability/extinction.
- It guarantees that the A/F ratio stays within the safe combustion envelope at all times, even before the slower damper actuator can catch up with the faster fuel valve.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Main Pressure Air/Fuel Ratio Control** scheme for an oil-fired boiler. Clearly show the master pressure controller, the fuel loop, the air loop, the A/F ratio station, and the cross-limiting logic.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A boiler burns furnace oil with empirical formula **CH₁.₈S₀.₀₃**. Calculate: (i) theoretical O₂ in mol/mol fuel, (ii) theoretical air in mol/mol fuel, (iii) theoretical air in kg/kg fuel, (iv) actual air supplied with 18 % excess air in kg/kg fuel.

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation**

Refer to the master block diagram in Section 4.1. The scheme operates as follows:

1. The **steam header pressure** $P_{\text{meas}}$ is compared to the set-point $P_{\text{sp}}$. The error $e = P_{\text{sp}} - P_{\text{meas}}$ is fed to a **PI Pressure Controller (PC)**.
2. PC outputs the **fuel demand signal** $F_D$.
3. $F_D$ is sent in **two parallel paths**:
   - **Path 1 (Fuel path):** $F_D$ → Fuel Valve Controller (FVC) → Fuel Valve actuator → Burner.
   - **Path 2 (Air path):** $F_D$ → **A/F Ratio Station (λ)** → Air Demand $A_D$ → **Cross-Limit Block** → Air Valve Controller (AVC) → FD-fan inlet damper.
4. The A/F ratio station multiplies $F_D$ by $\lambda$ (typically 1.15–1.25) to generate $A_D$.
5. The cross-limit block uses the **measured** air-flow signal $A_{\text{meas}}$ and **measured** fuel-flow signal $F_{\text{meas}}$ to enforce:
   - High-limit on fuel: $F_{\text{out}} = \min(F_D, \alpha \cdot A_{\text{meas}})$
   - Low-limit on air: $A_{\text{out}} = \max(A_D, F_{\text{meas}} / \alpha)$
6. The furnace heat release raises the steam pressure, closing the loop.
7. **O₂ trim** (a secondary slow loop) measures stack O₂ and trims $\lambda$ to maintain ~2–3 % O₂ in the flue gas.

**Part (b) — Stoichiometric Calculation**

Given: fuel formula **C₁H₁.₈S₀.₀₃** (i.e., m = 1, n = 1.8, p = 0.03, q ≈ 0).

#### (i) Theoretical O₂ per mole of fuel

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = m + \frac{n}{4} + p - \frac{q}{2}$$

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = 1 + \frac{1.8}{4} + 0.03 - 0$$

$$O_{2,\text{theo}} = 1 + 0.45 + 0.03$$

$$\boxed{O_{2,\text{theo}} = 1.48 \quad \text{mol O}_2 / \text{mol fuel}}$$

#### (ii) Theoretical air per mole of fuel

$$A_{\text{theo}} = \frac{O_{2,\text{theo}}}{0.21} = \frac{1.48}{0.21}$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{theo}} = 7.048 \quad \text{mol air / mol fuel}}$$

#### (iii) Theoretical air in kg per kg fuel

Molar mass of fuel:
$$M_{\text{fuel}} = 12(1) + 1(1.8) + 32(0.03) = 12 + 1.8 + 0.96 = 14.76 \quad \text{kg/kg-mol}$$

Mass of theoretical air per kg-mol of fuel:
$$m_{\text{air,theo}} = 7.048 \times 28.97 = 204.18 \quad \text{kg air / kg-mol fuel}$$

Mass of theoretical air per kg of fuel:
$$\boxed{A_{\text{theo, mass}} = \frac{204.18}{14.76} = 13.83 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

#### (iv) Actual air supplied with 18 % excess air

$$A_{\text{actual, mass}} = (1 + 0.18) \times 13.83$$

$$\boxed{A_{\text{actual, mass}} = 16.32 \quad \text{kg air / kg fuel}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **necessity of cross-limiting** in combustion control. With the help of a sketch, describe how it is implemented during a load-up transient and a load-down transient.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A coal-fired boiler has the following ultimate analysis: **C = 62 %, H = 4 %, S = 1 %, O = 8 %, N = 1 %, Ash = 10 %, Moisture = 14 %** (by mass). Calculate (i) theoretical air in kg/kg coal, (ii) actual air supplied with 30 % excess air, (iii) flue-gas mass per kg coal (assuming stoichiometric combustion products only).

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Cross-Limiting Necessity & Implementation**

Already covered conceptually in Section 2.3 and Section 3.3. Implementation summary for the answer:

- **Load-up transient:** Pressure drops → PC raises $F_D$. The **fuel valve is not allowed to open beyond $F_{\max} = A_{\text{meas}} / \lambda$**, i.e., until the measured air flow has actually risen to support the requested fuel. This is a *high-limit* on the fuel demand.
- **Load-down transient:** Pressure rises → PC lowers $F_D$. The **air damper is not allowed to close beyond $A_{\min} = \lambda \cdot F_{\text{meas}}$**, i.e., until the measured fuel flow has actually dropped. This is a *low-limit* on the air demand.
- The **implementation** is via two **selectors** (high-select and low-select function blocks) inside the DCS, plus the A/F ratio station.

**Part (b) — Coal Combustion Calculation**

Working on **1 kg of dry coal (excluding moisture)** since ultimate analysis is reported on dry basis. For simplicity, take basis = 1 kg of coal as-fired including moisture (mass fractions).

Per 1 kg of coal: C = 0.62, H = 0.04, S = 0.01, O = 0.08, N = 0.01, Ash = 0.10, Moisture = 0.14 kg.

#### (i) Theoretical O₂ requirement per kg of coal

The oxygen supplied by the fuel itself = 0.08 kg.
Moles of C in 1 kg coal = 0.62 / 12 = 0.0517 kmol.
Moles of H₂ (since H in fuel is reported as kg of H atoms; for H₂O formation we treat H as 2H → H₂) = (0.04 / 2) / 2 = 0.0100 kmol H₂.
Moles of S = 0.01 / 32 = 0.0003125 kmol.

O₂ required:
- For C: $0.0517$ kmol O₂
- For H₂: $0.0100 / 2 = 0.0050$ kmol O₂
- For S: $0.0003125$ kmol O₂

Total O₂ needed = $0.0517 + 0.0050 + 0.0003 = 0.0570$ kmol O₂.

O₂ available in fuel = 0.08 / 32 = 0.0025 kmol O₂.

Net O₂ from air = 0.0570 − 0.0025 = 0.0545 kmol O₂ = **1.554 kg O₂** (since 32 kg/kmol).

Theoretical air = O₂ / 0.232 (mass basis; air has 23.2 % O₂ by mass).

$$\boxed{A_{\text{theo, mass}} = \frac{1.554}{0.232} = 6.70 \quad \text{kg air / kg coal}}$$

#### (ii) Actual air with 30 % excess air

$$\boxed{A_{\text{actual, mass}} = 1.30 \times 6.70 = 8.71 \quad \text{kg air / kg coal}}$$

#### (iii) Mass of flue gas per kg coal

Inlet air = 8.71 kg (of which O₂ = 8.71 × 0.232 = 2.021 kg, N₂ = 8.71 × 0.768 = 6.689 kg).

Stoichiometric products per kg coal:
- CO₂ from C: 0.62 × (44/12) = 2.273 kg.
- H₂O from H (combustion): 0.04 × (18/2) = 0.36 kg; plus moisture = 0.14 kg; total H₂O in flue gas = 0.36 + 0.14 = 0.50 kg.
- SO₂ from S: 0.01 × (64/32) = 0.020 kg.
- N₂ from fuel: 0.01 × (28/28) = 0.010 kg; plus N₂ from air = 6.689 kg; total N₂ = 6.699 kg.
- Excess O₂ in flue gas = 2.021 − 1.554 = 0.467 kg.

Total flue gas mass per kg coal:

$$m_{\text{flue gas}} = 2.273 + 0.500 + 0.020 + 6.699 + 0.467$$

$$\boxed{m_{\text{flue gas}} = 9.959 \approx 9.96 \quad \text{kg / kg coal}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using volume % (21 %) instead of mass % (23.2 %)** for air composition in mass-basis calculations — yields wrong answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract fuel's intrinsic oxygen** when applying the stoichiometric O₂ formula — loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Drawing only a single-loop block diagram** without the cross-limit logic — examiner expects the cross-limit and O₂ trim blocks clearly labelled.
> 4. **Confusing the direction of cross-limit** — remember: during load-up, **air leads, fuel lags**; during load-down, **fuel leads, air lags**.
> 5. **Forgetting the moisture content** in coal while calculating flue-gas mass — causes ~1 kg error in flue-gas mass per kg coal.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Combustion control has **three interlocked loops**: **Fuel, Air, Draft (FD/ID)**.
- ✅ The **steam header (or drum) pressure** is the **master demand signal** in the Main-Pressure A/F Ratio Control.
- ✅ The **A/F ratio station** multiplies the fuel demand by $\lambda$ (1.15–1.35) to compute the air demand.
- ✅ **Cross-limiting** prevents fuel-rich transients (load-up) and air-flush transients (load-down).
- ✅ **Stoichiometric O₂** for CₘHₙSₚO_q = $m + n/4 + p - q/2$ mol O₂ / mol fuel.
- ✅ **Theoretical air** = $O_{2,\text{theo}} / 0.21$ (volume basis) or $O_{2,\text{theo}} / 0.232$ (mass basis).
- ✅ **Excess air** for oil = 15–25 %; for coal = 25–35 %; for gas = 10–15 %.
- ✅ **O₂ trim** is a slow secondary loop that adjusts $\lambda$ to keep stack O₂ ≈ 2–3 %.
- ✅ **Furnace draft** is held at −10 to −25 mmH₂O by the ID-fan damper.
- ✅ **Cross-limit implementation** uses two **selector blocks** (high-select and low-select) in the DCS.
- ✅ **Stack heat loss** rises with excess air; **incomplete-combustion loss** falls with excess air — optimum is at the minimum of the sum.
- ✅ In exam answers, always **label the cross-limit block, the A/F ratio station, and the O₂ trim feedback** in the block diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Furnace Draft — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Furnace Draft** is defined as the *small negative pressure (below atmospheric)* maintained inside the furnace/combustion chamber of a boiler relative to the surrounding atmospheric pressure. This negative pressure is what drives the flow of air into the furnace and the flow of combustion gases (flue gases) out through the chimney/stack.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$\Delta P_{\text{draft}} = P_{\text{furnace}} - P_{\text{atmosphere}} \quad \text{(typically negative, expressed in mmWC or Pa)}$$

The standard unit of measurement in the Indian power plant industry is **mm of Water Column (mmWC)**. The conversion is:

$$1 \text{ mmWC} \approx 9.806 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.0394 \text{ inWC}$$

Typical operating draft in a pulverized coal-fired boiler of 210 MW capacity lies in the range of **−3 mmWC to −10 mmWC** at the furnace throat, and may be as deep as **−25 mmWC to −50 mmWC** at the ID fan suction.

> [!NOTE]
> A negative sign convention is universally adopted: **−ve sign implies suction (vacuum)**, while +ve sign implies positive pressure inside the furnace.

---

## 1.2 Why Draft is Needed — Physical Reasoning

Three simultaneous objectives must be satisfied inside a boiler furnace:

1. **Air Supply** — Continuous inflow of combustion air (O₂) for the fuel.
2. **Flue Gas Removal** — Continuous evacuation of hot combustion products.
3. **Operator & Equipment Safety** — Prevention of flame/hot gas puff-back into the working area, and prevention of structural damage to the furnace walls.

Without controlled negative pressure, hot gases would leak through openings, ash hoppers, observation ports and inspection doors, endangering personnel and equipment.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Vacuum Cleaner Principle"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of your household vacuum cleaner. The fan inside creates a pressure slightly *lower* than atmospheric pressure at the suction nozzle. This negative pressure (draft) "sucks" dust and air into the cleaner. Similarly, a furnace draft system creates a controlled vacuum inside the boiler that pulls combustion air in through the FD fan and pushes the flue gases out through the ID fan and stack. The bigger the pressure difference, the stronger the "suction" — but too strong a draft can also pull heat out of the furnace before it transfers to the water walls!

---

## 1.4 Types of Furnace Draft — Classification

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects a clear understanding of **four categories** of draft systems used in thermal power plants:

### (a) Natural Draft
- Achieved purely by the **buoyancy effect** of hot flue gases rising through a tall chimney.
- No moving parts; no electrical power consumption for draft generation.
- Requires very tall stacks (≈ 100–275 m in modern supercritical units).
- Used in older installations and in oil/gas-fired plants where fuel flow rates are moderate.

### (b) Forced Draft (FD)
- A **Forced Draft Fan** (typically an axial-flow fan) located *upstream* of the boiler pushes preheated air into the furnace under **positive pressure**.
- Furnace operates under **+ve pressure** (a few mmWC).
- Risk of hot gas leakage if pressure control fails — hence limited to small/medium boilers.

### (c) Induced Draft (ID)
- A **Induced Draft Fan** is located *downstream* of the boiler (before the stack).
- It creates suction that pulls flue gases out of the furnace.
- Furnace operates under **−ve pressure**.

### (d) Balanced Draft (BD) — Most common in modern thermal power plants
- **Combination of FD + ID fans** working together.
- FD fan pressurizes wind box → pushes air through burners → creates +ve pressure at burner zone.
- ID fan creates suction at the back end → removes flue gases.
- Net furnace pressure is maintained at **slightly negative (≈ −2 to −5 mmWC)** throughout the furnace, providing safety + flow control.
- Standard configuration for all pulverized coal-fired boilers of 60 MW and above.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** A *balanced draft* boiler is *not* a boiler with zero net pressure. It is a boiler in which FD and ID fans **balance** the air-gas flow such that the **net furnace pressure is slightly negative** (≈ −2 to −5 mmWC) for safety. The word "balanced" refers to the *flow* balance, not the *pressure* balance.

---

## 1.5 Excess Air — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Excess Air (EA)** is the quantity of air supplied to the combustion process in *excess* of the **theoretically (stoichiometric) required air** for complete combustion of the fuel.

$$\% \, \text{Excess Air} = \frac{\text{Actual Air Supplied} - \text{Theoretical Air Required}}{\text{Theoretical Air Required}} \times 100$$

A related term **Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR)** is often quoted:

$$\text{AFR}_{\text{actual}} = (1 + e) \cdot \text{AFR}_{\text{stoich}} \quad \text{where } e = \text{excess air fraction (e.g., 0.15 for 15% EA)}$$

### Why Excess Air Cannot Be Zero in Practice
- **Mixing Imperfection:** Fuel and air are never 100% homogeneously mixed at the molecular level inside a furnace.
- **Time Constraint:** Combustion must complete within the furnace residence time (≈ 2–4 seconds).
- **Fuel Bound Nitrogen** and **Volatile Matter** in coal demand extra oxygen.
- Therefore, a controlled surplus of 10–30% is always provided.

### Typical % Excess Air Values (Boards Favourite)

| Fuel Type | Recommended % EA | Resulting % O₂ in dry flue gas |
|---|---|---|
| Natural Gas | 5 – 10 % | 1.0 – 2.0 % |
| Furnace Oil (LSHS) | 5 – 15 % | 1.0 – 3.0 % |
| Bituminous Coal (PC fired) | 15 – 25 % | 3.0 – 5.0 % |
| Lignite (high moisture) | 25 – 35 % | 5.0 – 6.5 % |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Students frequently confuse "**% O₂ in flue gas**" with "**% Excess Air**". They are **not** the same! For a coal-fired boiler with 4% O₂ in dry flue gas, the corresponding excess air is approximately **22–24%**, not 4%.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Natural Draft — Theoretical Foundation

Natural draft is generated entirely by the **density difference** between the hot flue gas column inside the stack and the colder atmospheric air column outside. Treating both as static fluid columns:

$$\Delta P_{\text{ND}} = H \, g \, (\rho_{\text{air,amb}} - \rho_{\text{gas,avg}})$$

Where:
- $H$ = effective chimney height (m)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $\rho_{\text{air,amb}}$ = density of ambient air (kg/m³)
- $\rho_{\text{gas,avg}}$ = average density of hot flue gas in stack (kg/m³)

A more practical working formula (using ideal gas law and ambient temperature reference) is:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{\text{ND}} = 353 \, H \left( \frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g} \right) \quad \text{[mmWC]}}$$

Where:
- $H$ = chimney height in **metres**
- $T_a$ = absolute ambient air temperature in **K**
- $T_g$ = average absolute flue gas temperature in the stack in **K**
- 353 = combined constant = $(p_a \, g \, M_{\text{air}}) / (R \cdot 1000) \cdot (1 - M_{\text{gas}}/M_{\text{air}})$ simplified for standard pressure and average molecular mass of flue gas.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Natural draft *increases* with (a) taller chimneys, (b) higher flue gas temperatures, and (c) lower ambient temperatures. In cold winter conditions, natural draft is strongest; in hot summer, it weakens — this is why tall stacks are mandatory for plants relying solely on natural draft.

---

## 2.2 Forced & Induced Draft — Fan Laws

Modern FD and ID fans are **centrifugal** or **axial** type. Their operating point is determined by the system resistance curve. The fundamental power consumption equation for a fan is:

$$P_{\text{fan}} = \frac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of gas (m³/s)
- $\Delta P$ = total pressure rise developed by the fan (Pa)
- $\eta_{\text{fan}}$ = fan efficiency (typically 75–85%)

### Fan Affinity Laws (Kerala Board Favourite)

| Law | Speed Change | Geometrically Similar Fans |
|---|---|---|
| Flow $\propto$ Speed | $Q_2 / Q_1 = N_2 / N_1$ | $Q \propto N D^3$ |
| Pressure $\propto$ Speed² | $\Delta P_2 / \Delta P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$ | $\Delta P \propto N^2 D^2$ |
| Power $\propto$ Speed³ | $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | $P \propto N^3 D^5$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Favourite:** A 5% increase in fan speed leads to a **15.76%** increase in power consumption ($1.05^3 = 1.1576$). This cubic relationship is what makes fan speed control the most energy-efficient throttling method compared to damper control.

---

## 2.3 Balanced Draft — Pressure Zones Inside a Boiler

A 210 MW tangentially-fired balanced-draft boiler has *three distinct pressure zones*:

1. **Wind Box / FD Fan Discharge:** **+150 to +400 mmWC** (high positive pressure, downstream of FD fan).
2. **Furnace (combustion zone):** **−2 to −5 mmWC** (slightly negative for safety).
3. **Economizer outlet / Air Heater outlet / ID fan suction:** **−50 to −250 mmWC** (deepest negative).

The pressure gradient drives:
- Air flow: **Atmosphere → FD Fan → Wind Box → Burners → Furnace**
- Flue gas flow: **Furnace → Boiler Bank → Economizer → Air Heater → ID Fan → ESP → Stack → Atmosphere**

---

## 2.4 Excess Air — Stoichiometric Combustion Calculations

For a fuel with ultimate analysis given on a **mass basis** as C, H, O, S, N (in %), the theoretical O₂ required per kg of fuel is:

$$O_{2,\text{theor}} = \frac{32}{12}C + \frac{32}{4}H - \frac{32}{32}O + \frac{32}{32}S \quad \text{[kg O₂ per kg fuel]}$$

The theoretical air required is:

$$A_{\text{theor}} = \frac{100}{23} \cdot O_{2,\text{theor}} \quad \text{(since dry air is 23% O₂ by mass)}$$

The actual air supplied is:

$$A_{\text{actual}} = A_{\text{theor}} \cdot (1 + e)$$

The **mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel** (for complete combustion) is:

$$m_g = \frac{C}{100} \cdot \frac{44}{12} + \frac{H}{100} \cdot \frac{18}{2} \cdot (\text{as water}) + \frac{S}{100} \cdot \frac{44}{32} + \frac{N_{\text{air}} \cdot 0.79 + N_{\text{fuel}}}{100} \cdot 28 + \text{Excess O}_2 \cdot 32$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU exam, only dry-flue-gas analysis (Orsat) is asked. The Orsat apparatus measures **CO₂, O₂, and CO** in flue gas (water vapour is condensed out). **N₂ is obtained by difference**: $N_2 = 100 - (CO_2 + O_2 + CO)$.

---

## 2.5 The Three Universal Excess Air Formulas (MUST memorize)

### Formula 1 — From Orsat % O₂ (for hydrocarbon fuels with negligible fuel-N₂):

$$\boxed{\% \, EA = \frac{21 \cdot O_2}{21 - O_2} = \frac{20.9 \cdot O_2}{20.9 - O_2}}$$

The factor 20.9 (or 21) replaces 23 (mass basis) because Orsat readings are on a **mole/volumetric basis** (dry air is 20.9% O₂ by volume).

### Formula 2 — General Orsat-based formula (works for all fuels including coal):

$$\boxed{\% \, EA = \frac{O_2 - \tfrac{1}{2}CO}{0.264 \, N_2 - \left(O_2 - \tfrac{1}{2}CO\right)} \times 100}$$

The constant **0.264 = (79/21) × (32/28) × (21/100) — actually it's derived as the ratio (N₂/O₂) at 0% EA**. The exact derivation:

$$0.264 = \frac{79}{21} \times \frac{28}{32} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{N_2^{\text{dry}}}{O_2^{\text{stoich}}}$$

### Formula 3 — From % CO₂ alone (for a given fuel, useful when only CO₂ meter is installed):

$$\% \, EA = \frac{\%CO_{2,\text{max}} - \%CO_{2,\text{actual}}}{\%CO_{2,\text{actual}}} \times 100$$

Where $\%CO_{2,\text{max}}$ is the theoretical maximum CO₂ in dry flue gas for that specific fuel (≈ 20.2% for pure carbon, ≈ 11.7% for methane, etc.).

---

## 2.6 High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need for the KTU ESE on this topic. Pay special attention to units — examiners deduct marks for unit mismatches.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P_{\text{ND}} = 353 H \left(\tfrac{1}{T_a} - \tfrac{1}{T_g}\right)$ | Natural draft pressure | mmWC |

| 3 | $P_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta}$ | Fan shaft power | W |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why This Concept Matters |
|---|---|
| **Boiler efficiency optimization** | Each 1% reduction in excess air ≈ 0.1–0.2% gain in boiler efficiency. |
| **NOx emission control** | Lower excess air ⇒ lower thermal NOx (extended Zeldovich mechanism). |
| **Fan power savings** | ID fan accounts for ~4–6% of auxiliary power. Optimal draft control saves crores per year. |
| **Furnace pressure transients** | Furnace implosion/explosion protection: if pressure goes below −50 mmWC, ID fan must trip. |
| **Coal mill primary air** | FD PA fans supply air for coal drying + transportation — closely linked to FD control. |
| **ESP performance** | Excess air affects flue gas volume, hence ESP collection efficiency. |
