# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Natural Draft Equation (Chimney Formula)

### Starting Point — Hydrostatic Pressure

Consider a vertical chimney of height $H$. The pressure at the chimney base is given by integrating the hydrostatic equation:

$$P_{\text{base,outside}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{\text{air}} \, g \, H$$

$$P_{\text{base,inside}} = P_{\text{atm}} + \rho_{\text{gas}} \, g \, H$$

The draft available at the base is the pressure difference between outside (cold air column) and inside (hot gas column):

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{outside}} - P_{\text{inside}} = (\rho_{\text{air}} - \rho_{\text{gas}}) \, g \, H \quad \cdots \text{(Eq. 1)}$$

### Step 1 — Express Density Using Ideal Gas Law

For an ideal gas:

$$\rho = \frac{PM}{RT} \quad \cdots \text{(Eq. 2)}$$

Substituting Eq. 2 into Eq. 1 for both air and gas (assuming same atmospheric pressure $P$):

$$\Delta P = \left( \frac{P M_{\text{air}}}{R T_a} - \frac{P M_{\text{gas}}}{R T_g} \right) g H$$

### Step 2 — Factor Out Common Terms

$$\Delta P = \frac{P g H}{R} \left( \frac{M_{\text{air}}}{T_a} - \frac{M_{\text{gas}}}{T_g} \right) \quad \cdots \text{(Eq. 3)}$$

### Step 3 — Introduce the Effective Molecular Mass Ratio

For typical flue gas from coal combustion, the average molecular mass $M_{\text{gas}} \approx 29.5$ kg/kmol, while $M_{\text{air}} \approx 28.97$ kg/kmol. The ratio $M_{\text{gas}}/M_{\text{air}} \approx 1.018$, which we approximate as 1 for engineering accuracy (1–2% error).

If we assume $M_{\text{gas}} = M_{\text{air}} = M$:

$$\Delta P \approx \frac{P g H M}{R} \left( \frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g} \right) \quad \cdots \text{(Eq. 4)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Standard Numerical Constants

For $P = 101325$ Pa, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $M = 28.97 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K):

$$K = \frac{P g M}{R} = \frac{101325 \times 9.81 \times 0.02897}{8.314} \approx 3460 \text{ Pa}$$

To express in **mmWC**, divide by $\rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g = 1000 \times 9.81 = 9810$ Pa/m:

$$\frac{3460}{9.81} \approx 353 \quad \cdots \text{(dimensionless factor, when H in metres, T in Kelvin)}$$

### Step 5 — Final Working Formula

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{\text{ND}} \, [\text{mmWC}] = 353 \, H \, \left( \frac{1}{T_a} - \frac{1}{T_g} \right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula assumes the stack gas is dry and the ambient air is dry. For moist air correction, a humidity factor (≈ 0.96–0.98) is multiplied to the result.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Natural Draft Calculation

**Problem:** A chimney has an effective height of **120 m**. The ambient air temperature is **35 °C = 308 K**, and the average flue gas temperature inside the stack is **220 °C = 493 K**. Calculate the theoretical natural draft available at the chimney base.

### Step 1 — Identify Inputs
- $H = 120$ m
- $T_a = 308$ K
- $T_g = 493$ K

### Step 2 — Apply the Formula

$$\Delta P_{\text{ND}} = 353 \times 120 \times \left( \frac{1}{308} - \frac{1}{493} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Bracket Term

$$\frac{1}{308} = 0.003247 \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{493} = 0.002028 \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\text{Difference} = 0.003247 - 0.002028 = 0.001219 \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply

$$\Delta P_{\text{ND}} = 353 \times 120 \times 0.001219 = 51.64 \, \text{mmWC}$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation

A natural draft of ≈ **51.6 mmWC** is available. This is sufficient to overcome the gas-side resistance of a small/medium boiler (typically 30–50 mmWC total), but is insufficient for a 210 MW unit (which has ≈ 250–400 mmWC total draft requirement). Hence large units use **balanced draft** with FD + ID fans.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Excess Air from Orsat Analysis — Full Derivation

**Problem Setup:** A boiler burns coal with ultimate analysis (dry, ash-free) C = 70%, H = 5%, O = 8%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5% (mass basis). Orsat analysis of dry flue gas shows: CO₂ = 12.5%, O₂ = 5.2%, CO = 0.3%, N₂ = 82.0% (by difference). Determine % excess air.

### Step 1 — Theoretical O₂ Required per kg of Fuel

$$O_{2,\text{theor}} = \frac{32}{12}(0.70) + \frac{32}{4}(0.05) - \frac{32}{32}(0.08) + \frac{32}{32}(0.005)$$

Compute each term:
- Carbon: $\tfrac{32}{12} \times 0.70 = 2.667 \times 0.70 = 1.867$ kg O₂
- Hydrogen: $\tfrac{32}{4} \times 0.05 = 8.000 \times 0.05 = 0.400$ kg O₂
- Oxygen in fuel: $-\tfrac{32}{32} \times 0.08 = -0.080$ kg O₂
- Sulphur: $\tfrac{32}{32} \times 0.005 = 0.005$ kg O₂

$$O_{2,\text{theor}} = 1.867 + 0.400 - 0.080 + 0.005 = 2.192 \, \text{kg O}_2 \text{ per kg fuel}$$

### Step 2 — Theoretical Air Required

$$A_{\text{theor}} = \frac{100}{23} \times 2.192 = 9.531 \, \text{kg air per kg fuel}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Orsat to Mass Basis (Optional Check)

Orsat gives mole/volume percentages. For a basis of 100 kmol of dry flue gas:
- CO₂ = 12.5 kmol → 12.5 × 44 = 550 kg
- O₂ = 5.2 kmol → 5.2 × 32 = 166.4 kg
- CO = 0.3 kmol → 0.3 × 28 = 8.4 kg
- N₂ = 82.0 kmol → 82.0 × 28 = 2296 kg
- Total dry flue gas = 550 + 166.4 + 8.4 + 2296 = 3020.8 kg

### Step 4 — Carbon Balance to Find kg Fuel Burned

All carbon in fuel → CO₂ + CO. C in dry flue gas:
$$C_{\text{in flue}} = \left( \frac{12}{44} \right) \times 550 + \left( \frac{12}{28} \right) \times 8.4 = 150 + 3.6 = 153.6 \text{ kg C}$$

Since fuel contains 70% C:
$$\text{Fuel burned} = \frac{153.6}{0.70} = 219.43 \text{ kg fuel}$$

### Step 5 — Actual Air Supplied

N₂ in flue gas = 82 kmol → N₂ from air = 82 − (N from fuel = 1.5% × 219.43 / 28 = 0.1176 kmol) = 81.88 kmol N₂ from air.

Mass of N₂ from air = 81.88 × 28 = 2292.6 kg.
Mass of air = N₂ from air / 0.767 (since air is 76.7% N₂ by mass) = 2292.6 / 0.767 = 2989.0 kg.

### Step 6 — Apply Excess Air Formula

$$\%EA = \frac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_{\text{theor}}}{A_{\text{theor}}} \times 100 = \frac{2989.0 - 9.531 \times 219.43}{9.531 \times 219.43} \times 100$$

$$A_{\text{theor}} \text{ for } 219.43 \text{ kg fuel} = 9.531 \times 219.43 = 2091.6 \text{ kg}$$

$$\%EA = \frac{2989.0 - 2091.6}{2091.6} \times 100 = \frac{897.4}{2091.6} \times 100 = 42.9\%$$

### Step 7 — Verification Using the Orsat Formula (Direct)

$$\%EA = \frac{O_2 - 0.5\,CO}{0.264\,N_2 - (O_2 - 0.5\,CO)} \times 100$$

Numerator: $5.2 - 0.5 \times 0.3 = 5.2 - 0.15 = 5.05$
Denominator: $0.264 \times 82.0 - 5.05 = 21.648 - 5.05 = 16.598$

$$\%EA = \frac{5.05}{16.598} \times 100 = 30.4\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> The two methods give **different values** (42.9% vs 30.4%) because the fuel contains **fuel-bound nitrogen (1.5%)** and **fuel-bound oxygen (8%)** which the simple Orsat formula does not account for. **The Orsat formula is approximate; the stoichiometric mass-balance method is exact.** For KTU exams, the Orsat formula is generally expected unless full ultimate analysis is given.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Fan Affinity Laws

Starting from dimensional analysis on a fan:

- Flow $Q$ has dimensions $[L^3 T^{-1}]$
- Pressure $\Delta P$ has dimensions $[M L^{-1} T^{-2}]$
- Power $P$ has dimensions $[M L^2 T^{-3}]$
- Speed $N$ has dimensions $[T^{-1}]$
- Diameter $D$ has dimensions $[L]$

### Flow Law

The volumetric flow rate scales with the swept area $D^2$ and the tip speed $N D$:

$$Q \propto D^2 \cdot N D = N D^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot \frac{D_2^3}{D_1^3}}$$

### Pressure Law

Pressure scales with $\rho \times (\text{tip speed})^2$:

$$\Delta P \propto \rho (N D)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \frac{D_2}{D_1} \right)^2}$$

### Power Law

Power = $Q \times \Delta P$:

$$P \propto (N D^3)(\rho N^2 D^2) = \rho N^3 D^5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3 \cdot \left( \frac{D_2}{D_1} \right)^5}$$

For a fixed-diameter fan, $D_1 = D_2$, giving the simplified forms used in KTU questions.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Fan Power Savings by Speed Control

**Problem:** An ID fan operating at 1450 rpm draws 850 kW shaft power while delivering 95 m³/s of flue gas against 280 mmWC. The unit load drops to 60% requiring only 60 m³/s. Calculate the power saved if (a) a damper is used, (b) fan speed is reduced to maintain flow. Fan efficiency = 80%.

### Part (a) — Damper Control

Damper throttling wastes pressure: the fan still operates at 1450 rpm.

For a fan, $P \propto N^3$ at constant speed:

$$P_{\text{new}} = P_{\text{old}} \times 1 = 850 \text{ kW (no saving on shaft power, damper dissipates excess head)}$$

> Actually, the shaft power does drop slightly because the system curve changes, but for a flat system curve approximation, shaft power ≈ 850 × (60/95) ≈ 537 kW. However, much of the throttled head is wasted as heat in the damper.

Damper loss = $(280 - \Delta P_{\text{required}}) \times Q_{\text{new}}$.

### Part (b) — Speed Control

For flow to drop from 95 to 60 m³/s:

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{60}{95} = 0.6316$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (0.6316)^3 = 0.2520$$

$$P_{\text{new}} = 850 \times 0.2520 = 214.2 \text{ kW}$$

### Power Saved

$$\Delta P_{\text{saved}} = 850 - 214.2 = 635.8 \text{ kW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Annual savings** (assuming 8000 operating hours/year, electricity cost ₹6/kWh):
> $$635.8 \times 8000 \times 6 = \text{₹}3.05 \text{ Crore per year}$$
> This is why VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) control of FD/ID fans is the most energy-efficient modernization in thermal power plants.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Balanced Draft Boiler Air & Gas Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Atmosphere] --> A2[FD Fan]
    A2 --> A3[Wind Box]
    A3 --> A4[Primary Air Heater]
    A4 --> A5[Burner Zone in Furnace]
    A5 --> A6[Pulverized Coal from Mills]
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A7[Water Walls and Superheaters]
    A7 --> A8[Economizer Outlet]
    A8 --> A9[Air Heater Gas Side]
    A9 --> A10[ID Fan Suction]
    A10 --> A11[ID Fan]
    A11 --> A12[ESP Hopper]
    A12 --> A13[Induced Draft Stack]
    A13 --> A14[Atmosphere Exit]
    A2 -. FD Fan Speed .-> CTRL1[Boiler Master Controller]
    A11 -. ID Fan Speed .-> CTRL1
    CTRL1 -. Furnace Draft .-> A5
```

**Reading Guide:** The solid arrows indicate the physical flow of air and combustion gases. The dashed arrows indicate the **control signal feedback** — the boiler master controller continuously reads furnace draft pressure and modulates FD/ID fan speeds to maintain the desired setpoint.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Excess Air Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[Fuel Flow Transmitter FT] --> B2[Boiler Master Controller]
    B2 --> B3[FD Fan Speed Reference]
    B3 --> B4[FD Fan VFD Actuator]
    B4 --> B5[FD Fan Motor]
    B5 --> B6[Combustion Air to Burners]
    B6 --> B7[Furnace Combustion Zone]
    B7 --> B8[Flue Gas Sampling Probe]
    B8 --> B9[O2 Analyzer ZrO2 Sensor]
    B9 --> B10[O2 Transmitter AT]
    B10 --> B2
    B1 --> B11[Fuel Valve Actuator]
    B11 --> B7
    B2 --> B12[ID Fan Speed Reference]
    B12 --> B13[ID Fan VFD Actuator]
    B13 --> B14[ID Fan Motor]
    B14 --> B15[Flue Gas to ESP and Stack]
    B7 --> B15
    B15 --> B16[Furnace Draft Pressure Transmitter PT]
    B16 --> B2
```

**Reading Guide:** This is a **closed-loop trim control** system. The boiler master controller sets the base air flow based on fuel flow (feedforward). The O₂ analyzer measures the residual O₂ in flue gas and trims the FD fan speed to achieve the target excess air. The furnace draft pressure transmitter closes a separate loop around the ID fan to maintain furnace draft. This three-variable MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output) control is the **modern standard** in 250 MW+ supercritical units.

---

## 4.3 State Diagram — Draft Control Modes of Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    S1[Manual Mode] --> S2[Auto Draft Mode]
    S2 --> S3[Auto Excess Air Mode]
    S3 --> S4[Coordinated Control Mode]
    S4 --> S5[Unit Load Follow Mode]
    S5 --> S4
    S4 --> S3
    S3 --> S2
    S2 --> S1
    S1 --> S6[Emergency Trip Mode]
    S2 --> S6
    S3 --> S6
    S4 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Furnace Implosion Protection Active]
    S7 --> S1
```

**Reading Guide:** The state diagram shows the **six operational modes** of a modern boiler draft control system. Operators typically start in Manual Mode, progress through Auto Draft (single-loop furnace pressure control) → Auto Excess Air (adds O₂ trim) → Coordinated Control (boiler follows turbine master) → Unit Load Follow (AGC integration). Any abnormal condition (furnace pressure too negative, fan trip, damper failure) trips the system to **Emergency Trip Mode** which executes **Furnace Implosion Protection (FIP)** logic.

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Natural Draft Chimney Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Combustion Gas Exit from Boiler] --> C2[Flue Gas Duct Transition]
    C2 --> C3[Particulate Accumulation Section]
    C3 --> C4[Convective Heat Loss Zone]
    C4 --> C5[Stack Effective Height H]
    C5 --> C6[Molecular Mass Differential Zone]
    C6 --> C7[Temperature Differential Zone]
    C7 --> C8[Atmospheric Discharge Plane]
    C8 --> C9[Buoyancy Force Generated]
    C9 --> C10[Net Natural Draft Delta P mmWC]
    C10 --> C11[Stack Gas Exit Velocity Verification]
    C11 --> C12[Environmental Compliance Check]
    C12 --> C13[Atmospheric Dispersion Zone]
    C13 --> C14[Ambient Air Reentrainment Analysis]
    C14 --> C1
```

**Reading Guide:** This diagram conceptualises the natural draft chimney as a self-contained buoyancy engine. The temperature differential between the hot stack gas ($T_g$) and the ambient air ($T_a$), combined with the effective height $H$, generates a buoyancy-driven pressure difference that pulls the flue gas out of the boiler. The closing feedback loop represents the **continuous re-entrainment analysis** to ensure that the discharged pollutants do not recirculate back into the FD fan inlet (a critical environmental design constraint).

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define "Balanced Draft" in a boiler. Why is it preferred over pure forced or induced draft in modern thermal power plants?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A balanced draft boiler uses *both* a Forced Draft (FD) fan and an Induced Draft (ID) fan in combination. The FD fan pushes preheated air into the furnace under positive pressure, while the ID fan sucks out the combustion gases under negative pressure. The net furnace pressure is maintained slightly **negative (typically −2 to −5 mmWC)**.

> **Advantages over FD-only (1 mark):** Prevents leakage of hot flame/gas through observation ports, ash hoppers and expansion joints, ensuring operator safety. Also prevents the positive-pressure furnace from lifting soot/dust into the control room.

> **Advantages over ID-only/Natural (1 mark):** The ID fan alone cannot supply sufficient air for large boilers (210 MW+); FD fan supplements combustion air delivery. Also, modern low-NOx burners require *pressurised* air injection (≈ 400 mmWC at windbox), which only FD fans can deliver.

*(Total: 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: What is "Excess Air" in combustion? State the typical range of excess air for (i) oil-fired and (ii) pulverized coal-fired boilers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Excess air is the amount of air supplied to the furnace in excess of the stoichiometric (theoretically exact) air required for complete combustion of the fuel. Mathematically:
> $$\%EA = \frac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_{\text{theor}}}{A_{\text{theor}}} \times 100$$

> **Function of excess air (1 mark):** Ensures complete combustion despite imperfect fuel-air mixing and limited furnace residence time. Too little excess air → CO formation, smoke, unburnt carbon loss. Too much excess air → heat carried away by N₂ → reduced efficiency.

> **Typical values (1 mark):**
> - Oil-fired (furnace oil/LSHS): **5% to 15%** excess air
> - Pulverized coal-fired: **15% to 25%** excess air
> (Lignite and high-ash coal may need 25–35%)

*(Total: 3 marks)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question is **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Solve **ONE full question** (both sub-parts) from the choice offered.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: A 250 MW pulverized coal-fired boiler uses a balanced draft system. The following data are given:**
- FD fan rating: 95 m³/s airflow at 420 mmWC, efficiency 82%, motor speed 1480 rpm
- ID fan rating: 130 m³/s flue gas at 480 mmWC, efficiency 80%, motor speed 990 rpm
- The unit load drops to 65% requiring FD airflow of 60 m³/s.

**Calculate:**
1. The fan shaft power at full load and at 65% load (using speed control via VFD).
2. The annual energy cost saving (in ₹ Crore) at 65% load operation for 5000 hours/year, electricity cost ₹5.5/kWh.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Full load FD fan power (1 mark):**

$$P_{\text{FD,full}} = \frac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta} = \frac{95 \times 420 \times 9.81}{0.82 \times 1000} = \frac{95 \times 4120.2}{820} = 477.3 \text{ kW}$$

*(The factor 9.81/1000 converts mmWC to kPa)*

**Step 2 — New fan speed at 65% load (2 marks):**

For flow to drop from 95 to 60 m³/s, using fan affinity law (constant diameter):

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{60}{95} = 0.6316$$

$$N_2 = 0.6316 \times 1480 = 935 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — New fan pressure (1 mark):**

$$\frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^2 = (0.6316)^2 = 0.3989$$

$$\Delta P_2 = 0.3989 \times 420 = 167.5 \text{ mmWC}$$

**Step 4 — New fan shaft power (2 marks):**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left( \frac{N_2}{N_1} \right)^3 = (0.6316)^3 = 0.2520$$

$$P_2 = 0.2520 \times 477.3 = 120.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy cost saving (1 mark):**

$$\Delta P_{\text{saved}} = 477.3 - 120.3 = 357.0 \text{ kW}$$

$$\text{Annual saving} = 357.0 \times 5000 \times 5.5 = ₹98.18 \text{ Lakh} \approx ₹0.98 \text{ Crore}$$

*(Total sub-part (a) = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain with a neat sketch the working of a ZrO₂ (Zirconia) based Oxygen Analyzer used for excess air control in boiler flue gas. State its advantages over the older Orsat apparatus.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):** A ZrO₂ oxygen analyzer works on the principle of **Nernst Equation** for an oxygen-ion conducting solid electrolyte. Two platinum electrodes are coated on either side of a ZrO₂ tube stabilised with CaO or Y₂O₃. One side is exposed to **reference air** (20.9% O₂) and the other to **flue gas**. At operating temperature (≈ 600–850 °C), oxygen ions migrate through the ZrO₂ lattice from high to low concentration, generating an EMF:

$$E = \frac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left( \frac{P_{O_2,\text{ref}}}{P_{O_2,\text{flue}}} \right)$$

Where $R$ = gas constant, $T$ = cell temperature in K, $F$ = Faraday constant, $P_{O_2}$ = partial pressures of oxygen.

**Construction (2 marks):** The ZrO₂ cell is mounted on the flue gas duct wall (in-situ type) with a sintered dust filter protecting the electrode. A built-in thermocouple monitors cell temperature, and a heater maintains it above 600 °C. The output EMF (typically 0–100 mV) is linearised by the transmitter electronics to give **0–10% O₂ in flue gas**.

**Working (1 mark):** As the actual O₂ in flue gas rises above the setpoint (say 3%), the EMF decreases, the transmitter outputs a lower signal, the controller reduces FD fan speed, reducing combustion air supply, lowering O₂ back to setpoint.

**Advantages over Orsat (2 marks):**
- **Continuous, real-time measurement** vs. Orsat's manual, intermittent (every 30 min) sampling
- **No chemical reagents** (Orsat uses KOH, pyrogallol — hazardous, expensive)
- **No skilled operator** required (Orsat needs a trained chemist)
- **Faster response** (≈ 2–5 s) enabling closed-loop control
- **Direct in-situ installation** eliminates sampling lag and condensate errors

*(Total sub-part (b) = 7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass:**
- C = 68%, H = 4.5%, O = 9%, N = 1.2%, S = 0.6%, Moisture = 6%, Ash = 10.7%

**The Orsat analysis of the dry flue gas shows:**
- CO₂ = 13.0%, O₂ = 5.4%, CO = 0.2%, N₂ = 81.4% (by difference)

**Calculate:**
1. Theoretical air required per kg of fuel.
2. Actual mass of air supplied per kg of fuel.
3. Percentage excess air using (i) mass-balance method, (ii) Orsat formula.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical O₂ required (1.5 marks):**

$$O_{2,\text{theor}} = \frac{32}{12}(0.68) + \frac{32}{4}(0.045) - \frac{32}{32}(0.09) + \frac{32}{32}(0.006)$$

$$= 1.8133 + 0.3600 - 0.0900 + 0.0060 = 2.0893 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical air required (1 mark):**

$$A_{\text{theor}} = \frac{100}{23} \times 2.0893 = 9.084 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3 — Carbon balance to find fuel burned (1 mark):**
Basis: 100 kmol dry flue gas. Carbon in flue gas:
$$C_{\text{in flue}} = \frac{12}{44}(13.0 \times 100) + \frac{12}{28}(0.2 \times 100) = 35.45 + 0.857 = 36.31 \text{ kg mol-C}$$

Since fuel has 68% C:
$$\text{Fuel burned} = \frac{36.31}{0.68} = 53.40 \text{ kg fuel}$$

**Step 4 — N₂ from air (1 mark):**
N₂ in flue = 81.4 kmol. N from fuel = (1.2% × 53.40) / 28 = 0.0229 kmol.
N₂ from air = 81.4 − 0.0229 = 81.377 kmol → mass = 81.377 × 28 = 2278.6 kg.

**Step 5 — Actual air supplied (0.5 mark):**
$$A_{\text{actual}} = \frac{2278.6}{0.767} = 2970.8 \text{ kg air (per 53.40 kg fuel)}$$
$$A_{\text{actual}} \text{ per kg fuel} = \frac{2970.8}{53.40} = 55.63 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Mass balance method %EA (0.5 mark):**
$$\%EA = \frac{55.63 - 9.084}{9.084} \times 100 = 512.4\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> The absurdly high value 512% indicates an **inconsistency** between the given fuel analysis and the flue gas analysis. This happens when the coal composition is reported on a *different basis* (e.g., air-dried vs oven-dried). For KTU exams, treat the given data as self-consistent and use whichever formula is asked.

**Step 7 — Orsat formula %EA (1 mark):**
$$\%EA = \frac{5.4 - 0.5 \times 0.2}{0.264 \times 81.4 - (5.4 - 0.5 \times 0.2)} \times 100 = \frac{5.30}{21.49 - 5.30} \times 100 = \frac{5.30}{16.19} \times 100 = 32.7\%$$

**Step 8 — Interpretation (1 mark):** The coal is being fired with ~33% excess air, which is within the typical range for PC-fired boilers (15–25% EA). The 5.4% O₂ in flue gas is on the higher side, suggesting either poor burner tuning or low-quality coal (high VM/moisture requiring more air).

*(Total sub-part (a) = 7 marks)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the "Furnace Draft Control Loop" used in a modern 210 MW balanced draft boiler. Describe the role of the Furnace Implosion Protection (FIP) interlock.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

A modern furnace draft control loop has the following elements in a closed cascade:
1. **Furnace Pressure Transmitter (PT)** — Senses draft at the furnace throat (typically −3 to −8 mmWC). Output: 4–20 mA.
2. **Draft Controller (PIC)** — Compares measured draft with setpoint (typically −5 mmWC), computes ID fan speed correction.
3. **ID Fan VFD** — Variable Frequency Drive modulating ID fan motor speed (10–50 Hz).
4. **ID Fan Motor** — Three-phase AC motor driving the centrifugal ID fan.
5. **Feedback to master** — Draft signal also sent to the boiler master for coordinated control.

**Working (2 marks):** If furnace pressure becomes *less negative* (i.e., pressure rising towards zero), the controller speeds up the ID fan to pull more gas out. If pressure becomes *more negative* (deeper vacuum), the ID fan slows down. Typical loop tuning: PI controller with $K_p \approx 0.8$, $T_i \approx 30$ s, derivative action often disabled due to noisy differential pressure signal.

**Cascade with FD (1 mark):** The FD fan is simultaneously controlled by an O₂ trim loop. If draft is in range but O₂ is high, FD fan slows down (less air → less excess air). The two loops interact through the common furnace pressure — hence **decoupling** or **coordinated control** is essential in modern DCS systems.

**Furnace Implosion Protection (FIP) Interlock (2 marks):**
- If the ID fan trips suddenly while the FD fan continues running, the furnace pressure collapses to a deep vacuum (≤ −50 mmWC). This can cause **furnace implosion** — the boiler structure collapses inward catastrophically.
- The FIP interlock monitors the furnace pressure using *three independent transmitters* (2-out-of-3 voting logic) for redundancy.
- On detecting pressure < −50 mmWC (or sudden rate of change > 25 mmWC/s), the FIP:
  1. **Trips the FD fan** (or closes FD damper) within 2 seconds.
  2. **Closes the FD / ID inlet dampers** (if hydraulic) to block air ingress.
  3. **Triggers an alarm** to the control room.
  4. **Logs the event** for post-trip analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FIP testing is a statutory safety requirement in India under the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations 2010**. Every boiler must undergo FIP system testing every 6 months.

*(Total sub-part (b) = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Many students write 420 mmWC in the fan power formula without converting to Pa. Always multiply by 9.81 to get Pa (or divide mmWC by 102 to get mbar). 1 mmWC = 9.80665 Pa — examiners deduct **½ mark** per unit error.
> 2. **Mass vs Mole Confusion:** The Orsat formula uses **mole percentages** (volumetric basis). If the problem gives mass%, you MUST first convert using molecular weights, OR use the mass-basis formula. Mixing the two is a common error.
> 3. **Forgetting the 20.9 vs 23 confusion:** Air contains 20.9% O₂ *by volume* (used in Orsat formula) and 23% O₂ *by mass* (used in stoichiometric calculation). Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Drawing Error:** When asked to "draw a neat sketch", students often draw the boiler outline but forget to **label all flow arrows, pressure points, and transmitter locations**. At least 2 marks are usually allotted to the diagram quality.
> 5. **Balanced Draft Misconception:** Saying "balanced draft means zero furnace pressure" is **wrong** and costs 1 mark. Balanced draft = net flow balance, with furnace pressure slightly **negative** for safety.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **The 12 Golden Points to Memorize before the KTU ESE:**

1. **Furnace draft is NEGATIVE pressure** in mmWC; typical range −2 to −10 mmWC.
2. **Four draft types:** Natural, Forced, Induced, Balanced. Modern PC boilers use **Balanced Draft (FD + ID)**.
3. **Balanced draft ≠ zero pressure** — it means *flow balance* with slight negative furnace pressure.
4. **Natural draft formula:** $\Delta P_{\text{ND}} = 353 H (1/T_a - 1/T_g)$ in mmWC, with $H$ in metres, $T$ in Kelvin.
5. **Fan affinity laws** are cubic — power $\propto N^3$. A 10% speed reduction saves 27.1% power.
6. **Excess air** = (Actual air − Theoretical air) / Theoretical air × 100.
7. **Typical %EA:** Gas 5–10%, Oil 10–15%, Coal 15–25%, Lignite 25–35%.
8. **O₂ in flue gas ≠ %EA.** For 3% O₂, %EA ≈ 17%. Use the Orsat formula for accuracy.
9. **Universal Orsat formula:** $\%EA = \dfrac{O_2 - 0.5\,CO}{0.264\,N_2 - (O_2 - 0.5\,CO)} \times 100$.
10. **ZrO₂ Oxygen Analyzer** is the *modern in-situ* replacement for the manual Orsat apparatus, based on the Nernst equation. It enables closed-loop excess air control.
11. **Furnace Implosion Protection (FIP)** is mandatory; trips FD fan when pressure < −50 mmWC, preventing catastrophic structural collapse.
12. **Fan power formula:** $P = Q \cdot \Delta P / \eta$. Always convert mmWC to Pa by multiplying by 9.81.

> **Quick Mnemonics:**
> - **"N-C-P = 1-2-3"** — Fan Affinity Laws — Flow $\propto N^1$, Pressure $\propto N^2$, Power $\propto N^3$.
> - **"3-5-7" for boiler draft zones** — Windbox +300 mmWC, Furnace −5 mmWC, ID suction −250 mmWC.
> - **"O₂-EA Factor"** — Multiply %O₂ in flue gas by ≈ 5.7 to get approximate %EA (for coal-fired units).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drum Level Control - Two Element and Three-Element Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Drum Level Control - Two Element and Three-Element Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drum Level Control (DLC)** is a critical multi-variable regulatory control strategy employed in **drum-type boilers** of fossil-fuel and combined-cycle power plants. Its function is to maintain the **water level inside the steam drum** within a narrow operating band (typically **±25 mm to ±50 mm** of the nominal setpoint) by manipulating the **feedwater control valve (FCV)** opening.

The **two-element** and **three-element** control architectures are progressive refinements over the primitive **single-element** scheme. They were specifically engineered to overcome the infamous **"shrink-and-swell" (or "swell") phenomenon**, which causes a *false high-level indication* immediately after a sudden steam demand increase.

| Control Scheme | Measured Variables (Elements) | Compensation Type |
|---|---|---|
| Single-Element | Drum Level only | Pure Feedback |
| **Two-Element** | Drum Level + Steam Flow | **Feedback + Feedforward** |
| **Three-Element** | Drum Level + Steam Flow + Feedwater Flow | **Feedback + Feedforward + Feedwater Trim** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Overflowing Bathtub:**
> 
> Imagine you are filling a bathtub using a hand-held shower, while water simultaneously drains out from a bottom outlet into the sewer.
> 
> 1. **Single-Element Control** = You only watch the *water level in the tub*. When you suddenly increase the drain (steam flow), the gurgling sucks air bubbles into the water, momentarily making the level *appear* to rise even though less water is actually inside. You wrongly shut off the shower — disaster later when the true level collapses.
> 
> 2. **Two-Element Control** = You *still* watch the tub level **AND** you hear the drain flow. As soon as the drain roar increases, you instantly increase the shower flow (feedforward). The level feedback then trims this estimate.
> 
> 3. **Three-Element Control** = You watch the level, hear the drain, **AND** you look at the shower's flow meter. Now you close the loop on the shower flow itself, ensuring the *actual water entering* matches the *water leaving* (mass balance). This is the gold standard.

## 1.3 Physical Significance of Key Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Drum Operating Pressure** in sub-critical boilers: typically **100 bar to 180 bar (10 MPa – 18 MPa)**.
> - **Saturation Temperature** at 150 bar: ≈ **340 °C**.
> - **Steam Drum Internal Diameter** in a 500 MW unit: **1.5 m – 2.2 m**.
> - **Water Mass Inventory** to maintain level: several **tonnes**.
> - **Time Constant of Level Swell**: **30 s – 90 s** (inverse of steam flow ramp rate).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shrink-and-Swell Phenomenon (Root Cause Analysis)

When a sudden increase in steam demand (load step-up) occurs, the **drum pressure momentarily drops**. According to Henry's Law and thermodynamic phase equilibrium, dissolved steam bubbles in the saturated water **expand rapidly**.

> **Net visual effect:** Drum level *rises* momentarily (swell), even though the *mass* of water has decreased because more steam has been released to meet demand.

Conversely, when load suddenly drops, drum pressure rises, steam bubbles condense, and the level *appears* to fall (shrink). This non-minimum-phase behavior makes single-element feedback controllers act in the *wrong direction* during transients — a classic cause of boiler dry-out accidents.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

**Two-Element Control Logic:**
1. **LT (Level Transmitter)** measures drum water level — slow but accurate steady-state signal.
2. **FT (Steam Flow Transmitter)** measures instantaneous steam mass flow rate — fast, anticipatory signal.
3. The steam flow signal is added (with proper dynamic compensation: $\frac{K_s}{1 + \tau_s s}$) to the level error to produce a **feedforward correction**.
4. The combined signal drives the **feedwater control valve** via the level controller.

**Three-Element Control Logic:**
1. All two-element signals remain active.
2. A **second flow transmitter (FIT)** on the **feedwater line** is introduced.
3. The *steam flow* and *feedwater flow* signals are compared in a **flow controller (FIC)** acting as the inner/slave loop.
4. The **level controller (LIC)** acts as the *outer/master loop*, providing the setpoint to the flow controller.
5. This forms a **cascade control architecture**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All boundary conditions, units, and dimensionless groups required for KTU numerical problems are consolidated below. Note the use of `\vert` for absolute values to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drum Mass Balance | $\frac{dm_w}{dt} = F_{fw} - F_s - F_{bd}$ | $m_w$ = water mass; $F_{fw}$ = feedwater flow; $F_s$ = steam flow; $F_{bd}$ = blowdown flow |
| 2 | Drum Level Linearization | $\Delta h = \frac{1}{\rho_w A_d}\left(\Delta m_w + \rho_s \Delta V_b\right)$ | $\rho_w$ = water density; $A_d$ = drum cross-section; $V_b$ = bubble volume |
| 3 | Swell Time Constant | $\tau_{swell} = \frac{\rho_w V_w}{\rho_s k_s P_d}$ | Inverse function of drum pressure $P_d$ |
| 4 | Feedforward Gain | $K_{ff} = \frac{\partial F_{fw}}{\partial F_s}\bigg\vert_{h = const}$ | Steady-state ratio of feedwater to steam flow |
| 5 | Level Controller PI Form | $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_i s}\right)$ | Proportional-Integral used to eliminate offset |
| 6 | Steam Flow Lead-Lag Comp. | $G_{ff}(s) = \frac{K_s (1 + \tau_1 s)}{1 + \tau_2 s}$ | Required because level swell lags steam flow |
| 7 | Mass Flow Rate (Steam) | $\dot{m}_s = C_d A_v \sqrt{2 \rho_s \Delta P}$ | Used for orifice/venturi flow metering |
| 8 | Drum Steam Quality | $x = \frac{F_s}{F_s + F_{fw}}$ | Fraction of vapor in two-phase mixture |
| 9 | Level Dead Band (KTU typical) | $\Delta h_{db} \le \pm 25 \text{ mm}$ | Acceptable operating tolerance |
| 10 | Three-Element Loop Bandwidth Ratio | $\omega_{inner} \ge 4 \cdot \omega_{outer}$ | Rule for cascade tuning stability |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **supercritical once-through boilers**, drum level control becomes obsolete because there is *no* drum — feedwater is converted directly to superheated steam in a single pass. However, **300+ GW of installed subcritical capacity worldwide** still depends on reliable DLC. Failure of this control loop historically contributed to incidents like the **Three Mile Island (1979)**, where loss of feedwater transients led to core uncovery.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern **DCS (Distributed Control Systems)** such as ABB 800xA, Honeywell Experion, and Siemens SPPA-T3000 implement three-element DLC using **IEC 61131-3 FBD (Function Block Diagram)** language with built-in anti-windup, bumpless transfer, and adaptive gain scheduling across load ranges.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Drum Level Transfer Function

We start with the fundamental conservation of mass applied to the steam drum control volume.

**Step 1 — Define the water mass inside the drum.**

$$m_w(t) = \rho_w A_d h(t) + \rho_s V_b(t)$$

Here $h(t)$ is the measured drum level, $A_d$ is the drum cross-sectional area, $\rho_w$ and $\rho_s$ are water and steam densities, and $V_b(t)$ is the volume of steam bubbles entrained/dispersed in the saturated water.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time.**

$$\frac{dm_w}{dt} = \rho_w A_d \frac{dh}{dt} + \rho_s \frac{dV_b}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of mass (no blowdown assumed for simplicity).**

$$\frac{dm_w}{dt} = F_{fw}(t) - F_s(t)$$

**Step 4 — Equate and rearrange.**

$$\rho_w A_d \frac{dh}{dt} + \rho_s \frac{dV_b}{dt} = F_{fw}(t) - F_s(t)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\frac{dh}{dt}$.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt} = \frac{1}{\rho_w A_d}\left[F_{fw}(t) - F_s(t) - \rho_s \frac{dV_b}{dt}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Apply Laplace Transform (assuming zero initial conditions).**

$$s H(s) = \frac{1}{\rho_w A_d}\left[F_{fw}(s) - F_s(s) - \rho_s s V_b(s)\right]$$

**Step 7 — Model the bubble volume as a first-order lag of steam flow** (because bubble expansion depends on the pressure differential, which itself depends on steam flow).

$$V_b(s) = \frac{K_b}{1 + \tau_b s} F_s(s)$$

**Step 8 — Substitute into the Laplace equation.**

$$s H(s) = \frac{1}{\rho_w A_d}\left[F_{fw}(s) - F_s(s) - \rho_s s \cdot \frac{K_b}{1 + \tau_b s} F_s(s)\right]$$

**Step 9 — Factor out $F_s(s)$ and simplify.**

$$\begin{aligned}
s H(s) &= \frac{1}{\rho_w A_d}\left[F_{fw}(s) - F_s(s)\left(1 + \frac{\rho_s K_b s}{1 + \tau_b s}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
s H(s) &= \frac{1}{\rho_w A_d}\left[F_{fw}(s) - F_s(s)\left(\frac{1 + (\tau_b + \rho_s K_b) s}{1 + \tau_b s}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 10 — Express in compact transfer function form (with $F_{fw}$ as input).**

$$H(s) = \frac{K_1}{s(1 + \tau_b s)} F_{fw}(s) - \frac{K_2(1 + \tau_3 s)}{s(1 + \tau_b s)} F_s(s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **integrating-with-lag-plus-lead** transfer function that defines the drum level process. The $\frac{1}{s}$ term represents the **pure integrator** of the drum; the $(1 + \tau_3 s)$ in the numerator is the **non-minimum-phase zero** responsible for the swell effect.

## 3.2 Derivation of Steady-State Feedforward Gain

At steady state, $h$ is constant, so $\frac{dh}{dt} = 0$.

**Step 1 — From the steady-state mass balance.**

$$F_{fw,ss} = F_{s,ss} + F_{bd,ss}$$

**Step 2 — Neglecting blowdown for simplicity ($F_{bd} \ll F_s$).**

$$F_{fw,ss} \approx F_{s,ss}$$

**Step 3 — Define the feedforward gain.**

$$K_{ff} = \frac{\Delta F_{fw}}{\Delta F_s}\bigg\vert_{h = const}$$

For a unit step in steam flow, the feedforward controller must command a *unit step in feedwater flow* to maintain level:

$$K_{ff} \approx 1.0$$

**Step 4 — With blowdown correction.**

$$K_{ff} = 1 + \frac{F_{bd,ss}}{F_{s,ss}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, $K_{ff}$ ranges from **1.02 to 1.08** in operating utility boilers with continuous blowdown.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Metric | Single-Element | Two-Element | Three-Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steady-state offset | Zero (with PI) | Zero | Zero |
| Response to load step-up | **Poor — false swell** | Good — feedforward action | **Excellent** |
| Response to feedwater pressure disturbance | Slow, oscillatory | Slow, oscillatory | **Fast (inner loop)** |
| Number of transmitters | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| Controller complexity | Simple | Moderate | Cascade |
| Tuning sensitivity | Low | Moderate | **High (two PIDs)** |
| Fail-safe on sensor loss | Acceptable | Marginal | **Critical — requires voting** |
| Typical application | < 50 TPH boilers | 50–250 TPH | **> 250 TPH / utility grade** |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A utility boiler has drum cross-section $A_d = 3.0 \text{ m}^2$, water density $\rho_w = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$, steam density $\rho_s = 90 \text{ kg/m}^3$. During a load step, steam flow rises by $\Delta F_s = 20 \text{ kg/s}$ over 5 seconds while feedwater flow lags by 3 seconds. Calculate the apparent level rise due to swell.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the bubble expansion rate.**

Assume the bubble volume tracks steam flow with $K_b = 0.0008 \text{ m}^3\cdot\text{s/kg}$ and $\tau_b = 8 \text{ s}$.

$$\frac{dV_b}{dt}\bigg\vert_{peak} = \frac{K_b}{\tau_b} \Delta F_s = \frac{0.0008}{8} \times 20 = 0.002 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent level rise rate.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt}\bigg\vert_{swell} = -\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_w A_d} \cdot \frac{dV_b}{dt} = -\frac{90}{740 \times 3.0} \times 0.002$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$\frac{dh}{dt}\bigg\vert_{swell} = -8.1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = -0.081 \text{ mm/s}$$

The negative sign here is the *true* mass-loss component; the **apparent swell** arises from the bubble term being mis-attributed to mass by a single-element controller. Over 5 seconds, the false level rise indicated = approximately **+12 mm** if uncorrected.

**Step 4 — Required feedwater correction.**

$$K_{ff} \times \Delta F_s = 1.05 \times 20 = 21 \text{ kg/s}$$

The three-element controller immediately commands 21 kg/s of feedwater, *avoiding* the transient dry-out.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Two-Element Drum Level Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    setpoint[Level Setpoint] --> sumJunct
    levelTx[LT: Drum Level Transmitter] --> sumJunct
    sumJunct{Summing Junction} --> levelPID[LIC: Level Controller PI]
    levelPID --> ffBlock[Feedforward Compensator Kff]
    steamTx[FT: Steam Flow Transmitter] --> ffBlock
    ffBlock --> valveCmd{Control Signal}
    valveCmd --> fcv[FCV: Feedwater Control Valve]
    fcv --> feedwaterLine[Feedwater Line to Drum]
    feedwaterLine --> drumVessel[Steam Drum]
    drumVessel --> levelTx
    drumVessel --> steamOutlet[Steam Outlet to Turbine]
    steamOutlet --> steamTx

    style setpoint fill:#e1f5ff
    style levelPID fill:#fff4cc
    style ffBlock fill:#fff4cc
    style drumVessel fill:#ffd6d6
    style fcv fill:#d6f5d6
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Three-Element Drum Level Control (Cascade)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph outerLoop[OUTER LOOP - Master Level Control]
        sp[Level Setpoint 100 percent] --> sj1{Summing Junctor 1}
        lt[LT: Drum Level] --> sj1
        sj1 --> lic[LIC: Master Level Controller PI]
    end

    subgraph innerLoop[INNER LOOP - Slave Flow Control]
        lic -- Setpoint --> sj2{Summing Junctor 2}
        ftFw[FIT: Feedwater Flow Transmitter] --> sj2
        sj2 --> fic[FIC: Slave Flow Controller PI]
        fic --> fcvAdd{Feedforward Sum}
    end

    ftSteam[FIT: Steam Flow Transmitter] --> ffwGain[Kff = 1.05]
    ffwGain --> fcvAdd
    fcvAdd --> fcv[FCV: Feedwater Control Valve]
    fcv --> drumVessel[Steam Drum]
    drumVessel --> lt
    drumVessel --> steamOut[Steam to Turbine HP]
    steamOut --> ftSteam

    style outerLoop fill:#e1f5ff
    style innerLoop fill:#fff4cc
    style drumVessel fill:#ffd6d6
    style fcv fill:#d6f5d6
    style ffwGain fill:#ffe0b2
```

## 4.3 Sequence-of-Operations Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Plant Startup Initiated]) --> ramp[Ramp Boiler to 30 percent load]
    ramp --> mode1{Single Element Active}
    mode1 -- Level within band --> mode1
    mode1 -- Load > 40 percent --> activate2[Auto-Switch to Two-Element]
    activate2 --> check2{Steam Flow > 25 percent?}
    check2 -- No --> activate2
    check2 -- Yes --> activate3[Auto-Switch to Three-Element]
    activate3 --> monitor[Continuous Monitoring by DCS]
    monitor --> fault{Any Sensor Fault?}
    fault -- Yes --> alarm[Operator Alarm + Trip to Two-Element]
    fault -- No --> monitor
    alarm --> manual[Manual Operator Intervention]

    style start fill:#d6f5d6
    style activate3 fill:#fff4cc
    style alarm fill:#ffd6d6
```

## 4.4 Physical Layout Reference (Drum Instrumentation)

| Tag | Service | Location on Drum | Signal Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| LT-101 | Drum Level (Primary) | Front water column | 4–20 mA / HART |
| LT-102 | Drum Level (Redundant) | Rear water column | 4–20 mA / HART |
| LT-103 | Drum Level (High Integrity) | Differential pressure cell | 4–20 mA / HART |
| FIT-201 | Steam Flow | Outlet header | 4–20 mA / HART |
| FIT-202 | Feedwater Flow | Economizer inlet | 4–20 mA / HART |
| FT-203 | Feedwater Flow (Backup) | Pump discharge | Pulse / 4–20 mA |
| FCV-301 | Feedwater Regulating Valve | Economizer inlet | Pneumatic / Electric |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern **N+1 redundancy** mandates that three independent level measurements vote via a **2-out-of-3 (2oo3) median selector** before the level signal enters the LIC. This eliminates single-point-of-failure-induced dry-outs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Explain the term **"shrink and swell effect"** in a boiler steam drum. Why does it cause problems for single-element drum level control?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The shrink-and-swell effect is the apparent change in drum water level caused by expansion or contraction of steam bubbles entrained in the saturated water. When steam demand suddenly increases, drum pressure drops, the bubbles expand, and the level *appears* to rise (swell) even though the actual water mass has decreased. Conversely, a sudden load decrease causes a "shrink."
> 
> In **single-element control**, the level transmitter reports this false level. The PI controller then wrongly *closes* the feedwater valve during a swell, accelerating dry-out. **(1 mark)** for definition, **(1 mark)** for cause, **(1 mark)** for the controller consequence.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> List the **three measured variables** in a three-element drum level control system and state the function of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Variable | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Drum Level (LT)** | Master feedback — sets long-term accuracy and removes offset via PI action. **(1 mark)** |
| 2 | **Steam Flow (FT)** | Feedforward — anticipates load changes, compensates swell. **(1 mark)** |
| 3 | **Feedwater Flow (FT)** | Inner cascade loop — trims valve response to feedwater pressure disturbances. **(1 mark)** |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working of a two-element drum level control system**. How does it overcome the limitations of single-element control? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the transfer function relating drum level to feedwater and steam flows. Clearly identify the term responsible for the swell effect. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Two-Element Drum Level Control (7 marks)**

> **Block Diagram (2 marks)** — Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.
> 
> **Working (3 marks):** The level transmitter (LT) measures drum level and feeds it to the LIC. Simultaneously, the steam flow transmitter (FT) sends a signal proportional to instantaneous steam demand. This signal passes through a **feedforward compensator** with gain $K_{ff}$ and dynamic lead-lag element $G_{ff}(s) = \frac{K_s(1+\tau_1 s)}{1+\tau_2 s}$. The summed output drives the feedwater control valve. When steam flow suddenly rises, the feedforward path immediately opens the feedwater valve *before* the swell-induced level rise can mislead the feedback controller.
> 
> **Advantage over single-element (2 marks):** The feedforward signal acts on the *cause* (load change), not the *effect* (false swell level), thereby eliminating the inverse controller action and dramatically reducing transient level deviations from ±150 mm to ±25 mm.

**(b) Drum Level Transfer Function Derivation (7 marks)**

> Full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes. Key marks allocation:
> - Mass balance equation: **(2 marks)**
> - Laplace transformation & bubble dynamics: **(2 marks)**
> - Final transfer function form: **(2 marks)**
> - Identification of non-minimum-phase zero $(1 + \tau_3 s)$ as swell source: **(1 mark)**
> 
> $$\boxed{H(s) = \frac{K_1}{s(1 + \tau_b s)} F_{fw}(s) - \frac{K_2(1 + \tau_3 s)}{s(1 + \tau_b s)} F_s(s)}$$

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a **three-element drum level control system** and explain the cascade structure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **performance of single, two, and three-element control** under (i) sudden load step-up, (ii) feedwater pump discharge pressure drop, and (iii) gradual load ramp. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Element Cascade Block Diagram (7 marks)**

> **Diagram (2 marks)** — Refer to Section 4.2.
> 
> **Cascade Explanation (5 marks):**
> - **Outer (master) loop:** LIC compares measured drum level to setpoint and outputs a *feedwater flow setpoint* to the FIC. Slow dynamics, integral action ensures zero steady-state offset.
> - **Inner (slave) loop:** FIC compares actual feedwater flow (FIT) to the dynamic setpoint from LIC and adjusts the FCV. Fast dynamics — typically 4× to 10× faster than the outer loop per bandwidth rule $\omega_{inner} \ge 4 \omega_{outer}$.
> - **Feedforward:** Steam flow signal enters with $K_{ff}$ to anticipate load changes.
> - **Advantage:** The slave loop rejects feedwater-side disturbances (pump pressure variations, valve hysteresis) *before* they reach the drum level, providing decoupled, robust control.

**(b) Comparative Performance Analysis (7 marks)**

| Scenario | Single-Element | Two-Element | Three-Element |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Sudden load step-up | **Worst** — false swell, valve closes wrongly | Good — feedforward compensates | **Best** — feedforward + flow cascade |
| (ii) Feedwater pressure drop | Slow recovery, level sags | Same as single-element (no feedwater measurement) | **Best** — inner loop corrects within seconds |
| (iii) Gradual load ramp | Acceptable | Acceptable | Acceptable |

> Award **(2 marks)** per scenario discussion, **(1 mark)** for tabular synthesis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Examiner Observations:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "swell" with "shrink":** Students frequently interchange the two. Remember — load *increase* = pressure drop = bubble *expansion* = **SWELL (level appears UP)**. Load *decrease* = **SHRINK**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the cascade tuning bandwidth rule:** In three-element control, the inner loop must be **at least 4× faster** than the outer loop. Students often tune both loops independently, leading to loop interaction and oscillation. KTU examiners specifically look for this rule.
> 
> 3. **Wrong sign convention in feedforward:** The feedforward action must *add* to the master controller output during load increase (i.e., open the FCV). Drawing an incorrect summing junction sign is a **3-mark loss** on a 7-mark sub-question.
> 
> 4. **Omitting the lead-lag compensator:** The steam flow feedforward signal **must** pass through a dynamic compensator $G_{ff}(s) = \frac{K_s(1+\tau_1 s)}{1+\tau_2 s}$ to align its dynamics with the slower drum level response. Drawing just a gain $K_{ff}$ loses **2 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Not mentioning redundancy:** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes safety instrumentation (per IS/IEC 61511). Always mention **2oo3 voting** and **sensor fault detection** for full marks on industrial-grade answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Drum Level Control**
> 
> - **Definition:** Drum Level Control maintains water level in a steam drum by manipulating feedwater flow. **(Always define drum level control first in any KTU answer.)**
> - **Three architectures:** Single-Element (level only) → Two-Element (+ steam flow) → Three-Element (+ feedwater flow).
> - **Shrink-and-Swell:** Non-minimum-phase artifact from bubble dynamics; causes inverse controller response in single-element schemes.
> - **Swell Direction:** Load increase ⇒ pressure drop ⇒ bubbles expand ⇒ **level appears to RISE** (false high).
> - **Two-Element:** Adds **feedforward** compensation; eliminates swell-induced false action.
> - **Three-Element:** Adds **cascade control**; outer loop = level (LIC), inner loop = feedwater flow (FIC).
> - **Cascade Tuning Rule:** $\omega_{inner} \ge 4 \cdot \omega_{outer}$.
> - **Feedforward Gain:** $K_{ff} \approx 1.05$ (with blowdown correction).
> - **Transfer Function:** $\frac{H(s)}{F_{fw}(s)} = \frac{K_1}{s(1+\tau_b s)}$ — integrating-with-lag behavior.
> - **Non-minimum-phase zero:** $(1 + \tau_3 s)$ in the steam flow path is the **mathematical signature** of the swell effect.
> - **Redundancy:** Modern systems use **2oo3 voting** on three independent level transmitters.
> - **Standards:** IEC 61511 (Safety), IEC 61131-3 (DCS programming), ASME Boiler Code (operating limits).
> - **Dead Band:** ±25 mm typical operating tolerance.
> - **Once-through boilers:** No drum, hence drum level control does not apply — note this in comparative answers.
> - **Master Controller:** LIC (Level Indicating Controller).
> - **Slave Controller:** FIC (Flow Indicating Controller).
> - **Final Control Element:** FCV (Feedwater Control Valve) — typically pneumatic diaphragm-actuated with smart positioner.

**Mnemonic for quick recall — "L-F-S-D-R":**
**L**evel (master) → **F**low (slave) → **S**team flow (feedforward) → **D**rum (process) → **R**edundancy (2oo3 voting).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Main and Reheat Steam Temperature Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Main and Reheat Steam Temperature Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Main Steam Temperature Control** refers to the closed-loop instrumentation strategy employed in a steam power plant to regulate the temperature of superheated steam leaving the final superheater (and entering the High-Pressure / HP turbine) at a setpoint typically between **535 °C and 565 °C** for sub-critical units and up to **600 °C–620 °C** for ultra-supercritical units.

**Reheat Steam Temperature Control** is the analogous control strategy that regulates the temperature of steam leaving the reheater (cold reheat enters HP exhaust at ~330–400 °C and exits the reheater at ~535–565 °C) before it enters the Intermediate-Pressure / IP turbine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Main and Reheat Steam Temperature Control is a *high-weightage* topic. Students must master the **Attemperator (Spray Type)**, **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)**, **Burner Tilt**, and **Gas Bypass Damper** mechanisms. Questions of 7–14 marks are routinely asked from this unit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Pressure Cooker Whistle"**
> Imagine boiling dal in a pressure cooker. If the cooker whistles too aggressively, the steam is too hot and dry — your safety valve (analogous to a turbine blade) is being damaged. If it doesn't whistle at all, the steam is too wet and your food doesn't cook properly. The cook constantly adjusts the weight on the whistle (analogous to the **attemperator**) or turns the gas knob up and down (analogous to **flue gas recirculation**). A power plant does the same thing — but with sensors, PID controllers, and actuators instead of a human ear.

The key idea is that **steam temperature = energy balance** between heat added in the boiler and heat removed (either by turbine work or by deliberate cooling water spray). The controller manipulates the cooling/heating split.

### 1.3 Why Steam Temperature Control is Mission-Critical

- **HP Turbine Blade Life:** A 10 °C overshoot can reduce creep life of blades by **≈ 50 %**. Creep rate roughly doubles for every 10–15 °C increase above design.
- **Cycle Efficiency:** A 1 °C drop in reheat temperature reduces plant efficiency by **0.05–0.1 %**.
- **LP Turbine Erosion:** If reheat temperature is too low, the steam reaching the LP turbine becomes wet, causing **blade erosion**.
- **Thermal Stress:** Rapid temperature swings cause cyclic thermal stress in thick-walled headers → **thermal fatigue cracking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard KTU metric to remember: **Target main steam temperature = 535 ± 5 °C**; **Target reheat steam temperature = 535 ± 5 °C** for a 500 MW subcritical unit.

### 1.4 Common Manipulated Variables (Final Control Elements)

| # | Variable | Speed | Suitability |
|---|----------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Attemperator Spray Water Flow** | Fast (seconds) | Main steam only |
| 2 | **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR) %** | Slow (tens of seconds) | Both |
| 3 | **Burner Tilt Angle** | Slow (minutes) | Both (most powerful for reheat) |
| 4 | **Gas Bypass Damper Position** | Slow | Both |
| 5 | **Excess Air / O₂ setpoint** | Slow | Trim only |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Attemperator (Spray Type) — Detailed Theory

An **attemperator** is a heat exchanger installed between two superheater stages (or just before the final superheater) that sprays finely atomized feedwater directly into the steam path. The water evaporates, absorbing its **latent heat of vaporization (~ 2000 kJ/kg at 250 bar)** from the steam, thereby *cooling* it.

**Key physical components:**
1. **Spray nozzle** — atomizes water to droplet size **< 100 µm**.
2. **Water control valve** — modulates spray flow (1–2 % of main steam flow).
3. **Header** — distributes water uniformly across the duct cross-section.
4. **Protection:** A *minimum-flow* recirculation line prevents nozzle overheating when spray is at zero.

### 2.2 Logic Steps of Spray Attemperation

1. Measure **T_main** (or T_att_out) using a sheathed **Type-K / Type-N thermocouple** in a thermowell at the final superheater outlet.
2. Compare T_main to setpoint T_SP (e.g., 535 °C) → error e(t) = T_SP − T_main.
3. The **temperature controller (master / primary)** computes the required spray flow setpoint.
4. The **flow controller (slave / secondary)** adjusts the spray water control valve.
5. The **final control element (FCV)** modulates water flow → steam temp converges to setpoint.
6. **Feedforward** from unit load (MW) is added to compensate for the large disturbance from load changes (the steam mass flow itself changes by 1:1 with load).

### 2.3 Why Cascade Control is Mandatory (KTU favourite question!)

A single-loop controller on T_main alone is too slow because:
- The **dead time** between spray valve movement and steam-temp response is **15–30 seconds** (transport + mixing).
- Steam flow and load disturbances can change T_main by 30 °C in < 1 minute.

**Cascade control structure:**
- **Primary (Master) loop:** T_main → T_controller → produces setpoint for the secondary loop.
- **Secondary (Slave) loop:** Spray water flow → F_controller → valve position.
- The slave loop rejects spray-water pressure disturbances *before* they affect T_main.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|c|}
\hline
\textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Description} & \textbf{Boundary / Range} & \textbf{Units} \\
\hline
Q_{spray} = \dot{m}_{st}\, c_{p,st}\, \Delta T & \text{Spray heat duty} & \Delta T = 10{-}30~^{\circ}\text{C} & \text{kW} \\
\hline
\dot{m}_{spray} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{st}\, c_{p,st}\, \Delta T}{h_{st} - h_{w}} & \text{Spray water mass flow} & 1{-}2\% \text{ of main steam flow} & \text{kg/s} \\
\hline
\tau_{plant} = \dfrac{V_{duct}}{v_{st}} & \text{Transport dead time} & 5{-}30~\text{s} & \text{s} \\
\hline
N_{tilt} \propto \Delta T_{reheat} & \text{Burner tilt effect on reheat} & \pm 30^{\circ} \text{ tilt} & \text{°C} \\
\hline
\dot{m}_{FGR} = (0.15{-}0.30)\,\dot{m}_{fg} & \text{Recirculation ratio} & 15{-}30\% & \text{kg/s} \\
\hline
\eta_{cycle} \approx \eta_{Carnot} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen} & \text{Efficiency chain} & 35{-}42\% \text{ net} & \% \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table:** All vertical bars in math expressions use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent breaking Markdown table syntax. The cells above contain raw math with no unescaped pipes.

### 2.5 Reheat Steam Temperature Control — Theory

Reheat steam is at **lower pressure (≈ 25 % of main steam pressure)** and **larger volume flow**. Spray attemperation is *avoided* for reheat because:
- It reduces cycle efficiency (you spent fuel re-heating the steam!).
- The added water increases moisture content in the **LP turbine** → blade erosion.

Therefore, the preferred methods are:
1. **Burner Tilt (preferred):** Tilting burners down shifts the heat-release zone toward the reheat section, raising reheat temperature. Range: **± 30°** tilt → **± 50 °C** reheat temp change. Very slow (5–10 min response).
2. **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR):** Recirculating 15–30 % of flue gas back into the furnace lowers the peak flame temperature and shifts heat distribution toward the reheat section.
3. **Gas Bypass Damper:** A damper diverts some hot flue gas around the reheater.

**Reheat spray attemperation is used ONLY as an emergency / fast-acting override** during large transients (e.g., sudden load rejection).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **500 MW NTPC / KSEB unit** uses a 3-element attemperator with **two parallel control valves** for redundancy.
- **Supercritical once-through boilers** have **zero steam drum** — there is no thermal storage. Hence, even a **1-second** delay in T_main control can cause a **T_main swing of 8–10 °C** → cascade + feedforward control is **non-negotiable**.
- **DCS Implementation:** Modern plants use **ABB 800xA, Siemens SPPA-T3000, or Honeywell Experion** with the attemperator cascade logic built into standard control libraries (e.g., Siemens CFC chart "REATEMP").

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Required Spray Water Flow Rate

**Given:** A 210 MW boiler has a main steam mass flow $\dot{m}_{st} = 180~\text{kg/s}$ at $p = 140~\text{bar}$, $T_{in} = 510~^{\circ}\text{C}$ (entering attemperator). The required $T_{out} = 505~^{\circ}\text{C}$ (target). The attemperator water is taken from the feedwater at $T_w = 245~^{\circ}\text{C}$ and $p_w = 160~\text{bar}$.

**Goal:** Find the spray water flow $\dot{m}_{spray}$.

**Step 1 — Enthalpies (from steam tables):**

$$
h_{st,\,in} = h_g(140~\text{bar},~510~^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 3386~\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_{st,\,out} = h_g(140~\text{bar},~505~^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 3360~\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_w = h_f(160~\text{bar},~245~^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 1066~\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_{st,\,vaporized} = h_g(140~\text{bar},~245~^{\circ}\text{C}) \approx 2801~\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance on the attemperator (steady state, no work, no heat loss):**

$$
\dot{m}_{st}\, h_{st,\,in} + \dot{m}_{spray}\, h_w = \dot{m}_{st}\, h_{st,\,out} + \dot{m}_{spray}\, h_{st,\,vaporized}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $\dot{m}_{spray}$:**

$$
\dot{m}_{spray}\,(h_{st,\,vaporized} - h_w) = \dot{m}_{st}\,(h_{st,\,in} - h_{st,\,out})
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = \dot{m}_{st}\,\dfrac{h_{st,\,in} - h_{st,\,out}}{h_{st,\,vaporized} - h_w}
$$

**Step 4 — Plug in numbers:**

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = 180 \times \dfrac{3386 - 3360}{2801 - 1066} = 180 \times \dfrac{26}{1735}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = 180 \times 0.01499 = 2.70~\text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check (as % of steam flow):**

$$
\%\,\text{spray} = \dfrac{2.70}{180} \times 100 = 1.5\,\%
$$

This lies in the **expected 1–2 %** range — consistent with KTU textbook values. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation — Process Gain of Attemperator Loop

The **process gain** $K_p$ (°C drop per kg/s of spray) determines controller tuning.

$$
K_p = \dfrac{\Delta T_{st}}{\dot{m}_{spray}} = \dfrac{h_{st,\,vaporized} - h_w}{\dot{m}_{st}\, c_{p,st}}
$$

Using $c_{p,st} \approx 2.6~\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ at these conditions:

$$
K_p = \dfrac{1735}{180 \times 2.6} = \dfrac{1735}{468} = 3.71~^{\circ}\text{C per (kg/s)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Each 1 kg/s of additional spray cools the steam by **3.71 °C**. A 10 °C setpoint change requires ≈ 2.7 kg/s spray change.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Control Methods

| **Parameter** | **Attemperator** | **Burner Tilt** | **FGR** | **Gas Bypass** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Response time | 15–30 s | 5–10 min | 30–60 s | 30–60 s |
| Control range | ± 20 °C | ± 50 °C | ± 15 °C | ± 10 °C |
| Efficiency penalty | Small | None | Slight | None |
| Cost | Medium | High | Medium | Medium |
| Use for **Main** steam | ✔ Preferred | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |
| Use for **Reheat** steam | ✘ (emergency) | ✔ Preferred | ✔ | ✔ |
| Suitable for transient | ✔ Yes | ✘ No | △ | △ |

### 3.4 Numerical Example — T_main Setpoint Calculation

A 500 MW unit has the following energy balance around the final superheater:

- $\dot{m}_{st} = 425~\text{kg/s}$, $p = 250~\text{bar}$
- Steam enters at $T = 510~^{\circ}\text{C}$, must leave at $T_{SP} = 540~^{\circ}\text{C}$
- Heat added in final superheater: $Q = 60~\text{MW}$ (thermal)
- Attemperator water at $T_w = 290~^{\circ}\text{C}$

**Required attemperator heat duty:**

$$
Q_{att} = \dot{m}_{st}\, c_{p,st}\, (T_{in} - T_{SP}) = 425 \times 2.6 \times (510 - 540)
$$

$$
Q_{att} = 425 \times 2.6 \times (-30) = -33{,}150~\text{kW} = -33.15~\text{MW}
$$

The negative sign confirms the attemperator *removes* 33.15 MW of thermal energy. Since the final superheater adds 60 MW, the **net heat addition is 26.85 MW** — exactly the right value to raise steam by 30 °C.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Attemperator Cascade Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    setpSP["TEMP SETPOINT 540C"] --> compA
    load["UNIT LOAD MW FEEDFORWARD"] --> compA
    ttMain["TEMP TRANSMITTER FINAL SUPERHEATER"] --> compA
    compA["ERROR COMPARATOR"] --> tCtrl["TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER MASTER"]
    tCtrl --> compB
    ftt["FLOW TRANSMITTER SPRAY WATER"] --> compB
    compB["ERROR COMPARATOR 2"] --> fCtrl["FLOW CONTROLLER SLAVE"]
    fCtrl --> fcv["SPRAY WATER CONTROL VALVE"]
    fcv --> attInlet
    attInlet["ATTEMPERATOR BODY"] --> attOutlet
    attOutlet["ATTEMPERATOR OUTLET TO FINAL SUPERHEATER"] --> ttMain
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *outer* loop measures the final steam temperature (TT-Main) and compares it to the setpoint. The *inner* loop measures spray water flow and regulates the FCV. The feedforward arrow from unit load pre-positions the valve *before* the disturbance actually changes T_main — this dramatically reduces overshoot during load ramping.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of the Complete Steam-Temperature Management System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FURNACE
        burner["BURNERS WITH TILT ACTUATOR"]
        fgrFan["FGR FAN DAMPER"]
        bypassDamp["GAS BYPASS DAMPER"]
    end
    subgraph SUPERHEATER
        sh1["PRIMARY SUPERHEATER"]
        att["ATTEMPERATOR SPRAY TYPE"]
        sh2["FINAL SUPERHEATER"]
    end
    subgraph REHEATER
        rh1["COLD REHEAT INLET"]
        rh2["FINAL REHEATER"]
        rh3["HOT REHEAT OUTLET"]
    end
    subgraph TURBINE
        hp["HP TURBINE"]
        ip["IP TURBINE"]
    end
    burner --> sh1
    sh1 --> att
    att --> sh2
    fgrFan --> burner
    bypassDamp --> rh2
    hp --> rh1
    rh1 --> rh2
    rh2 --> rh3
    rh3 --> ip
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reheat Steam Temperature Cascade (Tilt + FGR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rhSP["REHEAT SETPOINT 535C"] --> cmpR
    ttR["REHEAT TEMP TRANSMITTER"] --> cmpR
    cmpR["ERROR"] --> rCtrl["REHEAT TEMP CONTROLLER"]
    rCtrl --> tiltAct["BURNER TILT ACTUATOR"]
    rCtrl --> fgrAct["FGR DAMPER ACTUATOR"]
    tiltAct --> rhFurnace["REHEAT SECTION FURNACE"]
    fgrAct --> rhFurnace
    rhFurnace --> ttR
```

### 4.4 Component-Level Architecture (Attemperator Internals)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fwIn["FEEDWATER INLET 245C"] --> sprayNoz["SPRAY NOZZLE MANIFOLD"]
    sprayNoz --> mixing["STEAM WATER MIXING CHAMBER"]
    steamIn["SUPERHEATED STEAM 540C"] --> mixing
    mixing --> outletT["ATTEMPERATED STEAM 510C"]
    outletT --> ttAtt["OUTLET THERMOCOUPLE"]
    ttAtt --> tControl
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an attemperator. Why is it used only for main steam and not for reheat steam in normal operation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **attemperator** is a heat exchanger installed in the steam path that sprays feedwater into superheated steam to control its temperature. It works on the principle of **latent heat absorption** as the spray water evaporates, withdrawing energy from the steam. **(1 mark)**

In normal operation, attemperators are used for **main steam temperature control** because the response is fast (15–30 s) and the amount of spray needed is small (1–2 % of steam flow). **(1 mark)**

For **reheat steam**, attemperation is avoided because:
- It reduces the **thermal efficiency** of the regenerative cycle (reheating exists to *improve* efficiency, and adding water defeats this).
- The added moisture travels into the **IP and LP turbines**, causing **blade erosion**.
- Reheat is therefore controlled by slower methods — **burner tilt, FGR, or gas bypass**. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three methods of main steam temperature control. State which is the fastest.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Attemperator (spray type)** — fastest, 15–30 s response. **(1 mark)**
2. **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)** — 30–60 s response. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **Burner Tilt** — 5–10 min response (slowest of the three). **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Gas Bypass Damper** — 30–60 s. **(optional)**

The **attemperator (spray type)** is the fastest method of main steam temperature control. **(1 mark)** — *Full 3 marks awarded.*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the *definition* and skip the *energy-balance derivation* for attemperator calculations. The energy-balance equation is the **core 4-mark component** of any 14-mark question. Also, students frequently confuse **flue gas recirculation (FGR)** with **gas bypass** — remember, FGR mixes flue gas with combustion air, whereas gas bypass diverts hot gas around the reheat section.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 style]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cascade control scheme** used for main steam temperature control using a spray attemperator. Justify why cascade control is preferred over single-loop control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 500 MW boiler has a main steam flow of $425~\text{kg/s}$ at $140~\text{bar}$. The attemperator is required to cool the steam from $510~^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $490~^{\circ}\text{C}$. The spray water is at $245~^{\circ}\text{C}$ from the feedwater line. Using the energy balance, calculate the required spray water flow rate. Use steam-table values: $h_{st,\,510} = 3386~\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{st,\,490} = 3290~\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{st,\,vap,\,245} = 2801~\text{kJ/kg}$, $h_{w,\,245} = 1066~\text{kJ/kg}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP1["T_SP"] --> sumA
    TT["T_MAIN TRANSMITTER"] --> sumA
    sumA --> TC["TEMP CONTROLLER MASTER"]
    TC --> sumB
    FT["SPRAY FLOW TRANSMITTER"] --> sumB
    sumB --> FC["FLOW CONTROLLER SLAVE"]
    FC --> FCV["SPRAY VALVE"]
    FCV --> ATT["ATTEMPERATOR"]
    ATT --> TT
```

2. **Working (2 marks):** The *primary (master)* temperature controller TC compares T_main with the setpoint and produces a *flow setpoint* for the *secondary (slave)* flow controller FC. FC compares this with the measured spray water flow (from FT) and modulates the spray control valve. The two control valves are arranged in series.

3. **Justification — why cascade? (3 marks):**
   - The **dead time** in the attemperator loop is 15–30 s; a single-loop PI controller would either be slow or unstable. **(1 mark)**
   - The **inner flow loop** rejects spray-water pressure disturbances (e.g., feed-pump header pressure swing) within 1–2 s, *before* T_main is affected. **(1 mark)**
   - **Non-linearity** of the control valve is compensated by the inner loop's integral action. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Energy Balance Equation (3 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{st}\, h_{st,\,in} + \dot{m}_{spray}\, h_w = \dot{m}_{st}\, h_{st,\,out} + \dot{m}_{spray}\, h_{st,\,vap}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = \dot{m}_{st}\,\dfrac{h_{st,\,in} - h_{st,\,out}}{h_{st,\,vap} - h_w}
$$

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = 425 \times \dfrac{3386 - 3290}{2801 - 1066}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = 425 \times \dfrac{96}{1735} = 425 \times 0.05533
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{spray} = 23.51~\text{kg/s}
$$

**Sanity check & result (2 marks):**

As a % of main steam flow: $23.51 / 425 = 5.5\,\%$. This is **higher than the typical 1–2 %** because the required $\Delta T = 20~^{\circ}\text{C}$ is large. In a real plant, this large a swing would be trimmed additionally by burner tilt or FGR. **Final answer: $\dot{m}_{spray} \approx 23.5~\text{kg/s}$.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 style]

**(a)** Explain the working of **Flue Gas Recirculation (FGR)** as a method of reheat steam temperature control. State its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **burner tilt method** of reheat steam temperature control. How is the tilt angle controlled automatically? What is the typical range of tilt angle and the corresponding change in reheat temperature? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Working (3 marks):** A portion of the **hot flue gas** leaving the economizer (15–30 % by mass) is drawn by an FGR fan and re-injected into the **furnace wind-box** (below the burners) or into the **over-fire air** zone. This dilutes the combustion products, **lowers the peak flame temperature**, and **shifts the heat-release profile** along the furnace length. As a result, **less heat** is absorbed in the superheater section and **more heat** is available in the reheater section — *raising* the reheat temperature. Conversely, reducing FGR raises superheat at the cost of reheat. (3 marks)

2. **Advantages (2 marks):**
   - Continuous, smooth control. **(1 mark)**
   - No water injection, so **no efficiency penalty** and **no LP turbine erosion risk**. **(1 mark)**

3. **Limitations (2 marks):**
   - Recirculation fan is a **power consumer** (~0.5–1 % of unit output). **(1 mark)**
   - Response is moderate (30–60 s) — not fast enough for large transients. **(1 mark)**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Working of Burner Tilt (3 marks):** Each burner is mounted on a **pivot** and can be tilted **up or down** by ± 30° from horizontal. Tilting **down** lowers the burner, so the flame moves *closer to the reheat section* — more radiant and convective heat reaches the reheater. Tilting **up** moves heat *toward the superheater section*. Thus, **burner tilt = primary control knob for reheat temperature**.

2. **Automatic Control (2 marks):** The tilt angle is actuated by an **electro-hydraulic actuator** commanded by the **reheat-temperature controller**. A **position feedback LVDT** confirms actual tilt angle to the DCS. Cascade with feed-forward from load (MW) is standard.

3. **Range (2 marks):** Tilt range = **± 30°**. Reheat temperature sensitivity ≈ **± 1.5–2 °C per degree of tilt**, giving a total swing capability of **± 50 °C**. Response time: 5–10 min (the slowest but most powerful of the reheat-control methods).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

- ✔ **Attemperator** = spray water injected into steam; uses **latent heat of vaporization**; *fastest* main-steam control.
- ✔ **Reheat spray** is *avoided* normally — used only as an *emergency* due to LP-turbine erosion and efficiency loss.
- ✔ **Burner tilt** is the *primary* reheat control mechanism; range **± 30°**; effect **± 50 °C**; slow (5–10 min).
- ✔ **FGR ratio = 15–30 %** of flue-gas flow; shifts heat from superheater to reheater.
- ✔ **Cascade control** is mandatory — primary is T_main, secondary is spray flow.
- ✔ **Feedforward from unit load** is added to pre-position the spray valve during load ramps.
- ✔ Energy-balance equation: $\dot{m}_{spray} = \dot{m}_{st} \cdot (h_{in} - h_{out}) / (h_{vap} - h_w)$.
- ✔ Typical spray flow = **1–2 %** of main steam flow.
- ✔ 10 °C overshoot in T_main → **50 % reduction** in HP-turbine blade creep life.
- ✔ Standard setpoints: **T_main ≈ 535 °C**, **T_reheat ≈ 535 °C** for subcritical; **600+ °C** for ultra-supercritical.
- ✔ KTU favourite sub-topics: attemperator derivation, cascade control, comparison of reheat control methods, FGR vs. gas-bypass.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Main and Reheat Steam Temperature Control.** These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PEICT523 – Power Plant Instrumentation**. For best results, practice the **energy-balance derivation** at least three times before the exam — it is worth **4–7 marks** whenever it appears.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boiler Following Mode Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Boiler Following Mode Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boiler Following Mode (BFM) Operation** is a unit coordinated control strategy in a thermal power plant in which the **turbine governor acts as the primary (master) controller** responding to load demand variations, while the **boiler acts as the follower (slave)**, adjusting its fuel, air, and feedwater inputs to maintain the steam pressure (header pressure) at its set-point.

In strict KTU terminology, Boiler Following is also called **Turbine-Lead / Boiler-Lag (TLBL)** control mode. The turbine inlet valves (TIV) take the lead in admitting high-pressure steam to the turbine, and the boiler subsequently catches up by manipulating its combustion parameters to restore the steam generator pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identifiers of BFM:**
> - **Master Controller:** Turbine Governor (speed/load controller)
> - **Slave Controller:** Boiler Combustion + Feedwater Controller
> - **Controlled Variable (Turbine):** MW output / Turbine speed
> - **Controlled Variable (Boiler):** Steam header pressure (Drum pressure)
> - **Time constant:** Boiler is the slow loop, Turbine is the fast loop

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car with a **manual handbrake and accelerator**:

- The **driver's foot on the accelerator (Turbine)** is the fast reaction — it pushes fuel to the engine (steam to the turbine) the *instant* the road inclines.
- The **fuel pump feeding petrol to the tank (Boiler)** is the slow reaction — it takes time to fill up the line.
