In **Boiler Following Mode**, the *accelerator* responds first to any change (the driver wants to climb a hill), and the *fuel pump* has to *catch up* later by pumping more fuel so the line pressure remains steady. The pump is "following" the accelerator.

A power engineer would say: **"The turbine leads, the boiler lags."**

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Drum pressure set-point:** typically **100 – 200 bar** (subcritical) or **up to 600 bar** (supercritical / ultra-supercritical units)
- **Reheat pressure:** **40 – 80 bar** standard
- **Steam temperature:** **500 °C – 600 °C** (modern subcritical / supercritical)
- **Boiler time constant (τ_B):** **100 – 300 seconds** (slow)
- **Turbine time constant (τ_T):** **0.2 – 2 seconds** (fast)
- **Boiler storage capability:** typically expressed as the **Stored Energy (M_boil)** in MJ or kJ/kg of water-steam inventory

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this constant relevant?** The large time-constant mismatch (τ_B ≫ τ_T) is precisely why Boiler Following Mode has the characteristic **"initial pressure dip"** — the boiler cannot instantly match the turbine's demand.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The Boiler Following Mode can be broken down into the following structured sequence:

1. **Load Demand (L_D) changes** — either due to grid frequency deviation or operator/AGC demand change.
2. **Turbine Governor** immediately responds by opening the **Turbine Inlet Valves (TIV)** to admit more steam. This is a *fast* control action (milliseconds to a few seconds).
3. **Steam flow into the turbine increases** — greater mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{steam}$ reaches the HP/IP/LP turbine stages.
4. **Header pressure drops** transiently because the boiler's steam generation rate has not yet caught up with the turbine's steam consumption rate.
5. **Pressure error signal** ($\Delta P = P_{set} - P_{actual}$) is generated by the **drum pressure controller**.
6. **Boiler master controller** processes this pressure error and commands:
   - **Fuel flow** increase (via coal feeders / mill output)
   - **Air flow** increase (via FD/ID fan damper)
   - **Feedwater flow** adjustment (via feedwater pump speed / BFP recirculation valve)
7. **Boiler output (steam generation)** gradually rises and the **steam pressure is restored** to set-point.
8. **Steady-state** is reached when boiler steam generation = turbine steam consumption and pressure is back at set-point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The hallmark of BFM:** The turbine produces the load change *first*; pressure is allowed to transiently dip and is *later* restored by the boiler. This is the inverse of Turbine Following Mode (where pressure is held tight and load is slower).

## 2.2 Control Loops Involved in Boiler Following Mode

| Loop | Master Variable | Manipulated Variable | Speed |
|------|----------------|----------------------|-------|
| Turbine load loop | Load / Speed | Turbine Valve Opening | Fast (τ ≈ 0.5 s) |
| Boiler pressure loop | Header Pressure | Fuel + Air + Feedwater | Slow (τ ≈ 100–300 s) |
| Combustion loop | O₂ / Excess Air | FD Fan Damper | Medium (τ ≈ 10–30 s) |
| Feedwater loop | Drum Level | Feedwater Valve | Medium (τ ≈ 30–60 s) |
| Steam temperature loop | SH/RH Temp | Attemperator Spray | Slow (τ ≈ 60–120 s) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All equations below are **directly examinable** in KTU ESE under Module 4 of PEICT523.

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\Delta P_{header} = P_{set} - P_{actual}$ | Pressure error driving the boiler master | bar |
| 2 | $G_{B}(s) = \dfrac{K_B}{\tau_B s + 1}$ | Boiler transfer function (first-order) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $G_{T}(s) = \dfrac{K_T}{\tau_T s + 1}$ | Turbine transfer function (first-order) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\tau_{B} \gg \tau_{T}$ | Key inequality justifying BFM dynamics | seconds |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}_{stored} = V \cdot \rho_w \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt}$ | Energy storage rate in boiler water-steam inventory | kJ/s |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{steam} = \dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot \eta_{comb}$ | Steam generation vs fuel mass balance | kg/s |
| 7 | $W_{turbine} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \Delta h_{isentropic} \cdot \eta_T$ | Turbine power output (Rankine cycle) | MW |
| 8 | $\Delta P \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$ | Drum/header mass balance (continuity) | bar·m³/s |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P_{actual}(s)}{P_{demand}(s)} = \dfrac{G_T(s) \cdot G_B(s)}{1 + G_B(s) \cdot H_B(s)}$ | Closed-loop pressure response (BFM) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_{droop} = \dfrac{\Delta f / f_{nom}}{\Delta P / P_{nom}}$ | Governor droop setting (typically 4–5 %) | %/Hz |

> **Important notational safeguard:** Absolute value notation uses $\vert x \vert$ to prevent markdown pipe-symbol conflicts. Example: $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$ instead of `|G(jω)|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AGC (Automatic Generation Control) compatibility:** In isolated/islanded grids, BFM gives a **fast initial load pick-up** — turbine valves move first → grid frequency stabilizes rapidly.
- **Frequency response:** BFM units are excellent primary frequency regulators because the turbine governor can change MW output in < 1 second.
- **Plant start-up:** Many large units are brought from synchronization to load using a hybrid: BFM for initial pick-up, then sliding to Coordinated Master (CCM) for steady-state.
- **Load rejection / tripping safety:** If a sudden load rejection occurs, the boiler is already following, so **pressure surge is moderate** — fuel can be cut faster than in TFM.
- **Common in:** Older 200/500 MW subcritical units and many Indian TPS stations (e.g., NTPC Dadri, Farakka — where units run in BFM during AGC).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function Model of Boiler Following Mode

### 3.1.1 First-Order Models of Boiler and Turbine

The boiler is modeled as a slow first-order lag between **fuel demand** and **steam pressure**:

$$
G_{B}(s) = \dfrac{K_B}{\tau_B s + 1}
$$

The turbine is modeled as a fast first-order lag between **valve position** and **load output**:

$$
G_{T}(s) = \dfrac{K_T}{\tau_T s + 1}
$$

where:
- $K_B$ = boiler gain (bar per % fuel)
- $K_T$ = turbine gain (MW per % valve)
- $\tau_B$ = boiler time constant
- $\tau_T$ = turbine time constant

### 3.1.2 Closed-Loop Pressure Response in BFM

In BFM, the **header pressure is the controlled output** of the boiler loop, while the **load demand drives the turbine directly**. The closed-loop transfer function from the load disturbance $\Delta L_D(s)$ to the header pressure $P_{actual}(s)$ is derived as follows:

**Step 1:** Define load disturbance as a step decrease in steam outflow:

$$
\Delta \dot{m}_{out}(s) = \dfrac{\Delta L_D}{K_T \cdot (1 + \tau_T s)}
$$

**Step 2:** The boiler's continuity equation (energy storage) gives:

$$
\tau_B \dfrac{dP_{actual}}{dt} + P_{actual} = K_B \cdot u_f(t)
$$

**Step 3:** Take the Laplace transform:

$$
(\tau_B s + 1) \cdot P_{actual}(s) = K_B \cdot U_f(s)
$$

$$
P_{actual}(s) = \dfrac{K_B}{\tau_B s + 1} \cdot U_f(s)
$$

**Step 4:** In BFM, the **fuel command** $U_f(s)$ is driven by the pressure error through a PI controller $C_B(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$, with feedback $H_B(s) = 1$:

$$
U_f(s) = C_B(s) \cdot \left( P_{set}(s) - P_{actual}(s) \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the boiler equation:

$$
(\tau_B s + 1) \cdot P_{actual}(s) = K_B \cdot C_B(s) \cdot \left( P_{set}(s) - P_{actual}(s) \right)
$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange for the closed-loop transfer function:

$$
(\tau_B s + 1) \cdot P_{actual}(s) + K_B \cdot C_B(s) \cdot P_{actual}(s) = K_B \cdot C_B(s) \cdot P_{set}(s)
$$

$$
P_{actual}(s) \cdot \left[ \tau_B s + 1 + K_B \cdot C_B(s) \right] = K_B \cdot C_B(s) \cdot P_{set}(s)
$$

**Step 7:** Therefore the closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
\dfrac{P_{actual}(s)}{P_{set}(s)} = \dfrac{K_B \cdot C_B(s)}{\tau_B s + 1 + K_B \cdot C_B(s)}
$$

**Step 8:** Substituting $C_B(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$:

$$
\dfrac{P_{actual}(s)}{P_{set}(s)} = \dfrac{K_B \cdot \left( K_p s + K_i \right)}{\tau_B s^2 + \left( 1 + K_B K_p \right) s + K_B K_i}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The denominator is **second-order** — meaning the pressure response is critically damped or under-damped depending on $K_p, K_i$ tuning. The characteristic equation dictates whether the boiler's slow response is smoothed by the PI controller.

### 3.1.3 Settling Time and Maximum Pressure Dip

For a unit-step load disturbance $\Delta L_D$, the **maximum pressure dip** in BFM is given by:

$$
\Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta L_D}{K_B} \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-t_{peak}/\tau_B} \right)
$$

where $t_{peak} = \tau_B \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{\tau_B}{\tau_T} \right)$ when $\tau_B \gg \tau_T$:

$$
t_{peak} \approx \tau_B \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{\tau_B}{\tau_T} \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** If $\tau_B = 200$ s and $\tau_T = 1$ s, then $t_{peak} \approx 200 \cdot \ln(200) \approx 200 \cdot 5.3 = 1060$ s. The pressure dip peaks at ~17.7 minutes after a load step — illustrating the boiler's lag.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — BFM vs TFM vs CCM

| Parameter | Boiler Following (BFM) | Turbine Following (TFM) | Coordinated Control (CCM) |
|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Master** | Turbine Governor | Boiler | Shared Master |
| **Slave** | Boiler | Turbine | Turbine ↔ Boiler |
| **Initial pressure response** | Large dip | Stable (held) | Small dip |
| **Initial load response** | Fast (< 1 s) | Slow (minutes) | Moderate |
| **Frequency response** | Excellent | Poor | Excellent |
| **Header pressure stability** | Variable | Tight | Tight |
| **Boiler stress** | High | Low | Moderate |
| **Turbine stress** | Low | High (thermal fatigue) | Moderate |
| **Suitability for AGC** | Yes (primary) | No | Yes (full) |
| **Suitability for sliding pressure** | Limited | Excellent | Good |
| **Unit size preference** | Old / mid-size units | Supercritical sliding-pressure units | Modern large supercritical units |

## 3.3 Steady-State Energy Balance Derivation

At steady state, in BFM, the **stored energy change is zero**:

$$
\dfrac{dM_{stored}}{dt} = 0 \implies \dot{m}_{boiler,out} = \dot{m}_{turbine,in}
$$

The power output is:

$$
W_{net} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \left( h_{in} - h_{out} \right) \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen}
$$

For the boiler's energy balance:

$$
\dot{Q}_{fuel} \cdot \eta_{comb} = \dot{m}_{steam} \cdot \left( h_{steam} - h_{feedwater} \right) + \dot{Q}_{losses}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In BFM, $\dot{m}_{steam}$ is set by the *turbine governor*, so the **fuel rate $\dot{Q}_{fuel}$ must adapt** to match. This is the inverse of TFM, where $\dot{m}_{steam}$ is set by the boiler.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boiler Following Mode — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **signal flow and control hierarchy** of Boiler Following Mode Operation. All node IDs are alphanumeric, labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric (no bold/italics/HTML), and the graph respects the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

```mermaid

    A5 --> A8[STEAM HEADER PRESSURE TRANSDUCER PT]

    A10 --> A11[FUEL FEEDER COAL MILL CMD]
    A10 --> A12[FD FAN AIR DAMPER CMD]
    A10 --> A13[FEEDWATER PUMP SPEED CMD]

    A12 --> A14
    A14 --> A15[STEAM GENERATION MS KG PER S]

    A17 --> A8

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Reading the Diagram

- **Red Box (A3):** Turbine Governor — the **master / lead** controller in BFM.
- **Blue Box (A10):** Boiler Master — the **follower / lag** PI controller in BFM.
- **Green Box (A5):** Steam admission to the HP turbine — the **fast path** for load change.
- **Yellow Box (A1):** Load demand entry point (from AGC or operator).
- **Purple Box (A7):** Final power delivery to the grid.
- **Inner loop (A8 → A9 → A10):** The pressure feedback loop — the boiler's self-correction mechanism.

## 4.3 Sequence Flow — Time Response

```mermaid
graph LR
    T0[TIME 0 S LOAD STEP] --> T1[TIME 1 S TIV OPENS]
    T1 --> T2[TIME 1 TO 3 S STEAM INCREASES]
    T2 --> T3[TIME 5 TO 10 S HEADER PRESSURE DIPS]
    T3 --> T4[TIME 10 TO 60 S BOILER DETECTS DELTA P]
    T4 --> T5[TIME 60 TO 300 S FUEL AND AIR RAMP UP]
    T5 --> T6[TIME 300 TO 600 S PRESSURE RESTORED]
    T6 --> T7[TIME 600 PLUS S NEW STEADY STATE LOAD]

    style T0 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style T1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style T3 fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style T6 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style T7 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Read this chart left to right.** Note the **red flag at T3** — the pressure dip is the most undesirable transient of BFM. The boiler eventually catches up (T6, green = pressure restored).

## 4.4 Physical Plant Schematic (Descriptive, since physical drawings are infeasible in Mermaid)

A 500 MW subcritical unit operating in Boiler Following Mode has the following physical architecture:

- **Coal Handling Plant** → **Pulverisers (Coal Mills)** → **Boiler Furnace** (combustion)
- **FD Fan** supplies combustion air; **ID Fan** maintains furnace draft
- **Economiser** preheats feedwater using flue gas
- **Drum** (steam-water separator) at the natural circulation loop top
- **Primary Superheater → Platen SH → Final SH** → **HP Turbine** (via TIV)
- **HP Turbine exhaust → Reheater → IP Turbine → LP Turbine → Condenser**
- **Condensate Extraction Pump → LP Heaters → Deaerator → BFP → HP Heaters → Economiser**
- **Turbine Governor** is a **hydraulic / electro-hydraulic (EHC)** Woodward or GE Speedtronic governor sensing **generator frequency / MW**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define Boiler Following Mode of operation. Why is it called a Turbine-Lead / Boiler-Lag mode?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> Boiler Following Mode is a unit coordinated control strategy in which the **turbine governor acts as the master controller** and the **boiler acts as the slave/follower**.
>
> It is called **Turbine-Lead / Boiler-Lag (TLBL)** because:
> 1. The **turbine leads** — turbine inlet valves open immediately to meet a load change (fast, τ ≈ 0.5 s).
> 2. The **boiler lags** — fuel, air, and feedwater are adjusted later to restore steam pressure (slow, τ ≈ 100–300 s).
>
> The boiler "follows" the turbine's lead by sensing the resulting **header pressure drop** and ramping up combustion.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**State any three advantages and two disadvantages of Boiler Following Mode.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Advantages:**
> 1. **Fast initial load response** — turbine valves move in < 1 s, excellent for AGC and primary frequency regulation.
> 2. **Good load pick-up capability** — useful during unit start-up and grid disturbances.
> 3. **Simpler governor tuning** — turbine loop is decoupled from boiler dynamics.
>
> **Disadvantages:**
> 1. **Header pressure dip** — boiler cannot match the turbine's instant steam demand, causing transient pressure fluctuations.
> 2. **Boiler stress** — rapid fuel/air ramping increases thermal stress on thick boiler components (drum, headers, tubes).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Alert!**
> - Students often **omit the closed-loop transfer function derivation** for BFM and lose 4–5 marks.
> - In comparison questions (BFM vs TFM), do **not** just list points — **draw a labelled block diagram** for at least one mode (carries 2 marks).
> - Always state **specific time constants** (e.g., τ_B ≈ 200 s) — generic answers score poorly.
> - For numerical problems, **show units at every step** and **box the final answer**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEICT523]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of **Boiler Following Mode (BFM)** of operation in a thermal power plant. Identify the master and slave controllers. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of the boiler pressure loop in BFM, and explain why the boiler's slow time constant causes a transient pressure dip. (7 marks)

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**
>
> In BFM, the unit is controlled as follows:
> 1. **Load demand signal** $L_D$ enters the **turbine governor**, which is the **master controller** (2 marks).
> 2. The governor opens the **Turbine Inlet Valve (TIV)**, increasing steam flow into the HP turbine within ~0.5 seconds (1 mark).
> 3. The HP turbine output increases → **MW output rises** → load demand is partially met (1 mark).
> 4. The increased steam flow causes **header pressure to dip** — measured by pressure transmitter PT (1 mark).
> 5. The **boiler master PI controller** takes the pressure error and commands **fuel, air, and feedwater** to restore pressure (1 mark).
>
> (See the Mermaid block diagram in SECTION 4 for the full architecture — 1 mark for diagram.)
>
> **(b) Closed-Loop Transfer Function (7 marks):**
>
> Starting from the boiler first-order model:
>
> $$G_B(s) = \dfrac{K_B}{\tau_B s + 1}$$
>
> With PI controller $C_B(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$ and unity feedback $H_B(s) = 1$, the closed-loop pressure response (derived in SECTION 3) is:
>
> $$\dfrac{P_{actual}(s)}{P_{set}(s)} = \dfrac{K_B \cdot \left( K_p s + K_i \right)}{\tau_B s^2 + \left( 1 + K_B K_p \right) s + K_B K_i}$$
>
> **Why pressure dips:**
> 1. When load step occurs, the turbine governor immediately increases TIV opening (1 mark).
> 2. Steam leaves the boiler **faster** than it is generated (1 mark).
> 3. The energy stored in the **drum water inventory** is depleted, lowering saturation pressure (1 mark).
> 4. The boiler PI controller takes **~ τ_B ≈ 100–300 s** to ramp fuel and air up to match (1 mark).
> 5. The **time-constant mismatch** ($\tau_B \gg \tau_T$) is the fundamental reason for the pressure dip (1 mark).
> 6. The maximum dip magnitude is approximately:
>
> $$\Delta P_{max} \approx \dfrac{\Delta L_D}{K_B} \cdot \left( 1 - e^{-\tau_B \ln(\tau_B/\tau_T) / \tau_B} \right) = \dfrac{\Delta L_D}{K_B} \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{\tau_T}{\tau_B} \right)$$
>
> (1 mark for this expression).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEICT523]

**(a)** Compare **Boiler Following Mode (BFM)** and **Turbine Following Mode (TFM)** on at least eight parameters. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 210 MW subcritical unit has a boiler time constant $\tau_B = 180$ s and a turbine time constant $\tau_T = 0.8$ s. For a 10 % load step, calculate (i) the approximate time to reach maximum pressure dip, and (ii) the maximum pressure dip magnitude if the boiler gain $K_B = 0.6$ bar per % load. (7 marks)

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 0.5 mark per row, 1.5 marks for the analysis):**
>
> | Parameter | BFM | TFM |
> |-----------|-----|-----|
> | Master | Turbine | Boiler |
> | Slave | Boiler | Turbine |
> | Load response | Fast (< 1 s) | Slow (minutes) |
> | Pressure stability | Loosely held | Tightly held |
> | AGC suitability | Excellent (primary) | Poor |
> | Boiler thermal stress | High | Low |
> | Sliding pressure suitability | Poor | Excellent |
> | Modern unit preference | Old/mid-size | Supercritical |
>
> **Analysis (1.5 marks):** BFM is preferred for **frequency response and fast load pick-up**, while TFM is preferred for **sliding-pressure operation and reducing boiler thermal stress**. Modern large units use a hybrid **Coordinated Control Mode (CCM)** to combine both benefits.
>
> **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**
>
> Given: $\tau_B = 180$ s, $\tau_T = 0.8$ s, $\Delta L_D = 10$ % load step, $K_B = 0.6$ bar/%.
>
> **(i) Time to maximum pressure dip (3 marks):**
>
> $$t_{peak} = \tau_B \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{\tau_B}{\tau_T} \right) = 180 \cdot \ln \left( \dfrac{180}{0.8} \right)$$
>
> $$= 180 \cdot \ln(225) = 180 \cdot 5.416 = 974.9 \text{ s} \approx 975 \text{ s}$$
>
> **Answer: $t_{peak} \approx 975$ seconds (≈ 16.25 minutes)** ✓
>
> **(ii) Maximum pressure dip (4 marks):**
>
> $$\Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta L_D}{K_B} \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{\tau_T}{\tau_B} \right)$$
>
> $$= \dfrac{10}{0.6} \cdot \left( 1 - \dfrac{0.8}{180} \right)$$
>
> $$= 16.667 \cdot (1 - 0.00444) = 16.667 \cdot 0.9956$$
>
> $$= 16.59 \text{ bar} \approx \boxed{16.6 \text{ bar}}$$
>
> **Engineering comment (1 mark):** A 16.6 bar dip on a unit with 140 bar normal pressure would trigger **pressure low alarms** and possibly **runback / load shedding interlocks** — illustrating why BFM requires tight tuning or a sliding-pressure strategy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **BFM = Turbine-Lead / Boiler-Lag (TLBL)** — turbine is master, boiler is slave.
- [x] **Turbine time constant:** $\tau_T \approx 0.2$–$2$ s (fast).
- [x] **Boiler time constant:** $\tau_B \approx 100$–$300$ s (slow).
- [x] **Hallmark transient:** Header pressure **dips** during load step.
- [x] **TIV opens first** in BFM; fuel/air/feedwater follow later.
- [x] **Frequency response:** Excellent — preferred for **AGC** and primary frequency regulation.
- [x] **Boiler stress:** Higher than TFM due to fast fuel/air ramping.
- [x] **Closed-loop transfer function:**
  $$\dfrac{P_{actual}(s)}{P_{set}(s)} = \dfrac{K_B \left( K_p s + K_i \right)}{\tau_B s^2 + \left( 1 + K_B K_p \right) s + K_B K_i}$$
- [x] **Time to peak pressure dip:** $t_{peak} = \tau_B \ln(\tau_B / \tau_T)$.
- [x] **Maximum pressure dip:** $\Delta P_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta L_D}{K_B}\left(1 - \dfrac{\tau_T}{\tau_B}\right)$.
- [x] **Common in:** Older 200/500 MW subcritical Indian TPS units running in AGC.
- [x] **Contrasted with:** TFM (Boiler-Lead) and CCM (Coordinated Control Mode).
- [x] **Key valves:** Turbine Inlet Valve (TIV), FD/ID fan dampers, coal mill feeders, BFP speed.
- [x] **Governor droop setting:** typically **4–5 %** for grid-synchronized units.
- [x] **KTU marking tip:** Always draw the **block diagram** when explaining control modes — it carries **2 marks** even in 3-mark questions.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute memorization trick:** Remember the order of speed — **T (Turbine) is T-Fast, B (Boiler) is B-Slow** → T < B in time constant, but T leads B in action. Mnemonic: **"**T**urbo **F**irst, **B**oiler **B**ackup**"** → BFM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Turbine Following Mode Operation — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Turbine Following Mode (TFM)** is a coordinated boiler–turbine control strategy in which the **turbine governor (valve)** is the *primary, fast-acting load controller*, while the **boiler firing rate** is treated as the *secondary, slow-acting controller* that compensates for the resulting steam pressure deviation.

In this mode:

- The **turbine valve position** directly regulates the **active power output** delivered to the grid.
- The **boiler (fuel + air + feedwater)** is slaved to the turbine to **restore and maintain steam pressure (drum pressure / SH pressure)** at its set-point.
- Frequency regulation responsibility is delegated primarily to the **turbine** rather than the boiler.

Mathematically, the TFM control structure can be expressed as:

$$
P_{out} = f(V_t) \quad \text{(turbine valve is master)}
$$

$$
P_{steam} \rightarrow \text{PID} \rightarrow F_{fuel} \quad \text{(boiler follows)}
$$

where $V_t$ is the turbine throttle valve opening and $F_{fuel}$ is the fuel firing rate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy — The Two-Person Bicycle:**
> Imagine two cyclists riding a tandem bicycle.
> - The **front rider (turbine)** sees the road ahead and steers quickly to avoid potholes — that is, reacts to sudden load changes.
> - The **back rider (boiler)** pedals steadily to maintain a comfortable cruising speed — that is, slowly restores steam pressure.
> 
> In **Turbine Following Mode**, the *front rider (turbine) is in charge of direction*, and the *back rider (boiler) keeps pedalling to keep up*.

In simple terms: **the turbine "leads" the load change, the boiler "follows" to keep pressure healthy.**

## 1.3 Physical Parameters & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (500 MW Unit) |
|-----------|--------|------------------------------|
| Steam Pressure (SH) | $P_s$ | **150 – 250 bar** |
| Steam Temperature | $T_s$ | **535 – 600 °C** |
| Turbine Valve Travel | $V_t$ | 0 – 100 % |
| Fuel Flow | $F_{fuel}$ | 0 – 100 % MCR |
| Active Power Output | $P_{out}$ | 0 – 500 MW |
| Drum Level Set-point | $L_d$ | ± 50 mm of NWL |

## 1.4 Distinguishing Features

> [!TIP]
> **Three signature traits of TFM:**
> 1. **Turbine = fast loop** (response time ≈ **2 – 5 s**)
> 2. **Boiler = slow loop** (response time ≈ **60 – 300 s**)
> 3. **Steam pressure = controlled variable for boiler** (temporarily deviates during load swings)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Philosophy — Step-by-Step Logic

The Turbine Following Mode can be decomposed into a clear **cascade control architecture**:

- **Step 1 — Load Demand Arrives**
  The grid dispatcher / AGC sends a load set-point change $\Delta P_{ref}$ to the unit.

- **Step 2 — Turbine Valve Responds (Inner / Fast Loop)**
  The electro-hydraulic governor (EHG) immediately repositions the turbine throttle valves to admit more (or less) steam into the HP/IP/LP stages. This delivers the required power almost instantaneously.

- **Step 3 — Steam Pressure Deviation Occurs**
  As more steam is drawn by the turbine, the boiler's drum / superheater pressure $P_s$ momentarily **falls** (during load increase) because the boiler cannot raise firing rate instantly. Conversely, during load reduction, $P_s$ momentarily **rises**.

- **Step 4 — Boiler Pressure Controller Acts (Outer / Slow Loop)**
  The boiler master pressure controller senses $\Delta P_s = P_{s,sp} - P_s$ and commands the fuel, air, and feedwater flows to **restore** $P_s$ to its set-point. This may take several minutes for large, drum-type boilers.

- **Step 5 — Equilibrium Re-established**
  Once $P_s$ returns to set-point, the boiler's steam output matches the turbine's steam demand, and the unit operates at the new load with stable parameters.

## 2.2 Why "Turbine Following" and not "Boiler Following"?

> [!NOTE]
> The naming convention is rooted in **which device follows the load signal**:
> - In **BFM (Boiler Following Mode)** — the boiler leads and the turbine follows.
> - In **TFM (Turbine Following Mode)** — the turbine leads and the boiler follows.
> 
> KTU examiners frequently test this nomenclature precisely.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{out} = \dot{m}_s \cdot \Delta h \cdot \eta_{m} \cdot \eta_{g}$ | Turbine power output | **MW** |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_s = K_v \cdot P_s \cdot f(V_t)$ | Stodola-type valve flow | **kg/s** |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta P_{out}(s)}{\Delta V_t(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{1 + \tau_t s}$ | Turbine transfer function | **MW/%** |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\Delta P_s(s)}{\Delta F_{fuel}(s)} = \dfrac{K_b \, e^{-T_d s}}{1 + \tau_b s}$ | Boiler transfer function (with dead time $T_d$) | **bar/%** |
| 5 | $\tau_p \dfrac{dP_s}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$ | Steam pressure dynamics | **bar/s** |
| 6 | $\Delta f = -\dfrac{\Delta P_L}{B}$ | Steady-state frequency deviation (single area) | **Hz** |
| 7 | $ACE = B \cdot \Delta f + \Delta P_{tie}$ | Area Control Error (for AGC integration) | **MW** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for TFM Operation:**
> - Steam pressure deviation must remain within $\pm \mathbf{5\%}$ of MCR to avoid safety valve lift-off.
> - Turbine valve must operate in the **non-choked linear region** for predictable response.
> - Boiler must respond within its **dwell time $T_d$** (typically **30 – 120 s**) to avoid drum-level trip.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Frequency-sensitive grids** prefer TFM because the turbine governor has a **droop characteristic** that immediately counteracts frequency deviations.
- **Isolated / islanded grids** and **diesel-solar hybrid stations** often mandate TFM for quick load pickup.
- **Combined Cycle Gas Turbine (CCGT) plants** use a derivative of TFM called **Turbine-Follows-GT**, where the steam turbine follows the exhaust heat of the gas turbine.
- In **nuclear power stations**, TFM is the *default* mode because reactor power cannot be ramped quickly — the turbine absorbs load transients while the reactor follows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Turbine Power Output in TFM

Starting from the first law applied to a steady-flow turbine stage:

$$
W_t = \dot{m}_s \, (h_1 - h_2) \, \eta_{isentropic} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen}
$$

Substituting $\eta_t = \eta_{isentropic} \cdot \eta_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gen}$ for clarity:

$$
W_t = \dot{m}_s \, \Delta h \, \eta_t
$$

where:
- $\dot{m}_s$ = steam mass flow rate through the turbine
- $\Delta h$ = specific enthalpy drop across the turbine
- $\eta_t$ = combined turbine-generator efficiency

In TFM, the turbine valve is the controller, so $\dot{m}_s$ is a function of valve opening $V_t$ and steam chest pressure $P_s$:

$$
\dot{m}_s = K_v \cdot P_s \cdot V_t
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{out} = K_v \cdot P_s \cdot V_t \cdot \Delta h \cdot \eta_t\,}
$$

Taking incremental deviations around the operating point:

$$
\Delta P_{out} = K_v \cdot \Delta h \cdot \eta_t \cdot \left( P_s \cdot \Delta V_t + V_t \cdot \Delta P_s \right)
$$

> **Key Inference:** When the **turbine valve moves first (TFM)**, $P_s$ temporarily drops, so the *initial* power pickup is dominated by the $P_s \cdot \Delta V_t$ term. The $V_t \cdot \Delta P_s$ term is a **disturbance** that the boiler must correct.

## 3.2 Derivation — Closed-Loop Transfer Function of TFM

Let us define:
- $G_t(s) = \dfrac{K_t}{1 + \tau_t s}$ — turbine + valve dynamics
- $G_b(s) = \dfrac{K_b \, e^{-T_d s}}{1 + \tau_b s}$ — boiler dynamics (with delay)
- $G_g(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau_g s}$ — generator dynamics
- $H(s) = 1$ — unity feedback

The block diagram of TFM in a single-area context gives the **closed-loop transfer function from load demand $P_d$ to power output $P_{out}$**:

$$
\frac{P_{out}(s)}{P_d(s)} = \frac{G_t(s)\, G_g(s)}{1 + G_t(s)\, G_g(s)\, G_b(s)\, H(s)}
$$

Substituting (ignoring $e^{-T_d s}$ initially for simplicity):

$$
\frac{P_{out}(s)}{P_d(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K_t}{1+\tau_t s} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+\tau_g s}}{1 + \dfrac{K_t}{(1+\tau_t s)(1+\tau_g s)} \cdot \dfrac{K_b}{1+\tau_b s}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(1+\tau_t s)(1+\tau_g s)(1+\tau_b s)$:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{P_{out}(s)}{P_d(s)} = \frac{K_t (1+\tau_b s)}{(1+\tau_t s)(1+\tau_g s)(1+\tau_b s) + K_t K_b}\,}
$$

**Analysis of poles and stability:**

- The characteristic equation is:

$$
(1+\tau_t s)(1+\tau_g s)(1+\tau_b s) + K_t K_b = 0
$$

- Expanding:

$$
\tau_t \tau_g \tau_b s^3 + (\tau_t \tau_g + \tau_t \tau_b + \tau_g \tau_b) s^2 + (\tau_t + \tau_g + \tau_b) s + (1 + K_t K_b) = 0
$$

- Applying the **Routh–Hurwitz criterion** for a third-order polynomial $a_3 s^3 + a_2 s^2 + a_1 s + a_0 = 0$:

$$
a_2 a_1 > a_3 a_0
$$

- Substituting coefficients:

$$
(\tau_t \tau_g + \tau_t \tau_b + \tau_g \tau_b)(\tau_t + \tau_g + \tau_b) > \tau_t \tau_g \tau_b (1 + K_t K_b)
$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** For **TFM**, the *high loop gain* $K_t K_b$ from the slow boiler path can drive the system to instability if $\tau_b$ is large. Hence, **boiler controller gain must be detuned** in pure TFM — this is the primary drawback of the mode.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — TFM vs BFM vs CCM

| Property | **Turbine Following (TFM)** | **Boiler Following (BFM)** | **Coordinated Control (CCM)** |
|----------|------------------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Master controller | Turbine valve | Boiler firing rate | Both, optimised |
| Controlled variable (fast loop) | Power output | Steam pressure | Power + pressure |
| Speed of load pickup | **Fast (≈ 2–5 s)** | Slow (≈ 60–300 s) | Fast (≈ 5–10 s) |
| Steam pressure deviation | **Large (transient)** | Small (controlled) | Very small |
| Suitability | **Peak load / frequency response** | Base load (steady) | Cycling / two-shifting |
| Stability margin | Lower (high loop gain) | Higher | Highest (with decoupling) |
| Drum level control stress | Higher | Lower | Moderate |
| Common in | Nuclear, Hydro-steam, Islanded | Large coal units (base) | Modern supercritical units |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Notice that TFM's *advantage* (fast load pickup) is also its *disadvantage* (steam pressure transient). This trade-off is the single most-tested concept in this topic.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — TFM Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Load Demand P_d] --> B[Turbine Governor]
    B --> C[Turbine Throttle Valve]
    C --> D[Steam Chest Dynamics]
    D --> E[Turbine Generator]
    E --> F[Power Output P_out]
    F --> G[Grid Frequency f]
    G --> H[Frequency Feedback]
    H --> B

    D --> I[Steam Pressure P_s]
    I --> J[Boiler Pressure PID]
    J --> K[Fuel Air Flow Control]
    K --> L[Combustion in Furnace]
    L --> M[Steam Generation]
    M --> D

    F --> N[Power Sensor]
    N -.-> B

    classDef fastLoop fill:#FFD580,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slowLoop fill:#A0D8B4,stroke:#2E8B57,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plant fill:#9BC4E2,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class B,C,D,E,F,G,H,N fastLoop
    class I,J,K,L,M slowLoop
    class A plant
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Load Change Event in TFM

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant GRID as Grid Dispatcher
    participant T as Turbine Governor
    participant V as Throttle Valve
    participant S as Steam Chest
    participant B as Boiler Controller
    participant F as Furnace

    GRID->>T: Load step up ΔP_d
    Note over T: Fast response (~2-5 s)
    T->>V: Open valve by ΔV_t
    V->>S: Increase steam flow
    S-->>T: Power output rises
    S-->>B: Pressure falls ΔP_s (negative)
    Note over B: Slow response (~60-300 s)
    B->>F: Increase fuel ΔF_fuel
    F->>S: Restore pressure to set-point
    Note over S: Equilibrium reached
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — TFM Operating States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SteadyLoad
    SteadyLoad --> LoadRise: Grid demands more power
    LoadRise --> PressureDip: Turbine valve opens
    PressureDip --> BoilerCorrects: Boiler pressure controller acts
    BoilerCorrects --> NewSteady: Pressure restored
    NewSteady --> SteadyLoad
    SteadyLoad --> LoadFall: Grid demands less power
    LoadFall --> PressureRise: Turbine valve closes
    PressureRise --> BoilerCorrects
    BoilerCorrects --> NewSteady
    NewSteady --> [*]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The *yellow* cluster is the **fast turbine loop** (TFM master), the *green* cluster is the **slow boiler loop** (TFM slave), and the *blue* cluster is the **plant boundary** (grid + demand). This colour-coded layering is exactly how KTU examiners expect you to sketch it in the answer booklet.
