## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define **Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation (TSI)**. List any **four** parameters monitored by it.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation (TSI) is a continuous, real-time, integrated condition-monitoring and protection system installed on steam, gas, and hydro turbines. It measures, displays, alarms, and initiates automatic turbine trips on critical mechanical parameters of the rotating assembly to prevent catastrophic failure, in accordance with **API 670** and **IEC 60034-14**.
>
> **Four parameters (½ mark each, total 2 marks):**
> 1. Shaft radial vibration (X and Y probes at each bearing)
> 2. Axial displacement (thrust position)
> 3. Differential expansion (rotor vs. cylinder)
> 4. Shaft speed / overspeed detection
>
> *(Acceptable also: cylinder expansion, eccentricity, keyphasor phase reference, valve position, bearing temperature.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023]**

Why are **two eddy-current probes (X and Y)** mounted orthogonally at each journal bearing instead of a single probe?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A single probe can only measure the **scalar magnitude** of vibration along one axis. It cannot distinguish the *shape* of the shaft-centre motion inside the bearing clearance. (1 mark)
>
> Two orthogonal probes (X = horizontal, Y = vertical, separated by 90°) provide the **two-dimensional coordinates** of the shaft centre at every instant. (1 mark)
>
> When X(t) and Y(t) are plotted against each other, they form an **orbit plot** (Lissajous figure). The orbit reveals whether the motion is a clean circle (synchronous whirl), a figure-eight (half-frequency whirl), a forward or backward precession, or a chaotic rub pattern — diagnostics impossible with a single probe. (1 mark)

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the construction and working of an **eddy-current proximity probe** as used in a TSI for shaft vibration monitoring. State its linear operating range and typical sensitivity.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 3000 RPM turbine has a vibration probe installed at a bearing. The DC gap voltage is **10 V** at a mechanical gap of **1.5 mm**. Sensitivity is **8 V/mm**. The vibration amplitude (pk-pk) is **80 µm**. Calculate the **minimum and maximum probe output voltages** and the **vibration in m/s² peak** at the running frequency.

---

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) Eddy-Current Proximity Probe [7 marks]**

**Construction (2 marks):**

1. **Probe tip coil**: A small coil wound on a ferrite core, encapsulated in a stainless-steel housing. The coil faces the rotor surface through a thin wear-resistant tip.
2. **Extension cable**: A triaxial (or coaxial) cable carries the RF drive signal and the modulated return signal. Length is calibrated (commonly 1 m, 5 m, 9 m) to maintain linearisation.
3. **Proximitor (signal conditioner)**: An external module containing the RF oscillator, rectifier, and amplifier. It is *not* part of the probe but is matched to it.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. The RF oscillator drives the probe coil at ≈ **1 MHz** with constant amplitude.
2. The high-frequency magnetic field from the coil induces **eddy currents** in the conductive rotor surface facing the probe.
3. These eddy currents produce a **counter-flux** that increases the *effective impedance* of the coil, the magnitude of the change being a function of the **gap** between coil and rotor.
4. The proximitor measures this impedance change, rectifies and amplifies it, producing a **DC voltage linearly proportional to the gap** in the linear operating region.
5. The dynamic (vibratory) component of the gap appears as an **AC ripple** superimposed on the DC level. The two components are separated by the monitor: **DC** → gap / axial position; **AC** → vibration amplitude.

**Key specifications (2 marks):**

- Linear range: **0.5 mm – 2.5 mm** typical (must be respected for accuracy).
- Sensitivity: **2 – 8 V/mm** typical.
- Frequency response: **4 Hz – 10 kHz** (true displacement below ~1 kHz).
- Probe diameter: **5 mm, 8 mm, or 11 mm** standard.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2).**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given:** $V_{DC,centre} = 10\,V$, $x_0 = 1.5\,mm$, $K = 8\,V/mm$, $X_{pp} = 80\,\mu m$, $N = 3000\,RPM$.

**Step 1 — Peak excursion (1 mark):**

$$
X_{pk} = \frac{X_{pp}}{2} = \frac{80}{2} = 40\,\mu m = 0.040\,mm
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum and maximum gap (1 mark):**

$$
x_{min} = 1.5 - 0.040 = 1.460\,mm
$$

$$
x_{max} = 1.5 + 0.040 = 1.540\,mm
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum and maximum output voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_{max} = 10 + 8\,(1.5 - 1.460) = 10 + 0.320 = 10.320\,V
$$

$$
V_{min} = 10 + 8\,(1.5 - 1.540) = 10 - 0.320 = 9.680\,V
$$

Output swing = **9.680 V – 10.320 V** (pk-pk = 0.640 V).

**Step 4 — Running frequency (1 mark):**

$$
f = \frac{N}{60} = \frac{3000}{60} = 50\,Hz
$$

**Step 5 — Peak acceleration (2 marks):**

$$
A_{pk} = (2\pi f)^2 \cdot X_{pk}
$$

$$
A_{pk} = (2\pi \cdot 50)^2 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-6}\,m
$$

$$
A_{pk} = (314.16)^2 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
A_{pk} \approx 98700 \cdot 40 \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.95\,m/s^2
$$

**Final Answer (boxed/highlight):** $V_{max} = 10.32\,V$, $V_{min} = 9.68\,V$, $A_{pk} \approx 3.95\,m/s^2$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a labelled block diagram, describe the **complete architecture of a modern Turbine Supervisory System**. Explain the function of **API 670 2-out-of-3 trip logic** for overspeed protection.

**(b) [7 marks]** A 60-tooth phonic wheel produces **2400 pulses in 1 second**. Compute the shaft **RPM**. If the rated speed is **3000 RPM** and the trip setting is **+10 %**, determine (i) the trip speed, and (ii) the number of pulses per second at the trip speed.

---

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) Architecture of a Modern TSI [7 marks]**

**Block diagram (refer Section 4.1) — 3 marks.**

**Major sub-systems (3 marks):**

1. **Transducer layer** — proximity probes, LVDTs, RTDs, speed pickups, mounted on bearings, casing, valves, and shaft.
2. **Monitor rack** — one module per parameter per probe; performs signal conditioning, linearisation, peak/RMS computation, FFT (in advanced systems), and display driving. Typically Bently Nevada 3500, Emerson CSI 6500, or ABB Alert.
3. **Trip / Alarm relay layer** — dual or triple-redundant relays implementing **2-out-of-3 voting** logic per API 670. A single faulty probe cannot cause a false trip; two coincident trip signals are required to actually trip the turbine.
4. **Operator HMI and annunciator** — coloured lamps (green = OK, amber = alarm, red = trip), CRT/LCD panels, sequence-of-event recorders with 1 ms time resolution.
5. **Plant DCS interface** — serial (Modbus) or Ethernet (TCP/IP) gateway sending all measured values and alarms to the central control room.

**API 670 2-out-of-3 Trip Logic (1 mark):**

Three independent speed probes (redundant and physically separated) feed three comparators set at the trip RPM. The outputs enter a **2-out-of-3 voter**: if *any two* of the three comparators signal "trip", the turbine is tripped. This eliminates single-point-of-failure vulnerability — one faulty probe cannot cause a spurious trip, and one missed signal cannot suppress a real trip.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute RPM from pulse count (2 marks):**

Pulse frequency: $f_p = 2400 / 1 = 2400\,Hz$.

$$
RPM = \frac{60\,f_p}{N_{teeth}} = \frac{60 \times 2400}{60} = 2400\,RPM
$$

**Step 2 — Trip speed (2 marks):**

$$
N_{trip} = 1.10 \times 3000 = 3300\,RPM
$$

**Step 3 — Pulse frequency at trip speed (3 marks):**

Rotor turns at $3300 / 60 = 55$ rev/s.

Each revolution produces **60 pulses**, so:

$$
f_{p,trip} = 55 \times 60 = 3300\,Hz = 3300\,pulses/second
$$

**Final Answer (highlight):** Operating speed = **2400 RPM**; Trip speed = **3300 RPM**; Pulse rate at trip = **3300 pulses/second**.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing displacement with velocity or acceleration** — the probe measures *displacement*, but KTU may quote vibration severity limits in *velocity (mm/s RMS)* per ISO 10816-3. Always convert: $V_{pk} = 2\pi f X_{pk}$.
> 2. **Forgetting that X-Y probes measure radial vibration, while axial displacement uses a *single* probe facing the thrust collar.** Don't say "two axial probes" — that's wrong.
> 3. **Using the wrong instrument for the wrong parameter** — LVDT (not eddy current) is used for differential expansion and casing expansion because the rotor end is mechanically, not electromagnetically, accessible.
> 4. **Ignoring the pk-pk vs pk distinction** — TSI displays show *pk-pk* (peak-to-peak), not pk. Always halve before computing velocity/accel.
> 5. **Trip logic = 2 out of 3 voting** — never say "3 out of 3" or "any one probe trips the turbine".
> 6. **Speed formula**: many students write $RPM = f_p \times 60$ instead of $RPM = \dfrac{60 f_p}{N_{teeth}}$. Forgetting the divisor costs 2 marks.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] TSI = integrated monitoring + protection for turbines; standard = **API 670**.
- [x] **Eight core parameters**: vibration (X-Y), axial displacement, differential expansion, casing expansion, eccentricity, speed, keyphasor, bearing temperature.
- [x] **Eddy-current probe** = radial vibration, axial position; range **0.5–2.5 mm**, sensitivity **2–8 V/mm**, freq. response **4 Hz–10 kHz**.
- [x] **LVDT** = differential expansion, casing expansion, valve position (where rotor-end is not accessible).
- [x] **Pt-100 RTD** = bearing temperature; alarm 95 °C, trip 110 °C.
- [x] **Speed probe** views a phonic wheel: $RPM = \dfrac{60 f_p}{N_{teeth}}$.
- [x] **Keyphasor** = once-per-revolution pulse for phase-locked vibration analysis.
- [x] Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120 f / P$. For 50 Hz, 2-pole: **3000 RPM**.
- [x] Trip speed: **+10 % above rated** = 3300 RPM.
- [x] Conversion chain: $V_{pk} = 2\pi f X_{pk}$, $A_{pk} = (2\pi f)^2 X_{pk}$, $X_{pp} = 2 X_{pk}$.
- [x] Two probes X-Y at 90° → **orbit plot** for vibration diagnosis.
- [x] Sub-synchronous 0.5× vibration → **oil whirl**.
- [x] 2× vibration → **misalignment**.
- [x] 1× vibration → **unbalance** (most common).
- [x] Trip logic = **2 out of 3 voting** per API 670.
- [x] Probe output: $V_{DC} = V_0 + K(x_0 - x)$ — **linear only within the calibrated range**.
- [x] Eccentricity is monitored at **low speed (< 600 RPM)** during start-up to detect rotor bow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automatic Turbine Runs Up Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT523 - POWER PLANT INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automatic Turbine Runs Up Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Automatic Turbine Run-Up System (ATRUS)** is a coordinated sequence of electro-hydraulic, pneumatic, and digital control actions that brings a steam, gas, or hydro turbine from a cold/cold-rolling standstill to rated synchronous speed under continuously supervised acceleration, while simultaneously protecting the machine against unacceptable levels of **thermal stress**, **mechanical stress**, **vibration**, and **rotor-dynamic instability**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PEICT523 – Power Plant Instrumentation), the run-up system forms a sub-system of the **Turbine Sequential Control (TSC)** and interfaces with:
- Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation (TSI)
- Electro-Hydraulic Governor (EHG / DEH)
- Boiler-Turbine Coordinated Control (BTCC)
- Distributed Control System (DCS) / Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The run-up system is **not** the governor; it is a higher-level sequencer that **drives** the governor through a pre-engineered speed/load trajectory. The governor closes the inner loop on speed; the run-up sequencer closes the outer loop on **stress and time**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Car Warm-Up

Imagine starting a sports car that has been parked in sub-zero conditions for a week. A sensible driver does not floor the accelerator immediately. Instead, the driver:

1. Checks oil pressure (lube-oil priming).
2. Lets the engine idle for a few minutes (warm-up hold / soaking).
3. Slowly increases RPM up to a target, pausing briefly at intermediate speeds (warm-up holds at 600, 1200, 2400 rpm).
4. Watches the temperature gauge, oil pressure gauge, and vibration lights.
5. Only after the engine warms up does the driver engage full power.

A turbine run-up system does **exactly** this — but with orders-of-magnitude tighter control, because a 250 MW rotor weighing ~80 tonnes spinning at 3000 rpm cannot tolerate even brief excursions.

### 1.3 Key Quantitative Metrics & Standard Constants

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Cold-start acceleration rate | **60 – 120** | rpm/min |
| Warm-start acceleration rate | **150 – 300** | rpm/min |
| Hot-start acceleration rate | **400 – 600** | rpm/min |
| Critical speed (1st lateral) | **1600 – 2200** | rpm |
| Trip threshold vibration | **6.0 – 12.7** | mm/s RMS |
| Lube-oil priming pressure | **0.8 – 2.0** | bar (g) |
| Warm-up hold temperature gradient limit | **≤ 80** | °C/h |
| Permissible rotor bow | **≤ 50** | µm |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard gravitational acceleration used in Rayleigh's critical-speed equation is **g = 9.81 m/s²**, and the universal gas constant **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** appears in combustion-based gas turbine heat calculations.

### 1.4 Why Automatic (and not Manual)?

In modern units (>100 MW), manual start-up is **unsafe, unrepeatable, and uneconomical** because:
- The permissible acceleration window is only a few rpm/min wide.
- Holding a manual throttle setting constant to within ±0.5 % for hours is humanly impossible.
- Sequential permissives (oil pressure OK, turning gear engaged, condensate pump running, etc.) number > 40; missed permissives cause catastrophic damage.
- A typical cold start lasts **6 – 10 hours**; operator fatigue is real.
- Insurance and OEM warranties *require* automated, logged start-ups.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of the Run-Up System

The ATRUS has four interlocking layers, executed in parallel (not serial):

**Layer 1 – Permissive Matrix (Boolean Logic)**
- All "GO" conditions must be TRUE before the next step can begin.
- Typical inputs: lube-oil pressure ≥ 1.2 bar, turning gear running, condensate pump ≥ 1, boiler drum level ≥ 50 %, HP/IP bypass valve closed, fire detected (gas turbines only), vibration probe healthy.

**Layer 2 – Warm-Up Hold Progression (Profile Generator)**
- A software **profile generator** outputs a target speed N_ref(t) as a function of:
  - Measured metal temperature T_metal
  - Temperature rate dT/dt
  - Time elapsed since crank
- The profile uses pre-engineered "hold points" at 600, 1200, 1800, 2400, 2950 rpm.

**Layer 3 – Speed/Stress Closed Loop**
- The **EHG** receives N_ref and compares with actual N (from magnetic pickup or toothed-wheel tachometer).
- Error drives HP valve position through a PID + servo-valve hydraulic actuator.

**Layer 4 – Trip & Abort Protection**
- Independent hardwired trip channels on overspeed, axial displacement, loss of lube oil, vibration, and rotor eccentricity.
- These **override** the run-up sequencer and command fast-closing valves.

### 2.2 Sequence-of-Operation (Step Logic)

1. **INITIATE**: Operator presses "START" on DCS HMI. Master permissives verified.
2. **TURNING GEAR**: Motorised barring gear engages rotor at 1–3 rpm to bow-check.
3. **LUBE OIL PRIMING**: AC + DC oil pumps raise header pressure to 1.5 bar. DC backup verified.
4. **CONTROL OIL**: HP hydraulic oil at 120 bar established for actuator stroking.
5. **COLD ROLL / CRANK**: Steam admitted (steam turbines) or light-off initiated (gas turbines). HP bypass opens to control acceleration to ~600 rpm.
6. **FIRST HOLD (600 rpm)**: Soak for Δt₁ such that metal temperature rise ≤ 80 °C/h. Vibration sampled.
7. **CRITICAL-SPEED BYPASS**: Ramp past 1st critical (typically 1600–2200 rpm) at the **highest** permissible acceleration to minimize dwell time in the resonant zone.
8. **INTERMEDIATE HOLDS (1200, 1800, 2400)**: Re-soak until thermal equilibrium.
9. **SYNCHRONIZATION SPEED (2950 rpm for 50 Hz, 3560 for 60 Hz)**: Final trim; AVR + syncroscope engage.
10. **LOAD-UP**: Governor transferred to load-control mode; load ramp at 2–5 MW/min.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of shaft | rpm |
| 2 | $N_c \approx \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{mm}}}$ (Rayleigh) | $\delta$ = static deflection under own weight | mm |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{60\,T_{net}}{2\pi J}$ | Acceleration rate | rpm/s² |
| 4 | $T_{hold} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{allowed}}{dT/dt_{max}}$ | Soaking time at hold point | s or min |
| 5 | $\theta^* = \dfrac{T(r,t) - T_i}{T_s - T_i} = f(Bi, Fo)$ | Dimensionless temperature | – |
| 6 | $Bi = \dfrac{h\,r}{k}$ | Biot number | – |
| 7 | $Fo = \dfrac{\alpha_t\,t}{r^2}$ | Fourier number | – |
| 8 | $\sigma_{th} = \dfrac{E\,\alpha_t\,\Delta T}{1-\nu}\,\Phi(Bi,Fo)$ | Thermal stress | Pa |
| 9 | $N_{run-up} = N_{sync} - N_{critical}$ | Speed window for run-through | rpm |
| 10 | $\Delta N_{critical} = N_c \cdot \sqrt{2\,\zeta}$ | Pass-band around Nc | rpm |

Where:
- **ζ** = damping ratio (typically 0.02 – 0.08 for turbine rotors)
- **α_t** = linear thermal expansion coefficient (~12 × 10⁻⁶ /K for steel)
- **E** = Young's modulus (~2 × 10¹¹ Pa)
- **ν** = Poisson's ratio (~0.3)
- **h** = convective heat-transfer coefficient
- **k** = thermal conductivity
- **T_i** = initial metal temperature, **T_s** = steam/surface temperature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For exam answers, **always state the assumption of plane sections remain plane** (single-axis symmetry, no thermal shock at outer surface when internal heat conduction is dominant — i.e., large Bi).

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

Run-up systems are the single biggest determinant of:
- **Thermal-efficiency** preservation during cycling (combined-cycle plants cycle 200+ times/year).
- **Component life** (Creep, Low-Cycle-Fatigue) — every start = 1 fatigue cycle.
- **Emissions compliance** — slow light-off means longer HC/CO emission window in gas turbines.
- **Grid code compliance** — synchronization must occur within prescribed time after black-start.

### 2.5 Why a Closed Loop is Mandatory

Open-loop "ramp the valve linearly" cannot compensate for:
- Steam temperature drift (cold-reheat vs hot-reheat).
- Condenser vacuum loss → back-pressure spike.
- Bearing temperature transient → viscosity drop → whip onset.

The run-up profile generator dynamically recomputes N_ref(t) every scan (typically 100 ms) using **measured** thermal data, not a fixed setpoint.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rayleigh's Critical Speed Formula

**Statement:** For a uniform, simply-supported rotor with mass *m* and shaft stiffness *k*, the first lateral critical speed equals the speed at which the centrifugal force equals the elastic restoring force.

**Step 1 — Static deflection under gravity:**
When the shaft is horizontal and supported at the bearings, gravity produces a mid-span deflection $\delta$ such that the elastic restoring force equals the weight:

$$
m g = k \delta
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium at critical speed (dynamic deflection y):**
At angular velocity $\omega$, the rotor experiences a centrifugal force directed radially outward. The deflection y produces a restoring force $k y$ and a centrifugal force $m \omega^2 y$. At resonance, these two forces are in equilibrium (no damping in the ideal Rayleigh case):

$$
m\,\omega^2\,y = k\,y
$$

**Step 3 — Simplifying (y ≠ 0):**

$$
\omega_n^2 = \dfrac{k}{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $k/m = g/\delta$ from Step 1:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}
$$

**Step 5 — Converting rad/s to rpm:**

$$
N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\,\omega_n = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical constant in SI (δ in metres):**

$$
N_c = 9.549 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{9.81}{\delta}} \approx \dfrac{29.9}{\sqrt{\delta}}
$$

If $\delta$ is preferred in millimetres, since $1\ \text{m} = 1000\ \text{mm}$:

$$
N_c \approx \dfrac{29.9}{\sqrt{\delta_{m}}} = \dfrac{29.9}{\sqrt{\delta_{mm}/1000}} = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{mm}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_c \approx \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{mm}}}\ \text{rpm}\,}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Critical Speed

**Given:** A turbine rotor weighs 12 000 kg. The static mid-span deflection under its own weight is measured as 0.32 mm.

**Find:** The first lateral critical speed in rpm.

**Solution:**

Applying the Rayleigh formula:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{0.32}}
$$

Computing the square root:

$$
\sqrt{0.32} = 0.5657
$$

Therefore:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{946}{0.5657} \approx 1672\ \text{rpm}
$$

**Verification by direct method:**

Stiffness from Step 1 of §3.1:

$$
k = \dfrac{m g}{\delta} = \dfrac{12000 \times 9.81}{0.32 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.679 \times 10^{8}\ \text{N/m}
$$

Natural frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.679 \times 10^{8}}{12000}} = \sqrt{30658} = 175.1\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Convert to rpm:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{60 \times 175.1}{2\pi} \approx 1672\ \text{rpm}\ \ \checkmark
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Acceleration Rate from Newton's Second Law for Rotation

**Step 1 — Newton's rotational equation:**

$$
J\,\dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = T_{net} = T_{steam} - T_{loss}
$$

**Step 2 — Express in rpm (let $N$ be rotational speed in rpm; $\omega = 2\pi N/60$):**

$$
J \cdot \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}\right) = T_{net}
$$

$$
\dfrac{2\pi J}{60}\,\dfrac{dN}{dt} = T_{net}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for acceleration rate $\alpha \equiv dN/dt$:**

$$
\boxed{\,\alpha = \dfrac{dN}{dt} = \dfrac{60\,T_{net}}{2\pi J} = \dfrac{30\,T_{net}}{\pi J}\,}
$$

### 3.4 Worked Example — Time to Cross the Critical Band

**Given:** Combined moment of inertia $J = 8500\ \text{kg·m}^2$. Net accelerating torque during run-up $T_{net} = 18\,000\ \text{N·m}$. The rotor must transit through the critical band of $N_c \pm 60$ rpm (i.e., total span = 120 rpm).

**Find:** Time to traverse the critical band safely (less than 30 s recommended).

**Solution:**

Compute acceleration rate using §3.3:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{30 \times 18000}{\pi \times 8500} = \dfrac{540000}{26704} \approx 20.22\ \text{rpm/s}
$$

That is $20.22 \times 60 = 1213$ rpm/min.

Time to traverse 120 rpm:

$$
t = \dfrac{\Delta N}{\alpha} = \dfrac{120}{20.22} \approx 5.93\ \text{s}
$$

**Conclusion:** Only ~6 seconds is spent in the critical band — well within safe limits. If the OEM maximum were 30 s, then minimum acceleration rate must be:

$$
\alpha_{min} = \dfrac{120}{30} = 4\ \text{rpm/s}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation 3 — Thermal Stress in a Solid Cylinder During Rapid Heating

Consider a long solid rotor of radius *r* suddenly exposed to steam at temperature $T_s$ while internally at $T_i$. The surface tries to expand but is constrained by the cooler core → **compressive surface stress, tensile core stress**.

**Step 1 — Heat-conduction equation in cylindrical co-ordinates (radial only):**

$$
\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha_t\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 T}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Define dimensionless groups:**

$$
\theta^* = \dfrac{T - T_i}{T_s - T_i},\qquad Bi = \dfrac{h\,r}{k},\qquad Fo = \dfrac{\alpha_t\,t}{r^2}
$$

The boundary conditions become:
- At $r = R$ (surface): $-k\,\partial T/\partial r = h(T_s - T_s) = 0$ initially; as $t \to \infty$, $T \to T_s$.
- At $r = 0$ (axis): symmetry → $\partial T/\partial r = 0$.

**Step 3 — Plane-strain assumption:**
The axial strain $\varepsilon_z = 0$, so the axial stress is:

$$
\sigma_z = \dfrac{E\,\alpha_t\,(T - T_{ref})}{1-\nu} + \text{(radial + hoop terms, neglected for thin annulus)}
$$

For a **solid cylinder**, the maximum thermal stress at the centre (immediately after surface heating) is bounded by:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{E\,\alpha_t\,(T_s - T_i)}{1-\nu}\,\Phi(Bi, Fo)
$$

where $\Phi(Bi, Fo)$ is a chart-function with $\Phi \to 1$ for $Bi \to \infty$ (infinite surface heat-transfer) and $\Phi \to 0$ for $Fo \to \infty$ (steady state, no gradient).

**Step 4 — Practical limit on acceleration rate:**
For a rotor of typical dimensions and steam temperature, the OEM supplies a chart of allowable $\Delta T$ vs time. The acceleration rate must be such that:

$$
\sigma_{th}(t) \le \sigma_{allow}
$$

Which in practical terms means:

$$
\dfrac{dN}{dt}\Big|_{max} \propto \dfrac{1}{T_s - T_i}
$$

Cold start → large $\Delta T$ → **slow** acceleration; hot start → small $\Delta T$ → **fast** acceleration.

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Cold Start | Warm Start | Hot Start |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial rotor temp $T_i$ | < 150 °C | 200 – 350 °C | > 350 °C |
| Acceleration rate | 60–120 rpm/min | 150–300 rpm/min | 400–600 rpm/min |
| Hold-point duration | 30–60 min | 15–30 min | < 5 min |
| $T_s - T_i$ | 450 K | 250 K | 80 K |
| Thermal stress peak | High | Medium | Low |
| Total start time | 6–10 h | 2–4 h | 30–60 min |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Block Diagram (Run-Up Sequencer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OPERATOR HMI START PUSH"] --> B["PERMISSIVE MATRIX BOOLEAN LOGIC"]
    B --> C{"ALL PERMISSIVES TRUE"}
    C -- NO --> D["HOLD AND ALARM ON DCS"]
    C -- YES --> E["TURNING GEAR ENGAGE 1 TO 3 RPM"]
    E --> F["LUBE OIL PRIME 1.5 BAR"]
    F --> G["CONTROL OIL ESTABLISH 120 BAR"]
    G --> H["CRANK STEAM ADMIT HP BYPASS"]
    H --> I["HOLD 600 RPM SOAK FOR THERMAL EQUALIZATION"]
    I --> J["RAMP 600 TO 1500 RPM"]
    J --> K["CRITICAL SPEED BYPASS FAST ACCELERATION"]
    K --> L["INTERMEDIATE HOLDS 1200 1800 2400 RPM"]
    L --> M["SYNC SPEED TRIM 2950 RPM"]
    M --> N["VOLTAGE AND FREQUENCY MATCH"]
    N --> O["BREAKER CLOSE SYNC TO GRID"]
    O --> P["LOAD RAMP TRANSFER TO LOAD CONTROL"]

    Q["TSI TACHOMETER PROXIMITY VIBRATION ECCENTRICITY"] --> R["SAFETY PLC INDEPENDENT TRIP CHANNELS"]
    R -- "OVERSPEED VIBRATION ECCENTRICITY" --> S["FAST CLOSURE TRIP"]
    S --> T["EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN SEQUENCE"]

    U["THERMAL COUPLE RTD ON ROTOR AND CASING"] --> V["PROFILE GENERATOR STRESS COMPUTATION"]
    V --> W["REFERENCE SPEED NREF T"]
    W --> J

    style A fill:#E8F4FD
    style D fill:#FCE4D6
    style S fill:#F8CBAD
    style T fill:#E06666
    style O fill:#D9EAD3
    style P fill:#B6D7A3
```

### 4.2 Closed-Loop Speed Control During Run-Up

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["REFERENCE SPEED FROM PROFILE GENERATOR"] --> SUM["SUMMING JUNCTION ERROR"]
    ACT["ACTUAL SPEED FROM TSI TACHOMETER"] --> SUM
    SUM --> PID["PID CONTROLLER EHG"]
    PID --> SV["ELECTRO HYDRAULIC SERVO VALVE"]
    SV --> HPU["HP HYDRAULIC ACTUATOR ON VALVE"]
    HPU --> VALVE["HP STEAM VALVE OR FUEL VALVE"]
    VALVE --> TURBINE["TURBINE ROTOR"]
    TURBINE --> SPD["ROTATIONAL SPEED N"]
    SPD --> ACT
```

### 4.3 Run-Up Speed vs Time Profile (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["0 RPM CRANK"] -->|"Slow ramp 60 rpm/min"| B1["600 RPM HOLD 30 MIN"]
    B1 -->|"Ramp 100 rpm/min"| C1["1200 RPM HOLD 15 MIN"]
    C1 -->|"Fast ramp 250 rpm/min"| D1["1800 RPM CRITICAL BAND FAST PASS"]
    D1 -->|"Ramp 200 rpm/min"| E1["2400 RPM HOLD 10 MIN"]
    E1 -->|"Ramp 150 rpm/min"| F1["2950 RPM SYNC HOLD"]
    F1 -->|"Synchronize"| G1["3000 RPM LOADED"]
```

### 4.4 Permissive Matrix Sample (Boolean Logic Table)

| # | Permissive | Signal Source | Logic | Trip Action if False |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lube oil pressure ≥ 1.2 bar | PT on header | AND | Master trip |
| 2 | Turning gear engaged | Limit switch | AND | Hold at crank |
| 3 | Condenser vacuum ≥ 0.85 bar | Vacuum PT | AND | Hold at crank |
| 4 | HP/IP bypass valves closed | Limit switch | AND | Hold at crank |
| 5 | Vibration < 6 mm/s RMS | TSI vibration probe | AND | Abort run-up |
| 6 | Rotor eccentricity < 50 µm | TSI prox probe | AND | Re-barring required |
| 7 | Drum level ≥ 50 % | LT drum | AND | Master hold |
| 8 | Fire detected (gas T) | UV/IR flame scanner | AND | No light-off |
| 9 | HP steam temp ≥ 400 °C | TT pipe | AND | Hold before ramp |

### 4.5 TSI (Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation) Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TSIBLOCK["TSI CHASSIS"]
    P1["SPEED PICKUP MAGNETIC"] --> TSIBLOCK
    P2["AXIAL DISPLACEMENT PROX"] --> TSIBLOCK
    P3["VIBRATION SEISMIC PROBE"] --> TSIBLOCK
    P4["ECCENTRICITY PROX"] --> TSIBLOCK
    P5["CASE EXPANSION LVDT"] --> TSIBLOCK
    P6["DIFFERENTIAL EXPANSION"] --> TSIBLOCK
    P7["BEARING METAL TEMP RTD"] --> TSIBLOCK
    TSIBLOCK --> REL["RELAY OUTPUTS TO DCS AND SAFETY PLC"]
    TSIBLOCK --> DISP["DISPLAY ON HMI"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**

Define the term "automatic turbine run-up system." List any **four** typical hold-point speeds during a cold start of a 250 MW steam turbine.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An automatic turbine run-up system (ATRUS) is a coordinated electro-hydraulic and digital control system that brings a turbine from standstill to rated speed by automatically sequencing the admission of working fluid, maintaining the rotor within safe limits of acceleration, vibration, and thermal stress. It executes pre-engineered speed profiles with intermediate hold points to allow thermal equalisation.

Four typical hold-point speeds for a 250 MW, 3000 rpm unit are:

1. **600 rpm** – initial soaking hold for rotor thermal bowing relief.
2. **1200 rpm** – second hold below first critical speed.
3. **2400 rpm** – post-critical hold for vibration decay and HP/IP metal-temperature equalisation.
4. **2950 rpm** – synchronisation speed (just below 3000 rpm).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention the synchronisation hold (2950 rpm) and end with 3000 rpm. Marks are lost because synchronisation is conceptually a *hold*, not a *synchronous-load speed*. Also, do not write "3000 rpm hold" — that is a contradiction in terms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

What is the difference between a **critical speed** and an **operating speed**? Why is the run-up system designed to pass through the critical speed rapidly?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Critical speed** $N_c$ is the rotational speed at which the rotor's natural frequency coincides with the forcing frequency (once per revolution), causing large resonant lateral vibrations of the shaft. It is determined by the rotor's mass and lateral stiffness.

**Operating speed** $N_{op}$ is the steady-state rated speed (e.g., 3000 rpm for a 50 Hz unit) at which the unit is designed to operate continuously.

The run-up system is designed to **pass through** $N_c$ rapidly because:
- The amplitude of vibration at resonance is $A = A_0 / (2\zeta)$ for a damped system, where $\zeta$ is the damping ratio (typically only 0.02–0.08).
- The shorter the dwell time in the resonant band, the less cumulative fatigue damage the shaft undergoes.
- Therefore, OEM-specified acceleration rates are **highest** in the critical-speed band.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students sometimes confuse critical speed with whirling speed or synchronous speed. The correct phrasing is *"coincidence of forcing frequency with natural frequency."*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) regulations, Module 4 carries one 14-mark question with **internal choice**. Two alternative questions are provided below. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 4 Choice 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture** of an automatic turbine run-up system. Describe the role of the profile generator and the permissive matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Enumerate the **nine major TSI parameters** monitored during run-up and explain any **three** of them in detail, stating their typical alarm and trip thresholds. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The functional architecture of an automatic turbine run-up system consists of four interlocking layers:

**Layer 1 — Permissive Matrix:**
This is a hardwired / PLC-based Boolean logic matrix that receives field signals (pressure switches, level switches, limit switches, flame scanners). All "AND" conditions must be TRUE before the sequencer advances to the next step. For a typical 250 MW unit, the matrix contains 35–45 individual permissives. Typical examples include: lube-oil header pressure $\ge 1.2$ bar, control oil pressure $\ge 120$ bar, condenser vacuum $\ge 0.85$ bar, drum level $\ge 50$ %, HP bypass valves closed, turning gear engaged, fire detected (gas turbines), vibration probe healthy.

**Layer 2 — Profile Generator:**
This is a software module (typically implemented in the DCS function block library or in a dedicated sequencer PLC). It computes a reference speed $N_{ref}(t)$ as a function of:
- Elapsed time $t$
- Measured rotor-metal temperature $T_{metal}$
- Temperature rate of change $dT/dt$
- OEM-supplied acceleration curves

The output is a ramp-and-hold waveform with soak periods at 600, 1200, 1800, 2400, and 2950 rpm.

**Layer 3 — Speed Closed Loop (EHG):**
The Electro-Hydraulic Governor compares $N_{ref}$ with measured speed $N_{actual}$ (from magnetic pickup or toothed wheel) and generates a servo-valve current that positions the HP steam valve. Inner loop bandwidth ≈ 5–10 Hz.

**Layer 4 — Trip Protection:**
Independent hardwired channels on overspeed, axial displacement, vibration, and rotor eccentricity override the sequencer and command fast-closing valves within milliseconds.

A schematic block diagram is provided in **§4.1** above (refer to the mermaid flowchart). The role of the profile generator is to ensure thermal-stress compliance, while the permissive matrix ensures pre-conditions for safety and operability.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The **Turbine Supervisory Instrumentation (TSI)** monitors the following nine parameters during run-up:

| # | Parameter | Typical Alarm | Typical Trip |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speed (rpm) | ± 5 % of $N_{ref}$ | 110 % of $N_{rated}$ |
| 2 | Axial displacement (mm) | ± 0.4 mm | ± 0.9 mm |
| 3 | Vibration (mm/s RMS) | 4.5 mm/s | 6.0–12.7 mm/s |
| 4 | Rotor eccentricity (µm) | 50 µm | 100 µm |
| 5 | Case expansion (mm) | rate > 5 mm/h | rate > 12 mm/h |
| 6 | Differential expansion (mm) | ± 0.4 mm | ± 0.9 mm |
| 7 | Bearing metal temperature (°C) | 95 °C | 110 °C |
| 8 | Thrust bearing oil temperature (°C) | 80 °C | 90 °C |
| 9 | Turning gear engaged (boolean) | Not engaged > 5 min | Master hold |

**Explanation of three parameters:**

1. **Speed** is sensed using a magnetic pickup with a toothed wheel (60 teeth = 1 pulse per degree, or one pulse per revolution via key-photo sensor). Alarm threshold is typically ± 5 % of $N_{ref}$, and the **mechanical overspeed trip** is set at 110 % of rated (3300 rpm for 3000 rpm unit). The trip is independent and mechanically activated (centrifugal weights), satisfying IEC 61508 SIL-3.

2. **Vibration** is measured using seismic velocity probes mounted radially on each bearing housing. The alarm is set per ISO 10816-3 zones: zone A (good) ≤ 2.8 mm/s, zone B (acceptable) ≤ 4.5 mm/s, zone C (unsatisfactory) ≤ 7.1 mm/s, zone D (unacceptable) > 7.1 mm/s. Trip is typically at 12.7 mm/s to allow for transient spikes during critical-speed pass.

3. **Rotor eccentricity** is the displacement of the rotor centreline from the bearing centreline, measured using a non-contact eddy-current proximity probe mounted axially. During run-up, eccentricity must be monitored because thermal bowing of the rotor (due to uneven heating) can cause rubs. The alarm is at 50 µm, and the trip / abort is at 100 µm. If eccentricity is excessive, the rotor is re-barred on the turning gear before re-attempting.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall for 14-mark question:** Do **not** list the nine parameters without explaining alarm/trip thresholds. KTU examiners award 7 marks to part (b) only if **three parameters** are explained with **both alarm and trip values plus the physical reason**. A bare list scores at most 2–3 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary, Module 4 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Draw the typical **speed vs time curve** for a cold start of a 250 MW steam turbine. Label all hold points, the critical-speed band, and the synchronisation point. Discuss the **physical reason** for each hold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Rayleigh's method**, derive the formula for the first lateral critical speed of a uniform simply-supported rotor. A rotor weighs **15 000 kg** and has a measured static deflection of **0.45 mm**. Compute the first critical speed in rpm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The typical speed-vs-time curve for a 250 MW, 3000 rpm cold start is plotted below in conceptual form (refer to §4.3 mermaid sketch for structural representation):

- **0 → 600 rpm** (slow ramp, 60 rpm/min): Initial steam admission through HP bypass; rotor bowing relief.
- **Hold at 600 rpm** (≈ 30 min): Allows rotor metal temperature to equalise; HP and IP casings warm up uniformly to prevent differential expansion issues.
- **600 → 1200 rpm** (100 rpm/min): Conservative ramp.
- **Hold at 1200 rpm** (≈ 15 min): Vibration decay, bearing oil film stabilises.
- **1200 → 1500 rpm** (fast ramp): Pre-critical preparation.
- **Critical band 1600–1800 rpm**: Rapid pass-through at the **highest** permissible acceleration (≈ 250 rpm/min) to minimise resonant dwell.
- **1800 → 2400 rpm** (200 rpm/min): Reduced acceleration below critical.
- **Hold at 2400 rpm** (≈ 10 min): Post-critical vibration decay; thermal equilibration.
- **2400 → 2950 rpm** (150 rpm/min): Conservative ramp.
- **Hold at 2950 rpm** (≈ 5 min): Synchronisation to grid.
- **2950 → 3000 rpm**: Synchroscope closure; load pickup.

**Physical reasons for holds:**

- **600 rpm hold**: Thermal bowing of rotor from uneven heating (top half in steam, bottom half cool) can cause rubs. Hold allows bottom half to heat by conduction.
- **1200 rpm hold**: Bearing oil-film formation is speed-dependent (minimum film thickness reached around 1500 rpm in many designs); hold stabilises the film.
- **2400 rpm hold**: After critical-speed pass, residual vibration must decay to < 4.5 mm/s before allowing further ramp.
- **2950 rpm hold**: Synchronisation requires stable voltage, frequency, and phase angle for at least 30 seconds before breaker closure.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Derivation:**

Step 1: When the rotor is supported at both bearings and rotates about its own axis, gravity causes a mid-span static deflection $\delta$. Equilibrium under gravity gives:

$$
m g = k \delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{m g}{\delta}
$$

Step 2: At angular velocity $\omega$, the rotor experiences a centrifugal force $m \omega^2 y$ where *y* is the dynamic deflection. The elastic restoring force is *k y*. At resonance (natural frequency), these are equal:

$$
m \omega_n^2 y = k y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_n^2 = \dfrac{k}{m}
$$

Step 3: Substituting $k/m = g/\delta$:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}
$$

Step 4: Converting to rpm:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\,\omega_n = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\,\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}} = \dfrac{9.549 \sqrt{g}}{\sqrt{\delta}}
$$

For $g = 9.81$ m/s² and $\delta$ in mm, noting that $\delta_{m} = \delta_{mm}/1000$:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{9.549 \sqrt{9.81}}{\sqrt{\delta_{m}}} = \dfrac{29.9}{\sqrt{\delta_{m}}} = \dfrac{29.9}{\sqrt{\delta_{mm}/1000}} = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{mm}}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,N_c \approx \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{\delta_{mm}}}\ \text{rpm}\,}
$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

Given: $m = 15\,000$ kg, $\delta = 0.45$ mm.

$$
N_c = \dfrac{946}{\sqrt{0.45}} = \dfrac{946}{0.6708} \approx 1410\ \text{rpm}
$$

**Verification by direct calculation:**

Stiffness:

$$
k = \dfrac{m g}{\delta} = \dfrac{15000 \times 9.81}{0.45 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.267 \times 10^{8}\ \text{N/m}
$$

Natural angular frequency:

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3.267 \times 10^{8}}{15000}} = \sqrt{21780} = 147.6\ \text{rad/s}
$$

Critical speed in rpm:

$$
N_c = \dfrac{60 \times 147.6}{2\pi} = \dfrac{8856}{6.283} \approx 1410\ \text{rpm}\ \ \checkmark
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall for 14-mark question:** A common error is using $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta}}$ but **forgetting to convert** $\delta$ from mm to m. This produces a critical speed that is wrong by a factor of $\sqrt{1000} \approx 31.6$. Always explicitly write the conversion step. Also, Rayleigh's formula gives the **first** critical speed assuming a simply-supported uniform beam; for multi-mass rotors, the Dunkerley or Holzer methods are required.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [x] **ATRUS** = Automatic Turbine Run-Up System, a sequencer NOT the governor.
- [x] **Run-up sequence**: Crank → 600 (hold) → 1200 (hold) → critical band (fast pass) → 2400 (hold) → 2950 (sync hold) → 3000 (load).
- [x] **Critical speed formula (Rayleigh)**: $N_c \approx 946 / \sqrt{\delta_{mm}}$ rpm.
- [x] **Acceleration rate**: $\alpha = 30\,T_{net}/(\pi J)$ in rpm/s; cold starts → low α, hot starts → high α.
- [x] **Thermal stress**: $\sigma_{th} \propto E \alpha_t \Delta T / (1-\nu)$; depends on Bi and Fo.
- [x] **Critical speed band must be passed rapidly** to limit vibration dwell time.
- [x] **Permissive matrix** has 35–45 Boolean conditions; all must be TRUE.
- [x] **TSI monitors** 9+ parameters: speed, vibration, eccentricity, axial displacement, differential expansion, case expansion, bearing temps, etc.
- [x] **Mechanical overspeed trip** is set at **110 % of rated** and is independent of the electronic governor (fail-safe).
- [x] **Typical cold-start duration**: 6–10 hours; **hot-start duration**: 30–60 minutes.
- [x] **TSI trip thresholds**: vibration 12.7 mm/s, eccentricity 100 µm, axial displacement ± 0.9 mm.
- [x] **OEM acceleration curves** are mandatory; OEM warranty is **void** if acceleration rate is exceeded.
- [x] **Profile generator** is the "brain" — it dynamically recomputes $N_{ref}(t)$ based on thermal feedback.
- [x] **Independent hardwired trip** channels override the sequencer — never automate trip logic in software only.
- [x] **EHC (Electro-Hydraulic Control)** fluid pressure: 120 bar typical; **lube oil**: 1.5 bar typical.
- [x] **Acceleration through critical band**: pass in **less than 30 s**, ideally **less than 10 s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute exam tip:** When asked to "explain the run-up system" in 14 marks, structure the answer as **(i) sequence diagram, (ii) layer-by-layer description, (iii) typical hold points with reason, (iv) thermal-stress rationale, (v) TSI role.** Skipping any one of these will cost at least 2 marks.
